name: SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION

topics.title: Introduction to different types of materials used in shipbuilding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to different types of materials used in shipbuilding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shipbuilding Materials** are the carefully selected engineering materials — ferrous, non-ferrous, composite, and timber-based — that satisfy the combined demands of **structural integrity**, **marine corrosion resistance**, **weldability**, **formability**, and **classification-society approval** for the construction of hulls, superstructures, propellers, machinery foundations, and outfit components of a marine vessel.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, *shipbuilding materials* refer to the **graded, certified, and traceable raw stock** (plates, sections, castings, forgings, pipes, and rivets) procured from approved steelmakers and supplied under a **Mill Test Certificate (MTC)** that conforms to the rules of classification societies such as **IRS (Indian Register of Shipping)**, **Lloyd's Register**, **DNV**, **ABS**, or **Bureau Veritas**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus opens the course by tracing the **evolution of shipbuilding materials** from naturally available timber → wrought iron → mild steel → high-tensile steel → aluminium alloys → fibre-reinforced composites. The student must appreciate *why* each transition happened in response to the demands of larger tonnage, higher speed, deeper draughts, and harsher operational environments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ship is a Floating City"

Imagine you are constructing a **floating city** that must:
- Carry its own weight plus cargo across thousands of kilometres,
- Survive **continuous saltwater spray**, **UV radiation**, **temperature cycling**, and **wave slamming**,
- Remain afloat even if **partially flooded**,
- Be **built fast, cheaply, and reliably** in a shipyard.

Just as a civil engineer selects *bricks for walls*, *steel rods for RCC*, *glass for windows*, and *aluminium for window frames*, a naval architect selects **different materials for different parts of the ship**:

| Ship Part | Material Analogy (Civil Building) | Marine Material Used |
|---|---|---|
| Hull shell (skin) | RCC columns & beams | Mild Steel / AH36 / DH36 plates |
| Superstructure | Brick walls | HTS plates (lighter weight) |
| Funnel / cowl | Aluminium window frames | Aluminium alloy (5083, 6082) |
| Propeller | Cast iron gears | Manganese Bronze / Ni-Al Bronze |
| Mast | Steel scaffolding | Tubular steel / Aluminium |
| Small boats on deck | Wooden furniture | FRP / GRP composite |
| Doors, hatches | Timber doors | Marine plywood / aluminium |
| Pipes | GI pipes | Copper-nickel (Cu-Ni 90/10, 70/30) |

This **"one ship – many materials"** philosophy is the single most important takeaway of Module 1.

## 1.3 Categories of Shipbuilding Materials — Master Classification

The complete family of shipbuilding materials is organised into **four broad families**:

1. **Ferrous Materials** (iron + carbon + alloying elements)
   - Mild Steel (MS / Grade A, B, D, E)
   - High-Tensile Steel (HTS) — AH32, AH36, DH32, DH36, EH32, EH36
   - Stainless Steel (304, 316, duplex)
   - Cast Iron, Cast Steel, Forged Steel

2. **Non-Ferrous Metals & Alloys**
   - Aluminium alloys (5083-O, 5086, 6082-T6, 5383)
   - Copper alloys — Naval Brass, Muntz Metal, Aluminium Bronze, Manganese Bronze, Cu-Ni
   - Titanium alloys (submarines, seawater piping)
   - Zinc, Magnesium (sacrificial anodes)

3. **Composites & Polymers**
   - Glass-Reinforced Plastic (GRP / FRP)
   - Sandwich composites (PVC / balsa / honeycomb core)
   - Marine paints, rubber linings, neoprene

4. **Traditional / Auxiliary Materials**
   - Timber (teak, sal, oak) — decking, fenders, boat hulls
   - Marine plywood, decorative laminates
   - Glass wool, rock wool (insulation)
   - Concrete (ballast)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Ship Material Selection:**
> A marine material must simultaneously satisfy **four mandatory criteria** — (i) adequate **mechanical strength** at design temperature, (ii) proven **corrosion resistance** in seawater/salt-spray atmosphere, (iii) **weldability** with standard shipyard procedures, and (iv) **classification society approval**. Failure on any one criterion disqualifies the material — no matter how excellent it is on the other three.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Historical Evolution — *Why* Each Material Family Emerged

Understanding the **historical drivers** of material transition is a favourite KTU 2-mark and 3-mark question.

### Phase 1 — The Wooden Age (≈3000 BC – 1800 AD)
- **Material:** Teak, oak, sal, cedar, fir.
- **Driver:** Naturally available, easy to carve, buoyant, repairable.
- **Limitation:** Vulnerable to **marine borer attack** (*Teredo navalis*), **dry rot**, fire, and length-limited by tree height (~30 m hull max).

### Phase 2 — Wrought Iron & Composite Construction (1800 – 1880)
- **Material:** Wrought-iron straps over timber frames.
- **Driver:** Industrial Revolution enabled rolling of iron plates.
- **Limitation:** Low ductility, prone to brittle fracture.

### Phase 3 — The Steel Revolution (1880 – 1945)
- **Material:** Mild steel plates produced by the **Siemens-Martin (open-hearth)** and later **Bessemer / LD converter** processes.
- **Driver:** Ships became larger (e.g., *Great Eastern*, *Titanic*) — timber could not supply the volume.
- **Breakthrough:** Steel delivered **yield strength ≈ 235 MPa** with excellent weldability and ductility.

### Phase 4 — High-Tensile & Special Steels (1945 – 1990)
- **Material:** Normalised, controlled-rolled, and TMCP (Thermo-Mechanically Controlled Processed) steels such as **AH36, DH36, EH36**.
- **Driver:** Demand for **higher strength-to-weight ratio** → thinner plates → increased payload.
- **Key property:** Yield strength elevated to **315 – 355 MPa** while retaining good low-temperature toughness.

### Phase 5 — Aluminium, Composites & Hybrid Hulls (1990 – Present)
- **Materials:** Al-Mg alloys (5083, 5086, 5383), GRP, sandwich composites, CFRP, titanium.
- **Driver:** **Lightweighting** for high-speed craft, naval vessels, and specialised applications (yachts, patrol boats, fast ferries, LNG carriers' secondary structures).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Mnemonic — "W-Iron-Steel-HTS-Al/Comp":**
> **W**ood → **Iron** → **Steel** → **HTS** → **Aluminium/Composites**

## 2.2 Required Engineering Properties — The "Marine Performance Index"

A ship is simultaneously subjected to **static** (cargo, buoyancy), **dynamic** (wave slamming, sloshing), **thermal** (engine room heat, polar waters), and **chemical** (seawater, bilge, oils) loads. The selected material must perform under all of these. The **mandatory properties** are tabulated below:

| Property | Why it matters at sea | Typical shipbuilding value |
|---|---|---|
| Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$) | Resists permanent deformation under hull bending | 235 MPa (MS) – 355 MPa (EH36) |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_u$) | Provides safety margin before fracture | 400 – 490 MPa |
| Elongation ($\% \varepsilon$) | Ductility to absorb impact / collision | $\geq 22\%$ |
| Charpy Impact Energy | Resistance to brittle fracture in cold seas | 27 J @ 0°C (Grade A) → 27 J @ -40°C (EH36) |
| Corrosion Rate in seawater | Determines maintenance interval | < 0.1 mm/yr for steels with coating |
| Weldability (CE) | Must be joinable by SAW / SMAW / GMAW | Carbon Equivalent $\leq 0.45\%$ |
| Density ($\rho$) | Governs lightweighting & displacement | Steel = 7850 kg/m³, Al = 2700 kg/m³ |
| Modulus of Elasticity ($E$) | Controls hull deflection under bending | 210 GPa (steel), 70 GPa (Al) |
| Fatigue Limit ($\sigma_f$) | Cyclic wave loading endurance | 0.4 – 0.5 of $\sigma_u$ for steels |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table — Save This for the ESE**

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Engineering stress under axial load | MPa |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Engineering strain (elongation) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{M y}{I}$ | Flexure formula — hull girder bending | MPa |
| 4 | $\tau = \dfrac{V Q}{I b}$ | Shear stress in webs of girders | MPa |
| 5 | $\dfrac{P}{L} = w \cdot b$ | Hull weight per unit length | N/m |
| 6 | $\rho_{\text{ship}} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\nabla}$ | Ship density = displacement / volume | kg/m³ |
| 7 | $CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$ | **IIW Carbon Equivalent** for weldability | \% |
| 8 | $R_a = \dfrac{\sigma_u}{\sigma_y}$ | Strength ratio / safety reserve | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\sigma_f \approx 0.5 \sigma_u$ | Rotating-bending fatigue limit (steels) | MPa |
| 10 | $\Delta_{\text{light}} = \rho_{\text{water}} \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Archimedes buoyancy equation | N |
| 11 | $\eta_{\text{weld}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{weld}}}{\sigma_{\text{base}}}$ | Weld joint efficiency (typical 0.85 – 1.0) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $W_{\text{charpy}} \propto \dfrac{K_{IC}^2}{E}$ | Fracture-toughness relation (Irwin) | J vs MPa$\sqrt{m}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula above, $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa, $\sigma_u = 400$ MPa, $E = 210$ GPa are the **canonical values for Grade A mild steel** that KTU examiners expect as defaults unless a problem specifies otherwise.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Warships & Naval Vessels:** Use **HSLA steels (HY-80, HY-100)** with $\sigma_y \approx 550$–$690$ MPa for blast and shock resistance.
- **LNG Carriers:** Employ **9% Ni steel** for cryogenic tanks operating at $-163°$C.
- **Superstructure light-weighting:** Replacing steel with aluminium saves **~ 65% weight** at only **~ 35% stiffness penalty** — hence aluminium dominates the upper decks.
- **Propulsion:** Manganese–aluminium bronze and Ni-Al bronze propellers resist **cavitation erosion** and **seawater corrosion** simultaneously.
- **Piping:** Cu-Ni 90/10 for fire-fighting & cooling, Cu-Ni 70/30 for more corrosive brine service.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Hull Girder Bending Stress

A 150 m long cargo vessel is treated as a **box-girder hull** simply supported at its ends (extreme approximation) and loaded by a uniformly distributed weight from cargo and buoyancy. Derive the maximum bending stress at the deck (mid-span).

### Given Data
- Length between perpendiculars: $L = 150$ m
- Section modulus at deck: $Z_{\text{deck}} = 12.5 \times 10^6$ mm³
- Uniform load intensity: $w = 120$ kN/m
- Hull acts as a simply supported beam

### Step 1 — Total load on the equivalent beam

$$W_{\text{total}} = w \times L$$

Substituting the values:

$$W_{\text{total}} = 120 \text{ kN/m} \times 150 \text{ m}$$

$$W_{\text{total}} = 18{,}000 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 2 — Maximum bending moment at mid-span (for UDL)

For a simply supported beam carrying a uniformly distributed load, the maximum bending moment at the mid-span is given by the classical formula:

$$M_{\max} = \dfrac{w L^2}{8}$$

This is derived by integrating the shear diagram (zero at mid-span) from the support. We will use this directly.

$$M_{\max} = \dfrac{120 \times 150^2}{8}$$

$$M_{\max} = \dfrac{120 \times 22{,}500}{8}$$

$$M_{\max} = \dfrac{2{,}700{,}000}{8}$$

$$M_{\max} = 337{,}500 \text{ kN·m}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to N·mm (engineering SI consistency)

$$M_{\max} = 337{,}500 \times 10^3 \text{ N} \times 10^3 \text{ mm}$$

$$M_{\max} = 3.375 \times 10^{11} \text{ N·mm}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the flexure formula

$$\sigma_{\text{deck}} = \dfrac{M_{\max}}{Z_{\text{deck}}}$$

$$\sigma_{\text{deck}} = \dfrac{3.375 \times 10^{11}}{12.5 \times 10^6}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{deck}} = 2.7 \times 10^4 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 27 \text{ MPa}}$$

### Step 5 — Verification against Grade A mild steel

The allowable yield for Grade A mild steel is $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa. The factor of safety achieved is:

$$FoS = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{\text{deck}}}$$

$$FoS = \dfrac{235}{27} \approx 8.7$$

This **enormous safety margin** is expected — it confirms that **still-water bending stress** alone does not govern the hull girder design. The critical loads are **wave-induced (sagging + hogging) bending moments**, typically 3–4× higher.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this derivation matters in KTU exams:** Every alternate KTU ESE paper asks a hull-girder bending problem. Memorise $M_{\max} = wL^2/8$ for UDL and $M_{\max} = wL/4$ for central point load.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Carbon-Equivalent (CE) Weldability Check

A ship plate of chemical composition **C = 0.18 %, Mn = 1.20 %, Cr = 0.05 %, Mo = 0.02 %, V = 0.01 %, Ni = 0.03 %, Cu = 0.04 %** is offered for ship hull construction. Using the **IIW formula**, determine whether the plate is weldable as per standard shipyard practice.

### Step 1 — Write down the IIW formula

$$CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$

### Step 2 — Compute each contribution numerically

Carbon contribution:

$$C = 0.180\%$$

Manganese contribution:

$$\dfrac{Mn}{6} = \dfrac{1.20}{6} = 0.200\%$$

Chromium + Molybdenum + Vanadium contribution:

$$\dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} = \dfrac{0.05 + 0.02 + 0.01}{5} = \dfrac{0.08}{5} = 0.016\%$$

Nickel + Copper contribution:

$$\dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15} = \dfrac{0.03 + 0.04}{15} = \dfrac{0.07}{15} = 0.00467\%$$

### Step 3 — Sum all contributions

$$CE = 0.180 + 0.200 + 0.016 + 0.00467$$

$$CE \approx 0.4007\%$$

### Step 4 — Compare with acceptance criteria

| CE Range | Weldability Verdict |
|---|---|
| $\leq 0.40$ | Excellent — no preheat needed |
| $0.40 < CE \leq 0.45$ | Good — preheat to ~ 75°C recommended |
| $0.45 < CE \leq 0.60$ | Difficult — preheat + post-heat mandatory |
| $> 0.60$ | Not recommended for shipbuilding |

### Step 5 — Conclusion

$$\boxed{CE \approx 0.40\% \implies \text{Plate is weldable but preheating to } 75°\text{C is recommended.}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Many students calculate CE correctly but **forget to state the welding consequence**. Always finish with a *practical recommendation* — that is what fetches full marks.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Material Selection Snapshot

| Property / Family | Mild Steel (MS) | High-Tensile Steel (HTS) | Aluminium 5083-O | GRP Composite | Cu-Ni 90/10 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | **7850** | **7850** | **2700** | **1500 – 1800** | **8940** |
| $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | 235 | 315 – 355 | 125 – 215 | 150 – 400 (laminate) | 105 |
| $\sigma_u$ (MPa) | 400 – 490 | 440 – 530 | 275 – 350 | 200 – 600 | 280 |
| Modulus $E$ (GPa) | 210 | 210 | 70 | 10 – 30 (laminate) | 135 |
| Corrosion in seawater | Poor (needs coating) | Poor (needs coating) | Good (oxide film) | Excellent | Excellent |
| Weldability | Excellent | Good (with care) | Good (TIG/MIG) | Not welded — bonded | Good (TIG) |
| Cost (relative) | 1.0× | 1.15× | 3.5× | 4.0× | 6.0× |
| Typical shipboard use | Hull plates | Hatch covers, upper hull | Superstructure, fast craft | Small boats, radomes | Piping, heat exchangers |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how aluminium gives **only ~ 1/3 the stiffness** of steel — that is why aluminium superstructures must be **stiffened more aggressively** with closely spaced frames.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flow — Shipbuilding Materials Tree

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **complete hierarchy** of materials used in modern shipbuilding, mirroring the IACS (International Association of Classification Societies) rule-book categorisation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Shipbuilding Materials] --> B1[Ferrous Metals]
    A --> B2[Non-Ferrous Metals]
    A --> B3[Composites and Polymers]
    A --> B4[Auxiliary Materials]

    B1 --> C1A[Carbon Steel]
    B1 --> C1B[High Tensile Steel]
    B1 --> C1C[Stainless Steel]
    B1 --> C1D[Cast Iron and Cast Steel]

    C1A --> D1A[Grade A and B - Normal Strength]
    C1A --> D1B[Grade D and E - Low Temp Toughness]

    C1B --> D2A[AH32 and AH36 - Hull Structural]
    C1B --> D2B[DH32 and DH36 - Higher Toughness]
    C1B --> D2C[EH32 and EH36 - Arctic Service]
    C1B --> D2D[HSLA HY80 and HY100 - Naval]

    C1C --> D3A[Austenitic 304 and 316]
    C1C --> D3B[Duplex 2205 and 2507]

    B2 --> C2A[Aluminium Alloys]
    B2 --> C2B[Copper Alloys]
    B2 --> C2C[Titanium Alloys]
    B2 --> C2D[Zinc and Magnesium - Anodes]

    C2A --> D4A[5083 O - Marine Grade]
    C2A --> D4B[5086 H116 - Structural]
    C2A --> D4C[6082 T6 - Deck Fittings]

    C2B --> D5A[Naval Brass and Muntz Metal]
    C2B --> D5B[Manganese Bronze - Propellers]
    C2C --> D6A[Ti Grade 2 - Piping]
    C2C --> D6B[Ti Grade 5 - Submarine Hulls]

    B3 --> C3A[GRP and FRP]
    B3 --> C3B[Sandwich Composites]
    B3 --> C3C[Marine Paints and Rubber]

    C3A --> D7A[Hand Lay Up - Small Boats]
    C3A --> D7B[Resin Infusion - Patrol Boats]

    B4 --> C4A[Timber and Plywood]
    B4 --> C4B[Insulation Materials]
    B4 --> C4C[Concrete - Ballast]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style B1 fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    style B2 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style B3 fill:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff

```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Material Selection Logic for a Component

When a naval architect chooses a material, the decision flows through a deterministic logic sequence governed by **function → load → environment → fabrication → classification**. The block diagram below captures this expert reasoning.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Component to be designed] --> S2{Is it a primary hull structural part?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Apply IACS UR S21 Rules]
    S2 -- No --> S4{Is it superstructure or above deck?}
    S3 --> S5{Required yield strength more than 235 MPa?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Select HTS AH36 or DH36]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Select Mild Steel Grade A]

    S4 -- Yes --> S8{Is weight saving critical?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Select Aluminium 5083 or 6082]
    S8 -- No --> S10[Select HTS or MS as per rule]

    S4 -- No --> S11{Is it piping or heat exchanger?}
    S11 -- Yes --> S12[Select Cu-Ni 90/10 or 70/30]
    S11 -- No --> S13{Is it propeller or shaft?}
    S13 -- Yes --> S14[Select Manganese Bronze or Ni-Al Bronze]
    S13 -- No --> S15{Is it small craft or radome?}
    S15 -- Yes --> S16[Select GRP FRP Composite]
    S15 -- No --> S17[Refer Classification Society Rules]

    S6 --> E1[End: Material Selected and Approved]
    S7 --> E1
    S9 --> E1
    S10 --> E1
    S12 --> E1
    S14 --> E1
    S16 --> E1
    S17 --> E1

    style S1 fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    style E1 fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style S6 fill:#f59e0b
    style S9 fill:#f59e0b
    style S12 fill:#f59e0b
    style S14 fill:#f59e0b
    style S16 fill:#f59e0b
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Ship Cross-Section Showing Material Zoning

The following **block-level functional architecture** illustrates how a single ship cross-section is zoned by material, with the reasoning behind each zone.

```
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  ZONE A — SUPERSTRUCTURE                    |
|        Material : Aluminium 5083-O (lightweighting)         |
|        Reason   : Reduces top-weight, raises GM stability   |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  ZONE B — UPPER HULL PLATING                |
|        Material : HTS AH36 (higher strength, thinner plate) |
|        Reason   : Same scantlings, reduced steel weight     |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  ZONE C — MAIN HULL (BELT, BOTTOM, SIDES)   |
|        Material : Mild Steel Grade A / B (workhorse)        |
|        Reason   : Excellent weldability, low cost, easy     |
|                   repair at any shipyard worldwide          |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  ZONE D — DOUBLE BOTTOM / BALLAST TANKS     |
|        Material : Mild Steel + cementitious coating         |
|        Reason   : Corrosion allowance + sacrificial margin   |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  ZONE E — PROPELLER & SHAFT                 |
|        Material : Manganese-Aluminium Bronze (Ni-Al bronze) |
|        Reason   : Cavitation + corrosion resistance         |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  ZONE F — SEAWATER PIPING                   |
|        Material : Cu-Ni 90/10 (90% Cu, 10% Ni)              |
|        Reason   : Biofouling resistance, low corrosion      |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked *"Why is the superstructure made of aluminium?"*, your answer must contain three keywords — **(i) weight reduction**, **(ii) increased metacentric height (GM)**, **(iii) above the waterline so corrosion less critical**. Missing any keyword costs a mark.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PENST414]`

**"List any four mechanical properties that a steel plate used in ship hull construction must possess, with one-line justifications."**

**Model Answer (4 × 0.75 marks per point):**

1. **High Yield Strength ($\sigma_y \geq 235$ MPa):** To resist hull girder bending moments caused by wave loading and cargo distribution without permanent deformation.

2. **Adequate Toughness (Charpy Impact Energy $\geq 27$ J at service temperature):** To prevent brittle fracture, especially in cold-water service (polar routes) where the ductile-to-brittle transition temperature is critical.

3. **Good Elongation ($\% \varepsilon \geq 22\%$):** Provides ductility so that the plate can absorb collision or berthing impact energy without catastrophic cracking.

4. **Weldability (Carbon Equivalent $CE \leq 0.45$):** Allows the plate to be joined using standard shipyard arc-welding processes (SMAW, SAW, GMAW) without hydrogen-induced cracking.

5. *(Bonus point)* **Corrosion Resistance or Adequate Corrosion Allowance:** The plate is supplied with a calculated extra thickness (corrosion allowance ~ 1–2 mm per side) so that structural integrity is maintained throughout the design life.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PENST414]`

**"Differentiate between Mild Steel (Grade A) and High-Tensile Steel (AH36) used in shipbuilding. State any three points."**

**Model Answer (3 × 1 mark per point):**

| # | Mild Steel (Grade A) | High-Tensile Steel (AH36) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Yield strength $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa (lower) | Yield strength $\sigma_y = 355$ MPa (~ 50% higher) |
| 2 | Used in **lightly stressed** hull regions — bottom, side shell, bulkheads | Used in **highly stressed** regions — sheer strake, hatch coamings, upper decks where weight saving matters |
| 3 | Better weldability, no preheating required up to 25 mm thickness | Requires controlled welding procedure with possible preheating above 25 mm thickness |
| 4 | Lower cost (~ 1.0× baseline) | Slightly costlier (~ 1.15×) but saves tonnage and reduces plate thickness |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **ONE full question** (either Q-A or Q-B). Both choices are kept at the same cognitive level (Apply / Analyse) per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **historical evolution of shipbuilding materials** from the wooden age to the modern composite era. Highlight the engineering driver behind each transition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier of length $L = 180$ m is treated as a simply supported hull girder under a uniformly distributed still-water load of $w = 95$ kN/m. The deck section modulus is $Z_{\text{deck}} = 14.6 \times 10^6$ mm³. The plate material is Grade A mild steel with $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa. Compute:
   (i) The maximum still-water bending moment at mid-span.
   (ii) The maximum bending stress at the deck.
   (iii) The factor of safety against yielding.
   (iv) Comment on whether still-water bending alone governs the design. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

The answer must be structured as a **timeline table or short paragraphs** covering:

| Era | Material | Engineering Driver |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-1800 | Timber (teak, oak) | Naturally available, buoyant, easily carved |
| 1800 – 1880 | Wrought iron + composite | Industrial revolution enabled iron-rolling |
| 1880 – 1945 | Mild steel (Bessemer / open-hearth) | Larger ships, longer hulls, deeper draughts |
| 1945 – 1990 | HTS (AH/DH/EH grades, TMCP) | Demand for lighter, stronger hulls |
| 1990 – Present | Aluminium, GRP, CFRP, hybrid | Lightweighting, high-speed craft, corrosion-free superstructures |

Award 1 mark per era + 2 marks for "engineering driver" reasoning.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**(i) Maximum still-water bending moment**

The hull girder under uniform load (simply supported) has the classical mid-span bending moment:

$$M_{\max} = \dfrac{w L^2}{8}$$

Substituting the values:

$$M_{\max} = \dfrac{95 \times 180^2}{8}$$

$$M_{\max} = \dfrac{95 \times 32{,}400}{8}$$

$$M_{\max} = \dfrac{3{,}078{,}000}{8}$$

$$\boxed{M_{\max} = 384{,}750 \text{ kN·m} \approx 3.85 \times 10^8 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**(ii) Maximum bending stress at the deck**

Converting the moment into N·mm:

$$M_{\max} = 384{,}750 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 3.8475 \times 10^{11} \text{ N·mm}$$

Applying the flexure formula:

$$\sigma_{\text{deck}} = \dfrac{M_{\max}}{Z_{\text{deck}}}$$

$$\sigma_{\text{deck}} = \dfrac{3.8475 \times 10^{11}}{14.6 \times 10^6}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{deck}} = 2.636 \times 10^4 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 26.4 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(iii) Factor of safety**

$$FoS = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{\text{deck}}} = \dfrac{235}{26.4}$$

$$\boxed{FoS \approx 8.9}$$

**(iv) Design Comment**

> Still-water bending stress gives a FoS of ~ 8.9 which is **very high**. However, hull girder design is **NOT** governed by still-water bending alone. The critical design loads are **wave-induced hogging and sagging moments**, which can be 3 – 4 times the still-water value. Classification society rules (e.g., IACS Common Structural Rules) require combining still-water + wave bending to obtain the design bending moment $M_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{sw}} + M_{\text{wv}}$. Hence still-water stress is only a fraction of the actual stress the hull experiences during service.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **categories of non-ferrous materials** used in shipbuilding. Mention specific alloys, their composition, and at least one typical shipboard application for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship plate has the following chemical composition by weight: **C = 0.16 %, Mn = 1.40 %, Cr = 0.08 %, Mo = 0.03 %, V = 0.02 %, Ni = 0.05 %, Cu = 0.04 %.** Using the **IIW Carbon-Equivalent formula**, calculate the CE value and state whether the plate is weldable. If preheating is needed, recommend an approximate preheat temperature. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

The answer must cover **all four sub-families** of non-ferrous materials with at least one named alloy each:

**1. Aluminium Alloys (lightweight construction)**
- *5083-O* — Al + 4.5% Mg + 0.7% Mn +微量 Cr — Excellent seawater corrosion resistance — **Superstructure panels, deckhouses, helidecks.**
- *6082-T6* — Al + Mg + Si — Heat-treatable, higher strength — **Masts, gangways, lifeboat davits.**

**2. Copper Alloys (piping, propellers, valves)**
- *Naval Brass (Cu-39% Zn-1% Sn)* — Good corrosion in seawater — **Tube plates, condenser shells.**
- *Manganese Bronze / Ni-Al Bronze* — Cu + 10–13% Mn + Al + Fe + Ni — High strength + cavitation resistance — **Ship propellers, pump impellers.**
- *Cu-Ni 90/10 and 70/30* — Cu + 10–30% Ni + Fe + Mn — Outstanding biofouling resistance — **Seawater cooling pipes, heat-exchanger tubes.**

**3. Titanium Alloys (high-end, corrosion-critical)**
- *CP Titanium Grade 2* — 99% Ti — Outstanding resistance to chloride pitting — **Submarine seawater piping, exhaust liners.**
- *Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5)* — High strength + light weight — **Submarine pressure hull fittings, fasteners.**

**4. Zinc and Magnesium (sacrificial anodes for cathodic protection)**
- *Zinc anodes — Zn + 0.1% Al + 0.05% Cd* — Protect steel hulls from galvanic corrosion.
- *Mg anodes — used in freshwater or low-conductivity environments (e.g., ballast tanks).*

*(Awarding: 1 mark per category + 1 mark for composition + 1 mark for application = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — State the IIW formula explicitly before plugging numbers:**

$$CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute each value:**

$$CE = 0.16 + \dfrac{1.40}{6} + \dfrac{0.08 + 0.03 + 0.02}{5} + \dfrac{0.05 + 0.04}{15}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each fraction:**

$$\dfrac{Mn}{6} = \dfrac{1.40}{6} = 0.2333\%$$

$$\dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} = \dfrac{0.13}{5} = 0.026\%$$

$$\dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15} = \dfrac{0.09}{15} = 0.006\%$$

**Step 4 — Sum all contributions:**

$$CE = 0.16 + 0.2333 + 0.026 + 0.006$$

$$\boxed{CE = 0.4253\% \approx 0.43\%}$$

**Step 5 — Weldability verdict:**

| CE Range | Verdict |
|---|---|
| $\leq 0.40$ | No preheat |
| $0.40 < CE \leq 0.45$ | **Preheat to 75 – 100°C** |
| $> 0.45$ | Preheat + post-heat |

Since $CE = 0.43\%$ falls in the **0.40 – 0.45** band, the plate is **weldable but preheating to 75°C** is recommended before commencing arc welding to prevent hydrogen-assisted cracking in the heat-affected zone (HAZ).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Students write $1.40$ as $1.40/6 = 0.23$ but forget the **% sign** in the final answer. Always carry the % unit through.
> 2. **Not stating the IIW formula explicitly** — Examiners award 1 mark purely for stating the formula even before substitution.
> 3. **Leaving the answer without a verdict** — A numerical CE without a concluding "weldable / not weldable" sentence loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Ignoring the recommendation** — Without the preheat temperature recommendation, the answer is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read This Twice Before the ESE**

- [x] **Four families of shipbuilding materials:** Ferrous, Non-Ferrous, Composites, Auxiliary.
- [x] **Historical chain:** Wood → Iron → Mild Steel → HTS → Aluminium / Composites.
- [x] **Grade A mild steel:** $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa, $\sigma_u = 400$ MPa, Charpy 27 J @ 0°C.
- [x] **HTS grades AH/DH/EH36:** $\sigma_y = 355$ MPa; EH stands for "Extra-High toughness" — for Arctic service.
- [x] **Aluminium 5083-O** is the marine workhorse for superstructure.
- [x] **Manganese-Aluminium Bronze** for propellers — cavitation + corrosion resistance.
- [x] **Cu-Ni 90/10** for seawater piping; **70/30** for more aggressive service.
- [x] **IIW Carbon Equivalent formula** must be memorised **letter-perfect** — every term with its denominator.
- [x] **CE ≤ 0.40** → no preheat; **0.40–0.45** → preheat 75°C; **> 0.45** → strict procedure.
- [x] **Hull girder formula** $M_{\max} = w L^2/8$ for UDL on a simply supported beam.
- [x] **Flexure formula** $\sigma = M/Z$ — know both bending and shear variants.
- [x] **Stiffness rule of thumb:** Aluminium gives **1/3 the stiffness** of steel → more stiffeners required.
- [x] **Selection criteria mnemonic — "SSWC":** **S**trength + **S**ea-water resistance + **W**eldability + **C**lassification approval.
- [x] **Mill Test Certificate (MTC)** is mandatory for every ship plate — without MTC, no classification society approval.
- [x] **Wood is still used today** for decking, fenders, and traditional boat hulls — it is *not* obsolete.
- [x] **Lightweighting justification:** Aluminium superstructure → reduces top-weight → raises GM → improves ship stability.
- [x] **Cryogenic steel (9% Ni)** for LNG carrier inner tanks; operate at $-163$°C.
- [x] **Composite GRP** is dominant in **small boats, fast ferries, and naval stealth vessels** (radar-absorbing).
- [x] **Always** end numerical answers with a **practical engineering conclusion** (e.g., "preheat to 75°C recommended", "factor of safety is acceptable").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material fabrication and service requirement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Material fabrication and service requirement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Fabrication and Service Requirement** in shipbuilding refers to the integrated engineering discipline that governs the selection, processing (fabrication), and in-service performance validation of metallic and non-metallic materials used in the construction of marine vessels, offshore structures, and submarine components. It bridges the gap between **material metallurgy**, **manufacturing processes**, and **operational demands** imposed by the marine environment.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic is studied as a foundational pillar because every subsequent module on corrosion, protection, and failure analysis depends on understanding:

1. **Fabrication Routes** – How raw materials are transformed into ship-grade components (rolling, forging, casting, welding, heat treatment).
2. **Service Requirements** – The mechanical, physical, chemical, and environmental demands a material must satisfy during its operational life at sea.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exam:**
> - **Fabrication** = *Manufacturing-oriented* (How the part is made).
> - **Service Requirement** = *Performance-oriented* (How the part performs in the ocean).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are building a **lifeboat for Antarctic expeditions**. You cannot simply use the same material that is used for a tropical river ferry. Why?

- The **fabrication** is like the **"kitchen recipe"** — you decide whether to forge the hull plate, roll it, or cast the propeller. Each "cooking method" gives a different grain structure and strength.
- The **service requirement** is like the **"weather forecast"** — you need to know if the boat will face freezing temperatures (cryogenic toughness), salty waves (corrosion), heavy cargo loads (yield strength), or deep-sea pressure (compressive strength).

So, *Material Fabrication and Service Requirement* is essentially the **"recipe + weather" compatibility chart** that shipbuilders use to pick the right metal (typically **marine-grade steel** as per classifications like **AH36, DH36, EH36** of IACS — International Association of Classification Societies) for the right job.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following **bold** constants and standards are routinely tested in KTU examinations:

- **Density of mild steel (ρ): 7.85 g/cm³ (7850 kg/m³)**
- **Density of seawater: 1.025 g/cm³ (1025 kg/m³)**
- **Young's Modulus of steel (E): 200 GPa (2 × 10⁵ MPa)**
- **Yield strength of mild steel (σ_y): 250 MPa**
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) of mild steel: 410 MPa**
- **IACS Grade Notation:** A, B, D, E (for normal strength); AH32, DH32, EH32, AH36, DH36, EH36 (for higher strength)
- **Design Temperature Range:** -30 °C to +50 °C (typical marine service)
- **Reference Standards:** ASTM A131, ABS Rules, DNV-OS-B101, Lloyd's Register, IRS Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** The classification societies (IACS members) define the **minimum yield strength** of shipbuilding steel as **235 MPa** for grade A and **315 MPa / 355 MPa** for grade AH32/AH36 respectively.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — Material Fabrication

Material fabrication in shipbuilding encompasses **primary forming processes** and **secondary joining processes**. Each process alters the **microstructure**, which in turn dictates the **service performance**.

### 2.1.1 Primary Forming Processes

| Process | Description | Effect on Material | Shipboard Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Casting** | Molten metal poured into mould | Coarse grain, possible porosity | Propellers, stern frames, valves |
| **Forging** | Plastic deformation under compressive force | Refined grain, directional strength | Shafts, connecting rods, anchors |
| **Rolling** | Hot/cold compression between rollers | Preferred grain orientation, high strength | Hull plates, deck plates |
| **Extrusion** | Forcing metal through a die | Uniform cross-section | Pipes, tubular masts |
| **Drawing** | Pulling through die to reduce diameter | Increased tensile strength, work hardening | Cables, wire ropes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is rolling the most common in shipbuilding?**
> Because hull plates are large, flat, and require **uniform thickness**. Hot-rolled plates (followed by controlled cooling) provide the optimal balance of strength, ductility, and weldability.

### 2.1.2 Secondary Joining & Finishing Processes

- **Welding** (arc, MIG, TIG, submerged arc) — dominant joining method; introduces **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)**.
- **Heat Treatment** (normalizing, quenching, tempering, annealing) — relieves residual stress, refines grain.
- **Surface Treatment** (shot blasting, pickling) — prepares surface for coatings and corrosion resistance.
- **Forming** (bending, pressing, flanging) — shapes plates into hull curvature.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Breakdown — Service Requirements

A ship at sea faces a hostile **multiaxial stress environment**. The material must simultaneously satisfy:

### A. Mechanical Service Requirements
1. **Static Strength:** Yield strength (σ_y) ≥ design stress.
2. **Dynamic Strength:** Fatigue endurance under wave-induced cyclic loading.
3. **Fracture Toughness:** Resistance to brittle fracture, especially in cold seas (Charpy V-notch test at **-40 °C** for grade E steel).
4. **Ductility:** % elongation ≥ 22% (per IACS for grade A).

### B. Environmental Service Requirements
1. **Corrosion Resistance** to seawater (3.5% NaCl equivalent).
2. **Resistance to Marine Atmosphere** (salt spray).
3. **Cavitation Resistance** for propellers.
4. **Biofouling Resistance** of hull.

### C. Operational Service Requirements
1. **Weldability** (Carbon Equivalent Value — CEV).
2. **Formability** (cold bending radius).
3. **Fire Resistance** (for naval vessels — grade FR).
4. **Weight Optimization** (high strength-to-weight ratio).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **most frequently tested** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) for Module 1.

| S.No | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Stress | MPa | Static strength check |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Strain | Dimensionless | Ductility measurement |
| 3 | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{P_y}{A_0}$ | Yield stress | MPa | Design against yielding |
| 4 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Young's modulus | GPa | Stiffness calculation |
| 5 | $K_{IC} = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ | Fracture toughness | MPa·√m | Crack propagation |
| 6 | $CEV = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Carbon Equivalent | % (≤ 0.45 for ships) | Weldability assessment |
| 7 | $R_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\sigma_y}$ | Strength ratio | ≤ 0.70 for plates | Material toughness indicator |
| 8 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure | Pa | Hull plating design |
| 9 | $\sigma_{fatigue} = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ | Fatigue stress range | MPa | Cyclic loading |
| 10 | $B = \dfrac{F}{v}$ | Buoyancy force | N | Stability analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Note on CEV:**
> For shipbuilding steel (per IACS), the **CEV must be ≤ 0.45%** for grade E and ≤ 0.40% for grade EH36 to ensure weldability without hydrogen-induced cracking.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

| Real-World Application | Connection to Topic |
|---|---|
| **Hull Plate Selection** | Rolling → weldability → CEV check |
| **Propeller Manufacturing** | Casting → cavitation resistance |
| **Propulsion Shaft** | Forging → fatigue endurance |
| **Offshore Platform Jackets** | High-strength steel → deep-water service |
| **Submarine Pressure Hull** | HY-80 / HY-100 steel → fracture toughness |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure on Hull Plating

A ship is floating in seawater. The hull plating at depth **h** below the waterline experiences hydrostatic pressure. We need to derive the relationship between pressure, depth, and the corresponding hoop stress in a cylindrical pressure hull (e.g., submarine).

### Step 1: State the Governing Physical Law

Pressure in a static fluid is given by Pascal's principle:

$$P = \rho g h$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of seawater = **1025 kg/m³**
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $h$ = depth below waterline (in metres)

### Step 2: Convert to Stress Acting on Hull

For a thin cylindrical pressure vessel (submarine hull), the **hoop (circumferential) stress** is:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $D$ = internal diameter of hull (m)
- $t$ = thickness of hull plating (m)

### Step 3: Substitute Pressure Expression

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{(\rho g h) \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$$

### Step 4: Rearrange for Required Thickness

$$t = \dfrac{\rho g h D}{2 \sigma_{allow}}$$

Where $\sigma_{allow}$ is the **allowable design stress** = $\sigma_y / n$ (with safety factor n).

### Step 5: Introduce Safety Factor

For marine pressure vessels, typically **n = 1.5 to 2.0**.

$$t = \dfrac{n \cdot \rho g h D}{2 \sigma_y}$$

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A submarine has a cylindrical hull of diameter **D = 8 m**, made of **HY-80 steel (σ_y = 550 MPa)**. Operating depth is **h = 250 m**. Using safety factor **n = 2.0**, find the required hull thickness.

**Solution:**

$$t = \dfrac{2.0 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 250 \times 8}{2 \times 550 \times 10^6}$$

Numerator:

$$2.0 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 250 \times 8 = 40,222,200 \; N/m$$

Denominator:

$$2 \times 550 \times 10^6 = 1,100,000,000 \; N/m^2$$

$$t = \dfrac{40,222,200}{1,100,000,000} = 0.0366 \; m \approx 36.6 \; mm$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A hull plate of approximately **37 mm thickness** is required. This matches real HY-80 submarine hull designs (typically 30–50 mm).

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV) and Weldability

### Step 1: State the IIW (International Institute of Welding) Formula

$$CEV = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$

### Step 2: Standard Reference (KTU 2024)

For shipbuilding steel grades (per IACS W31), the **maximum permissible CEV** is:

- Grade A: CEV ≤ 0.35%
- Grade E: CEV ≤ 0.38%
- Grade AH36: CEV ≤ 0.38%
- Grade EH36: CEV ≤ 0.40%

### Step 3: Worked Example

> **Problem:** A ship's hull plate has the following composition by weight: C = 0.18%, Mn = 1.40%, Cr = 0.05%, Mo = 0.02%, Ni = 0.03%, Cu = 0.04%. Determine if it qualifies as **Grade AH36** (CEV ≤ 0.38%).

**Solution:**

$$CEV = 0.18 + \dfrac{1.40}{6} + \dfrac{0.05 + 0.02}{5} + \dfrac{0.03 + 0.04}{15}$$

Compute each term step by step:

- First term: **0.180**
- Second term: $1.40 \div 6 = 0.2333$
- Third term: $0.07 \div 5 = 0.0140$
- Fourth term: $0.07 \div 15 = 0.0047$

$$CEV = 0.180 + 0.2333 + 0.0140 + 0.0047 = 0.4320 \; \%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Result: CEV = 0.432% > 0.38%**
> This material **DOES NOT qualify** for Grade AH36. It would need reduced carbon or preheating/welding procedure qualification.

### Step 4: Why is High CEV a Problem?

A higher CEV means:
- **Harder Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** after welding.
- Increased risk of **hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC)** and **cold cracking**.
- Reduced **toughness** of welded joint.

To counter this, shipyards use:
- **Preheating** of plates to 100–200 °C before welding.
- **Post-Weld Heat Treatment (PWHT)**.
- **Low-hydrogen electrodes** (e.g., basic-coated E7018).

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Strength Ratio (R_m) for Hull Plates

### Step 1: Definition

$$R_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\sigma_{yield}}$$

### Step 2: KTU Specification

IACS requires **R_m ≤ 0.70** for plates to ensure adequate **strain capacity** and **ductility** during collision or grounding events.

### Step 3: Interpretation

A lower R_m means the material has a **longer plastic region** before fracture. This is critical for:
- **Crashworthiness** of the hull.
- **Plastic deformation** before brittle failure.
- **Energy absorption** during impact.

### Step 4: Example

If a steel plate has $\sigma_y = 355 \; MPa$ (AH36) and $\sigma_{UTS} = 490 \; MPa$:

$$R_m = \dfrac{490}{355} = 1.38$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wait — this is the UTS/yield ratio, not the YS/UTS ratio as commonly used.** The correct IACS interpretation uses the **yield-to-tensile ratio check** to ensure strain hardening capacity. The conventional **UTS/YS ratio** of 1.38 indicates a normal-strength steel with good strain hardening.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Fabrication vs. Service Requirement Mapping

| Fabrication Process | Microstructural Result | Service Property Achieved | Typical Ship Component |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hot Rolling | Fine pearlite + ferrite | High strength + weldability | Hull plates (AH36) |
| Cold Rolling | Work-hardened grains | High surface hardness | Deck plating |
| Casting (Sand) | Coarse dendritic | Vibration damping | Propeller, pump body |
| Centrifugal Casting | Directional columnar | Cavitation resistance | Cylinder liners |
| Forging (Open-die) | Refined, fibrous grain | Fatigue resistance | Crankshaft, rudder stock |
| Normalizing | Uniform fine grain | Impact toughness | Structural brackets |
| Quenching + Tempering | Tempered martensite | High strength + toughness | Propeller shaft (F30) |
| Solution Annealing | Homogenized austenite | Corrosion resistance | Stainless steel fittings |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Material Selection Workflow for Shipbuilding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Service Requirement] --> B{Loading Type}
    B -->|Static| C[Yield Strength Check]
    B -->|Dynamic| D[Fatigue Analysis]
    B -->|Impact| E[Charpy Toughness Test]
    C --> F[Fabrication Route Selection]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{Joining Method}
    G -->|Welding| H[Check CEV <= 0.45]
    G -->|Riveting| I[Check Formability]
    G -->|Casting| J[Check Castability]
    H --> K[Final Material Grade Selection]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[AH36 / DH36 / EH36 / HY80]
    L --> M[Quality Test and Approval]
    M --> N[Ship Component in Service]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Service Requirement Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship in Service] --> B[Mechanical Loads]
    A --> C[Environmental Loads]
    A --> D[Operational Loads]
    B --> B1[Static - Hull Weight]
    B --> B2[Dynamic - Wave Slamming]
    B --> B3[Impact - Berthing]
    C --> C1[Corrosion - Seawater]
    C --> C2[Temperature - Arctic to Tropical]
    C --> C3[Biofouling]
    D --> D1[Vibration - Machinery]
    D --> D2[Fire - Engine Room]
    D --> D3[Fatigue - Cyclic Loading]
    B1 --> E[Material Property Demands]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    C1 --> E
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Yield / UTS / Toughness / CEV / Corrosion Rate]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Fabrication Route Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Input] --> B{Component Geometry}
    B -->|Flat Plate| C[Hot Rolling]
    B -->|Cylindrical Shaft| D[Forging]
    B -->|Complex 3D Shape| E[Casting]
    B -->|Thin Sheet| F[Deep Drawing]
    C --> G[Heat Treatment]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> G1{Normalize}
    G1 --> G2{Quench and Temper}
    G2 --> H[Surface Treatment]
    H --> H1[Shot Blasting]
    H1 --> H2[Pickling]
    H2 --> I[Primer Coating]
    I --> J[Ready for Ship Assembly]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — IACS Grade Hierarchy and Strength Levels

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Grade A - 235 MPa] --> A2[Grade B - 235 MPa]
    A2 --> A3[Grade D - 235 MPa]
    A3 --> A4[Grade E - 235 MPa]
    A4 --> B1[AH32 - 315 MPa]
    B1 --> B2[DH32 - 315 MPa]
    B2 --> B3[EH32 - 315 MPa]
    B3 --> C1[AH36 - 355 MPa]
    C1 --> C2[DH36 - 355 MPa]
    C2 --> C3[EH36 - 355 MPa]
    C3 --> D1[FH36 - 355 MPa - Cryogenic]
    D1 --> D2[AH40 - 390 MPa]
    D2 --> D3[DH40 - 390 MPa]
    D3 --> D4[EH40 - 390 MPa]
    D4 --> E1[HY-80 - 550 MPa - Naval]
    E1 --> E2[HY-100 - 690 MPa - Submarine]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Code:** The suffix letter indicates impact test temperature:
> - **A** = tested at +20 °C
> - **B** = tested at 0 °C
> - **D** = tested at -20 °C
> - **E** = tested at -40 °C
> - **F** = tested at -60 °C (cryogenic / LNG carrier)

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV) and state its significance in shipbuilding material selection.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV)** is a numerical index that expresses the combined effect of carbon and other alloying elements (Mn, Cr, Mo, V, Ni, Cu) on the **weldability** of steel. It is calculated using the IIW formula:
>
> $$CEV = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$
>
> **Significance in shipbuilding:**
> - Indicates susceptibility to **hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC)** in the weld Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ).
> - IACS mandates CEV ≤ 0.45% for shipbuilding plates to ensure **weldability without preheating** in most cases.
> - A high CEV requires **preheating and post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)**, increasing fabrication cost.
> - Critical for selection of **AH36, DH36, EH36** grades used in hull construction.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between hot working and cold working processes in shipbuilding with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hot Working | Cold Working |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | Above recrystallization temperature (≥ 0.6 T_m) | Below recrystallization temperature |
| **Grain Structure** | Refined, equiaxed after recrystallization | Stretched, work-hardened |
| **Strength** | Moderate, uniform | Higher (strain hardening) |
| **Ductility** | High | Reduced |
| **Example 1** | Hot rolling of hull plates | Cold rolling of thin deck sheets |
| **Example 2** | Hot forging of propeller shaft | Cold drawing of wire ropes |
| **Shipboard Use** | Heavy structural components | Precision thin components |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks)

> **Module 1 — KTU 2024 ESE Pattern: Answer any ONE full question from this choice.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **mechanical and environmental service requirements** that govern the selection of materials for ship hull construction. Discuss how **IACS classification** addresses these requirements.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mechanical Service Requirements:**

1. **Yield Strength (σ_y):** Hull plates must withstand hydrostatic pressure and cargo loads without permanent deformation. IACS specifies minimum σ_y of **235 MPa (Grade A)** up to **355 MPa (Grade AH36)**.

2. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_UTS):** Provides reserve strength beyond yield. Typical values are 400–530 MPa for shipbuilding grades.

3. **Fracture Toughness (Charpy V-Notch):** Critical for brittle fracture prevention in cold waters. Tested at:
   - Grade A: +20 °C
   - Grade D: -20 °C
   - Grade E: -40 °C
   - **Minimum energy absorbed: 27 J** (longitudinal) per IACS UR S6.

4. **Fatigue Strength:** Hull experiences wave-induced cyclic loading (~10⁸ cycles over 25 years). Endurance limit is approximately **0.5 × σ_UTS** for welded joints (S-N curve approach).

5. **Ductility (% Elongation):** Must be ≥ 22% in 50 mm gauge length to allow plastic deformation during grounding or collision (crashworthiness).

**Step 2 — Environmental Service Requirements:**

1. **Seawater Corrosion Resistance:** Seawater (3.5% NaCl) is highly aggressive. Hull plates require **cathodic protection + coating** rather than relying on inherent corrosion resistance (which is uneconomical for carbon steel).

2. **Marine Atmosphere Corrosion:** Salt-laden air in splash zone accelerates pitting. Requires specialized **zinc-rich primers**.

3. **Temperature Variation:** Arctic service requires low-temperature toughness (Grade F tested at -60 °C).

4. **Biofouling Resistance:** Hull smoothness (controlled by **antifouling paint**) reduces drag and biological growth.

**Step 3 — IACS Classification Response:**

The **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** unifies rules across 12 member societies (ABS, DNV, Lloyd's, BV, IRS, etc.). It specifies:

- Minimum **σ_y** for each grade.
- Mandatory **Charpy V-notch tests** at specific temperatures.
- **CEV limits** to ensure weldability.
- **Through-thickness ductility (Z-grade)** for plates in way of primary structural nodes to prevent lamellar tearing.
- **Ultrasonic testing (UT)** for internal soundness.

**Conclusion:** Material selection is a **multi-objective optimization** problem balancing strength, toughness, weldability, and corrosion resistance — fully addressed by IACS.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A ship hull plate of **Grade AH36** is to be welded. Compute the **CEV** from the following composition and comment on its weldability. Also suggest **two pre-weld procedures** if CEV exceeds the limit.

**Given composition (% by weight):**
- C = 0.16, Mn = 1.45, Cr = 0.08, Mo = 0.03, Ni = 0.05, Cu = 0.04, Si = 0.30, S = 0.02, P = 0.02

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply IIW Formula:**

$$CEV = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$

$$CEV = 0.16 + \dfrac{1.45}{6} + \dfrac{0.08 + 0.03 + 0}{5} + \dfrac{0.05 + 0.04}{15}$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term:**

- $0.16$
- $1.45 \div 6 = 0.2417$
- $0.11 \div 5 = 0.0220$
- $0.09 \div 15 = 0.0060$

**Step 3 — Sum:**

$$CEV = 0.16 + 0.2417 + 0.0220 + 0.0060 = 0.4297 \; \%$$

**Step 4 — Compare with IACS Limit for AH36:**

Limit: **CEV ≤ 0.38%** for grade AH36 (per IACS W31).

**Observed: 0.4297% > 0.38%** → **Exceeds limit by 13.1%**

**Step 5 — Weldability Comment:**

The plate **fails** the CEV criterion. Risk of:
- Hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC) in HAZ.
- Hard martensite formation on rapid cooling.
- Reduced weld metal toughness.

**Step 6 — Two Pre-Weld Procedures Suggested:**

1. **Preheating to 150–200 °C:** Slows cooling rate, allows hydrogen to diffuse out, prevents martensite formation.
2. **Use of Low-Hydrogen Electrodes (e.g., E7018 basic-coated):** Reduces diffusible hydrogen to < 5 ml/100g weld metal (vs. 15 ml for rutile-coated).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> - Forgetting to **convert weight % to decimal** before substitution. Always use the same units in CEV formula.
> - Not stating the **IACS reference** (W31 / S6 / Z23) when justifying limits.
> - Missing **post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)** as a third option.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches (block diagrams), explain the **fabrication process flow** for a typical **marine-grade steel hull plate** from raw steel to finished product ready for ship assembly.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Raw Material Preparation:**

The process begins with **steelmaking** in a Basic Oxygen Furnace (BOF) or Electric Arc Furnace (EAF). Liquid steel is continuously cast into **slabs** of thickness 200–300 mm.

**Step 2 — Hot Rolling (Primary Forming):**

The slabs are reheated to **1200–1250 °C** in a reheat furnace and passed through a **roughing mill** (reduces thickness to 30–50 mm) and then a **finishing mill** (reduces to 6–50 mm final plate thickness). Controlled rolling (TMCP — Thermo-Mechanical Controlled Processing) refines the grain.

**Step 3 — Cooling:**

The hot-rolled plate is cooled using **accelerated cooling (ACC)** to achieve the desired mechanical properties. Some grades require **normalizing** (heating to 910 °C, holding, air-cooling) to refine grain.

**Step 4 — Heat Treatment (Optional):**

For high-strength grades like **EH36** or **HY-80**:
- **Quenching** (rapid water cooling) → produces martensite.
- **Tempering** (reheating to 600–700 °C) → tempered martensite with good toughness.

**Step 5 — Leveling & Cutting:**

Plates are passed through a **roller leveler** to remove flatness defects, then **plasma or oxy-fuel cut** to required shapes per the ship's nesting plan.

**Step 6 — Surface Preparation:**

- **Shot blasting** removes mill scale and rust.
- **Pickling** (acid bath) eliminates residual oxide.
- **Primer coating** (typically zinc silicate shop primer 15–20 µm) applied within 24 hours to prevent re-rusting.

**Step 7 — Quality Testing:**

- **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** checks for internal laminations.
- **Charpy V-Notch test** at -40 °C for grade E.
- **Tensile test** confirms σ_y and σ_UTS.
- **Chemical analysis** confirms CEV.

**Step 8 — Marking & Dispatch:**

Each plate is marked with grade, heat number, and is shipped to the shipyard for nesting, forming, and welding.

**Schematic Block Diagram:**

```
Steel Slab → Reheat Furnace → Hot Rolling Mill → ACC Cooling
    → Heat Treatment (Optional) → Leveling → Cutting
    → Surface Preparation → Primer Coating → Quality Testing
    → Marking → Dispatch to Shipyard
```

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A submarine operates at a depth of **300 m** in seawater. The pressure hull is cylindrical with internal diameter **7 m**, made of **HY-80 steel (σ_y = 550 MPa)**. Using a safety factor of **2.0**, calculate the **required hull thickness**. Discuss why **HY-80** is preferred over **mild steel** for this application.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure Calculation:**

$$P = \rho g h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 300$$

$$P = 3,016,477.5 \; Pa \approx 3.02 \; MPa$$

**Step 2 — Hoop Stress Equation:**

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Safety Factor:**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n} = \dfrac{550}{2.0} = 275 \; MPa$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Thickness:**

$$t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}} = \dfrac{3.02 \times 10^6 \times 7}{2 \times 275 \times 10^6}$$

$$t = \dfrac{21.14 \times 10^6}{550 \times 10^6} = 0.0384 \; m \approx 38.4 \; mm$$

**Step 5 — Comparison Table: HY-80 vs. Mild Steel**

| Property | Mild Steel (Grade A) | HY-80 |
|---|---|---|
| Yield Strength | 235 MPa | 550 MPa |
| Required thickness (this case) | ~90 mm | 38.4 mm |
| Weight penalty | High | Low |
| Weldability | Excellent | Requires preheat + low-H electrodes |
| Cost | Low | ~3× higher |
| Impact toughness at -40 °C | Marginal | Excellent |
| Application | Surface ships | Submarines, naval combat vessels |

**Step 6 — Discussion — Why HY-80?**

1. **High Strength-to-Weight Ratio:** A thinner hull allows for **larger payload, more fuel, more weapons**, and **better maneuverability**.
2. **Deep Diving Capability:** Required thickness scales with depth. Mild steel would make a 300 m submarine impractically heavy.
3. **Fracture Toughness:** HY-80 undergoes **quenching and tempering**, giving it superior **Charpy impact energy (~80 J at -40 °C)** vs. mild steel (~27 J).
4. **Fatigue Resistance:** Submarine hulls experience cyclic loading from diving; HY-80's tempered martensite resists crack initiation.
5. **Crashworthiness & Survivability:** Naval submarines need to withstand shock loads from underwater explosions — HY-80 has higher **dynamic tear strength**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often forget to apply the **safety factor** — costing 2 marks.
> - Confusing **σ_y/UTS** with **σ_allow** in the formula.
> - Not stating **why** a specific steel is chosen in part (b) — must include **2 reasons minimum** for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Material Fabrication and Service Requirement**

### Key Concepts:
- ✅ Material fabrication covers **casting, forging, rolling, extrusion, drawing, welding, and heat treatment**.
- ✅ Service requirements include **mechanical (strength, toughness, fatigue), environmental (corrosion, temperature), and operational (weldability, formability) demands**.
- ✅ **IACS classification** is the global standard for shipbuilding steel grades.
- ✅ The IACS suffix letters indicate **impact test temperature**: A=+20°C, B=0°C, D=-20°C, E=-40°C, F=-60°C.
- ✅ Hull plate grades follow a strength hierarchy: **A (235 MPa) → AH32/DH32 (315 MPa) → AH36/DH36 (355 MPa) → HY-80 (550 MPa) → HY-100 (690 MPa)**.

### Key Formulas:
- ✅ **CEV** (Carbon Equivalent Value) — IIW formula, limit ≤ 0.45% for ship plates.
- ✅ **Hydrostatic pressure**: $P = \rho g h$.
- ✅ **Hoop stress in cylinder**: $\sigma = \dfrac{PD}{2t}$.
- ✅ **Required thickness with safety factor**: $t = \dfrac{n \rho g h D}{2 \sigma_y}$.
- ✅ **Strength ratio**: $R_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\sigma_y}$.

### Key Numbers for KTU Exam:
- ✅ Seawater density = **1025 kg/m³**.
- ✅ Mild steel density = **7850 kg/m³**.
- ✅ E (steel) = **200 GPa**.
- ✅ Grade AH36 minimum σ_y = **355 MPa**.
- ✅ Charpy V-notch requirement = **27 J (longitudinal), 20 J (transverse)** at specified temperature.
- ✅ Min % elongation = **22%** (in 50 mm).

### Common Mistakes to Avoid:
- ❌ Confusing σ_y and σ_UTS.
- ❌ Forgetting units in CEV (% vs. decimal).
- ❌ Not applying the safety factor.
- ❌ Confusing UTS/yield ratio (R_m) with its limit.
- ❌ Ignoring the impact test temperature in grade selection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** Always **cite the IACS standard** (e.g., UR S6, UR W31, UR Z23) when answering material-related questions — it shows examiner-level depth and earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification society requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification society requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Classification Society** is a non-governmental, independent technical organization that establishes and applies technical rules and standards concerning the design, construction, and survey of marine vessels and offshore structures, with the primary objective of ensuring structural integrity, seaworthiness, and operational safety. In the context of shipbuilding materials and corrosion prevention, classification societies prescribe mandatory rules for material selection, welding procedures, coating specifications, cathodic protection, and periodic survey protocols.

The major international body that coordinates these societies is the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)**, founded in **1968**, which represents **12 member societies** controlling approximately **90% of the world's gross tonnage** of merchant ships.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Classification society requirements are foundational to Module 1 because they govern every downstream decision in shipbuilding — from steel grade selection (e.g., Grade A, B, D, E hull plates) to coating thickness specifications in ballast tanks as per **PSPC (Performance Standard for Protective Coatings)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a classification society like a **third-party quality auditor at a car manufacturing plant** — but far more rigorous. When Toyota builds a car, it doesn't just trust its own engineers; external agencies (like Euro NCAP for crash tests) verify the vehicle meets safety standards.

Similarly, a **shipowner cannot simply build a vessel and claim it is safe**. The ship must be "classed" by a recognized society. The society:
- Reviews the design drawings
- Inspects the steel mill certificates
- Witnesses welding and NDT (Non-Destructive Testing)
- Issues a **Classification Certificate** and **Statutory Certificates** on behalf of flag states

**Plain English:** A classification society is the maritime world's "school examiner" — without its stamp (the classification symbol/mark on the hull), the ship cannot legally trade, obtain insurance, or enter most ports.

## 1.3 Key Classification Societies (IACS Members)

| Society | Country | Founded | Common Symbol |
|---------|---------|---------|---------------|
| Lloyd's Register (LR) | United Kingdom | 1760 | Ⓛ |
| Bureau Veritas (BV) | France | 1828 | Ⓥ |
| DNV (Det Norske Veritas) | Norway | 1864 | ✠ |
| American Bureau of Shipping (ABS) | USA | 1862 | Ⓐ |
| ClassNK (Nippon Kaiji Kyokai) | Japan | 1899 | Ⓝ |
| Germanischer Lloyd (GL) → merged with DNV | Germany | 1867 | (GL) |
| Registro Italiano Navale (RINA) | Italy | 1861 | Ⓡ |
| Korean Register (KR) | South Korea | 1960 | Ⓚ |
| Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) | India | 1975 | Ⓘ |
| China Classification Society (CCS) | China | 1956 | Ⓩ |
| Polish Register of Shipping (PRS) | Poland | 1936 | Ⓟ |
| Croatian Register of Shipping (CRS) | Croatia | 1949 | Ⓒ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Lloyd's Register (1760)** is the world's oldest classification society — older than the United States itself!

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **IACS Unified Requirements (UR):** Technical resolutions applicable across all 12 member societies
- **Common Structural Rules (CSR) — for Bulk Carriers and Oil Tankers**, harmonized in 2006
- **PSPC coating DFT (Dry Film Thickness) for ballast tanks:** **320 µm (epoxy)** nominal
- **Steel yield strength standard reference:** **235 N/mm²** (Grade A mild steel)
- **Annual survey interval:** Every **12 months** from keel laying

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Why Classification Societies Exist — The Core "Why"

Maritime transport carries approximately **80% of global trade by volume**. The inherent risks include:
- Total loss of vessel and cargo
- Pollution (oil spills, hazardous materials)
- Loss of life at sea
- Massive economic liability

Classification societies exist to **transfer technical risk** from flag states (which often lack maritime expertise) to expert third-party organizations. They function on a **"Zero Acceptable Failure"** philosophy for critical structural members.

## 2.2 The Two-Tier Certification System

A vessel carries **two distinct types** of certificates:

### Tier 1: Classification Certificates (Voluntary but Practically Mandatory)
- Hull classification certificate
- Machinery classification certificate
- Issued by the classification society on its own authority

### Tier 2: Statutory Certificates (Mandatory under International Conventions)
- SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea)
- MARPOL (Marine Pollution)
- Load Line Convention
- Issued by the classification society **on behalf of the Flag State Administration**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common student pitfall: Confusing **Classification** with **Statutory** certification. Classification is the society's own rule; Statutory is delegated authority under IMO conventions.

## 2.3 Survey Schedule (Operational Lifecycle)

Every classed vessel undergoes a **continuous survey cycle**:

| Survey Type | Timing | Scope |
|-------------|--------|-------|
| **Classification Survey during Construction** | At shipyard | Plan approval → material certification → welding inspection → NDT → sea trials |
| **Annual Survey** | Within 12 months of delivery | General examination, hull, machinery |
| **Intermediate Survey** | Within 24–36 months | More detailed, ballast tanks inspection |
| **Special Survey (Class Renewal)** | Every 5 years | Comprehensive — vessel may be dry-docked |
| **Continuous Survey** | Rolling programme | Alternative to special survey |

## 2.4 Material & Corrosion Protection Requirements

Classification societies issue **Rules for Ships** (e.g., Lloyd's Register Rules and Regulations) which prescribe:

### A. Hull Structural Steel Requirements
- **Hull plate grades (as per IACS UR Z23/Z25):** Grade A, B, D, E (for plates ≤ 100 mm)
- **Higher tensile steel (HTS):** AH32, DH32, EH32, AH36, DH36, EH36 (yield strength **315 N/mm²** and **355 N/mm²**)
- **Through-thickness properties (Z-direction):** Required for critical connections to prevent lamellar tearing
- **Charpy V-notch impact testing** at specified temperatures (e.g., 0°C, -20°C, -40°C)

### B. Corrosion Protection Requirements
- **Coating systems** for ballast tanks per **PSPC (Resolution MSC.215(82))**
- **Cathodic protection** by sacrificial anodes (zinc, aluminium) or impressed current
- **Corrosion allowances** added to plate thickness calculations
- **IMO PSPC compliant shop primers** must be ≤ 30 µm DFT

## 2.5 The Steel Grade Naming Convention Explained

Take **"AH36"** as an example:
- **A** = Grade A quality (highest ductility, basic strength)
- **H** = High tensile strength designation
- **36** = Yield strength in **kgf/mm²** (36 × 9.81 ≈ 355 N/mm²)

For **"DH36"**:
- **D** = Cold forming/lower temperature grade (Charpy tested at -20°C)

For **"EH36"**:
- **E** = Extra-low temperature (Charpy tested at -40°C)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The higher the letter (A → D → E), the more stringent the impact testing temperature, and consequently the more expensive the steel.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet Table

| Concept | Key Fact | KTU Relevance |
|---------|----------|---------------|
| Number of IACS Members | **12 societies** | Direct fact question |
| Oldest Classification Society | **Lloyd's Register (1760)** | Direct fact question |
| PSPC Standard | **Resolution MSC.215(82)** | Coating compliance |
| PSPC Ballast Tank DFT | **320 µm epoxy nominal** | Numerical question |
| Class Renewal Survey | Every **5 years** | Survey question |
| Annual Survey | Every **12 months** | Survey question |
| Grade A yield strength | **235 N/mm²** | Material question |
| Grade AH36 yield strength | **355 N/mm²** | Material question |
| PSPC IMO resolution | **MSC.215(82)** + **MSC.244(83)** | Coating question |
| Common Structural Rules (CSR) | **IACS 2006** for tankers/bulkers | Design question |
| Cathodic protection current density (seawater) | **100–150 mA/m²** initially | Corrosion question |
| Sacrificial anode material (seawater) | **Aluminium-Zinc-Indium** | Material question |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Procedural Derivations

## 3.1 The Classification Process — Step-by-Step Procedural Derivation

Since this topic is procedural and standards-based, the "derivation" is the logical sequence through which a vessel obtains and maintains its class. Each step is critical and examined by KTU.

### Step 1: Initial Design Approval
The shipyard submits **general arrangement plans, structural drawings, and material specifications** to the chosen classification society. The society's engineering department reviews:
- Scantlings (structural member dimensions)
- Material grades proposed
- Welding procedure specifications (WPS)
- Corrosion protection philosophy

The society issues an **Approval in Principle (AIP)** or design approval letter.

### Step 2: Material Certification at Source
Every batch of steel plate is delivered with a **Mill Test Certificate (MTC)** traceable to a unique heat number. The classification society surveyor:
- Verifies MTC compliance with ordered grade
- Witnesses **Charpy impact tests** at the steel mill (or accepts works test certificates per IACS UR)
- Confirms chemical composition (C, Mn, S, P limits)

### Step 3: Fabrication Surveillance
During shipbuilding, the society's surveyor:
- Inspects **welding procedure qualification tests** (WPQT)
- Witnesses **NDT**: Ultrasonic Testing (UT), Radiographic Testing (RT), Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)
- Checks dimensional accuracy and alignment
- Approves **NDT personnel certification** (per ISO 9712 / IACS UR Z17)

### Step 4: Pre-Sea Trial Inspection
- Hull marking (classification symbol) is verified
- Anchor, chain, and mooring equipment surveyed
- Lifeboats, fire-fighting systems examined
- Main engine, generators, steering gear tested

### Step 5: Sea Trials
The vessel performs speed, maneuverability, and endurance trials in the presence of the surveyor.

### Step 6: Delivery and Class Certificate Issuance
Upon satisfactory completion, the society issues:
- **Class Certificate** (Hull + Machinery)
- **Statutory Certificates** delegated by the flag state

### Step 7: Operational Survey Cycle
The vessel enters the **5-year class cycle** with annual, intermediate, and special surveys.

## 3.2 Derivation of Corrosion Allowance (Analytical Example)

For a hull plate in a seawater ballast tank, classification societies require a **corrosion allowance** added to the calculated net scantling thickness.

The procedure:

$$
t_{gross} = t_{net} + t_{corrosion} + t_{manufacturing}
$$

Where:
- $t_{gross}$ = As-built plate thickness (mm)
- $t_{net}$ = Net required thickness (from strength calculation)
- $t_{corrosion}$ = Corrosion allowance (typically **0.5 mm to 2.0 mm** per side depending on location)
- $t_{manufacturing}$ = Manufacturing tolerance (typically **0.5 mm**)

**Example Walk-through:**

Given a bottom plate of Grade A steel with:
- Net required thickness: $t_{net} = 12.0$ mm
- Corrosion allowance: $t_{corrosion} = 1.5$ mm
- Manufacturing tolerance: $t_{manufacturing} = 0.5$ mm

$$
t_{gross} = 12.0 + 1.5 + 0.5 = 14.0 \text{ mm}
$$

The next standard plate thickness ordered is **15.0 mm** (commercial standard rounding).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Corrosion allowance is **not the same** as the design corrosion margin in PSPC. PSPC uses coating to *replace* steel thickness; a higher coating life allows a lower corrosion allowance, reducing steel weight and improving cargo capacity.

## 3.3 Cathodic Protection Design Sizing (Analytical Derivation)

For sacrificial anode cathodic protection of a ballast tank (per DNV-RP-B401 or similar):

The required anode mass is calculated as:

$$
M = \frac{I_{req} \times T \times 8760}{U \times 1000}
$$

Where:
- $M$ = Anode mass per tank (kg)
- $I_{req}$ = Total required current (A), calculated as current density × wetted area
- $T$ = Design life (years), typically **5 years**
- $U$ = Anode utilization factor (typically **0.85–0.90** for aluminium anodes)
- **8760** = hours per year
- **1000** = unit conversion factor

**Example Numerical Derivation:**

For a ballast tank with wetted surface area $A = 1500$ m² and design current density $i_c = 120$ mA/m²:

$$
I_{req} = \frac{A \times i_c}{1000} = \frac{1500 \times 120}{1000} = 180 \text{ A}
$$

$$
M = \frac{180 \times 5 \times 8760}{0.90 \times 1000} = \frac{7{,}884{,}000}{900} = 8760 \text{ kg}
$$

Therefore, **8760 kg of aluminium anodes** are required for 5-year protection of this ballast tank.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The classification society must be satisfied that this calculation methodology (from recognized standards like DNV-RP-B401, NACE SP0176, or EN 12473) has been followed.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Classification Society vs. Flag State vs. Port State

| Authority | Role | Authority Level |
|-----------|------|-----------------|
| **Classification Society** | Technical rules, surveys, classification certificate | Non-governmental, delegated |
| **Flag State** | Owns legal jurisdiction over vessel; issues statutory certificates | Governmental |
| **Port State** | Inspects foreign vessels calling at its ports (PSC inspection) | Governmental |
| **IACS** | Coordinates societies, issues Unified Requirements | Inter-society body |
| **IMO** | Sets international maritime conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL) | UN specialized agency |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vessel Classification Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shipyard Design Office] --> B[Submit Plans to Classification Society]
    B --> C{Plan Approval by Society Engineers}
    C -->|Approved| D[Approval in Principle Issued]
    C -->|Rejected| A
    D --> E[Steel Mill Material Procurement]
    E --> F[Mill Test Certificate Verification]
    F --> G[Fabrication Phase in Shipyard]
    G --> H[Welding and NDT Inspection]
    H --> I[Surveyor Witnessing at Critical Stages]
    I --> J[Pre-Sea Trial Inspection]
    J --> K[Sea Trials at Sea]
    K --> L{All Tests Passed?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Class Certificate Issued]
    L -->|No| G
    M --> N[Vessel Delivered to Owner]
    N --> O[Annual Survey - Year 1]
    O --> P[Intermediate Survey - Year 2-3]
    P --> Q[Special Survey - Year 5 Class Renewal]
    Q --> R{Class Maintained?}
    R -->|Yes| O
    R -->|No| S[Class Suspended or Withdrawn]
```

## 4.2 Corrosion Protection Decision Tree (as Required by Classification Societies)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Ship Construction] --> B{Structural Location?}
    B -->|Underwater Hull| C[Anti-fouling Coating + Impressed Current Cathodic Protection]
    B -->|Ballast Tank| D[PSPC Epoxy Coating 320 micron + Sacrificial Anodes]
    B -->|Cargo Oil Tank| E[Coating per IACS SICS Requirement]
    B -->|Void Spaces| F[No Coating but Drying Provisions]
    C --> G[Society Survey During Docking]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Coating Condition Acceptable?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Class Endorsed]
    H -->|No| J[Repair or Reapplication Required]
    J --> G
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Relationship Between Authorities in Ship Certification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IMO[International Maritime Organization - Sets Conventions]
    IACS[IACS - Coordinates Classification Societies]
    FS[Flag State - Legal Authority]
    CS[Classification Society - Technical Authority]
    OWNER[Shipowner - Customer]
    PORT[Port State Control - Enforcement]

    IMO -->|Issues SOLAS, MARPOL| FS
    IMO -->|Provides framework| IACS
    IACS -->|Sets Unified Requirements UR| CS
    FS -->|Delegates statutory surveys| CS
    OWNER -->|Engages services| CS
    CS -->|Issues Class Certificate| OWNER
    CS -->|Issues Statutory Certificate| OWNER
    PORT -->|Inspects foreign ships| OWNER
    PORT -.->|May request class records| CS
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Steel Grade Selection Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Determine Structural Member Type] --> B{Location in Ship?}
    B -->|Hull Bottom, Side Shell| C[Grade A or AH36 - mild]
    B -->|Strength Deck, Sheer Strake| D[Grade AH36 or DH36]
```
