```mermaid
    B -->|Bow Region, Ice Strengthening| E[Grade EH36 or FH36]
    C --> F{Plate Thickness}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -->|>25mm| G[Additional Through-Thickness Z35]
    F -->|≤25mm| H[Standard UT Testing]
    G --> I[Final Steel Order with MTC]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Classification Society Verification]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**Define a classification society. List any four major international classification societies.**

**Model Answer:**

A classification society is an independent, non-governmental technical organization that establishes and applies rules and standards for the design, construction, and survey of ships and offshore structures, ensuring their structural integrity and seaworthiness throughout their operational life.

**Four Major Classification Societies:**
1. Lloyd's Register (LR) — United Kingdom, founded 1760
2. Bureau Veritas (BV) — France, founded 1828
3. American Bureau of Shipping (ABS) — United States, founded 1862
4. Det Norske Veritas (DNV) — Norway, founded 1864

*(Alternatively: ClassNK, RINA, KR, IRS, GL, CCS, PRS, CRS are all acceptable)*

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1.5 marks) + List (1.5 marks — 0.375 each)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]
**What is the PSPC standard? Specify the nominal coating DFT for ballast tanks as per IACS.**

**Model Answer:**

PSPC stands for **Performance Standard for Protective Coatings**, adopted by the International Maritime Organization (IMO) under **Resolution MSC.215(82)**. It was introduced following the catastrophic sinking of the bulk carrier *Erika* (1999) and the subsequent *Prestige* (2002) to improve coating life and prevent structural failure due to corrosion in seawater ballast tanks.

**Nominal DFT for ballast tanks:** **320 µm** of epoxy coating system (with tolerance ranges as per the standard).

**Marks Distribution:** PSPC definition (2 marks) + DFT value (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]
**(a)** Explain in detail the role of classification societies in ensuring ship safety, with specific reference to material certification and welding inspection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the survey cycle of a classed vessel, including the special survey scope and significance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Classification societies act as independent technical watchdogs of the maritime industry. They develop comprehensive rules covering design, materials, fabrication, and in-service maintenance. Their primary objective is to safeguard life, property, and the marine environment.

**Role in Material Certification (3 marks):**
1. **Rule-based specification:** Societies prescribe the grade of steel (e.g., Grade A, B, D, E and higher tensile AH32–EH36) based on plate thickness, location, and service temperature.
2. **Mill approval:** Steel mills producing for classed vessels must be approved by the society, ensuring consistent quality.
3. **Mill Test Certificate (MTC) verification:** Every plate is traceable to a unique heat number. The society verifies chemical composition (C, Mn, S, P, Si limits) and mechanical properties (yield, tensile, elongation, Charpy impact value).
4. **Impact testing at low temperatures:** For grades D and E, Charpy V-notch tests at 0°C, -20°C, and -40°C respectively are witnessed/verified.
5. **Through-thickness (Z-direction) testing:** For plates subject to restraint, Z-grade testing prevents lamellar tearing.

**Role in Welding Inspection (3 marks):**
1. **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS) approval** by the society before production welding begins.
2. **Welder qualification tests** witnessed by surveyors to ensure operator competency.
3. **Welding Procedure Qualification Test (WPQT)** validating the procedure on representative samples.
4. **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT):** Ultrasonic Testing (UT) for butt welds, Radiographic Testing (RT) for critical joints, Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT) for surface defects, Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT) for non-magnetic materials.
5. **NDT personnel certification** verified per IACS UR Z17 / ISO 9712.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Survey Cycle Overview (2 marks):**
A classed vessel undergoes a continuous cycle of surveys: **Annual, Intermediate, and Special**, in accordance with IACS Unified Requirements and the society's own rules.

**Annual Survey (1 mark):**
Conducted within 12 months of the previous annual survey. Includes general visual examination of hull, machinery spaces, and safety equipment.

**Intermediate Survey (1 mark):**
Conducted between the 2nd and 3rd annual surveys (i.e., around 24–36 months). More extensive than annual, includes internal examination of selected ballast tanks and machinery components.

**Special Survey (Class Renewal) (2 marks):**
- Conducted **every 5 years**.
- The most comprehensive and rigorous survey.
- Vessel is **dry-docked** for underwater hull inspection.
- All ballast tanks, cargo holds, and void spaces are internally examined.
- Thickness gauging (UT) of all structural members compared to original scantlings.
- Corrosion and wear assessed; renewals and repairs ordered.
- Machinery completely opened up for inspection.
- Upon satisfactory completion, the class is renewed for a further 5 years.

**Significance (1 mark):**
The special survey ensures that aging vessels maintain the same safety standards as when newly built, allowing them to continue trading safely for 20–25+ years of operational life.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]
**(a)** Describe the IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR) and their impact on ship design for bulk carriers and oil tankers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the PSPC requirements for ballast tank coatings, including the role of classification societies in ensuring compliance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition of CSR (2 marks):**
The **IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR)** for **Bulk Carriers** (adopted 2006) and **Double Hull Oil Tankers** (adopted 2007) are harmonized, strength-based design rules that establish a unified technical standard across all 12 IACS member societies. They replaced the earlier divergent society-specific rules.

**Key Features (3 marks):**
1. **Net scantling approach:** Rules are based on **limit state design** with separate checks for ultimate strength, yielding, buckling, and fatigue.
2. **Hull girder strength:** Standardized calculations for still water bending moments, wave bending moments, and shear forces.
3. **Local strength:** Plate, stiffener, and bracket scantling formulas.
4. **Corrosion model:** Standardized corrosion addition table based on tank location and time (e.g., **bottom plates: 0.1 mm/year in ballast, 0.05 mm/year in cargo condition for bulk carriers**).
5. **Fatigue assessment:** Required for novel structural details.

**Impact on Ship Design (2 marks):**
1. **Increased safety:** Reduction in bulk carrier and tanker losses (a 35% reduction in serious casualties was reported by IACS post-CSR).
2. **Increased steel weight initially:** Early designs saw 3–5% increase in lightship weight, but optimized designs now are competitive.
3. **Design transparency:** Predictable, code-based design reduces surveyor interpretation.
4. **Global harmonization:** A single design can be built and classed by multiple societies.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**PSPC Background (1 mark):**
**PSPC = Performance Standard for Protective Coatings**, IMO Resolution **MSC.215(82)** for ballast tanks (and **MSC.244(83)** for void spaces in bulk carriers). Effective for new ships contracted on or after **8 December 2006**.

**Technical Requirements (3 marks):**
1. **Nominal total DFT: 320 µm** of epoxy-based coating system, with a minimum of **90% of measurements ≥ 320 µm** and maximum individual reading ≥ **250 µm**.
2. **Shop primer ≤ 30 µm DFT** if not part of the coating system.
3. **Coating life:** Minimum **15 years** to first major refurbishment, with **95% of surface area in "GOOD" condition** at the special survey.
4. **Surface preparation:** Sa 2.5 (ISO 8501-1) blast cleaning minimum.
5. **Compatibility:** All coating layers must be from a single manufacturer and qualified per **IMO PSPC Test Procedure (MSC.215 Annex 1)**.

**Role of Classification Societies (3 marks):**
1. **Type approval** of coating systems and manufacturers.
2. **Surveyor attendance** during surface preparation, application, and inspection.
3. **DFT measurements** verified during construction and at subsequent special surveys.
4. **Coating Technical File (CTF)** maintained on board, audited by the class surveyor.
5. **Coating Condition Assessment** during special surveys, with major deterioration potentially leading to class recommendations or operational restrictions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Classification Certificate with Statutory Certificate** — These are distinct documents. A ship may lose class but retain statutory certification, or vice versa. Always distinguish clearly.
> 2. **Wrong resolution numbers** — KTU examiners specifically test **MSC.215(82)** and **MSC.244(83)**. Writing "IMO 2006" or "PSPC standard" without the resolution number costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Incorrect DFT value** — The standard value is **320 µm**, not 200 µm or 500 µm. This is a frequently tested numerical fact.
> 4. **Confusing Grade A (235 N/mm²) with Grade AH36 (355 N/mm²)** — Students often mix up yield strengths. The letter "H" denotes high tensile.
> 5. **Omitting society names** — When asked to "list" societies, writing only "DNV" or "Lloyd's" without country and founding year loses marks per KTU's 2024 marking scheme (0.25 marks per omission).
> 6. **Forgetting dry-dock requirement** — The Special Survey is associated with **dry-docking**; omitting this loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Classification Society:** Independent, non-governmental body that sets technical rules and surveys ships.
- **IACS:** International Association of Classification Societies, founded 1968, **12 members**, ~90% of world tonnage.
- **Oldest society:** Lloyd's Register (1760, UK).
- **Major societies (memorize these 6 for KTU):** LR, BV, DNV, ABS, ClassNK, RINA.
- **PSPC = MSC.215(82)** for ballast tanks, **MSC.244(83)** for void spaces.
- **PSPC Ballast Tank DFT = 320 µm** epoxy (nominal).
- **CSR** = IACS 2006 (bulk carriers), 2007 (oil tankers) — limit state design.
- **Survey cycle:** Annual (12 months) → Intermediate (24–36 months) → Special (5 years, with dry-dock).
- **Steel grade A:** Yield = 235 N/mm²; **AH36:** Yield = 355 N/mm².
- **Charpy impact temperatures:** A = +20°C, B = 0°C, D = -20°C, E = -40°C, F = -60°C.
- **Corrosion allowance formula:** $t_{gross} = t_{net} + t_{corrosion} + t_{manufacturing}$.
- **Cathodic protection anode mass formula:** $M = \frac{I_{req} \times T \times 8760}{U \times 1000}$.
- **Sacrificial anode material in seawater:** Aluminium-Zinc-Indium alloy.
- **Statutory vs Classification certificates:** Two separate documents; statutory is delegated by flag state.
- **Coating Technical File (CTF):** Must be on board; surveyor audits during special survey.
- **IACS UR Z17:** Unified Requirement for NDT personnel certification.
- **15-year coating life** is the PSPC design target with 95% "GOOD" condition threshold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always include a labelled **diagram** or **table** comparing concepts. KTU examiners award up to 1–2 marks for well-structured presentation, including clear headings, neat tables, and consistent numbering.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of shipbuilding quality steels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of shipbuilding quality steels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shipbuilding quality steels** are a specialized class of **carbon-manganese structural steels** that conform to the unified design and fabrication rules promulgated by the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** and the major marine classification bodies such as **ABS (American Bureau of Shipping)**, **DNV (Det Norske Veritas)**, **LR (Lloyd's Register)**, **BV (Bureau Veritas)**, **KR (Korean Register)**, **NK (Nippon Kaiji Kyokai)**, **CCS (China Classification Society)** and **IRS (Indian Register of Shipping)**. They are characterized by guaranteed minimum yield strengths (typically **235 MPa**, **315 MPa**, **355 MPa** or **390 MPa**), controlled chemical composition (especially low **Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV)**), excellent weldability without pre-heating, and verified notch toughness through **Charpy V-notch impact testing** at sub-zero temperatures down to **−60 °C**.

The steels are graded on the basis of two simultaneous performance axes:

1. **Strength level** — Ordinary Strength (OS), Higher Strength (HTS 32, HTS 36, HTS 40).
2. **Toughness level** — Grades **A / B / D / E / F** corresponding to impact test temperatures of **0 °C / 0 °C / −20 °C / −40 °C / −60 °C**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Maritime Material Menu":**
> Imagine you are building a ship. You walk into a steel warehouse. The shelves are not arranged by brand, but by **two big questions**:
> - *"How strong must the steel be?"* → that is the **strength family** (235 / 315 / 355 / 390 MPa).
> - *"How cold will the ocean get where this ship sails?"* → that is the **toughness grade** (A through F).
>
> Just like ordering a coffee (size + temperature), a naval architect orders **EH36** steel: a **E**xtra-tough, **H**igher-tensile (yield **36** kgf/mm² ≈ **355 MPa**) plate that survives a **−40 °C** Arctic deck.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (in Bold)

- **Minimum Yield Strength (ReH):** **235 MPa** to **390 MPa**
- **Tensile Strength Range (Rm):** **400 – 520 MPa** (OS); **440 – 590 MPa** (HTS)
- **Minimum Elongation (A₅):** **22 %**
- **Charpy Impact Energy (KV):** **27 J** longitudinal, **20 J** transverse (minimum)
- **Carbon Equivalent (CEV) — IIW formula:** typically **≤ 0.40 %** for thicknesses up to **50 mm**
- **Standard Reference:** IACS Unified Requirements **UR S1**, **UR S6**, **UR W1**; also **ASTM A36 / A131 / A573**, **EN 10025** series.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Shipbuilding Quality Steels — The Logical Breakdown

Marine structural steels are organized into **three strength families** and **five toughness grades**, giving a 3 × 5 matrix of standard commercial plate grades.

#### A. Strength Families
- **Ordinary Strength (OS) Steels** — *ReH ≥ 235 MPa*, *Rm = 400 – 520 MPa*.
  - Designation: **Grade A, B, D, E**
- **Higher Tensile Strength (HTS) Steels** — *ReH ≥ 315 MPa* (32 kg grade), *ReH ≥ 355 MPa* (36 kg grade), *ReH ≥ 390 MPa* (40 kg grade).
  - Designation: **AH32, DH32, EH32, FH32**; **AH36, DH36, EH36, FH36**; **AH40, DH40, EH40, FH40**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for HTS Designation:** The letter that precedes "32 / 36 / 40" tells you the **toughness grade**.
> - **A** → tested at **0 °C**
> - **D** → tested at **−20 °C**
> - **E** → tested at **−40 °C**
> - **F** → tested at **−60 °C**

#### B. Toughness Grades (Charpy V-Notch Test Temperatures)

| Grade Letter | Test Temperature | Typical Service Region |
|--------------|------------------|------------------------|
| **A** | **0 °C** | Tropical / Temperate waters |
| **B** | **0 °C** | Same as A but stricter deoxidation |
| **D** | **−20 °C** | North Atlantic, North Sea |
| **E** | **−40 °C** | Arctic, Baltic winter |
| **F** | **−60 °C** | Polar / Ice-strengthened hulls |

#### C. Deoxidation Practice

| Deoxidation Type | Typical Grade | Si content (max) |
|------------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Rimmed (R)** | A | ≤ 0.05 % (rare today) |
| **Semi-killed (S)** | A | 0.10 – 0.35 % |
| **Al-killed (K)** | B, D, E, F | ≥ 0.015 % Acid-Soluble Al |
| **Fine-grain fully Al-killed normalized (N)** | E, F | ASTM grain size ≥ 5 |

### 2.2 The Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does CEV matter?** — It is the single most important weldability index. A **higher CEV** means the steel will form **hard, brittle martensite** in the **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** adjacent to the weld, leading to **cold cracking (hydrogen-induced cracking)**. Ship plates therefore keep CEV **≤ 0.40 %** to allow welding without costly pre-heating.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Name | Formula / Definition | Boundary / Typical Value |
|--------|------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| $\text{CEV}_{\text{IIW}}$ | IIW Carbon Equivalent | $\text{C} + \dfrac{\text{Mn}}{6} + \dfrac{\text{Cr} + \text{Mo} + \text{V}}{5} + \dfrac{\text{Ni} + \text{Cu}}{15}$ | $\leq 0.40\,\%$ for $t \leq 50\,\text{mm}$ |
| $\text{Pcm}$ | Critical Metal Parameter (Japanese) | $\text{C} + \dfrac{\text{Si}}{30} + \dfrac{\text{Mn}+\text{Cu}+\text{Cr}}{20} + \dfrac{\text{Ni}}{60} + \dfrac{\text{Mo}}{15} + \dfrac{\text{V}}{10} + 5\text{B}$ | $\leq 0.22\,\%$ for high-heat-input welding |
| $R_{eH}$ | Upper Yield Strength | Stress at upper yield point | $\geq 235\,\text{MPa}$ (OS); $\geq 355\,\text{MPa}$ (HTS-36) |
| $R_{m}$ | Ultimate Tensile Strength | Max load / original area | $400$–$520\,\text{MPa}$ (OS); $490$–$620\,\text{MPa}$ (HTS-36) |
| $A_{5}$ | Percentage Elongation | $\dfrac{L_{u} - L_{0}}{L_{0}} \times 100$ | $\geq 22\,\%$ |
| $KV$ | Charpy V-Notch Energy | Measured at test temperature | $\geq 27\,\text{J}$ longitudinal |
| $\sigma_{\text{perm}}$ | Permissible Stress (UR S11) | $\dfrac{R_{eH}}{\gamma_{m}}$, with $\gamma_{m}=1.15$ | Designer-checks |
| $t$ | Plate Thickness | — | $5$ to $100\,\text{mm}$ typical |

> All values are **as per IACS UR S6 / UR S1**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hull plating** uses OS grades (A, B, D) up to the sheer strake.
- **High-stress regions** — sheer strake, bilge strake, hatch coamings, rudder horns — use **AH36 / DH36 / EH36**.
- **Polar-class vessels** (PC1–PC7, IACS Polar Code) demand **FH36 / FH40** plates.
- **Submarine pressure hulls** use **HY-80** ($R_{eH} \geq 550\,\text{MPa}$) and **HY-100** ($R_{eH} \geq 690\,\text{MPa}$) per **MIL-S-16216**.
- **LNG carrier membrane walls** use **304L / 316L austenitic stainless** or **9 % Ni steel** for cryogenic service down to **−163 °C**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Properties of Standard IACS Grades

| IACS Grade | Yield $R_{eH}$ (MPa) | Tensile $R_{m}$ (MPa) | Min. $A_{5}$ (%) | Charpy Test (°C) | Min. $KV$ (J) | Deoxidation |
|------------|----------------------|------------------------|-------------------|------------------|----------------|--------------|
| **A** | 235 | 400 – 520 | 22 | 0 | 27 | Semi-killed or Al-killed |
| **B** | 235 | 400 – 520 | 22 | 0 | 27 | Al-killed |
| **D** | 235 | 400 – 520 | 22 | −20 | 27 | Al-killed, normalized |
| **E** | 235 | 400 – 520 | 22 | −40 | 27 | Al-killed, fine-grain, normalized |
| **AH32** | 315 | 440 – 590 | 22 | 0 | 31 | Al-killed |
| **DH32** | 315 | 440 – 590 | 22 | −20 | 31 | Al-killed, normalized |
| **EH32** | 315 | 440 – 590 | 22 | −40 | 31 | Al-killed, fine-grain |
| **AH36** | 355 | 490 – 620 | 21 | 0 | 34 | Al-killed |
| **DH36** | 355 | 490 – 620 | 21 | −20 | 34 | Al-killed, normalized |
| **EH36** | 355 | 490 – 620 | 21 | −40 | 34 | Al-killed, fine-grain |
| **FH36** | 355 | 490 – 620 | 21 | −60 | 34 | Al-killed, fine-grain |
| **AH40** | 390 | 510 – 660 | 20 | 0 | 34 | Al-killed |
| **DH40** | 390 | 510 – 660 | 20 | −20 | 34 | Al-killed, normalized |
| **EH40** | 390 | 510 – 660 | 20 | −40 | 34 | Al-killed, fine-grain |
| **FH40** | 390 | 510 – 660 | 20 | −60 | 34 | Al-killed, fine-grain |

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — CEV for a Typical EH36 Plate

A typical **EH36** plate from a steel mill has the following ladle analysis (wt-%):

$$
\text{C} = 0.16,\quad \text{Mn} = 1.45,\quad \text{Si} = 0.30,\quad \text{Cr} = 0.05,\quad \text{Ni} = 0.10,\quad \text{Mo} = 0.02,\quad \text{V} = 0.005,\quad \text{Cu} = 0.05
$$

**Step 1 — Write down the IIW formula.**

$$
\text{CEV}_{\text{IIW}} = \text{C} + \frac{\text{Mn}}{6} + \frac{\text{Cr} + \text{Mo} + \text{V}}{5} + \frac{\text{Ni} + \text{Cu}}{15}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the chemical composition.**

$$
\text{CEV}_{\text{IIW}} = 0.16 + \frac{1.45}{6} + \frac{0.05 + 0.02 + 0.005}{5} + \frac{0.10 + 0.05}{15}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term one at a time.**

The manganese contribution:
$$
\frac{1.45}{6} = 0.2417
$$

The Cr + Mo + V contribution:
$$
\frac{0.05 + 0.02 + 0.005}{5} = \frac{0.075}{5} = 0.0150
$$

The Ni + Cu contribution:
$$
\frac{0.10 + 0.05}{15} = \frac{0.15}{15} = 0.0100
$$

**Step 4 — Sum all contributions.**

$$
\text{CEV}_{\text{IIW}} = 0.16 + 0.2417 + 0.0150 + 0.0100
$$

$$
\text{CEV}_{\text{IIW}} = 0.4267\ \%
$$

**Step 5 — Engineer’s interpretation.**

$$
0.4267\ \% \approx 0.43\ \%
$$

This is **slightly above the 0.40 % threshold** for **as-rolled EH36** plates of thickness $t \leq 50\,\text{mm}$. The steelmaker would either:

- **Option (i):** Reduce **Mn** to **1.40 %** (lowering the Mn term to **0.2333**) which drops CEV to **≈ 0.418 %** — still above, requiring **Option (ii)**.
- **Option (ii):** Apply **Thermo-Mechanically Controlled Processing (TMCP)**. Under **IACS UR S6**, TMCP plates are allowed a **+0.02 % relaxation** on the CEV limit, so the acceptance threshold becomes **0.42 %**, and the plate passes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight** — Modern ship plates (post-2010) are mostly **TMCP** rather than normalized because TMCP gives a **finer grain (≈ 10–20 µm)** and **higher toughness** at **lower CEV**, eliminating expensive heat-treatment furnaces.

### 3.3 Worked Derivation — Permissible Bending Radius for EH36 Plate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in shipbuilding** — Plates are bent into curved hull sections using **line-heating** or **cold-rolling**. Cracks form if the bend radius is too small relative to the plate thickness and its ductility.

The classification society rule (IACS UR S6, §4.3) for **cold forming** is:

$$
R_{\min} = k \cdot t \quad \text{when} \quad t \leq 25\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
R_{\min} = (k + 0.5) \cdot t \quad \text{when} \quad t > 25\,\text{mm}
$$

where $k = 2.0$ for grades A / B / D / E and $k = 2.5$ for HTS grades (AH/DH/EH/FH 32/36/40).

**Worked Example** — Cold-bend an **EH36** plate of thickness $t = 30\,\text{mm}$.

Since $t = 30\,\text{mm} > 25\,\text{mm}$, we use the second formula:

$$
R_{\min} = (k + 0.5) \cdot t = (2.5 + 0.5) \cdot 30 = 3.0 \cdot 30 = 90\,\text{mm}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{R_{\min} = 90\,\text{mm} = 9\,\text{cm}}
$$

If the part has to be **hot-formed** at **≈ 950 °C**, the rule relaxes to $R_{\min} = 1.0\,t$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the IACS Grade Naming System

The naming convention is a **three-tier hierarchy**. The figure below maps how a single grade code (e.g., **EH36**) is decoded.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Grade Code Input] --> B{Letter A or no prefix?}
    B -- Yes --> C[OS Family ReH >= 235 MPa]
    B -- No --> D{Number 32, 36 or 40?}
    D -- 32 --> E[HTS-32 Family ReH >= 315 MPa]
    D -- 36 --> F[HTS-36 Family ReH >= 355 MPa]
    D -- 40 --> G[HTS-40 Family ReH >= 390 MPa]
    C --> H{Initial Letter A B D E F}
    E --> I{Initial Letter A D E F}
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H -- A --> J[Test at 0 deg C Tropical]
    H -- B --> K[Test at 0 deg C Al killed]
    H -- D --> L[Test at minus 20 deg C]
    H -- E --> M[Test at minus 40 deg C]
    H -- F --> N[Test at minus 60 deg C Polar]
    I -- A --> O[Test at 0 deg C]
    I -- D --> P[Test at minus 20 deg C]
    I -- E --> Q[Test at minus 40 deg C]
    I -- F --> R[Test at minus 60 deg C Polar]
    J --> S[Final Grade e.g. A]
    K --> T[Final Grade e.g. B]
    L --> U[Final Grade e.g. D or D32 D36 D40]
    M --> V[Final Grade e.g. E or E32 E36 E40]
    N --> W[Final Grade e.g. F or F32 F36 F40]
    O --> X[Final Grade e.g. AH32 AH36 AH40]
    P --> Y[Final Grade e.g. DH32 DH36 DH40]
    Q --> Z[Final Grade e.g. EH32 EH36 EH40]
    R --> AA[Final Grade e.g. FH32 FH36 FH40]
```

### 4.2 Ship Cross-Section Showing Typical Steel Application Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Hull[Hull Cross Section of a Bulk Carrier] --> DeckZone[Strength Deck]
    Hull --> SideShell[Side Shell]
    Hull --> BilgeStrake[Bilge Strake]
    Hull --> BottomShell[Bottom Shell]
    DeckZone --> S1[Deck Plate use EH36 355 MPa]
    SideShell --> S2[Side Shell use Grade A 235 MPa]
    BilgeStrake --> S3[Bilge Strake use EH36 355 MPa high fatigue]
    BottomShell --> S4[Bottom Plate use Grade A or D]
    S1 --> Note1[Reason high tensile at deck for longitudinal bending]
    S3 --> Note2[Reason bilge is stress concentration zone]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow for Selecting the Correct Ship Plate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start[Start Selection] --> Step1[Determine design yield strength]
    Step1 --> Step2[Choose strength family OS or HTS 32 36 40]
    Step2 --> Step3[Determine minimum service temperature]
    Step3 --> Step4[Choose toughness grade A B D E F]
    Step4 --> Step5[Check plate thickness against CEV limit]
    Step5 --> Step6[Check weldability preheat or PWHT]
    Step6 --> Step7[Confirm with classification society rules]
    Step7 --> End[Final Grade Selected e.g. EH36]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams** — These block diagrams replace a 2-D physical drawing of a hull plate, focusing instead on the **functional decision tree** that a naval architect walks through when specifying steel.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define shipbuilding quality steel. Mention any two classification society grade designations.**

**Model Answer:**
Shipbuilding quality steel is a carbon-manganese structural steel conforming to IACS Unified Requirements, possessing guaranteed minimum yield strength, controlled chemical composition with low Carbon Equivalent Value, and verified notch toughness via Charpy impact testing.
Two grade designations: **EH36** and **DH32**.
*EH36* = HTS-36 family ($R_{eH} \geq 355\,\text{MPa}$) tested at **−40 °C**. *DH32* = HTS-32 family ($R_{eH} \geq 315\,\text{MPa}$) tested at **−20 °C**.

#### Question 2 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is Carbon Equivalent Value? Write the IIW formula.**

**Model Answer:**
Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV) is an empirical index predicting weldability — it represents the combined hardening effect of alloying elements expressed as an equivalent percentage of carbon.
**IIW formula:**
$$
\text{CEV}_{\text{IIW}} = \text{C} + \frac{\text{Mn}}{6} + \frac{\text{Cr} + \text{Mo} + \text{V}}{5} + \frac{\text{Ni} + \text{Cu}}{15}
$$
Higher CEV → greater risk of **HAZ hydrogen cracking** during welding.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Classify shipbuilding quality steels based on **strength** and **toughness**. Explain the meaning of grade **EH36** with reference to its yield strength, tensile strength, elongation, and Charpy impact test requirements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plate of grade **EH36** has the following ladle analysis: **C = 0.15 %, Mn = 1.40 %, Si = 0.25 %, Cr = 0.04 %, Ni = 0.08 %, Mo = 0.01 %, V = 0.003 %, Cu = 0.03 %**. Compute the IIW Carbon Equivalent Value and comment on its weldability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Ship steels are classified on **two axes**:

- **Strength families:** Ordinary Strength (OS) with $R_{eH} \geq 235\,\text{MPa}$, and Higher Tensile Strength (HTS) with $R_{eH} \geq 315, 355, 390\,\text{MPa}$ corresponding to grades 32, 36, 40.
- **Toughness grades:** A (0 °C), B (0 °C, Al-killed), D (−20 °C), E (−40 °C), F (−60 °C).

For **EH36**:
- Strength: $R_{eH} \geq \mathbf{355\ \text{MPa}}$.
- Tensile strength range: $\mathbf{490\ –\ 620\ \text{MPa}}$.
- Minimum elongation: $\mathbf{21\ \%}$.
- Charpy V-notch energy: $\mathbf{34\ J}$ (longitudinal) at test temperature $\mathbf{-40\ ^\circ\text{C}}$.
- Deoxidation: **Al-killed, fine-grain, normalized or TMCP**.

**(b) CEV Calculation:**

Given: C = 0.15, Mn = 1.40, Si = 0.25, Cr = 0.04, Ni = 0.08, Mo = 0.01, V = 0.003, Cu = 0.03.

**Step 1:** Compute $\dfrac{\text{Mn}}{6}$:
$$
\frac{1.40}{6} = 0.2333
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\dfrac{\text{Cr} + \text{Mo} + \text{V}}{5}$:
$$
\frac{0.04 + 0.01 + 0.003}{5} = \frac{0.053}{5} = 0.0106
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\dfrac{\text{Ni} + \text{Cu}}{15}$:
$$
\frac{0.08 + 0.03}{15} = \frac{0.11}{15} = 0.0073
$$

**Step 4:** Sum:
$$
\text{CEV} = 0.15 + 0.2333 + 0.0106 + 0.0073 = 0.4012\ \% \approx 0.40\ \%
$$

**Comment on weldability:**
A CEV of **0.40 %** is **exactly at the upper limit** permitted by IACS UR S6 for as-rolled EH36 plates of thickness $t \leq 50\,\text{mm}$. The plate is therefore weldable without mandatory pre-heating up to a **heat input of about 2.0 kJ/mm**. For higher heat inputs or thicker plates, **TMCP** processing is recommended.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the significance of **Charpy V-notch impact testing** in the classification of shipbuilding steels. Discuss the role of **deoxidation practice** (rimmed, semi-killed, killed, fine-grain) in determining the toughness grade. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipyard is building an Arctic vessel that will operate at minimum sea temperatures of **−45 °C**. Recommend a suitable plate grade for the side shell and justify your selection with reference to IACS rules. The plate thickness required is **28 mm**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Significance of Charpy V-notch test:
The Charpy V-notch test measures the **energy absorbed** during fracture of a notched specimen struck by a pendulum. For shipbuilding steels, it provides a **quantitative measure of toughness** at service temperatures, guarding against **brittle fracture** of the hull — the most catastrophic failure mode in marine structures (e.g., the **Liberty Ship fractures of WWII**).
A minimum energy of **27 J to 34 J** must be absorbed at the appropriate test temperature corresponding to the grade (A through F).

**Role of deoxidation:**
- **Rimmed steel** (Grade A): Effervesces during solidification → high O₂ → poor toughness → not used for cold-service.
- **Semi-killed** (Grade A): Partial deoxidation → moderate toughness.
- **Al-killed** (Grades B, D, E, F): Aluminum fixes nitrogen as AlN → suppresses grain growth → improves toughness.
- **Fine-grain Al-killed, normalized** (Grades E, F): ASTM grain size **≥ 5** → transition temperature lowered by ≈ 30 °C → enables sub-zero service.

**(b) Recommendation for Arctic Vessel:**
Minimum service temperature = **−45 °C**.
Test temperature must be **lower than service temperature** by at least **20 °C** per IACS UR S6 → required test temperature $\leq -65\ ^\circ\text{C}$.
The **F-grade** is tested at **−60 °C**, which is just **5 °C above the required −65 °C** — borderline. For full safety margin, the **EH36** plate at **−40 °C** test (acceptable margin for plate thickness 28 mm with reduced heat-input welding) is often chosen, but the **recommended grade for true polar service is FH36**.

**Selection:** **FH36** plate of 28 mm thickness.
- Strength: $R_{eH} \geq 355\,\text{MPa}$ (high stress regions of side shell near ice-belt).
- Toughness: Charpy at **−60 °C** with $KV \geq 34\,\text{J}$ → guarantees ductile behaviour at the **−45 °C** service temperature.
- Process: **TMCP fine-grain, Al-killed** per IACS UR S6.
- Weldability: CEV $\leq 0.42\,\%$ due to TMCP relaxation → welding without pre-heating.
- Class notation: Polar Class **PC6 or PC7** per IACS Polar Code.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Wrong letter decoding** — Students often confuse *D-grade* (−20 °C) with *E-grade* (−40 °C). Remember: **A=0, D=−20, E=−40, F=−60**.
> 2. **Missing the suffix number** — In EH**36**, "36" denotes **yield strength 355 MPa (= 36 kgf/mm²)**, NOT the toughness test temperature.
> 3. **Skipping units in CEV calculation** — Always write **%** symbol and show **each step individually** in the IIW formula.
> 4. **Forgetting deoxidation** — A full answer on toughness grades *must* mention **Al-killed fine-grain normalization**; the examiner allocates **2 marks** specifically for the metallurgical reason.
> 5. **Confusing CEV (IIW) with Pcm** — Both are carbon equivalents but **different formulas**; Pcm is used for **low-carbon, low-alloy TMCP steels** while IIW is universal.
> 6. **No thickness reference** — Always state the plate **thickness (t)** when quoting a CEV limit because the threshold **increases** for thicker plates.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- Shipbuilding steels follow the **IACS Unified Requirements (UR S1, S6, S11, W1)** adopted by all major classification societies.
- **Two axes** of classification: **Strength** (OS / HTS-32 / HTS-36 / HTS-40) and **Toughness** (A / B / D / E / F).
- **Yield strengths:** 235 MPa (OS), 315 MPa (HTS-32), 355 MPa (HTS-36), 390 MPa (HTS-40).
- **Charpy test temperatures:** 0 °C / 0 °C / −20 °C / −40 °C / −60 °C for A / B / D / E / F respectively.
- **IIW CEV formula:** $\text{C} + \text{Mn}/6 + (\text{Cr} + \text{Mo} + \text{V})/5 + (\text{Ni} + \text{Cu})/15$.
- CEV must be $\leq \mathbf{0.40\ \%}$ for $t \leq 50\,\text{mm}$ (as-rolled); TMCP allows $\leq \mathbf{0.42\ \%}$.
- **Deoxidation practice** governs toughness: **Al-killed, fine-grain, normalized** = best toughness for E and F grades.
- **Standard sample format** for Charpy: $10 \times 10 \times 55\,\text{mm}$ with 2 mm deep 45° V-notch.
- **Elongation (A₅)** minimum: 22 % (OS), 22 % (HTS-32), 21 % (HTS-36), 20 % (HTS-40).
- **Cold-bending radius:** $R_{\min} = 2.5\,t$ (for HTS, $t \leq 25\,\text{mm}$); adds $0.5\,t$ when $t > 25\,\text{mm}$.
- **High-stress zones** (deck, bilge strake, sheer strake) → use **HTS-36** or higher.
- **Polar / Ice-class hulls** → use **F-grade** plates (tested at −60 °C).
- **Submarine HY-80 / HY-100** are special quenched-and-tempered steels for **pressure hulls**.
- **LNG carriers** use **9 % Ni steel** or **stainless 304L** for cryogenic service at **−163 °C**.
- Always **state classification society** (ABS / DNV / LR / BV / IRS) and **IACS UR reference** in KTU answers to gain full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mild steels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mild steels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mild steel** (also called **low-carbon steel** or **plain-carbon steel**) is a ferrous alloy of iron and carbon in which the carbon content is restricted to a maximum of approximately **0.25 % C** (typically 0.15 %–0.25 % C), with manganese content generally limited to **1.0 %–1.5 % Mn**, and trace levels of residual elements such as silicon, sulfur, and phosphorus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Mild steel is a type of carbon steel where the percentage of carbon is in the range of 0.15 % to 0.30 % by weight. It is the most common form of steel used in shipbuilding due to its excellent weldability, ductility, formability, and cost-effectiveness.

In the context of **shipbuilding (PENST414)**, mild steel forms the structural backbone of approximately 70 %–80 % of a vessel's hull, deck plating, bulkheads, and stiffeners. Classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV) refer to it by **designation grades** such as **Grade A, Grade B, Grade D, and Grade E** (for normal-strength shipbuilding steel) with minimum yield strength of **235 MPa**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a giant structure using a material that needs to:
- Bend without breaking (to shape curved hull plates),
- Be welded easily (to join thousands of pieces),
- Resist impact from waves (toughness),
- Cost relatively little (huge tonnages of steel are used),
- Be cut, rolled, and formed with standard workshop tools.

**Mild steel is the "reliable workhorse" of shipbuilding** — like a sturdy cotton T-shirt compared to a delicate silk shirt. A silk shirt is beautiful and strong in tension, but you cannot weld it, hammer it, or repair it at sea. A cotton T-shirt (mild steel) is not the strongest or most glamorous, but it is **ductile, weldable, machinable, formable, and economical** — exactly the qualities a shipyard needs in bulk structural material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuitive Takeaway:** Mild steel is chosen for ship hulls not because it is the strongest steel, but because it offers the **best balance** of strength, ductility, weldability, and cost for a 200-metre-long floating structure that must survive a 25-year service life in seawater.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following table summarizes the **reference mechanical and physical metrics** that every KTU 2024 student must memorize for mild steel:

| Property | Symbol | Typical Value (Mild Steel) | Unit |
|----------|--------|---------------------------|------|
| Density | $\rho$ | **7850** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **200–210** | $\text{GPa}$ |
| Shear Modulus | $G$ | **80–82** | $\text{GPa}$ |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | **0.29–0.30** | dimensionless |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | $UTS$ | **400–550** | $\text{MPa}$ |
| Yield Strength | $\sigma_y$ | **235–250** | $\text{MPa}$ |
| Elongation at fracture | $\varepsilon_f$ | **22–30** | % |
| Brinell Hardness | $HB$ | **120–180** | - |
| Melting Point | $T_m$ | **1480–1520** | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Specific Heat | $c_p$ | **486** | $\text{J/(kg·K)}$ |
| Thermal Conductivity | $k$ | **45–55** | $\text{W/(m·K)}$ |
| Coefficient of Thermal Expansion | $\alpha$ | **11.6 × 10⁻⁶** | $^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ |

> [!TIP]
> The yield strength of **235 MPa** is the single most important number to remember. Classification societies (LR, DNV, ABS) have standardized around this number for "normal-strength" shipbuilding plate (NSS).

## 1.4 Historical & Shipbuilding Context

The transition from iron to mild steel in shipbuilding occurred in the late 19th century. Prior to **1860**, ships were built of wrought iron and riveted plates. After the **Bessemer process** (1856) and the **Siemens-Martin open-hearth process** (1860s) made mild steel economically available, shipbuilders rapidly adopted it because:

1. **Higher strength-to-weight ratio** than wrought iron (≈ 25 % stronger).
2. **Homogeneous properties** (no laminations like in wrought iron).
3. **Superior weldability** once arc welding matured in the 1920s–1940s.
4. **Better corrosion resistance in seawater** when coupled with coating systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Hint:** Whenever a question mentions "why mild steel is preferred for ship hulls over wrought iron or high-carbon steel", the four reasons above form the textbook answer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemical Composition of Mild Steel

The properties of mild steel are governed primarily by its chemical composition. The KTU 2024 syllabus and classification society rules (IACS Unified Requirements) prescribe tight limits on each element.

### 2.1.1 Detailed Composition Breakdown

**1. Iron (Fe):** The base metal, typically **98.5 %–99.5 %** by weight. Provides the **BCC (Body-Centered Cubic) crystal lattice** at room temperature (α-ferrite).

**2. Carbon (C):** The most influential alloying element. In mild steel it is limited to **0.15 %–0.25 %**.
- Below **0.008 % C** → technically "iron" (no interstitial strengthening).
- **0.008 %–0.25 % C** → mild / low-carbon steel.
- **0.25 %–0.55 % C** → medium-carbon steel.
- Above **0.55 % C** → high-carbon steel (tools, springs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why carbon matters:** Carbon sits in the interstitial sites of the α-Fe lattice, distorting it and **pinning dislocations**, which increases hardness and tensile strength but **decreases ductility and weldability**. Each 0.01 % increase in C raises tensile strength by roughly **6–8 MPa** but lowers elongation by 1 %–2 %.

**3. Manganese (Mn):** Present at **0.60 %–1.50 %**. Functions:
- **Deoxidizer** during steelmaking (removes FeO inclusions).
- **Sulfide former** — converts harmful FeS into MnS, reducing "hot shortness" (cracking during hot rolling).
- Mild solid-solution strengthener (adds ≈ **50–100 MPa**).

**4. Silicon (Si):** Present at **0.10 %–0.35 %**. Acts as a **deoxidizer** (kills the steel) and gives slight solid-solution strengthening.

**5. Sulfur (S):** Limited to **≤ 0.035 %**. Excess S forms **FeS**, a low-melting-point compound that causes **hot shortness** — the steel cracks when hot-rolled or forged above 1000 °C.

**6. Phosphorus (P):** Limited to **≤ 0.035 %**. Excess P causes **cold shortness** — the steel becomes brittle at low temperatures. P strengthens by solid-solution but severely reduces toughness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *hot shortness* (S) and *cold shortness* (P). Memorize: **S**ulfur → **S**izzles → **H**ot. **P**hosphorus → **P**olar cold → **C**old.

### 2.1.2 Typical Shipbuilding Mild Steel Composition (Grade A)

| Element | Min (%) | Max (%) | Function |
|---------|---------|---------|----------|
| C | — | **0.21** | Strength, hardness |
| Mn | **0.60** (≥ 2.5×C for ≤ 0.16 %C) | **1.40** (≤ 1.20 for Grade A, ≤ 1.40 for Grade D/E) | Toughness, deoxidation |
| Si | — | **0.50** | Deoxidation |
| S | — | **0.035** | Limit (hot shortness) |
| P | — | **0.035** | Limit (cold shortness) |
| Al | **0.015** (for killed steel, Grade D/E) | — | Grain refinement |
| Nb, V, Ti | trace | trace | Optional microalloying |

## 2.2 Mechanical Properties — The "Why" Behind Each Value

### 2.2.1 Stress-Strain Behavior

When mild steel is loaded in tension, the stress-strain curve has **four distinct regions**:

1. **Elastic Region (0 → $\sigma_y$):** Linear, slope = $E$. Fully recoverable.
2. **Yield Point Phenomenon:** Mild steel exhibits a **discontinuous yield** — upper yield point ($\sigma_{yu}$) followed by a drop to lower yield point ($\sigma_{yl}$), then a plateau (Lüders bands).
3. **Strain-Hardening Region:** Plastic deformation with rising stress due to dislocation multiplication.
4. **Necking and Fracture:** Localized reduction in area, ultimate tensile strength, then fracture at strain $\varepsilon_f$.

> [!NOTE]
> The **yield point phenomenon** is unique to low-carbon steels and is caused by **interstitial carbon and nitrogen atoms** pinning dislocations (Cottrell atmospheres). When the upper yield is exceeded, dislocations break free, the Lüders bands propagate at constant stress, and the phenomenon disappears in steels with > 0.3 % C or after temper-rolling.

### 2.2.2 Toughness and Impact Testing

Shipbuilding steel is tested using the **Charpy V-notch impact test** at specified temperatures:
- **Grade A:** 27 J at **+20 °C** (longitudinal), 20 J at +20 °C (transverse).
- **Grade B:** 27 J at **0 °C**.
- **Grade D:** 27 J at **−20 °C**.
- **Grade E:** 27 J at **−40 °C** (used for polar / ice-strengthened vessels).

### 2.2.3 Formability and Ductility

The high **% elongation (22 %–30 %)** and **reduction of area (45 %–55 %)** allow ship plates to be:
- **Cold-formed** into curved hull sections (roll-bending).
- **Hot-formed** above 900 °C for severe curves (bilge strakes, stem).
- **Flame-bent** for very thick plates (e.g., bulbous bow transitions).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists every formula you must know for the KTU ESE on mild steel. **Memorize all of them.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | Engineering stress | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Engineering strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sigma_t = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$ | True stress (uniaxial) | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_t = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$ | True strain | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{F_y}{A_0}$ | Yield strength | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 6 | $\sigma_{uts} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0}$ | Ultimate tensile strength | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 7 | $\%\text{El} = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | Percent elongation | % |
| 8 | $\%\text{RA} = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | Percent reduction in area | % |
| 9 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ (elastic) | Young's modulus | $\text{GPa}$ |
| 10 | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Shear modulus | $\text{GPa}$ |
| 11 | $\sigma_H = 3\sigma_{uts} - \sigma_y$ | Hardness-tensile (Meyer) | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 12 | $HB \approx 0.35 \times \sigma_{uts}$ | Brinell–UTS correlation | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 13 | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ (von Mises) | Equivalent stress (2D) | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 14 | $\sigma_f = \sigma_{uts} \left( \dfrac{m}{2e} \right)^{m/e}$ | Stress at fracture (Hollomon) | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 15 | $\alpha_{avg} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0 \, \Delta T}$ | Coefficient of thermal expansion | $^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ |
| 16 | $\sigma_T = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ | Thermal stress (constrained) | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 17 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E \, \alpha}$ | Temperature drop for yielding | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 18 | $L_t = L_0 (1+\alpha \, \Delta T)$ | Thermal expansion of length | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Note:** Formula #16 ($\sigma_T = E\alpha\Delta T$) is the basis of all *distortion / warping* problems in welded ship plates. When a plate is welded and cools, this stress tries to deform it.

## 2.4 Types of Mild Steel Used in Shipbuilding

### 2.4.1 By Carbon Content

| Type | Carbon (%) | Characteristics | Shipboard Use |
|------|------------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Very-low-carbon (rimmed)** | 0.05–0.10 | Excellent formability, low strength | Internal panels, light bulkheads |
| **Low-carbon (killed)** | 0.15–0.25 | Balanced strength, ductility, weldability | **Hull plates, main structural** |
| **Medium-carbon** | 0.25–0.45 | Higher strength, less weldable | Shafts, gears, connecting rods |

### 2.4.2 By Deoxidation Practice (KTU high-yield!)

This is a **favourite KTU question topic**:

**1. Rimmed Steel:**
- Incompletely deoxidized (only Si, no Al).
- During solidification, gas (CO) bubbles erupt at the surface, creating a tough "rim" with a porous core.
- **Disadvantages:** Inhomogeneous composition, **not allowed** for shipbuilding of critical structures by IACS rules.
- Cheap, good surface finish, deep-drawing quality.

**2. Capped Steel:**
- Same as rimmed but the ingot top is mechanically capped to stop gas evolution.
- Slightly more uniform than rimmed.
- Rarely used in modern shipbuilding.

**3. Semi-Killed Steel:**
- Partially deoxidized (some Si, no Al).
- Moderate gas evolution, some pipe shrinkage, balanced properties.
- Used for general structural sections.

**4. Killed Steel (fully deoxidized):**
- Fully deoxidized using **Al, Si, Mn**.
- No gas evolution, ingot is sound throughout, homogeneous composition.
- **Mandatory for shipbuilding plates** (especially Grades D and E).
- Contains a **silicon content ≥ 0.10 %** as a marker of deoxidation.
- Required for **through-thickness properties** (resistance to lamellar tearing in T-joints).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** **R**immed → **R**ough core, **R**ejected for ships. **K**illed → **K**ey material for shipbuilding, **K**lean (homogeneous).

### 2.4.3 By IACS Classification Society Grade

| IACS Grade | Yield (MPa) | Tensile (MPa) | Impact Test | Application |
|------------|-------------|---------------|-------------|-------------|
| **A** | 235 | 400–520 | 27 J @ +20 °C | Hull, mild climate |
| **B** | 235 | 400–520 | 27 J @ 0 °C | Hull, temperate |
| **D** | 235 | 400–520 | 27 J @ −20 °C | Hull, cold seas |
| **E** | 235 | 400–520 | 27 J @ −40 °C | Hull, polar / ice-class |
| **AH32 / DH32 / EH32** | **315** | 440–590 | 27 J @ 0/−20/−40 °C | **Higher-strength** hull |
| **AH36 / DH36 / EH36** | **355** | 490–620 | 27 J @ 0/−20/−40 °C | High-strength hull |
| **AH40 / DH40 / EH40** | **390** | 510–660 | 27 J @ 0/−20/−40 °C | Ultra-high strength |

> [!NOTE]
> Grades A, B, D, E are called **"normal-strength" (NS)** shipbuilding steel. Grades 32, 36, 40 are **"higher-strength" (HS)** — obtained by normalizing, thermomechanical controlled processing (TMCP), or quenching & tempering.

## 2.5 Heat Treatment of Mild Steel

Mild steel is rarely quenched (carbon too low to form hard martensite), but it undergoes several important thermal cycles in shipyards:

**1. Normalizing:**
- Heat to **880 °C–920 °C** (above $A_{c3}$), hold 1 hour per 25 mm thickness, air cool.
- **Purpose:** Refines coarse grain, improves toughness and impact strength.
- **Applied to:** Grades D and E (mandatory for D, E per IACS UR W).

**2. Stress-Relief Annealing:**
- Heat to **550 °C–650 °C**, hold, slow cool.
- **Purpose:** Removes residual stresses from rolling, forming, or welding.
- Common after heavy fabrication or before machining large components.

**3. Hot Forming / Hot Rolling:**
- Mild steel plates are produced by **controlled rolling** at 900 °C–1100 °C.
- The final rolling passes are performed below the recrystallization temperature (~ 950 °C) to refine grain — this is the **TMCP** (Thermo-Mechanical Controlled Processing) route.

**4. Temper-Rolling (Skin-Pass):**
- Light cold reduction (0.5 %–2 %) to suppress yield-point elongation and prevent **stretcher-strain marks** during subsequent forming.
- Done by the steel mill on cold-rolled sheet products.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Mild steel **cannot be hardened by quenching** because the carbon content is below the threshold (~ 0.30 % C) needed to form a significant volume of martensite. Surface hardening methods like **carburizing, nitriding, or induction hardening** are required if a hard surface is needed.

## 2.6 Welding Characteristics of Mild Steel

Mild steel's most prized property in shipbuilding is its **excellent weldability**.

**Weldability rating:** **Excellent** — can be welded by **SMAW, GMAW, FCAW, SAW, GTAW, PAW** without preheating (for thin plates ≤ 20 mm) and with **mild preheating (100 °C–150 °C)** for thicker plates or high-heat-input multi-pass welds.

**Carbon Equivalent (CE) — IIW Formula:**

$$CE_{IIW} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$$

For mild shipbuilding steel, $CE_{IIW} \leq 0.40$% — this is the threshold below which **cold cracking** in the heat-affected zone (HAZ) is not a concern, and no preheating is required.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** Always show the IIW Carbon Equivalent calculation in a welding-related problem. If $CE \leq 0.40$, **no preheat needed**; if $0.40 < CE \leq 0.60$, preheat to 100 °C–150 °C; if $CE > 0.60$, preheat to 200 °C+ and use low-hydrogen electrodes.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mild steel in shipbuilding is the material that enables:
- **Pre-fabrication** of large panels (40 m × 4 m) in a yard, then lifting them onto the slipway.
- **One-sided welding** of stiffeners using **FCW (flux-cored wire)** at speeds of 30–50 cm/min.
- **Automated panel lines** with multi-head welding gantries.
- **Repair at sea** with portable stick-electrode equipment.
- **Recycling** — 95 %+ of mild steel from scrapped ships is melted and re-used.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: True Stress vs Engineering Stress

The engineering stress is calculated using the **original** cross-sectional area $A_0$:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$$

However, during plastic deformation the cross-section continuously decreases. The **true stress** accounts for this:

$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{F}{A_i}$$

where $A_i$ is the instantaneous cross-sectional area.

**Step 1 — Volume constancy (incompressible plastic flow):**

$$A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $A_i$:**

$$A_i = A_0 \dfrac{L_0}{L_i}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering strain definition:**

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{L_i - L_0}{L_0} = \dfrac{L_i}{L_0} - 1$$

So:

$$\dfrac{L_i}{L_0} = 1 + \varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the volume constancy equation:**

$$A_i = A_0 \dfrac{1}{1+\varepsilon}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the true stress equation:**

$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{F}{A_i} = \dfrac{F}{A_0} \cdot (1+\varepsilon) = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_t = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)}$$

**Step 6 — True strain (logarithmic strain):**

$$\varepsilon_t = \int_{L_0}^{L_i} \dfrac{dL}{L} = \ln\left(\dfrac{L_i}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example #1: True Stress Calculation

**Problem:** A mild steel tensile specimen has an original diameter $d_0 = 12.5$ mm and original gauge length $L_0 = 50$ mm. During testing, the load reaches $F = 50{,}000$ N at an engineering strain of $\varepsilon = 0.20$. Calculate the engineering stress, the true stress, and the true strain.

**Step 1 — Calculate original cross-sectional area:**

$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (12.5)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (156.25)}{4} = 122.72 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Engineering stress:**

$$\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{122.72} = 407.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — True stress:**

$$\sigma_t = \sigma(1+\varepsilon) = 407.4 \times (1+0.20) = 407.4 \times 1.20 = 488.9 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — True strain:**

$$\varepsilon_t = \ln(1+\varepsilon) = \ln(1.20) = 0.1823 = 18.23\%$$

**Step 5 — Volume check (instantaneous area):**

$$A_i = \dfrac{A_0}{1+\varepsilon} = \dfrac{122.72}{1.20} = 102.27 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Verification:**

$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{F}{A_i} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{102.27} = 488.9 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The true stress is 20 % higher than the engineering stress because the cross-section has shrunk. After yielding, only true stress represents the actual material flow strength.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example #2: Carbon Equivalent for Weldability

**Problem:** A ship hull plate of Grade A composition has C = 0.20 %, Mn = 1.00 %, Si = 0.30 %, S = 0.025 %, P = 0.025 %, and trace amounts of Cr, Mo, V, Ni, Cu (negligible). Calculate the IIW Carbon Equivalent and determine whether preheating is required for a 25 mm-thick plate.

**Step 1 — Apply the IIW formula:**

$$CE_{IIW} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$$

**Step 2 — Insert values (Cr, Mo, V, Ni, Cu ≈ 0):**

$$CE_{IIW} = 0.20 + \dfrac{1.00}{6} + 0 + 0$$

$$CE_{IIW} = 0.20 + 0.1667 = 0.3667 \approx 0.37\%$$

**Step 3 — Apply the decision rule:**

| CE Value | Action |
|----------|--------|
| $CE \leq 0.40$ | No preheat required |
| $0.40 < CE \leq 0.60$ | Preheat 100–150 °C |
| $CE > 0.60$ | Preheat 200 °C+, low-hydrogen electrodes |

Since $CE = 0.37 < 0.40$, **no preheating is required** for the 25 mm plate.

**Step 4 — However, also apply the combined thickness + CE rule (IACS UR W):**

If $t > 50$ mm **OR** $CE > 0.41$, then preheat to ≥ 100 °C. Here $t = 25$ mm and $CE = 0.37$, so still **no preheat needed**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example #3: Thermal Stress in a Restrained Plate

**Problem:** Two mild steel hull plates are welded together and rigidly restrained. The weld region cools by $\Delta T = 200$ °C from the stress-free temperature. Calculate the thermal stress developed. Given: $E = 200$ GPa, $\alpha = 11.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C.

**Step 1 — Apply the thermal stress formula:**

$$\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\sigma_T = (200 \times 10^9) \times (11.6 \times 10^{-6}) \times 200$$

**Step 3 — Compute step by step:**

$$E \alpha = 200 \times 10^9 \times 11.6 \times 10^{-6} = 2.32 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa/°C}$$

$$\sigma_T = 2.32 \times 10^6 \times 200 = 4.64 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 464 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compare to yield strength:**

$$\sigma_y \text{ (mild steel)} = 235 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_T = 464 \text{ MPa} > 235 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

The thermal stress **exceeds the yield strength** — the plate will yield, redistribute stress, and undergo plastic strain. This is the basis of **welding residual stresses** and is why ship hull plates are stress-relieved or preheated before welding thick sections.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example #4: Yield Point Phenomenon and Lüders Strain

**Problem:** During a tensile test on a mild steel specimen, the upper yield stress is 280 MPa and the lower yield stress is 220 MPa. The Lüders band propagates at constant stress over a strain range of 0.04 (4 %). Calculate the stress drop at yielding and the energy absorbed during yielding.

**Step 1 — Stress drop at yield:**

$$\Delta\sigma_y = \sigma_{yu} - \sigma_{yl} = 280 - 220 = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Energy density absorbed during Lüders propagation:**

Energy per unit volume in the plateau region = stress × strain:

$$U_{Lüders} = \sigma_{yl} \times \Delta\varepsilon_{Lüders} = 220 \text{ MPa} \times 0.04 = 8.8 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

This 8.8 MJ/m³ of energy is the "ductility reserve" that allows mild steel to absorb localized stress concentrations in ship structures without cracking — a key reason mild steel is preferred for crash-prone zones like the bow.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Selection Guide for Mild Steel Grade

| Service Condition | Recommended IACS Grade | Carbon Range (%) | Heat Treatment | Reason |
|-------------------|------------------------|------------------|----------------|--------|
| Inland waterways, tropical | **A** | 0.15–0.21 | As-rolled | Cheapest, adequate toughness |
| Coastal, temperate seas | **B** | 0.15–0.21 | Normalized | Improved notch toughness |
| Open ocean, North Sea | **D** | 0.15–0.21 | Normalized + Al-killed | Low-temp toughness (−20 °C) |
| Polar, ice-strengthened | **E** | 0.15–0.21 | Normalized + fine-grain Al | Service at −40 °C |
| High-stress deck, sheer strake | **AH36 / DH36** | 0.16–0.20 | TMCP / Quench-Tempered | Higher yield (355 MPa) |
| Warship hull, weight-critical | **AH40 / EH40** | 0.16–0.18 | Quench-Tempered | Yield 390 MPa, light weight |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define mild steel. List any four characteristic properties that make it suitable for shipbuilding. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Mild steel is a low-carbon ferrous alloy containing **0.15 % to 0.25 %** carbon along with small amounts of Mn, Si, S, and P, the balance being iron.

**Four characteristic properties (2 Marks — ½ Mark each):**

1. **Excellent weldability** — Can be welded by all standard arc-welding processes without preheating (for CE ≤ 0.40).
2. **High ductility** — Elongation of 22 %–30 % allows cold forming of curved hull plates.
3. **Good machinability and formability** — Easy cutting, drilling, rolling, and bending in the shipyard.
4. **Adequate strength with low cost** — Yield strength of 235 MPa is sufficient for most hull structures at a fraction of the cost of alloy steels.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between rimmed steel and killed steel. Why is killed steel mandatory for shipbuilding plates? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Rimmed Steel (1 Mark) | Killed Steel (1 Mark) |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Deoxidation** | Incomplete (no Al) | Complete (with Al, Si) |
| **Gas evolution** | Vigorous during solidification | None (fully deoxidized) |
| **Ingot structure** | Porous core, dense rim | Homogeneous throughout |
| **Composition uniformity** | Poor (segregation) | Excellent |
| **Surface quality** | Good (rim) | Moderate |
| **Si content** | < 0.10 % | ≥ 0.10 % |
| **Use in shipbuilding** | Not permitted | **Mandatory** (1 Mark) |

**Reason killed steel is mandatory:**
Killed steel has **homogeneous composition**, **no internal porosity**, and is free from gas holes. This gives:
- Uniform mechanical properties throughout the plate.
- Better **through-thickness properties** (resistance to lamellar tearing in welded T-joints).
- Superior **impact toughness** at low temperatures (mandatory for Grades D and E).
- Reliable performance in critical ship structures.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 1]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch of the stress-strain curve, explain the mechanical behaviour of mild steel under tensile loading. Discuss the significance of the **yield point phenomenon** and the **Lüders bands** in ship plate forming. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel specimen of gauge length 50 mm and diameter 12.5 mm is tested in tension. The load at the upper yield point is **43 kN**, at the lower yield point is **34 kN**, and at fracture is **25 kN** when the gauge length is 62 mm and the diameter at fracture is 9.2 mm. Calculate the **(i)** upper yield strength, **(ii)** lower yield strength, **(iii)** engineering stress at fracture, **(iv)** true stress at fracture, **(v)** percent elongation, and **(vi)** percent reduction in area. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Stress-strain curve description (3 Marks):**

```
       Stress σ (MPa)
        |
  500 - |                                          .
  450 - |                                    .         ← UTS
  400 - |                              .  
  300 - |          .─.  Upper yield 280
  280 - |        ╱     ╲
  220 - |      ╱        ────────────  ← Lower yield plateau
  200 - |    ╱                       .
  150 - |  ╱                       .   ← Strain hardening
  100 - |╱                      .
   50 - |                    .
    0 - |_________________________
        0    0.1  0.2  0.3  Strain ε
```

The four regions are:
1. **Elastic region** (0 → $\sigma_{yu}$): Linear, slope = $E = 200$ GPa.
2. **Yield point phenomenon** with discontinuous yield: sharp drop from upper to lower yield.
3. **Lüders plateau**: Constant stress ($\sigma_{yl}$) while plastic strain propagates heterogeneously as bands.
4. **Strain hardening + necking + fracture.**

**Step 2 — Mechanism of yield point (2 Marks):**

Carbon and nitrogen interstitials in the BCC iron lattice form **Cottrell atmospheres** around dislocations, pinning them. The **upper yield stress** is the stress required to tear dislocations free from these atmospheres. Once freed, dislocations multiply rapidly and travel at a lower stress (the **lower yield stress**). The localized plastic deformation is visible as **Lüders bands** (fronts of yielded material) that sweep across the specimen at constant stress.

**Step 3 — Significance in ship plate forming (2 Marks):**

- The **Lüders plateau** produces **visible surface markings** (stretcher strains) on formed plates — undesirable for painted hull surfaces.
- **Temper-rolling** (skin-pass) at the steel mill pre-deforms the sheet 0.5 %–2 % to raise the yield point and suppress the plateau, eliminating stretcher strains.
- The high uniform elongation in the plateau region gives mild steel its excellent **stretch-formability** for curved hull sections.

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $L_0 = 50$ mm, $d_0 = 12.5$ mm
- $F_{yu} = 43$ kN, $F_{yl} = 34$ kN, $F_f = 25$ kN
- $L_f = 62$ mm, $d_f = 9.2$ mm

**Step 1 — Original area:**

$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (12.5)^2}{4} = 122.72 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Final area at fracture:**

$$A_f = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (9.2)^2}{4} = 66.48 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — (i) Upper yield strength (1 Mark):**

$$\sigma_{yu} = \dfrac{F_{yu}}{A_0} = \dfrac{43{,}000}{122.72} = 350.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Lower yield strength (1 Mark):**

$$\sigma_{yl} = \dfrac{F_{yl}}{A_0} = \dfrac{34{,}000}{122.72} = 277.1 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — (iii) Engineering stress at fracture (1 Mark):**

$$\sigma_f = \dfrac{F_f}{A_0} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{122.72} = 203.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — (iv) True stress at fracture (1 Mark):**

$$\sigma_{t,f} = \dfrac{F_f}{A_f} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{66.48} = 376.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 7 — (v) Percent elongation (1 Mark):**

$$\%\text{El} = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{62 - 50}{50} \times 100 = 24.0\%$$

**Step 8 — (vi) Percent reduction in area (1 Mark):**

$$\%\text{RA} = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{122.72 - 66.48}{122.72} \times 100 = 45.83\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the paradox:** The engineering stress at fracture (203.7 MPa) is *less* than the lower yield strength (277.1 MPa), but the true stress at fracture (376 MPa) is *greater* than UTS — this is the engineering-vs-true stress distinction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the **IACS classification** of mild steel grades (A, B, D, E) used in ship hull construction. State the chemical composition limits, mechanical properties, and the impact test temperatures for each grade. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the **IIW Carbon Equivalent**? Calculate it for a hull plate with C = 0.18 %, Mn = 1.20 %, Si = 0.30 %, Cr = 0.10 %, Mo = 0.05 %, V = 0.02 %, Ni = 0.15 %, Cu = 0.10 %, and S = 0.025 %, P = 0.020 %. Based on the calculated CE, recommend the preheating temperature for welding a 30-mm-thick plate and justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — IACS background (1 Mark):**

The **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** has issued **Unified Requirements (UR W)** that standardize the chemical composition, mechanical properties, and impact testing of mild steel plates used in ship hull construction. Four normal-strength grades (A, B, D, E) are defined, each with a minimum yield strength of 235 MPa but with progressively lower impact test temperatures.

**Step 2 — IACS Grade Table (5 Marks — fill-in marks):**

| Grade | C max (%) | Mn range (%) | Si (%) | Impact Test | Min YS (MPa) | UTS (MPa) | %El (min) |
|-------|-----------|--------------|--------|-------------|--------------|-----------|-----------|
| **A** | 0.21 | 0.60–1.40 | ≤ 0.50 | **27 J @ +20 °C** | 235 | 400–520 | 22 |
| **B** | 0.21 | 0.60–1.40 | ≤ 0.50 | **27 J @ 0 °C** | 235 | 400–520 | 22 |
| **D** | 0.21 | 0.60–1.50 | ≤ 0.50 | **27 J @ −20 °C** | 235 | 400–520 | 22 |
| **E** | 0.18 | 0.70–1.60 | ≤ 0.50 | **27 J @ −40 °C** | 235 | 400–520 | 22 |

**Step 3 — Deoxidation requirement (1 Mark):**

- Grade A — may be rimmed or killed (modern practice: killed).
- Grade B — killed steel.
- Grade D — fully killed, **fine-grain treated with Al** (Al ≥ 0.015 %).
- Grade E — fully killed, Al-treated, **normalized** (mandatory heat treatment).

**Step 4 — Application guide (1 Mark — partial credit if mentioned):**

- A → tropical / inland
- B → coastal / temperate
- D → open ocean / North Sea
- E → polar / ice-strengthened vessels

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definition of IIW Carbon Equivalent (1 Mark):**

The **IIW Carbon Equivalent** is an empirical formula that lumps the effect of all major alloying elements into an equivalent carbon value. It predicts the **weldability and HAZ cold-cracking susceptibility** of carbon and low-alloy steels:

$$CE_{IIW} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$$

**Step 2 — Substitution of values (2 Marks):**

$$CE_{IIW} = 0.18 + \dfrac{1.20}{6} + \dfrac{0.10+0.05+0.02}{5} + \dfrac{0.15+0.10}{15}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term:**

$$CE_{IIW} = 0.18 + 0.200 + \dfrac{0.17}{5} + \dfrac{0.25}{15}$$

$$CE_{IIW} = 0.18 + 0.200 + 0.034 + 0.01667$$

$$CE_{IIW} = 0.4307 \approx 0.43\%$$

**Step 4 — Apply preheat decision rule (2 Marks):**

| CE Range | Action |
|----------|--------|
| CE ≤ 0.40 | No preheat |
| 0.40 < CE ≤ 0.60 | Preheat 100–150 °C, low-hydrogen electrode |
| CE > 0.60 | Preheat ≥ 200 °C, low-hydrogen, PWHT |

Since CE = 0.43 falls in the **0.40–0.60** range, **preheating to 100–150 °C** is recommended.

**Step 5 — Apply the IACS thickness-plus-CE combined rule (1 Mark):**

Per IACS UR W: if $t > 50$ mm **or** CE > 0.41, preheat to **≥ 100 °C**. Here $t = 30$ mm (no), but $CE = 0.43 > 0.41$ (**yes**), so preheat to **at least 100 °C** is mandatory.

**Step 6 — Justification (1 Mark):**

The CE value 0.43 % indicates **moderate HAZ hardening** risk. Hydrogen-induced cold cracking in the HAZ is likely if the weld cools too fast. Preheating slows the cooling rate, allows hydrogen to diffuse out of the HAZ, and reduces the risk of underbead cracking. Use of **low-hydrogen electrodes (E7018, basic coating)** is also recommended.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Mild-Steel Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final answer. MPa, °C, % must be stated explicitly. (Loses 0.5 mark per question.)
> 2. **Confusing hot shortness (S) with cold shortness (P).** Memorize: **S → Sizzles → Hot.** **P → Polar → Cold.**
> 3. **Using $\sigma_t = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$ outside the plastic regime** — this formula is valid only for **uniaxial plastic deformation** with constant volume.
> 4. **Omitting the constant-volume assumption** in true-stress derivations — full marks only if you show $A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$.
> 5. **Stating "mild steel can be hardened by quenching"** — WRONG. Carbon content is too low; only surface-hardening methods work.
> 6. **Forgetting Al ≥ 0.015 %** in the killed-steel specification for Grades D and E.
> 7. **Calculating %RA as $(d_0-d_f)/d_0$** instead of the correct area-based formula — the diameter ratio gives a wrong answer.
> 8. **Conflating Grade 36 yield (355 MPa) with Grade A yield (235 MPa)** — different categories of shipbuilding steel.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mild Steels**

- ☐ **Definition:** Low-carbon steel with **0.15 %–0.25 % C**, balance Fe with controlled Mn, Si, S, P.
- ☐ **Key number to memorize:** $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa (NSS Grade A/B/D/E), $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, $E = 200$ GPa, $\alpha = 11.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C.
- ☐ **Four IACS grades** (NS): **A, B, D, E** with impact test at **+20, 0, −20, −40 °C** respectively.
- ☐ **Killed steel mandatory** for grades D and E; Al-killed, normalized, fine-grain.
- ☐ **Hot shortness** = caused by S; **Cold shortness** = caused by P.
- ☐ **True stress:** $\sigma_t = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$. **True strain:** $\varepsilon_t = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$.
- ☐ **% Elongation** uses length change; **% RA** uses area change.
- ☐ **Weldability rule:** $CE \leq 0.40$ → no preheat; $0.40 < CE \leq 0.60$ → 100–150 °C; $CE > 0.60$ → ≥ 200 °C + low-H electrode.
- ☐ **Yield point phenomenon** unique to low-C steel; **Lüders bands** cause **stretcher strains**; suppressed by **temper rolling**.
- ☐ **Thermal stress in constrained plate:** $\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T$. For $\Delta T = 200$ °C, this equals 464 MPa, exceeding the yield of 235 MPa — explains welding residual stresses.
- ☐ **Rimmed steel NOT used for shipbuilding**; only killed or semi-killed allowed.
- ☐ **Mild steel cannot be quench-hardened** due to insufficient carbon; use carburizing/nitriding for surface hardness.
- ☐ **IACS higher-strength grades (HS):** 32 (315 MPa), 36 (355 MPa), 40 (390 MPa) — for weight-critical and high-stress areas.
- ☐ **Charpy impact test** with **27 J minimum** is the universal toughness acceptance criterion for IACS shipbuilding plate.
- ☐ **Brinell–UTS correlation:** $HB \approx 0.35 \times \sigma_{uts}$ (MPa).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normal strength steels (A, E classes)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Normal strength steels (A, E classes) ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Normal Strength Steels (NSS)** in shipbuilding are structural carbon-manganese steels with a specified minimum yield stress of **235 MPa** (N/mm²) at room temperature, governed by the unified requirements of the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)**. They are designated by letter grades (A, B, D, E) based on their **impact toughness at progressively lower service temperatures**, with **Class A** being the most basic grade and **Class E** representing the highest notch-toughness grade in the normal-strength category. | Class | Test Temperature | Primary Service Application | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | **A** | **+20 °C** | Hull plates in mild climates, superstructures | | **B** | **0 °C** | Hull plates in temperate waters | | **D** | **−20 °C** | Hull plates, longitudinals in cold regions | | **E** | **−40 °C** | Critical plates, hatch covers, ice-strengthened zones | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For the module on *Steel Classification for Shipbuilding*, the examiner specifically tests the difference in **chemical composition limits**, **deoxidation practice**, and **Charpy V-notch impact energy requirements** between Class A and Class E. Memorizing the test temperatures alone is **not sufficient** — you must know the underlying metallurgical reasons (killed vs. semi-killed, grain refinement). ### 1.2 Intuitive Overview (The "Shipyard Analogy") Imagine you are building a ship that sails from the **equator** to the **Arctic Ocean**. The hull is exposed to wildly different temperatures. A cheap steel that is perfectly fine in Kerala's 30 °C waters would become **brittle as glass** in −40 °C Arctic conditions. Think of the four classes (A, B, D, E) as a **"thermal safety rating"**: - **Class A steel** is the everyday cotton T-shirt — comfortable at room temperature but inadequate in the cold. - **Class E steel** is the heavy-duty arctic parka — engineered to absorb sudden impact energy even when the atoms inside are sluggish from extreme cold. The classification societies did not invent four random grades. They created a **temperature-toughness ladder** that allows a shipyard to **buy the cheapest steel that is safe for the intended route**. > [!NOTE] > **Definition — Charpy V-Notch (CVN) Impact Energy:** The energy (in Joules) absorbed by a standardised notched specimen when struck and fractured by a pendulum hammer. Higher CVN = tougher steel at that temperature. ### 1.3 Why "A" and "E" Are the Bookends - **Class A (the "warm-water baseline")**: Allowed to be **semi-killed** (partially deoxidised). It is the cheapest to produce because it doesn't require aluminium grain refinement. Used where the structure is **not subjected to dynamic, low-temperature loading**. - **Class E (the "premium cold-service grade")**: Must be **fully killed** with aluminium, treated for **fine grain**, and often subjected to **normalising rolling** or **normalising**. This is the metallurgical gold standard for hull plating that must survive **ice interaction** and **low-temperature brittle fracture**. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic to remember impact test temperatures:** **"A Boy Dares Everest"** → A (20), B (0), D (−20), E (−40). Read the temperature as degrees Celsius. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The IACS Unified Requirement UR W1 (formerly UR S1) All major classification societies (ABS, BV, DNV, Lloyd's Register, ClassNK, KR, CCS, RINA) subscribe to the **IACS Unified Requirements**, which have standardised the chemical and mechanical specifications for shipbuilding steels. The governing document for normal-strength hull steels is **UR W1** (previously UR S1). ### 2.2 Mechanical Property Requirements (Thickness ≤ 100 mm) For all four classes (A, B, D, E) of normal-strength steel, the **tensile properties are identical** — what differs is the **impact toughness** and the **deoxidation/grain-refining practice**. | Property | Specified Value | Unit | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | Minimum Yield Stress ($R_{eH}$) | **235** | MPa (N/mm²) | | Tensile Strength Range ($R_m$) | **400 – 520** | MPa | | Minimum Elongation on $L_0 = 5.65\sqrt{S_0}$ | **22** | % | | Charpy V-Notch Impact Energy (avg. of 3 specimens) | **27** | J | | Charpy V-Notch Impact Energy (single specimen min.) | **19** | J | > [!IMPORTANT] > The yield stress is the **same for A, B, D, and E**. Many students incorrectly write "Class E has higher yield strength." This is a common KTU board-exam trap. The differentiation is **purely toughness and metallurgy**, not strength. ### 2.3 Chemical Composition Requirements (Heat Analysis, % by mass max.) | Element | Class A | Class E | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | **C** | **0.21** | **0.18** | | **Mn** | ≥ 0.50 (semi-killed) / **2.5 × C** (for thickness > 25 mm) | **0.70 – 1.60** | | **Si** | **0.50** | **0.10 – 0.50** | | **P** | **0.035** | **0.035** | | **S** | **0.035** | **0.035** | | **Al (acid-soluble)** | — | **≥ 0.015** | | **Nb, V, Ti (microalloy, total)** | — | **0.05 – 0.12** | | **Cu, Cr, Ni, Mo (residual, each)** | — | **0.30** | **Key metallurgical inferences:** 1. **Lower carbon in Class E (0.18 vs 0.21)** — Reduces the risk of brittle martensite formation in the heat-affected zone (HAZ) during welding. 2. **Mandatory acid-soluble Al ≥ 0.015% in Class E** — Confirms that the steel is **fully aluminium-killed**, which produces a **fine-grain austenite** and removes dissolved nitrogen that would otherwise cause **strain-age embrittlement**. 3. **Controlled Mn range in Class E (0.70 – 1.60)** — Manganese raises the **ductile-to-brittle transition temperature** only mildly while massively increasing **solid-solution strengthening**.
