> The 2024 Scheme Module 1 places aluminium alloys as a *light-weight alternative* to steel for ship hulls, superstructures, deckhouses, masts, and high-speed craft. Their relevance stems from weight-sensitive naval architecture and superior corrosion resistance in marine environments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are building a paper boat versus a wooden boat. The paper boat is light, floats higher, and is easier to lift, but the wooden boat is heavier and stronger. **Aluminium alloys are the engineering "metal paper" of shipbuilding** — they are remarkably **lightweight** (like the paper boat) yet possess **structural strength comparable to mild steel** when properly alloyed. The alloying elements act like "rebar inside concrete" — invisible to the eye but vastly improving the strength of the base material.

Another useful analogy: **Pure aluminium is like soft clay** (malleable, ductile, weak). Add small quantities of **magnesium, silicon, or zinc** (alloying elements), and it transforms into a **hard, structural ceramic-like material** while still remaining light. This is exactly what we see in marine grade 5083 (Al-Mg) or 6061 (Al-Mg-Si) alloys used in patrol boats and fast ferries.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Property | Pure Aluminium (1xxx) | Marine Al-Alloy (5083-H116) | Mild Steel (for comparison) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density | **2.71 g/cm³** | **2.66 g/cm³** | 7.85 g/cm³ |
| Melting Point | **660 °C** | 574 – 638 °C | ~1500 °C |
| Young's Modulus | 69 GPa | 70.3 GPa | 200 GPa |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | 90 MPa | 305 – 385 MPa | 400 – 550 MPa |
| Corrosion Rate in Seawater | Moderate | **Very Low** (with proper protection) | High (without coating) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Aluminium Alloys

Aluminium alloys are broadly divided into two fundamental categories, as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus:

### A. Wrought Alloys (Worked into sheets, plates, extrusions, forgings)
These are designated by a **four-digit numerical system (1xxx – 8xxx)**.

### B. Cast Alloys (Cast into shape directly)
These are designated by a **three-digit system with a decimal (1xx.x – 9xx.x)**.

### 2.1.1 Wrought Alloy Series

| Series | Principal Alloying Element | Marine Significance |
|---|---|---|
| 1xxx | ≥ 99.0% Pure Al | Excellent corrosion resistance, low strength; used for chemical tanks |
| 2xxx | Copper (Cu) | High strength (like Al-Cu alloy 2014, 2024); **NOT** recommended for marine service due to SCC |
| 3xxx | Manganese (Mn) | Moderate strength, good formability; non-heat-treatable |
| 4xxx | Silicon (Si) | Low melting point; used as welding filler (e.g., 4043) |
| **5xxx** | **Magnesium (Mg)** | **★ Marine workhorse: 5083, 5086, 5454, 5456** — excellent corrosion resistance in seawater |
| **6xxx** | **Magnesium + Silicon** | **★ Structural marine alloy 6061, 6082** — heat-treatable, weldable |
| 7xxx | Zinc (Zn) | Very high strength (aerospace 7075); poor marine corrosion resistance |
| 8xxx | Miscellaneous (Li, Fe, etc.) | Al-Li for aerospace |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** For shipbuilding, focus is on **5xxx (Al-Mg)** and **6xxx (Al-Mg-Si)** series. Naval architects must remember that 5xxx alloys are **non-heat-treatable** (strengthened by cold work / strain hardening), whereas 6xxx alloys are **heat-treatable** (strengthened by precipitation hardening / T6 temper).

### 2.1.2 Cast Alloy System

The cast alloy designation uses a **decimal after the 3rd digit** to indicate product form:
- `xxx.0` → Castings
- `xxx.1` → Ingot
- `xxx.2` → Pig for remelting

Common marine cast alloys: **A356.0, A357.0, 508.0, 514.0** (used in marine hardware, propellers, pump housings).

## 2.2 Temper Designations (Critical for KTU)

The temper (mechanical/thermal condition) follows the alloy designation with a hyphen:

| Temper Code | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **F** | As Fabricated | 5083-F |
| **O** | Annealed (soft) | 5083-O |
| **H1x** | Strain-Hardened Only | 5083-H116 |
| **H2x** | Strain-Hardened + Partially Annealed | 5052-H32 |
| **H3x** | Strain-Hardened + Stabilized | 5083-H321 |
| **T4** | Solution Heat-Treated + Naturally Aged | 6061-T4 |
| **T6** | Solution Heat-Treated + Artificially Aged | 6061-T6 (peak strength) |
| **T7** | Solution Heat-Treated + Over-Aged (for SCC resistance) | 7050-T7 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Standard:** Naval and shipbuilding specifications (e.g., ASTM B928, MIL-DTL-46027) typically call for **5083-H116 / H321** (for hull plates) and **6061-T6** (for superstructures). The H116/H321 temper is *strain-hardened and stabilized* — essential for marine service to prevent *sensitization* and intergranular corrosion.

## 2.3 Heat Treatment Theory

### 2.3.1 Non-Heat-Treatable Alloys (1xxx, 3xxx, 4xxx, 5xxx series)
Strengthening is achieved by:
1. **Solid Solution Strengthening** (solute atoms distort the Al FCC lattice)
2. **Strain Hardening (Work Hardening)** — dislocations multiply and entangle
3. **Grain Size Refinement** (Hall-Petch effect)

The Hall-Petch relationship is critical:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_y$ = yield strength
- $\sigma_0$ = friction stress
- $k_y$ = Hall-Petch coefficient
- $d$ = average grain diameter

### 2.3.2 Heat-Treatable Alloys (2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx, some 4xxx)
Strengthening occurs via **Precipitation Hardening (Age Hardening)**, a three-stage process:

**Stage 1 — Solution Treatment:** Alloy is heated to ~**530 °C** for 2xxx/6xxx/7xxx, held until all soluble phases dissolve into a single-phase α solid solution, then **quenched** in water to retain a supersaturated solid solution (SSSS).

**Stage 2 — Quenching:** Rapid cooling "freezes" solute atoms in solution, producing a metastable structure. The alloy is now soft but ductile.

**Stage 3 — Aging (Natural or Artificial):**
- **Natural Aging (T4):** Room temperature, days to weeks → formation of **GP zones (Guinier-Preston)**
- **Artificial Aging (T6):** Elevated temperature (160 – 200 °C), several hours → formation of transition precipitates (e.g., **β″ in Al-Mg-Si**, **θ′ in Al-Cu, η′ in Al-Zn**) which are coherent/semi-coherent with the matrix and impede dislocation motion, **maximizing strength**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Over-Aging (T7):** Prolonged aging causes precipitates to grow and become incoherent, *reducing* strength but improving **stress corrosion cracking (SCC) resistance** — important for thick marine extrusions.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Strength (Strength-to-Weight ratio) | $R_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\rho \cdot g}$ | $\sigma_{UTS}$ in MPa, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $R_{sp}$ in kN·m/kg |
| 2 | Weight Savings vs Steel | $W_{sav}(\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{St}}\right) \times 100 \approx 65.5\%$ | For same volume part |
| 3 | Hall-Petch (Grain Refinement) | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Smaller grain $d$ → higher yield strength |
| 4 | Corrosion Penetration Rate | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}$ | K = 534 (for mm/yr, mg, cm², hr) |
| 5 | Thermal Conductivity | $k_{Al} \approx 167 \, \text{W/m·K}$ (vs $k_{St} \approx 50$) | 3× steel — useful in heat exchangers |
| 6 | Linear Thermal Expansion | $\alpha_{Al} \approx 23 \times 10^{-6} /°C$ (vs steel $\approx 12 \times 10^{-6}$) | Critical for bi-metallic joints |
| 7 | Galvanic Series Position | Al is *anodic* to steel, Cu, brass | Avoid direct contact in seawater |
| 8 | Minimum Bending Radius (Plate) | $R_{min} \approx 1.5 \cdot t$ (for 5083-O) | $t$ = plate thickness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In high-speed patrol vessels, ferries, and naval craft, the specific strength governs fuel efficiency. A displacement reduction of ~40% allows higher speeds for the same engine power. This is why modern Littoral Combat Ships and SWATH vessels extensively use 5083/6082.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Weight Reduction Calculation for an Aluminium Hull vs Steel Hull

**Problem Statement:** A small patrol vessel hull requires $V = 50 \, \text{m}^3$ of structural material. Compare the weight if built from mild steel (density $\rho_{St} = 7850 \, \text{kg/m}^3$) versus marine aluminium alloy 5083 (density $\rho_{Al} = 2660 \, \text{kg/m}^3$).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Recall the fundamental mass-density relationship.

$$m = \rho \cdot V$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values for the steel hull.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{St} &= \rho_{St} \cdot V \\
&= 7850 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \times 50 \, \text{m}^3 \\
&= 392{,}500 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values for the aluminium hull.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{Al} &= \rho_{Al} \cdot V \\
&= 2660 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \times 50 \, \text{m}^3 \\
&= 133{,}000 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the absolute mass saved.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta m &= m_{St} - m_{Al} \\
&= 392{,}500 - 133{,}000 \\
&= 259{,}500 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the percentage weight reduction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{sav}(\%) &= \left( \frac{\Delta m}{m_{St}} \right) \times 100 \\
&= \left( \frac{259{,}500}{392{,}500} \right) \times 100 \\
&\approx 66.1\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Marine Architects:** A **66% weight reduction** translates directly into lower displacement, higher speed at the same engine power, improved stability, and reduced fuel consumption. This is the **mathematical foundation** justifying aluminium use in fast ferries and naval combatants.

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Strength Comparison

**Problem:** Compare the specific strength of 5083-H116 ($\sigma_{UTS} = 317 \, \text{MPa}$, $\rho_{Al} = 2660 \, \text{kg/m}^3$) and mild steel ($\sigma_{UTS} = 450 \, \text{MPa}$, $\rho_{St} = 7850 \, \text{kg/m}^3$).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Define specific strength (strength per unit mass, multiplied by g to remove gravity dependence).

$$R_{sp} = \frac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\rho}$$

**Step 2:** Compute for aluminium 5083-H116.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{sp, Al} &= \frac{317 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa}}{2660 \, \text{kg/m}^3} \\
&= 119{,}172 \, \text{J/kg} \\
&\approx 119.2 \, \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute for mild steel.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{sp, St} &= \frac{450 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa}}{7850 \, \text{kg/m}^3} \\
&= 57{,}325 \, \text{J/kg} \\
&\approx 57.3 \, \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{R_{sp, Al}}{R_{sp, St}} &= \frac{119.2}{57.3} \\
&\approx 2.08
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** 5083-H116 aluminium has a specific strength **2.08× that of mild steel**, making it superior for weight-critical marine structures despite lower absolute strength.

## 3.3 Derivation: Corrosion Penetration Rate (ASTM G1 Standard)

**Problem:** A 5083-H116 specimen of area $A = 20 \, \text{cm}^2$ loses mass $W = 245 \, \text{mg}$ in seawater over $t = 240 \, \text{h}$. Find the corrosion rate in mm/yr.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Recall the ASTM G1 formula (K = 534 for these units).

$$CR = \frac{K \cdot W}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \frac{534 \times 245}{2660 \times 20 \times 240} \\
&= \frac{130{,}830}{12{,}768{,}000} \\
&= 0.01024 \, \text{mm/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Categorize per NACE classification (mm/yr):

| Rating | mm/yr | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Outstanding | < 0.025 | **5083 falls here** |
| Good | 0.025 – 0.12 | – |
| Fair | 0.12 – 0.25 | – |
| Poor | > 0.25 | – |

> [!NOTE]
> The calculated 0.010 mm/yr places 5083 in the **"Outstanding"** corrosion category — empirical proof of its marine suitability.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Marine Aluminium Alloys

| Application | Recommended Alloy | Temper | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hull plating (high-speed craft) | **5083** | H116 / H321 | Best seawater corrosion resistance; high weld strength retention |
| Superstructure / Deckhouse | **6082** | T6 | High strength, good weldability, architectural finish |
| Patrol boat hull | **5456** | H116 | Higher strength than 5083 (yield 255 MPa) |
| Mast / Pole | **6061** | T6 | Excellent extrudability, good fatigue resistance |
| Marine castings (propeller hub) | **A356.0** | T6 | Castable, heat-treatable, good strength |
| LNG cryogenic tanks | **5083-O** | O | Excellent low-temp toughness to -270 °C |
| Chemical tanker internal | **3003** | H14 | Good corrosion to chemicals, moderate strength |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Aluminium Alloys

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Aluminium Alloys] --> B1[Wrought Alloys]
    A0 --> B2[Cast Alloys]

    B1 --> C1[Non-Heat-Treatable]
    B1 --> C2[Heat-Treatable]

    C1 --> D1[1xxx - Pure Al]
    C1 --> D2[3xxx - Al-Mn]
    C1 --> D3[4xxx - Al-Si]
    C1 --> D4[5xxx - Al-Mg]

    C2 --> E1[2xxx - Al-Cu]
    C2 --> E2[6xxx - Al-Mg-Si]
    C2 --> E3[7xxx - Al-Zn]
    C2 --> E4[8xxx - Al-Li]

    B2 --> F1[2xx.x - Al-Cu Cast]
    B2 --> F2[3xx.x - Al-Si-Mg Cast]
    B2 --> F3[5xx.x - Al-Mg Cast]

    D4 --> G1[Marine 5083 H116]
    D4 --> G2[Marine 5086 H116]
    D4 --> G3[Marine 5456 H116]
    E2 --> G4[Marine 6061 T6]
    E2 --> G5[Marine 6082 T6]
    F2 --> G6[Marine A356.0 T6]
```

## 4.2 Age-Hardening (T6) Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[As-Cast or Hot Rolled Alloy] --> stepB[Solution Treatment at 530 C for 1 to 2 hours]
    stepB --> stepC[Quench in Cold Water 20 to 40 C]
    stepC --> stepD[Supersaturated Solid Solution - SOFT and DUCTILE]
    stepD --> stepE[Artificial Aging at 175 C for 8 to 12 hours]
    stepE --> stepF[Fine Coherent Precipitates Form]
    stepF --> stepG[Peak Strength T6 Condition Achieved]
    stepG --> stepH{Over-Aging?}
    stepH -->|No| stepI[Service in Marine Environment]
    stepH -->|Yes T7| stepJ[Coarser Precipitates - Lower Strength but Better SCC Resistance]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Marine Alloy Selection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Marine Component Requirement] --> S1{Load Type}
    S1 -->|Static Hull Plate| S2[Choose 5xxx series - Work Hardened]
    S1 -->|Dynamic Superstructure| S3[Choose 6xxx series - T6 Temper]
    S1 -->|Complex Casting| S4[Choose 3xx.x Cast Alloy]

    S2 --> S5{Corrosion Environment}
    S5 -->|Seawater Hull| S6[5083 H116 or 5456 H116]
    S5 -->|Atmospheric Deck| S7[5052 H32]

    S3 --> S8{Weldability Required}
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[6082 T6 or 6061 T6]
    S8 -->|No - Machined| S10[2011 T6 Free Machining]

    S4 --> S11[Propeller - A356.0 T6 or Ni-Al-Bronze]
    S4 --> S12[Valve Body - 356.0 T6]

    S6 --> S9a[Final Check per ASTM B928 or EN 13195]
    S9 --> S9a
    S7 --> S9a
    S10 --> S9a
    S11 --> S9a
    S12 --> S9a
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Galvanic Series in Seawater (Block-Level Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GAL1[Most Noble Cathodic] --> GAL2[Graphite and Gold]
    GAL2 --> GAL3[Nickel and Copper Alloys]
    GAL3 --> GAL4[Titanium]
    GAL4 --> GAL5[Stainless Steel Passive]
    GAL5 --> GAL6[Lead]
    GAL6 --> GAL7[Steel Cast Iron]
    GAL7 --> GAL8[Aluminium Alloys 5xxx and 6xxx]
    GAL8 --> GAL9[Zinc]
    GAL9 --> GAL10[Magnesium]
    GAL10 --> GAL11[Most Active Anodic]

    NOTE1>Important - In seawater the 5xxx and 6xxx aluminium alloys are anodic to steel copper and stainless steel. This means aluminium will corrode preferentially. Mitigate by using insulating gaskets and isolation bushings between dissimilar metals.]
    GAL8 -.-> NOTE1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** When aluminium must be joined to steel (e.g., a steel deck plate welded to an aluminium superstructure), use **bi-metallic transition inserts**, **insulating neoprene gaskets**, and **zinc sacrificial anodes** to break the galvanic circuit. This is a frequent KTU examination question topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**"List any three wrought aluminium alloy series suitable for marine applications and state the principal alloying element in each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Series | Principal Alloying Element | Marine Example |
|---|---|---|
| 5xxx | Magnesium (Mg) | 5083, 5086, 5456 |
| 6xxx | Magnesium + Silicon (Mg + Si) | 6061, 6082 |
| 3xxx | Manganese (Mn) | 3003 (for non-structural use) |

The **5xxx series (Al-Mg)** is the most widely used for ship hulls owing to its excellent corrosion resistance in seawater and good weld strength retention. (3 marks)

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**"Distinguish between heat-treatable and non-heat-treatable aluminium alloys with one example each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Heat-Treatable | Non-Heat-Treatable |
|---|---|---|
| Strengthening mechanism | Precipitation (age) hardening | Cold work / strain hardening |
| Example | 6061-T6 (Al-Mg-Si) | 5083-H116 (Al-Mg) |
| Required thermal process | Solution + quench + age | Annealing or rolling only |
| Weld strength loss | Significant (e.g., 6061 loses ~60%) | Minimal (5083 retains ~95%) |

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style]**

**(a) Classify aluminium alloys based on the AA designation system. Explain the salient features of 5xxx and 6xxx series with relevance to shipbuilding. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Aluminium alloys are classified by the **Aluminum Association (AA)** into **wrought** (1xxx – 8xxx) and **cast** (1xx.x – 9xx.x) categories. Wrought alloys are used as rolled, extruded, or forged products; cast alloys are poured into moulds.

**5xxx Series (Al-Mg):**
- Principal alloying element: **Magnesium (0.8 – 5.5%)**
- **Non-heat-treatable**; strengthened by solid solution and strain hardening
- Marine grade: **5083-H116** (4.5% Mg), used for hull plates
- Excellent **corrosion resistance in seawater** (forms protective Mg-oxide film)
- **Weldable** with negligible strength loss in the heat-affected zone
- Used in patrol boats, fast ferries, and LNG tank shells

**6xxx Series (Al-Mg-Si):**
- Principal alloying elements: **Mg (0.4 – 1.2%) + Si (0.2 – 1.0%)**
- **Heat-treatable**; reaches T6 strength of ~310 MPa
- Marine grade: **6082-T6**, **6061-T6** for superstructures, masts, deck fittings
- Excellent **extrudability** for complex profiles
- Slightly lower weld strength retention than 5xxx — uses 4043 / 5356 filler
- Architectural-finish quality (anodizing compatibility)

(7 marks)

**(b) With neat sketches, describe the age-hardening (T6) heat treatment process for 6xxx series aluminium alloys. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The age-hardening (T6) process involves three stages, schematically shown below:

**Stage 1 — Solution Treatment:**
The alloy is heated to **525 – 535 °C** and held for 1 – 2 hours. This dissolves Mg₂Si and any other soluble phases into a single-phase α solid solution.

**Stage 2 — Quenching:**
Rapid cooling in **cold water (20 – 40 °C)** traps the solute atoms in a **supersaturated solid solution (SSSS)**. At this stage, the alloy is soft and ductile.

**Stage 3 — Artificial Aging:**
Reheating to **160 – 180 °C** for 8 – 12 hours causes precipitation of:
- First: **GP zones** (Guinier-Preston zones — clusters of Mg and Si atoms)
- Then: **β″ precipitates** (coherent with the Al matrix — peak strength)
- Finally: **β′ and β (Mg₂Si)** if over-aged (T7)

The coherent β″ precipitates obstruct dislocation motion, raising yield strength to ~**275 MPa** in 6061-T6.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Alloy 6061 As Quenched Soft] --> A2[Solution at 530 C] --> A3[Quench in Water] --> A4[SSSS - Reverted Soft] --> A5[Age at 175 C 10 hr] --> A6[Peak Strength T6]
```

(7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style]**

**(a) Explain why aluminium alloys are preferred for shipbuilding despite their higher initial cost. List any four marine applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Although aluminium alloys have a higher initial cost per kg than mild steel, they are preferred in shipbuilding due to the following long-term and operational benefits:

1. **High specific strength (Strength/Weight ratio):** Specific strength of 5083-H116 is ~2× that of mild steel. (covered in derivation above)
2. **Weight reduction (~65%):** Lower displacement → higher speed, lower fuel consumption, increased payload.
3. **Excellent corrosion resistance:** Al forms a self-healing Al₂O₃ passive film; corrosion rate in seawater is "Outstanding" (< 0.025 mm/yr).
4. **Non-magnetic:** Critical for naval mine countermeasure vessels (MCMVs) and degaussing.
5. **High thermal conductivity (167 W/m·K):** Useful for heat exchangers and reduces fire propagation.
6. **Ease of fabrication:** Extrudable into complex shapes; readily weldable with MIG/TIG.
7. **Reflectivity:** Al reflects ~80% of incident radiation → cooler compartments in tropical waters.

**Four Marine Applications:**
1. Hull plates of high-speed patrol boats (5083-H116)
2. Superstructure / wheelhouse (6082-T6)
3. Masts and king posts (6061-T6)
4. LNG carrier cargo containment system (5083-O)
5. Helicopter decks on frigates
6. Propeller hubs and pump casings (A356-T6)

(7 marks)

**(b) Discuss the corrosion behaviour of aluminium alloys in seawater with reference to galvanic corrosion and pitting. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**General Corrosion:** Aluminium forms a thin, tenacious, and self-repairing **Al₂O₃** passive film (~4 nm thick). In clean seawater, this film protects the substrate, giving corrosion rates < 0.025 mm/yr (Outstanding per NACE).

**Pitting Corrosion:** In chloride-rich environments (seawater), localized breakdown of the passive film occurs at microscopic defects or intermetallic particles (e.g., Al₃Fe, Al-Mn). The result is **deep, narrow pits** which can perforate plates. **Mitigation:**
- Use **5xxx series** (which form β-Al₃Mg₂ precipitates that are anodic to the matrix, distributing attack broadly)
- Apply protective coatings (epoxy, polyurethane, anti-fouling)
- Use cathodic protection with **zinc or Al-Zn-In sacrificial anodes**

**Galvanic Corrosion:** Aluminium is **anodic** to most metals used in ships (steel, copper, brass, bronze, stainless steel) in the galvanic series. When dissimilar metals are coupled in seawater, **aluminium corrodes preferentially and severely**, with current density proportional to area ratio (cathode:anode).

**Example Calculation of Galvanic Risk:**
- A small bronze seacock ($A_c$) coupled to a large aluminium hull ($A_a$)
- The ratio $A_c/A_a$ is small → current density on bronze is high → moderate attack on bronze
- If reversed (small Al anode, large steel cathode), the **aluminium is rapidly destroyed**

**Prevention Methods:**
1. **Isolate dissimilar metals** with neoprene, rubber gaskets, or plastic bushings
2. **Use bi-metallic transition inserts** at hull/superstructure joints
3. **Sacrificial anodes** of zinc or Al-alloy (Al-Zn-In) to protect the hull
4. **Avoid copper-based alloys** in seawater lines if aluminium is nearby
5. **Use proper welding consumables** (e.g., 5356 or 5183 for 5xxx base)

(7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mark Loss Zone 1:** Students often confuse **2xxx and 7xxx** with marine alloys — both are explicitly *excluded* from ship hull service due to SCC susceptibility. Always specify the **5xxx and 6xxx** series in marine context.
> 2. **Mark Loss Zone 2:** Failing to mention the **temper designation** (H116 for 5083 hull plates, T6 for 6061 superstructures) costs 1–2 marks. KTU examiners look for "**5083-H116**," not just "5083."
> 3. **Mark Loss Zone 3:** When discussing corrosion, always mention **galvanic isolation** and **cathodic protection** — a common sub-question.
> 4. **Mark Loss Zone 4:** For heat treatment questions, name the **precipitate species** (e.g., β″ Mg-Si for 6xxx; θ′ Al₂Cu for 2xxx; η′ MgZn₂ for 7xxx) — generic "precipitation" is insufficient.
> 5. **Mark Loss Zone 5:** Forget to convert units in the corrosion rate formula → marks lost. Always show the final value in **mm/yr** explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Recall for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- ⭐ **Density of Al-alloy:** **2.66 – 2.71 g/cm³** (≈ 1/3 of steel)
- ⭐ **Specific strength of 5083-H116:** ~**2× that of mild steel**
- ⭐ **Primary marine series:** **5xxx (Al-Mg, non-heat-treatable)** and **6xxx (Al-Mg-Si, heat-treatable)**
- ⭐ **Most widely used marine alloy:** **5083-H116** (hull plates)
- ⭐ **Marine superstructure alloy:** **6082-T6** (European) / **6061-T6** (American)
- ⭐ **Temper for hulls:** H116 / H321 (strain hardened, Mg-stabilized)
- ⭐ **Temper for extruded superstructures:** T6 (solution + artificial aging)
- ⭐ **Age-hardening peak:** Coherent **β″ Mg-Si** precipitates in 6xxx alloys
- ⭐ **Solution treatment temp for 6xxx:** **525 – 535 °C**
- ⭐ **Aging temp for T6:** **160 – 180 °C** for 8 – 12 hours
- ⭐ **Galvanic position:** Aluminium is **ANODIC** to steel, copper, stainless steel
- ⭐ **Pitting in seawater:** Driven by Cl⁻ ions; mitigated by coatings + cathodic protection
- ⭐ **Filler wire for 5xxx:** **5356 or 5183**; for 6xxx: **4043**
- ⭐ **Cryogenic marine (LNG):** **5083-O** retains toughness down to **-270 °C**
- ⭐ **Common cast alloy:** **A356.0-T6** (propeller hubs, valve bodies)
- ⭐ **Standard:** ASTM B928, EN 13195 (marine plate), ISO 6361 (wrought products)
- ⭐ **Two KTU-mandated corrosion prevention methods:** **(1) Cathodic protection with Zn/Al anodes**, **(2) Galvanic isolation using gaskets and transition joints**
- ⭐ **Hall-Petch relationship:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ — finer grain = higher strength
- ⭐ **Over-aging (T7):** Strength ↓, **SCC resistance ↑** — important for thick extrusions
- ⭐ **Aluminium's Young's Modulus:** **70 GPa** (≈ 1/3 of steel → 200 GPa) — higher deflection in design
- ⭐ **Thermal expansion:** $\alpha_{Al} \approx 23 \times 10^{-6}/°C$ → 2× that of steel → joint design must accommodate

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alloy designation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Alloy designation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

**Alloy Designation** is a standardised alphanumeric coding system used to identify and classify engineering alloys (steels, aluminium alloys, copper alloys, nickel alloys, etc.) based on their **chemical composition**, **mechanical properties**, **heat treatment condition (temper)**, and **intended application**. For shipbuilding, alloy designation systems are governed by bodies such as the **American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM)**, the **Unified Numbering System (UNS)**, the **Aluminum Association (AA)**, the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)**, and **Classification Societies** (Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, Bureau Veritas, IRS).

In marine engineering, an alloy designation functions as a "**metal passport**" — a single short code (e.g., **AISI 316L**, **AA 5083-H116**, **Cu-Ni 90/10**, **AH36**) that unambiguously communicates the alloy's identity to designers, shipyards, regulatory bodies, and welding inspectors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module 1, you are expected to master the designation systems relevant to **structural shipbuilding steels**, **marine-grade aluminium alloys**, **copper-nickel alloys (for piping and heat exchangers)**, and **nickel-aluminium bronzes (for propellers)**. Memorise both the *rule* and *one example* for every system.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Code" of Metals

Imagine each engineering alloy as a **citizen** of a country. The alloy designation is the **postal address**:
- The **first digits** are the **state** (family of alloys).
- The **middle digits** are the **district** (alloying additions and purity).
- The **trailing letters/numbers** are the **house number** (carbon content or temper condition).

Just as postal codes tell a postman where a letter must go, the alloy designation tells a naval architect, a welder, a classification surveyor, and a procurement officer precisely *which* metal must be used for *which* part of the ship. Misreading **"316"** instead of **"304"** stainless steel could cost a shipyard millions in premature corrosion failures of a seawater cooling line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Engineering Fact:** An alloy designation is **not a trade name** (like *Monel* or *Inconel*). It is a *standardised code* issued by a recognised body so that any supplier worldwide can produce a chemically and mechanically equivalent product.

### The Five Major Designation Families Encountered in Shipbuilding

| # | Designation System | Governing Body | Material Covered | Marine Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **AISI-SAE** (4-digit) | AISI / SAE | Carbon & alloy steels | Shafts, gears, fasteners |
| 2 | **ASTM / UNS** | ASTM | Universal — all metals | Plates, pipes, castings |
| 3 | **AA (Aluminum Association)** | The Aluminum Association | Wrought & cast aluminium | Superstructures, fast craft, LNG carriers |
| 4 | **ISO / EN** (e.g., EN 10025) | ISO / CEN | European structural steels | European-built hulls |
| 5 | **Classification Society Grades** (A, B, D, E, AH, DH, EH, FH) | IACS members | Hot-rolled ship plate | Hull plating, decks, bulkheads |

The physical **constants** to remember are:
- **1 MPa = 1 N/mm² ≈ 0.102 kgf/mm²**
- **1 ksi = 6.895 MPa**
- For ship plate grades **"32" → 315 MPa yield**, **"36" → 355 MPa yield** (these are the two common strength levels).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 AISI–SAE Four-Digit Steel Designation System

This is the foundational system for **carbon** and **alloy steels**. The code has the form:

$$\text{Steel Grade} = XYZZ$$

where each digit carries meaning:

- **X (1st digit) — Major alloying family**

| Digit | Alloy Family |
|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Carbon steel (plain) |
| 2 | Nickel steel |
| 3 | Nickel–chromium steel |
| 4 | Molybdenum steel |
| 5 | Chromium steel |
| 6 | Chromium–vanadium steel |
| 7 | Tungsten steel |
| 8 | Nickel–chromium–molybdenum steel |
| 9 | Silicon–manganese steel |

- **Y (2nd digit) — Modification indicator** — denotes a **secondary alloying element** or a **modification of the standard composition** within that family. A "0" means the *basic* alloy of the family; any non-zero digit indicates a variation in the percentages.
- **ZZ (last two digits) — Nominal carbon content in hundredths of one percent (wt % C × 100)**.

**Worked example:** AISI **4340**
- **4** → Molybdenum steel family.
- **3** → Modification "3" (typically indicates the presence of nickel as a secondary alloying element → Ni–Cr–Mo).
- **40** → 0.40 wt % carbon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine application example:** AISI 316L stainless steel → **3** = Ni–Cr family, **1** = second modification (≈ 2–3 % Mo added for chloride resistance), **6** ≈ 0.06 % C; the trailing **"L"** denotes *Low carbon* (≤ 0.03 % C) to prevent sensitisation and intergranular corrosion in welded shipboard piping.

### 2.2 ASTM / Unified Numbering System (UNS)

The UNS prefix is a single letter that designates the metal family:

| UNS Prefix | Metal Family | Example |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **F** | Cast irons | F10006 |
| **G** | Carbon and alloy steels | G43400 |
| **S** | Stainless & heat-resisting steels | S31603 (316L) |
| **N** | Nickel & nickel alloys | N04400 (Monel 400) |
| **C** | Copper & copper alloys | C70600 (Cu-Ni 90/10) |
| **A** | Aluminium & aluminium alloys | A95083 (AA 5083) |
| **T** | Tool steels | T30403 |
| **J** | Cast steels | J03002 |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick conversion trick:** The UNS code for a given AISI/AA/CDA alloy is obtained by inserting a family letter and adjusting the digit count. E.g., AISI 4340 → UNS **G**43400 (5 digits); AISI 316L → UNS **S**31603.

### 2.3 Aluminium Association (AA) Designation System — *Critical for Naval Architects*

Wrought aluminium alloys are designated by a **four-digit number**:

$$\text{AA Grade} = \text{ABxx}$$

- **A (1st digit) — Principal alloying element**

| Series | Principal Alloying Element |
|:---:|:---|
| 1xxx | ≥ 99.00 % pure Al (≥ 99.0 % aluminium) |
| 2xxx | Aluminium + **Copper** (Al-Cu) |
| 3xxx | Aluminium + **Manganese** (Al-Mn) |
| 4xxx | Aluminium + **Silicon** (Al-Si) |
| 5xxx | Aluminium + **Magnesium** (Al-Mg) |
| 6xxx | Aluminium + **Magnesium + Silicon** (Al-Mg-Si) |
| 7xxx | Aluminium + **Zinc** (Al-Zn) |
| 8xxx | Other elements (e.g., Fe, Li) |

- **B (2nd digit) — Modification** — "0" = original alloy; 1–9 = successive modifications.
- **xx (last two digits) — Within-series identifier** — for 1xxx, the last two digits give the aluminium purity **above 99 %** to two decimal places; e.g., AA 1050 = 99.50 % Al minimum.

**Temper Designation (Suffix)** — this is **as important as the alloy number** in marine applications:

| Temper | Meaning |
|:---:|:---|
| **O** | Annealed (soft) |
| **H** | Strain-hardened (e.g., H116, H321 — both relevant for marine 5xxx) |
| **T** | Thermally treated (solution + age). Sub-codes T1, T4, T6 are most common. |
| **F** | As-fabricated (no guarantees) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine critical temper rule:** For 5xxx series alloys used in ship hulls (AA 5083, 5086, 5456), the temper must be **H116** or **H321** — these tempers guarantee resistance to **SCC (Stress Corrosion Cracking)** in marine service. A plain H112 or H32 temper is **not acceptable** for welded hull plating under classification society rules.

### 2.4 Copper Alloy Designation (UNS C-series)

- **C10000–C79999** → wrought coppers, brasses, bronzes.
- **C70000–C72999** → **Copper–Nickel** alloys (highly marine-relevant).
- **C60800–C64200** → **Aluminium bronzes**.
- **C9xxxx** → special tin bronzes & gun metals.

The CDA (Copper Development Association) name uses the **nominal composition** in percent. E.g., **"Cu-Ni 90/10"** = UNS **C70600** = 90 % Cu, 10 % Ni, with small Fe and Mn additions for corrosion resistance in seawater.

### 2.5 Classification Society Ship-Plate Grades (IACS Unified Requirements)

| Grade | Yield Strength (min) | Tensile Strength (min) | Typical Application |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **A**, **B**, **D**, **E** | 235 MPa | 400–520 MPa | Normal-strength hull plate |
| **AH32 / DH32 / EH32 / FH32** | 315 MPa | 440–590 MPa | Higher-strength hull plate (32 kg class) |
| **AH36 / DH36 / EH36 / FH36** | 355 MPa | 490–620 MPa | Higher-strength hull plate (36 kg class) |
| **AH40 / DH40 / EH40 / FH40** | 390 MPa | 510–660 MPa | Ultra-high strength (40 kg class, modern) |

- The **letter before the number** indicates the **impact-test temperature**:
  - **A** = impact test at **+20 °C** (room temperature).
  - **B** = impact test at **0 °C** (rare in IACS; "B" grade itself = 0 °C).
  - **D** = impact test at **−20 °C** (cold-service hulls).
  - **E** = impact test at **−40 °C** (Arctic, ice-class).
  - **F** = impact test at **−60 °C** (Polar Class, icebreakers).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Rule | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Carbon content from last 2 AISI digits | $C(\%) = \dfrac{ZZ}{100}$ | wt % (for plain-carbon steels) |
| Yield strength from "32" / "36" grade | $\sigma_y$ | **32 → 315 MPa**, **36 → 355 MPa** |
| Tensile strength (typical) | $\sigma_u \approx 1.5\,\sigma_y$ | MPa, empirical for ship plate |
| UNS carbon-steel prefix | G + AISI digits → 5-digit UNS | e.g., 1020 → **G**10200 |
| UNS stainless-steel prefix | S + AISI digits → 5-digit UNS | e.g., 316L → **S31603** |
| Aluminium purity from 1xxx series | $P_{Al} = 99 + \dfrac{xx}{100}$ | %, for 1xxx alloys only |
| Marine temper rule (5xxx) | H116 **or** H321 | mandatory for welded hull |
| Cu-Ni alloy composition (C70600) | Cu 90 %, Ni 10 %, + Fe / Mn | 90/10 designation |
| NAB propeller composition | Cu + Al + Ni + Fe + Mn | UNS C95800 (≈ 9 % Al, 4.5 % Ni) |
| Impact temperature (A/B/D/E/F) | +20, 0, −20, −40, −60 °C | IACS standard |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Understanding these designations allows a naval architect to **select materials by code from any shipyard in the world** — a Korean-built VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier) using **AH36** is metallurgically equivalent to a European-built AH36 hull plate. This interchangeability is the bedrock of global shipbuilding supply chains.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Extracting Carbon Content from an AISI-SAE Steel Designation

**Problem context.** A marine propeller-shaft forging is specified as **AISI 1045**. Find the nominal carbon content in wt % and classify the steel as low-, medium-, or high-carbon.

**Step 1 — Identify the four digits.**

$$\text{Grade} = 1\,0\,45$$

- First digit = **1** → **plain carbon steel** family.
- Second digit = **0** → **base** composition of the family (no nickel or other major modification).
- Last two digits = **45** → encoded carbon content.

**Step 2 — Decode the carbon content using the rule.**

The AISI–SAE rule states:

$$C_{nom}\,(wt\%) = \frac{\text{Last two digits}}{100}$$

Substituting the value:

$$C_{nom} = \frac{45}{100} = 0.45\,wt\%$$

**Step 3 — Classify the carbon steel.**

The classification bands used in marine engineering are:

$$C \le 0.25\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Low-carbon steel}$$

$$0.25\% < C \le 0.60\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Medium-carbon steel}$$

$$C > 0.60\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{High-carbon steel}$$

Since $0.25 < 0.45 \le 0.60$:

$$\boxed{\,\text{AISI 1045 is a medium-carbon steel with 0.45 wt\% C.}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Marine engineering interpretation.**

Medium-carbon steel of 0.45 wt % C can be **through-hardened by quenching and tempering** to a typical hardness of **HRc 40–50**, giving high fatigue and wear resistance — perfect for a **propeller shaft** carrying a large marine propeller. The shaft is *not* welded; it is forged and machined, so the higher carbon content does not cause weld-related cracking.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Extracting Aluminium Purity from a 1xxx-Series Designation

**Problem context.** A non-structural marine component is specified as **AA 1050-H14**. Find the minimum aluminium purity, classify the alloy, and explain the temper.

**Step 1 — Identify the series.**

The first digit is **1** → 1xxx series → **commercially pure aluminium**.

**Step 2 — Apply the purity rule for 1xxx series.**

For the 1xxx series, the last two digits represent the aluminium purity **above 99 %**, to two decimal places:

$$P_{Al,min} = 99.00\% + \frac{xx}{100}$$

Substituting $xx = 50$:

$$P_{Al,min} = 99.00\% + \frac{50}{100} = 99.00\% + 0.50\% = 99.50\%$$

**Step 3 — Classify the temper "H14".**

The "H" family means strain-hardened. The second digit indicates the **degree of strain-hardening** (½-H, ¼-H, ¾-H, full-H, etc.), and the third digit indicates a **special condition**.

The "H1x" sub-group means **strain-hardened only** (no subsequent thermal treatment). The numeral **4** in the units place indicates **¾ hard**.

$$\boxed{\,\text{AA 1050-H14} = 99.50\% \text{ Al, strain-hardened to three-quarter hard.}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Marine interpretation.**

AA 1050 is **not used for hull plating** (too soft, low strength). It is used for **non-critical marine trim, signboards, heat shields, nameplates, and freshwater tanks** aboard a ship. The H14 temper gives moderate strength (UTS ≈ 110–130 MPa) and good formability for bending.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Identifying a Ship-Plate Steel from Yield Strength and Impact Temperature

**Problem context.** A shipyard receives a steel plate certified to a minimum yield strength of **315 MPa** and a Charpy V-notch impact test temperature of **−40 °C**. Decode its IACS grade.

**Step 1 — Map yield strength to the numeric part.**

The numeric part of the IACS grade is keyed to the yield-strength class:

$$\sigma_y = 315\ \text{MPa} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Numeric} = 32$$

$$\sigma_y = 355\ \text{MPa} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Numeric} = 36$$

$$\sigma_y = 390\ \text{MPa} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Numeric} = 40$$

So the numeric is **32**.

**Step 2 — Map the impact-test temperature to the prefix letter.**

Using the IACS standard test temperatures:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{test} = +20\,°C &\;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Prefix} = A \\
T_{test} = \phantom{+}0\,°C &\;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Prefix} = B \\
T_{test} = -20\,°C &\;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Prefix} = D \\
T_{test} = -40\,°C &\;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Prefix} = E \\
T_{test} = -60\,°C &\;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Prefix} = F
\end{aligned}$$

For $T_{test} = -40\,°C$, the prefix is **E**.

**Step 3 — Assemble the grade.**

$$\boxed{\,\text{Grade} = \text{E32}\,}$$

Equivalently, written as **EH32** if we add the H (high-strength) suffix used by some societies. This steel is specified for **Arctic-class hull plating and ice-strengthened bottoms**.

**Step 4 — Numerical sanity check.**

The minimum Charpy impact energy required by IACS for **E-class** 32-kg plate is typically **27 J at −40 °C** (longitudinal). IACS UR S6 / S7 must be cross-referenced for the exact acceptance value.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Selecting the Right Alloy Designation for a Given Shipboard Component

| Shipboard Component | Preferred Designation | Reason |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Hull shell plate (tanker, warm waters) | **AH36** | 355 MPa yield, +20 °C impact adequate |
| Hull shell plate (Arctic tanker / LNG) | **EH36** or **FH36** | High yield + low-T impact toughness |
| Propeller (large vessel) | **Ni-Al Bronze**, UNS **C95800** cast | Cavitation + corrosion resistance in seawater |
| Propeller shaft | **AISI 1045 / 4140** forged | High fatigue strength, not welded |
| Seawater cooling pipe | **Cu-Ni 90/10**, UNS **C70600** | Excellent seawater corrosion resistance |
| Condenser tubes | **Cu-Ni 70/30**, UNS **C71500** | Higher strength, better impingement resistance |
| Superstructure (fast patrol craft) | **AA 5083-H116** | Light weight, weldable, marine-grade temper |
| LNG cargo tank membrane | **AA 5083-O** annealed | Cryogenic ductility at −163 °C |
| Rudder, stem, brackets | **AH36** or **DH36** cast / forged | High yield, good weldability |
| Shipboard fasteners (bolts) | **AISI 316 / 316L** | Stainless, chloride-resistant |
| Mooring chains | **Grade R3 / R4** (IACS UR W22) | Specific chain-steel grades with impact tested at 0 °C |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam tip (KTU):** When asked *"Which alloy is suitable for the hull of an LNG carrier and why?"* — your answer must mention **AA 5083-O** and the reason: the **O (annealed) temper** retains high ductility and toughness at LNG temperatures of **−163 °C**, whereas the H116 temper would become brittle.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**
*Decode the following shipbuilding alloy designation: **AA 5083-H116**. State the alloy family, principal alloying element, aluminium purity (if applicable), the temper condition, and explain why this specific temper is mandatory for welded marine hulls.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Alloy family / series:** 5xxx — Aluminium–Magnesium series. (1 mark)
- **Principal alloying element:** Magnesium (typically 4.0–4.9 wt %), with Mn and Cr additions. (0.5 mark)
- **Aluminium purity:** Not applicable (1xxx purity rule applies only to series 1xxx). (0.5 mark)
- **Temper H116:** A special strain-hardened temper with mechanical-property limits and **guaranteed resistance to Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** in marine service. (0.5 mark)
- **Why mandatory:** Classification societies (LR, DNV, ABS) require either H116 or H321 for welded 5xxx-series hull plates because standard tempers such as H112 can suffer SCC in the heat-affected zone after welding, leading to catastrophic hull failure. (0.5 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**
*An IACS-grade ship plate is specified as **EH36**. State (i) its minimum yield strength, (ii) the Charpy V-notch impact test temperature, and (iii) one typical marine application.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- (i) Minimum yield strength = **355 MPa** (the "36" indicates the 36 kg/mm² class). (1 mark)
- (ii) Charpy V-notch impact test temperature = **−40 °C** (the "E" prefix). (1 mark)
- (iii) Typical marine application: **Arctic-class hull plating, ice-strengthened bottom plates, and structural members of ice-class tankers and LNG carriers.** (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) — *Marine Steel Designation Systems*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, 14-Mark Question, Sub-part Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **IACS unified grading system for ship-plate steels** with reference to the letters **A, B, D, E, F** and the numbers **32, 36, 40**. Draw a clearly labelled table showing the yield strength and the impact-test temperature for each grade, and provide **two real examples** of grades used in (i) a warm-water bulk carrier hull, and (ii) a Polar-class icebreaker hull. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A marine shaft is specified as **AISI 4140**. Decode this designation fully, state the **nominal carbon content**, the **alloy family**, and explain why this steel is preferred for a propeller shaft (which is not welded) rather than a hull plate (which is welded). Justify your answer with reference to weldability and mechanical performance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) IACS grading system (7 marks):**
- The IACS (International Association of Classification Societies) unified grade for ship plate has the form **<Letter><Number>** (sometimes followed by H for "high-strength").
- The **letter** indicates the **Charpy V-notch impact test temperature**:
  - A → +20 °C, B → 0 °C, D → −20 °C, E → −40 °C, F → −60 °C. (1 mark)
- The **number** indicates the **yield-strength class** (in kgf/mm², equivalent to ×9.81 MPa):
  - No number = 235 MPa (normal strength).
  - 32 = 315 MPa, 36 = 355 MPa, 40 = 390 MPa. (1 mark)
- A clean labelled table — students should reproduce a table similar to the IACS table in §2.5 above. (2 marks)
- **Example for warm-water bulk carrier hull:** **AH36** (high strength 36, tested at +20 °C). (1 mark)
- **Example for Polar-class icebreaker hull:** **FH36** or **FH40** (high strength 36/40, tested at −60 °C). (1 mark)
- A schematic / explanation note that classification societies (LR, DNV, ABS, BV, Class NK, KR) all subscribe to IACS UR S6/S7 and use the same grading. (1 mark)

**(b) AISI 4140 decoding (7 marks):**
- **4** → Molybdenum steel family. (1 mark)
- **1** → Modification "1" — the steel contains chromium and additional alloying variations of the base 4xxx family. (1 mark)
- **40** → Carbon content = **0.40 wt %**. (1 mark)
- **UNS equivalent:** G41400. (0.5 mark)
- **Alloy family:** Cr–Mo low-alloy steel with typical composition 0.40 C, 1.0 Cr, 0.2 Mo. (1 mark)
- **Mechanical performance for a propeller shaft:** 4140 can be **quenched and tempered** to a tensile strength of 850–1000 MPa and yield of 650–800 MPa, with excellent fatigue and wear resistance. As a **medium-carbon low-alloy steel** it gives the high torque-carrying capacity required for a marine shaft. (1 mark)
- **Weldability reasoning:** Medium-carbon 4140 has a **carbon equivalent (CE) > 0.45**, making it prone to **hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC)** in the weld HAZ. Hull plates must be **weldable**, so they are **low-carbon (CE ≤ 0.40)**, e.g., AH36 with C ≤ 0.18 %. The shaft is **not welded** (it is forged and joined by flanges and fitted bolts), so the carbon content is not a welding concern. (1.5 marks)

---

#### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — *Aluminium and Copper Alloys in Marine Use*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, 14-Mark Question, Sub-part Choice]**

**(a)** With the aid of a **clearly labelled four-digit AA code decoder**, describe how the **Aluminum Association** classifies wrought aluminium alloys. Highlight the series that is **most commonly used for ship hulls** and **justify** the choice of temper with reference to **stress-corrosion-cracking resistance in welded marine service**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A seawater cooling system on a tanker is to be built from a **copper–nickel alloy**. Compare **Cu-Ni 90/10 (UNS C70600)** and **Cu-Ni 70/30 (UNS C71500)** in terms of (i) composition, (ii) mechanical strength, (iii) corrosion behaviour in seawater, and (iv) typical application. Recommend one grade for a **main seawater cooling line** and justify your choice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) AA designation (7 marks):**
- The four-digit code is **ABxx** where A = series, B = modification, xx = within-series number. (1 mark)
- Series meaning: 1xxx pure Al, 2xxx Al-Cu, 3xxx Al-Mn, 4xxx Al-Si, 5xxx Al-Mg, 6xxx Al-Mg-Si, 7xxx Al-Zn, 8xxx others. (2 marks)
- A clean labelled block diagram of the decoder (students should reproduce the diagram in §4.2). (1 mark)
- **Most common for ship hulls: AA 5083 (5xxx series, Al-Mg).** Reason: high strength (UTS ≈ 275–350 MPa after H116), excellent weldability, and good corrosion resistance in seawater. (1 mark)
- **Temper justification:** The marine grades **H116** and **H321** are mandatory because the stabilised Mg-content and controlled processing routes guarantee **SCC resistance in the HAZ of welded joints**. A standard temper (H112) may have Mg in unstable solid solution and fail by SCC after welding. (1 mark)
- One extra credit fact: For **LNG membrane tanks** the same alloy is supplied in the **O (annealed)** temper because the annealed structure retains ductility at cryogenic temperatures (−163 °C). (1 mark)

**(b) Cu-Ni 90/10 vs 70/30 (7 marks):**
- (i) **Composition:** 90/10 = 90 % Cu, 10 % Ni, + 1–2 % Fe, + 1 % Mn; 70/30 = 70 % Cu, 30 % Ni, + 0.5 % Fe, + 0.5 % Mn. (1 mark)
- (ii) **Mechanical strength:** 90/10 has UTS ≈ 310 MPa, yield ≈ 110 MPa; 70/30 has UTS ≈ 380 MPa, yield ≈ 130 MPa. The 70/30 is **stronger**. (1 mark)
- (iii) **Corrosion in seawater:** Both form a protective **Cu₂O / NiO surface film**; 90/10 is adequate for most shipboard seawater lines, 70/30 offers **superior resistance to impingement attack, erosion, and biofouling** at higher velocities (> 2.5 m/s). (1.5 marks)
- (iv) **Typical application:** 90/10 → ship hulls, seawater cooling lines, fire-fighting systems; 70/30 → **condenser tubes, heat-exchanger tubes, and high-velocity seawater service**. (1 mark)
- **Recommendation for the main seawater cooling line of a tanker:** **Cu-Ni 90/10 (C70600).** Justification: lower cost, adequate corrosion resistance at the typical flow velocities in a tanker cooling system, easier to weld and bend during shipboard installation, and meets IACS / classification society requirements. (2 marks)
- A small caveat: 70/30 is selected only when flow velocity or impingement conditions demand it (e.g., at pump discharges, tube inlets). (0.5 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse the "**B**" in 5xxx (modification) with the "B" in IACS grade (impact test at 0 °C). The same letter has different meanings in different systems.
> 2. **Do not** say *"AISI 316L is a low-carbon steel"* in a generic sense — always say *"AISI 316L is a low-carbon **austenitic stainless** steel"*; otherwise the examiner will deduct marks.
> 3. **Do not** write "AH36 has 36 kg/cm² yield strength." The correct unit is **kgf/mm² = N/mm² = MPa**; 36 kgf/mm² = **355 MPa**.
> 4. **Do not** recommend AA 5083-H112 or H32 for welded hull plating — it will be marked **wrong** because these tempers are not in the classification-society-approved list.
> 5. **Do not** confuse **monel** (Ni-Cu alloy, UNS N04400) with **Cu-Ni 90/10** (UNS C70600). Monel is the *opposite* ratio — mostly nickel with copper — and is not the same as cupronickel.
> 6. **For Part B (14-mark) questions, students frequently score only 8–10 marks** because they give textbook definitions but **fail to give a marine application or to justify the choice**. Always end with a *"…therefore this alloy is preferred for [component] in [service]."* sentence.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AISI–SAE 4-digit code:** First digit = alloy family, second digit = modification, last two digits = carbon in 0.01 % units. Example: **4340 = 0.40 % C, Ni-Cr-Mo low-alloy steel**.
- **UNS prefix letters** to remember cold: **F = cast iron, G = carbon steel, S = stainless, N = nickel, C = copper, A = aluminium, T = tool steel.**
- **Aluminium Association (AA) 4-digit code:** First digit = principal alloying element series (1xxx to 8xxx); 1xxx purity = 99 % + (xx/100).
- **5xxx (Al-Mg) is the marine workhorse**; temper must be **H116 or H321** for welded hull plating.
- **2xxx (Al-Cu) is NOT recommended for marine use** — susceptible to SCC in saltwater.
- **IACS ship-plate grade letter** = Charpy test temperature (A +20, B 0, D −20, E −40, F −60 °C).
- **IACS ship-plate grade number** = yield strength class (32 → 315 MPa, 36 → 355 MPa, 40 → 390 MPa).
- **Cu-Ni 90/10 (C70600)** is the **standard marine copper alloy** for seawater piping, hull sheathing, and heat exchangers.
- **Cu-Ni 70/30 (C71500)** is the **high-performance choice** for condenser tubes and high-velocity seawater.
- **Ni-Al Bronze (UNS C95800)** is the **standard propeller material** for merchant ships.
- **AISI 316L stainless steel** = UNS S31603 = the marine-grade austenitic stainless for piping, fasteners, and trim.
- **AA 5083-O** (annealed) is used in **LNG cryogenic membrane tanks** for ductility at −163 °C.
- **Carbon equivalent (CE)** of ship plate is kept ≤ 0.40 for weldability; propeller shafts are not welded, so higher CE is acceptable.
- **Always end an alloy-designation question** with a *justification sentence* linking the grade to its marine service — that single sentence is often worth 2–3 marks.
- **The five IACS members most relevant to KTU study:** Lloyd's Register (LR), DNV (Norway), ABS (USA), Bureau Veritas (BV, France), Class NK (Japan). All follow the same unified grade rules.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Welding requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **shipbuilding**, **Welding Requirements** refer to the comprehensive set of **mechanical, metallurgical, geometrical, and quality-related specifications** that welded joints must satisfy to ensure the structural integrity, seaworthiness, fatigue life, and safety of a vessel. These requirements are dictated by **Classification Societies** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, Bureau Veritas, Indian Register of Shipping — IRS) and standards such as **IACS Unified Requirements (UR W series)**, **AWS D1.1**, **EN 1090**, and **ISO 3834**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Welding requirements in shipbuilding encompass the specification of base metal grades, filler metal selection, joint design, heat input control, preheat/interpass temperature, post-weld heat treatment, non-destructive testing (NDT), and mechanical property verification (tensile strength, yield strength, elongation, and Charpy V-notch impact toughness) that a weldment must satisfy to be classified as fit for marine service.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tailor's Stitching" Analogy

Imagine you are stitching two pieces of a heavy winter coat. The thread must be **strong**, the **stitches close enough** to prevent tearing, the **needle must be hot enough** to pierce thick cloth without fraying, and the seam must **not unravel** in cold weather. Now magnify this 10,000 times: the cloth becomes a **20 mm steel plate**, the thread is a **welding electrode**, and the seam is called a **weld joint** on a ship's hull.

Just as a tailor checks thread strength, stitch density, and seam neatness, a shipyard welding engineer must check:

| Tailor's Check | Shipbuilder's Welding Check |
|----------------|---------------------------|
| Thread tensile strength | Weld metal **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** |
| Stitch neatness | **Weld bead profile** & under-cut limits |
| Cold-weather durability | **Charpy V-notch toughness** at –20°C or –40°C |
| Cloth doesn't tear near seam | **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** properties |
| Whole garment fit | **Dimensional accuracy** of fabricated hull block |
| Stitch spacing | **Pitch of weld passes** & weld leg length |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Ship Welding:** *"The weld must be at least as strong, as tough, and as corrosion-resistant as the base metal it joins."* A ship is only as strong as its **weakest weld**.

## 1.3 Why Shipbuilding Welding is Special

A modern merchant ship may contain **hundreds of kilometres of welds**. The welding requirements are unusually strict because:

1. **Dynamic Loading** — The hull is subjected to cyclic wave loading, vibration, and slamming forces.
2. **Brittle Fracture Risk** — Ships sail in cold seas (Arctic, North Atlantic) where low-temperature embrittlement can cause catastrophic failure (e.g., the Liberty ship fractures of WWII).
3. **Corrosive Environment** — Salt water, marine atmosphere, and bilge water demand weld metal resistant to corrosion.
4. **Welding Tonnage** — A VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier) requires over **4000 tonnes of weld metal** deposition.
5. **Regulatory Oversight** — Classification societies survey every critical weld through qualified NDT procedures.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constants Used in Welding Science (Memorize for KTU):**
> - **Arc Efficiency (η):** Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) → **0.75–0.85**, Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG) → **0.70–0.85**, Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) → **0.95–1.00**, Flux Cored Arc Welding (FCAW) → **0.80–0.90**.
> - **Specific Heat of Steel (c):** **0.49 kJ/(kg·K)** at 20°C.
> - **Thermal Conductivity of Steel (λ):** **45 W/(m·K)**.
> - **Density of Steel (ρ):** **7850 kg/m³**.
> - **Melting Point of Steel:** **~1500°C**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Shipbuilding Welding Requirements

### **Pillar 1 — Base Metal (Parent Plate) Requirements**

Ship plates are graded primarily by their **minimum yield strength** and **Charpy V-notch impact energy**. The International Association of Classification Societies (IACS) recognises common grades:

| Grade | Yield Strength (min) | Tensile Strength | Typical Use |
|-------|---------------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Grade A** | 235 MPa | 400–520 MPa | Hull shell (mild) |
| **Grade B** | 235 MPa | 400–520 MPa | Hull shell (improved) |
| **Grade D** | 235 MPa | 400–520 MPa | Cold service (–20°C) |
| **Grade E** | 235 MPa | 400–520 MPa | Cold service (–40°C) |
| **AH32 / DH32 / EH32** | 315 MPa | 440–590 MPa | Higher strength |
| **AH36 / DH36 / EH36** | 355 MPa | 490–620 MPa | Hatch coamings, sheerstrakes |
| **AH40 / DH40 / EH40** | 390 MPa | 510–660 MPa | Heavy sections |

> **Notation Convention:** A = Normal, B = Improved, D = –20°C, E = –40°C, F = –60°C. The digit (32, 36, 40) indicates the **minimum yield strength in ksi × 10** divided by 10... actually: it represents **minimum yield strength in N/mm² × (1/10) for early systems; modernly it is the yield in ksi × 10**.

### **Pillar 2 — Weld Metal Mechanical Property Requirements**

The deposited weld metal must satisfy:

$$
\sigma_y \geq \sigma_{y,\text{base}}, \quad \sigma_u \geq \sigma_{u,\text{base}}, \quad \text{CVN} \geq 27 \text{ J at service temperature}
$$

> **The "27 J Rule":** IACS requires a minimum Charpy V-notch impact energy of **27 J** at the design temperature (typically 0°C, –20°C, or –40°C) for all welds in primary hull structure.

### **Pillar 3 — Geometrical & Dimensional Requirements**

- **Fillet weld leg length (z):** Specified per drawing; minimum **z ≥ 0.7 × plate thickness** (for tee joints).
- **Weld profile:** Convexity ≤ **+1.0 mm**, concavity not permitted.
- **Undercut:** ≤ **0.5 mm** deep for grade A plates, ≤ **0.3 mm** for higher grades.
- **Porosity:** Maximum **2.5% of weld area**; no single pore > **3 mm**.
- **Lack of penetration:** **Not permitted** in full-strength welds.

## 2.2 Heat Input — The Single Most Critical Welding Parameter

**Heat input (H)** is the **energy delivered per unit length of weld** and is the master control variable for shipbuilding welding.

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \times 10^{-3} \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = **arc thermal efficiency** (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
- $V$ = **arc voltage** in **Volts**
- $I$ = **welding current** in **Amperes**
- $S$ = **travel speed** in **mm/s**
- The factor $10^{-3}$ converts W·s/mm (= J/mm) to kJ/mm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Input Limits (per IACS UR W11 / DNV / LR):**
> - For **Grade A normal-strength** steel: H ≤ **4.0 kJ/mm**
> - For **AH/DH/EH 32 & 36** high-strength steel: H ≤ **3.5 kJ/mm** (sometimes 2.5 kJ/mm per AWS D1.1)
> - **Excessive heat input** → coarse grains in HAZ → reduced toughness.
> - **Insufficient heat input** → lack of fusion, slag inclusions.

## 2.3 Cooling Time (t8/5) — The "8/5 Rule"

The **cooling time between 800°C and 500°C** (t8/5) is the metallurgical fingerprint of a weld. It controls:
- **HAZ grain size** (lower t8/5 = finer grains = higher toughness)
- **Hardness** (lower t8/5 = higher hardness in carbon steels — risk of HAZ cracking)

For a **2D heat flow** model (thick plate, > 25 mm) per **Uwer-Höhne (1983)**:

$$
t_{8/5} = \frac{\eta \cdot U \cdot I}{v} \left[ \frac{1}{(500-T_0)} - \frac{1}{(800-T_0)} \right] \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi\lambda c\rho} \times 10^6
$$

For a **3D heat flow** model (thin plate, < 25 mm) per **Rineboldt & Degenkolbe**:

$$
t_{8/5} = \frac{690 \cdot H_n \cdot F_2}{(T_0 - 20)} \left[ \frac{1}{500-T_0} - \frac{1}{800-T_0} \right] \times 100
$$

Where:
- $H_n$ = **heat input in kJ/mm**
- $T_0$ = **interpass temperature in °C**
- $F_2$ = **3D correction factor** = $1.0$ for 25 mm plate, decreases for thicker plates
- $c \cdot \rho$ = volumetric specific heat ≈ $4.5 \times 10^6$ J/(m³·K)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for Exams:** The target $t_{8/5}$ for shipbuilding steels is typically **5 to 15 seconds** for plate thicknesses 15–30 mm.

## 2.4 Preheat Temperature Determination — The Ito-Bessyo & CE Method

The **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** quantifies weldability. The **Ito-Bessyo formula (PCM)** is the modern IACS-preferred method:

$$
P_{CM} = C + \frac{Si}{30} + \frac{Mn+Cu}{20} + \frac{Cr}{20} + \frac{Ni}{60} + \frac{Mo}{15} + \frac{V}{10} + 5B
$$

| $P_{CM}$ (%) | Required Preheat ($T_0$) |
|--------------|-------------------------|
| ≤ 0.20 | Room temperature (no preheat) |
| 0.20 – 0.25 | 50°C |
| 0.25 – 0.30 | 100°C |
| 0.30 – 0.35 | 150°C |
| > 0.35 | Special procedure qualification |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{S} \times 10^{-3}$ | Heat input | kJ/mm |
| 2 | $H_{\text{eff}} = H \times k_{\text{layer}}$ | Effective heat input (multi-layer) | kJ/mm |
| 3 | $t_{8/5} = \dfrac{6700 - Q(T_0 - 20)}{Q(500-T_0)^2}$ | Empirical t8/5 (Rineboldt) | s |
| 4 | $P_{CM} = C + \dfrac{Si}{30} + \dfrac{Mn+Cu}{20} + \dfrac{Cr}{20} + \dfrac{Ni}{60} + \dfrac{Mo}{15} + \dfrac{V}{10} + 5B$ | Carbon equivalent (PCM) | % |
| 5 | $H_{v,\text{HAZ}} = 802 + 293 P_{CM} + 75 P_{CM}^2 + 7.2 t_{8/5}$ | Vickers hardness in HAZ | HV |
| 6 | $CVN_{\min} = 27$ | IACS minimum Charpy | J |
| 7 | $R_p \geq R_{e,\text{base}}$ | Yield strength matching | MPa |
| 8 | $\sigma_{fatigue} = \dfrac{\Delta K}{Y\sqrt{\pi a}}$ | Fracture mechanics (Paris) | MPa√m |

> **Pro Tip:** For KTU, **memorize Formulas 1, 4, and 5**. Questions are almost always drawn from these three.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input from First Principles

### **Starting Point — Definition of Heat Input**

Heat input is defined as the **arc power delivered per unit length of weld**.

**Step 1 — Write down the arc power.**
The arc acts as an electrical load. The power dissipated in the arc is:

$$
P_{\text{arc}} = V \cdot I \quad [\text{Watts} = \text{Volts} \times \text{Amperes}]
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the thermal efficiency factor.**
Not all arc energy reaches the workpiece. A fraction $\eta$ (the **arc efficiency** or **heat transfer coefficient**) is actually transferred to the base metal:

$$
P_{\text{useful}} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \quad [\text{W}]
$$

**Step 3 — Convert power to energy per unit length.**
The torch moves at travel speed $S$ in **mm/s**. In one second, the arc covers $S$ mm of weld length. Therefore, the energy per mm is:

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \quad [\text{J/mm}]
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to standard unit (kJ/mm).**
Since 1 kJ = 1000 J:

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \times 10^{-3} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]
$$

This is the **standard shipbuilding heat input formula** used in all classification society codes.

---

### **Worked Numerical Example 1 (KTU Exam Style)**

A shipyard welds a Grade DH36 plate using **SAW** with the following parameters:
- Voltage $V$ = **30 V**
- Current $I$ = **600 A**
- Travel speed $S$ = **5 mm/s** (= 300 mm/min)
- Arc efficiency $\eta$ = **0.95** (SAW)

**Find:** Heat input and verify it is within IACS limits.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \times 10^{-3} \\
  &= \frac{0.95 \times 30 \times 600}{5} \times 10^{-3} \\
  &= \frac{17100}{5} \times 10^{-3} \\
  &= 3420 \times 10^{-3} \\
  &= 3.42 \text{ kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Check against IACS UR W11 limit:** For DH36, maximum H = **3.5 kJ/mm**. Since 3.42 < 3.5, the procedure is **acceptable**. 

> **Note on unit conversion:** If travel speed is given in **mm/min**, the formula changes to $H = \dfrac{\eta V I \times 60}{S_{\text{mm/min}}}$ because per minute, the arc traverses $S$ mm, and power $\times$ 60 s = energy per minute.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cooling Time t8/5 (3D Heat Flow)

The **Rineboldt–Degenkolbe 3D model** is derived by solving the **Rosenthal moving point source** equation for a thin plate and integrating heat capacity between 500°C and 800°C.

**Step 1 — Start with Rosenthal's 2D solution for a moving point source:**

$$
T - T_0 = \frac{Q}{2\pi\lambda} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{v(r+x)}{2a}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{r}
$$

Where $a$ = thermal diffusivity, $Q$ = heat input per unit time (W), $v$ = travel speed, $r$ = radial distance from heat source.

**Step 2 — Identify the cooling time as the time between T = 800°C and T = 500°C.** At a point just behind the arc on the centreline ($r \approx 0$, $x \to -\infty$ for cool zone), the cooling rate gives:

$$
t_{8/5} = \int_{500}^{800} \frac{dT}{dT/dt} = \text{(integration yields Rineboldt formula)}
$$

**Step 3 — Final 3D empirical form (Rineboldt-Degenkolbe, 1980):**

$$
t_{8/5} = \frac{690 \cdot H_n \cdot F_2}{T_0 - 20} \left[ \frac{1}{500-T_0} - \frac{1}{800-T_0} \right] \times 100
$$

Where:
- $H_n$ = heat input in kJ/mm
- $T_0$ = interpass temperature in °C
- $F_2$ = plate thickness factor (1.0 for 20 mm, 0.85 for 30 mm, 0.75 for 40 mm)

---

### **Worked Numerical Example 2 (KTU Exam Style)**

For a **25 mm thick EH36 plate**:
- Heat input $H_n$ = **2.5 kJ/mm**
- Interpass temperature $T_0$ = **150°C**
- Plate thickness factor $F_2$ = **0.9**

**Find:** $t_{8/5}$ and assess HAZ quality.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{8/5} &= \frac{690 \times 2.5 \times 0.9}{150 - 20} \left[ \frac{1}{500-150} - \frac{1}{800-150} \right] \times 100 \\
        &= \frac{1552.5}{130} \left[ \frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{650} \right] \times 100 \\
        &= 11.94 \times \left[ 0.002857 - 0.001538 \right] \times 100 \\
        &= 11.94 \times 0.001319 \times 100 \\
        &= 1.575 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assessment:** $t_{8/5} \approx 1.6$ s is **very fast cooling** — this produces **martensitic/bainitic HAZ** which is hard and brittle. The weld may have **HAZ hydrogen cracking** risk. **Recommendation:** Increase preheat to 200°C OR reduce H to 1.5 kJ/mm.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Carbon Equivalent (Pcm)

The **PCM (Parameter of Critical Metal)** is derived from the **Phase Transformation studies** of Ito and Bessyo (1968). They correlated **HAZ maximum hardness** with chemical composition of **low-carbon low-alloy steels**.

**Step 1 — Start with the hardenability concept.** The HAZ hardness increases linearly with each alloying element:

$$
H_{v,\text{HAZ}} \propto C + a\cdot Si + b\cdot Mn + c\cdot Cr + d\cdot Ni + e\cdot Mo + f\cdot V + g\cdot B
$$

**Step 2 — Multi-regression on hundreds of test data** gave the coefficients:

$$
P_{CM} = C + \frac{Si}{30} + \frac{Mn + Cu}{20} + \frac{Cr}{20} + \frac{Ni}{60} + \frac{Mo}{15} + \frac{V}{10} + 5B
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.** A $P_{CM}$ of 0.20 means the steel is "as weldable" as a 0.20% plain-carbon steel. Modern ship steels aim for $P_{CM} \leq 0.20\%$ for the easiest welding.

---

### **Worked Numerical Example 3 (KTU Exam Style)**

A DH36 plate has the following chemical analysis (in wt.%):
C = 0.14, Si = 0.30, Mn = 1.40, Cr = 0.10, Ni = 0.20, Mo = 0.05, Cu = 0.10, V = 0.005, B = 0.0005.

**Find:** $P_{CM}$ and recommend the preheat.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{CM} &= 0.14 + \frac{0.30}{30} + \frac{1.40+0.10}{20} + \frac{0.10}{20} + \frac{0.20}{60} + \frac{0.05}{15} + \frac{0.005}{10} + 5(0.0005) \\
       &= 0.14 + 0.010 + 0.075 + 0.005 + 0.00333 + 0.00333 + 0.0005 + 0.0025 \\
       &= 0.1397 + 0.010 + 0.075 + 0.005 + 0.00333 + 0.00333 + 0.0005 + 0.0025 \\
       &= 0.2397 \approx 0.24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommendation:** Since $0.20 < P_{CM} = 0.24 < 0.25$, the required preheat is **50°C minimum** (per IACS table).

> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting the factor **5B** in the PCM formula. Boron is a *very* potent hardenability element even at ppm levels, hence the multiplier of 5.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting Welding Parameters for Ship Steels

| Steel Grade | Plate Thickness (mm) | Process | H (kJ/mm) max | Preheat (°C) | t8/5 (s) | NDT |
|-------------|----------------------|---------|---------------|--------------|----------|-----|
| Grade A | ≤ 20 | SMAW | 4.0 | 20 (RT) | 4–12 | 100% Visual + 20% UT |
| Grade A | > 20 | SAW | 4.5 | 50 | 6–15 | Visual + UT |
| DH36 | 15–25 | FCAW | 3.5 | 75 | 5–12 | 100% Visual + 20% UT + 10% RT |
| EH36 | 20–30 | SAW | 3.0 | 100 | 8–14 | 100% UT (critical) |
| EH40 | 25–40 | SAW | 2.5 | 150 | 10–15 | 100% UT + 20% RT |
| EH40 (Arctic) | 30–50 | SAW + PWHT | 2.0 | 175 | 12–18 | 100% UT + 100% RT |

> [!NOTE]
> **NDT abbreviations:** UT = Ultrasonic Testing, RT = Radiographic Testing, MT = Magnetic Particle Testing, PT = Dye Penetrant Testing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
