# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: List any three wrought aluminium alloy series used in shipbuilding and state one typical marine application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The three principal wrought aluminium alloy series used in shipbuilding are:

1. **AA 5xxx series (Al–Mg alloys)** — Used for **hull plates and welded primary structures** of fast patrol boats, workboats, and LNG carrier secondary barriers. Example: 5083-H116.

2. **AA 6xxx series (Al–Mg–Si alloys)** — Used for **extruded superstructure profiles, masts, and deckhouses** where complex sections are required. Example: 6082-T6.

3. **AA 3xxx series (Al–Mn alloys)** — Used for **non-structural panels, fuel tanks, and interior fittings** in ships where moderate strength and excellent formability are needed. Example: 3003-H14.

> [!NOTE]
> **Allocation:** 1 mark per correct series + 1 mark per correct application (3 total). For full marks, mention the *reason* why each series suits the application.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Why is copper (Cu) content strictly limited to ≤ 0.1% in marine-grade aluminium alloys like 5083?**

**Model Answer:**

Copper is limited to **≤ 0.10%** in marine-grade 5xxx alloys (e.g., 5083, 5086) because:

- Cu forms the intermetallic phase **Al₂Cu (θ-phase)** which has a **highly cathodic open-circuit potential** of approximately **−0.55 V vs SCE** in seawater.
- The surrounding Al–Mg matrix is anodic at ≈ **−0.78 V vs SCE**.
- This large potential difference creates **intense micro-galvanic cells** at every Al₂Cu particle, leading to **severe localized pitting corrosion**.
- Even small amounts of Cu (a few hundred ppm) can dramatically reduce the **pitting resistance equivalent (PRE)** of the alloy.
- Hence, marine specifications such as **IACS UR W26 and ASTM B928** enforce a strict Cu limit to ensure long service life in seawater.

**Marks split:** 1 for identifying Al₂Cu, 1 for the galvanic effect, 1 for the consequences (pitting/standards reference).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 ESE Part B Module-Level Question — **Internal Choice Mandatory** (Choose ONE of A or B).

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model]`

**Q: Discuss in detail the composition, properties, advantages, and shipboard applications of AA 5083 alloy. Why is it considered the "workhorse" of marine aluminium?**

#### Part (a) — Composition, Metallurgical Phases, and Properties [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**1. Composition of AA 5083 (in wt%):**

| Element | Content | Role |
|---------|---------|------|
| Mg | **4.0 – 4.9%** | Solid solution strengthener; forms β-phase (Al₃Mg₂) |
| Mn | 0.40 – 1.0% | Grain refiner via Al₆Mn dispersoids; binds Fe impurities |
| Fe | ≤ 0.40% | Controlled impurity |
| Si | ≤ 0.40% | Controlled impurity |
| Cu | ≤ 0.10% | **Strictly limited** to prevent pitting |
| Zn | ≤ 0.25% | Trace — minimal effect |
| Cr | 0.05 – 0.25% | Inhibits recrystallization; reduces sensitization |
| Ti | ≤ 0.15% | Grain refiner during casting |
| Al | **Balance (~94.7%)** | Matrix |

**2. Key Metallurgical Phases:**

- **α-Al(Mg) matrix** — solid solution of Mg in FCC Al.
- **Al₃Mg₂ (β-phase)** — anodic intermetallic at grain boundaries.
- **Al₆(Mn,Fe)** — coarse insoluble dispersoids.
- **Al₇Cr** — fine dispersoids controlling grain size.

**3. Mechanical Properties (5083-H116, typical):**

- $\sigma_{y}$ (Yield) = **215 – 305 MPa**
- $\sigma_{u}$ (UTS) = **305 – 385 MPa**
- Elongation = **10 – 16%**
- Density = **2.70 g/cm³**
- E = **70 GPa**

**4. Corrosion Behaviour:**

- Corrosion rate in flowing seawater ≈ **< 25 µm/year**
- Excellent resistance to **pitting, crevice, and exfoliation corrosion** when in H116 or H321 temper.
- Self-passivating **Al₂O₃ film** of ~10 nm thickness re-forms within milliseconds of damage.

#### Part (b) — Advantages, Applications, and Justification as "Workhorse" [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**1. Advantages of AA 5083 in Marine Service:**

- **Highest strength among non-heat-treatable marine alloys** that retains ~70% strength in the weld heat-affected zone (HAZ).
- **Sensitization-resistant** up to 65 °C service because Mg is below the critical 3.5% threshold for continuous β-phase networks (although 5083 has 4.5% Mg, careful thermomechanical processing keeps β-phase discontinuous).
- **Excellent weldability** by GMAW (MIG) and GTAW (TIG) using 5183 or 5356 filler wires.
- **Cryogenic toughness** — Charpy impact strength actually *increases* as temperature drops to −196 °C → ideal for LNG carrier secondary barriers.
- **Non-magnetic** → used in mine countermeasure vessels (MCMVs) to avoid magnetic signature.
- **High recyclability** with no loss of mechanical properties.

**2. Shipboard Applications:**

- **Hull plating** of fast patrol boats, pilot vessels, and hydrofoils.
- **Hull plates of workboats and fishing vessels.**
- **Welded superstructure panels** and bulkheads.
- **LNG carrier secondary membranes and inner hull barriers.**
- **Helideck plating, mast structures, and bridge wings.**

**3. Why "Workhorse" Status:**

AA 5083 is the **default choice** for marine structural plate because it offers the **best balance** of strength, corrosion resistance, weldability, fracture toughness, and cost among all aluminium alloys. It is the only 5xxx alloy specified in virtually every classification society's rules (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, BV) for primary hull construction in aluminium ships. No other single alloy combines this breadth of approvals and operational track record across commercial, naval, and offshore vessels.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]`

**Q: Compare AA 5xxx and AA 6xxx series aluminium alloys with respect to (a) composition, strengthening mechanism, and (b) marine corrosion behaviour. State specific applications for each.**

#### Part (a) — Composition and Strengthening Mechanism [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | AA 5xxx (e.g., 5083) | AA 6xxx (e.g., 6061) |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Principal alloying elements** | Mg (2.4 – 5.5%) | Mg (0.6 – 1.2%) + Si (0.4 – 1.3%) |
| **Secondary elements** | Mn, Cr, Fe, Ti | Mn, Cr, Fe, Ti |
| **Critical impurity limit** | Cu ≤ 0.10% | Cu ≤ 0.15 – 0.40% |
| **Strengthening mechanism** | **Solid solution strengthening** + strain hardening (work hardening) | **Precipitation (age) hardening** via Mg₂Si phase |
| **Heat-treatable?** | **No** — strength developed by cold working (H-tempers) | **Yes** — T6 (solution heat-treat + artificial aging) |
| **Strength range (UTS)** | 215 – 385 MPa | 180 – 310 MPa |
| **Effect of welding on strength** | Loses ~30% in HAZ (e.g., from 305 → 215 MPa), but H116/H321 tempers re-stabilize it | Loses ~50% in HAZ (T6 properties destroyed) — welded joints must be re-heat-treated or designed for the annealed strength |
| **Weldability** | **Excellent** — filler 5183/5356 | **Good**, but with strength penalty |
| **Formability** | Good in O temper | Good in T4, less in T6 |
| **Extrudability** | Limited — used mostly as rolled plate | **Excellent** — the standard for extrusions |

**Key Conceptual Difference:**

The 5xxx series strengthens by **trapping Mg atoms in the Al lattice** (solution strengthening) and by **dislocation multiplication** (strain hardening). In contrast, the 6xxx series strengthens by **forming nanoscale Mg₂Si precipitates** that block dislocation motion — this requires a **specific thermal cycle** (heat to 530 °C → quench → age at 175 °C for 8–12 hours) which is impossible to perform in-situ on a ship's hull. Hence, welded 6xxx structures always have lower strength than the unwelded parent.

#### Part (b) — Marine Corrosion Behaviour and Applications [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Corrosion Behaviour in Seawater:**

| Feature | AA 5xxx (5083) | AA 6xxx (6061) |
|---------|----------------|----------------|
| **Matrix potential (SCE)** | −0.78 V | −0.78 V |
| **Intermetallic potential** | Al₃Mg₂ at **−1.10 V** (anodic) | Mg₂Si at **−0.30 V** (cathodic) |
| **Mechanism of attack** | Uniform anodic dissolution of β-phase | Pitting at Mg₂Si sites due to matrix dissolution around cathodic precipitates |
| **Corrosion rate (flowing seawater)** | < 25 µm/year (very low) | 50 – 200 µm/year localized |
| **Pitting tendency** | Low | Moderate to high |
| **SCC susceptibility** | Low (when properly tempered) | Low to moderate |
| **Suitability for full immersion** | **Yes — recommended** | **No — limited to atmospheric exposure** |

**Applications:**

**AA 5xxx Applications:**
- Hull plates, bottom shell, side shell of fast craft
- Welded superstructure panels
- LNG carrier secondary barriers (5083-O)
- Decks and helicopter platforms
- Mine countermeasure vessel hulls (non-magnetic)

**AA 6xxx Applications:**
- **Extruded mast sections, handrails, and window frames**
- **Deckhouse cladding and stiffeners**
- **Bunks, ladders, and furniture** (atmospheric exposure)
- **Engine intake manifolds and pump housings** (cast 356)
- **Helicopter platform support frames** (T6 condition, away from direct immersion)

**Design Rule (KTU takeaway):**

> **Use 5xxx where the structure is in contact with seawater (hull, shell plates, tanks). Use 6xxx where the structure is above the waterline or in atmospheric exposure (extruded superstructure, masts, fittings).**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make in This Topic — and Where Marks Are Lost:**
>
> 1. **Writing "5083" without the temper** → Examiner deducts **1 mark**. Always write "**AA 5083-H116**" or "**AA 5083-H321**" for marine plate.
>
> 2. **Confusing 5xxx and 6xxx corrosion behaviour** → Many students wrongly claim 6xxx is more corrosion-resistant because it is heat-treated. The opposite is true. Loss of **2 marks** possible.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the Cu limit** → Stating alloy composition without mentioning the strict Cu ≤ 0.10% limit in 5083 is incomplete. Examiner expects it. Loss of **1 mark**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the filler wire** → In a welding question, the parent alloy 5083 must be welded with **AA 5183 or 5356 filler** — not with 5083 filler (which has higher Cu and is not made as filler). Common omission.
>
> 5. **Saying "aluminium does not corrode"** → It DOES corrode, but via a self-passivating oxide. Marks lost for incorrect statement.
>
> 6. **Mixing up the role of Mg vs Mn** → Mn is for grain refinement, Mg is for strength and corrosion mechanism. Don't interchange.
>
> 7. **Wrong density used** → Aluminium density is **2.70 g/cm³**, not 2.85. Calculation errors cascade.
>
> 8. **Omitting classification society rules** — IACS UR W26, DNVGL-OS-C401, ASTM B928 — these are syllabus-relevant standards and earn extra credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 — Composition of Aluminium Alloys in Ship Building — Quick-Recall Bulletins:**

- ☐ **5xxx series (Al-Mg) is the primary marine alloy family** — 5083 is the workhorse.
- ☐ **6xxx series (Al-Mg-Si) is for extrusions and superstructures** — 6061 and 6082.
- ☐ **7xxx series (Al-Zn-Mg) is reserved for naval/defence use only** — 7017, 7020.
- ☐ **Cu limit ≤ 0.10%** in marine 5xxx alloys (5083, 5086) to prevent pitting.
- ☐ **Mg level controls strength AND corrosion risk** — high Mg = strong but sensitization-prone above 65 °C.
- ☐ **H116 and H321 tempers are mandatory for marine hull plate** (not O, not H112 alone).
- ☐ **T6 temper applies to 6xxx/7xxx** — solution heat-treat + artificial aging.
- ☐ **Welding 5xxx uses 5183 or 5356 filler wire** — never parent alloy as filler.
- ☐ **Aluminium's modulus (70 GPa) is 1/3 of steel** — plates must be thicker to match stiffness.
- ☐ **Density 2.70 g/cm³** gives ~50–60% weight saving vs steel hulls.
- ☐ **Al₂O₃ passive film** is self-healing and provides inherent corrosion resistance.
- ☐ **Al-Mg alloys dissolve uniformly (β-phase is anodic)** — pitting-resistant.
- ☐ **Al-Mg-Si alloys pit around Mg₂Si** (cathodic) — less suitable for immersion.
- ☐ **Cryogenic service: use 5083-O** (annealed for max toughness at −196 °C).
- ☐ **Ballistic/naval: use 5456 or 7017** with cathodic protection.
- ☐ **Cast marine fittings: A356, A357** (Al-Si-Mg, T6 temper).
- ☐ **Class approvals:** DNVGL-OS-C401, IACS UR W26/W27, ASTM B209/B221/B928, BS EN 13195.
- ☐ **Non-magnetic nature** → critical for mine countermeasure vessels (MCMVs).
- ☐ **Self-corrosion rate in seawater for 5083: < 25 µm/year** — extremely low.
- ☐ **Always pair alloy + temper in the answer** (e.g., 5083-H116, 6082-T6).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages of using aluminium over steel in ship building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advantages of using aluminium over steel in ship building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In marine and shipbuilding engineering, the comparative selection between **aluminium alloys** and **carbon/mild steel** as the primary hull and superstructure material is governed by a multi-criterion decision matrix. **Aluminium** (specifically marine-grade alloys of the 5xxx and 6xxx series such as **AA 5083, AA 5086, AA 5454, AA 5456, and AA 6082**) offers a superior combination of **specific strength (strength-to-weight ratio)**, **inherent corrosion resistance**, **non-magnetic behaviour**, and **ease of fabrication** when contrasted with conventional shipbuilding steel (grades A, D, E, AH32, DH36 as per classification society rules).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Under the 2024 Scheme, students must be able to *enumerate, compare, and justify* the material selection rationale between ferrous and non-ferrous marine alloys. The question is frequently framed as a **"Compare and contrast" (5 marks)** or **"Discuss the advantages" (10–14 marks)** type in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

## 1.2 Material Property Snapshot

| Property | Aluminium (AA 5083-H116) | Mild Steel (Grade A) |
|---|---|---|
| Density ($\rho$) | **2700 kg/m³** | **7850 kg/m³** |
| Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$) | ~215 MPa | ~235 MPa |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_u$) | ~305 MPa | ~400–490 MPa |
| Specific Strength | ~107 kNm/kg | ~30 kNm/kg |
| Young's Modulus ($E$) | **70 GPa** | **210 GPa** |
| Melting Point | 660 °C | 1500 °C |
| Corrosion in Seawater | Excellent (self-passivating) | Poor (requires coating) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Backpack vs. Suitcase" Intuition

Imagine you are a traveller who must carry a heavy load (the ship’s cargo and equipment) over a long distance (the ocean). A **steel ship** is like carrying that load in a **heavy iron suitcase** — it is strong, but you spend most of your energy just moving the suitcase itself. An **aluminium ship** is the same load carried in a **lightweight aluminium-frame backpack** — it is engineered to be just as strong for the load it carries, but the *container itself* weighs only one-third as much.

The result? You walk faster, burn less fuel, carry more payload, and the container does not rust when it rains (seawater splash). This everyday analogy directly maps to the engineering reality of ship design: **lightweight hull → higher speed, lower fuel consumption, larger payload, and lower lifecycle cost**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:**
> Aluminium does **not** beat steel in *raw* strength or *raw* stiffness. It beats steel in the **specific** (per-unit-weight) properties that actually matter for a floating vessel, where weight is the enemy of buoyancy, speed, and fuel economy.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

The KTU 2024 Scheme for **PENST414** explicitly tests the student's ability to:

- **CO1:** *Identify and classify shipbuilding materials and their properties.*
- **CO2:** *Analyse the corrosion behaviour and environmental suitability of marine materials.*

This topic sits at the intersection of **CO1 and CO2** and almost always appears as either a Part-A (3 mark) short question or as a sub-part (7 mark) of a 14-mark Part-B question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ten Pillars of Aluminium Advantage in Shipbuilding

### Pillar 1 — Weight Reduction (Lightweight Displacement)

The single most decisive advantage. The density of aluminium is:

$$\rho_{Al} = 2700 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}, \quad \rho_{Fe} = 7850 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}$$

The **weight saving factor** is:

$$f_{w} = \frac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{Fe}} = \frac{2700}{7850} \approx 0.344$$

This means an aluminium structure weighs approximately **34.4%** of an equivalent steel structure of the same volume — a **65.6% weight reduction** for the *hull and superstructure envelope*.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** A 15–20% reduction in lightship weight typically translates to a **12–18% increase in payload**, a **10–15% reduction in fuel consumption**, or a **8–12% increase in top speed** for the same installed propulsion power.

### Pillar 2 — Specific Strength (Strength-to-Weight Ratio)

$$\text{Specific Strength} = \frac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho}$$

For AA 5083:
$$SS_{Al} = \frac{305 \ \text{MPa}}{2700 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}} = 1.13 \times 10^{5} \ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$

For Mild Steel Grade A:
$$SS_{Fe} = \frac{400 \ \text{MPa}}{7850 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}} = 5.10 \times 10^{4} \ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$

Aluminium offers roughly **2.2× the specific strength** of mild steel.

### Pillar 3 — Corrosion Resistance (Self-Passivating Oxide Film)

Aluminium spontaneously forms a thin, adherent, and self-healing oxide layer upon exposure to air or water:

$$4 \text{Al} + 3 \text{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \text{Al}_{2}\text{O}_{3}$$

This **alumina (Al₂O₃) film** is approximately **2–10 nm thick** and is **insoluble in neutral seawater** (pH 6.5–8.5). It reforms instantaneously if scratched, providing a permanent barrier. Steel, by contrast, forms **Fe₂O₃ (rust)**, which is *porous, non-protective, and flakes off*, exposing fresh metal to further attack.

### Pillar 4 — Non-Magnetic Behaviour

Aluminium is **paramagnetic** with a relative permeability $\mu_r \approx 1.000022$, essentially indistinguishable from vacuum. Steel is **ferromagnetic** with $\mu_r \approx 100$ to $5000$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **mission-critical** property for:
> - **Minesweepers** (must not trigger magnetic-influence mines)
> - **Naval research vessels** (require low magnetic signature)
> - **Submarine-deck equipment foundations** (compass-binnacle mounting)

### Pillar 5 — Cryogenic / Low-Temperature Toughness

Unlike steel, which undergoes a **ductile-to-brittle transition** (DBTT) at low temperatures, aluminium alloys **do not exhibit a sharp DBTT**. They actually show *improved* tensile strength and *preserved* toughness at sub-zero temperatures, making them ideal for **LNG carriers**, **polar research vessels**, and **refrigerated cargo holds**.

### Pillar 6 — Ease of Fabrication & Welding

- Aluminium can be **extruded** into complex profiles impossible with steel (reducing the number of stiffeners and joints).
- **GTAW (TIG)** and **GMAW (MIG)** welding of aluminium is faster per unit length than steel SMAW.
- Cold-formability is excellent — sheets can be rolled, bent, and spun without cracking.

### Pillar 7 — Reduced Maintenance & Lifecycle Cost

| Maintenance Item | Steel Ship | Aluminium Ship |
|---|---|---|
| Repainting (anti-corrosive) | Every 2–3 years | Every 5–7 years (or never for 5xxx series) |
| Cathodic Protection (ICCP/SACP) | Mandatory | Often minimal/eliminated |
| Plate replacement (corrosion wastage) | Every 12–20 years | Rare (in 5xxx series) |

### Pillar 8 — Higher Speed & Fuel Efficiency

By Archimedes' principle, reducing displacement directly increases the **Froude number** $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ for a given power, or alternatively allows the same $F_n$ to be achieved at lower thrust, hence lower **brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC)**.

### Pillar 9 — Recyclability & Sustainability

Aluminium is **100% recyclable** with only **5% of the energy** required to produce primary aluminium from bauxite. Ships at end-of-life yield high scrap value.

### Pillar 10 — Payload & Deadweight Advantage

For a fixed-size vessel, a lighter hull means the **deadweight tonnage (DWT)** can be increased — directly improving commercial revenue for cargo vessels.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_w = \rho_{Al}/\rho_{Fe}$ | Weight saving factor | dimensionless | Hull weight comparison |
| 2 | $SS = \sigma_{uts}/\rho$ | Specific strength | $\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$ or $\text{kNm/kg}$ | Material selection index |
| 3 | $\Delta W = V(\rho_{Fe} - \rho_{Al})g$ | Weight saved per unit hull volume | N | Payload gain estimation |
| 4 | $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$ | Froude number | dimensionless | Speed-length ratio |
| 5 | $P \propto W^{2/3} \cdot V^{3}$ | Power–displacement–speed (Holtrop) | kW | Resistance & propulsion |
| 6 | $E_{eff} = E_{Al} / (E_{Fe}) \cdot (\rho_{Fe}/\rho_{Al})^{1/2}$ | Effective specific stiffness | — | Panel-buckling comparison |
| 7 | $\mu_r$ (permeability) | Magnetic property | dimensionless | Minesweeper suitability |
| 8 | $\sigma_y / \rho$ | Specific yield strength | $\text{kNm/kg}$ | Hull-girder scantling |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:** When writing absolute value $\vert x \vert$ inside a table cell, always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX — never the raw pipe symbol `\|` — to avoid breaking markdown table parsing.

## 2.3 Why the Theory Matters

Every formula above directly informs a **classification society rule** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, IRS). For instance, DNV's *Hull Structural Design — Ships with Aluminium Hulls* (DNV-CG-0326) explicitly uses the **specific yield strength** to determine minimum plate thickness. The **weight-saving factor** drives the **lightweight ship premium** in commercial shipbuilding contracts. A KTU student who masters these equations can move seamlessly from theory to design-office practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Hull-Weight Reduction Quantification

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A high-speed patrol vessel is to be redesigned. The original steel hull has a structural volume of $V = 1200 \ \text{m}^{3}$. Calculate the structural weight saving if the hull is rebuilt in AA 5083 aluminium alloy, and estimate the additional payload (in tonnes) this enables, assuming the displacement limit remains unchanged.

### Given Data

$$V = 1200 \ \text{m}^{3}, \quad \rho_{Fe} = 7850 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}, \quad \rho_{Al} = 2700 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}$$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute steel hull mass.**

$$m_{Fe} = \rho_{Fe} \cdot V = 7850 \ \text{kg/m}^{3} \times 1200 \ \text{m}^{3} = 9{,}420{,}000 \ \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute aluminium hull mass (same volume envelope).**

$$m_{Al} = \rho_{Al} \cdot V = 2700 \ \text{kg/m}^{3} \times 1200 \ \text{m}^{3} = 3{,}240{,}000 \ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute mass (weight) saved.**

$$\Delta m = m_{Fe} - m_{Al} = 9{,}420{,}000 - 3{,}240{,}000 = 6{,}180{,}000 \ \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to tonnes (1 tonne = 1000 kg).**

$$\Delta m = 6180 \ \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 5 — Express as a percentage.**

$$\text{\% Saving} = \frac{\Delta m}{m_{Fe}} \times 100 = \frac{6{,}180{,}000}{9{,}420{,}000} \times 100 = 65.6\%$$

**Step 6 — Account for thickness compensation.** Because aluminium has a lower Young's modulus ($E_{Al} = 70$ GPa vs $E_{Fe} = 210$ GPa), plates must be thickened by a factor of:

$$k_t = \sqrt[3]{\frac{E_{Fe}}{E_{Al}}} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{210}{70}} = \sqrt[3]{3} \approx 1.442$$

So the effective aluminium mass becomes:

$$m_{Al,eff} = 3{,}240{,}000 \times 1.442 = 4{,}672{,}080 \ \text{kg}$$

**Step 7 — Recompute net saving.**

$$\Delta m_{net} = 9{,}420{,}000 - 4{,}672{,}080 = 4{,}747{,}920 \ \text{kg} \approx 4748 \ \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 8 — Real-world net saving percentage.**

$$\text{Net \% Saving} = \frac{4{,}747{,}920}{9{,}420{,}000} \times 100 \approx 50.4\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** Even *after* accounting for the thickness penalty due to lower stiffness, an aluminium hull still saves **~50% of the structural weight** — a transformational gain for a naval architect.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Specific Strength Comparison

**Problem Statement:**
Compare the specific strength of AA 5083 (UTS = 305 MPa) and Mild Steel Grade A (UTS = 400 MPa). Which material is more efficient on a *per-kg* basis for a marine structural application?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Define specific strength.**

$$SS = \frac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho}$$

**Step 2 — Compute for aluminium.**

$$SS_{Al} = \frac{305 \times 10^{6} \ \text{Pa}}{2700 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}} = 1.13 \times 10^{5} \ \frac{\text{N·m}}{\text{kg}} = 113 \ \text{kNm/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute for steel.**

$$SS_{Fe} = \frac{400 \times 10^{6} \ \text{Pa}}{7850 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}} = 5.10 \times 10^{4} \ \frac{\text{N·m}}{\text{kg}} = 51.0 \ \text{kNm/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute ratio.**

$$\frac{SS_{Al}}{SS_{Fe}} = \frac{113}{51.0} \approx 2.22$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.** Aluminium is **2.22 times** more efficient per kilogram of weight carried in tension. For a high-speed craft, patrol boat, or hydrofoil where every kilogram of structural mass is a kilogram of *lost* payload or *wasted* fuel, aluminium decisively wins.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Advantages Summary Table

| Sl. No. | Advantage | Quantitative Basis | Beneficiary Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Low density | 2700 vs 7850 kg/m³ | High-speed crafts, patrol boats |
| 2 | High specific strength | 113 vs 51 kNm/kg | Weight-critical structures |
| 3 | Corrosion resistance | Al₂O₃ passive film | All marine service |
| 4 | Non-magnetic | $\mu_r \approx 1$ | Minesweepers, research vessels |
| 5 | Cryogenic toughness | No DBTT | LNG carriers, polar ships |
| 6 | Fabricability | Extrudable, weldable | Complex superstructure |
| 7 | Low maintenance | No repainting for 5–7 yrs | Lifecycle cost reduction |
| 8 | Fuel efficiency | ∝ W^{2/3} V^{3} | Commercial shipping |
| 9 | Recyclability | 95% energy saving | Sustainability compliance |
| 10 | Higher payload | ~50% net hull saving | Cargo revenue |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Comparative Decision Architecture for Material Selection

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions between the two material systems and the decision drivers** in a ship design context. It is a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow as mandated by the protocol when a physical cross-section is not required.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Design Requirement] --> B{Critical Design Driver}
    B -- Weight Critical --> C[Select Aluminium]
    B -- Cost Critical --> D[Select Steel]
    B -- Magnetic Signature Critical --> C
    B -- Cryogenic Service --> C
    C --> E[Aluminium Family]
    E --> F1[5xxx Al-Mg Alloys]
    E --> F2[6xxx Al-Mg-Si Alloys]
    E --> F3[5xxx Marine Grade AA 5083]
    F1 --> G1[Superstructure Panels]
    F2 --> G2[Helideck, Mast]
    F3 --> G3[Hull Plates and Extrusions]
    D --> H[Steel Family]
    H --> I1[Mild Steel Grade A]
    H --> I2[AH32 DH36 High Tensile]
    I1 --> J1[Bulk Carrier Hull]
    I2 --> J2[Container Ship Hatch Coaming]
    G1 --> K1[Weight Saved 50 to 60 percent]
    G3 --> K2[Corrosion Resistance Inherent]
    G2 --> K3[Non Magnetic and Recyclable]
    J1 --> L1[Lower Initial Cost]
    J2 --> L2[Higher Stiffness]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Interpretation

- **Node A** represents the **initial design brief** (e.g., a 45 m fast patrol craft for coast guard use).
- **Node B** is the **decision gate** based on the dominant requirement. For a fast patrol craft, *weight is critical* → flow goes left to **C (Aluminium)**.
- **Nodes E, F1–F3, G1–G3** describe the **aluminium alloy sub-selection** and their **shipboard application**:
  - *AA 5083* → hull plates (most common marine alloy).
  - *AA 6082* → helideck and mast (good extruded structural shape).
  - *AA 5456* → high-stress bottom plating.
- **Nodes D, H, I1–I2, J1–J2** describe the **steel branch** for ships where cost or stiffness dominate (bulk carriers, VLCCs, large container ships).
- **Nodes K1–K3 and L1–L2** capture the **outcome advantage** or **outcome trade-off** respectively.

## 4.3 Comparative Cross-Section Schematic (Textual)

For a plate under the same bending load, the design thickness ratio is governed by:

$$\frac{t_{Al}}{t_{Fe}} = \left(\frac{\sigma_{y,Fe}}{\sigma_{y,Al}} \cdot \frac{E_{Al}}{E_{Fe}}\right)^{1/3}$$

Plugging in standard values ($\sigma_{y,Fe} = 235$ MPa, $\sigma_{y,Al} = 215$ MPa, $E_{Al} = 70$ GPa, $E_{Fe} = 210$ GPa):

$$\frac{t_{Al}}{t_{Fe}} = \left(\frac{235}{215} \cdot \frac{70}{210}\right)^{1/3} = (0.763)^{1/3} \approx 0.913$$

Wait — this is the *bending stress* governed ratio. For **buckling-governed** panels (which dominate in ship plating), the thickness ratio is:

$$\frac{t_{Al,plate}}{t_{Fe,plate}} = \left(\frac{E_{Fe}}{E_{Al}}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{210}{70}\right)^{1/3} = 3^{1/3} \approx 1.44$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Aluminium plates must be **~44% thicker** than equivalent steel plates to provide the same buckling resistance. This thickness penalty is what reduces the naïve 65.6% weight saving down to the realistic **~50% net saving**, as derived in Section 3.1.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** List any **three advantages** of using aluminium alloys over steel in shipbuilding.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Lower density (Weight saving):** Aluminium has a density of 2700 kg/m³, which is only about one-third that of steel (7850 kg/m³). This results in a hull weight reduction of 50–65%, directly increasing payload and reducing fuel consumption.

2. **Superior corrosion resistance in seawater:** Aluminium forms a self-healing passive alumina (Al₂O₃) film on its surface that is highly resistant to chloride attack. Steel, in contrast, forms porous rust (Fe₂O₃) and requires regular painting and cathodic protection.

3. **Non-magnetic property:** Aluminium is essentially non-magnetic (relative permeability ≈ 1), making it ideal for minesweepers and naval vessels that require a low magnetic signature. Steel is ferromagnetic.

*(Any 3 of the 10 listed in Section 2.1 are acceptable.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define **specific strength** and state its value for AA 5083 and mild steel.

**Model Answer:**

**Specific strength** is defined as the ultimate tensile strength of a material divided by its density. It is a key figure of merit for weight-sensitive applications such as ship hulls and aerospace structures.

$$\text{Specific Strength} = \frac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho}$$

For **AA 5083 aluminium**: $\sigma_{uts} = 305$ MPa, $\rho = 2700$ kg/m³
$$SS_{Al} = \frac{305 \times 10^{6}}{2700} = 1.13 \times 10^{5} \ \text{N·m/kg} = 113 \ \text{kNm/kg}$$

For **Mild Steel Grade A**: $\sigma_{uts} = 400$ MPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³
$$SS_{Fe} = \frac{400 \times 10^{6}}{7850} = 5.10 \times 10^{4} \ \text{N·m/kg} = 51.0 \ \text{kNm/kg}$$

The specific strength of aluminium is approximately **2.2 times** that of mild steel, demonstrating its superior efficiency for marine applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Discuss in detail the **advantages of using aluminium over steel** in shipbuilding. Compare the two materials with respect to **(a) weight, specific strength, and stiffness** and **(b) corrosion resistance and magnetic properties**. State typical marine-grade aluminium alloys used.

#### Part (a) — Weight, Specific Strength, and Stiffness (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Aluminium and its alloys are widely preferred over steel in modern ship construction — particularly for **high-speed crafts, naval patrol vessels, hydrofoils, and superstructures** — due to a superior combination of physical and mechanical properties.

**(1) Weight (Density):**
The single most important advantage is the much lower density of aluminium. Steel has a density of $\rho_{Fe} = 7850 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}$, while aluminium has $\rho_{Al} = 2700 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}$.

$$\frac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{Fe}} = \frac{2700}{7850} \approx 0.344$$

So an aluminium hull weighs only ~34% of a comparable steel hull. After compensating for the increased plate thickness required to offset aluminium's lower stiffness, the *net* hull weight saving is **~50%**.

**(2) Specific Strength:**
Specific strength is the UTS-to-density ratio. AA 5083 (UTS = 305 MPa) gives a specific strength of ~113 kNm/kg, whereas mild steel (UTS = 400 MPa) gives only ~51 kNm/kg. Aluminium is therefore **~2.2× more efficient per kilogram**, which directly translates to higher payload capacity.

**(3) Stiffness (Young's Modulus):**
The Achilles' heel of aluminium is its lower stiffness. $E_{Al} = 70$ GPa, only one-third of $E_{Fe} = 210$ GPa. This requires plate thicknesses to be increased by a factor of $(E_{Fe}/E_{Al})^{1/3} = 3^{1/3} \approx 1.44$ for equivalent buckling resistance. Despite this penalty, the net weight saving remains substantial.

#### Part (b) — Corrosion Resistance, Magnetic Properties, and Alloys (7 Marks)

**(4) Corrosion Resistance:**
Aluminium spontaneously forms a thin (~2–10 nm), adherent, self-healing **alumina (Al₂O₃)** film on exposure to air or water:

$$4 \text{Al} + 3 \text{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \text{Al}_{2}\text{O}_{3}$$

This film is **insoluble in near-neutral seawater** (pH 6.5–8.5) and reforms instantly if scratched. Steel, by contrast, forms porous, non-protective rust (Fe₂O₃·xH₂O) that flakes off and exposes fresh metal, requiring frequent repainting and cathodic protection. Aluminium ships, especially those built in **5xxx series (Al-Mg) alloys**, can often be left unpainted below the waterline with only sacrificial anodes.

**(5) Magnetic Properties:**
Aluminium is **paramagnetic** with $\mu_r \approx 1.000022$, essentially non-magnetic. Steel is **ferromagnetic** with $\mu_r$ ranging from 100 to 5000. This makes aluminium indispensable for **magnetic-influence minesweepers (MCMs)**, degaussing-sensitive naval auxiliaries, and **research vessels** requiring low magnetic signature.

**(6) Typical Marine Aluminium Alloys:**

| Alloy Series | Composition | Marine Application |
|---|---|---|
| **AA 5083** (Al-4.5Mg-Mn) | Most common marine hull plate | Hull plating, superstructure |
| **AA 5086** (Al-4.0Mg) | Slightly lower strength | Small craft, superstructure |
| **AA 5454** (Al-2.7Mg-Mn) | Good weldability | Pressure vessels, deckhouses |
| **AA 5456** (Al-5.1Mg-Mn) | Highest strength 5xxx | High-speed hull bottoms |
| **AA 6082 / 6061** (Al-Mg-Si) | Heat-treatable, extrudable | Masts, helidecks, structural extrusions |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Do NOT state that aluminium is "stronger" than steel** — it is *weaker in absolute terms* (lower UTS and lower yield strength). The advantage is in *specific* (per-kg) properties.
> - **Do NOT forget the thickness compensation factor** of $3^{1/3} \approx 1.44$. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if this penalty is ignored.
> - **Do NOT confuse 5xxx with 6xxx alloys** — 5xxx is the Al-Mg (non-heat-treatable, weldable) family used for hull plates; 6xxx is the Al-Mg-Si (heat-treatable, extrudable) family used for structural sections.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** With the help of suitable numerical examples, explain why aluminium alloys are preferred over steel in **(a) high-speed patrol boats** and **(b) cryogenic LNG carrier service**. Include in your answer the metallurgical reason for aluminium's corrosion resistance in seawater.

#### Part (a) — Aluminium in High-Speed Patrol Boats (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

High-speed patrol boats (e.g., 40–50 m coast guard interceptor craft) operate in a **weight-critical regime** where every additional kilogram of hull weight demands more engine power, more fuel, and reduces top speed and range.

**Numerical Example:**
Consider a patrol boat with a hull structural volume $V = 600 \ \text{m}^{3}$.

- Steel hull mass: $m_{Fe} = 7850 \times 600 = 4{,}710{,}000 \ \text{kg} = 4710$ tonnes.
- Aluminium hull mass: $m_{Al} = 2700 \times 600 = 1{,}620{,}000 \ \text{kg} = 1620$ tonnes.
- Naïve weight saving = 4710 − 1620 = **3090 tonnes (65.6%)**.

After compensating for the thickness factor of $3^{1/3} \approx 1.44$:
- Effective aluminium mass = $1620 \times 1.44 = 2332.8$ tonnes.
- **Net weight saving = 4710 − 2332.8 = 2377.2 tonnes (50.5%)**.

This 2377-tonne saving allows the vessel to either carry 2377 more tonnes of fuel and armament, achieve a top speed 8–12% higher at the same engine power, or operate at a 15–20% longer range. The specific strength of AA 5083 (113 kNm/kg) vs steel (51 kNm/kg) further confirms the weight-efficiency advantage.

#### Part (b) — Aluminium in Cryogenic LNG Service (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas) is transported at **−162 °C**. At such cryogenic temperatures, mild steel undergoes a **ductile-to-brittle transition (DBTT)**, suffering a catastrophic loss of fracture toughness and becoming prone to brittle fracture. Aluminium alloys, in contrast, **do not exhibit a sharp DBTT** — their tensile strength actually *increases* slightly at cryogenic temperatures, and their fracture toughness is preserved or improved.

**Numerical Comparison of Toughness at −162 °C:**

| Material | Charpy Impact at −162 °C |
|---|---|
| Mild Steel Grade A | < 27 J (fails impact test) |
| AA 5083-O | > 100 J (excellent) |

**Metallurgical Reason for Seawater Corrosion Resistance:**
Aluminium's corrosion resistance in seawater arises from the formation of a **tenacious, self-healing, nanometric alumina (Al₂O₃) passive film**:

$$2 \text{Al} + \tfrac{3}{2} \text{O}_{2} \rightarrow \text{Al}_{2}\text{O}_{3}$$

This film is **amorphous to nanocrystalline**, has a **Pilling–Bedworth ratio** close to 1 (so it does not spall), and is **insoluble in the pH range 4–9** that encompasses natural seawater (~pH 8.2). If mechanically damaged, the film **re-forms within milliseconds** by reaction with dissolved O₂. Steel rust (Fe₂O₃·xH₂O), in contrast, has a Pilling–Bedworth ratio > 2, is **porous and flaky**, and offers no barrier protection.

Common aluminium alloys used in cryogenic LNG service include **AA 5083-O** (annealed, for primary membranes) and **AA 304L-mod stainless-clad** systems, but the dominant cryogenic alloy remains AA 5083 for membrane containment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - In Part (a), students often quote the *raw* weight saving of 65.6% and forget the **thickness correction factor** of 1.44. Always present the *net* saving (~50%) for a realistic engineering answer.
> - In Part (b), students forget to write the **chemical equation** for alumina formation — examiners specifically look for $4\text{Al} + 3\text{O}_{2} \to 2\text{Al}_{2}\text{O}_{3}$.
> - Many students confuse **ferromagnetic** (steel) with **paramagnetic** (aluminium) — note that $\mu_r(\text{Al}) \approx 1$ while $\mu_r(\text{steel}) \approx 100\text{–}5000$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Density:** Al = **2700 kg/m³**, Steel = **7850 kg/m³** → Al is ~1/3 the weight.
- ✅ **Specific Strength of AA 5083 ≈ 113 kNm/kg** vs **Mild Steel ≈ 51 kNm/kg** → Al is **2.2× more efficient per kg**.
- ✅ **Young's Modulus:** $E_{Al} = 70$ GPa, $E_{Fe} = 210$ GPa → Al plates must be **~1.44× thicker** to match steel's buckling resistance.
- ✅ **Net hull weight saving after thickness compensation ≈ 50%**, not the naïve 65.6%.
- ✅ **Corrosion resistance** comes from the self-healing **Al₂O₃ passive film** formed by $4\text{Al} + 3\text{O}_{2} \to 2\text{Al}_{2}\text{O}_{3}$.
- ✅ **Aluminium is non-magnetic** ($\mu_r \approx 1$); ideal for **minesweepers** and **naval auxiliaries**.
- ✅ **No DBTT in aluminium** → ideal for **LNG** and **cryogenic** service.
- ✅ **Common marine alloys:** **AA 5083** (hull), **AA 5456** (high-stress bottom), **AA 6082** (extruded structures).
- ✅ **Holtrop relation** $P \propto W^{2/3} V^{3}$ — lower $W$ from aluminium reduces required power for the same speed.
- ✅ **5xxx series** = Al-Mg (non-heat-treatable, weldable, marine primary).
- ✅ **6xxx series** = Al-Mg-Si (heat-treatable, extrudable, structural sections).
- ✅ **Recyclability:** Aluminium scrap retains 95% of the energy value of primary aluminium.
- ✅ **Froude Number** $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$ — aluminium's lower $W$ allows higher $V$ for a given $F_n$ (planing regime) or lower $F_n$ resistance (displacement regime).
- ✅ **Maintenance saving:** No repainting for 5–7 years (5xxx); cathodic protection often eliminated.
- ✅ **Failure modes to recall in exam:** *NOT* galvanic corrosion only — also **crevice corrosion**, **pitting**, and **exfoliation** in improperly heat-treated alloys.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of corrosion in marine environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of corrosion in marine environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — TYPES OF CORROSION IN MARINE ENVIRONMENT

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion in a Marine Environment** is defined as the physico-chemical interaction (typically electrochemical in nature) between a metal/alloy and its surrounding seawater or marine atmosphere, resulting in the progressive degradation of the metal's useful properties (mechanical strength, ductility, surface finish, etc.). Per KTU 2024 syllabus (PENST414 Module 2), the marine environment is classified into **five corrosion zones** based on ISO 12944 / NACE classification:
> 
> 1. **Atmospheric Zone** (Splash + above waterline)
> 2. **Splash Zone** (intermittent wet-dry)
> 3. **Tidal Zone** (regular wet-dry cycling)
> 4. **Submerged Zone** (continuously immersed)
> 5. **Mud/Soil Zone** (below seabed)

The **seawater corrosion cell** is conventionally described by:
$$\text{Anode (Metal Oxidation)} : M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-}$$

$$\text{Cathode (Reduction)} : O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Sinking Ship Iron Trick"**
> 
> Imagine a ship is like a giant battery you didn't ask for. When two *different* metals touch underwater (steel hull + bronze propeller), the sea acts like lemon juice in a lemon-cell. The sea's dissolved **salt ions (Na⁺, Cl⁻)** and **dissolved oxygen** act as the electrolyte. The "weaker" metal (steel) becomes the *sacrificial loser* — it dissolves away to protect the bronze. Even a *single* metal in seawater forms millions of tiny invisible batteries on its rough surface (peaks act as cathodes, valleys as anodes), causing pitting. Different "moods" of the sea (calm splash zone, raging submerged zone, muddy seabed) cause *different* corrosion "personalities" — this is why marine corrosion is studied as **types**, not just as one phenomenon.

### 1.3 The Five Marine Zones — A Quick Snapshot

| Zone | Description | Dominant Corrosion Type |
|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric | Salt-laden air, intermittent wet-dry | Uniform + Atmospheric |
| Splash | Wave action, UV, high aeration | **Most severe** — uniform + erosion |
| Tidal | Half wet / half dry, biological fouling | Crevice, pitting, biofouling-accelerated |
| Submerged | Continuous immersion, low O₂ at depth | Uniform, pitting, galvanic |
| Mud | Anaerobic, sulphate-reducing bacteria (SRB) | MIC, pitting, concentration cells |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Marine Constant:** Seawater conductivity ≈ **4–5 S/m** (≈ 1,000× higher than fresh water). Salinity ≈ **35 ppt (3.5%)**. Chloride ion concentration ≈ **19,000 ppm**. These values drive the *aggressiveness* of every corrosion type below.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Marine Corrosion Types

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups marine corrosion into the following **ten principal types**:

1. Uniform (General) Corrosion
2. Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion
3. Pitting Corrosion
4. Crevice Corrosion
5. Intergranular Corrosion
6. Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)
7. Erosion–Corrosion
8. Cavitation Corrosion
9. Fretting Corrosion
10. Concentration Cell (Differential Aeration) Corrosion

Plus the special marine category:
11. Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC)

---

### 2.2 Type-by-Type Theoretical Breakdown

#### **Type 1 — Uniform (General) Corrosion**

- **Mechanism:** Metal loss occurs at the **same rate** over the entire exposed surface. Anodes and cathodes are *micro-scale* and constantly switch positions.
- **Marine signature:** Classic in submerged mild-steel plates; follows **Tafel kinetics**.
- **Why it matters for ships:** Predictable; thickness allowance (corrosion allowance) can be designed in. Most ship hulls are *sized* assuming uniform corrosion of ~**0.1–0.5 mm/year**.

#### **Type 2 — Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion**

- **Mechanism:** When two dissimilar metals are *electrically connected* and immersed in an electrolyte (seawater), the **more anodic** (electronegative) metal corrodes preferentially.
- **Marine driving force:** Galvanic series in flowing seawater defines the potential difference ΔE.
- **Shipboard example:** Steel hull + bronze propeller → severe galvanic attack on the hull. Cathodic protection with Zn/Al anodes is mandatory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical "Rule of Thumb":** A potential difference **> 0.25 V** in the galvanic series indicates a *dangerous* galvanic couple in seawater.

#### **Type 3 — Pitting Corrosion**

- **Mechanism:** Localized, **autocatalytic** attack producing deep cavities ("pits"). The pit interior becomes anodic, acidified (Cl⁻ migrates in), while the surrounding surface is cathodic and aerated.
- **Marine signature:** Stainless steel (e.g., 304) propeller shafts, heat-exchanger tubes. Pitting is **insidious** — low mass loss but high penetration depth.
- **Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN):**
$$\text{PREN} = \%Cr + 3.3 \times \%Mo + 16 \times \%N$$
A PREN **> 40** is considered "seawater resistant" (e.g., super-duplex 2507).

#### **Type 4 — Crevice Corrosion**

- **Mechanism:** Occurs in **gaps** (gaskets, bolt heads, riveted lap joints, under marine growth) where oxygen access is restricted. Differential aeration between the occluded cell (anode) and the exterior (cathode) drives the attack.
- **Shipboard example:** Tubular plate heat exchangers, riveted hull seams, fouled propeller hubs.

#### **Type 5 — Intergranular Corrosion**

- **Mechanism:** Attack preferentially along **grain boundaries** due to chromium depletion (in austenitic stainless steels — "sensitization") or precipitation of brittle phases.
- **Marine relevance:** Critical for austenitic stainless-steel pipework carrying seawater; "weld decay" in 304/316 is classic.

#### **Type 6 — Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)**

- **Mechanism:** Simultaneous action of **tensile stress** (residual or applied) and a **specific corrosive environment** leads to brittle fracture. No general corrosion visible — sudden failure.
- **Marine classics:**
  - **Chloride SCC** of austenitic SS at > 60 °C.
  - **Caustic SCC** of boiler tubes.
  - **Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE)** of high-strength propeller shaft steels in seawater + cathodic protection over-protection.

#### **Type 7 — Erosion–Corrosion**

- **Mechanism:** Combined action of **mechanical wear** (high-velocity water/slurry) + **electrochemical corrosion**. The protective oxide film is mechanically removed, exposing fresh metal.
- **Marine signature:** Sand-laden seawater eroding pump impellers, copper alloy condenser tubes near tube-sheet inlets (impingement attack at velocities > **2–3 m/s**).

#### **Type 8 — Cavitation Corrosion**

- **Mechanism:** Vapor bubbles form in regions of **low pressure** (e.g., rapidly spinning propeller blade tips) and **collapse violently** near the surface, creating shock waves that disrupt passive films and fatigue the metal.
- **Shipboard example:** Bronze propellers, high-speed pump impellers, diesel engine cylinder liners.

#### **Type 9 — Fretting Corrosion**

- **Mechanism:** Small relative **slip motion** between two clamped surfaces under load, in a corrosive medium. Wears oxide film → re-corrosion → oxide debris acts as abrasive → cycle.
- **Marine relevance:** Propeller-shaft-to-hub taper fits, mooring chain links, bolted flanges.

#### **Type 10 — Concentration Cell (Differential Aeration) Corrosion**

- **Mechanism:** Same metal in two regions of **different dissolved oxygen** concentration. Low-O₂ region = anode (corrodes); high-O₂ region = cathode (protected).
- **Marine relevance:** Crevice corrosion is a *special case*; also seen under marine fouling patches and under deposits on hull bottoms.

#### **Type 11 — Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC)**

- **Mechanism:** Sulphate-Reducing Bacteria (SRB, e.g., *Desulfovibrio*) in anaerobic mud zones reduce SO₄²⁻ to S²⁻, producing H₂S that attacks steel.
- **Marine relevance:** Mud-zone pile corrosion, bilge-line corrosion, seawater ballast-tank bottoms.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL:** Replace any `|` symbol with `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown tables.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's law — mass of metal dissolved | $m$ in g; $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; $n$ = valency |
| 2 | $\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | Corrosion rate (mils per year) | $W$ = mass loss (mg); $D$ = density (g/cm³); $A$ = area (in²); $T$ = time (h) |
| 3 | $\text{CR (mm/yr)} = 0.0254 \times \text{CR (mpy)}$ | Conversion | mpy → mm/yr |
| 4 | $\Delta E = E_c - E_a$ | Galvanic driving potential | V; if $\Delta E > 0.25$ V → dangerous couple |
| 5 | $\text{PREN} = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N$ | Pitting resistance | PREN > 40 = seawater grade |
| 6 | $V_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{k}{D^{0.2}}$ | Critical impingement velocity (erosion) | Approx. 2–3 m/s for Cu-Ni in seawater |
| 7 | $i_{\text{corr}} = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(\dfrac{-\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$ | Butler–Volmer equation (full kinetics) | $i_0$ = exchange current density |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{ev}} = P_v(T) - P_{\text{local}}$ | Cavitation condition (vapor pressure deficit) | Bubbles form when $\eta_{\text{ev}} > 0$ |
| 9 | $K_{ISCC} = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | SCC fracture mechanics threshold | $a$ = crack length; $Y$ = geometry factor |

### Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Faraday's Law** is used by ship surveyors to *back-calculate* corrosion current density from measured plate thickness loss over a docking interval.
- **mpy formula** is the *universal currency* in NACE/IMO coating performance reports.
- **PREN** drives the **material selection** for seawater piping — 904L, AL-6XN, super-duplex.
- **Butler–Volmer** underpins cathodic-protection design (ICCP impressed current) on offshore platforms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: From Faraday's Law to Corrosion Rate in mm/year

This is the **most commonly asked KTU derivation** linking mass loss to penetration depth.

### Given
- Mass loss $W$ (grams) measured after exposure time $T$ (hours).
- Exposed area $A$ (in²) and alloy density $D$ (g/cm³).
- Valency of metal $n$, atomic mass $M$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Faraday's Law (starting point).** 
The mass of metal dissolved by a current $I$ over time $t$ is:
$$m = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \cdot M$$

**Step 2 — Convert current to current density.**
Recognising that $I = i \cdot A_{\text{SI}}$ (current density × area in SI units), and using $1\ \text{A·s} = 1\ \text{C}$:
$$m = \frac{i \cdot A_{\text{SI}} \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to engineering (mpy) units.**
- $W$ in mg (multiply g by 1000)
- $A$ in in² (convert cm²)
- $T$ in hours (convert seconds)
- Density $D$ in g/cm³

Substituting and simplifying with the identity $1\ \text{in} = 2.54\ \text{cm}$ and $1\ \text{h} = 3600\ \text{s}$, the engineering convention yields the **constant 534**:
$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$$

**Step 4 — Convert mpy to mm/yr.**
Since $1\ \text{mil} = 0.0254\ \text{mm}$:
$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = 0.0254 \times \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T} = \frac{13.56 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A steel plate of area **0.1 m²** is exposed to seawater for **180 days**. Mass loss = **240 g**. Density of steel = **7.85 g/cm³**. Find the corrosion rate in **(a) mpy** and **(b) mm/year**.

### Part (a): Convert units
- $W = 240\ \text{g} = 240{,}000\ \text{mg}$
- $A = 0.1\ \text{m}^2 = 0.1 \times 1550 = 155\ \text{in}^2$
- $T = 180 \times 24 = 4320\ \text{h}$
- $D = 7.85\ \text{g/cm}^3$

### Apply formula
$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{534 \times 240{,}000}{7.85 \times 155 \times 4320}$$

Calculate denominator first:
$$7.85 \times 155 = 1216.75$$
$$1216.75 \times 4320 = 5{,}256{,}360$$

Numerator:
$$534 \times 240{,}000 = 128{,}160{,}000$$

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{128{,}160{,}000}{5{,}256{,}360} \approx 24.38\ \text{mpy}$$

### Part (b): Convert to mm/yr
$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = 0.0254 \times 24.38 \approx 0.619\ \text{mm/yr}$$

### Interpretation
A corrosion rate of **0.62 mm/year** is **moderate-to-high** for marine service (NACE: < 0.025 mm/yr = excellent; > 0.5 mm/yr = severe). The plate will lose ~**6 mm in 10 years** — typical of unprotected mild steel in temperate seawater.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When Each Type Dominates

| Corrosion Type | Visual Appearance | Most Likely Zone on a Ship | Severity if Unattended |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform | Dull, even rust | Submerged hull (mild steel) | Medium — predictable |
| Galvanic | Localised groove at joint | Hull–propeller interface | High |
| Pitting | Deep narrow holes | SS tubes, shafts | Critical (perforation) |
| Crevice | Under deposit | Gaskets, lap joints | High |
| Intergranular | "Sugary" cracking along grains | Welded SS piping | Critical |
| SCC | Branching cracks, no visible metal loss | Boiler, propeller shafts | Critical (sudden) |
| Erosion | Horseshoe/directional scallops | Tube inlets, pump bowls | High |
| Cavitation | Spongy, honeycomb | Propeller tips | High |
| Fretting | Brown oxide debris + pits | Shaft–hub taper | Medium |
| Concentration | Patchy under fouling | Hull bottom under weeds | Medium |
| MIC | Tubercles + black sulfide mud | Mud zone, bilges | High |

---

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Marine Corrosion Types

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Marine Corrosion Types]
    ROOT --> A[Electrochemical]
    ROOT --> B[Mechanical Chemical]
    ROOT --> C[Bio Influenced]

    A --> A1[Uniform]
    A --> A2[Galvanic]
    A --> A3[Pitting]
    A --> A4[Crevice]
    A --> A5[Intergranular]
    A --> A6[Concentration Cell]

    B --> B1[Erosion Corrosion]
    B --> B2[Cavitation Corrosion]
    B --> B3[Fretting Corrosion]
    B --> B4[Stress Corrosion Cracking]

    C --> C1[MIC by SRB]
    C --> C2[MIC by Iron Bacteria]

    A3 --> A3A[Autocatalytic Cl Pit]
    A4 --> A4A[Differential Aeration]
    B4 --> B4A[Tensile Stress plus Environment]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Pitting Autocatalytic Mechanism

```mermaid
graph LR
    OUTER[Outer Cathodic Surface]
    PIT[Pit Interior Anode]
    FILM[Passive Film Broken]
    M[Metal Dissolution M to M n plus]
    HPLUS[H plus Generation]
    CLMIGR[Cl minus Migration Inward]
    O2RED[Oxygen Reduction Outward]

    FILM --> M
    M --> HPLUS
    HPLUS --> CLMIGR
    CLMIGR --> PIT
    PIT --> OUTER
    OUTER --> O2RED
    O2RED --> PIT
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Galvanic Cell on a Ship

```mermaid
graph TD
    HULL[Steel Hull Anode more Active]
    PROP[Bronze Propeller Cathode more Noble]
    WIRE[Electrical Contact Through Shaft plus Bearings]
    SEA[Seawater Electrolyte Cl minus Na plus]
    CP[Zinc Sacrificial Anode ICCP System]

    HULL -- oxidizes loses electrons --> SEA
    SEA -- carries ions --> PROP
    PROP -- accepts electrons reduces O2 --> HULL
    WIRE -- completes metallic path --> PROP

    CP -. mitigates .-> HULL
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — SCC Mechanism Flow (Three Conjoint Conditions)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Condition 1 Susceptible Alloy e.g. Austenitic SS]
    S2[Condition 2 Specific Environment e.g. Hot Cl minus Water]
    S3[Condition 3 Tensile Stress Residual plus Applied]

    S1 --> SCC[Stress Corrosion Cracking Brittle Fracture]
    S2 --> SCC
    S3 --> SCC

    SCC --> OUT1[Crack Initiation]
    SCC --> OUT2[Stage I Crack Propagation]
    SCC --> OUT3[Stage II Fast Fracture]

    MIT1[Temperatures above 60 C] -. mitigates .-> S2
    MIT2[Shot Peening Residual Compression] -. mitigates .-> S3
    MIT3[Use Inconel 625 or Ti] -. mitigates .-> S1
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Crevice vs. Pitting Decision Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q[Localized Attack Detected]
    Q --> R1{Geometry Visible Gap or Joint?}
    R1 -- yes --> CR[Crevice Corrosion]
    R1 -- no --> R2{Deep Narrow Holes on Smooth Surface?}
    R2 -- yes --> PT[Pitting Corrosion PREN Check]
    R2 -- no --> R3{Under Marine Deposit or Fouling?}
    R3 -- yes --> CC[Concentration Cell Differential Aeration]
    R3 -- no --> R4{Grain Boundary Visible Cracking?}
    R4 -- yes --> IG[Intergranular Corrosion]
    R4 -- no --> R5{Branching Cracks plus Tensile Stress?}
    R5 -- yes --> SC[Stress Corrosion Cracking]
    R5 -- no --> R6{High Velocity Fluid Impingement?}
    R6 -- yes --> ER[Erosion Corrosion]
    R6 -- no --> R7{Vibrating Contact Surface?}
    R7 -- yes --> FR[Fretting Corrosion]
    R7 -- no --> R8{Anaerobic Black Sulfide Mud?}
    R8 -- yes --> MIC[Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion]
```
