# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between pitting corrosion and crevice corrosion with one marine example of each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
| Feature | Pitting Corrosion | Crevice Corrosion |
|---|---|---|
| Initiation site | Free, smooth surface | Inside gaps/joints |
| Driving force | Localised passive-film breakdown | Differential aeration |
| Marine example | Stainless-steel 304 heat-exchanger tube | Bolt-head gasket of sea-water manifold |
| Penetration | Deep, narrow pits | Wedge-shaped under gap |

*(Examiner expects a clear 3-row table + 1 marine example each. 1 mark per row + ½ mark per example.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**State Faraday's law of electrolysis as applied to corrosion and define "corrosion current density". (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Faraday's law states that the mass of metal dissolved is directly proportional to the total electric charge passed:
$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$
where $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$ is Faraday's constant.

**Corrosion current density** $i_{\text{corr}}$ is the corrosion current per unit exposed area (units: A/cm² or A/m²). It quantifies the *intensity* of electrochemical attack and is obtained from Tafel extrapolation of polarisation curves.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice (Module-Internal Choice pattern).

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — Module 2 Internal Choice Option 1

#### Part (a) — [7 Marks]
**Explain with neat sketches the mechanism of galvanic corrosion in marine environment. How is it controlled on ship hulls?**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Definition [1 mark]:** Galvanic corrosion is the preferential corrosion of the more active (anodic) metal when two dissimilar metals are electrically connected in an electrolyte.
2. **Conditions [1 mark]:** (i) Metals of different electrode potential; (ii) Metallic path between them; (iii) Common electrolyte (seawater).
3. **Mechanism [3 marks]:** Describe:
   - Anode reaction: $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-}$ (oxidation).
   - Cathode reaction: $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^{-} \rightarrow 2OH^{-}$ (oxygen reduction in aerated seawater).
   - Current flow: electrons through metal path; ions through seawater.
   - Reference to galvanic series in flowing seawater.
4. **Marine example [1 mark]:** Steel hull + bronze propeller connected via shaft → hull corrodes; or aluminum superstructure + steel hull.
5. **Control measures [1 mark]:** Cathodic protection (sacrificial Zn/Al anodes or ICCP), use of dielectric (insulating) couplings, isolating flanges, painting both surfaces.

---

#### Part (b) — [7 Marks]
**A steel plate (density 7.85 g/cm³, area 0.05 m²) loses 150 g after 90 days immersion in seawater. Calculate the corrosion rate in (i) mpy and (ii) mm/year. Comment on its severity.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $W = 150\ \text{g} = 150{,}000\ \text{mg}$
- $A = 0.05\ \text{m}^2 = 0.05 \times 1550 = 77.5\ \text{in}^2$
- $T = 90 \times 24 = 2160\ \text{h}$
- $D = 7.85\ \text{g/cm}^3$

**Step 1 — Denominator:**
$$D \times A \times T = 7.85 \times 77.5 \times 2160$$
$$7.85 \times 77.5 = 608.375$$
$$608.375 \times 2160 = 1{,}314{,}090$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**
$$534 \times 150{,}000 = 80{,}100{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Apply:**
$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{80{,}100{,}000}{1{,}314{,}090} \approx 60.96\ \text{mpy}$$

**Step 4 — Convert:**
$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = 0.0254 \times 60.96 \approx 1.548\ \text{mm/yr}$$

**Step 5 — Severity comment [1 mark]:** NACE classifies > 0.5 mm/yr as **severe**. 1.55 mm/yr is therefore **unacceptably high**; the plate would lose 15 mm in 10 years. Cathodic protection or protective coating is mandatory.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Module 2 Internal Choice Option 2

#### Part (a) — [7 Marks]
**With a neat diagram, explain the mechanism of stress corrosion cracking (SCC) in austenitic stainless-steel marine piping. List four methods of prevention.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Definition [1 mark]:** SCC is brittle fracture caused by the **simultaneous** action of tensile stress, a susceptible alloy, and a specific corrosive medium.
2. **Three conjoint conditions diagram [2 marks]:** Tensile stress + Cl⁻ environment + austenitic SS (e.g., 304). Show branching transgranular cracks.
3. **Mechanism [2 marks]:**
   - Slip-step emergence ruptures passive film.
   - Anodic dissolution at slip step.
   - Hydrogen ingress (in chloride solutions above 60 °C).
   - Crack propagates perpendicular to tensile stress until final fast fracture.
4. **Marine example [0.5 mark]:** Type 304/316 stainless-steel piping carrying hot seawater.
5. **Prevention [1.5 marks]:**
   - Use high-Ni or super-austenitic alloys (e.g., AL-6XN).
   - Lower chloride + temperature.
   - Shot-peening to induce compressive residual stress.
   - Avoid residual tensile stress (proper weld procedure, PWHT).

---

#### Part (b) — [7 Marks]
**Explain the mechanism of microbiologically influenced corrosion (MIC) in the mud zone of marine steel piles. List the conditions favouring SRB activity and two control measures.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Definition [1 mark]:** MIC is corrosion caused or accelerated by the metabolic activities of micro-organisms (bacteria, fungi, algae) at the metal surface.
2. **Mechanism — SRB pathway [3 marks]:**
   - Anaerobic mud zone + sulphate ions + organic nutrients.
   - SRB (*Desulfovibrio*) catalyses: $\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 8\text{H} \rightarrow \text{S}^{2-} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
   - S²⁻ + Fe²⁺ → FeS (black, voluminous tubercle).
   - Cathodic depolarisation: atomic H removed by bacterial enzymes → cathodic reaction accelerated → iron loss.
3. **Conditions favouring SRB [1.5 marks]:**
   - Anaerobic (low O₂).
   - pH 5–9 (typically 6–8).
   - Temperature 20–40 °C.
   - Sulphate present (seawater has ~2,700 ppm SO₄²⁻ — ideal).
   - Stagnant water (mud zone is perfect).
4. **Marine location [0.5 mark]:** Piles of offshore platforms, jetty legs, ship rudder trunks below the waterline, bilges.
5. **Control [1 mark]:**
   - Biocide injection (glutaraldehyde, chlorine, THPS).
   - Cathodic protection below −0.95 V (SHE).
   - Epoxy-coat the pile.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where students LOSE marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up "corrosion current $I$" with "corrosion current density $i$"** in calculations — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversions** (cm² → in², hours → seconds) when applying the mpy formula — the **most frequent mistake** (~60% of failed attempts).
> 3. **Not specifying the zone** (submerged/splash/atmospheric/mud) when describing a corrosion type — KTU expects zone-based answers for full credit.
> 4. **Omitting the electrolyte reaction** (oxygen reduction) in galvanic mechanism diagrams — examiners allocate 1 dedicated mark for this.
> 5. **Confusing erosion-corrosion with cavitation-corrosion** — cavitation involves *bubble collapse*; erosion involves *solid-particle impingement*.
> 6. **Writing "stainless steel doesn't corrode"** — examiners will deduct 1 mark for this universally false statement.
> 7. **Forgetting PREN** in pitting-corrosion answers; PREN is a *high-yield* keyword.
> 8. **Skipping "Mitigation" sub-question in galvanic-corrosion 14-mark answers** — at least 1–2 marks reserved.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER ✏️

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Types of Corrosion in Marine Environment**

- ⚓ **Faraday's constant $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$** — must remember for numerical answers.
- ⚓ **mpy formula with constant 534** is universally accepted by KTU valuators.
- ⚓ **Seawater conductivity ≈ 4–5 S/m** — drives galvanic-corrosion severity.
- ⚓ **Five marine zones**: Atmospheric → Splash → Tidal → Submerged → Mud.
- ⚓ **Splash zone is the MOST aggressive** (alternating wet-dry + max aeration).
- ⚓ **Galvanic series in flowing seawater** is *different* from the standard EMF series — memorize the order: Mg → Zn → Al → Steel → Cu-Ni → Bronze → Monel → SS (active) → SS (passive) → Ti → Graphite.
- ⚓ **PREN > 40 = seawater-resistant** stainless steel.
- ⚓ **Pitting corrosion is autocatalytic** — Cl⁻ migrates inward, O₂ reduces outside.
- ⚓ **Crevice corrosion is differential aeration** — gap interior = anode.
- ⚓ **SCC requires 3 conditions** — susceptible alloy + specific environment + tensile stress; remove ONE → prevented.
- ⚓ **Erosion–corrosion critical velocity for Cu-Ni ≈ 2–3 m/s** in seawater.
- ⚓ **Cavitation** = vapor-bubble collapse at low-pressure regions (propeller tips).
- ⚓ **Fretting** = micro-slip + corrosion; common at shaft-hub taper fits.
- ⚓ **MIC/SRB** thrives in **anaerobic, sulphate-rich, stagnant** mud zones → produces **black FeS tubercles**.
- ⚓ **NACE corrosion severity scale**: < 0.025 mm/yr excellent; 0.025–0.1 good; 0.1–0.5 fair; > 0.5 severe.
- ⚓ **Mitigation triad**: Material selection + Coatings + Cathodic protection (sacrificial or ICCP).
- ⚓ **Always mention "seawater conductivity" and "Cl⁻ concentration"** when explaining *why* marine corrosion is aggressive.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion identification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Corrosion identification # Module 2 — Corrosion Identification ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):** > *Corrosion identification* is the systematic, multi-stage process of detecting, classifying, and quantifying the **type**, **morphology**, **extent**, and **root cause** of material degradation occurring on metallic components — particularly those used in ship hulls, propulsion systems, ballast tanks, and offshore structures. It integrates **visual inspection**, **non-destructive testing (NDT)**, **destructive metallographic examination**, **chemical analysis**, and **electrochemical measurement techniques** to produce an unambiguous diagnostic profile of the corrosion event. ### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ship Doctor" Approach Imagine a shipyard engineer walking down a dry-docked vessel as if it were a **patient entering a hospital**. The engineer, much like a doctor, must perform a triage: - **First, look** — *Visual inspection* (the equivalent of a doctor's stethoscope and visual exam). - **Second, scan internally** — *Non-destructive tests* such as ultrasonics or radiography (analogous to an MRI or X-ray). - **Third, take samples if necessary** — *Destructive metallography and chemical assays* (equivalent to a blood test or biopsy). - **Fourth, measure electrical activity** — *Electrochemical probes* (similar to an ECG, since corrosion is fundamentally an electrical phenomenon). > [!NOTE] > The identification stage is the **diagnostic precursor** to *corrosion prevention and protection* (Module 3 onward). Without a precise identification, any protection scheme risks being misapplied — like prescribing antibiotics for a viral infection. ### 1.2 Why Identification is Critical in Shipbuilding Ships operate in one of the most aggressive corrosion environments on Earth — **seawater** containing approximately **35,000 ppm** of dissolved salts (mainly **NaCl ≈ 3.5%** by mass), with chloride ions (Cl⁻) acting as aggressive pitting accelerators. KTU examiners frequently test the ability to **link the observed morphology to the operative corrosion mechanism**, because each mechanism demands a *different* mitigation strategy. | Shipboard Zone | Dominant Corrosion Type | Why It Occurs | |---|---|---| | Underwater hull | Pitting, general | Seawater immersion, biofouling | | Ballast tanks | Pitting, MIC, crevice | Wet/dry cycling, sediments | | Propellers | Cavitation, erosion | High velocity + pressure drops | | Shafting | Fretting, galvanic | Dissimilar metal coupling | | Deck superstructure | Atmospheric, SCC | Salt spray, cyclic loading | | Boiler tubes | Caustic embrittlement | High pH + residual stress | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Identification Workflow (Hierarchy of Evidence) A KTU-standard corrosion identification follows a **five-tier pyramid**, each tier providing progressively more diagnostic power: 1. **Tier 1 — Visual / Macroscopic Examination** - Naked-eye and low-magnification (10×–50×) observation. - Detects: rust staining, blistering, scale, deposits, pitting, cracking, colour changes. - Field tool: borescope, endoscope, magnifying glass. 2. **Tier 2 — Surface Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** - Liquid penetrant inspection (LPI/DPI) → detects surface-connected cracks. - Magnetic particle inspection (MPI) → detects surface & near-surface cracks in ferromagnetic steels. - Eddy current testing (ECT) → detects surface cracks, thinning, coating thickness. - Ultrasonic testing (UT) → measures remaining wall thickness, detects internal flaws. 3. **Tier 3 — Volumetric / Subsurface NDT** - Radiographic testing (RT) → uses X-rays / γ-rays (Ir-192, Co-60). - Phased Array UT (PAUT) and Time-of-Flight Diffraction (TOFD). - Detects: laminations, voids, sub-surface cracks, wastage. 4. **Tier 4 — Metallographic & Chemical Analysis (Destructive)** - Sectioning, mounting, grinding, polishing, etching. - Optical / SEM microscopy → grain structure, intergranular attack, decarburisation. - EDS / WDS / XRF → elemental composition, corrosion product identification. - XRD → identification of crystalline corrosion products (e.g., FeOOH, Fe₃O₄, β-FeOOH). 5. **Tier 5 — Electrochemical Diagnostic Methods** - Reference electrode potential measurement (E_corr). - Linear Polarisation Resistance (LPR). - Potentiodynamic polarisation (Tafel slopes → i_corr). - Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS). - Electrochemical Noise (EN) analysis. ### 2.2 Why "Identification" is More Than Just Naming the Form Every corrosion form leaves **morphological fingerprints** — shape, location, colour, growth rate, and associated deposits. The engineer must match these fingerprints to a known mechanism: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Mnemonic — "SCALES Mn":** A diagnostic checklist taught in marine metallurgy. > **S** — Shape (pits, cracks, flakes) > **C** — Colour of corrosion product > **A** — Adjacent environment (immersion, splash, atmospheric) > **L** — Location relative to welds, fasteners, joints > **E** — Extent (depth, area fraction) > **S** — Sound (e.g., hydrogen evolution bubbling) > **Mn** — Material involved (carbon steel, Cu-Ni, Al bronze, SS) ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relationship | Meaning | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's 1st Law — mass loss from corrosion current | $\Delta m$ = mass loss [g], $I$ = current [A], $t$ = time [s], $M$ = molar mass [g/mol], $n$ = electrons transferred, $F = \mathbf{96\,485\ C/mol}$ | | 2 | $v_{corr} = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | Corrosion rate from current density | $i_{corr}$ [A/m²], $\rho$ = density [kg/m³], $v_{corr}$ [m/s] | | 3 | $CR\,[mpy] = 0.129 \cdot \dfrac{i_{corr}}{\rho} \cdot \dfrac{M}{n}$ | Empirical mpy (mils per year) conversion | $i_{corr}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$, $\rho$ in g/cm³ | | 4 | $R_p = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}\bigg\vert_{E=E_{corr}}$ | Linear Polarisation Resistance (LPA) | $R_p$ in $\Omega \cdot cm^2$; **inverse proxy for corrosion rate** | | 5 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_p}$ | Stern-Geary equation | $B = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} \approx \mathbf{26\,mV}$ (active) or $\mathbf{52\,mV}$ (passive) | | 6 | $\Delta E_{corr} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Driving voltage for galvanic corrosion | Galvanic Series lookup (mV vs SCE) | | 7 | $Z = Z' - j\,Z''$ | EIS complex impedance | $Z'$ real, $Z''$ imaginary; Nyquist plot | | 8 | $P = \dfrac{d_{max}}{d_{avg}}$ | Pitting factor | $P=1$ → uniform; $P \gg 1$ → severe pitting | > [!NOTE] > The **Faraday constant $F = 96\,485\ C/mol$** and the **Stern-Geary constant $B$** are the two numerical values KTU examiners most frequently expect students to recall in Part A (3-mark) questions. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Naval Architecture The data acquired from identification feeds directly into: - **Classification society rules** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS) — thickness allowances for hull plates. - **Maintenance planning** — inspection intervals based on measured $v_{corr}$. - **Coating and cathodic protection design** — current demand calculation. - **Failure analysis reports** — required for insurance claims and warranty disputes. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Polarisation Resistance (LPA) Method The LPA method is the **cornerstone electrochemical identification tool** in the field — it gives an *instantaneous* corrosion rate without disturbing the system. **Step 1 — Start with the Butler–Volmer equation** governing net current density near $E_{corr}$: $$\begin{aligned} i &= i_{corr}\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c}\right)\right] \end{aligned}$$ where $\eta = E - E_{corr}$ is the **overpotential**, $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$ are the **anodic** and **cathodic Tafel slopes** [V/decade]. **Step 2 — Restrict the polarisation to a small amplitude** ($\vert \eta \vert \le 10\,\text{mV}$), which is the *linear polarisation regime*. Within this narrow window, the exponentials can be replaced by the **first two terms of their Maclaurin series**: $e^x \approx 1 + x$ and $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$. $$\begin{aligned} i &\approx i_{corr}\left[\left(1 + \dfrac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \left(1 - \dfrac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c}\right)\right] \\[6pt] &= i_{corr} \cdot 2.303\,\eta \left(\dfrac{1}{\beta_a} + \dfrac{1}{\beta_c}\right) \\[6pt] &= i_{corr} \cdot 2.303\,\eta \left(\dfrac{\beta_a + \beta_c}{\beta_a\,\beta_c}\right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Define the polarisation resistance** $R_p = \dfrac{\eta}{i}$: $$\begin{aligned} R_p &= \dfrac{\eta}{i_{corr} \cdot 2.303\,\eta \left(\dfrac{\beta_a + \beta_c}{\beta_a\,\beta_c}\right)} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{\beta_a\,\beta_c}{2.303\,i_{corr}\,(\beta_a + \beta_c)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain the Stern–Geary equation**: $$\begin{aligned} i_{corr} &= \dfrac{\beta_a\,\beta_c}{2.303\,R_p\,(\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \dfrac{B}{R_p} \end{aligned}$$ where the **Stern–Geary constant** is: $$\begin{aligned} B &= \dfrac{\beta_a\,\beta_c}{2.303\,(\beta_a + \beta_c)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Convert $i_{corr}$ to mass-loss rate** using Faraday's Law: $$\begin{aligned} v_{corr} &= \dfrac{i_{corr}\,M}{n\,F\,\rho} \end{aligned}$$ **Numerical example (KTU-style):** For a carbon-steel coupon in seawater, suppose an LPA probe measures $R_p = 800\ \Omega\cdot cm^2$. Assume $\beta_a = 0.06\ \text{V/dec}$, $\beta_c = 0.12\ \text{V/dec}$, $n = 2$, $M = 55.85\ \text{g/mol}$ (Fe), $\rho = 7.87\ \text{g/cm}^3$. $$\begin{aligned} B &= \dfrac{0.06 \times 0.12}{2.303 \times (0.06 + 0.12)} = \dfrac{0.0072}{0.4145} \approx 0.01737\ \text{V} \\[6pt] i_{corr} &= \dfrac{0.01737}{800} = 2.17 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{A/cm}^2 = 21.7\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2 \\[6pt] CR\,[mpy] &= 0.129 \times 21.7 \times \dfrac{55.85}{2 \times 7.87} \approx 0.129 \times 21.7 \times 3.549 \approx 9.9\ \text{mpy} \end{aligned}$$ This corresponds to roughly **0.25 mm/year** — a moderate corrosion rate typical of unprotected mild steel in seawater (KTU benchmark: 0.1–1.0 mm/year). --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Pitting Factor (Pitting Severity Metric) The **pitting factor** quantifies how localised — as opposed to uniform — the corrosion attack is: $$\begin{aligned} P &= \dfrac{d_{max}}{d_{uniform}} = \dfrac{d_{max}}{\dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho \cdot A}} \end{aligned}$$ where $d_{max}$ is the deepest pit measured metallographically, and $d_{uniform}$ is the equivalent uniform thickness loss derived from weight loss. **Interpretation table:** | $P$ value | Severity | Typical mechanism | |---|---|---| | $P = 1$ | Uniform | General corrosion | | $1 < P \le 3$ | Mild pitting | Beginnings of breakdown | | $3 < P \le 6$ | Moderate pitting | Crevice / incipient pitting | | $P > 6$ | Severe pitting | Aggressive Cl⁻ / SCC precursor | --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Identification by Visual Fingerprint | Observed Fingerprint | Probable Form | Diagnostic Confirmation | |---|---|---| | Broad, even rust layer | General / uniform corrosion | Weight-loss coupon, LPR | | Discrete, deep, narrow holes with bright interior and dark rim | Pitting corrosion | Metallographic depth, pitting factor $P$ | | Grooves at gasket / joint interfaces | Crevice corrosion | Section through crevice, EDS of Cl⁻ concentration | | Branching "lightning" cracks transgranular, near welds | Stress corrosion cracking (SCC) | Slow Strain Rate Test (SSRT), fractography | | Cracks following grain boundaries | Intergranular corrosion (IGC) | ASTM A262 Practice A/B/E (for austenitic SS) | | Horseshoe-shaped scallops on Cu alloys | Dealloying / selective leaching | EDS of porous Cu-rich sponge | | Wavy, horseshoe-shaped attack aligned with flow | Erosion–corrosion | Flow-loop simulation, surface profile | | Honeycomb on condenser tubes | Impingement attack | Hydrodynamic analysis | | Dark sludge + H₂S smell, tubercle mounds | MIC (SRB activity) | Culture test, DNA analysis | | Fretting debris + red/brown powder at contacts | Fretting corrosion | SEM of oxide debris, contact-pressure analysis | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Five-Tier Identification Pyramid ```mermaid flowchart TB T5["Tier 5: Electrochemical Diagnostics
LPR, Tafel, EIS, E_corr, EN"] T4["Tier 4: Metallography and Chemical
Optical SEM, EDS, XRD, XRF"] T3["Tier 3: Volumetric NDT
RT, PAUT, TOFD"] T2["Tier 2: Surface NDT
LPI DPI, MPI, Eddy Current, UT"] T1["Tier 1: Visual and Macroscopic
Naked eye, borescope, 10x to 50x"] T5 --> T4 --> T3 --> T2 --> T1 T5 -.feedback.-> T1 ``` The pyramid is **inverted in cost and time** as one descends. Tier 1 takes minutes and costs almost nothing; Tier 5 may require days and specialised potentiostats. **Always begin at Tier 1 and only escalate as evidence demands.** ### 4.2 Block Diagram — LPA Probe Set-up for On-board Hull Monitoring ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FIELD ["Field Installation on Ship Hull"] WE["Working Electrode
Steel Coupon"] RE["Reference Electrode
Saturated Calomel SCE"] CE["Counter Electrode
Platinum or Graphite"] SOL["Seawater Electrolyte"] end subgraph POT ["Portable Potentiostat"] SRC["DC Signal Source plus Potentiostat Circuit"] AMP["Current Amplifier"] ADC["ADC plus Microcontroller"] end subgraph OUT ["Output Stage"] DSP["LCD Display of E_corr and i_corr"] LOG["Data Logger SD Card"] ALM["Alarm Threshold Trip"] end WE --- SOL RE --- SOL CE --- SOL WE --> SRC RE --> SRC CE --> AMP AMP --> ADC ADC --> DSP ADC --> LOG ADC --> ALM ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Failure Analysis Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Observed Degradation on Hull Component"] B{"Cracks Visible?"} C{"Surface Deposits or Tubercles?"} D{"Attack Localised at Joints or Crevices?"} E["Conduct MPI or LPI for crack mapping"] F["Conduct SEM fractography and SSRT"] G["XRD plus EDS for corrosion product"] H["Culture test for SRB, MIC evaluation"] I["Section through gasket or weld"] J["Galvanic series check, mask analysis"] K["Metallographic depth measurement"] A --> B B -- Yes --> E B -- No --> C C -- Yes --> G G --> H C -- No --> D D -- Yes --> I I --> J D -- No --> K E --> F ``` ### 4.4 Functional Architecture of a Shipboard Corrosion Identification System ```mermaid flowchart LR SENSE["Sensor Layer
ER probes, LPR, bimetallic sensors"] ACQ["Data Acquisition
Multiplexer plus ADC"] PROC["Processing Unit
Embedded processor"] DECISION["Decision Logic
Threshold alarm and trend"] HM["Human Machine Interface
Bridge display and engine room panel"] STORE["Storage
Cloud plus local SSD"] SENSE --> ACQ --> PROC --> DECISION DECISION --> HM DECISION --> STORE STORE -.feedback to trend AI.-> DECISION ``` > [!NOTE] > The system above is what modern smart-ships (e.g., those complying with **DNV GL Digital Ship** notations) deploy for continuous corrosion identification. KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to *describe* the role of each block, not to design the circuitry. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]** **Q1. (3 marks)** Define **linear polarisation resistance (LPR)** and state the **Stern–Geary equation** that relates it to the corrosion current density. Mention the typical numerical value of the Stern–Geary constant $B$ for an actively corroding system. **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Linear Polarisation Resistance (LPR)* is defined as the slope of the polarisation curve $\dfrac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}$ evaluated at the corrosion potential $E_{corr}$, measured by applying a small (typically $\pm 10\ \text{mV}$) perturbation. It is inversely proportional to the corrosion rate, making it a rapid, non-destructive field tool. $$\begin{aligned} i_{corr} &= \dfrac{B}{R_p}, \qquad B = \dfrac{\beta_a\,\beta_c}{2.303\,(\beta_a + \beta_c)} \end{aligned}$$ For an actively corroding carbon-steel / seawater system, **$B \approx 26\ \text{mV}$**; for passive systems $B \approx 52\ \text{mV}$. *Mark split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for $B$ value.* --- **Q2. (3 marks)** List any **four visual fingerprints** used to differentiate between **pitting corrosion** and **uniform (general) corrosion** during a hull survey. **Model Answer (4 points, any 3×1 mark = 3 marks):** 1. **Pitting** produces *localised, deep cavities* with a polished interior and a dark/brown rim, whereas uniform corrosion is a *broad, even* loss of section. 2. Pitting factors $P = d_{max}/d_{uniform}$ for pitting are typically $\gg 1$, whereas $P \to 1$ for uniform attack. 3. Pitting is often concealed by corrosion *products* covering small orifices; uniform corrosion is openly visible. 4. Pitting preferentially initiates beneath *deposits, barnacles, or under bolts*, while uniform attack proceeds over all wetted surfaces. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > When a question asks to **"describe various methods of corrosion identification"**, students commonly lose marks because they: > (a) Confine their answer to *only* visual inspection — earning at most 4/14. > (b) Fail to mention **electrochemical methods** (LPR / Tafel / EIS) — losing 3–4 marks. > (c) Do not link each method to the **type of information it provides** (rate vs. mechanism vs. extent). Examiners expect a **method → information → limitation** triadic structure. Mentioning limitations earns the additional CO-level mark. --- #### **Question A — 14 Marks** *[Internal Choice Option 1]* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a) (7 marks)** Describe in detail the **non-destructive testing (NDT) methods** employed for corrosion identification in ship hulls and marine structures. State at least two limitations of each method. **(b) (7 marks)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Linear Polarisation Resistance (LPR) technique**. Derive the Stern–Geary equation and compute the corrosion rate in **mm/year** for a steel coupon in seawater given $R_p = 1200\ \Omega\cdot cm^2$, $\beta_a = 0.060\ \text{V/dec}$, $\beta_c = 0.120\ \text{V/dec}$, $M = 55.85\ \text{g/mol}$, $n=2$, $\rho = 7.87\ \text{g/cm}^3$. ($1\ \text{year} = 3.156 \times 10^7\ \text{s}$.) --- **Model Solution for Q-A (a):** **NDT methods for corrosion identification** *(5 methods × ~1.4 marks each; limitation 0.5 mark)*: 1. **Visual Inspection (VI) + Borescope** - *Information:* Surface defects, deposit patterns, blistering, cracks. - *Limitation:* Cannot detect sub-surface flaws; subjective; requires cleanliness. 2. **Liquid Penetrant Inspection (LPI / DPI)** - *Information:* Surface-connected cracks, porosity, laps. - *Limitation:* Only detects flaws breaking the surface; requires smooth, clean surface; no depth information. 3. **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)** - *Information:* Surface and near-surface (< 6 mm) cracks in ferromagnetic materials. - *Limitation:* Ineffective on austenitic stainless steels and non-ferrous alloys; needs demagnetisation post-test. 4. **Eddy Current Testing (ECT)** - *Information:* Surface cracks, thickness measurement, coating thickness, conductivity changes. - *Limitation:* Sensitive to lift-off and probe wobble; limited depth penetration; calibration needed for each alloy. 5. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT) — including thickness gauging** - *Information:* Remaining wall thickness, internal laminations, sub-surface cracks. - *Limitation:* Requires skilled operator; couplant needed; difficult on rough/coated surfaces; "dead zone" near surface. 6. **Radiographic Testing (RT)** - *Information:* Volumetric view of internal flaws, weld porosity, slag inclusions. - *Limitation:* Ionising radiation hazard; expensive; 2-D projection; poor for planar defects oriented parallel to beam. 7. **Phased Array UT (PAUT) and TOFD** - *Information:* Precise sizing and positioning of flaws in 3-D. - *Limitation:* High capital cost; complex calibration. --- **Model Solution for Q-A (b):** **Working sketch:** A two-electrode or three-electrode cell with the steel coupon as **Working Electrode (WE)**, a **Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE)** as Reference, and a Pt counter-electrode; connected to a potentiostat that sweeps $E$ over $\pm 10\ \text{mV}$ around $E_{corr}$ and records current. **Derivation** (see Section 3.1 above for full step-by-step): $$\begin{aligned} R_p &= \dfrac{\beta_a\,\beta_c}{2.303\,i_{corr}\,(\beta_a + \beta_c)} \;\Rightarrow\; i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_p} \end{aligned}$$ **Numerical evaluation:** $$\begin{aligned} B &= \dfrac{0.060 \times 0.120}{2.303 \times (0.060 + 0.120)} = \dfrac{0.00720}{0.41454} = 1.7369 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{V} \\[6pt] i_{corr} &= \dfrac{1.7369 \times 10^{-2}}{1200} = 1.447 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{A/cm}^2 \\[6pt] v_{corr}\ [\text{cm/s}] &= \dfrac{i_{corr}\,M}{n\,F\,\rho} = \dfrac{(1.447\times10^{-5})(55.85)}{(2)(96485)(7.87)} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{8.082 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5187 \times 10^{6}} = 5.321 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{cm/s} \\[6pt] v_{corr}\ [\text{mm/yr}] &= 5.321 \times 10^{-10} \times 10 \times 3.156 \times 10^{7} \\[6pt] &= 0.168\ \text{mm/year} \end{aligned}$$ **Answer: Corrosion rate ≈ 0.17 mm/year** *(considered low–moderate; indicates a partially protective corrosion product layer or active cathodic protection).* --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks** *[Internal Choice Option 2]* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]** **(a) (7 marks)** With the help of a **decision flow-chart**, discuss how an engineer would systematically identify the *type* of corrosion observed on a shipboard component. **(b) (7 marks)** Explain the **principle, procedure, and applications** of **metallographic examination** as a destructive but highly reliable corrosion identification method. List the standard sample preparation steps in order. --- **Model Solution for Q-B (a):** The systematic decision flow-chart *(see Section 4.3 Block Diagram above)* begins with the **observation** of the failure and walks through logical branches: - **Step 1 — Is cracking visible?** - YES → Use **LPI/MPI** to map crack distribution. - Crack pattern dendritic → **SCC**. - Crack follows grain boundaries → **IGC**. - Mechanical overload features (dimpling) → **Pure mechanical fracture**. - **Step 2 — Are deposits/tubercles present?** - YES → Run **XRD/EDS** on the deposit. - Iron sulphides + H₂S smell + anaerobic biofilm → **MIC (SRB)**. - Calcareous deposit (CaCO₃) → **CP-induced, hence protective**. - **Step 3 — Is the attack localised at joints/crevices/gaskets?** - YES → Section through the joint, analyse Cl⁻ enrichment → **Crevice corrosion**. - Check dissimilar metal coupling → **Galvanic corrosion**. - **Step 4 — Is the attack on a Cu-alloy and has a "wood-grain" appearance?** - YES → EDS for Zn / Al depletion → **Dealloying**. - **Step 5 — Surface smooth and uniform?** - YES → **General corrosion** — confirm by coupon weight loss or LPR. *Marks allocation: flowchart 3 marks + 4 branches × 1 mark each = 7 marks.* --- **Model Solution for Q-B (b):** **Principle:** A small specimen is cut from the affected zone, mounted, ground, polished to a mirror finish, and etched. The resulting microstructure is examined under an optical microscope (50×–1000×) or SEM (up to 50,000×) to reveal **grain structure, phase distribution, depth and morphology of attack**, and corrosion-product penetration. **Procedure (8 sequential steps — list any 7):** 1. **Selection** — cut sample from a region representative of the attack; include unaffected base metal. 2. **Sectioning** — use abrasive cut-off wheel with copious coolant to avoid heat-induced alteration. 3. **Mounting** — hot compression mounting in phenolic or epoxy resin (cold-mount for brittle samples). 4. **Grinding** — sequential SiC papers: 120 → 240 → 400 → 600 → 800 → 1200 grit. 5. **Polishing** — diamond paste 6 µm → 1 µm → 0.25 µm on cloth wheels. 6. **Final polishing** — optional colloidal silica (0.05 µm) for SEM/EBSD work. 7. **Etching** — chemical (Nital 2% for carbon steel; Kalling's for SS) or electrolytic. 8. **Examination** — bright-field / dark-field / DIC optical microscopy, then SEM-EDS if needed. **Applications in marine corrosion identification:** - Measuring **pitting depth** and identifying whether pits are crystallographic (faceted) or elliptical. - Detecting **intergranular attack** (sensitisation in austenitic SS — ASTM A262 Practice A). - Identifying **decarburisation, hydrogen damage, exfoliation**. - Confirming **weld HAZ cracking** and **HISC** (hydrogen-induced stress cracking) in subsea pipelines. *Marks split: principle 2 + procedure 3 + applications 2 = 7 marks.* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Corrosion Identification** - ☐ Corrosion identification is a **5-tier pyramid**: Visual → Surface NDT → Volumetric NDT → Metallography/Chemical → Electrochemical. - ☐ Always **start with Tier 1 (visual)** and escalate only when evidence demands it. - ☐ The two **field-electrochemical** techniques you must know cold: **LPR** (gives $i_{corr}$, instantaneous) and **Tafel extrapolation** (gives Tafel slopes). - ☐ Memorise: $\mathbf{F = 96\,485\ C/mol}$ and $\mathbf{B \approx 26\ mV}$ (active) / $\mathbf{B \approx 52\ mV}$ (passive). - ☐ **Stern–Geary equation**: $i_{corr} = B / R_p$ — the *inverse* relationship between polarisation resistance and corrosion rate. - ☐ **Pitting factor** $P = d_{max}/d_{uniform}$ — values $\gg 1$ indicate localised attack; $P = 1$ is uniform. - ☐ Each corrosion form has a **visual fingerprint** — use the **SCALES Mn** mnemonic. - ☐ **NDT tool selection** depends on: material (ferromagnetic? austenitic?), defect type (surface? sub-surface?), and access (one-sided? two-sided?). - ☐ **Metallography is destructive** but gives the **definitive** answer on grain-boundary attack, pitting depth, and phase identification. - ☐ **MIC** is identified by **black iron-sulphide deposits + H₂S odour + biofilm** — confirmed by microbiological culture / DNA. - ☐ Always convert $i_{corr}$ to a **practical engineering rate** (mm/year) before reporting; classification societies demand mm/yr. - ☐ **Galvanic corrosion** identification requires reference to the **galvanic series in seawater**, not the standard EMF series. - ☐ In shipboard service, the **most commonly missed** identification is **crevice corrosion** under pad-eyes, flanges, and beneath marine growth — always section through such regions. ---

topics.title: Factors affecting corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors affecting corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> **Factors affecting corrosion** are the intrinsic (metallurgical) and extrinsic (environmental, mechanical, and design) parameters that alter the **thermodynamic tendency** and the **kinetic rate** at which a metal undergoes anodic dissolution in a given electrolyte. Mathematically, they shift the **mixed (corrosion) potential ($E_{corr}$)** and/or the **corrosion current density ($i_{corr}$)** as defined by the **Evans diagram** and quantified through **Faraday's law of electrolysis**.

In the context of **shipbuilding materials (PENST414)**, these factors are critical because a marine vessel operates in one of the most aggressive corrosion environments on Earth — the **saline, aerated, chloride-rich, biologically active seawater medium** — while simultaneously being subjected to mechanical stresses, heat from welding, and galvanic contact between dissimilar hull alloys.

## 1.2 The Three Primary Classes of Corrosion Factors

| Class | Nature | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Intrinsic / Metallurgical** | Built into the metal | Composition, purity, grain size, microstructure, surface condition, oxide film, cold work, heat treatment |
| **Extrinsic / Environmental** | Imposed by service medium | pH, dissolved $O_2$, temperature, salinity, fluid velocity, conductivity, aggressive ions, biological activity |
| **Mechanical / Design** | Imposed by stress and geometry | Stray currents, applied/cyclic loads, galvanic couples, crevices, heat-affected zones from welding |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Corrosion Triangle"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Think of corrosion like **human illness**.
> 1. **Genetics of the Patient** → *Intrinsic metal factors* (purity, microstructure).
> 2. **The Surrounding Environment** → *Extrinsic factors* (seawater, humidity, pH).
> 3. **Lifestyle & Stress** → *Mechanical and design factors* (welding, loads, galvanic joints).
>
> Just as a person with weak genetics living in a polluted city under chronic stress falls sick faster, a **welded low-purity carbon-steel hull plate in warm, aerated, chloride-laden tropical seawater** corrodes rapidly — even though none of the factors alone would be catastrophic.

## 1.4 The Thermodynamic Foundation

The fundamental driver is the **Gibbs free energy change** of the oxidation–reduction half-reactions:

$$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$$

- A **negative** $\Delta G$ means corrosion is **spontaneous** (thermodynamically favourable).
- However, the **rate** is governed by kinetics, exchange current densities, and the **Tafel slopes** ($\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$).

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Gold:** A question will often test whether a student can distinguish between *thermodynamic tendency* (does corrosion occur?) and *kinetic rate* (how fast does it occur?). Many factors shift one but not the other.

## 1.5 The Mixed-Potential Concept (Evans Diagram — Preview)

When a metal M corrodes in acid, two simultaneous half-reactions occur on its surface:

$$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^- \quad \text{(anodic, oxidation)}$$

$$2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 \uparrow \quad \text{(cathodic, reduction)}$$

These half-reactions polarize each other until they reach a common **mixed (corrosion) potential $E_{corr}$** at which the **anodic current equals the cathodic current ($i_a = i_c = i_{corr}$)**. **Every factor in this module essentially displaces $E_{corr}$ or $i_{corr}$** on this diagram.

---

## 1.6 Why This Topic Matters in Shipbuilding

Ships use **a wide variety of metals in electrical contact**: mild steel hulls, copper-nickel piping, aluminum superstructures, bronze propellers, stainless-steel fasteners, and zinc sacrificial anodes. Add **seawater (3.5% NaCl equivalent)** as the electrolyte, **stray DC currents** from cathodic-protection systems and shipboard electronics, and **mechanical loads from waves**, and you have a textbook environment for almost *every* corrosion mechanism in the curriculum.

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Factors

### 2.1.1 Metallurgical (Intrinsic) Factors

**A. Purity of the Metal**
- Impurities act as **local cathodes**, creating micro-galvanic cells.
- Higher purity → fewer cathodic sites → **lower corrosion rate**.
- Example: 99.99% pure Zn in $H_2SO_4$ corrodes **very slowly**; commercial Zn (with Fe/Pb impurities) corrodes **rapidly**.

**B. Physical State and Grain Size**
- **Smaller grain size** → more grain boundaries → more anodic sites → higher corrosion rate in many environments.
- Cold-worked metals have **higher internal energy (stored strain energy)** and are thermodynamically more reactive than annealed metals.
- Dislocations and slip bands emerge at the surface and act as **anodic initiation sites**.

**C. Heat Treatment and Microstructure**
- Different phases have different electrode potentials.
- Example: In **pearlitic steel**, ferrite ($\alpha$-Fe) is anodic relative to cementite ($Fe_3C$). The $Fe_3C$ lamellae act as **distributed cathodes**, accelerating corrosion of the surrounding ferrite.
- Sensitization in **austenitic SS 304** (Cr-depleted zones at grain boundaries) → **intergranular corrosion**.

**D. Surface Condition**
- Rough surfaces trap moisture, salts, and pollutants → differential aeration.
- Polished surfaces have **more uniform passive films** and corrode more uniformly.
- Pre-formed **mill scale ($Fe_3O_4$/$Fe_2O_3$)** is cathodic relative to steel beneath → pitting once scale cracks.

**E. Oxide Films and Passivity**
- A continuous, adherent, low-porosity oxide film **blocks anodic dissolution**.
- Quantified by the **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** (see Section 3 derivation).

**F. Position in the Galvanic Series**
- Metals higher (more active) in the series → **more anodic** → corrode preferentially.
- Critical for **bimetallic joints** in ships (e.g., Cu-Ni pipes bolted to steel hull).

**G. Relative Cathode-to-Anode Area**
- **Small anode : Large cathode** is the **most dangerous** configuration.
- Cathodic current is spread over a large area (low $i_c$); anodic current is concentrated (very high $i_a$) → **deep, rapid pitting**.
- Ship rule: **riveted steel plates with bronze rivets** → steel is small anode, bronze is large cathode → severe attack around rivets (historical lesson from riveted ship hulls).

### 2.1.2 Environmental (Extrinsic) Factors

**A. Temperature**
- Increases ionic mobility and reaction kinetics.
- Approximately **doubles the corrosion rate for every 10 °C rise** in many systems (Arrhenius behaviour).
- Exception: In **oxygen-limited** systems, higher T → **lower** dissolved $O_2$ → corrosion may *decrease* (e.g., closed hot-water tanks).

**B. Humidity and Moisture**
- Electrolyte film forms only above the **critical humidity** (≈ **60–70% RH** for many metals).
- Below this, corrosion is negligible.

**C. Effect of pH**
- For amphoteric metals (Al, Zn, Pb): corrosion is **high in both acid and alkali** (V-shaped curve).
- For Fe: corrosion is high in **acid** (cathodic $H^+$ reduction), low in **neutral**, and a stable passive film forms in **strongly alkaline** (pH > 10).
- The **Pourbaix diagram** (potential vs pH) is the authoritative map for this factor.

**D. Dissolved Oxygen**
- Acts as the **primary cathodic depolarizer** in neutral/alkaline aerated media:

$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^- \quad (E^\circ = +0.401 \text{ V})$$

- More $O_2$ → higher cathodic current → **faster corrosion** (in neutral/alkaline media).
- This is the cause of **differential aeration corrosion** (waterline attack on ships).

**E. Conductivity of the Medium**
- Higher conductivity (e.g., seawater ≈ 50,000 $\mu S/cm$) → easier ionic current flow → **larger galvanic currents** → faster corrosion.
- Distilled water → very low conductivity → negligible corrosion despite being thermodynamically reactive.

**F. Velocity of Fluid Flow**
- **Low velocity** → stagnant → differential aeration, crevice, and MIC.
- **Moderate velocity** → uniform supply of $O_2$ → uniform corrosion.
- **High velocity** → mechanical removal of protective film → **erosion–corrosion**, **impingement attack**, **cavitation**.

**G. Aggressive Ions (Halides, especially $Cl^-$)**
- $Cl^-$ ions are small, highly mobile, and **aggressively penetrate passive films** (especially on stainless steel and Al).
- They cause **pitting corrosion** and **stress-corrosion cracking** in marine environments.
- Ship hulls in tropical waters face chloride concentrations up to **≈ 19,000 ppm** in the open ocean and higher in brackish estuaries.

**H. Biological / Microbial Factors (MIC)**
- Sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) in anaerobic sediments under hull plates produce $H_2S$ → **severe localized corrosion**.
- Biofouling creates differential aeration cells.

**I. Concentration Cells**
- **Differential aeration cell** —$O_2$-rich area is cathodic, $O_2$-starved area is anodic (e.g., under a barnacle, under a deposit, inside a crevice).
- **Salt-concentration cell** — lower-salt region is anodic (e.g., metal half-immersed in brine).

### 2.1.3 Mechanical and Design Factors

**A. Stray Currents**
- DC leakage from shipboard welding machines, impressed-current cathodic protection, or shore-power grounding can flow through the hull and discharge into seawater, causing severe localized attack at the discharge (anodic) site.
- Mitigation: **dielectric breaks** in piping, **isolated grounding**, **bonding straps**.

**B. Stress and Strain**
- **Tensile stress + corrosive medium** → **stress-corrosion cracking (SCC)**.
- **Cyclic stress + corrosion** → **corrosion fatigue**.
- Welded joints with residual stress are classic SCC sites.

**C. Design Geometry**
- Sharp corners, crevices at gasket joints, dead-end pipes, and water traps all create differential aeration and deposit attack.

---

## 2.2 ⚡ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these equations.** They appear in 80% of numerical Part B questions on this module.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot E.W.}{96500}$ | $W$=mass corroded (g), $I$=current (A), $t$=time (s), $M$=molar mass, $n$=valency, $F$=96500 C/mol, $E.W.$=equivalent weight | Faraday's law — convert measured corrosion current to mass loss |
| 2 | $CR \,(\text{mpy}) = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | $W$=weight loss (mg), $D$=density (g/cm³), $A$=area (in²), $T$=time (hr) | Corrosion rate in **mils per year** (ASTM standard) |
| 3 | $CR \,(\text{mm/yr}) = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | $W$ in mg, $D$ in g/cm³, $A$ in cm², $T$ in hr | Corrosion rate in **mm/year** (SI) |
| 4 | $CR \,(\text{mdd}) = \dfrac{W \cdot 24}{A_{dm^2} \cdot T_{hr}}$ | $A$ in dm², $T$ in hours, $W$ in mg | Corrosion rate in **mg per dm² per day** |
| 5 | $E = E^\circ + \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log \dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$ (at 25 °C) | $E$=half-cell potential, $E^\circ$=standard potential, $n$=electrons transferred | **Nernst equation** — equilibrium electrode potential under non-standard conditions |
| 6 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot R_p \cdot (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | $\beta_a,\beta_c$=Tafel slopes, $R_p$=polarization resistance ($\Omega$) | **Stern–Geary equation** — obtain $i_{corr}$ from $R_p$ measurement |
| 7 | $\text{PBR} = \dfrac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \dfrac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}$ | $V$=molar volume, $M$=molar mass, $\rho$=density, $n$=metal atoms per oxide formula | **Pilling–Bedworth ratio** — predicts protectiveness of oxide film |
| 8 | $\text{Cathode Efficiency} = \dfrac{I_{\text{used for }H^+/O_2\text{ reduction}}}{I_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | — | Cathodic protection design |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{10\text{°C}} \Rightarrow \text{Rate} \times 2$ (rule of thumb) | — | Arrhenius doubling for activation-controlled corrosion |
| 10 | $E_{cell} = E_c - E_a$ | $E_c$=cathode potential, $E_a$=anode potential (both in SHE scale) | Predicts galvanic-corrosion driving force |

> [!TIP]
> **Note for tables:** All absolute-value bars (e.g., $\vert n \vert$) are written with `\vert` so they never break the markdown table pipe syntax.

---

## 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Factor — Engineering Utility

**1. Why does the cathode-to-anode area ratio matter?**
Because **current density** ($i = I/A$) is what controls dissolution rate, not the total current. A small anodic area forced to carry the same total current has a *huge* $i_a$ → **pits grow extremely fast** (mm/year to mm/month).

**2. Why does $Cl^-$ cause pitting?**
$Cl^-$ adsorbs competitively on passive films, displaces $O^{2-}$, forms soluble complexes (e.g., $AlCl_3$, $FeCl_3$), and locally depassivates the film → autocatalytic pit growth.

**3. Why does the Pilling–Bedworth ratio predict protectiveness?**
If the oxide occupies **less volume** than the metal it replaces (PBR < 1), the film is **porous and non-protective**. If it occupies **too much** (PBR > 2), compressive stresses cause the film to **spall and crack**. A PBR in **1 to 2** gives a **protective, adherent film** — this is the design rule of nature for Al, Cr, Ti.

**4. Why is seawater so aggressive?**
Three reasons: **(a) high $Cl^-$** pits passive films; **(b) high conductivity** enables large galvanic currents; **(c) dissolved $O_2$ ≈ 6–8 ppm** supports cathodic reaction indefinitely.

**5. Why does a riveted bronze-to-steel hull fail at the rivet?**
Bronze is the **cathode** (noble), steel is the **anode** (active), and the small rivet area surrounded by large steel plates (or vice-versa for steel rivets in bronze plates) creates a dangerous area ratio plus crevice → rapid, deep attack.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Faraday's Law of Corrosion (Mass Loss from Current)

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting principle:** By definition of the **faraday (F)**, **1 mole of electrons (96,485 C)** liberates **1 gram-equivalent of any element** at an electrode.

**Step 1 — Charge passed in time $t$ at current $I$:**

$$Q = I \cdot t \quad \text{(Coulombs)}$$

**Step 2 — Number of moles of electrons passed:**

$$n_{e^-} = \dfrac{Q}{F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

**Step 3 — Each atom of metal M with valency $n$ requires $n$ electrons to become $M^{n+}$. So 1 mole of metal needs $n$ moles of electrons.**

$$\text{Moles of metal dissolved} = \dfrac{n_{e^-}}{n} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of metal dissolved = moles × atomic mass:**

$$W = \left(\dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}\right) \cdot M = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 5 — Define Equivalent Weight $E.W. = M/n$:**

$$\boxed{\,W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot E.W.}{F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot E.W.}{96500}\,}$$

This is **Faraday's Law** applied to corrosion.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Corrosion Rate in mpy (Mils per Year)

**Step 1 — Convert $W$ (g) to mg, area (cm²) to in²:**

$$1 \text{ g} = 1000 \text{ mg}, \quad 1 \text{ in}^2 = 6.4516 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Corrosion rate as depth loss per unit time. Volume loss:**

$$V = \dfrac{W}{D} \quad \text{(cm}^3\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Depth loss (one-sided exposure):**

$$d = \dfrac{V}{A} \quad \text{(cm)}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mils and years. 1 mil = $2.54 \times 10^{-3}$ cm, 1 year = 8760 hr (for 365 days):**

$$CR\,(\text{mpy}) = \dfrac{d\,(\text{cm})}{2.54 \times 10^{-3}} \times \dfrac{1}{T\,(\text{hr})} \times 8760$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $d = W/(D \cdot A)$ and combine constants:**

$$CR = \dfrac{W \cdot 8760}{D \cdot A \cdot T \cdot 2.54 \times 10^{-3}}$$

The numerical constant evaluates to:

$$\dfrac{8760}{2.54 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 3.449 \times 10^{6}$$

Re-checking with unit conversions (W in mg, A in in², T in hr) collapses the constant to **534**:

$$\boxed{\,CR\,(\text{mpy}) = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}\,}$$

where $W$ is in **mg**, $D$ in **g/cm³**, $A$ in **in²**, $T$ in **hours**.

Similarly, for SI mm/yr, the constant is **87.6** (with $A$ in cm², $W$ in mg).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Nernst Equation

**Step 1 — Start from the Gibbs free energy of the half-cell reaction $aA + ne^- \rightleftharpoons bB$:**

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln \left(\dfrac{[B]^b}{[A]^a}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substituting $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$:**

$$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln \left(\dfrac{[B]^b}{[A]^a}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $E$:**

$$E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left(\dfrac{[B]^b}{[A]^a}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Convert $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ using $\ln x = 2.303 \log x$:**

$$E = E^\circ - \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log \left(\dfrac{[B]^b}{[A]^a}\right)$$

**Step 5 — At standard temperature $T = 298 \text{ K}$ (25 °C), substitute $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K and $F = 96500$ C/mol:**

$$\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96500} = 0.0591 \text{ V}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log \left(\dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}\right)\,}$$

> **Engineering utility:** Predicts how the **half-cell potential shifts** with ion concentration — essential for **concentration-cell corrosion** and **Pourbaix-diagram construction**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)

**Step 1 — Oxide layer: $2M + \tfrac{n}{2} O_2 \rightarrow M_2O_n$ (per mole basis).** One mole of metal $M$ (molar mass $M_m$, density $\rho_m$) yields $\tfrac{1}{2}$ mole of oxide $M_2O_n$ (molar mass $M_{ox}$, density $\rho_{ox}$).

**Step 2 — Volume of metal consumed per mole:**

$$V_{metal} = \dfrac{M_m}{\rho_m}$$

**Step 3 — Volume of oxide produced per mole of metal:**

$$V_{oxide} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{M_{ox}}{\rho_{ox}}$$

**Step 4 — The PBR is the ratio:**

$$\text{PBR} = \dfrac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \dfrac{M_{ox} \cdot \rho_m}{2 \cdot M_m \cdot \rho_{ox}}$$

In the generalized form for any oxide $M_a O_b$:

$$\boxed{\,\text{PBR} = \dfrac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{a \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}\,}$$

**Interpretation Table (HIGH-YIELD for exams):**

| PBR Range | Film Behaviour | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **PBR < 1** | Tensile stress → porous, **non-protective** | Mg, Ca, alkali metals |
| **1 ≤ PBR ≤ 2** | Compressive stress, adherent → **protective** | Al, Cr, Ti, Cu, Ni |
| **PBR > 2** | Excessive compressive stress → **cracks, spalls** | Fe, Mo, W oxides |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step)

> **Problem:** A mild-steel plate ($Fe$, $E.W. = 27.92$, $D = 7.86$ g/cm³) of area **10 cm²** is exposed to seawater. After **30 days**, the **weight loss is 220 mg**. Compute (a) corrosion rate in mpy, (b) corrosion rate in mm/yr, (c) average corrosion current $I_{corr}$.

**(a) Corrosion rate in mpy:**

Convert area to in²: $A = 10 / 6.4516 = 1.55$ in². Time: $T = 30 \times 24 = 720$ hr.

$$CR = \dfrac{534 \times 220}{7.86 \times 1.55 \times 720} = \dfrac{117{,}480}{8768.4} \approx 13.4 \text{ mpy}$$

**(b) Corrosion rate in mm/yr (keeping $A = 10$ cm²):**

$$CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times 220}{7.86 \times 10 \times 720} = \dfrac{19{,}272}{56{,}592} \approx 0.34 \text{ mm/yr}$$

**(c) Average corrosion current:**

First convert $T$ to seconds: $t = 720 \times 3600 = 2.592 \times 10^{6}$ s.

$$I = \dfrac{W \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot t} = \dfrac{0.220 \times 2 \times 96500}{55.85 \times 2.592 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$I = \dfrac{42{,}460}{1.4477 \times 10^{8}} \approx 2.93 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A} = 0.293 \text{ mA}$$

Corresponds to $i_{corr} = I/A = 0.293 \text{ mA} / (10 \text{ cm}^2) = 29.3 \text{ } \mu A/cm^2$.

> [!TIP]
> **Board valuation tip:** Show unit conversion at *every* step. Half the marks are lost when students skip from "10 cm²" to "1.55 in²" without writing the conversion.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Factor Dominates?"

| Service Environment | Dominant Corrosion Factor | Critical Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| **Marine immersion (ship hull)** | $Cl^-$ pitting + differential aeration + bio-fouling | $Cl^-$ > 19,000 ppm, biofilm present |
| **Splash zone (boot-top)** | Wet-dry cycling + $O_2$ differential + UV degradation of coating | — |
| **Engine room (hot)** | Temperature + $Cl^-$ + galvanic (Cu-Ni piping) | $T > 60$ °C, $Cl^-$ high |
| **Bilge / tank bottom** | MIC (SRB), deposit attack, low $O_2$ | sulfate + anaerobic sediment |
| **Propeller (Cu-alloy in seawater)** | Velocity + cavitation + galvanic (to steel shaft) | velocity > 3 m/s |
