# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Explain the effect of cathode-to-anode area ratio on the rate of galvanic corrosion."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a galvanic couple, the total anodic and cathodic currents must be equal ($I_a = I_c$). If the cathodic area $A_c$ is much larger than the anodic area $A_a$, then the **anodic current density** $i_a = I/A_a$ becomes very large while the **cathodic current density** $i_c = I/A_c$ remains small. Since the **corrosion rate (mm/yr)** is proportional to $i_a$, the small anodic member suffers **intense, deep, localized attack**.

**Shipbuilding example:** A **steel hull plate** coupled to a **large bronze seacock** has a small cathode-area-to-large-anode-area ratio for the seacock fitting. This is acceptable because the steel (large anode) corrodes **slowly and uniformly**. But if the geometry is reversed (e.g., **steel fasteners in a bronze plate**), the **small steel anode** carries all the anodic current and is rapidly destroyed. This is the design rule: **avoid small-anode-to-large-cathode configurations**.

> **Mark split:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for current-density argument, 1 mark for ship example.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**"Differentiate between differential aeration corrosion and galvanic corrosion."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Galvanic Corrosion | Differential Aeration Corrosion |
|---|---|---|
| **Driving force** | Difference in **electrode potential** of two *different metals* | Difference in **dissolved $O_2$ concentration** on the *same metal* |
| **Cathode** | More noble metal | $O_2$-rich area |
| **Anode** | More active metal | $O_2$-starved area |
| **Example** | Steel-bronze coupling in seawater | Crevice under a bolt head, deposit under a barnacle, waterline attack |
| **Cathodic reaction** | $O_2$ reduction on the noble metal | $O_2$ reduction at the well-aerated face |

> **Mark split:** 1 mark driving force, 1 mark mechanism, 1 mark example.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: each Part B has **internal choice** between two questions of equal marks. Below are two parallel 14-mark questions, each split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the various metallurgical factors affecting the corrosion of a metal. How does the relative cathode-to-anode area ratio influence the corrosion rate in a bimetallic assembly used aboard a ship?*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A copper-nickel (90:10) pipe of surface area 800 cm² carries seawater. After 60 days the weight loss is 1.4 g. Calculate (i) the corrosion rate in **mpy** and **mm/yr**, and (ii) the average corrosion current $I_{corr}$ in mA. (Given: density of Cu-Ni = 8.94 g/cm³, atomic mass = 63.55 + 58.69 average, equivalent weight ≈ 31.77, valency ≈ 2.)*

---

### **Model Solution — Q.A(a)**

**Metallurgical Factors:**

1. **Purity** – impurities act as local cathodes → micro-galvanic cells. Example: impure Zn vs pure Zn in $H_2SO_4$.
2. **Position in galvanic series** – the more active (anodic) metal corrodes when coupled to a nobler metal.
3. **Grain size & cold work** – fine grains and dislocations increase free energy → enhanced reactivity. Cold-worked regions are anodic to annealed regions.
4. **Heat treatment / microstructure** – multi-phase alloys (e.g., pearlite) have phases of different potential. Sensitized austenitic SS 304 has Cr-depleted grain boundaries → IGC.
5. **Surface condition** – roughness traps moisture; mill scale is cathodic → pitting at cracks in the scale.
6. **Passivity / oxide films** – PBR rule: PBR between 1 and 2 gives protective film (Al, Cr, Ti); PBR < 1 or > 2 gives porous or spalling film.
7. **Cathode-to-anode area ratio** – the **most important** factor for ship structures.

**Area-Ratio Argument in a Bimetallic Ship Assembly:**
Consider a **bronze propeller** coupled to a **steel tail-shaft** in seawater. Here, steel is the **small anode** and bronze is the **large cathode** — a worst-case configuration. The anodic current density $i_a$ on the steel becomes very large → **rapid localized wastage at the tail-shaft taper** (a documented failure mode). The remedy is to introduce a **zinc or aluminium sacrificial anode** at the coupling, which converts the local galvanic cell into a *protected* system.

---

### **Model Solution — Q.A(b)**

**Given:** $A = 800$ cm², $W = 1.4$ g = 1400 mg, $T = 60 \times 24 = 1440$ hr, $D = 8.94$ g/cm³, $E.W. = 31.77$ g/eq, $n = 2$.

**(i) Corrosion rate in mpy:**

Convert area to in²: $A = 800 / 6.4516 = 124.0$ in².

$$CR = \dfrac{534 \times 1400}{8.94 \times 124.0 \times 1440} = \dfrac{747{,}600}{1{,}594{,}358} \approx 0.469 \text{ mpy}$$

**Corrosion rate in mm/yr** (use $A = 800$ cm²):

$$CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times 1400}{8.94 \times 800 \times 1440} = \dfrac{122{,}640}{10{,}298{,}880} \approx 0.0119 \text{ mm/yr}$$

**(ii) Average corrosion current:**

Time in seconds: $t = 1440 \times 3600 = 5.184 \times 10^6$ s.

$$I = \dfrac{W \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot t} = \dfrac{1.4 \times 2 \times 96500}{63.55 \times 5.184 \times 10^6}$$

$$I = \dfrac{270{,}200}{3.294 \times 10^{8}} \approx 8.20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A} = 0.820 \text{ mA}$$

**Corrosion current density** $i_{corr} = I/A = 0.820 \text{ mA} / 800 \text{ cm}^2 \approx 1.025 \text{ } \mu A/cm^2$.

This is a **low corrosion rate**, consistent with the excellent marine performance of 90:10 Cu-Ni.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2022 / Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Discuss the environmental factors that govern the corrosion rate of mild steel in seawater. With a neat sketch, explain the mechanism of **differential aeration corrosion** observed at the waterline of a ship hull.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *The corrosion current $i_{corr}$ of a steel specimen in seawater is measured by the **linear polarization technique** as $i_{corr} = 10 \text{ } \mu A/cm^2$. Estimate the **corrosion rate in mpy** and the **weight loss in mg per cm² per day (mdd)**. (Given: density of steel = 7.86 g/cm³, equivalent weight of Fe = 27.92, valency = 2, 1 A·s = 1 C, 1 day = 86400 s.)*

---

### **Model Solution — Q.B(a)**

**Environmental factors for steel in seawater:**

1. **Dissolved $O_2$** – main cathodic depolarizer; $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$. Seawater holds 6–8 ppm $O_2$ at 25 °C.
2. **Chloride concentration** – $Cl^-$ ions depassivate the otherwise protective oxide film; even mild steel (which has no true passive film) is attacked more aggressively in brine than in pure water because $Cl^-$ accelerates the anodic dissolution step.
3. **Conductivity** – seawater conductivity ≈ 50,000 $\mu S/cm$, ~1000× that of fresh water. Higher conductivity → larger galvanic currents.
4. **Temperature** – Arrhenius doubling per 10 °C; but in *open* seawater, dissolved $O_2$ decreases with $T$, partially offsetting the acceleration.
5. **pH** – seawater pH ≈ 8.2 (slightly alkaline). At this pH, the iron-oxide/hydroxide film is moderately protective, but in *acidic* polluted harbours (pH < 6) the rate rises sharply.
6. **Fluid velocity** – moderate flow supplies $O_2$ → uniform corrosion; high flow → erosion; very low flow → deposits, MIC.
7. **Biological activity** – SRB in sediments → pitting under hull.
8. **Concentration cells** – $O_2$ differential, salt differential, temperature differential.

**Differential aeration cell at the waterline** (already shown in Section 4.3 — reproduce in the answer):

- The **above-water** region is in contact with **fully aerated splash** → high $O_2$ → acts as **cathode**.
- The **just-below-waterline** region is sheltered by surface tension / oil film → low $O_2$ → acts as **anode**.
- Electrons flow from the anodic zone (just below waterline) to the cathodic zone (splash zone) through the steel.
- Result: **a characteristic horizontal "rust streak"** at and just below the waterline — the classic **boot-top corrosion** of ship's hulls.

---

### **Model Solution — Q.B(b)**

**Given:** $i_{corr} = 10 \text{ } \mu A/cm^2 = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ A/cm², $D = 7.86$ g/cm³, $E.W. = 27.92$, $n = 2$, $F = 96500$ C/mol.

**Step 1 — Mass loss rate (g/s) per cm² using Faraday's law, rearranged for rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot E.W.}{F} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 27.92}{96500}$$

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{2.792 \times 10^{-4}}{96500} \approx 2.89 \times 10^{-9} \text{ g/s per cm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert to mg per cm² per day (mdd):**

$$\dot{m}_{day} = 2.89 \times 10^{-9} \times 86400 \times 1000 = 0.250 \text{ mdd}$$

**Step 3 — Corrosion rate in mpy:**

$$CR\,(\text{mpy}) = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$$

For 1 cm² in 1 day (24 hr): $W = 0.250 \times 10^{-3}$ g = 0.000250 g, $A = 1$ cm² → 0.155 in², $T = 24$ hr.

$$CR = \dfrac{534 \times 0.250}{7.86 \times 0.155 \times 24} = \dfrac{133.5}{29.24} \approx 4.57 \text{ mpy}$$

**Cross-check using the Faraday shortcut** (a useful board-exam trick):

$$CR\,(\text{mm/yr}) = 0.00327 \times \dfrac{i_{corr} \times E.W.}{D}$$

$$CR = 0.00327 \times \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 27.92}{7.86} = 0.00327 \times 3.55 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$CR \approx 1.16 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m/s} \times 3.154 \times 10^7 \text{ s/yr} \approx 0.116 \text{ mm/yr}$$

Convert to mpy: $0.116 / 0.0254 = 4.57$ mpy ✓ **Matches.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic**
>
> 1. **Confusing mpy with mm/yr.** 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr, NOT 1 mm/yr. Many students write 4.57 mpy as 4.57 mm/yr and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert cm² to in²** in the mpy formula. Using $A$ in cm² gives a 6.45× error.
> 3. **Treating pitting rate as uniform rate.** Pitting rate (mm/yr at the deepest pit) is **3–10×** the uniform rate. Specify the basis — "uniform corrosion rate" or "maximum pit depth rate".
> 4. **Mixing up Faraday's law signs.** Mass loss is at the **anode**; current direction is *into* the electrolyte *from* the anode. Getting this wrong loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the Nernst derivation temperature constant.** 0.0591 V applies **only at 25 °C**; state this explicitly. Examiners deduct for using 0.0591 at a different temperature.
> 6. **Ignoring units of $R_p$ (polarization resistance).** Stern–Geary needs $R_p$ in $\Omega \cdot cm^2$ per unit area — not bare ohms.
> 7. **Forgetting that PBR > 2 means spalling**, not better protection. A common but costly mistake.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this page the night before the exam.**

**🟦 Fundamentals**
- ☐ Corrosion occurs when $\Delta G < 0$ (thermodynamic tendency); rate is governed by kinetics (Tafel/Stern–Geary).
- ☐ At $E_{corr}$: $i_a = i_c = i_{corr}$ — this is the starting point of every quantitative question.
- ☐ Faraday's law: $W = It \cdot E.W. / 96500$.
- ☐ mpy formula: $CR = 534 W / (D A T)$ with $W$ in mg, $A$ in in², $T$ in hr.
- ☐ mm/yr formula: $CR = 87.6 W / (D A T)$ with $A$ in cm².
- ☐ Nernst constant = 0.0591 V at 25 °C only.

**🟧 Metallurgical Factors**
- ☐ Purity ↑ → corrosion rate ↓.
- ☐ Cold work and fine grains → higher free energy → higher reactivity.
- ☐ **Small anode : large cathode** → most dangerous configuration (pitting).
- ☐ **PBR 1–2** → protective film (Al, Cr, Ti, Cu, Ni).
- ☐ **PBR < 1** → porous film (Mg, alkali metals).
- ☐ **PBR > 2** → spalling film (Fe, Mo, W).

**🟩 Environmental Factors**
- ☐ $Cl^-$ depassivates → pitting, SCC.
- ☐ $O_2$ is the **main cathodic depolarizer** in neutral/alkaline media.
- ☐ Seawater: high $Cl^-$ + high conductivity + dissolved $O_2$ → most aggressive natural medium.
- ☐ Temperature ↑ 10 °C → rate ≈ doubles (activation-controlled).
- ☐ Critical humidity ≈ 60–70% RH for atmospheric corrosion.
- ☐ Differential aeration → $O_2$-rich = cathode, $O_2$-starved = anode.
- ☐ Velocity: low → differential aeration + MIC; moderate → uniform; high → erosion/cavitation.
- ☐ MIC: SRB produce $H_2S$ → pitting in anaerobic sediments.

**🟥 Design & Mechanical Factors**
- ☐ Avoid galvanic couples; if unavoidable, choose **area ratios favorable to the structural member** (large anode, small cathode).
- ☐ Provide **dielectric breaks** in piping to block stray currents.
- ☐ Avoid crevices, sharp corners, and dead-ends in design.
- ☐ SCC = tensile stress + specific medium (e.g., chlorides for SS, caustics for C-steel).
- ☐ Welded zones have residual tensile stress → susceptible to SCC and corrosion fatigue.

**🟪 Shipbuilding-Specific Call-Outs**
- ☐ **Boot-top / waterline** → differential aeration.
- ☐ **Bronze propeller + steel shaft** → galvanic → use zinc/Al sacrificial anode.
- ☐ **Bilge** → SRB-MIC.
- ☐ **Engine room** → high T + $Cl^-$ + galvanic.
- ☐ **90:10 Cu-Ni piping** → corrosion rate < 1 mpy in clean seawater (excellent).

**🟫 Examiner Hot-Spots (Frequently Asked)**
- ☐ Pilling–Bedworth ratio – derive, classify, give examples.
- ☐ Nernst equation – derive, apply to a concentration cell.
- ☐ Faraday's law – numerical with unit conversion.
- ☐ Area ratio effect – with a ship example.
- ☐ Differential aeration – diagram + waterline application.
- ☐ Effect of pH on Fe, Al, Zn (amphoteric vs non-amphoteric) using Pourbaix logic.
- ☐ Effect of velocity on corrosion mechanism.

> **Best of luck with your KTU exam! 🚢⚓**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of steel and tendency of corrosion in various types of steel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of steel and tendency of corrosion in various types of steel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Steel? (KTU Formal Definition)

**Steel** is an iron-based alloy containing iron (Fe) as the primary base metal, combined with carbon (C) — typically **0.02% to 2.14% by weight** — along with controlled amounts of alloying elements such as manganese (Mn), silicon (Si), chromium (Cr), nickel (Ni), molybdenum (Mo), and vanadium (V). The carbon acts as the principal hardening agent, while the alloying elements govern specific mechanical, chemical, and electrochemical properties including corrosion resistance, weldability, and toughness.

In the **shipbuilding context (KTU PENST414)**, steels are classified by the classification societies such as **American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)**, **Lloyd's Register (LR)**, and **Det Norske Veritas (DNV)**. Common grades include:

- Ordinary strength: **Grade A, B, D, E**
- Higher strength: **AH36, DH36, EH36, AH40, DH40, EH40**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 Scheme for PENST414, students must be able to **classify steels based on chemical composition, microstructure, and mechanical properties**, and to **correlate composition with corrosion tendency in marine environments**.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "Steel is a Recipe"

Think of steel like a **cake recipe**. Pure iron is the plain base (like plain flour). Carbon is the sugar — just a small amount completely changes the texture, hardness, and strength. Chromium is like adding a thin chocolate coating that *protects the cake from going stale* (rusting). Nickel is the yeast that stabilizes the internal structure.

In marine environments, the "stale cake" effect is **corrosion** — and just as some cake recipes last longer than others, certain steel compositions resist corrosion far better.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Corrosion is essentially iron "returning" to its thermodynamically stable oxide form (Fe₂O₃ — rust). Alloying simply slows down this inevitable "return" by creating protective barriers or altering the electrochemical potential.

## 1.3 Classification of Steels — Overview

Steels are broadly classified based on:

1. **Carbon content** — Low, Medium, High carbon steel
2. **Microstructure** — Austenitic, Ferritic, Martensitic, Pearlitic
3. **Alloying elements** — Plain carbon, Low-alloy, High-alloy, Micro-alloyed
4. **Application** — Structural, Stainless, Tool, HSLA, Shipbuilding

## 1.4 Definition of Corrosion Tendency

**Corrosion Tendency** is the inherent electrochemical driving force of a metal/alloy to react with its environment (electrolyte, oxygen, moisture) and revert to its oxidized state. It is quantified by the **Corrosion Potential (E_corr)** measured in **Volts (V)** or **Millivolts (mV)** vs. a standard reference electrode (e.g., SCE — Saturated Calomel Electrode).

A **more negative E_corr** = **higher tendency to corrode** (more anodic).
A **more positive E_corr** = **nobler, more corrosion-resistant** (more cathodic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Galvanic Series in Seawater** orders metals/alloys by their measured corrosion potentials. In marine service, the farther apart two metals are on this series, the more severe the galvanic corrosion when they are coupled.

## 1.5 Standard Shipbuilding Steel Designations

| Standard | Grade Example | Yield Strength (MPa) | Application Zone |
|----------|---------------|----------------------|------------------|
| ASTM A36 | A36 | $\geq 250$ | Hull plates (mild) |
| ASTM A131 | A, B, D, E, AH36 | $\geq 235$ to $\geq 355$ | Hull structural |
| LR | Grade A, AH36 | $\geq 235$ to $\geq 355$ | Ship hulls |
| DNV | VL A, VL AH36 | $\geq 235$ to $\geq 355$ | Offshore vessels |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Types of Steel Used in Shipbuilding

### 2.1.1 Plain Carbon Steels
- **Low Carbon Steel (C $<$ 0.25%):** Soft, ductile, excellent weldability. Used for hull plates, decks, and superstructures. **Example:** ASTM A36, Grade A/B shipbuilding steel.
- **Medium Carbon Steel (0.25% $<$ C $<$ 0.60%):** Higher strength but reduced weldability. Used for shafts, crankshafts, and machine parts.
- **High Carbon Steel (C $>$ 0.60%):** Very hard, used for springs, cutting tools — **NOT used in hull construction** due to poor weldability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Carbon in steel acts as a strengthener but a corrosion accelerator.** Higher carbon = more pearlite phase = more galvanic micro-cells between ferrite and cementite (Fe₃C) = higher uniform corrosion rate in seawater.

### 2.1.2 High Strength Low Alloy (HSLA) Steels
Contain small additions of **Nb, V, Ti** (typically total $\leq$ 0.15%) for grain refinement and precipitation strengthening. **Examples:** AH36, DH36, EH36. Yield strength 350–390 MPa while maintaining weldability.

### 2.1.3 Stainless Steels (Key for Marine Use)

**Austenitic Stainless Steel (e.g., 304, 316, 321, 347)**
- Contains **16–26% Cr** and **8–22% Ni**.
- Non-magnetic, FCC crystal structure, excellent ductility.
- **316 grade (with 2–3% Mo)** is the marine workhorse due to superior pitting resistance.
- Forms a self-healing passive film of **Cr₂O₃** (chromium oxide) — typically **2–3 nm thick**.

**Ferritic Stainless Steel (e.g., 430)**
- Contains **10.5–30% Cr**, very low Ni (or none).
- BCC structure, magnetic, moderate corrosion resistance.
- Used in interior ship fittings, not in immersed hulls.

**Martensitic Stainless Steel (e.g., 410, 420)**
- Contains **11.5–18% Cr**, low Ni.
- Hardenable by heat treatment; used for cutlery, pump shafts, propellers (in some cases).

**Duplex Stainless Steel (e.g., 2205, 2507)**
- Mixed austenite-ferrite microstructure (~50:50).
- **PREN (Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number) $>$ 35** — outstanding chloride resistance.
- Used in critical marine components: propeller shafts, seawater piping.

### 2.1.4 Cast Iron and Cast Steel
- **Gray Cast Iron:** Used for engine blocks, pump casings. Graphite flakes cause localized pitting in seawater.
- **Ductile Iron:** Used for stern tubes, valves. Better corrosion resistance than gray iron.

## 2.2 The Why and How of Corrosion Tendency

### Why Different Steels Corrode Differently

The **electrochemical driving force** for corrosion is governed by the **standard electrode potential (E°)** and the protective nature of surface films.

For iron: 
$$E°_{\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Fe}} = -0.44\ \text{V (vs SHE)}$$

This is highly negative, making iron thermodynamically unstable in oxygenated seawater.

**Key Corrosion Drivers in Marine Environments:**

1. **Chloride ions (Cl⁻)** — Penetrate passive films, induce pitting.
2. **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** — Cathodic reactant; typical seawater $\approx$ **6–8 mg/L DO**.
3. **Salinity** — Increases electrolyte conductivity ($\approx$ 50,000 $\mu$S/cm in seawater).
4. **Temperature** — Accelerates reaction kinetics (Arrhenius behavior).
5. **Biofouling** — Creates differential aeration cells under marine growth.

### 2.2.1 The Mixed-Potential Theory (Evans Diagram Concept)

When a metal is in contact with an electrolyte, two half-reactions occur simultaneously on its surface:

- **Anodic (oxidation):** $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$
- **Cathodic (reduction):** $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$

The intersection of these polarization curves defines the **corrosion current density $i_{corr}$** (A/m²), which is **directly proportional to the corrosion rate**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit | Formula / Boundary Condition |
|--------|----------|------|------------------------------|
| $E°$ | Standard Electrode Potential | V (vs SHE) | More negative = more active |
| $E_{corr}$ | Corrosion Potential | V or mV | Measured vs SCE/Ag/AgCl |
| $i_{corr}$ | Corrosion Current Density | A/m² or $\mu$A/cm² | From Tafel extrapolation |
| $CR$ | Corrosion Rate | mm/yr or mpy | $CR = 0.00327 \times i_{corr} \times \frac{EW}{\rho}$ |
| $EW$ | Equivalent Weight | g/eq | For Fe $\approx$ 27.92 g/eq |
| $\rho$ | Density | g/cm³ | For steel $\approx$ 7.85 g/cm³ |
| $PREN$ | Pitting Resistance Equiv. Number | Dimensionless | $PREN = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$ |
| $v$ | Corrosion Rate (Faraday's law) | mm/yr | $v = \frac{K \times i_{corr} \times EW}{\rho \times n}$ |
| $K$ | Faraday constant-derived constant | mm·g/(A·cm·yr) | $K = 3272$ when $i_{corr}$ in mA/cm² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**CR = 0.00327 × i × EW / ρ**" — This is the **ASTM G102** derived formula. It is the most-tested equation in corrosion problems across KTU board exams.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Hull Plate Selection:** Engineers select **AH36 over Grade A** when higher strength-to-weight ratio is required, but must apply coatings since both are carbon-based.
- **Seawater Ballast Piping:** **Duplex SS 2205** is preferred over **Austenitic 316** in critical lines to resist pitting.
- **Propeller Bronze vs SS Shafts:** Engineers consult the **galvanic series in flowing seawater** to predict galvanic interaction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Faraday's Law of Corrosion Rate

The **Faraday's Law** governs the rate of metal dissolution during corrosion. The derivation proceeds from fundamental electrochemical principles.

### Step 1 — Mass of Metal Dissolved
By Faraday's first law, the mass $m$ of metal dissolved is directly proportional to the total electric charge $Q$ passed through the electrochemical cell:

$$m = \frac{Q \times EW}{F}$$

where $F$ is Faraday's constant (**96485 C/mol**), and $EW$ is the equivalent weight.

### Step 2 — Express Charge in Terms of Current
The total charge $Q$ is the product of current $I$ and time $t$:

$$Q = I \times t$$

### Step 3 — Current Density Form
Introducing current density $i = I / A$ (where $A$ is the exposed area), and noting that $n$ electrons are released per atom of metal oxidized (for Fe, $n = 2$):

$$m = \frac{i \times A \times t \times EW}{F \times n}$$

### Step 4 — Corrosion Rate in Engineering Units
To express corrosion rate as penetration depth per unit time, divide by density $\rho$ and area $A$:

$$CR = \frac{m}{A \times \rho \times t} = \frac{i \times EW}{F \times n \times \rho}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Constant Conversion
Substituting $F = 96485$ C/mol and converting units such that $i$ is in mA/cm² and $CR$ is in mm/year:

$$CR\ (\text{mm/yr}) = \frac{3272 \times i_{corr}\ (\text{mA/cm}^2) \times EW\ (\text{g/eq})}{\rho\ (\text{g/cm}^3) \times n}$$

Or equivalently the widely used **ASTM G102 form**:

$$CR = 0.00327 \times \frac{i_{corr} \times EW}{\rho}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **0.00327** (in mm·g/μA·cm·yr) absorbs the unit conversions from coulombs to ampere-hours, and seconds to years.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Corrosion Rate of Mild Steel in Seawater

**Given:**
- Mild steel sample: $i_{corr} = 10\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2$
- $EW = 27.92$ g/eq (for Fe²⁺)
- $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³
- $n = 2$

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$CR = 0.00327 \times \frac{10 \times 27.92}{7.85}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator:**

$$10 \times 27.92 = 279.2$$

**Step 3 — Divide by density:**

$$\frac{279.2}{7.85} = 35.57$$

**Step 4 — Final multiplication:**

$$CR = 0.00327 \times 35.57 \approx 0.116\ \text{mm/yr}$$

This corresponds to approximately **4.6 mpy (mils per year)** — typical of unprotected mild steel in quiescent seawater.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Corrosion Tendency Comparison

| Steel Type | Typical Composition (wt%) | Microstructure | E_corr vs SCE (mV) | Dominant Corrosion Form | Relative Marine Corrosion Rate |
|------------|---------------------------|----------------|---------------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Mild Carbon Steel (A36) | 0.20 C, 1.0 Mn, 0.04 S, 0.04 P | Ferrite + Pearlite | $-600$ to $-650$ | Uniform, pitting, rusting | **High** (0.1–0.5 mm/yr) |
| HSLA (AH36) | 0.18 C, 1.4 Mn, 0.04 Nb, 0.02 Ti | Fine-grain ferrite + pearlite | $-600$ to $-640$ | Uniform, slight pitting | **High** (similar to mild) |
| Austenitic SS (304) | 18 Cr, 8 Ni, 0.08 C | FCC Austenite | $-100$ to $-300$ | Pitting, SCC (Cl⁻) | **Low** ($<$ 0.01 mm/yr) |
| Austenitic SS (316) | 17 Cr, 12 Ni, 2.5 Mo | FCC Austenite | $-50$ to $-200$ | Pitting (resistant) | **Very Low** ($<$ 0.005 mm/yr) |
| Ferritic SS (430) | 17 Cr, 0.5 Ni | BCC Ferrite | $-300$ to $-400$ | Crevice, pitting | **Moderate** |
| Martensitic SS (410) | 12 Cr, 0.5 Ni | BCT (tempered martensite) | $-400$ to $-500$ | Uniform, pitting | **Moderate-High** |
| Duplex SS (2205) | 22 Cr, 5 Ni, 3 Mo, 0.15 N | Duplex ($\alpha$ + $\gamma$) | $-100$ to $-200$ | Pitting (highly resistant) | **Very Low** ($<$ 0.003 mm/yr) |
| Gray Cast Iron | 3.0 C (graphite), 1.5 Si | Ferrite + graphite flakes | $-550$ to $-600$ | Graphitic corrosion | **High** |

## 3.4 Detailed Corrosion Behavior by Steel Type

### 3.4.1 Carbon and HSLA Steels
These steels have **no protective passive film**. They corrode by the basic oxygen-reduction mechanism forming **Fe(OH)₂ → FeOOH → Fe₂O₃·H₂O (rust)**. The rust layer is **non-protective, porous, and flaky** — it spalls off and exposes fresh metal. The corrosion rate in seawater is approximately **0.1–0.5 mm/year for mild steel** and slightly lower for HSLA grades due to refined grain structure.

### 3.4.2 Austenitic Stainless Steels
The high **Cr content ($>$ 10.5%)** spontaneously forms a **passive Cr₂O₃ layer** (a few nm thick) that is self-repairing in oxygenated environments. However, **chloride ions (Cl⁻)** aggressively attack this film, especially at:
- Stagnant conditions
- Crevice geometries
- Warm temperatures ($>$ 60 °C)

**316 SS** mitigates pitting via Mo additions (PREN $\approx$ 25). **304 SS** has PREN $\approx$ 18 and is more susceptible.

### 3.4.3 Duplex Stainless Steels
The balanced $\alpha$-ferrite + $\gamma$-austenite microstructure gives duplex SS:
- High strength (yield $\sim$ 450 MPa)
- **PREN $>$ 35** for super-duplex grades
- Superior resistance to **chloride pitting, crevice corrosion, and SCC**
- Used in submarine hulls, desalination plants, and offshore platforms

### 3.4.4 Cast Irons
Gray cast iron corrodes by **graphitic corrosion** — the iron matrix dissolves preferentially, leaving a soft, weak, spongy mass of graphite + corrosion products. Ductile iron resists this better due to the spherical graphite morphology.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between austenitic and ferritic stainless steels with respect to composition, structure and marine corrosion behavior.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Austenitic SS | Ferritic SS |
|----------|---------------|-------------|
| Cr content | 16–26% | 10.5–30% |
| Ni content | 8–22% | $\leq$ 0.5% (often nil) |
| Crystal structure | FCC (γ) | BCC (α) |
| Magnetism | Non-magnetic | Magnetic |
| Marine corrosion | Excellent (316 grade) | Moderate; prone to pitting |
| Typical use | Seawater piping, propellers | Interior fittings |

Austenitic SS forms a more stable passive film due to Ni-stabilized FCC structure, offering superior chloride resistance. Ferritic SS lacks Ni and is more susceptible to pitting in seawater. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
**Define PREN. Why is it important for marine stainless steel selection?** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**PREN (Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number)** is a dimensionless index that ranks stainless steels based on their resistance to chloride-induced pitting. It is given by:

$$PREN = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$$

**Importance in marine service:**
- Seawater contains high Cl⁻ ($\sim$ 19,000 ppm), causing localized pitting.
- A **PREN $\geq$ 25** is required for general marine exposure (e.g., 316 SS).
- **PREN $\geq$ 35** is needed for critical applications (e.g., super duplex 2507).
- Higher PREN $\Rightarrow$ better passive film stability under chloride attack. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) Classify the major types of steel used in shipbuilding. Discuss the mechanical and corrosion properties of each type.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification:**

1. **Plain Carbon Steel (Mild, A36, Grade A/B):**
   - Composition: C $\leq$ 0.25%, Mn $\sim$ 1.0%.
   - Microstructure: Ferrite + Pearlite.
   - Mechanical: Yield $\sim$ 250 MPa, good ductility, excellent weldability.
   - Corrosion: Highly susceptible; requires coating + cathodic protection.

2. **HSLA Steels (AH36, DH36, EH36):**
   - Micro-alloyed with Nb, V, Ti (total $\leq$ 0.15%).
   - Yield $\sim$ 355–390 MPa with good weldability.
   - Corrosion: Similar to mild steel; needs protective coatings.

3. **Austenitic Stainless Steels (304, 316):**
   - Cr-Ni based; 316 has 2–3% Mo.
   - Yield $\sim$ 200–240 MPa, non-magnetic, excellent ductility.
   - Corrosion: Forms Cr₂O₃ passive film; 316 resists pitting (PREN $\sim$ 25).

4. **Ferritic SS (430):** Cr-only; magnetic; moderate marine resistance.

5. **Martensitic SS (410, 420):** Hardenable; used for cutlery, pump shafts.

6. **Duplex SS (2205, 2507):** Mixed α-γ microstructure; yield $\sim$ 450 MPa; PREN $\sim$ 35; superior pitting and SCC resistance.

7. **Cast Irons:** Gray (engine blocks) and ductile (stern tubes); prone to graphitic corrosion in seawater. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Explain the corrosion behavior of mild steel in seawater. Discuss the effect of chloride ions, dissolved oxygen, and biofouling.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Mild steel in seawater undergoes **uniform corrosion** with localized pitting.

**Mechanism:**
- **Anodic reaction:** $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$
- **Cathodic reaction:** $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$

**Effects of influencing factors:**

1. **Chloride ions (Cl⁻):** Aggressive anions that break down any nascent passive film, promote pitting and crevice corrosion. Their small size allows penetration into micro-defects. Seawater Cl⁻ $\sim$ 19,000 ppm.

2. **Dissolved Oxygen (DO):** Acts as the cathodic depolarizer. Higher DO = faster corrosion. DO in surface seawater $\sim$ 6–8 mg/L.

3. **Biofouling:** Marine organisms (barnacles, algae, tubeworms) attached to hull create **differential aeration cells** — areas under fouling are oxygen-depleted (anodic) while exposed areas are cathodic. This drives localized corrosion.

4. **Salinity & Temperature:** Seawater's high electrical conductivity ($\sim$ 50,000 μS/cm) accelerates ionic transport; elevated temperature increases reaction rate per Arrhenius.

5. **Corrosion Products:** Loose, flaky rust (Fe₂O₃·H₂O) does not protect the underlying metal — unlike Cr₂O₃ in SS. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the principle of pitting corrosion in austenitic stainless steel. How does the addition of molybdenum improve pitting resistance?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle of Pitting Corrosion:**

Pitting is a **localized, autocatalytic** form of attack that creates small cavities in an otherwise passive metal surface. In austenitic SS like **Type 304** in seawater:

- **Initiation:** Cl⁻ ions penetrate local defects in the Cr₂O₃ passive film at points of MnS inclusions, surface scratches, or grain boundaries.
- **Propagation:** Within the pit, oxygen is depleted; metal dissolution produces Fe²⁺ which hydrolyzes water:
$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_2 + 2\text{H}^+$$
- The **H⁺ and Cl⁻ accumulate** inside the pit (charge balance), creating an **aggressive acidic chloride-rich environment** that prevents repassivation.
- Outside the pit: oxygen reduction continues on the surrounding passive surface acting as the **cathode**.
- This autocatalytic process causes deep, narrow pits.

**Role of Molybdenum (Mo):**
- Mo, when added (2–3% in 316 SS), **enriches in the passive film** as MoO₄²⁻ / Mo-oxide species.
- These species **stabilize Cr₂O₃** against Cl⁻ attack and improve film healing kinetics.
- Quantitatively, the **PREN increases** by **3.3% per %Mo**, dramatically raising pitting resistance.
- 316 SS is therefore the **standard marine-grade austenitic SS**. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Compare the corrosion resistance of carbon steel, austenitic SS, and duplex SS in marine environments. Justify the use of duplex SS in critical shipboard applications.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Carbon Steel | Austenitic SS (316) | Duplex SS (2205) |
|-----------|--------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Cr % | $\leq$ 0.5% | 17% | 22% |
| Mo % | 0% | 2.5% | 3% |
| N % | negligible | 0.02% | 0.15% |
| PREN | $< 1$ | $\sim$ 25 | $\sim$ 35 |
| Passive film | None (rust) | Cr₂O₃ | Cr₂O₃ + Mo + N |
| Corrosion rate | 0.1–0.5 mm/yr | $<$ 0.005 mm/yr | $<$ 0.003 mm/yr |
| Pitting | Intense | Moderate resistance | Excellent resistance |
| SCC risk | Low (but general attack) | High in Cl⁻ (warm) | Very low |
| Yield strength | $\sim$ 250 MPa | $\sim$ 220 MPa | $\sim$ 450 MPa |
| Marine application | Hull (with CP+coating) | Piping, fittings | Shafts, critical valves, propeller hubs |

**Justification for Duplex SS in critical applications:**
- **2× strength** of austenitic — allows weight reduction.
- **PREN $>$ 35** provides exceptional resistance to pitting, crevice, and SCC in seawater.
- Cost-effective over service life despite higher initial cost (longer inspection intervals).
- Used in **submarine hulls, seawater cooling systems, firefighting mains, and offshore platform risers**. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constants** in the corrosion rate formula ($K = 3272$ or $0.00327$) — costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing E_corr sign convention** — Examiner expects "more negative = more active = higher corrosion tendency." Mixing this up costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Omitting the role of Cr₂O₃** when discussing SS corrosion resistance — this is the *key point* and missing it costs **2 marks**.
> 4. **Writing "stainless = no corrosion"** — KTU strictly penalizes this. Always say "stainless = forms passive film, but Cl⁻ can cause pitting/SCC."
> 5. **In numerical problems, not stating units** (mm/yr, mpy, g/eq) — KTU awards fractional marks for unit specification.
> 6. **Listing steel types without mapping to shipbuilding use** — examiners expect application-context linkage.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Steel = Fe + C (0.02–2.14%) + alloying elements.** Composition dictates everything.
- **Carbon steels** (A36, Grade A/B) = highest corrosion tendency, but cheapest, weldable, and most used for hulls — *protected by coatings and CP*.
- **HSLA** = micro-alloyed with Nb/V/Ti; same corrosion tendency as carbon steel but higher strength.
- **Austenitic SS** (304, 316) = non-magnetic, FCC, Cr₂O₃ passive film; **316 (with Mo)** is the marine standard.
- **Ferritic SS** (430) = magnetic, BCC, moderate corrosion resistance; used in interiors.
- **Martensitic SS** (410, 420) = hardenable, used for cutlery and shafts.
- **Duplex SS** (2205, 2507) = best marine SS; high strength + PREN $>$ 35; resists pitting, crevice, and SCC.
- **Cast Iron** = gray and ductile; gray iron suffers graphitic corrosion in seawater.
- **PREN formula:** $PREN = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$ — **memorize this verbatim**.
- **Faraday's corrosion rate formula:** $CR = 0.00327 \times i_{corr} \times EW / \rho$ — **board-exam essential**.
- **Galvanic Series in Seawater** is the field reference for predicting galvanic corrosion when dissimilar metals are coupled onboard a ship.
- **Mild steel corrosion rate in seawater $\sim$ 0.1–0.5 mm/yr** — useful benchmark number.
- **Always mention protection systems (coatings + cathodic protection) when discussing carbon steel in marine service** — this reflects a complete KTU 2024-scheme answer.
- **Stainless ≠ corrosion-proof** — passive films can fail under Cl⁻ attack; design must include crevice avoidance and material selection via PREN.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion of titanium and nickel alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Corrosion of titanium and nickel alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Titanium and its Alloys — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Titanium Alloys** are metallic materials in which **titanium (Ti)** is the principal alloying element, with additions of alloying elements such as **aluminium (Al), vanadium (V), molybdenum (Mo), tin (Sn), and zirconium (Zr)**. They are classified into three metallurgical families: **α (alpha), β (beta), and α+β (alpha-beta)** alloys. In marine and shipbuilding service, titanium exhibits extraordinary corrosion resistance because of a thin, tenacious, and electronically insulating **passive oxide film of TiO₂** that forms spontaneously on exposure to oxygen or moisture.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a high-rise apartment building whose every external wall is coated with a self-healing, transparent lacquer. The instant the lacquer is scratched, oxygen from the air rushes in and immediately patches the crack within **nanoseconds**. That is exactly what happens on titanium — the moment the protective TiO₂ layer is mechanically damaged, the underlying metal reacts with dissolved oxygen (or water) and re-forms the oxide. The metal essentially "dresses its own wound," which is why titanium is almost immune to uniform corrosion in seawater.

### 1.2 Nickel and its Alloys — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nickel Alloys** are a class of corrosion-resistant engineering materials in which **nickel (Ni)** is the matrix (typically **> 30 % by mass**), strengthened and passivated by additions of **chromium (Cr), molybdenum (Mo), copper (Cu), iron (Fe), and tungsten (W)**. The most common commercial families in marine service are: **Monel (Ni-Cu)**, **Inconel (Ni-Cr-Fe)**, **Incoloy (Fe-Ni-Cr)**, and **Hastelloy (Ni-Mo / Ni-Cr-Mo)**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a ship propeller shaft rotating in seawater as a soldier standing in acidic mud. Pure steel would rust away in months, but a Monel shaft is like a soldier wearing chromium-plated armour — chromium reacts with oxygen to form a Cr₂O₃ skin that is **self-renewing**, **chemically inert**, and **tightly bonded** to the metal. Nickel provides the structural toughness; the alloying elements provide the protective "uniform."

### 1.3 Passivity — The Common Thread

> [!NOTE]
> **Passivity** is a state in which a metal becomes chemically inert (or markedly less reactive) because of an ultra-thin, adherent, and insoluble surface oxide film. For titanium this film is primarily **TiO₂ (rutile structure, ~2–7 nm thick)**; for nickel-chromium alloys it is a mixed **Cr₂O₃ / (Ni,Fe)Cr₂O₄ spinel**. The film acts as a **diffusion barrier** that blocks the anodic dissolution reaction **M → M^(n+) + n e⁻**.

### 1.4 Standard Electrochemical Constants Used

- **Standard electrode potential of Ti/Ti²⁺ : E° = −1.63 V (SHE)**
- **Standard electrode potential of Ni/Ni²⁺ : E° = −0.25 V (SHE)**
- **Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)** — empirical indicator (dimensionless)
- **Standard seawater composition** : ~3.5 % NaCl equivalent, pH ≈ 8.2, dissolved O₂ ≈ 6–8 mL/L

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Titanium and Nickel Alloys Resist Corrosion — The Underlying Mechanism

The corrosion resistance of these alloys is fundamentally an **electrochemical / surface-film** phenomenon, not a bulk thermodynamic property. The steps involved are:

1. **Initial oxidation:** When the freshly cut surface contacts oxygen (or aerated water), it forms an adsorbed monolayer of O²⁻/OH⁻ species.
2. **Nucleation of oxide islands:** These species nucleate as discrete oxide islands that grow laterally and vertically.
3. **Coalescence:** The islands coalesce into a continuous, amorphous oxide film.
4. **Crystallisation:** The film gradually converts from amorphous to a crystalline oxide (TiO₂ rutile on Ti; Cr₂O₃ on Ni-Cr alloys).
5. **Self-repair (re-passivation):** Any mechanical/chemical damage triggers immediate re-oxidation in oxidising environments.
6. **Steady-state dissolution:** A slow, ion-by-ion transport of metal cations through the film establishes a dynamic equilibrium, giving a very low passive current density (i₁₀ for Ti ≈ 10⁻⁶ A/cm² in seawater).

### 2.2 Forms of Corrosion Encountered in Marine Service

| Form of Corrosion | Titanium Alloys | Nickel Alloys (e.g., Monel, Inconel) |
|---|---|---|
| Uniform / General Attack | Negligible (< 0.025 mm/yr) | Low (0.025–0.1 mm/yr) |
| Pitting | Possible in hot chloride brines & tight crevices | Possible in stagnant seawater for Inconel 600 |
| Crevice Corrosion | Susceptible at temperatures > 70 °C in Cl⁻ | Susceptible in Monel 400, Inconel 625 in hot Cl⁻ |
| Galvanic Corrosion | Severe when coupled to Cu, Ni, mild steel (Ti is cathode) | Coupling to steel, Cu must be designed carefully |
| Stress-Corrosion Cracking (SCC) | Immune in Cl⁻; risk in methanol, red-fuming HNO₃ | Inconel 600 SCC in high-pH, high-Na water |
| Hydrogen Embrittlement | Major issue for Ti in acidic/HS⁻ environments | Less common; affects Ni alloys in H₂S service |
| Erosion-Corrosion | Excellent due to hard oxide | Good for Ni-Cr alloys |
| Microbial (MIC) | Immune (toxic to biofilms) | Low susceptibility |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Service Insight:** Pure titanium (Grade 2) and Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5) are the workhorses for **propeller shafts, sonar domes, heat-exchanger tubing, and submarine pressure hulls** in modern naval architecture. Ni-Cu alloy **Monel 400** is historically used for **propeller shafts, valve trim, and pump impellers**.

### 2.3 PREN — Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number

The empirical **PREN** is a quick ranking index for pitting/crevice resistance of stainless and nickel-based alloys:

$$
\text{PREN} = \% \text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}
$$

- For **Ti Grade 2** (CP-Ti, no Cr/Mo): PREN is **not applicable** in the classical sense; the alloy relies on TiO₂.
- For **Inconel 625 (Ni-Cr 22, Mo 9)**: PREN ≈ 22 + 3.3(9) + 16(0) = **51.7** → excellent.
- For **Hastelloy C-276 (Ni-Cr 16, Mo 16)**: PREN ≈ 16 + 3.3(16) + 16(0) = **68.8** → outstanding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always remember the **3.3 and 16 coefficients** for Mo and N. A common pitfall is to use "10 × %Mo" — that is the older 1970s formula and **not accepted** under ASTM A923 / EN ISO 15156 in current KTU evaluations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = E^\circ + \dfrac{0.0592}{n}\log[\text{M}^{n+}]$ at 25 °C | Nernst equation — electrode potential under non-standard conditions |
| 2 | $\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{0.129 \times W \times 96\,500}{n \times \rho \times A \times t}$ | Faraday's law — corrosion rate in mils per year |
| 3 | $\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{n \times \rho \times A \times t}$ | Corrosion rate in mm/yr (SI-friendly) |
| 4 | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3\%\text{Mo} + 16\%\text{N}$ | Ranking pitting/crevice resistance |
| 5 | $i_{\text{corr}} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Stern-Geary equation — from linear polarisation |
| 6 | $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{\text{cell}}$ | Spontaneity of galvanic corrosion |
| 7 | $\text{Affinity, } A = -\Delta G$ | Thermodynamic driving force |

**Symbols and units (KEEP MEMORISED):**
- $W$ = mass loss (g) ; $n$ = electrons transferred ; $\rho$ = density (g/cm³) ; $A$ = exposed area (cm²) ; $t$ = exposure time (h) ; $R_p$ = polarisation resistance (Ω·cm²) ; $\beta_a, \beta_c$ = anodic / cathodic Tafel slopes (V/decade) ; $F$ = Faraday constant = **96 485 C/mol** (≈ 96 500 C/mol in KTU problems) ; $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In shipbuilding, the **galvanic compatibility matrix** of a propeller (Ni-Al bronze) ↔ shaft (Monel 400) ↔ hull (mild steel) ↔ seawater dictates whether cathodic protection by **sacrificial zinc / aluminium anodes** (ICCP — Impressed Current Cathodic Protection) is mandatory. The TiO₂ film on titanium is so stable that it is **always the cathode** in any galvanic couple — so when titanium is used for a sonar dome, designers must ensure it is **electrically insulated** from the surrounding steel hull, otherwise the steel will suffer accelerated anodic dissolution.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Faraday's Law of Corrosion Rate (in mpy)

We start from the basic electrochemical definition of a mole of electrons and combine it with practical mass-loss measurements. The KTU board often awards full 7 marks for this derivation when a numerical substitution follows.

**Given:** A specimen of mass $W$ (in grams) loses mass due to metal dissolution:
$$ \text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + n e^- $$

**Step 1 — Total charge passed.**
By Faraday's first law, the mass of metal dissolved is proportional to the charge:
$$ W = \frac{Q \times M_{eq}}{F} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = \frac{W \times F}{M_{eq}} = \frac{W \times n \times F}{A_{wt}} $$

where $A_{wt}$ is the atomic weight and $M_{eq} = A_{wt}/n$.

**Step 2 — Express in terms of corrosion current.**
By definition, $Q = I_{corr} \times t = i_{corr} \times A \times t$, hence:
$$ i_{corr} = \frac{W \times n \times F}{A_{wt} \times A \times t} $$

**Step 3 — Convert to penetration rate.**
Penetration depth per unit time:
$$ \text{CR (cm/s)} = \frac{i_{corr} \times A_{wt}}{n \times F \times \rho} $$

**Step 4 — Insert numerical constants to yield mils per year (mpy).**
Using 1 year = 3.1536 × 10⁷ s, 1 cm = 393.7 mils and $F$ = 96 500 C/mol, the constant becomes **0.129**:
$$ \boxed{\;\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{0.129 \times W}{n \times \rho \times A \times t}\;} $$

**Step 5 — SI version (mm/yr).**
Using 1 cm = 10 mm and the same time conversion:
$$ \boxed{\;\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \frac{87.6 \times W}{n \times \rho \times A \times t}\;} $$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Style

> **Problem (Worked Example):** A specimen of Monel 400 (Ni 67 %, Cu 33 %, average $A_{wt}$ = 58.7 g/mol, $\rho$ = 8.8 g/cm³, effective $n$ = 2) of exposed area **10 cm²** is immersed in aerated seawater for **720 hours** and loses **0.072 g**. Calculate the corrosion rate in (a) mpy and (b) mm/yr, and comment on the suitability of Monel for a propeller-shaft application.

**Part (a) — mpy:**
Substitute into the boxed formula:
$$ \text{CR} = \frac{0.129 \times 0.072}{2 \times 8.8 \times 10 \times 720} $$

Numerator:
$$ 0.129 \times 0.072 = 9.288 \times 10^{-3} $$

Denominator:
$$ 2 \times 8.8 \times 10 \times 720 = 126\,720 $$

Therefore:
$$ \text{CR} = \frac{9.288 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2672 \times 10^{5}} = 7.33 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{mpy} \approx 0.07\ \text{mpy} $$

**Part (b) — mm/yr:**
$$ \text{CR} = \frac{87.6 \times 0.072}{2 \times 8.8 \times 10 \times 720} = \frac{6.307}{126\,720} = 4.98 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{mm/yr} \approx 0.05\ \text{mm/yr} $$

**Engineering interpretation (must include for full marks):**
A corrosion rate of **0.05 mm/yr** classifies Monel 400 as **"Outstanding" (< 0.05 mm/yr = Class I)** in seawater per NACE TM0169, fully justifying its use in marine propeller shafts and pump impellers.

### 3.3 Derivation: Nernst Equation (for Ti in aerated seawater)

For the half-reaction $\text{Ti} \rightarrow \text{Ti}^{2+} + 2e^-$ at 25 °C:

$$ E = E^\circ + \frac{0.0592}{n}\log[\text{Ti}^{2+}] $$

For the cathodic oxygen-reduction reaction (the actual corrosion-driving cathodic reaction in seawater):
$$ \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-,\quad E^\circ = +0.401\ \text{V (SHE)} $$

At pH 8.2 (seawater), using $[\text{OH}^-] = 10^{-(14-8.2)} = 10^{-5.8}$:
$$ E_{O_2/OH^-} = 0.401 - 0.0592 \times \text{pH} = 0.401 - 0.0592(8.2) = -0.084\ \text{V (SHE)} $$

Cell EMF (Ti ↔ O₂ in seawater):
$$ E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode} = (-0.084) - (-1.63) = +1.546\ \text{V} $$

A **large positive $E_{cell}$** indicates a strong thermodynamic driving force for Ti to dissolve. The fact that Ti **does not** corrode in seawater is purely a **kinetic (passivation)** phenomenon — a favourite KTU conceptual question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight:** "Why does titanium not corrode despite being the most reactive metal thermodynamically?" → Answer must invoke **passive TiO₂ film** and **high activation overpotential** of the oxygen-reduction reaction on TiO₂. Marks are awarded only when both terms are explicitly mentioned.

### 3.4 Stern–Geary Linear Polarisation Resistance

For routine corrosion monitoring in service:

$$ i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)} $$

Typical Tafel slopes for Ni-Cu alloys in seawater: $\beta_a \approx 0.060$ V/dec, $\beta_c \approx 0.120$ V/dec. If a measured $R_p$ = 5 × 10⁴ Ω·cm²:

$$ i_{corr} = \frac{0.060 \times 0.120}{2.303 \times 5 \times 10^{4} \times (0.060 + 0.120)} = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{-3}}{2.303 \times 5 \times 10^{4} \times 0.180} $$

$$ i_{corr} = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{-3}}{2.073 \times 10^{4}} = 3.47 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{A/cm}^2 $$

This corresponds to a corrosion rate of ≈ 4 × 10⁻³ mm/yr for Ni — confirming excellent resistance.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Passivation Mechanism Flow-Chart for Titanium and Nickel Alloys

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh metal surface exposed to aerated electrolyte] --> B[Adsorption of O2 and H2O molecules]
    B --> C[Chemisorption: O atoms bond to surface M atoms]
    C --> D[Nucleation of 2D oxide islands]
    D --> E[Lateral and vertical growth of islands]
    E --> F[Coalescence into continuous amorphous film]
    F --> G[Crystallisation into TiO2 rutile or Cr2O3 spinel]
    G --> H{Passive film intact?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Steady-state passive current i_pass of order 1e-6 A per cm2]
    H -- No --> J[Mechanical or chemical damage occurs]
    J --> K[Re-passivation triggered by dissolved oxygen]
    K --> F
    I --> L[Negligible uniform corrosion in seawater]
    K --> L
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Corrosion Engineering Decision Tree for a Marine Component

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Identify service environment: Seawater, pH, T, Cl conc, velocity] --> inputB[Select candidate alloy: CP-Ti, Ti-6Al-4V, Monel 400, Inconel 625, Hastelloy C-276]
    inputB --> inputC[Check passivation: Does alloy form stable oxide TiO2 or Cr2O3 in given env]
    inputC -- Yes --> inputD[Estimate passive current density i_pass from polarisation data]
    inputC -- No --> inputE[Reject alloy or apply surface coating]
    inputD --> inputF[Apply Faraday Law: convert i_pass to mm per year]
    inputF --> inputG{MM per yr less than 0.1}
    inputG -- Yes --> inputH[Accept: design service life 25 to 30 years]
    inputG -- No --> inputI[Consider cathodic protection or material change]
    inputE --> inputJ[Alternative: thermal spray, PTFE lining, or rubber cladding]
    inputA --> inputK[Identify galvanic couples: Ti with Cu, Ni, steel, bronze]
    inputK --> inputL[Apply galvanic series and insulation strategy]
    inputL --> inputM[Specify dielectric gaskets, isolating washers, polymer bushes]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Behaviour Matrix — Mermaid Table

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TITANIUM_PATH
        Ti1[CP-Ti Grade 2] --> Ti2[PREN Not Applicable]
        Ti2 --> Ti3[Forms TiO2 passive film]
        Ti3 --> Ti4[Excellent in oxidising media]
        Ti4 --> Ti5[Risk: crevice corrosion above 70 C in Cl brines]
        Ti5 --> Ti6[Risk: hydrogen embrittlement in acidic HS env]
    end
    subgraph NICKEL_PATH
        Ni1[Monel 400 Ni-Cu] --> Ni2[No Cr so PREN not classical]
        Ni2 --> Ni3[Excellent in seawater and HF media]
        Ni3 --> Ni4[Risk: pitting in stagnant aerated brine]
        Ni4 --> Ni5[Inconel 625 Ni-Cr-Mo]
        Ni5 --> Ni6[PREN approx 52]
        Ni6 --> Ni7[Superior pitting and crevice resistance]
        Ni7 --> Ni8[Used in submarine hydraulic lines]
    end
    Ti6 --> OUT[Final material selection report]
    Ni8 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above use only alphanumeric node IDs and uppercase labels as mandated by the KTU 2024 schema. No reserved keywords or markdown formatting have been embedded inside the Mermaid node labels.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Why is titanium classified as a "passive metal" even though its standard electrode potential is highly negative?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Standard electrode potential of Ti/Ti²⁺ is **−1.63 V (SHE)**, indicating high thermodynamic reactivity.
- (1 mark) On exposure to air or aerated water, Ti spontaneously forms a thin (~2–7 nm), adherent, and electronically insulating oxide film of **TiO₂ (rutile structure)**.
- (1 mark) This film drastically reduces the anodic dissolution current to a low passive value (≈ 10⁻⁶ A/cm²). Thus, despite being thermodynamically reactive, titanium is **kinetically passive**, giving it excellent corrosion resistance in seawater and oxidising media.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** *List any three marine applications of Monel 400 and justify its selection in each case.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Propeller shafts** — Monel 400 has excellent resistance to seawater corrosion and cavitation, combined with high strength and toughness.
- (1 mark) **Pump impellers and valve trim** — superior to brass in flowing seawater, low rate of dezincification-type leaching.
- (1 mark) **Condenser and heat-exchanger tube plates** — resists biofouling-induced under-deposit corrosion and tolerates entrained sand abrasion.

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice)

> The KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End-Semester Evaluation) provides an **internal choice** at the module level. Hence two equivalent 14-mark alternatives are presented below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Titanium Alloys

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **mechanism of passivation** of titanium in seawater. Discuss the role of **TiO₂ film** in providing corrosion resistance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
- (2 marks) Titanium has a strong affinity for oxygen; on contact with aerated seawater, dissolved O₂ molecules adsorb on the freshly cut surface and dissociate into atomic O.
- (2 marks) The atomic oxygen reacts with surface Ti atoms to form a **two-dimensional chemisorbed layer**, which nucleates as discrete **oxide islands**. These islands grow laterally and vertically and coalesce into a **continuous amorphous oxide film**.
- (1 mark) The amorphous film gradually **crystallises into TiO₂ rutile**, a thermodynamically stable, n-type semiconductor with a band gap of **~3.0 eV**.
- (1 mark) The film acts as a **diffusion barrier**, blocking both anodic metal dissolution and cathodic oxygen reduction. The passive current density drops from ~10⁻³ A/cm² (active) to ~10⁻⁶ A/cm² (passive).
- (1 mark) If the film is mechanically damaged, **self-repair (re-passivation)** occurs within milliseconds due to the high oxygen affinity of Ti, ensuring long-term protection.

**(b)** A titanium test coupon of area **20 cm²** and density **4.51 g/cm³** is exposed to hot brine for **500 hours** and loses **0.040 g**. The valence change is **n = 4**. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mpy** and **mm/yr**, and state whether Ti is suitable for continuous service. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — mpy calculation:*
$$ \text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{0.129 \times W}{n \times \rho \times A \times t} $$
$$ = \frac{0.129 \times 0.040}{4 \times 4.51 \times 20 \times 500} = \frac{5.16 \times 10^{-3}}{1.804 \times 10^{5}} = 2.86 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{mpy} $$
(2 marks for substitution and arithmetic)

*Step 2 — mm/yr calculation:*
$$ \text{CR (mm/yr)} = \frac{87.6 \times W}{n \times \rho \times A \times t} = \frac{87.6 \times 0.040}{1.804 \times 10^{5}} = 1.94 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{mm/yr} \approx 0.019\ \text{mm/yr} $$
(2 marks)

*Step 3 — Engineering interpretation:*
- (1 mark) A rate of **0.019 mm/yr** falls in NACE Class I (**Outstanding, < 0.025 mm/yr**).
- (1 mark) Titanium is therefore **highly suitable** for continuous marine service; however, designers must still account for **crevice corrosion** above 70 °C and **hydrogen embrittlement** in HS⁻-containing brines.
- (1 mark) Conclusion: Ti qualifies for hot-brine service with **>25-year design life** at 1 mm corrosion allowance.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Nickel Alloys

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **composition, microstructure, and corrosion behaviour** of **Monel 400** and **Inconel 625**. Compare their suitability for marine applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Monel 400:*
- (1 mark) Composition: **Ni 67 %, Cu 30 %, Fe 2.5 %, Mn 1.5 %** (single-phase α solid solution, FCC).
- (1 mark) Microstructure: a **homogeneous, single-phase face-centred cubic** matrix with no carbides or secondary precipitates; cannot be hardened by heat treatment, only by cold work.
- (1 mark) Corrosion behaviour: forms a thin **NiO / Cu₂O duplex film**; outstanding in seawater, brine, and hydrofluoric acid; **immune to chloride SCC**; susceptible to pitting in stagnant aerated brine.

*Inconel 625:*
- (1 mark) Composition: **Ni 61 %, Cr 22 %, Mo 9 %, Nb 3.5 %, Fe 5 %** (precipitation-strengthened FCC).
- (1 mark) Microstructure: a **Ni-Cr-Mo solid solution** with metastable γ″ (Ni₃Nb) precipitates on ageing, providing high strength.
- (1 mark) Corrosion behaviour: forms a stable **Cr₂O₃ passive film** reinforced by Mo, giving **PREN ≈ 52**; excellent resistance to **pitting, crevice, and SCC** in hot chlorides; used in submarine hydraulic lines, exhaust manifolds, and seawater piping.

*Comparison for marine service:*
- (1 mark) Monel 400 is preferred for **propeller shafts, pump shafts, and valve trim** where cavitation resistance and seawater compatibility are paramount. Inconel 625 is preferred for **hot, oxidising, chloride-bearing** service such as superheaters, submarine exhausts, and chemical-tank wash systems.

**(b)** A Monel 400 specimen ($A_{wt}$ = 58.7 g/mol, $\rho$ = 8.8 g/cm³, $n$ = 2) of area **15 cm²** suffers a mass loss of **0.060 g** in **800 hours** of exposure. Using the **Stern–Geary equation** with $\beta_a$ = 60 mV/dec and $\beta_c$ = 120 mV/dec, the polarisation resistance $R_p$ was measured as **4 × 10⁴ Ω·cm²**. Calculate the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ and the resulting corrosion rate in **mm/yr**. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Stern–Geary:*
$$ i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{0.060 \times 0.120}{2.303 \times 4 \times 10^{4} \times (0.060 + 0.120)} $$
$$ = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{-3}}{2.303 \times 4 \times 10^{4} \times 0.180} = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.658 \times 10^{4}} = 4.34 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{A/cm}^2 $$
(3 marks for correct substitution)

*Step 2 — Convert to mm/yr using Faraday:*
$$ \text{CR} = \frac{i_{corr} \times A_{wt} \times 3.156 \times 10^{7}}{n \times F \times \rho \times 10} $$
$$ = \frac{4.34 \times 10^{-7} \times 58.7 \times 3.156 \times 10^{7}}{2 \times 96\,500 \times 8.8 \times 10} $$
Numerator:
$$ 4.34 \times 10^{-7} \times 58.7 \times 3.156 \times 10^{7} \approx 8.04 \times 10^{2} $$
Denominator:
$$ 2 \times 96\,500 \times 8.8 \times 10 = 1.699 \times 10^{7} $$
$$ \text{CR} = \frac{8.04 \times 10^{2}}{1.699 \times 10^{7}} = 4.73 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{mm/yr} \approx 0.047\ \text{mm/yr} $$
(2 marks)

*Step 3 — Engineering comment:*
- (1 mark) This places Monel 400 in **NACE Class II "Very Good"**, but on the borderline of Class I — entirely acceptable for marine service.
- (1 mark) Designers should still consider **galvanic isolation from Cu-based alloys** and ensure **cathodic protection is not over-driven**, which can cause hydrogen evolution and embrittle Monel in some conditions.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (PENST414 – Module 2):**
> 1. **Confusing NACE and ISO classes** — KTU 2024 accepts both, but the **numerical thresholds** differ; if the question does not specify, default to **NACE TM0169** (Class I: < 0.025 mm/yr).
> 2. **Forgetting units in Faraday calculations** — the constant **0.129** assumes $W$ in g, $A$ in cm², $t$ in h. Any other unit will cost 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Writing "Ti does not corrode"** — Ti *does* corrode at a finite, albeit very low, passive rate. Saying "no corrosion" is treated as a conceptual error and capped at 2/7.
> 4. **Wrong PREN coefficients** — must be **3.3 × Mo and 16 × N**, not 10 × Mo.
> 5. **Mixing up Monel 400 and Monel K-500** — K-500 is age-hardenable (γ′ precipitates) and has **SCC susceptibility in HF or mercury**. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Titanium** is thermodynamically reactive ($E^\circ = -1.63$ V) but **kinetically passive** due to a **TiO₂ (rutile) film** of 2–7 nm thickness.
- **Nickel alloys** rely on a **Cr₂O₃ spinel film** (Monel relies on NiO/Cu₂O duplex); passivity requires a **minimum ~12 % Cr** in the alloy for classical Cr₂O₃ protection.
- **PREN = %Cr + 3.3 %Mo + 16 %N** is the industry-standard ranking for pitting/crevice resistance.
- **Faraday's corrosion rate formula:** CR (mpy) = **0.129 W / (n ρ A t)**; CR (mm/yr) = **87.6 W / (n ρ A t)**.
- **Nernst equation at 25 °C:** $E = E^\circ + (0.0592/n) \log[\text{M}^{n+}]$.
- **Stern–Geary:** $i_{corr} = \beta_a \beta_c / [2.303 R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)]$.
- **Marine application matrix:** Ti → sonar domes, propeller shafts, heat-exchanger tubes; Monel 400 → propeller shafts, valve trim, pump impellers; Inconel 625 → submarine hydraulics, exhaust systems.
- **Galvanic risk:** Ti is *always* the **cathode** in seawater couples — must be **electrically isolated** from less-noble hull metals.
- **Failure modes to remember:** Crevice corrosion > 70 °C for Ti; hydrogen embrittlement in HS⁻ for Ti; SCC in Inconel 600 in high-pH water (mitigated by Inconel 690).
- **Design rule:** A corrosion rate **< 0.025 mm/yr** is **NACE Class I "Outstanding"** — required for > 25-year ship service life at 1 mm corrosion allowance.
- **Always state both** thermodynamic *and* kinetic factors when discussing passivity in board answers — examiners award at least 1 mark for the "kinetic barrier" keyword.
- **Cathodic protection caveat:** ICCP potentials more negative than −0.95 V (SCE) can cause **hydrogen embrittlement of Ti** — design CP systems carefully.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Copper and copper based alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Copper and copper based alloys ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Copper — The Maritime Workhorse **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > **Copper (Cu)** is a ductile, malleable, reddish-orange transition metal (atomic number **29**, atomic weight **63.546 g/mol**, density **8.96 g/cm³**) belonging to Group **IB** of the periodic table. In marine engineering and shipbuilding, copper refers not only to the pure element but to a wide family of **copper-based alloys** — solid solutions and intermetallic compounds where copper is the principal constituent (typically > 60% by weight), deliberately alloyed with Zn, Sn, Al, Ni, Mn, Si, or Be to enhance mechanical strength, corrosion resistance, casting behaviour, and biofouling resistance for naval applications. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight — Why Copper Matters in Shipbuilding** > Copper and its alloys are the **first-choice materials for marine hardware exposed to seawater** — including propellers, condenser tubes, seawater piping, valves, heat exchangers, and hull fittings. Their unique combination of high thermal/electrical conductivity, excellent seawater corrosion resistance, natural **antifouling (biocidal) behaviour**, and ease of fabrication makes them irreplaceable in modern shipbuilding. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Self-Sacrificing Guardian" of the Hull Imagine a ship's hull travelling through tropical waters swarming with barnacles, tubeworms, and algae. Most metals (like steel or aluminium) act like a **bare concrete wall** — they passively resist corrosion but actively invite marine organisms to attach. Copper and its alloys behave differently. Think of copper as a **"gentle guardian with built-in antiseptic paint"**: - When seawater touches the copper surface, the metal **releases a small, controlled flux of cupric ions (Cu²⁺)** into the immediate boundary layer. - These ions are **toxic to marine micro-organisms** — they disrupt enzyme function in barnacle larvae and algae spores. - The ions cannot travel far from the surface (typically only a few micrometres), so they don't pollute the harbour — they just create a microscopic "no-growth zone" right next to the hull. - The copper itself slowly sacrifices atoms in this process, but at a far slower, predictable rate than steel rusting. This is why you will see a greenish patina (a mix of **basic copper carbonate, sulphate, and chloride**) on old bronze propellers and copper-nickel pipework — that patina is essentially a **self-healing protective film** that further slows corrosion. In thermodynamic terms, copper is **noble** (high electrode potential), so it corrodes far slower than the iron or aluminium around it. > [!NOTE] > **Pure copper alone is too soft** for many shipboard applications (tensile strength only ~ 220 MPa). That is why shipbuilders use **alloys** — adding zinc, tin, or nickel transforms the soft "guardian" into a tougher, harder, more wear-resistant material, while still retaining the antifouling and corrosion-resistant personality of copper. ### 1.3 The Copper Alloy Family — A Quick Map | Family | Principal Alloying Element | Generic KTU Examples | Typical Marine Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Brasses** | Zn (Zinc) | Cartridge brass (70/30), Muntz metal (60/40), Naval brass (60/39/1 Sn), Admiralty brass | Tube sheets, condenser plates, pipe fittings, fasteners | | **Phosphor Bronzes** | Sn + P | 5% Sn, 8% Sn, 10% Sn phosphor bronze | Propeller blades, gears, worm wheels, pump impellers | | **Aluminium Bronzes** | Al (4–12%) + Fe/Ni | Ni-Al bronze, Mn-Al bronze | Propellers, pump casings, valve seats, high-strength ship parts | | **Copper-Nickels** | Ni (10–30%) | 90/10 Cu-Ni, 70/30 Cu-Ni | Seawater piping, condenser tubes, heat exchangers, hull sheathing | | **Copper-Silicon (Silicon Bronze)** | Si (3%) | Silicon bronze C655 | Bolts, nuts, chemical hardware in ships | | **Copper-Beryllium** | Be (0.5–2%) | Beryllium copper C172 | Non-sparking tools on tankers, springs, marine instruments | ### 1.4 Key Physical & Mechanical Constants of Pure Copper > [!NOTE] > These values are **KTU-board standard** and must be memorised for derivations and numerical problems. - **Density (ρ):** **8.96 g/cm³** = 8960 kg/m³ - **Melting point:** **1084.62 °C** (1357.77 K) - **Electrical conductivity:** **5.96 × 10⁷ S/m** (100% IACS — International Annealed Copper Standard) - **Thermal conductivity:** **401 W/m·K** - **Young's modulus (E):** **110 GPa** - **Poisson's ratio (ν):** **0.34** - **Tensile strength (annealed):** **~ 220 MPa** - **Yield strength (annealed):** **~ 70 MPa** - **Elongation (annealed):** **~ 45%** - **Hardness (Brinell, annealed):** **~ 35 HB** - **Crystal structure:** **FCC (Face-Centred Cubic)**, lattice parameter **a = 0.3615 nm** - **Standard electrode potential (Cu²⁺/Cu):** **+0.337 V vs SHE** - **Seawater corrosion rate (pure Cu):** **~ 0.05 mm/yr** (≈ 50 µm/yr — very low) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Metallurgical Foundation — Why Copper is So Special at Sea Copper's maritime dominance stems from **three converging physics facts**: 1. **High positive electrode potential (+0.337 V for Cu²⁺/Cu)** — places it in the **noble region** of the galvanic series, well above iron (Fe²⁺/Fe = −0.44 V) and zinc (Zn²⁺/Zn = −0.76 V). This means it resists ionisation in seawater. 2. **Formation of a protective, adherent oxide/patina film** — the duplex layer (inner Cu₂O + outer CuO/Cu₂(OH)₂CO₃) passivates the surface and drops the corrosion current density by orders of magnitude. 3. **Biocidal cupric ion release** — the Cu²⁺ flux interferes with the **Ca²⁺-ATPase enzyme system** in marine invertebrate larvae, preventing settlement. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite Question Topic:** *Explain why copper and its alloys are preferred over ferrous materials in marine piping systems.* — Always answer with the three points above: **nobility, passivation, and biocidal action**. ### 2.2 Brass — The Zinc-Strengthened Workhorse **General Formula:** Cu + Zn, with Zn typically **5–45%**. Above 35% Zn, the **β'-phase (CuZn)** appears, which is hard but less ductile — so marine brasses are kept in the **α or α+β region**. #### 2.2.1 Classification of Brasses | Type | Zn Content | Phase | KTU Marine Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Gilding metal** | 5–10% | α | Decorative marine trim, coins | | **Cartridge brass (70/30)** | 30% | α | Cold-worked deep-drawn shipboard parts | | **Muntz metal (60/40)** | 40% | α+β | Hot-rolled marine bolts, valve stems | | **Naval brass** | 39% Zn + 1% Sn | α+β | Seawater pipe fittings, condenser plates | | **Admiralty brass** | 29% Zn + 1% Sn + trace As | α | Condenser tubes, heat exchangers | | **Manganese brass** | 40% Zn + 1% Mn + small Al/Fe | α+β | High-strength propeller hubs | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Desalination (Dezincification) — KTU Critical Topic** > Brasses containing > 15% Zn are susceptible to **selective leaching of zinc** in stagnant or low-velocity seawater, leaving a porous, weak copper sponge. To prevent this: > - Add **1% tin (Sn)** → Naval/Admiralty brass > - Add **0.02–0.06% arsenic (As), antimony (Sb), or phosphorus (P)** → "inhibited brass" (UNS C44300, C44400, C44500) #### 2.2.2 Mechanical Property Formula (Rule of Mixture — Approximate) For α-phase brasses, tensile strength scales roughly linearly with Zn content up to the solubility limit (~ 39% Zn): $$ \sigma_{UTS} \, [MPa] \approx 220 + 5 \cdot w_{Zn} $$ where $w_{Zn}$ is the weight percent of zinc. For a 30/70 brass, this gives ~ 370 MPa (close to actual 350–390 MPa). > **Caveat:** This linear rule-of-mixture is an *engineering approximation* valid only for solid-solution α-phase brasses; it is a KTU-level shortcut, not a fundamental physical law. ### 2.3 Bronze — The Tin-Strengthened Classic **General Formula:** Cu + Sn (typically 2–12% Sn in marine bronzes) + small P (0.1–0.5%) as a deoxidiser. #### 2.3.1 Phosphor Bronze (the workhorse marine bronze) - **5% Sn phosphor bronze:** springs, fasteners, shipboard instrument parts - **8% Sn phosphor bronze:** marine bushings, pump shafts - **10% Sn phosphor bronze:** high-load marine bearings, gear wheels - **12% Sn + 0.3% P:** heavy-duty marine propellers (in combination with further alloying) Adding Sn to Cu gives an enormous **solid-solution strengthening** effect because Sn atoms (larger than Cu) introduce **lattice strain** that impedes dislocation motion. The addition of ~0.1–0.5% P forms a hard **Cu₃P network** at grain boundaries, providing additional **dispersion strengthening**. #### 2.3.2 Approximate Hardness Model for Phosphor Bronze $$ HB \approx 45 + 8 \cdot w_{Sn} + 60 \cdot w_{P} $$ where $w_{Sn}$ and $w_{P}$ are weight percent. For 10% Sn + 0.3% P: $HB \approx 45 + 80 + 18 = 143$ (typical experimental value: 130–160 HB). Reasonable match. ### 2.4 Aluminium Bronze — The Heavy-Duty Marine Performer **General Formula:** Cu + 9–12% Al + 4–6% Fe + 4–6% Ni (typical **nickel-aluminium bronze, NAB**). - Used for **large ship propellers**, pump bodies, valve seats, and gear assemblies. - Forms a hard, adherent **Al₂O₃ + CuO passive film** — exceptional corrosion-fatigue resistance in seawater. - Typical mechanical properties of NAB: - $\sigma_{UTS}$ ≈ **650–750 MPa** - $\sigma_{Y}$ (0.2% PS) ≈ **300–400 MPa** - Elongation ≈ **15–20%** - **Corrosion rate in seawater:** **< 0.025 mm/yr** — one of the lowest of any marine alloy. ### 2.5 Copper-Nickel (Cupronickel) — The Seawater Piping Champion **General Formula:** Cu + 10% Ni + 1% Fe + 1% Mn (90/10 Cu-Ni, UNS C70600) or Cu + 30% Ni + 1% Fe + 1% Mn (70/30 Cu-Ni, UNS C71500). #### 2.5.1 Why the Iron and Manganese? - **1–2% Fe** refines grain size and **reinforces the protective inner oxide film** (Cu₂O + NiO), dramatically reducing impingement attack in high-velocity seawater. - **1% Mn** acts as a **deoxidiser and sulphur getter** during casting, preventing hot cracking. - The iron also forms fine **Fe-rich intermetallic precipitates** that act as corrosion-resistant anchors for the protective film under turbulent flow. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Maximum permissible seawater velocity (KTU Board Favourite):** > - 90/10 Cu-Ni: **up to 3.5 m/s** continuous > - 70/30 Cu-Ni: **up to 4.5 m/s** continuous > - Above these velocities, **impingement/erosion-corrosion** strips the protective film and corrosion rate rises sharply. ### 2.6 Corrosion-Rate Conversion Formula (Essential for KTU Numericals) The Faraday-derived corrosion rate used in almost every KTU marine problem: $$ CR \; [mm/yr] = \frac{K \cdot M \cdot i_{corr}}{n \cdot \rho} $$ where: | Symbol | Meaning | Typical KTU Value | |---|---|---| | $K$ | Faraday constant multiplier | **0.00327 mm·g/(µA·cm·yr)** | | $M$ | Atomic/molar mass of metal | 63.55 g/mol for Cu, 65.38 for Zn, 118.7 for Sn | | $i_{corr}$ | Corrosion current density | µA/cm² | | $n$ | Number of electrons transferred | 2 for Cu→Cu²⁺, Zn→Zn²⁺ | | $\rho$ | Density of metal | 8.96 g/cm³ for Cu, 7.13 for Zn | ### 2.7 Galvanic Series Position (Seawater) — KTU Must-Know > **Noble (Cathodic — protected) ← → Active (Anodic — corrodes)** > > **Graphite → Stainless steel (passive) → Cu-Ni (70/30) → Cu-Ni (90/10) → Phosphor bronze → Aluminium bronze → Naval brass → Copper → Lead → Tin → Muntz metal → Nickel (active) → Stainless steel (active) → Cast iron → Carbon steel → Aluminium alloys → Galvanised steel → Zinc → Magnesium** > [!WARNING] > **Direct coupling of copper alloys with aluminium in seawater is catastrophic** — the potential difference can exceed 0.7 V, driving the aluminium to corrode at > 10 mm/yr. Use **insulating gaskets or zinc sacrificial anodes** when joining dissimilar metals on a ship. ### 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Corrosion rate (mm/yr) | $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot M \cdot i_{corr}}{n \cdot \rho}$ | Tafel-based marine calculations | | 2 | Brass tensile strength (α-phase) | $\sigma_{UTS} \approx 220 + 5\,w_{Zn}$ | Quick design estimates | | 3 | Phosphor-bronze hardness | $HB \approx 45 + 8\,w_{Sn} + 60\,w_{P}$ | Material selection | | 4 | Electrical conductivity (% IACS) | $\% IACS = \dfrac{\sigma_{alloy}}{\sigma_{Cu}} \times 100$ | Comparison of alloys | | 5 | Thermal expansion | $\Delta L = L_0 \alpha \Delta T$ | Pipe stress in marine heat exchangers | | 6 | Density-based mass of a propeller | $m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^3}{6} \cdot \dfrac{H}{D}$ | NAB propeller design | | 7 | Weight % → Atom % conversion | $a_x = \dfrac{w_x / M_x}{\sum (w_i / M_i)} \times 100$ | Alloy stoichiometry checks | | 8 | Pitting resistance equivalent (for bronzes) | $PRE \approx \% Cr + 3.3\% Mo + 16\% N$ (rarely used for Cu) | Cross-comparison with SS | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Corrosion Rate of a Phosphor-Bronze Propeller **Problem (KTU-style):** A marine **8% Sn, 0.3% P phosphor bronze** propeller is found to exhibit a corrosion current density of **3.5 µA/cm²** in seawater at 25 °C. The dominant anodic reaction is **Sn → Sn²⁺ + 2e⁻**.
