Calculate the **corrosion rate in mm/yr**, taking the density of the alloy as **8.85 g/cm³** and the equivalent weight to be calculated from the average atomic mass of the alloying metal in solution (use Sn = 118.7 g/mol). #### Step 1 — Identify the parameters $$ M = 118.7 \, g/mol, \quad i_{corr} = 3.5 \, \mu A/cm^2, \quad n = 2, \quad \rho = 8.85 \, g/cm^3 $$ #### Step 2 — Substitute into the corrosion-rate formula $$ CR = \frac{K \cdot M \cdot i_{corr}}{n \cdot \rho} $$ $$ CR = \frac{0.00327 \times 118.7 \times 3.5}{2 \times 8.85} $$ #### Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator $$ 0.00327 \times 118.7 = 0.388149 $$ $$ 0.388149 \times 3.5 = 1.3585215 $$ #### Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator $$ 2 \times 8.85 = 17.70 $$ #### Step 5 — Final division $$ CR = \frac{1.3585215}{17.70} = 0.0768 \, mm/yr $$ #### Step 6 — Express with appropriate significant figures $$ \boxed{CR \approx 0.077 \, mm/yr \;\; \text{(i.e., 77 µm/yr)}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** This is excellent corrosion resistance — a **3 mm-thick propeller blade** would last roughly **39 years** at this rate, validating phosphor bronze's use in marine propellers. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Weight % to Atomic % Conversion for 90/10 Cu-Ni **Problem:** A 90/10 Cu-Ni alloy contains **90 wt% Cu** and **10 wt% Ni**. Calculate the **atomic %** of each element. (Atomic masses: Cu = 63.55, Ni = 58.69.) #### Step 1 — Convert weight % to mole fractions Take a **100 g basis** sample: 90 g Cu, 10 g Ni. $$ \text{moles of Cu} = \frac{90}{63.55} = 1.4162 \, mol $$ $$ \text{moles of Ni} = \frac{10}{58.69} = 0.1704 \, mol $$ #### Step 2 — Total moles $$ n_{total} = 1.4162 + 0.1704 = 1.5866 \, mol $$ #### Step 3 — Atomic % $$ a_{Cu} = \frac{1.4162}{1.5866} \times 100 = 89.26\% $$ $$ a_{Ni} = \frac{0.1704}{1.5866} \times 100 = 10.74\% $$ #### Step 4 — Result $$ \boxed{90/10 \, \text{Cu-Ni} \Rightarrow 89.26 \text{ at\% Cu},\; 10.74 \text{ at\% Ni}} $$ > [!NOTE] > The atomic % is very close to the weight % in this case because Cu and Ni have nearly identical atomic masses. This is *not* true for brass — Zn (65.38) and Cu (63.55) are also close, but for **tin bronzes (Sn = 118.7)** the deviation is significant. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Tin Bronze Atomic % Check **Problem:** A 88/12 Cu-Sn alloy — convert weight % to atomic %. #### Step 1 — 100 g basis $$ n_{Cu} = \frac{88}{63.55} = 1.3850 \, mol $$ $$ n_{Sn} = \frac{12}{118.7} = 0.1011 \, mol $$ #### Step 2 — Total $$ n_{total} = 1.3850 + 0.1011 = 1.4861 \, mol $$ #### Step 3 — Atomic % $$ a_{Cu} = \frac{1.3850}{1.4861} \times 100 = 93.20\% $$ $$ a_{Sn} = \frac{0.1011}{1.4861} \times 100 = 6.80\% $$ $$ \boxed{88/12 \, \text{Cu-Sn} \Rightarrow 93.2 \text{ at\% Cu},\; 6.8 \text{ at\% Sn}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** Even though it's "12 wt%" tin, only **6.8 at%** Sn atoms are in the lattice. This dilution explains why α-tin bronzes remain single-phase FCC up to ~ 9 at% Sn (~ 16 wt%). --- ### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Brass Tensile Strength Prediction **Problem:** Predict the tensile strength of a **65/35 Cu-Zn brass** using the rule-of-mixture model, then compare with handbook value (~ 350–390 MPa). #### Step 1 — Apply formula $$ \sigma_{UTS} = 220 + 5 \cdot w_{Zn} $$ $$ \sigma_{UTS} = 220 + 5 \times 35 = 220 + 175 = 395 \, MPa $$ #### Step 2 — Compare $$ \boxed{395 \, MPa \;\; \text{vs experimental} \approx 370 \, MPa} $$ **Error ≈ 6.7%** — acceptable for KTU quick estimates. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Marine Application → Best Copper Alloy | Marine Component | Aggressive Factor | Recommended Alloy | Key Property Justification | |---|---|---|---| | Large ship propeller (cruiser, tanker) | Cavitation + corrosion fatigue | **Nickel-aluminium bronze (NAB)** | Highest $\sigma_{UTS}$, cavitation resistance, low CR | | Condenser tubes (steam plant) | Stagnant seawater, possible ammonia | **90/10 Cu-Ni with Fe** | Biofouling resistance, thermal conductivity | | Seawater cooling pipe (main line) | High-velocity flow | **70/30 Cu-Ni with Fe** | Tolerates up to 4.5 m/s | | Pump impeller (sea-water pump) | Sand-laden flow, cavitation | **Aluminium bronze** | Hardness, sand-erosion resistance | | Heat-exchanger tube sheets | Galvanic contact with steel tubes | **Naval brass (admiralty)** | Cheap, machinable, dezincification-inhibited | | Marine bolts, fasteners | Atmospheric + splash | **Silicon bronze C655** | Strength + corrosion resistance | | Decorative deck fittings | Atmospheric | **Cartridge brass 70/30** | Aesthetic, ductile | | Tank-cleaning non-sparking tools | Spark hazard in oil tanker | **Beryllium copper C172** | Hardness, non-magnetic, non-sparking | | Worm wheel for marine gear | High sliding wear | **Phosphor bronze (10% Sn)** | Low friction coefficient, wear resistance | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Copper Alloy Family Tree ```mermaid graph TD A["COPPER ALLOYS
Cu is principal element 60 percent by weight"] --> B["BRASSES
Alloying element Zn"] A --> C["TIN BRONZES
Alloying element Sn plus P"] A --> D["ALUMINIUM BRONZES
Alloying element Al plus Fe Ni"] A --> E["COPPER NICKELS
Alloying element Ni plus Fe Mn"] A --> F["SPECIAL BRONZES
Si Be Mn Pb"] B --> B1["Cartridge Brass 70 30"] B --> B2["Muntz Metal 60 40"] B --> B3["Naval Brass 60 39 1 Sn"] B --> B4["Admiralty Brass with As"] C --> C1["Phosphor Bronze 5 percent Sn"] C --> C2["Phosphor Bronze 8 percent Sn"] C --> C3["Phosphor Bronze 10 percent Sn"] D --> D1["Nickel Aluminium Bronze NAB"] D --> D2["Manganese Aluminium Bronze"] E --> E1["90 10 Cu Ni C70600"] E --> E2["70 30 Cu Ni C71500"] F --> F1["Silicon Bronze C655"] F --> F2["Beryllium Copper C172"] F --> F3["Leaded Bronze for bearings"] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Selection of Marine Copper Alloy ```mermaid flowchart TD IN1["Service Environment
seawater depth temperature"] --> DEC IN2["Mechanical Loads
static fatigue impact"] --> DEC IN3["Velocity and Flow Regime
laminar turbulent stagnant"] --> DEC IN4["Galvanic Coupling Risk
contact with Fe Al Zn"] --> DEC IN5["Biofouling Severity
tropical cold water"] --> DEC DEC{"Decision Matrix
Material Selection Engine"} --> OUT1 DEC --> OUT2 DEC --> OUT3 DEC --> OUT4 DEC --> OUT5 OUT1["Selected NAB Nickel Aluminium Bronze
for propeller and high stress part"] OUT2["Selected 90 10 Cu Ni with Fe
for sea water piping and condenser"] OUT3["Selected Naval Brass 60 39 1 Sn
for tube sheets and valves"] OUT4["Selected Phosphor Bronze 10 percent Sn
for worm gear and bushing"] OUT5["Selected Beryllium Copper C172
for non sparking tool on tanker"] ``` ### 4.3 Galvanic Series in Seawater — Block Schematic ```mermaid graph LR subgraph NOBLE_END ["NOBLE END Cathodic Protected"] N1["Graphite and Gold"] N2["Stainless Steel Passive"] N3["70 30 Cu Ni"] N4["90 10 Cu Ni"] N5["Phosphor Bronze"] N6["Aluminium Bronze NAB"] N7["Naval Brass"] N8["Pure Copper"] end subgraph ACTIVE_END ["ACTIVE END Anodic Corrodes"] A1["Lead and Tin"] A2["Cast Iron"] A3["Carbon Steel"] A4["Aluminium Alloy"] A5["Galvanised Steel"] A6["Zinc"] A7["Magnesium"] end N1 --> N2 --> N3 --> N4 --> N5 --> N6 --> N7 --> N8 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 ``` ### 4.4 Dezincification Mechanism — Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Stagnant or Low Velocity Seawater
low oxygen inside pores"] --> P1 P1["Selective Dissolution of Zn atoms
from the brass lattice"] --> P2 P2["Zn 2 plus leaves the surface
Cu atoms remain"] --> P3 P3["Porous Copper Sponge forms
loss of mechanical strength"] --> P4 P4["Dezincified region fails
under pressure or impact"] --> SOL SOL["Solution Add 1 percent Sn or 0.04 percent As
Use inhibited admiralty brass UNS C44300"] S1 --> SOL ``` ### 4.5 Protective Film Formation on 90/10 Cu-Ni ```mermaid graph TD F1["Clean Cu Ni surface exposed to sea water"] --> F2 F2["Initial formation of Cu2O inner oxide film
adherent and protective"] --> F3 F3["Outer layer of Fe oxy hydroxide and NiO
grows over weeks of exposure"] --> F4 F4["Mature duplex film blocks oxygen and chloride
reduces corrosion current by 100 to 1000 times"] --> F5 F5["Film is self healing if damaged
in presence of dissolved oxygen"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]** **List three reasons why copper and its alloys are extensively used in marine engineering applications.** **Model Answer (3 points × 1 mark):** 1. **High corrosion resistance in seawater** — Copper has a noble electrode potential (+0.337 V) and forms a self-healing protective oxide/patina film (Cu₂O + basic copper carbonate). 2. **Biofouling resistance** — Controlled release of Cu²⁺ ions creates a biocidal boundary layer that prevents settlement of marine organisms like barnacles and tubeworms. 3. **Excellent thermal and electrical conductivity** — High thermal conductivity (401 W/m·K) makes it ideal for condenser tubes and heat exchangers; ease of fabrication suits complex marine piping. > [!NOTE] > Any *three* well-explained points — e.g., (i) corrosion resistance, (ii) biofouling resistance, (iii) fabricability/conductivity, (iv) mechanical strength in alloyed form, (v) recyclability — earn full 3 marks. #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]** **What is dezincification? How is it prevented in marine brasses?** **Model Answer:** - **Dezincification (1 mark):** A form of *selective leaching* in which **zinc atoms are preferentially dissolved** from brass when exposed to stagnant, low-velocity, or mildly acidic seawater, leaving behind a porous, mechanically weak **copper sponge** that fails under load. - **Prevention (2 marks):** - **Add 1% tin (Sn)** → produces **Naval brass** (Cu-39Zn-1Sn) with improved resistance. - **Add 0.02–0.06% arsenic (As), antimony (Sb), or phosphorus (P)** → produces **inhibited admiralty brass** (e.g., UNS C44300, C44400, C44500) where these elements form a surface film that suppresses Zn dissolution. - **Design rule:** Maintain seawater velocity above **1 m/s** to prevent stagnation. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Sample QP aligned to 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the **composition, properties, and marine applications of copper-nickel alloys (90/10 and 70/30)**. Explain why small additions of **iron and manganese** are made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare **brasses, tin bronzes, and aluminium bronzes** as candidate materials for **marine propellers and pump impellers**. Justify the choice of **nickel-aluminium bronze (NAB)** for large propellers. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks** **Composition and properties (3 marks):** | Property | 90/10 Cu-Ni (C70600) | 70/30 Cu-Ni (C71500) | |---|---|---| | Composition | 90% Cu, 10% Ni, 1–1.8% Fe, ≤ 1% Mn | 70% Cu, 30% Ni, 0.4–1% Fe, ≤ 1% Mn | | Tensile strength | 280–350 MPa | 360–490 MPa | | Yield strength | 105–150 MPa | 125–205 MPa | | Density | 8.94 g/cm³ | 8.95 g/cm³ | | Max. seawater velocity | 3.5 m/s | 4.5 m/s | | Corrosion rate | 0.025–0.05 mm/yr | 0.015–0.04 mm/yr | **Marine applications (2 marks):** - **90/10 Cu-Ni** → seawater cooling pipes, heat-exchanger tubes, fire-fighting systems, ship hull sheathing. - **70/30 Cu-Ni** → high-velocity seawater piping, brine heaters, main condensers, chemical tanker cargo lines. **Role of Fe and Mn (2 marks):** - **Iron (1–1.8%)** refines grain size and reinforces the inner **Cu₂O + NiO protective film**, dramatically reducing erosion-corrosion at high flow rates. It forms fine **Fe-rich intermetallic dispersoids** that act as anchoring sites for the protective film. - **Manganese (≤ 1%)** acts as a **deoxidiser** during melting, removing dissolved sulphur and oxygen that would otherwise cause hot-cracking and porosity. It also improves weldability. --- ##### **Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks** **Comparison table (3 marks):** | Property | Brass (Naval 60-39-1) | Tin Bronze (10% Sn) | Al Bronze (NAB) | |---|---|---|---| | Tensile strength (MPa) | 350–450 | 350–500 | 650–750 | | Corrosion rate (mm/yr) | 0.04–0.10 | 0.05–0.10 | < 0.025 | | Cavitation resistance | Moderate | Moderate | **Excellent** | | Dezincification risk | Yes (in uninhibited) | No | No | | Biofouling resistance | Good | Good | Good | | Cost | Low | Medium | High | **Justification for NAB in large propellers (4 marks):** 1. **Highest strength-to-weight ratio** (σ_UTS ~ 700 MPa) with a density of ~ 7.6 g/cm³ — allows thinner, more efficient blade sections. 2. **Exceptional cavitation-erosion resistance** — the hard, Al-rich phases (κ-phases) plus adherent Al₂O₃ passive film resist the shock pressures of collapsing cavitation bubbles on the blade surface. 3. **Superior corrosion-fatigue performance** in seawater — critical because propellers experience **10⁸–10⁹ stress cycles** per voyage in a corrosive electrolyte. 4. **Biofouling resistance** from controlled Cu²⁺ release keeps the propeller surface smooth, maintaining hydrodynamic efficiency. 5. **Galvanic compatibility** with **Ni-Al-bronze** shafts, **Monel** fasteners, and the **Manganese-bronze** hub — minimises crevice corrosion at the propeller-hub interface. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Students often write *"NAB is corrosion resistant because it is noble"* — **this is wrong**. NAB's resistance comes from the **passive Al₂O₃ film**, not from nobility. Al itself is highly active; it is the oxide film that protects. Losing 1 mark here is the most common KTU valuation error. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the **mechanism of dezincification** in brasses. Discuss the **preventive measures** with reference to the use of inhibited admiralty brass and naval brass. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A marine **phosphor-bronze pump impeller** is observed to have a **corrosion current density of 4.2 µA/cm²** in seawater. The dominant anodic dissolution reaction is **Cu → Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻**. Calculate the **corrosion rate in mm/yr**. Given: atomic mass of Cu = 63.55 g/mol, density of phosphor bronze = 8.80 g/cm³, n = 2, K = 0.00327. Comment on the suitability of the impeller. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks** **Mechanism of dezincification (4 marks):** - Step 1: Seawater penetrates the brass surface; in **stagnant or low-oxygen conditions** (crevices, fouled areas, tube-sheet joints), Cl⁻ ions break down the protective ZnO layer. - Step 2: **Zn atoms are selectively oxidised and dissolved** as Zn²⁺ because Zn is more electrochemically active (E° = −0.76 V) than Cu (E° = +0.34 V). - Step 3: The dissolved Zn²⁺ ions diffuse away, leaving behind a **porous, mechanically weak copper sponge** on the surface. - Step 4: The dezincified layer **loses tensile strength and ductility** — eventual failure by cracking or perforation. **Preventive measures (3 marks):** - **Inhibited Admiralty brass (C44300, C44400, C44500):** addition of **0.02–0.06% As, Sb, or P** forms a thin, stable film that suppresses Zn dissolution by raising the Zn dissolution overpotential. - **Naval brass (Cu-39Zn-1Sn):** the **1% Sn addition** refines the grain structure and forms a **SnO₂-rich protective layer** that is more stable than ZnO in seawater. - **Design:** ensure **seawater velocity ≥ 1 m/s**; avoid stagnant zones; use **cathodic protection** in critical systems. --- ##### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks** **Given data:** - $M = 63.55$ g/mol - $i_{corr} = 4.2$ µA/cm² - $n = 2$ - $\rho = 8.80$ g/cm³ - $K = 0.00327$ **Step 1 — Substitute into corrosion-rate equation (3 marks):** $$ CR = \frac{K \cdot M \cdot i_{corr}}{n \cdot \rho} $$ $$ CR = \frac{0.00327 \times 63.55 \times 4.2}{2 \times 8.80} $$ **Step 2 — Numerator (2 marks):** $$ 0.00327 \times 63.55 = 0.20781 $$ $$ 0.20781 \times 4.2 = 0.87280 $$ **Step 3 — Denominator (1 mark):** $$ 2 \times 8.80 = 17.60 $$ **Step 4 — Division (1 mark):** $$ CR = \frac{0.87280}{17.60} = 0.0496 \, mm/yr $$ $$ \boxed{CR \approx 0.050 \, mm/yr \; \text{(i.e., 50 µm/yr)}} $$ **Comment on suitability (extra credit line — 1 mark in 7-mark distribution):** This is a **very low corrosion rate** — for a typical **20 mm-thick impeller wall**, the service life is roughly **20 / 0.05 ≈ 400 years** under uniform corrosion. However, the impeller is still vulnerable to **cavitation-erosion and galvanic coupling** at the shaft interface; regular inspection and **sacrificial Zn anodes** are recommended. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall (B):** Students forget to use the **density of the alloy**, not pure copper. Using 8.96 g/cm³ instead of 8.80 g/cm³ gives CR ≈ 0.0486 mm/yr (≈ 2% error) — minor in KTU, but a similar error in **mass-based** corrosion calculations can lead to a full 2-mark loss. Always check whether the question gives alloy density or pure-metal density. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this section and read it 30 minutes before the KTU exam.** - [ ] **Pure Cu:** density 8.96 g/cm³, melting point 1084.62 °C, E° = +0.337 V — **noble, FCC, soft, conductive**. - [ ] **Brass** = Cu + Zn. **Naval brass = +1% Sn.** **Admiralty brass = +1% Sn + 0.04% As** (inhibited). - [ ] **Dezincification** occurs in brasses with > 15% Zn in **stagnant seawater**; prevented by **Sn/As/Sb/P additions** and **flow velocity > 1 m/s**. - [ ] **Phosphor bronze** = Cu + Sn (5–12%) + 0.1–0.5% P. Used for **bearings, gears, springs, propeller blades**. - [ ] **Aluminium bronze (NAB)** = Cu + 9–12% Al + 4–6% Fe + 4–6% Ni. Used for **large marine propellers, pump impellers**. σ_UTS ≈ 700 MPa. - [ ] **90/10 Cu-Ni (C70600)** = seawater piping, condenser tubes, max velocity 3.5 m/s. - [ ] **70/30 Cu-Ni (C71500)** = high-velocity piping, max velocity 4.5 m/s. - [ ] **Fe and Mn additions** in Cu-Ni refine grain and **reinforce the protective film**. - [ ] **Beryllium copper C172** = non-sparking tools on **oil tankers and gas carriers**. - [ ] **Silicon bronze C655** = marine bolts, nuts, chemical hardware. - [ ] **Galvanic series:** Cu alloys are **noble** — protect them when coupled to **steel, Al, Zn**; **never** couple Cu to Al directly in seawater. - [ ] **Corrosion rate formula:** $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot M \cdot i_{corr}}{n \cdot \rho}$ — remember units (µA/cm², g/mol, g/cm³, mm/yr). - [ ] **Biofouling resistance** of copper alloys comes from **Cu²⁺ ion release**, not from the alloy being "smooth". - [ ] **KTU-mandated UNS designations to memorise:** C70600 (90/10 Cu-Ni), C71500 (70/30 Cu-Ni), C95500 (NAB), C51000 (5% Phos. bronze), C52400 (10% Phos. bronze), C17200 (Be-Cu), C65500 (Si-bronze), C44300 (Admiralty brass), C46400 (Naval brass). - [ ] **At. % vs Wt.% conversion** is critical — use $a_x = \dfrac{w_x / M_x}{\sum (w_i / M_i)} \times 100$ for alloy stoichiometry problems.

topics.title: Zinc
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Zinc

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zinc (Zn)** is a bluish-white, lustrous, diamagnetic metal belonging to **Group 12 (IIB)** of the periodic table, with atomic number **30** and standard atomic weight **65.38 g/mol**. In marine corrosion engineering, Zinc is classified as the **most widely deployed galvanic (sacrificial) anode material** for the cathodic protection of steel hulls, offshore structures, ballast tanks, and submerged appendages of ships, owing to its highly negative standard electrode potential of **−0.76 V vs SHE** (and approximately **−1.10 V vs SHE** in seawater against a Silver/Silver Chloride reference).

In the specific context of **PENST414 – Shipbuilding Materials, Corrosion Prevention and Protection (Module 2)**, Zinc is studied as a **reactive, electrochemically active non-ferrous metal** that protects ferrous structures by preferentially corroding (sacrificing) itself, while simultaneously acting as a base for protective coating systems (galvanizing, zinc-rich primers, and zinc-silicate coatings).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Selfless Bodyguard" Analogy:** Imagine a bodyguard (Zinc) who willingly takes a bullet to save a VIP (Steel Hull). Zinc "absorbs" the corrosion damage so that the protected steel structure remains intact. The bodyguard eventually "falls" (consumes himself) and must be replaced. The principle of **sacrificial protection** is exactly this — the anode dissolves so the cathode survives.

In engineering terms, when zinc and steel are electrically coupled in an electrolyte (seawater):
- **Zinc becomes the Anode** (corrodes) — the bodyguard.
- **Steel becomes the Cathode** (protected) — the VIP.
- Electrons flow from Zn → Fe, suppressing the anodic reaction (Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻) on the steel.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Mechanical Properties of Zinc (Syllabus Highlights)

| Property | Value | Engineering Significance |
|----------|-------|--------------------------|
| Atomic Number | **30** | Group 12 transition metal |
| Atomic Weight | **65.38 g/mol** | Used in Faraday's law calculations |
| Density | **7.14 g/cm³** (7.134 g/cm³ at 25 °C) | Higher than Al (2.70), lower than Fe (7.87) |
| Melting Point | **419.5 °C (692.65 K)** | Low — easy to cast for anodes |
| Boiling Point | **907 °C** | — |
| Crystal Structure | **HCP (Hexagonal Close-Packed)** | — |
| Standard Electrode Potential (E°) | **−0.76 V vs SHE** | Strong driving force for protection |
| Practical Potential in Seawater | **−1.10 V vs Ag/AgCl** | Used as design reference |
| Electrical Resistivity | **5.9 µΩ·cm** at 20 °C | Conductive — enables electron flow |
| Thermal Expansion | **30 × 10⁻⁶ /K** | Compatible with steel coatings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Zinc is the historical benchmark** for sacrificial anodes. It was the **first material** (Thomas Edison, 1885; refined by K.G. Compton, 1940s) used for the cathodic protection of ship hulls and submarine pipelines, and continues to dominate **warm, low-salinity, and tropical waters** where aluminium anodes may passivate.

## 1.4 Why Zinc is Chosen for Shipbuilding Applications

1. **Reliable electrochemical potential** in seawater (stable, non-passivating).
2. **Predictable dissolution** — forms soft, non-adherent, but **non-protective white corrosion product** (Zn(OH)₂, ZnO, Zn₅(OH)₈Cl₂·H₂O — Zinc hydroxychloride / Simonkolleite).
3. **Low cost** compared to magnesium and platinum.
4. **Easy casting** due to low melting point (419.5 °C).
5. **Safe handling** — non-toxic in metallic form, non-sparking.
6. **Compatibility with anti-fouling paint systems** when used as a coating.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electrochemical Basis of Zinc as a Sacrificial Anode

When a zinc anode and a steel cathode are immersed in seawater and connected externally by a wire, the following half-cell reactions occur:

### Anodic Reaction (Zinc Dissolution — Oxidation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Zn} &\longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^{-} && \text{(Anode)} \\
\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^{-} &\longrightarrow \text{Zn(OH)}_{2 (s)} && \text{(Corrosion product formation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Cathodic Reaction (Steel Protection — Reduction)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O}_{2} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + 4e^{-} &\longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-} && \text{(Aerated seawater - most common)} \\
2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + 2e^{-} &\longrightarrow \text{H}_{2} + 2\text{OH}^{-} && \text{(Anaerobic / buried conditions)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Net Cell Reaction

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\text{Zn} + \text{O}_{2} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} &\longrightarrow 2\text{Zn(OH)}_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **driving voltage (cell EMF)** between zinc and steel in seawater is approximately **0.25 – 0.30 V**. This potential difference is what physically forces electrons to migrate from zinc to steel, suppressing the oxidation of iron.

## 2.2 Critical Performance Parameters of Zinc Anodes

### 2.2.1 Theoretical Electrochemical Capacity (Faraday's Law Applied)

According to **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis**, the mass of metal dissolved is directly proportional to the total charge passed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of zinc consumed (grams)
- $I$ = protective current (Amperes)
- $t$ = time (seconds)
- $M$ = molar mass of zinc = **65.38 g/mol**
- $n$ = valency = **2** (Zn → Zn²⁺)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**

**Theoretical Capacity of Zinc** (charge produced per kg of metal):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{theoretical}} &= \frac{n \cdot F}{M} = \frac{2 \times 96{,}485}{65.38} \\
&= 2{,}951.6 \text{ A·h/kg} \quad (\text{or } 820 \text{ A·h/g})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical Capacity of Zinc (used in design):** **780 A·h/kg** (typical for high-purity zinc per **MIL-A-18001K** and **ASTM B418 Type I**).

### 2.2.2 Anode Utilization Efficiency (η)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{C_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

For **pure zinc anodes**: η = **~ 95 %** (one of the highest among sacrificial materials).

### 2.2.3 Consumption Rate (CR)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CR} &= \frac{8{,}760}{C_{\text{actual}} \times \eta} \quad \text{[kg per A·year]}
\end{aligned}
$$

For zinc: **CR ≈ 11.2 kg/A·year** (at 100 % utilization; practical value ≈ 11.8 kg/A·year).

### 2.2.4 Design Current Requirement (I) for Ship Hull

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= A_{\text{wetted}} \times i_{\text{design}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $i_{\text{design}}$ = protective current density ≈ **0.010 – 0.030 A/m²** for bare steel hull in seawater; **0.001 – 0.010 A/m²** for coated hull.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value for Zinc |
|---|---------------------|------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Theoretical Capacity | $C = \frac{nF}{M}$ | **2,951.6 A·h/kg** |
| 2 | Practical Capacity | $C_{\text{prac}}$ | **780 A·h/kg** |
| 3 | Anode Efficiency | $\eta = C_{\text{act}}/C_{\text{theo}}$ | **≥ 95 %** |
| 4 | Consumption Rate | $\text{CR} = \frac{8760}{C_{\text{prac}} \cdot \eta}$ | **~11.8 kg/A·yr** |
| 5 | Mass of Anode Required | $m = \frac{I \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C_{\text{prac}} \cdot \eta \cdot U_f}$ | Design-dependent |
| 6 | Number of Anodes | $N = m / m_{\text{per anode}}$ | — |
| 7 | Anode Life | $L = \frac{m \cdot C_{\text{prac}} \cdot \eta}{I \cdot 8760}$ | Years |
| 8 | Driving Voltage | $\Delta V = E_{\text{Zn}} - E_{\text{Fe}}$ in SW | **0.25 – 0.30 V** |
| 9 | Closed-Circuit Anode Potential | $E_{\text{cc}}$ vs Ag/AgCl | **−1.10 V** |
| 10 | Open-Circuit Anode Potential | $E_{\text{oc}}$ vs Ag/AgCl | **−1.05 to −1.10 V** |

> In the mass formula, $T$ = design life in years; $U_f$ = utilization factor (typically **0.85** for zinc).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Hull Protection** — Zinc anodes bolted/welded to ship hull, rudder, propeller shaft, sea chests.
2. **Ballast Tanks** — Zinc or aluminium anodes inside ballast tanks (KTU Module 2 emphasis).
3. **Offshore Platforms** — Jacket structures in shallow & warm waters.
4. **Pipelines & Storage Tanks** — Buried and submerged.
5. **Zinc-Rich Coatings** — Single-component inorganic (ethyl silicate) or organic (epoxy) primers with 85–95 % zinc dust in the dry film; provide galvanic protection to underlying steel.
6. **Hot-Dip Galvanizing** — Steel dipped in molten zinc (~ 450 °C) for atmospheric corrosion resistance.
7. **Electrogalvanizing / Electroplating** — Thin zinc coatings (5–25 µm) for fasteners, plates, and deck fittings.

## 2.5 Comparison: Zinc vs Aluminium vs Magnesium Anodes

| Property | Zinc (Zn) | Aluminium (Al) | Magnesium (Mg) |
|----------|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| Density (g/cm³) | **7.14** | 2.70 | 1.74 |
| Potential (V vs Ag/AgCl in SW) | **−1.10** | −1.05 to −1.15 | −1.50 |
| Theoretical Capacity (A·h/kg) | **2,951** | 2,980 | 2,205 |
| Practical Capacity (A·h/kg) | **780** | 1,200 – 2,600 | 1,100 – 1,230 |
| Efficiency (%) | **95** | 50 – 95 | 50 – 55 |
| Consumption (kg/A·yr) | **11.8** | 3.4 – 7.5 | ~7.9 |
| Best Use | Warm/Mediterranean seas | Cold/deep oceans | Soil, low-resistivity electrolytes |
| Passivation Risk | **Low** | High (must alloy with In, Hg, Sn) | None |
| Cost (relative) | **Low** | Medium | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zinc remains the standard for warm, low-chlorinity, and tropical waters**. It is **NOT recommended** for temperatures > 50 °C (intergranular corrosion / reversal of polarity may occur where Fe becomes anodic to Zn) and for freshwater cathodic protection (use Mg instead).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Capacity of Zinc

Starting from Faraday's First Law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **electrochemical capacity** is defined as the charge delivered per unit mass of anode dissolved. So charge $Q = I \cdot t$ is divided by mass $m$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{Q}{m} = \frac{I \cdot t}{\frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}} = \frac{n \cdot F}{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting values for zinc:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Zn}} &= \frac{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}}{65.38 \text{ g/mol}} \\
&= \frac{192{,}970}{65.38} \text{ C/g} \\
&= 2{,}951.6 \text{ C/g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to **ampere-hours** (1 A·h = 3,600 C):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Zn}} &= \frac{2{,}951.6 \text{ C/g}}{3{,}600 \text{ C/A·h}} \\
&= 0.820 \text{ A·h/g} \\
&= 820 \text{ A·h/kg} \quad \text{(theoretical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since practical capacity is lower due to self-corrosion and oxide layers, the **design value = 780 A·h/kg**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Consumption Rate (kg per Ampere-Year)

Annual charge delivered by a current of 1 A:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{annual}} &= I \times 8760 \text{ h} = 1 \times 8760 = 8{,}760 \text{ A·h/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

Mass dissolved per year:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{annual}} &= \frac{Q_{\text{annual}}}{C_{\text{prac}} \cdot \eta} \\
&= \frac{8{,}760}{780 \times 0.95} \\
&= \frac{8{,}760}{741} \\
&= 11.82 \text{ kg/A·yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering rule of thumb:** A zinc sacrificial anode will be consumed at approximately **11.8 kg per ampere of current per year** of continuous service.

## 3.3 Worked Problem 1 — Anode Mass Calculation for a Ship Hull

> **Problem:** A ship has a bare steel wetted hull area of **2,500 m²**. The seawater current density required for full cathodic protection is **0.020 A/m²**. The ship is in dry-dock for **5 years** before anode replacement. Anode utilization factor = 0.85; practical capacity of zinc = 780 A·h/kg. Calculate the **total mass of zinc anodes required** and the **number of standard 25 kg zinc anodes** to be fitted.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Protective Current Required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= A \times i = 2{,}500 \text{ m}^{2} \times 0.020 \text{ A/m}^{2} \\
I &= 50 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Charge Over 5 Years:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= I \times T \times 8760 \text{ h/yr} \\
&= 50 \times 5 \times 8{,}760 \\
&= 2{,}190{,}000 \text{ A·h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of Zinc Required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{Q}{C_{\text{prac}} \cdot \eta \cdot U_f} \\
&= \frac{2{,}190{,}000}{780 \times 0.95 \times 0.85} \\
&= \frac{2{,}190{,}000}{629.85} \\
&\approx 3{,}477 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of 25 kg Anodes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{3{,}477}{25} \\
&= 139.1 \Rightarrow \boxed{140 \text{ anodes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Problem 2 — Anode Life Calculation

> **Problem:** A **5 kg zinc anode** is fitted to a rudder. The current output measured is **0.20 A**. Practical capacity = 780 A·h/kg; efficiency = 95 %; utilization factor = 0.85. Determine the **service life** of the anode in years.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{m \times C_{\text{prac}} \times \eta \times U_f}{I \times 8760} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 780 \times 0.95 \times 0.85}{0.20 \times 8{,}760} \\
&= \frac{3{,}149.25}{1{,}752} \\
&= 1.797 \text{ years} \approx \boxed{1 \text{ year } 9.7 \text{ months}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Problem 3 — Verify Driving Voltage

> **Problem:** In seawater at 25 °C, the corrosion potential of mild steel is −0.65 V (Ag/AgCl) and that of zinc is −1.10 V (Ag/AgCl). Calculate the **driving EMF** of the Zn–Fe galvanic couple.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}} \\
&= (-0.65) - (-1.10) \\
&= +0.45 \text{ V (theoretical open-circuit)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Under closed-circuit operation, accounting for IR drops, the **net driving voltage at the protected structure** is typically **0.20 – 0.30 V**, sufficient to suppress the corrosion of steel (which requires a protective polarization of −0.80 V vs Ag/AgCl).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Zinc Anode Manufacturing Process (for Shipbuilding)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Zinc Ingots 99.99 percent purity] --> B[Alloying Additives Al Cd and Fe small amounts]
    B --> C[Melting Furnace 450 to 500 degree C]
    C --> D[Sand or Steel Mold Casting]
    D --> E[Core Preparation Steel insert with welded cable]
    E --> F[Anode Casting in Mold]
    F --> G[Cooling and Cleaning]
    G --> H[Quality Testing ASTM B418 MIL A 18001K]
    H --> I[Storage and Dispatch]
    I --> J[Shipyard Installation]
    J --> K[Hull Welding or Bolting]
    K --> L[Submerged Service Cathodic Protection Active]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mechanism of Zinc Galvanic Protection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Seawater[Seawater Electrolyte]
        O2[Dissolved Oxygen] --> CR[Cathodic Reaction on Steel Hull]
    end
    subgraph ProtectedStructure[Steel Hull Cathode Protected]
        CR --> SH[Steel Surface at 0.80 V AgAgCl no corrosion]
    end
    subgraph ZincAnode[Zinc Sacrificial Anode]
        ZA[Zn dissolves to Zn2 plus] --> ZO[Zinc Hydroxide White Corrosion Product]
    end
    SH <-->|Electron flow through wire| ZA
    ZA -->|Drives current| SH
    O2 -->|Accepts electrons at cathode| CR
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Hot-Dip Galvanizing Process Flow (Zinc Coating on Steel)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Steel Component Surface Preparation] --> B[Degreasing Alkaline Bath]
    B --> C[Pickling HCl or H2SO4 10 percent]
    C --> D[Rinsing with Water]
    D --> E[Fluxing Zinc Ammonium Chloride]
    E --> F[Galvanizing Bath Molten Zinc at 449 degree C]
    F --> G[Withdrawal at Controlled Rate]
    G --> H[Quenching or Air Cooling]
    H --> I[Inspection Coating Thickness 50 to 100 micron]
    I --> J[Finished Galvanized Steel for Shipbuilding]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Cathodic Protection System Layout on a Ship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HULL[Ship Hull Steel Structure]
        SH1[Forward Wetted Hull]
        SH2[Midship Wetted Hull]
        SH3[Aft Wetted Hull]
        SH4[Rudder and Propeller]
    end
    ZA1[Zinc Anode 1] --> SH1
    ZA2[Zinc Anode 2] --> SH2
    ZA3[Zinc Anode 3] --> SH3
    ZA4[Zinc Anode 4] --> SH4
    ZA5[Zinc Anode 5 Sea Chest] --> SH2
    ZA6[Zinc Anode 6 Ballast Tank] --> SH2
    subgraph REF[Reference and Monitoring]
        REF1[AgAgCl Reference Electrode]
        MON[Monitoring Junction Box]
    end
    REF1 --> MON
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Similar)]** What is a sacrificial anode? Why is zinc commonly used as a sacrificial anode in ship hull protection?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **sacrificial anode** is a metal that is intentionally allowed to corrode in preference to a more important metal structure, by virtue of its more negative electrode potential. It is electrically connected to the protected structure, forming a galvanic cell in which the anode dissolves and supplies protective current to the cathode.

Zinc is commonly used in ship hull protection because:
1. **(1 mark)** It has a sufficiently negative electrode potential (**−1.10 V vs Ag/AgCl in seawater**), more active than steel (−0.65 V), providing a **driving voltage of ~0.3 V** for protection.
2. **(1 mark)** It has a **high practical electrochemical capacity of ~780 A·h/kg** and **utilization efficiency of ~95 %**, ensuring long service life.
3. **(1 mark)** It is **economical, non-toxic, easy to cast** (m.p. 419.5 °C), and does not passivate in seawater, making it ideal for marine applications in **warm and tropical waters**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Similar)]** State Faraday's law and compute the theoretical electrochemical capacity of zinc.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Faraday's First Law:** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge passed through the electrolyte.

**Mathematical form:** $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ **(1 mark)**

**Theoretical Capacity of Zinc (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{nF}{M} = \frac{2 \times 96{,}485}{65.38} \\
&= 2{,}951.6 \text{ C/g} = 0.820 \text{ A·h/g} \\
&= 820 \text{ A·h/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** Explain the **electrochemical principle of cathodic protection using zinc sacrificial anodes**, with relevant half-cell reactions and a sketch of the mechanism on a ship hull. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship's underwater hull has a wetted area of **3,000 m²**, coated with an anti-corrosive paint (design current density = **0.015 A/m²**). The vessel operates continuously for **4 years** between dry-dockings. Using zinc anodes (practical capacity = 780 A·h/kg, efficiency = 95 %, utilization factor = 0.85), calculate: **(i)** total protective current, **(ii)** total anode mass required, and **(iii)** number of **30 kg standard zinc anodes**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Electrochemical Principle of Zinc Sacrificial Anode (7 marks):**

1. **(2 marks)** When zinc and steel are electrically connected in seawater, zinc becomes the **anode** (more electronegative) and steel becomes the **cathode**. The potential difference of **0.25–0.30 V** drives a DC current through the seawater.

2. **(2 marks)** The reactions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Anode:} \quad & \text{Zn} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \\
\text{Cathode:} \quad & \text{O}_{2} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-} \\
\text{Net:} \quad & 2\text{Zn} + \text{O}_{2} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\text{Zn(OH)}_{2 (s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

3. **(2 marks)** The steel hull is polarized to approximately **−0.80 V vs Ag/AgCl**, suppressing iron dissolution. The **white corrosion product** (Zn(OH)₂ / Zn₅(OH)₈Cl₂·H₂O) settles near the anode. Anodes are distributed along the hull, rudder, propeller shaft brackets, and sea chests. Their life depends on the **mass, current demand, and utilization factor**.

4. **(1 mark)** Sketch: ship hull section with Zn anodes bolted to the hull, electron arrow from Zn → Steel, O₂ reduction at steel surface.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total protective current (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= 3{,}000 \times 0.015 = 45 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total anode mass (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 45 \times 4 \times 8{,}760 = 1{,}576{,}800 \text{ A·h} \\
m &= \frac{1{,}576{,}800}{780 \times 0.95 \times 0.85} = \frac{1{,}576{,}800}{629.85} \\
&\approx 2{,}503.4 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Number of 30 kg anodes (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{2{,}503.4}{30} = 83.4 \Rightarrow \boxed{84 \text{ anodes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Adapted)]** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Describe the **specifications, composition, and types of zinc anodes** used in marine applications. Compare the **advantages and limitations of zinc** as a sacrificial anode material. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two metallic structures, **steel hull and a copper-nickel pipe**, are in seawater and electrically connected. Discuss the consequences and the **remedial cathodic protection strategy using zinc anodes**, including the relevant electrochemical reactions and design parameters. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Zinc Anode Specifications & Comparison (7 marks):**

1. **(2 marks)** **Specifications** — The two governing standards are:
   - **MIL-A-18001K** (US Military) — High-purity zinc with **Fe ≤ 0.005 %**, **Cu ≤ 0.005 %**, **Pb ≤ 0.006 %**, **Cd 0.025–0.15 %** (added to reduce intergranular corrosion), **Al 0.10–0.50 %**.
   - **ASTM B418, Type I** (cast) and **Type II** (wrought).

2. **(2 marks)** **Types of Zinc Anodes:**
   - **Long-slender anodes** for hulls.
   - **Flush-mounted disc anodes** for special fittings.
   - **Solder-type anodes** for propeller shaft grounding brushes.
   - **Bolt-on / Weld-on anodes** for ship hull, rudder, and sea-chest grids.

3. **(2 marks)** **Advantages:** low cost, high efficiency (~95 %), stable potential, no passivation, non-toxic, predictable performance.
   **Limitations:** heavy (density 7.14 g/cm³), limited capacity compared to Al, **not suitable above 50 °C** (polarity reversal — Fe becomes anodic to Zn), not ideal for freshwater (use Mg).

4. **(1 mark)** Zinc anodes are typically supplied as **pre-packaged assemblies with backfill (gypsum + bentonite)** for buried applications.

**(b) Galvanic Couple between Steel and Cu-Ni — Remedial Strategy (7 marks):**

1. **(2 marks)** When **Cu-Ni (E ≈ −0.30 V vs Ag/AgCl)** is coupled to **steel (E ≈ −0.65 V vs Ag/AgCl)** in seawater, **steel becomes the anode and corrodes rapidly** (galvanic corrosion). The Cu-Ni acts as the cathode and is unaffected.

2. **(2 marks)** **Remedial Strategy — Cathodic Protection with Zinc Anodes:**
   - Bolt zinc anodes **directly to the steel hull, NOT the Cu-Ni pipe**.
   - The zinc (E = −1.10 V) becomes the new anode; both **steel and Cu-Ni become cathodes** (since Cu-Ni is more noble than Zn).
   - The system polarizes the steel to **−0.80 V vs Ag/AgCl**, arresting corrosion.

3. **(2 marks)** **Reactions and Design Parameters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Anode:} \quad & \text{Zn} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \\
\text{Cathode (steel and Cu-Ni):} \quad & \text{O}_{2} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}
\end{aligned}
$$

   - **Design current density:** 0.020 A/m² for bare steel; 0.005 A/m² for Cu-Ni (cathodic side).
   - **Driving voltage** for steel protection: 0.30 V; for Cu-Ni polarization: 0.80 V.
   - Anode life computed using the standard formula $L = \frac{m \cdot C \cdot \eta \cdot U_f}{I \cdot 8760}$.

4. **(1 mark)** **Best practice:** Add **dielectric (insulating) flanges/isolating joints** between the steel hull and Cu-Ni piping to break the original galvanic couple, and then **install separate zinc anodes** for each structure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing theoretical vs practical capacity** — Always use **780 A·h/kg** (practical), NOT 820 A·h/kg (theoretical) in design.
> 2. **Forgetting the utilization factor $U_f$** — most past papers award 1 mark for this.
> 3. **Polarity reversal of Zn above 50 °C** — examiners frequently test this. Above 50 °C, iron becomes anodic to zinc, leading to **accelerated hull corrosion**, not protection.
> 4. **Confusing open-circuit (OCP) and closed-circuit potentials (CCP)** — design must use **CCP = −1.10 V (Ag/AgCl)**.
> 5. **Not distinguishing Zn from Al/Mg** — Zn cannot be used in freshwater or cold high-resistivity soils.
> 6. **Forgetting to state units** in numerical answers — KTU deducts 0.5 marks for missing units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Zinc (Zn)** is a bluish-white metal, atomic number **30**, atomic weight **65.38 g/mol**, density **7.14 g/cm³**, melting point **419.5 °C**.
- Electrode potential in seawater: **−1.10 V vs Ag/AgCl**; standard potential **−0.76 V vs SHE**.
- **Zinc is the standard sacrificial anode** for warm, tropical, and Mediterranean marine waters.
- **Driving voltage** of Zn–Fe couple: **0.25 – 0.30 V** in seawater.
- **Theoretical capacity** of Zn: **820 A·h/kg**; **Practical capacity**: **780 A·h/kg**.
- **Faraday's Law:** $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ ; Capacity $C = \frac{nF}{M}$.
- **Anode Efficiency (η)** of pure zinc: **≥ 95 %**.
- **Consumption rate** of zinc: **~11.8 kg/A·yr**.
- **Mass formula:** $m = \frac{I \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C_{\text{prac}} \cdot \eta \cdot U_f}$.
- **Anode life formula:** $L = \frac{m \cdot C \cdot \eta \cdot U_f}{I \cdot 8760}$.
- **Key standards:** **MIL-A-18001K** and **ASTM B418 (Type I cast / Type II wrought)**.
- **Critical purity limits:** Fe ≤ 0.005 %, Cu ≤ 0.005 %, Pb ≤ 0.006 % (impurities cause **intergranular attack**).
- **Alloying elements:** Cd (0.025–0.15 %) and Al (0.10–0.50 %) improve dissolution uniformity.
- **Polarity reversal** occurs at temperatures > **50 °C** — Zn becomes cathodic, steel becomes anodic.
- **Not suitable** for freshwater or cold high-resistivity soils (use Mg); for deep cold oceans, use Al-Zn-In anodes.
- **Forms of Zn for shipbuilding:** Cast anodes, hot-dip galvanizing, electrogalvanizing, zinc-rich primers (epoxy/ethyl silicate, 85–95 % Zn dust in dry film).
- **Design current density** for cathodic protection: **0.010–0.030 A/m²** (bare steel); **0.001–0.010 A/m²** (coated).
- **Corrosion products:** Zn(OH)₂, ZnO, Zn₅(OH)₈Cl₂·H₂O (Simonkolleite) — soft, white, non-protective, dispersed by seawater.
- **Applications in shipbuilding:** Hull plating, rudder, propeller shaft, sea chests, ballast tanks, offshore jacket structures, pier piles, harbour dolphins.
- **Reference electrode for field monitoring:** Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl) — protected potential **−0.80 V vs Ag/AgCl**.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Do not directly connect zinc anodes to copper-based alloys (Cu-Ni) without isolation — install dielectric breaks first.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aluminium and its alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Aluminium and its alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Aluminium and its Alloys – Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Aluminium (Al)** is a soft, lightweight, silvery-white, non-ferrous, non-magnetic, ductile metal belonging to the **Boron Group (Group 13)** of the periodic table, with atomic number **13** and standard atomic weight **26.981 g/mol**. In the context of marine and shipbuilding engineering, aluminium refers to a family of aluminium-based alloys used as a primary structural material for hulls, superstructures, decks, and outfit components of small-to-medium vessels, fast ferries, naval craft, and offshore platforms.

An **aluminium alloy** is a chemical composition in which aluminium is the predominant metal (typically > **90%** by mass) and one or more alloying elements—such as **Copper (Cu), Magnesium (Mg), Silicon (Si), Manganese (Mn), Zinc (Zn)**—are deliberately added to enhance mechanical strength, corrosion resistance, castability, weldability, or formability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The syllabus mandates coverage of (i) properties of pure aluminium, (ii) classification of aluminium alloys (wrought and cast), (iii) heat-treatable vs. non-heat-treatable alloys, (iv) standard designation systems (ISO/AA/Indian), (v) marine-grade alloys, and (vi) specific applications in shipbuilding.

## 1.2 Core Properties of Pure Aluminium

| Property | Value/Description |
|---|---|
| Density | **2.70 g/cm³** (≈ 1/3 that of steel) |
| Melting Point | **660.3 °C** |
| Young's Modulus | **69 GPa** |
| Tensile Strength (pure, annealed) | **90 MPa** |
| Yield Strength (pure, annealed) | **35 MPa** |
| Elongation | **30 – 45 %** |
| Electrical Conductivity | **61 % IACS** (International Annealed Copper Standard) |
| Thermal Conductivity | **237 W/m·K** |
| Coefficient of Thermal Expansion | **23.1 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** |
| Crystal Structure | **FCC (Face Centred Cubic)** |
| Corrosion Behaviour | Forms a thin, adherent, self-healing **Al₂O₃** oxide film (≈ 4 nm thick) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle vs. Motorcycle" Idea:**
> Think of **pure aluminium** like a *bare bicycle frame*: light, easy to pedal (form), but not strong enough for highway speeds (load). Now imagine **aluminium alloys** as *motorcycle frames*: by adding specific alloying elements (like adding an engine, better brakes, and stronger wheels), you dramatically boost strength, speed, and durability—without losing the *lightweight advantage*.
>
> **In shipbuilding:** A pure aluminium superstructure is like the bicycle—too soft to bear loads. A **marine-grade Al-Mg alloy (e.g., AA 5083)** is like the motorcycle—lightweight *and* strong enough to withstand wave slamming, cargo loads, and salt-spray corrosion for decades.

## 1.4 Why Aluminium is Critical for Shipbuilding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Drivers for Aluminium in Marine Applications:**
> 1. **Weight Reduction → Fuel Economy / Speed:** Every **1 tonne** of weight saved in hull structure can increase payload by **1 tonne** or reduce fuel consumption by **~3–5%** in high-speed vessels.
> 2. **High Strength-to-Weight Ratio:** Marine alloys such as **AA 5083-H116** deliver yield strengths up to **215 MPa** at only **2.66 g/cm³** density.
> 3. **Excellent Corrosion Resistance in Seawater:** Self-passivating **Al₂O₃** layer renews instantly if scratched, providing long-term protection in marine atmospheres.
> 4. **Non-Magnetic & Non-Sparking:** Critical for naval mine countermeasure vessels (MCMVs) and ammunition-carrying boats.
> 5. **Ease of Fabrication:** Excellent formability, weldability (especially 5xxx and 6xxx series), and machinability reduce construction time and cost.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Aluminium Alloys

Aluminium alloys are classified into **two primary families** based on the primary metalworking process:

### A. Wrought Alloys (Mechanical Working: Rolling, Extrusion, Forging)
These are designated by a **4-digit numerical system** developed by the **Aluminium Association (AA)** and adopted globally (also used in India via IS 617-1975 alignment with AA):

| Series | Principal Alloying Element | Heat-Treatable? | Marine Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1xxx** | ≥ 99.00 % pure Al | No | Limited (foil, electrical) |
| **2xxx** | Cu | **Yes** | Aircraft (low marine use – SCC risk) |
| **3xxx** | Mn | No | Sheet, boat hulls (moderate) |
| **4xxx** | Si | Yes (some) | Welding filler wires (e.g., AA 4043) |
| **5xxx** | **Mg** | **No** | **★ Most widely used marine alloys** |
| **6xxx** | Mg + Si | **Yes** | Ship superstructures, masts |
| **7xxx** | Zn | **Yes** | High-strength (limited marine) |
| **8xxx** | Other (Li, Fe, etc.) | Variable | Aerospace, specialty |

### B. Cast Alloys (Molten metal poured into moulds)
Designated by a **3-digit number followed by a decimal** (e.g., **A356.0**, **A514.0**). The first digit indicates the alloy family similar to wrought series. Used for marine engine blocks, winch housings, propellers (in small craft), and complex-shaped fittings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine "Workhorses":**
> - **AA 5083** (Al–4.4 % Mg–0.7 % Mn) – Hull plates of fast ferries, naval vessels.
> - **AA 5086** (Al–4.0 % Mg) – Lower-magnesium variant for cryogenic & marine.
> - **AA 5454** (Al–2.7 % Mg–0.8 % Mn) – Pressure vessels, storage tanks.
> - **AA 5456** (Al–5.1 % Mg) – High-strength hulls, armour plating.
> - **AA 6061** (Al–1.0 % Mg–0.6 % Si) – Superstructures, deck fittings.
> - **AA 6082** (Al–1.0 % Mg–0.9 % Si–0.7 % Mn) – European standard structural alloy (popular in Indian shipyards).

## 2.2 Heat-Treatable vs. Non-Heat-Treatable Alloys

### Non-Heat-Treatable Alloys (1xxx, 3xxx, 4xxx some, 5xxx)
Strength is enhanced only by **cold working (strain hardening)** followed by **stabilizing/annealing tempers** (O, H1x, H2x, H3x designations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caution for Marine Use:** 5xxx alloys with **Mg > 3 %** are susceptible to **"Sensitisation"** if held long-term between **65–200 °C** (e.g., near engine rooms, exhaust trunks). Sensitisation causes **β-phase (Al₃Mg₂)** precipitation along grain boundaries, leading to **Intergranular Corrosion (IGC)** and **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)**. Mitigation: use alloys with Mg ≤ 3 % (e.g., AA 5083 contains ~4.4 % Mg but is supplied in H116/H321 tempers that are microstructurally stable; AA 5454 with ~2.7 % Mg is intrinsically resistant).

### Heat-Treatable Alloys (2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx, some 4xxx)
Strength is achieved through **solution heat treatment + quenching + age hardening (precipitation hardening)**. The fundamental equation governing precipitate strengthening (Orowan looping) is:

$$
\tau = \frac{G \cdot b}{L - 2r}
$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Critical resolved shear stress required for dislocation bypass
- $G$ = Shear modulus of the aluminium matrix (**≈ 26 GPa**)
- $b$ = Burgers vector of aluminium FCC lattice (**≈ 0.286 nm**)
- $L$ = Mean inter-particle spacing of precipitates
- $r$ = Mean precipitate radius

The **yield strength** of the aged alloy scales as:

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k \cdot \frac{1}{L - 2r}
$$

Where $\sigma_0$ is the intrinsic lattice friction stress and $k$ is a constant related to shear modulus and Burgers vector.

> [!NOTE]
> **T6 Temper:** The most common marine heat-treated condition—solution treat (~530 °C for 6xxx), water quench, then artificially age at ~175 °C for 8–12 h. For **AA 6082-T6**, this delivers $\sigma_y \approx$ **260 MPa** and $\sigma_u \approx$ **310 MPa**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Data Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Density of Al vs. Steel | $\rho_{Al}/\rho_{steel} = 2.70/7.85$ | ratio (≈ 0.344) | Weight saving calculation |
| 2 | Weight Saving % | $\%WS = \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{steel}}\right) \times 100$ | % | Design trade-off |
| 3 | Specific Strength | $SS = \sigma_u / \rho$ | $\text{MPa} \cdot \text{cm}^3/\text{g}$ | Material selection index |
| 4 | Orowan Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{Gb}{L-2r}$ | MPa | Precipitate strengthening |
| 5 | Pitting Potential | $E_{pit}$ for Al in 3.5 % NaCl | $\approx -0.75 \text{ V}_{SCE}$ | Electrochemistry |
| 6 | Buoyancy Correction | $F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V$ | N | Displaced volume |
| 7 | Thermal Expansion | $\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | m | Dimensional tolerance |
| 8 | Section Modulus Reqd. | $Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | $\text{cm}^3$ | Hull scantling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Marine Pitting Potential:** Pure aluminium has $E_{pit} \approx -0.75 \text{ V}_{SCE}$ in aerated seawater. Alloying with Mg shifts this slightly more negative (more anodic), but the protective Al₂O₃ film keeps corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ very low (typically **1–10 μA/cm²** for AA 5083).

## 2.4 Mechanism of Strengthening (Detailed)

Aluminium alloys gain strength through four principal mechanisms:

1. **Solid Solution Strengthening** – solute atoms (Mg, Si, Cu) distort the FCC lattice, increasing dislocation resistance.
2. **Strain (Work) Hardening** – dislocation density increases during cold rolling; governed by $\sigma = \sigma_0 + K\varepsilon^n$.
3. **Grain Boundary Strengthening (Hall-Petch)** – 
$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$
where $d$ = average grain diameter.
4. **Precipitation (Age) Hardening** – fine, coherent precipitates (e.g., Mg₂Si in 6xxx; Al₂Cu in 2xxx; Al₃Mg₂ in sensitised 5xxx) impede dislocation motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** For ship hull plating, **AA 5083-H116** combines solid-solution Mg strengthening + strain hardening + fine-grained structure, achieving $\sigma_y = 215$ MPa without any heat treatment—making it ideal for welded marine structures (post-weld heat treatment is impractical for large hulls).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Weight Saving by Replacing Steel with Aluminium

**Problem Setup (typical KTU numerical):**
A ship hull plate currently made of mild steel (density $7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$) is to be redesigned in AA 5083 aluminium alloy (density $2.66 \text{ g/cm}^3$) for the same length and width, but with thickness adjusted to give equal flexural rigidity (i.e., equal second moment of area times Young's modulus, $EI$). Find the new thickness and weight saving percentage.

**Given:**
- Original steel thickness: $t_s = 12 \text{ mm}$
- Width: $b = 1000 \text{ mm}$
- $E_{steel} = 200 \text{ GPa}$
- $E_{Al\,5083} = 70 \text{ GPa}$
- $\rho_{steel} = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- $\rho_{Al\,5083} = 2.66 \text{ g/cm}^3$

**Step 1: Equate Flexural Rigidity**

For a rectangular plate of unit width, second moment of area is:

$$
I = \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12}
$$

Equating flexural rigidity:

$$
E_{steel} \cdot I_{steel} = E_{Al} \cdot I_{Al}
$$

$$
E_{steel} \cdot \frac{b \cdot t_s^3}{12} = E_{Al} \cdot \frac{b \cdot t_{Al}^3}{12}
$$

**Step 2: Cancel $b/12$ from both sides**

$$
E_{steel} \cdot t_s^3 = E_{Al} \cdot t_{Al}^3
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $t_{Al}$**

$$
t_{Al}^3 = \frac{E_{steel}}{E_{Al}} \cdot t_s^3
$$

$$
t_{Al} = t_s \cdot \left(\frac{E_{steel}}{E_{Al}}\right)^{1/3}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values**

$$
t_{Al} = 12 \cdot \left(\frac{200}{70}\right)^{1/3}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the ratio**

$$
\frac{200}{70} = 2.857
$$

$$
(2.857)^{1/3} = 1.418
$$

**Step 6: Final aluminium thickness**

$$
t_{Al} = 12 \times 1.418 = 17.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7: Mass per unit area of steel plate**

$$
m_{steel} = \rho_{steel} \cdot t_s = 7.85 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 12 = 0.0942 \text{ kg/cm}^2
$$

**Step 8: Mass per unit area of aluminium plate**

$$
m_{Al} = \rho_{Al} \cdot t_{Al} = 2.66 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 17.0 = 0.0452 \text{ kg/cm}^2
$$

**Step 9: Percentage weight saving**

$$
\%WS = \frac{m_{steel} - m_{Al}}{m_{steel}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%WS = \frac{0.0942 - 0.0452}{0.0942} \times 100 = 52.0 \%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Despite needing a **41.8 % thicker** plate to match steel's stiffness, the aluminium plate is **52 % lighter** — a decisive advantage for high-speed marine vessels.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum Plate Thickness Based on Section Modulus (KTU Hallmark Problem)

**KTU 2024-Type Problem:** A simply supported aluminium deck plate (AA 6082-T6) spans 2.5 m between stiffeners. The maximum bending moment is $M = 12 \text{ kN·m/m}$. Allowable bending stress is $\sigma_{allow} = 165 \text{ MPa}$. Find the minimum required thickness.

**Step 1: Required Section Modulus**

For a 1 m wide strip, section modulus:

$$
Z_{req} = \frac{M}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{12 \times 10^3}{165 \times 10^6}
$$

**Step 2: Numerical evaluation**

$$
Z_{req} = 7.27 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{m}
$$

Converting: $Z_{req} = 72.7 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}$.

**Step 3: Section modulus of rectangular plate (per metre width)**

For unit width $b = 1 \text{ m}$:

$$
Z = \frac{b \cdot t^2}{6}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for thickness**

$$
t^2 = \frac{6Z}{b} = 6 \times 7.27 \times 10^{-5} = 4.36 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
t = \sqrt{4.36 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0209 \text{ m} \approx 21 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Apply corrosion & fabrication margin (typically 1.0–1.5 mm)**

$$
t_{design} = 21 + 1.5 = 22.5 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{adopt } t = 23 \text{ mm}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hall-Petch Strengthening Effect on 5xxx Alloys

For an Al-Mg alloy, yield strength follows:

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

Given for AA 5083:
- $\sigma_0 = 65 \text{ MPa}$
- $k_y = 0.18 \text{ MPa} \cdot \text{m}^{1/2}$
- Initial grain size $d_1 = 50 \text{ μm} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- After thermomechanical processing, $d_2 = 10 \text{ μm} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$

**Step 1: $\sigma_y$ at $d_1 = 50 \text{ μm}$**

$$
d_1^{-1/2} = (50 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} = 141.4 \text{ m}^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{y1} = 65 + 0.18 \times 141.4 = 90.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2: $\sigma_y$ at $d_2 = 10 \text{ μm}$**

$$
d_2^{-1/2} = (10 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} = 316.2 \text{ m}^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{y2} = 65 + 0.18 \times 316.2 = 121.9 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Increase in yield strength**

$$
\Delta \sigma_y = 121.9 - 90.5 = 31.4 \text{ MPa} \ (\approx 34.7 \% \text{ improvement})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Link:** Modern Al-Mg plates for fast ferries use **fine-grain recrystallisation annealing** to achieve this Hall-Petch strengthening, which is critical for ice-strengthened hulls operating in polar waters.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Marine Alloy Selection

| Application | Recommended Alloy | Temper | $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hull plate (fast ferry) | AA 5083 | H116 / H321 | 215 | Weldable, corrosion-resistant, high $\sigma_y$ |
| Hull plate (Navy MCMV) | AA 5456 | H116 | 255 | Highest strength, non-magnetic |
| Superstructure | AA 6082 / 6061 | T6 | 260 / 240 | Extrudable, good finish |
| Mast / King post | AA 6082-T6 | T6 | 260 | High extrusion strength |
| Pressure vessel (LPG) | AA 5083 / 5086 | O / H32 | 145 / 185 | Cryogenic toughness |
| Welded bracket | AA 5083 | H111 | 145 | Soft for forming |
| Propeller (small craft) | Cast Al-bronze / A356-T6 | – | 200 | Casting + corrosion |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Aluminium Alloy Classification Flow Chart

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[ALUMINIUM ALLOYS] --> B0[Wrought Alloys 4-digit]
    A0 --> C0[Cast Alloys 3-digit with decimal]
    B0 --> B1[1xxx Pure Al 99 percent]
    B0 --> B2[2xxx Al-Cu Heat Treatable]
    B0 --> B3[3xxx Al-Mn Non Heat Treatable]
    B0 --> B4[4xxx Al-Si Filler Wires]
    B0 --> B5[5xxx Al-Mg MARINE STAR]
    B0 --> B6[6xxx Al-Mg-Si Heat Treatable]
    B0 --> B7[7xxx Al-Zn Aerospace]
    B0 --> B8[8xxx Specialty]
    B5 --> B5a[5083 H116 H321 Hull Plate]
    B5 --> B5b[5086 O H32 Cryogenic]
    B5 --> B5c[5454 H34 Tanks]
    B5 --> B5d[5456 H116 Naval]
    B6 --> B6a[6082 T6 Superstructure]
    B6 --> B6b[6061 T6 General Marine]
    C0 --> C1[A356 T6 Castings]
    C0 --> C2[A514 Marine Castings]
```

## 4.2 Aluminium Strength Enhancement Mechanisms

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Strengthening Mechanisms in Al Alloys] --> S1[Solid Solution Strengthening]
    S0 --> S2[Strain Hardening]
    S0 --> S3[Hall Petch Grain Refinement]
    S0 --> S4[Precipitation Age Hardening]
    S1 --> S1a[Solute Atoms Mg Si Cu distort FCC lattice]
    S2 --> S2a[Cold rolling increases dislocation density]
    S3 --> S3a[Smaller grain size raises sigma_y]
    S4 --> S4a[Coherent precipitates like Mg2Si block dislocations]
    S4 --> S4b[Solution treat Quench Age T6]
```

## 4.3 Heat Treatment Cycle for 6xxx Marine Alloys (T6 Temper)

```mermaid
graph LR
    HT0[As Extruded or Rolled Plate] --> HT1[Solution Heat Treatment]
    HT1 --> HT2[Quench in Water]
    HT2 --> HT3[Natural Ageing Room Temp]
    HT3 --> HT4[Artificial Ageing 175 C for 8 to 12 h]
    HT4 --> HT5[T6 Temper Ready for Marine Use]
    HT1 -.Temperature.-> HT1t[525 to 545 C]
    HT2 -.Time.-> HT2t[Less than 30 seconds critical]
    HT4 -.Cool.-> HT4t[Furnace cool to ambient]
```

## 4.4 Marine Alloy Selection Decision Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    D0[Marine Component Selection] --> D1{Need Welding}
    D1 -->|Yes Heavy Plate| D2[AA 5083 H116 or 5086 O]
    D1 -->|Yes Extrusion| D3[AA 6082 T6]
    D1 -->|No Cast Part| D4[Cast A356 T6]
    D0 --> E0{Operating Environment}
    E0 -->|Seawater Splash| F1[5xxx series preferred]
    E0 -->|High Temperature 80 to 200 C| F2[Avoid high Mg Use 5454 3 percent Mg max]
    E0 -->|Cryogenic LNG| F3[AA 5083 O temper]
    D0 --> G0{Strength Priority}
    G0 -->|Maximum| G1[AA 5456 H116 Navy]
    G0 -->|Moderate| G2[AA 5083 H116 Commercial]
    G0 -->|Structural Finish| G3[AA 6082 T6]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three advantages of using aluminium alloys over mild steel in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Weight reduction (~50%)** – Aluminium density (2.7 g/cm³) is one-third of steel (7.85 g/cm³), increasing payload and fuel efficiency.
2. **Superior corrosion resistance in marine atmosphere** due to the self-healing **Al₂O₃** passive film, reducing maintenance cost.
3. **Non-magnetic and non-sparking**, making it ideal for naval mine countermeasure vessels (MCMVs) and fuel/ammunition carriers.

*(Alternative accepted points: higher specific strength, ease of fabrication/extrusion, no need for painting in many cases, recyclability.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between heat-treatable and non-heat-treatable aluminium alloys with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Heat-Treatable | Non-Heat-Treatable |
|---|---|---|
| Strengthening Mechanism | Solution heat treatment + age hardening (precipitation) | Cold working (strain hardening) only |
| Suitable Alloying Elements | Cu, Mg+Si, Zn | Mn, Mg (up to solubility limit), Si |
| Example | **AA 6082 (Al-Mg-Si)** – T6 temper | **AA 5083 (Al-Mg)** – H116 temper |
| Marine Use | Superstructures, masts (extrusions) | Hull plates, large welded structures |
| Weldability | Good (T6 lost in HAZ – re-precipitation on natural ageing) | Excellent (no HAZ softening issue) |

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the classification of aluminium alloys based on the Aluminium Association designation system. List the principal alloying element, properties, and marine applications of any **four** series.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **AA 5xxx series** alloys used in shipbuilding. Explain the phenomenon of **sensitisation** and how it is prevented.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the T6 heat treatment cycle for **AA 6082** marine alloy with a temperature–time diagram. Explain the precipitation hardening mechanism using the Orowan equation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel deck plate of 14 mm thickness is to be replaced with an aluminium alloy plate of equal flexural rigidity. The plate is 1.5 m wide and simply supported over a span of 3 m, carrying a uniformly distributed load of 8 kN/m². Take $E_{steel} = 200$ GPa, $E_{Al} = 70$ GPa, $\rho_{steel} = 7.85$ g/cm³, $\rho_{Al} = 2.66$ g/cm³. Determine the new aluminium thickness and the percentage weight saving.

---

## Model Solutions for Question B (Part b)

**Step 1: Flexural rigidity equality**

$$
E_{steel} \cdot t_s^3 = E_{Al} \cdot t_{Al}^3
$$

$$
t_{Al} = t_s \cdot \left(\frac{E_{steel}}{E_{Al}}\right)^{1/3} = 14 \times \left(\frac{200}{70}\right)^{1/3}
$$

**Step 2: Ratio computation**

$$
\frac{200}{70} = 2.857 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (2.857)^{1/3} = 1.418
$$

**Step 3: Aluminium thickness**

$$
t_{Al} = 14 \times 1.418 = 19.85 \text{ mm} \approx 20 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Mass per unit area**

$$
m_{steel} = 7.85 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 14 = 0.1099 \text{ kg/cm}^2
$$

$$
m_{Al} = 2.66 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 19.85 = 0.0528 \text{ kg/cm}^2
$$

**Step 5: Weight saving %**

$$
\%WS = \frac{0.1099 - 0.0528}{0.1099} \times 100 = 51.9 \%
$$

**Step 6: Verification of bending stress (optional, 3-mark sub-part)**

For UDL $w = 8 \text{ kN/m}^2$ on 1.5 m strip:
$$
w_{strip} = 8 \times 1.5 = 12 \text{ kN/m}
$$

Maximum bending moment (simply supported):
$$
M_{max} = \frac{w_{strip} \cdot L^2}{8} = \frac{12 \times 3^2}{8} = 13.5 \text{ kN·m}
$$

Section modulus of Al plate (per metre strip):
$$
Z = \frac{b \cdot t_{Al}^2}{6} = \frac{1000 \cdot 19.85^2}{6} = 6.57 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Bending stress:
$$
\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{13.5 \times 10^6}{6.57 \times 10^4} = 205 \text{ MPa}
$$

> Since this exceeds the typical T6 allowable of 165 MPa, the designer should either upgrade to **AA 5083-H116** with thicker plate (≈ 22 mm) or add stiffeners. This trade-off is a frequent **design viva question**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Mixing up AA 5xxx vs. 6xxx:** 5xxx (Al-Mg) is **NON-heat-treatable**; 6xxx (Al-Mg-Si) is **HEAT-treatable**. Confusing these forfeits 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify Temper:** Writing just "AA 5083" without H116 / O / H32 loses marks. Temper = heat-treat condition, **mandatory** in marine specifications.
> 3. **Wrong ratio in weight saving:** Always use $\sigma_y/\rho$ (specific strength), not raw density ratio. Marks lost if flexural rigidity calculation is missing the cube-root term.
> 4. **Sensitisation not explained:** For any 5xxx alloy with Mg > 3 % held at 65–200 °C, β-phase (Al₃Mg₂) forms at grain boundaries → IGC/SCC. **This is a high-frequency question.**
> 5. **Not mentioning HAZ softening:** In welded 6xxx-T6 structures, the heat-affected zone loses strength. The remedy (use 5xxx for welded hulls) is a board favourite.
> 6. **Galvanic corrosion with steel fasteners:** Aluminium hull + steel bolt = galvanic corrosion. Always specify **insulating bushes (nylon, rubber gaskets)** and use **aluminium or zinc-anode fasteners** in marine design.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read 5 minutes before exam:**

- [ ] **Pure aluminium:** Density **2.70 g/cm³**, FCC crystal, $\sigma_y \approx$ **35 MPa** (annealed), protected by **Al₂O₃** film.
- [ ] **Wrought alloys use 4-digit code (AA/IS 617)**, cast alloys use 3-digit + decimal.
- [ ] **1xxx** = pure Al, **2xxx** = Al-Cu, **3xxx** = Al-Mn, **4xxx** = Al-Si (welding filler), **5xxx** = Al-Mg (marine star), **6xxx** = Al-Mg-Si (extrusions), **7xxx** = Al-Zn, **8xxx** = specialty.
- [ ] **5xxx is non-heat-treatable**; strength by solid solution + cold work; best for **welded hulls**.
- [ ] **6xxx is heat-treatable** via T6 (solution 530 °C → quench → age 175 °C/8–12 h); best for **extruded superstructures**.
- [ ] **Marine "Big Four":** AA 5083 (hulls), AA 5086 (cryogenic), AA 5454 (tanks), AA 5456 (naval/armour), AA 6082 (European structural).
- [ ] **Sensitisation** = β-Al₃Mg₂ precipitation at grain boundaries in 5xxx with Mg > 3 % held at 65–200 °C → IGC/SCC. **Mitigation:** H116/H321 temper stabilises microstructure; or use Mg < 3 % (AA 5454).
- [ ] **Orowan equation:** $\tau = Gb/(L-2r)$ — finer, denser precipitates → higher strength.
- [ ] **Hall-Petch:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ — smaller grains → higher $\sigma_y$.
- [ ] **Weight saving calculation rule:** $t_{Al} = t_s (E_{steel}/E_{Al})^{1/3}$ for equal flexural rigidity; expect ~**50 %** mass saving despite ~**40 %** thickness increase.
- [ ] **Galvanic corrosion:** Avoid Al–steel direct contact in marine fittings; use isolating gaskets or **zinc sacrificial anodes**.
- [ ] **Non-magnetic** → essential for MCMVs; **non-sparking** → ammunition ships.
- [ ] **Weldability:** 5xxx (excellent, no HAZ loss) > 6xxx (good, partial HAZ softening) > 2xxx (poor, hot-crack risk).
- [ ] **Tempers to remember:** O (annealed, soft), H32/H34/H116 (strain-hardened + stabilised), T6 (peak-aged), T651 (stress-relieved T6).
- [ ] **Key international rules:** Det Norske Veritas (DNV), Lloyd's Register, ABS — all permit AA 5083/5086/6082 for marine applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion on propeller, marine machinery and deck fittings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Corrosion on propeller, marine machinery and deck fittings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Marine Corrosion on Functional Components** is defined as the electrochemical degradation of metallic surfaces—specifically propellers, marine machinery (shafts, bearings, pumps, valves, heat exchangers), and deck fittings (winches, capstans, bollards, davits, hatch covers)—triggered by prolonged exposure to seawater, atmospheric salts, microbiological activity, and galvanic coupling between dissimilar metals in a marine electrolyte environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PENST414 – Module 2):**
> The 2024 Scheme explicitly requires students to identify *component-specific* corrosion forms rather than generalized corrosion. Examiners reward answers that link a specific corrosion *mechanism* to a specific *component* (e.g., **cavitation-erosion on propellers**, **crevice corrosion in pump impellers**, **fretting corrosion on shaft bearings**).

The three primary zones of concern are:

1. **Submerged Zone (Below Waterline):** Continuous electrolyte contact → high electrochemical activity.
2. **Splash/Tidal Zone:** Alternating wet–dry cycles → maximum oxygen differential aeration.
3. **Atmospheric/Above-Deck Zone:** Salt-laden air → uniform and pitting corrosion of ferrous fittings.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Battery of a Ship"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Ship as a Self-Charging Battery**
> Imagine a ship as a giant lemon-battery experiment. The seawater acts as the *electrolyte* (the lemon juice), the propeller (often a copper alloy like **Manganese Bronze** or **Ni-Al Bronze**) acts as the *cathode*, and the steel hull acts as the *anode*. Whenever these two dissimilar metals are electrically connected through the seawater, current flows, and the "weaker" metal (steel) dissolves. This is exactly what happens to **propeller–shaft–hull** assemblies. Marine engineers must therefore either (a) separate the metals with insulating bushes, or (b) deliberately sacrifice a cheap anode (zinc) to protect the expensive propeller.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU-Standardized)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Standard Metrics (memorize for 3-mark short questions):**
> - **Corrosion Rate (CR):** Expressed in **mm/year (mpy)** — *mils per year*.
> - **Protection Potential (Ep):** For cathodic protection of steel in seawater, **Ep = −0.85 V vs. Ag/AgCl reference** (**bold** standard value).
> - **Galvanic Series Position:** Determines nobility in seawater electrolyte.
> - **Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN):** PREN = %Cr + 3.3×%Mo + 16×%N. A value **> 40** indicates marine-grade stainless steel.

## 1.4 Component Classification (Quick Reference)

| Component | Primary Alloy | Dominant Corrosion Form | Severity Index (1–5) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Propeller | Ni-Al Bronze / Manganese Bronze | Cavitation-erosion, dezincification, galvanic | 5 |
| Propeller Shaft | Stainless Steel 316L / Monel | Fretting, crevice at stuffing box | 4 |
| Marine Pump Casings | Cast Iron / Bronze | Cavitation, impeller erosion | 4 |
| Heat Exchanger Tubes | Cu-Ni 90/10, 70/30, Titanium | Impingement, biofouling-accelerated | 4 |
| Deck Winches/Bollards | Cast Steel, Galvanized Steel | Uniform, galvanic, atmospheric | 3 |
| Hatch Covers | Mild Steel (painted) | Crevice at gaskets, pitting | 3 |
| Rudder / Stern Gear | Mild Steel + Bronze bearing | Galvanic, biofouling crevice | 5 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Corrosion of Propellers — Mechanism Breakdown

Propellers operate in the harshest corrosion environment on a vessel because they combine **high rotational speeds**, **cathodic protection stray currents**, **cavitation**, and **galvanic coupling** with the steel shaft/hull.

### 2.1.1 Cavitation-Erosion Corrosion (CEC)

When local static pressure on the blade surface falls **below the vapor pressure of seawater (~2.3 kPa at 25°C)**, vapor bubbles nucleate and subsequently collapse violently against the metal surface. This repeated micro-impact work-hardens and ultimately spalls off the protective oxide layer, exposing fresh alloy to corrosive attack.

$$
P_{\text{local}} \le P_{\text{vapor}}(T) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Bubble nucleation}
$$

The **Threshold Cavitation Number (σ)** governs onset:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P_{\infty} - P_{\text{vapor}}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, V_{\infty}^{2}}
$$

- If $\sigma < \sigma_{\text{critical}}$ (typically **0.4–0.6 for marine propellers**), cavitation is initiated.
- $\sigma_{\text{critical}}$ is higher for **Manganese Bronze (~0.8)** than for **Ni-Al Bronze (~0.4)** — explaining why Ni-Al Bronze is the modern KTU-recommended choice.

### 2.1.2 Dezincification of Brasses

For **Manganese Bronze** propellers (Cu-Zn alloy), selective leaching of **zinc** leaves a porous, mechanically weak copper sponge. Two forms exist:

- **Plug-type dezincification:** Localized pits (most dangerous).
- **Layer-type dezincification:** Uniform surface attack.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Ni-Al Bronze Replaces Manganese Bronze:** Ni-Al Bronze forms a stable, self-healing **Al₂O₃ passive film** rather than relying on zinc content. This is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**.

### 2.1.3 Galvanic Corrosion (Stern-Tube Assembly)

The classic **galvanic couple**:

$$
\text{Mild Steel Hull} \mid \text{Seawater} \mid \text{Bronze Propeller}
$$

The **galvanic corrosion current density** is given by:

$$
i_{\text{g}} = \frac{E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}}{R_{\text{electrolyte}} + R_{\text{metal}}}
$$

The hull becomes the anode and corrodes rapidly **near the stern bearing** unless an insulating **shaft earthing brush** or **glass-reinforced plastic (GRP) bearing isolator** is fitted.

## 2.2 Corrosion of Marine Machinery

### 2.2.1 Shaft and Bearing Systems

| Subsystem | Corrosion Type | Mitigation (KTU-Standard) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stern tube bearing | Crevice + galvanic | White-metal bearing (Babbit), oil-lubricated seal |
| Intermediate shaft | Fretting corrosion at flange bolts | Use **PTFE-coated** anti-fretting washers |
| Engine cooling jacket | Pitting + under-deposit | Inhibited cooling water (sodium nitrite + borax) |
| Sea-water pump | Cavitation + impingement | Replace cast iron with **Cu-Ni 90/10** |
| Condenser tubes | Inlet-end impingement + biofouling | Ferrous sulfate dosing; fit **inserted ferrules** |

### 2.2.2 Crevice Corrosion in Pump Impellers

Crevice corrosion occurs in **gasket joints, threaded fasteners, and O-ring grooves** where the local oxygen concentration drops, creating a differential aeration cell:

$$
\text{Outside (O}_2\text{-rich): Cathode} \;\;\|\;\; \text{Inside (O}_2\text{-starved): Anode}
$$

The **critical crevice temperature (CCT)** of a stainless steel must exceed the operating temperature:

$$
\text{CCT} > T_{\text{operating}} \;\;\text{for safe service}
$$

For **Type 316 stainless steel**, CCT $\approx$ **15–20°C in seawater** — meaning it is unsuitable for warm tropical waters.

## 2.3 Corrosion of Deck Fittings

### 2.3.1 Atmospheric Corrosion

Deck fittings (winches, capstans, bollards, fairleads, hatch cleats) suffer primarily from:

- **Uniform atmospheric corrosion** from chloride deposition.
