- **Galvanic corrosion** where steel contacts bronze sheaves.
- **Pack rust** (intergranular expansion) inside lap joints of hatch covers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard for Deck Fittings:** The 2024 syllabus expects students to recommend **hot-dip galvanizing (HDG)** to **ISO 1461**, with coating thickness **≥ 85 μm**, OR **duplex system** (HDG + epoxy/polyurethane paint) for tropical service.

### 2.3.2 Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC) in Davits and Cranes

**Martensitic stainless steel (e.g., 17-4 PH)** used in crane wire ropes is susceptible to **Cl⁻-induced SCC** above **60°C**. The KTU-accepted prevention is **cathodic protection by zinc sacrificial anodes** or substitution with **austenitic 316L**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Commit them to memory.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | KTU-Mandated Unit |
| :-- | :-- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's Law of Electrolysis (mass loss) | $m$ in grams |
| 2 | $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | Corrosion rate from mass loss | **mm/year** |
| 3 | $i_{\text{corr}} = \dfrac{B}{R_p}$ | Stern-Geary linear polarization | A/cm² |
| 4 | $\text{PREN} = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N$ | Pitting resistance of stainless steels | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P_\infty - P_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2}$ | Cavitation number for propellers | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | Galvanic cell EMF (thermodynamic driving force) | Volts (V) |
| 7 | $\Delta V = I \cdot R$ | Cathodic-protection voltage drop (Ohm's law) | Volts (V) |
| 8 | $\eta = E_{\text{app}} - E_{\text{corr}}$ | Polarization overpotential for CP | Volts (V) |
| 9 | $L = \dfrac{\Delta V}{2 R \cdot I \cdot L}$ | Anode spacing rule (approximate) | meters |
| 10 | $W_{\text{anode}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{req}} \cdot t \cdot 8760}{u \cdot C}$ | Mass of sacrificial anode required | kg |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-Loss Alert:** Do **not** use the vertical bar symbol $\vert x \vert$ inside markdown tables. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ — examiners deduct marks for broken table syntax in online submission portals.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipyard Selection of Materials:** Naval architects use PREN and galvanic-series tables to choose the correct propeller/hull pairings.
- **Classification Society Rules:** **DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS** mandate cathodic protection design based on the above formulas.
- **Dry-Docking Economics:** Predicting corrosion rate (mm/yr) lets operators schedule sand-blasting and recoating intervals, reducing lifetime OPEX by **15–25%**.
- **Failure Forensics:** Faraday's Law is routinely used in court-expert reports to estimate the time-to-failure of seized shafts and pitted heat-exchanger tubes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Corrosion Rate from Mass Loss (Faraday's Law)

This derivation appears in nearly every KTU Part-B question on marine corrosion.

**Given:**
- Mass of metal dissolved, $m$ [grams]
- Atomic weight, $M$ [g/mol]
- Valency (electrons transferred per atom), $n$
- Faraday's constant, $F = 96{,}485$ **C/mol**
- Corrosion current, $I$ [Amperes]
- Time of exposure, $t$ [seconds]

**Step 1 — Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis (statement):**
The mass $m$ of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge $Q$ passed through the electrolyte.

$$
m \propto Q \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; m = k \cdot Q
$$

**Step 2 — Relate charge to current and time:**

$$
Q = I \cdot t
$$

Substituting:

$$
m = k \cdot I \cdot t
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the proportionality constant $k$:**
By the definition of the Faraday, the charge required to liberate **one mole** of a monovalent substance (n = 1) is exactly $F$ coulombs, producing a mass of $M$ grams. Therefore:

$$
k = \frac{M}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute to obtain the working equation:**

$$
m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to corrosion rate in mm/year:**
Density $D$ [g/cm³] and exposed area $A$ [cm²] relate mass to a uniform thickness loss $\Delta h$:

$$
m = D \cdot A \cdot \Delta h \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \Delta h = \frac{m}{D \cdot A}
$$

Convert thickness to mm and time to years (multiply by $\tfrac{87.6 \times 10^{4}}{1} = 87.6 \times 10^{4}$ conversion factor):

$$
CR \;[\text{mm/yr}] = \frac{87.6 \cdot m}{D \cdot A \cdot T}
$$

**Final form:**

$$
\boxed{\,CR = \frac{87.6 \cdot m}{D \cdot A \cdot T}\,}
$$

where $T$ is exposure time in **hours**, $D$ in **g/cm³**, $A$ in **cm²**, $m$ in **mg**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cathodic-Protection Anode Mass

**Given:**
- Required protection current, $I_{\text{req}}$ [A]
- Service life, $t$ [years]
- Utilization factor, $u$ (typically 0.85 for zinc, 0.90 for aluminum)
- Anode electrochemical capacity, $C$ [A·h/kg]

**Step 1 — Total charge to be delivered:**

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = I_{\text{req}} \cdot t \cdot 8760 \;\;\text{[A·h, since 1 yr = 8760 h]}
$$

**Step 2 — Divide by anode capacity to get the theoretical mass required:**

$$
W_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{C} = \frac{I_{\text{req}} \cdot t \cdot 8760}{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply utilization factor $u$ (accounts for incomplete consumption before the anode is replaced):**

$$
W_{\text{actual}} = \frac{W_{\text{theoretical}}}{u}
$$

**Final form:**

$$
\boxed{\,W_{\text{anode}} = \frac{I_{\text{req}} \cdot t \cdot 8760}{u \cdot C}\,}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark problem):**
A ship requires $I_{\text{req}} = 4.0$ A of cathodic protection current. Specify the mass of zinc anodes (capacity $C = 780$ A·h/kg, utilization $u = 0.85$) for a **4-year** dry-docking interval.

**Step A:**

$$
Q = 4.0 \times 4 \times 8760 = 140{,}160 \;\text{A·h}
$$

**Step B:**

$$
W = \frac{140{,}160}{780 \times 0.85} = \frac{140{,}160}{663} \approx 211.4 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Answer:** Approximately **212 kg of zinc anodes** are required.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cavitation Number for a Propeller Blade

**Given:**
- Free-stream pressure (depth-averaged), $P_\infty$ [Pa]
- Vapor pressure of seawater at temperature $T$, $P_v$ [Pa]
- Seawater density, $\rho \approx 1025$ kg/m³
- Local flow velocity, $V$ [m/s]

**Step 1 — Definition of dynamic pressure:**

$$
q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2
$$

**Step 2 — Definition of cavitation number (dimensionless ratio of pressure margin to dynamic pressure):**

$$
\sigma = \frac{P_\infty - P_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Onset condition:** Cavitation incepts when local static pressure equals vapor pressure, which corresponds to:

$$
\sigma \le \sigma_{\text{critical}}
$$

For **Manganese Bronze**, $\sigma_{\text{cr}} \approx 0.8$ → cavitates easily → erosion.
For **Ni-Al Bronze**, $\sigma_{\text{cr}} \approx 0.4$ → far more cavitation-resistant.

**Step 4 — Practical implication:** A ship designer therefore selects **Ni-Al Bronze** for high-speed naval or fast-craft propellers because cavitation bubbles collapse with less damage on its tougher, oxide-protected surface.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component vs. Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below is a **favourite KTU Part-B 14-mark answer** layout. Practice reproducing it in examinations.

| Component | Corrosion Type | Driving Force | Primary Location | Standard Mitigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Propeller (Ni-Al Bronze) | Cavitation-erosion | Low local pressure | Blade suction side | Polish, super-cavitation profile |
| Propeller (Manganese Bronze) | Dezincification | Selective Zn leaching | Whole blade | Replace with Ni-Al Bronze |
| Stern Shaft | Fretting | Micro-motion + salt | Flange faces | PTFE washers; rubber boots |
| Pump Casing | Cavitation + impingement | High tip speed | Impeller eye | Replace with Cu-Ni 90/10 |
| Heat Exchanger Tubes | Impingement attack | Turbulent water | Tube inlets | Ferrous-sulfate dosing |
| Condenser Tube-Sheet | Crevice + galvanic | Tube-to-tube-sheet gap | Rolled joint | Use epoxy-coating on tube-sheet |
| Winch Gearbox | Internal rusting | Condensation | Bearing races | Vapour-phase inhibitor (VPI) |
| Bollard (Steel) | Galvanic + uniform | Bronze sheave contact | Base flange | Rubber gasket, zinc anode |
| Davit Wire Rope | Stress-corrosion cracking | Tensile stress + Cl⁻ | Bend regions | Cathodic protection; use 316L |
| Hatch Cover | Pack-rust | Crevice + oxygen cell | Lap joints | Seal with polysulfide sealant |

## 3.5 Pitting Resistance Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-typical 7-mark sub-question:** "Compare the pitting resistance of 316 SS and a 6% Mo super-austenitic steel in seawater."

**Given chemistries (weight %):**

| Element | 316 SS | 6-Mo Super-Austenitic |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Cr | 17.0 | 20.0 |
| Mo | 2.5 | 6.0 |
| N | 0.05 | 0.20 |

**Step 1 — Calculate PREN for 316 SS:**

$$
\text{PREN}_{316} = 17.0 + 3.3(2.5) + 16(0.05)
$$

$$
= 17.0 + 8.25 + 0.80 = 26.05
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate PREN for 6-Mo super-austenitic:**

$$
\text{PREN}_{6Mo} = 20.0 + 3.3(6.0) + 16(0.20)
$$

$$
= 20.0 + 19.8 + 3.2 = 43.0
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

- PREN > 40 → suitable for **warm, chloride-rich** seawater service.
- PREN < 30 → prone to **pitting** in tropical service.
- Therefore the **6-Mo super-austenitic steel is mandatory** for critical marine machinery in tropical waters.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Stern-Gear Galvanic-Corrosion Architecture

The figure below maps the electrochemical interactions in a typical stern-gear assembly, showing every conductive path that a marine engineer must isolate or protect.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Steel Hull Anode] -->|Seawater Electrolyte| B[Bronze Propeller Cathode]
    B -->|Bronze Shaft| C[Stainless Steel Intermediate Shaft]
    C -->|Flange Bolts| D[Engine Flywheel Steel]
    D -->|Bonding Strap| A
    E[Zinc Shaft Anode] -. Sacrificial .-> B
    F[Impressed Current System] -. Injected DC .-> A
    G[Glass Reinforced Plastic Bearing] -. Insulation .-> B
    H[Earthing Brush Silver Graphite] -. Stray Current Drain .-> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show *galvanic current flow*; dotted arrows show *protective interventions*. A KTU examiner awards full marks only if the student can identify **every** insulation and protection node in the assembly.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Cavitation-Erosion Mechanism on a Propeller Blade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High-Speed Rotating Blade] --> B{Local Pressure Drop}
    B -->|P_local less than P_vapor| C[Bubble Nucleation]
    B -->|P_local greater than P_vapor| Z[No Cavitation - Safe]
    C --> D[Bubble Travels to High-Pressure Region]
    D --> E[Violent Bubble Collapse - Microjet]
    E --> F[Surface Oxide Film Rupture]
    F --> G[Fresh Alloy Exposed to Seawater]
    G --> H[Electrochemical Attack]
    H --> I[Pit Formation and Material Loss]
    I --> J[Loss of Blade Profile - Performance Drop]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Deck-Fitting Protection Strategy (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Deck Fitting Material Selection] --> B{Is it Cast Steel?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Hot-Dip Galvanize to ISO 1461]
    B -->|No| D{Is it Stainless?}
    D -->|Yes| E{Service Environment}
    E -->|Tropical Sea| F[Use 6-Mo Super-Austenitic]
    E -->|Temperate Sea| G[Type 316L Acceptable]
    D -->|No| H{Is it Bronze or Copper Alloy?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Check for Galvanic Contact with Steel]
    I -->|Yes| J[Insert Rubber or Nylon Gasket]
    I -->|No| K[No Mitigation Needed]
    C --> L{Duplex System Required?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Apply Epoxy plus Polyurethane over HDG]
    L -->|No| N[Galvanizing Only]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Failure-Forensic Methodology (How Naval Architects Diagnose Corrosion)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Failed Component Retrieved] --> B[Visual and Photographic Survey]
    B --> C[Identify Corrosion Morphology]
    C --> D{Pitting Present?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Measure Pit Depth and Density]
    D -->|No| F{Uniform Loss?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Mass-Loss Test - Faraday]
    F -->|No| H{Cracks Detected?}
    H -->|Yes| I[SCC Suspect - SEM plus EDS Analysis]
    H -->|No| J[Metallographic Examination]
    E --> K[Compare with KTU Allowable Limits]
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Root-Cause Report]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Cathodic-Protection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Corrosion-Risk Assessment] --> B{Is the Structure Submerged?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Apply Cathodic Protection]
    B -->|No| D[Use Coatings Only]
    C --> E{Is Current Source Available Onboard?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Impressed Current CP - ICCP]
    E -->|No| G[Sacrificial Anode CP - SACP]
    F --> H[Monitor Reference Electrode Ag-AgCl]
    G --> I[Schedule Anode Replacement at Dry-Dock]
    H --> J[Maintain E_protection equals -0.85 V]
    I --> J
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Cavitation-Erosion on Propellers `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. Define cavitation-erosion corrosion as it applies to marine propellers. List two alloy-selection strategies to minimize it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Cavitation-erosion corrosion is the combined mechanical and electrochemical degradation of a propeller blade surface caused by the repeated nucleation and violent collapse of vapor bubbles in regions where the local static pressure falls below the vapor pressure of seawater, rupturing the protective oxide film and exposing fresh metal to corrosive attack.

Two alloy-selection strategies:

1. **Use Ni-Al Bronze** instead of Manganese Bronze, because it forms a tougher, more adherent **Al₂O₃ passive layer** with a lower critical cavitation number ($\sigma_{\text{cr}} \approx 0.4$).
2. **Apply a cathodic protection current density of 100–150 mA/m²** to bronze blades; the evolved hydrogen gas layer cushions bubble collapse.

---

### Question 2: Dezincification of Manganese Bronze `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. What is dezincification? Why are Ni-Al Bronze propellers preferred for modern naval vessels?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Dezincification is a form of **selective-leach corrosion** in which the zinc atoms of a copper-zinc alloy (such as Manganese Bronze, typically 60% Cu – 38% Zn – 2% Mn) are preferentially dissolved by seawater, leaving behind a porous, mechanically weak, spongy copper residue.

Ni-Al Bronze is preferred because:

1. It contains **no zinc** (composition ~78% Cu – 9–12% Al – 4–6% Fe – 4–6% Ni), eliminating dezincification entirely.
2. It develops a **self-healing alumina (Al₂O₃) passive film** that is highly resistant to chloride attack.
3. It exhibits superior **cavitation-erosion resistance** under KTU-standard seawater conditions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Galvanic Corrosion of Stern-Gear Assembly `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### (a) **7-Mark Sub-Question:**
**"With a neat schematic, explain the galvanic-corrosion phenomenon occurring in a typical propeller–shaft–hull assembly. Discuss the role of (i) zinc shaft anode, (ii) glass-reinforced plastic (GRP) bearing isolator, and (iii) silver-graphite earthing brush."**

**Model Answer:**

In a marine stern-gear assembly, three dissimilar metals — the **mild-steel hull** (anode), the **bronze propeller** (cathode), and the **stainless-steel intermediate shaft** — are electrically continuous through the **seawater electrolyte**. Because the bronze propeller is more noble in the galvanic series, the hull adjacent to the stern bearing acts as a sacrificial anode and corrodes rapidly. This is the well-known **galvanic corrosion of the stern gear**.

The current density of the galvanic cell is given by:

$$
i_{\text{g}} = \frac{E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}}{R_{\text{electrolyte}} + R_{\text{metal}}}
$$

To suppress this current, three proven interventions are used:

**(i) Zinc Shaft Anode:** A zinc disc is fitted to the propeller shaft and rotates with it. Because zinc is the **least noble** of the three metals, it preferentially corrodes (Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻), diverting the galvanic current away from the hull. The design life of the anode is calculated from:

$$
W_{\text{anode}} = \frac{I_{\text{req}} \cdot t \cdot 8760}{u \cdot C}
$$

**(ii) GRP Bearing Isolator:** A non-conductive glass-reinforced plastic sleeve is installed in the stern tube. This physically breaks the **metallic return path** of the galvanic current, forcing the current to flow only through the seawater — which has much higher resistivity, dramatically reducing the corrosion current.

**(iii) Silver-Graphite Earthing Brush:** The propeller shaft is the only path by which the bronze propeller is grounded to the hull. A spring-loaded silver-graphite brush rides on the shaft slip-ring, providing a low-resistance return path for **stray currents from ICCP systems** while still permitting the GRP isolator to break the *galvanic* loop. This prevents **induced a.c. corrosion** of the shaft bearings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners expect a diagram of the assembly showing the three metals and the protective devices. A 1-mark bonus is often awarded for a labelled diagram.**

---

#### (b) **7-Mark Sub-Question:**
**"A ship requires a cathodic-protection current of 3.5 A for a 4-year dry-docking interval. Calculate the mass of zinc anodes required, given anode capacity C = 780 A·h/kg and utilization factor u = 0.85. If aluminium anodes (C = 2700 A·h/kg, u = 0.90) were used instead, what mass saving is achieved?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Total charge to be delivered over 4 years:**

$$
Q = I_{\text{req}} \cdot t \cdot 8760 = 3.5 \times 4 \times 8760
$$

$$
Q = 122{,}640 \;\text{A·h}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of zinc anode required:**

$$
W_{\text{Zn}} = \frac{Q}{u \cdot C_{\text{Zn}}} = \frac{122{,}640}{0.85 \times 780}
$$

$$
W_{\text{Zn}} = \frac{122{,}640}{663} \approx 185.0 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of aluminium anode required:**

$$
W_{\text{Al}} = \frac{Q}{u_{\text{Al}} \cdot C_{\text{Al}}} = \frac{122{,}640}{0.90 \times 2700}
$$

$$
W_{\text{Al}} = \frac{122{,}640}{2430} \approx 50.5 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass saving using aluminium:**

$$
\Delta W = 185.0 - 50.5 = 134.5 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage reduction:**

$$
\% \text{ saving} = \frac{134.5}{185.0} \times 100 \approx 72.7\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Switching from zinc to aluminium anodes yields a **mass saving of 134.5 kg (≈ 72.7%)** over the 4-year interval, justifying the higher unit cost of aluminium anodes for large ocean-going vessels.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Cavitation Number and Pitting Resistance `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### (a) **7-Mark Sub-Question:**
**"Derive the cavitation number expression for a marine propeller. Explain how the cavitation number guides the selection of propeller material."**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**

Consider a fluid element passing over a propeller blade at free-stream velocity $V$ and ambient pressure $P_\infty$. The dynamic pressure (velocity head) of the flow is:

$$
q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2
$$

The local static pressure at any point on the blade can drop to as low as:

$$
P_{\text{local}} = P_\infty - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2
$$

Cavitation initiates when $P_{\text{local}}$ falls to or below the vapour pressure of seawater, $P_v$. The **cavitation number** $\sigma$ is defined as the dimensionless ratio of the *available pressure margin* (above vapour pressure) to the *dynamic pressure* of the flow:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P_\infty - P_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2}
$$

**Onset criterion:** Cavitation occurs if

$$
\sigma \le \sigma_{\text{cr}}
$$

**Material selection:**

The **critical cavitation number** $\sigma_{\text{cr}}$ is a material property that quantifies the threshold below which cavitation erosion begins.

- **Manganese Bronze:** $\sigma_{\text{cr}} \approx 0.8$ → cavitates easily → severe erosion.
- **Ni-Al Bronze:** $\sigma_{\text{cr}} \approx 0.4$ → resists cavitation onset → modern choice.
- **Super-cavitation propellers** (special high-speed craft): operate at $\sigma < 0.1$ → use stainless-steel or titanium alloys.

For most commercial propellers operating at $\sigma \approx 0.5$–$0.7$, Ni-Al Bronze is the KTU-recommended material.

---

#### (b) **7-Mark Sub-Question:**
**"Two stainless-steel grades are considered for a marine heat-exchanger tube application: (i) Type 316 with 17% Cr, 2.5% Mo, 0.05% N; and (ii) a 6-Mo super-austenitic steel with 20% Cr, 6% Mo, 0.20% N. Calculate and compare their PREN values. Recommend the more suitable grade for tropical service."**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — PREN for Type 316 SS:**

$$
\text{PREN}_{316} = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N
$$

$$
= 17.0 + 3.3(2.5) + 16(0.05)
$$

$$
= 17.0 + 8.25 + 0.80 = 26.05
$$

**Step 2 — PREN for 6-Mo super-austenitic steel:**

$$
\text{PREN}_{6Mo} = 20.0 + 3.3(6.0) + 16(0.20)
$$

$$
= 20.0 + 19.8 + 3.2 = 43.0
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison and recommendation:**

The 6-Mo super-austenitic steel has a PREN value of **43.0**, which is **> 40** and indicates excellent pitting resistance in chloride-rich, warm seawater. Type 316 SS has a PREN of only **26.05** (< 30), making it vulnerable to pitting in tropical service. Therefore, the **6-Mo super-austenitic steel** is recommended for tropical marine heat-exchanger applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in corrosion-rate questions:** Always quote CR in **mm/year** (or mpy) and explicitly state density in **g/cm³** and area in **cm²**.
> 2. **Confusing dezincification with graphitic corrosion:** Graphitic corrosion occurs in cast irons, NOT in copper alloys. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing "cathode" for "anode" in galvanic cells:** The *more noble* metal is the cathode (does not corrode); the *less noble* metal is the anode (corrodes). Hull = anode, propeller = cathode.
> 4. **Omitting the 8760 conversion factor:** Cathodic-protection anode-mass problems *always* require this factor to convert years to hours. Forgetting it costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the utilization factor $u$:** Theoretical mass must be divided by $u$ — examiners often include a 2-mark sub-question specifically testing this.
> 6. **Wrong PREN formula:** Some students write $\%Cr + \%Mo$ — the KTU-accepted formula is the **weighted** form with **3.3 and 16 coefficients**.
> 7. **Failing to state reference electrode:** Whenever cathodic protection potentials are quoted, the reference electrode (typically **Ag/AgCl/seawater**) must be mentioned.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (last-minute KTU preparation):**

- **Cavitation Number:** $\sigma = \dfrac{P_\infty - P_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2}$; cavitation when $\sigma \le \sigma_{\text{cr}}$; Ni-Al Bronze has $\sigma_{\text{cr}} \approx 0.4$ vs. Manganese Bronze's $\sigma_{\text{cr}} \approx 0.8$.
- **Dezincification:** Selective leaching of Zn from Cu-Zn alloys; prevented by using **Ni-Al Bronze** (Zn-free).
- **Galvanic Cell of Stern Gear:** Hull = anode (corrodes), Bronze = cathode (protected); mitigate with **zinc shaft anode**, **GRP bearing isolator**, and **silver-graphite earthing brush**.
- **Crevice Corrosion:** Differential aeration cell inside gaskets/fasteners; mitigated by design (eliminate crevices), use of **PREN > 40** alloys.
- **Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number:** $\text{PREN} = \%Cr + 3.3\,\%Mo + 16\,\%N$; threshold **> 40** for marine service.
- **Atmospheric Corrosion of Deck Fittings:** Mitigated by **HDG (ISO 1461, ≥ 85 μm)** plus optional **duplex epoxy/polyurethane topcoat**.
- **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC):** Affects martensitic stainless above **60°C** in Cl⁻ environments; avoided with 316L or cathodic protection.
- **Cathodic Protection Potential:** **E = −0.85 V vs. Ag/AgCl** for steel in seawater.
- **Anode Mass Formula:** $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot 8760}{u \cdot C}$ — memorize with units.
- **Corrosion Rate Formula:** $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot m}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ in mm/yr — memorize with units.
- **Impingement Attack:** Localized erosion in condenser tubes at tube inlets; fix with **ferrous-sulfate dosing** and **inserted ferrules**.
- **Fretting Corrosion:** Micro-motion damage at shaft flange faces; fix with **PTFE anti-fretting washers** and rubber boots.
- **Cavitation-Erosion:** Combined mechanical-electrochemical attack on propeller suction face; mitigated by **Ni-Al Bronze** and **cathodic hydrogen cushion**.
- **Pack Rust:** Crevice expansion in hatch-cover lap joints; sealed with **polysulfide sealant** or replaced by **one-piece fabricated covers**.
- **Biofouling-Accelerated Attack:** Marine growth on heat-exchanger tubes creates differential aeration cells; control with **chlorination dosing (0.5–1.0 ppm residual Cl₂)** and **tube-cleaning intervals**.
- **KTU-Favourite Exam Lines:** "PREN > 40," "Ag/AgCl reference," "Ni-Al Bronze over Manganese Bronze," "8760 hours per year," "σ_cr = 0.4 for Ni-Al Bronze."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material storage and preservation in shipyard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Material storage and preservation in shipyard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Material storage and preservation in a shipyard** refers to the systematic, engineered management of receiving, identifying, stacking, protecting, and issuing all construction materials (steel plates, sections, pipes, welding consumables, paints, machinery, outfit items, and electrical equipment) such that their mechanical, chemical, and dimensional properties are retained unaltered from the point of receipt to the point of consumption in ship construction.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, this concept is governed by the dual objectives of:
- **Material Integrity (MI):** Prevention of corrosion, deformation, contamination, and deterioration during the storage period.
- **Material Traceability (MT):** Maintenance of documented chain-of-custody linking every kilogram of input material to its final installed position in the hull, machinery, or outfit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> Material storage and preservation in a shipyard encompasses the methodologies, yard infrastructure, environmental controls, and protective packaging systems used to maintain the fitness-for-purpose of shipbuilding materials from the moment they enter the shipyard boundary until they are consumed in fabrication, sub-assembly, or block erection.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **pharmaceutical warehouse** that stores thousands of life-saving medicines. Every tablet must remain sterile, dry, at the correct temperature, and traceable to its batch number, expiry date, and patient. A shipyard material store behaves identically, except the "medicines" are **multi-tonne steel plates, electrodes, and propeller shafts** instead of tablets, and the "patient" is a 300-metre ship that must sail the ocean for 25–30 years without failure.

If a tablet loses its sterility, only one patient is affected. If a **20 mm thick hull plate** develops **deep pitting corrosion** during six months of careless outdoor storage, the fatigue life of the entire ship hull can be compromised — a single defect originating in the store can manifest as a structural failure a decade later at sea.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world consequence:** Shipyards that ignore proper preservation incur an estimated **3–8% material wastage** annually due to re-conditioning, blasting, and rejection of corroded stock. For a mid-sized yard fabricating 100,000 tonnes of steel per year, this translates to roughly **5,000–8,000 tonnes of rejected material per year**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Standard KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **Lay-down area** | Designated open or covered yard zone for stacking plates, sections, and sub-assemblies. |
| **Stockyard** | The largest outdoor storage area, typically used for bulk plates and rolled sections. |
| **Bonded store** | A secure, controlled warehouse for high-value or hazardous items (paint, gas cylinders, instruments). |
| **Preservation** | Application of protective measures (oil, grease, VCI, dehumidification) to prevent material degradation. |
| **Shelf life** | The maximum duration a material may be stored before mandatory re-inspection or rejection. |
| **Custody transfer** | The formal handover of a material from store-keeper to fabrication/erection team, with sign-off. |
| **Traceability** | A documented record that links each material item to its heat number, certificate, and installed location. |
| **Stock rotation** | First-In-First-Out (FIFO) usage policy to prevent over-ageing of stock. |
| **Fitting-out quay** | The waterfront zone where the completed hull is berthed for outfitting, requiring on-board preservation. |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Shipyard Storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **bold** metrics are the standard KTU-board numerical benchmarks for material storage design:
>
> - **Plate stack height limit: 1.5 m to 2.0 m maximum** (varies by plate thickness; thinner plates → lower stacks)
> - **Standard storage humidity ceiling: 60% Relative Humidity (RH)** for coated/painted items
> - **Critical humidity threshold for corrosion initiation on steel: 60% RH** (below this, atmospheric corrosion rate drops dramatically)
> - **VCI protection shelf life: 12 to 24 months** in sealed packaging
> - **Welding electrode re-drying temperature: 300–350 °C** for basic low-hydrogen electrodes
> - **Paint storage temperature range: 5 °C to 30 °C** (most epoxy systems)
> - **Plate-to-plate contact corrosion prevention: timber or polymer spacers every 1.0–1.5 m**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Five Pillars of Shipyard Material Storage

The discipline of material storage and preservation in a shipyard rests on **five engineering pillars**, each of which is examined individually below.

#### Pillar 1 — Material Receiving Inspection

When material arrives at the shipyard gate, it must pass through a **Receiving Inspection Station (RIS)**. The RIS performs the following logical sequence:

1. **Document verification** — Mill certificate, test certificate, heat number, grade, dimensions, quantity vs. purchase order.
2. **Visual inspection** — Surface condition, dents, edge damage, distortion, packaging integrity.
3. **Dimensional check** — Length, width, thickness, squareness, camber, flatness.
4. **Marking and tagging** — Affixing a unique yard-internal ID code (e.g., `MAT-2024-PLT-00145`) that links the item to the digital MRP/ERP system.
5. **Allocation decision** — Direct issue to production, or transfer to stockyard/bonded store.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A steel plate that arrives with a mill certificate marked "Grade AH36" but is **internally segregated** (lamination defect) is unfit for a critical hull location. KTU expects students to know that acceptance/rejection at receipt is the **first and most powerful line of defence** against in-service failure.

#### Pillar 2 — Stacking, Spacing, and Mechanical Handling Logic

Different materials demand different stacking geometries. The generic decision tree is:

- **Flat plates** → Stacked horizontally on **timber or concrete sleepers** (100 mm × 100 mm cross-section), with **spacer strips** between every plate to allow fork-truck sling access and air circulation.
- **Rolled sections (angles, channels, I-beams)** → Stacked in **nests** on level ground, often with the flanges nested together to maximise density.
- **Pipes and tubes** → Stored in **circular racks** or stacked on **saddle supports** to prevent ovality.
- **Long bars and shafts** → Stored in **A-frame racks** to prevent sag and surface contact corrosion.
- **Coils** → Stored **vertically (eye-to-sky)** on dedicated coil cradles, never lying flat (which causes coil "candy-floss" deformation).
- **Cylinders (gas, paint)** → Stored **upright, chained, and capped** in well-ventilated cages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanical-handling rule:** Every stack must be approachable from at least two sides by an overhead crane or fork-lift. The aisle width between stacks is governed by the turning radius of the largest yard vehicle, typically **6 m to 8 m** for heavy lift.

#### Pillar 3 — Environmental Control

The most common degradation mode of stored steel is **atmospheric corrosion**. The governing principle is the **critical humidity rule**:

> Below **60% RH**, the corrosion rate of unprotected carbon steel drops to negligible levels (less than 10 µm/year). Above **60% RH**, corrosion rate accelerates roughly **exponentially**.

Therefore, the storage philosophy splits into two regimes:

- **Outdoor storage (stockyard):** Material receives **temporary shop primer (blast primer or pre-construction primer, 15–25 µm DFT)** at receipt. The primer is the *primary defence*; the yard provides drainage and slope to prevent ponding.
- **Indoor storage (bonded store, paint store, electrode store):** Environment is **actively controlled** with dehumidifiers, HVAC, and thermometers. RH held below **60%**, temperature held between **5 °C and 30 °C**.

#### Pillar 4 — Preservation Methods (The Core Engineering Knowledge)

This is the most heavily examined area in KTU Module 3. The seven primary preservation techniques are:

| # | Method | Working Principle | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Temporary shop primer (TSP)** | Thin epoxy or zinc-rich coating (15–25 µm) sprayed on blasted steel. Sacrificial or barrier protection. | All stockyard plates and sections. |
| 2 | **Oiling / greasing** | Mineral oil or petroleum jelly film excludes water and oxygen from the metal surface. | Machined shafts, bearings, gear teeth, hydraulic rods. |
| 3 | **Wrapping (polythene / tarpaulin)** | Physical barrier against rain, dust, salt spray. | Spare machinery, instruments, small parts. |
| 4 | **Volatile Corrosion Inhibitor (VCI)** | VCI chemicals sublime into vapour; vapour molecules adsorb on metal surface forming a mono-molecular protective film. | Inside pipe bores, gearboxes, electric motors, sealed crates. |
| 5 | **Desiccant bags (silica gel)** | Adsorbs moisture inside sealed enclosures, holds local RH below 40%. | Inside instrument crates, switchboard panels. |
| 6 | **Dehumidification (DH)** | Mechanical or desiccant dehumidifier maintains room RH below 50% in a closed space. | Electronic stores, paint store, electrode store. |
| 7 | **Sealed plastic caps/plugs** | Closes threaded openings, pipe ends, valve ends. | Pipe spools, valves, flanges, hydraulic ports. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Board-Favourite Question Pattern:** "Explain VCI. Why is it preferred over oil for internal preservation of gearboxes?" — Answer: VCI vapour reaches *every internal surface* including complex cavities that oil cannot coat, and the part is *ready to use* (no degreasing required).

#### Pillar 5 — Traceability and Stock Rotation

Every modern shipyard operates a **digital MRP/ERP system** (e.g., SAP, AMOS, or proprietary yard software). When a plate is issued, the system records:

- Heat number → linked to mill certificate
- Yard internal ID → linked to drawing and welding procedure
- Issued to → specific panel/block/erection zone
- Installed position → final hull coordinate (frame, deck, strake)

The issuance rule is **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)**, with the exception of **FEFO (First-Expired-First-Out)** for paints and chemicals with explicit shelf lives.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$t_{stack,max} = \frac{2 \sigma_y \cdot b^2}{\pi^2 \cdot E} \cdot \text{SF}^{-1}$$ | $t$ = max stack height (m), $\sigma_y$ = yield strength (MPa), $b$ = plate width (m), $E$ = Young's modulus (MPa), $SF$ = safety factor | Theoretical limit to avoid plate-stack buckling; in practice limited to **2.0 m** by yard rules. |
| 2 | $$\text{CR} = \frac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$$ | $CR$ = corrosion rate (g/m²·yr), $\Delta m$ = mass loss (g), $A$ = exposed area (m²), $t$ = exposure time (yr) | Quantifies how much steel is lost during storage; basis for re-blasting decisions. |
| 3 | $$\text{CR}_{depth} = \frac{8.76 \times \text{CR}_{mass}}{\rho_{Fe}}$$ | $CR_{depth}$ = µm/yr, $CR_{mass}$ = g/m²·yr, $\rho_{Fe} = 7.85$ g/cm³ | Converts mass-loss corrosion rate into thickness-loss per year. |
| 4 | $$\text{RH}_{critical} \approx 60\%$$ | — | Threshold below which atmospheric corrosion becomes negligible. |
| 5 | $$n_{sleeper} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{plate}}{1.0 \text{ m}} \right\rceil + 1$$ | $n_{sleeper}$ = number of sleeper rows, $L_{plate}$ = plate length (m) | Minimum number of spacers to prevent plate sag in stack. |
| 6 | $$\text{Aisle}_{min} = 2 \cdot R_{turn} + L_{load}$$ | $R_{turn}$ = vehicle turning radius (m), $L_{load}$ = load length (m) | Minimum aisle width between stacks for fork-lift access. |
| 7 | $$\text{Dew point: } T_d = T - \frac{100 - RH}{5}$$ | $T_d$ = dew point (°C), $T$ = air temperature (°C), $RH$ in % | Used in paint store to predict condensation risk. |
| 8 | $$\text{Vapour pressure: } P_v = P_{sat}(T) \cdot \frac{RH}{100}$$ | $P_v$ = partial vapour pressure (Pa), $P_{sat}$ = saturation pressure (Pa) | Determines whether a sealed enclosure will sweat. |
| 9 | $$\text{Electrode re-dry time: } t_{dry} = k \cdot d_{electrode}$$ | $t_{dry}$ = hours, $d$ = electrode diameter (mm), $k \approx 1$ h/mm | Standard rule: 1 hour per mm of electrode diameter at 300–350 °C. |
| 10 | $$\text{Storage life} = f(\text{Temp}, \text{Humidity}, \text{Sealing})$$ | Qualitative | Paint shelf life roughly halves for every 10 °C rise above 25 °C. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do **not** use the pipe symbol `|` inside any formula within an answer table. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead, as required by the markdown-table syntax. A broken table in the answer sheet is read as **zero comprehension** by some KTU examiners.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

The above framework is the backbone of every classification society audit (DNV, LR, BV, ABS, IRS). Before a ship is delivered, the classification surveyor audits the **material traceability file** for the entire vessel. If a single 8 mm plate in the side shell cannot be traced back to its mill certificate, the **class certificate can be withheld**, delaying delivery by weeks and costing the yard millions in liquidated damages. Material storage and preservation is therefore not a "back-office" function — it is a **legal, financial, and safety-critical** engineering activity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Corrosion Rate Conversion (Mass Loss to Thickness Loss)

A common 3-mark KTU question asks: *"A steel plate exposed in the stockyard for 6 months loses 245 g/m². Convert this to a thickness loss in micrometres."* The full working is shown below.

**Step 1 — Identify the knowns**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass loss per unit area: } \Delta m_{A} &= 245 \text{ g/m}^2 \\
\text{Steel density: } \rho_{Fe} &= 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
\text{Time: } t &= 6 \text{ months} = 0.5 \text{ year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the mass-loss corrosion rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR_{mass} &= \frac{\Delta m_{A}}{t} \\
&= \frac{245 \text{ g/m}^2}{0.5 \text{ yr}} \\
&= 490 \text{ g/(m}^2 \cdot \text{yr)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert mass loss to volume loss**

Volume of steel lost per unit area is mass divided by density:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V_A &= \frac{\Delta m_{A}}{\rho_{Fe}} \\
&= \frac{245 \text{ g/m}^2}{7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert units consistently**

Converting $\rho_{Fe}$ to g/m³: $\rho_{Fe} = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3 \times 10^6 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}^3 = 7.85 \times 10^6 \text{ g/m}^3$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V_A &= \frac{245}{7.85 \times 10^6} \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2 \\
&= 3.121 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert volume loss per unit area to thickness loss**

Since the lost volume is spread over 1 m², the thickness loss equals the volume loss per unit area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \delta &= 3.121 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \\
&= 31.21 \text{ µm} \quad (\text{over 6 months})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Annualise the rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR_{depth} &= \frac{31.21 \text{ µm}}{0.5 \text{ yr}} \\
&\approx 62.4 \text{ µm/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result interpretation:** 62.4 µm/yr is consistent with the **C2–C3 atmospheric corrosivity category** of ISO 12944 (low-to-medium corrosivity), which is typical for an industrial-port shipyard. The KTU board expects the student to *state the ISO category* alongside the numerical result for full 3-mark credit.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Number of Sleepers in a Plate Stack

A 12 m long plate is to be stored flat. The rule is one sleeper row per metre of plate length, plus one at each end. Find the minimum number of sleeper rows.

**Step 1 — Apply the spacing rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{sleeper} &= \left\lceil \frac{L_{plate}}{s_{max}} \right\rceil + 1 \\
\text{where } s_{max} &= 1.0 \text{ m} \text{ (maximum spacing between sleeper rows)} \\
L_{plate} &= 12 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{sleeper} &= \left\lceil \frac{12}{1.0} \right\rceil + 1 \\
&= 12 + 1 \\
&= 13 \text{ sleeper rows}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add end-bearing sleepers for stability**

For very long plates (> 10 m), a yard rule often requires **2 sleepers at each end** stacked side-by-side. Therefore total sleepers for the stack = **15 rows** (2 at each end, 11 in between). This number is what the KTU board expects in a 7-mark derivation answer.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dew Point and Condensation Risk in Paint Store

The paint store is at 28 °C and 75% RH. Will condensation form on a cold 14 °C steel drum entering the store?

**Step 1 — Compute dew point using the simplified Magus formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_d &= T - \frac{100 - RH}{5} \\
&= 28 - \frac{100 - 75}{5} \\
&= 28 - 5 \\
&= 23 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare drum surface temperature to dew point**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{drum} &= 14 \text{ °C} \\
T_d &= 23 \text{ °C} \\
T_{drum} &< T_d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion**

Since the drum surface is **cooler than the dew point**, water vapour in the store will condense on the drum. This is unacceptable for paint containers (label degradation, rust on lid, water contamination).

**Step 4 — Engineering control**

The store-keeper must either (a) **pre-warm the drum to above 23 °C** before introducing it, or (b) **lower the store RH to below ~50%** (so dew point falls to 14 °C and equalises with the drum).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Material-Type-Specific Storage Specification

| Material Type | Storage Zone | Spacing/Sleeper | Humidity Limit | Temperature Limit | Preservation Method | Re-Inspection Interval |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild steel plates (uncoated) | Stockyard, on sleepers | 1 m max, 100 mm sleeper | Outdoor, drain sloping | Outdoor | Shot-blast + shop primer 20 µm | 3 months |
| AH36/DH36/EH36 plates | Stockyard, marked separately | 1 m max | Outdoor | Outdoor | Shop primer + edge stripe coat | 3 months |
| Stainless steel plates | Covered shed, separate from carbon steel | Rubber-padded sleepers | < 70% RH | Ambient | Wrapped in polythene | 6 months |
| Pipes (carbon steel) | Pipe racks, saddle-supported | Saddle every 3 m | Outdoor | Outdoor | End caps + internal VCI emitter | 6 months |
| Stainless steel pipes | Separate covered rack | Rubber-padded saddles | < 70% RH | Ambient | End caps + sealed PE bag | 6 months |
| Welding electrodes (low-H) | Bonded electrode store, RH < 60% | On pallets, racks | < 60% RH | 5 °C – 30 °C | Original sealed canister; re-dry 350 °C / 1 h per mm | 4 hours out of oven |
| Paints (epoxy) | Paint store, fire-rated | On pallets, segregated by type | < 75% RH | 5 °C – 30 °C | Sealed original drum, FIFO | 12 months typical shelf life |
| Solvents / thinners | Fire-rated chemical store, ventilated | On bunded pallets | Ambient | < 30 °C | Sealed drum, away from oxidisers | 12 months |
| Valves and flanges | Covered shed, on timber pallets | Edge-protection foam | < 70% RH | Ambient | End-caps + VCI + PE shrink-wrap | 6 months |
| Machined shafts | Vertical A-frame rack | Covered | < 60% RH | Ambient | Oiled + VCI wrap | 3 months |
| Electric motors | Indoor store | On pallets | < 50% RH (DH) | 5 °C – 35 °C | Sealed crate + silica gel + VCI | 6 months |
| Gas cylinders (O₂, Ar, CO₂) | Open-air cylinder rack, chained | Upright, capped valve | Outdoor | Outdoor (shaded) | Valve cap on, chain restraint | 12 months |
| Outfit items (furniture, joinery) | Covered dry shed | On pallets | < 65% RH | Ambient | Wrapped PE, away from sunlight | 6 months |
| Copper-nickel / titanium tubes | Covered, segregated | Rubber-padded | < 60% RH | Ambient | Sealed ends + VCI + PE wrap | 6 months |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Shipyard Material Flow Architecture (From Gate to Installation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Arrival at Gate] --> B[Receiving Inspection Station RIS]
    B --> C{Material OK?}
    C -- No --> D[Reject and Return to Vendor]
    C -- Yes --> E[Assign Yard ID and Heat Number]
    E --> F{Material Type}
    F -- Steel Plates and Sections --> G[Stockyard with Shop Primer]
    F -- Pipes and Valves --> H[Pipe Rack and Valve Shed]
    F -- Welding Consumables --> I[Electrode Store RH less than 60 percent]
    F -- Paints and Chemicals --> J[Bonded Paint Store Fire Rated]
    F -- Machinery and Motors --> K[Indoor Store with Dehumidifier]
    F -- Outfit Items --> L[Outfit Store]
    G --> M[Fabrication Shop]
    H --> M
    I --> M
    M --> N[Sub Assembly Shop]
    N --> O[Block Erection Area]
    O --> P[Pre Outfitting and Hull Preservation]
    P --> Q[Launch and Fit Out Quay]
    Q --> R[S trials and Delivery]
    K --> Q
    J --> Q
    L --> Q
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above flowchart maps the entire chain-of-custody. Note that **preservation steps occur at multiple nodes** — the shop primer is applied at the stockyard, VCI is inserted at the pipe shed, and dehumidification operates continuously at the indoor store.

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Decision Logic for Preservation Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Item Entering Store] --> B{Is the item metallic?}
    B -- No --> C[Wrap in polythene and store in dry shed]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is the item machinery with internal cavities?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Insert VCI emitter and seal enclosure]
    D -- No --> F{Is the item a large flat surface steel plate?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Apply shot blast and shop primer 20 micrometre DFT]
    F -- No --> H{Is the item a machined sliding surface?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Apply oil or petroleum jelly film]
    H -- No --> J{Is the item sensitive electronic instrument?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Sealed crate with silica gel and VCI]
    J -- No --> L[Apply standard wrapping and label with preservation date]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Plate Stack Cross-Section (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ground Level Compacted Earth] --> B[Concrete or Timber Foundation Sleepers 100 by 100 mm]
    B --> C[First Plate with Shop Primer 20 micrometre]
    C --> D[Timber Spacer Strip 25 mm thick]
    D --> E[Second Plate with Shop Primer 20 micrometre]
    E --> F[Timber Spacer Strip 25 mm thick]
    F --> G[Third Plate with Shop Primer 20 micrometre]
    G --> H[Top Cover Plate Optional Tarpaulin]
    H --> I[End Identification Plate showing Heat Number Grade and Date]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key points visible in the diagram:** (i) Spacer strips between every plate allow sling access and air circulation; (ii) The top cover plate is optional but reduces rain ingress and UV degradation of the primer; (iii) End identification is mandatory for KTU 2024 traceability marks.

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Bonded Store Layout (Top-Down Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Yard[Shipyard Material Storage Complex]
        direction TB
        subgraph S1[Zone A Stockyard Open Air]
            P1[Plate Stack A1]
            P2[Plate Stack A2]
            P3[Section Nest A3]
        end
        subgraph S2[Zone B Covered Shed]
            T1[Pipe Racks B1]
            T2[Valve and Flange Pallets B2]
            T3[Stainless Steel Storage B3 segregated from carbon steel]
        end
        subgraph S3[Zone C Bonded Stores Climate Controlled]
            E1[Electrode Store RH less than 60 percent]
            E2[Paint Store Fire Rated 5 to 30 degree C]
            E3[Machinery Store with Dehumidifier]
        end
        subgraph S4[Zone D Hazardous Stores]
            H1[Gas Cylinder Cage Upright Chained]
            H2[Solvent Store Bunded Ventilated]
            H3[Chemical Store]
        end
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional zoning rationale:** The yard is divided into four zones by **risk class**. Zone A is open and low-value. Zone B is covered and medium-value. Zone C is climate-controlled for sensitive items. Zone D is segregated for hazardous materials. This zoning minimises cross-contamination (e.g., carbon-steel dust on stainless steel) and complies with HSE regulations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Define "Temporary Shop Primer" (TSP). State its typical Dry Film Thickness (DFT) and explain its role in stockyard preservation.*

**Model Answer:**

A Temporary Shop Primer (TSP), also called a pre-construction primer or blast primer, is a thin protective coating applied to freshly blast-cleaned steel (typically to Sa 2½ standard) immediately after the plate is received and accepted at the Receiving Inspection Station. The DFT is in the range of **15 µm to 25 µm**, considerably thinner than a full paint system. The TSP serves three roles in the stockyard: (i) it provides a sacrificial or barrier layer preventing rust formation during the months of outdoor storage between receipt and fabrication; (ii) it is weldable and blastable — meaning the fabricator can weld through it and remove it later with a high-pressure water jet or abrasive blast without contaminating the weld; (iii) it allows the plate to be identified by its colour-coded shop primer (e.g., red oxide, grey, blue) according to grade. KTU expects mention of DFT range, weldability, and the Sa 2½ surface preparation standard for full 3-mark credit.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *State the critical humidity threshold for atmospheric corrosion of steel. List two methods of preserving steel components stored in a shipyard.*

**Model Answer:**

The critical humidity threshold for the onset of significant atmospheric corrosion of unprotected carbon steel is **60% Relative Humidity (RH)**. Below this value, the adsorbed water film on the metal surface is too thin to support the electrolytic corrosion reaction, and the corrosion rate becomes negligible. Above 60% RH, the adsorbed layer thickens, ionic conduction becomes possible, and the corrosion rate increases approximately exponentially with further RH rise. Two preservation methods are: (i) **Temporary shop primer** — a 15–25 µm epoxy or zinc-rich coating applied to the blasted plate surface at the receiving inspection station; and (ii) **Oiling/greasing** — application of a mineral oil, petroleum jelly, or temporary rust preventive (e.g., Tectyl or CRC) film on machined surfaces such as shaft journals, gear teeth, and hydraulic rods to exclude moisture and oxygen. (Other acceptable answers: VCI, dehumidification, sealed wrapping, desiccants.)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, Part B questions carry 14 marks each, typically split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. **Question A OR Question B** choice is given. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Material Identification, Receiving, and Traceability**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3, Dec 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **Receiving Inspection procedure** for a consignment of Grade AH36 shipbuilding steel plates arriving at the shipyard. Include in your answer: (i) the documents to be verified, (ii) the visual and dimensional checks to be carried out, (iii) the surface preparation standard required for shop primer application, and (iv) the marking/identification system to be used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **storage and stacking procedure** for Grade AH36 plates in the stockyard. Mention the maximum stack height, the type of sleepers, the requirement for spacers, the role of the shop primer, and the drainage requirement of the stockyard floor.

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Solution — Receiving Inspection Procedure for AH36 Plates**

**Step 1 — Document Verification (2 marks)**
The yard clerk verifies the following documents at the gate office:
- **Mill Test Certificate (MTC):** issued by the steel plant, contains chemical composition (C, Mn, Si, S, P, Al, Nb, V, Ti), mechanical properties (yield ≥ 355 MPa for 16 mm AH36, tensile 490–620 MPa, elongation ≥ 21%), and heat number.
- **Purchase Order (PO):** confirms grade, thickness tolerance (EN 10029 Class A or B), dimensions, and quantity.
- **Delivery Challan and Invoice:** for goods-receipt notes.
- **Type Approval Certificate:** the plant must be approved by IACS (e.g., approval to UR W11 for rolled plates).

**Step 2 — Visual Inspection (2 marks)**
- Look for **surface defects**: cracks, scabs, laps, seams, and roll marks.
- Check **edge condition** for burrs or laminations.
- Confirm **no visible mill scale flaking** or pre-existing rust beyond acceptable limits.
- Verify the plate is **free from oil, grease, or salt deposits**.

**Step 3 — Dimensional and Ultrasonic Verification (1.5 marks)**
- Measure length, width, and thickness (using ultrasonic thickness gauge) at 4 corners and centre.
- Check **flatness, squareness, and edge camber** to EN 10029 tolerances.
- For critical applications, perform **ultrasonic testing (UT)** to detect internal laminations.

**Step 4 — Surface Preparation and Shop Primer (1 mark)**
- The accepted plate is moved to a **shot-blasting unit**.
- Blasted to **Sa 2½** visual standard (ISO 8501-1) with surface profile **50–100 µm**.
- Within 4 hours, apply **shop primer 15–25 µm DFT** (e.g., epoxy or zinc silicate).
- Stack in stockyard only after primer is touch-dry.

**Step 5 — Marking and Identification (0.5 marks)**
- Affix yard tag with unique code (e.g., `MAT-2024-AH36-00145`) showing heat number, grade, thickness, receipt date, and stock location.
- Update ERP/MRP system.

**(b) Solution — Stockyard Stacking Procedure**

**Step 1 — Site Preparation (1.5 marks)**
- Stockyard floor must be **compacted, level, and free-draining** with a minimum slope of **1:100** to prevent water ponding.
- A **gravel sub-base** or **concrete apron** is preferred.
- Floor load-bearing capacity must support **5 tonnes/m²** for heavy plate stacks.

**Step 2 — Sleeper Placement (2 marks)**
- Use **timber sleepers (100 mm × 100 mm)** or **concrete sleepers** at **1.0 m maximum spacing** along plate length.
- Sleepers must be aligned vertically (one stack directly above another) to transfer load uniformly to the ground.
- Add **2 sleepers side-by-side at each end** for stability of long plates.

**Step 3 — Plate Placement (2 marks)**
- Place first plate on the sleeper grid.
- Insert **25 mm thick timber spacers** between successive plates to:
  - Allow sling access for the crane.
  - Permit air circulation (preventing under-plate condensation).
  - Prevent plate-to-plate contact corrosion.
- Maximum stack height: **1.5–2.0 m** (for plates ≤ 25 mm thick); lower for thinner plates.
- **Top cover plate** (optional): a clean plate placed on top to protect the stack from rain and direct sunlight.

**Step 4 — Shop Primer Role (1 mark)**
- The shop primer applied at receipt provides the **primary corrosion barrier** during the entire storage period.
- Primer is weldable (no need to remove before welding) and blastable (removed later during pre-erection blasting).
- **Re-inspect the primer every 3 months**; if chalking, flaking, or rust spots are observed, re-blast and re-prime.

**Step 5 — Identification and Drainage (0.5 marks)**
- Each stack is labelled at the end with grade, heat number range, and date of stacking.
- Stockyard should have **open drainage channels** at 5–10 m spacing to evacuate rainwater rapidly.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Preservation Techniques and Electrode/Paint Storage**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3, Dec 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle and application of **Volatile Corrosion Inhibitor (VCI)** in shipyard material preservation. Compare it with conventional oiling/greasing. State three typical applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **correct storage procedure for welding electrodes and paints** in a shipyard bonded store. Include humidity, temperature, re-drying, and FIFO/FEFO rules.

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) Solution — VCI: Principle, Application, and Comparison with Oil**

**Step 1 — Principle of VCI (3 marks)**
Volatile Corrosion Inhibitors (VCI) are organic chemicals (e.g., amine nitrites, amine carboxylates, benzotriazole derivatives) that possess a measurable **vapour pressure at ambient temperature**. They are formulated as paper, foam chips, powder, tablets, or plastic film. The working principle is:
1. VCI molecules **sublime** from the carrier into the surrounding enclosed air.
2. The vapour molecules **diffuse** throughout every internal cavity, even into narrow gaps and blind holes that liquid oil cannot reach.
3. VCI molecules **adsorb** on the metal surface, forming a **mono-molecular hydrophobic film** approximately 3–10 nm thick.
4. This film displaces moisture and **passivates the metal surface**, preventing the anodic dissolution reaction of corrosion.

The protective film is **self-healing** — if scratched, additional VCI vapour deposits to re-protect the area. Typical protection duration in a sealed enclosure is **12–24 months**.

**Step 2 — Comparison with Oiling/Greasing (2 marks)**

| Parameter | VCI | Oiling/Greasing |
|---|---|---|
| Application | Paper emitter, foam, or film placed inside enclosure | Brush/spray oil film on surface |
| Coverage of internal cavities | **Yes** — vapour reaches everywhere | **No** — oil cannot coat internal surfaces |
| Cleaning before use | **Not required** (volatile, evaporates) | **Required** (solvent degreasing) |
| Suitability for food-grade parts | Limited (amine odour) | Suitable (food-grade oils available) |
| Outdoor exposure | Limited (vapour escapes) | Excellent (oil film thick) |
| Cost per m² | Medium | Low–medium |

**Step 3 — Three Typical Applications (2 marks)**
1. **Inside pipe bores** — VCI foam plugs inserted into pipe ends to protect internal bore during storage and shipment.
2. **Inside gearboxes and electric motors** — VCI paper or chips placed inside the housing before crating.
3. **Inside instrument crates and switchboard panels** — VCI film wrapping + silica gel desiccant for combined moisture and corrosion protection.
4. (Acceptable: machined shaft packing, hydraulic cylinder bores, ball-valve cavities.)

**(b) Solution — Storage of Welding Electrodes and Paints**

**Step 1 — Electrode Store Specification (3 marks)**
- **Relative Humidity:** must be maintained **below 60% RH**, ideally **40–50%** for low-hydrogen electrodes (e.g., E7018, E8018).
- **Temperature:** **5 °C to 30 °C**, with a recommended storage temperature of **15–25 °C** to prevent moisture absorption.
- **Ventilation:** adequate but not excessive (to avoid dust ingress).
- **Racking:** electrodes stored on **pallets or racks above floor level** (minimum 200 mm clearance) to prevent wicking of floor moisture.
- **Identification:** each canister must be marked with electrode classification, batch number, manufacturing date, and re-drying status.

**Step 2 — Re-Drying Procedure (2 marks)**
- **Basic low-hydrogen electrodes** (e.g., E7018): re-dry at **300–350 °C for 1 hour per mm of electrode diameter** (i.e., 4 mm electrode → 4 hours; 5 mm → 5 hours).
- **Cellulosic electrodes** (e.g., E6010): do **not** re-dry; store in original sealed canister only.
- **Stainless steel electrodes**: re-dry at **200–250 °C for 1–2 hours**.
- Re-dried electrodes must be **used within 4 hours** of removal from the drying oven, or stored in a **quiver at 80–100 °C** until issue.

**Step 3 — Paint Store Specification (1.5 marks)**
- **Fire-rated construction** (1–2 hour fire wall) and **explosion-proof electrical fittings**.
- **Ventilation:** mechanical exhaust, **6–10 air changes per hour**.
- **Temperature:** **5 °C to 30 °C**; most epoxies cure poorly below 5 °C and degrade above 35 °C.
- **RH:** below **75%** in the paint store (paint drums are sealed, but labelling and metal lids can degrade).
- **Segregation:** store **incompatible products separately** (oxidising agents away from solvents; acids away from alkalis).
- **Bunding:** each drum on a **bund pallet** (110% containment of the largest drum).

**Step 4 — Stock Rotation: FIFO and FEFO (0.5 marks)**
- **FIFO** (First-In-First-Out) is the default for electrodes and general consumables.
- **FEFO** (First-Expired-First-Out) is mandatory for paints with explicit shelf-life labels — the paint with the earliest expiry date is issued first.
- Every issue is recorded in the **paint stock register** with batch number, quantity, and issue date.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks Lost on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing RH and Dew Point.** Students often write "paint store RH must be below 60%" — this is the **electrode** rule. Paint store is **75% RH max**. Mixing the two costs 1 full mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to weld through shop primer.** A common error is to state the shop primer must be "removed before welding" — it does not; modern TSPs are **weldable**. Saying it must be removed costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Stating "VCI is a coating."** VCI is a **vapour-phase inhibitor**, not a coating. Calling it a coating will be marked wrong.
> 4. **Missing the FIFO/FEFO distinction.** Always mention both; for KTU 2024, FEFO is specifically tagged for paints and chemicals with expiry.
> 5. **Using "ISO 8501 Sa 3" for shop primer preparation.** Sa 2½ is the standard. Sa 3 (white metal) is for higher-spec final coats and is over-spec for TSP.
> 6. **Skipping the chain-of-custody step.** Traceability is a KTU 2024 high-priority topic. Always state the **heat number → yard ID → installation position** chain.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Five pillars of shipyard material storage:** Receiving Inspection → Stacking → Environmental Control → Preservation → Traceability.
- **Receiving Inspection Station (RIS):** Verifies mill certificate, dimensions, surface condition, and assigns a unique yard ID.
- **Critical humidity for steel corrosion:** **60% RH** is the threshold; below this, atmospheric corrosion is negligible.
- **Shop primer DFT:** **15–25 µm**, applied to Sa 2½ blasted surface, weldable, blastable, and the **first line of defence** for stockyard plates.
- **Maximum plate stack height:** **1.5–2.0 m** with timber spacers and 100 × 100 mm sleepers at **1 m max spacing**.
- **VCI principle:** Sublimating organic molecules form a mono-molecular hydrophobic film on metal — **reaches internal cavities** that oil cannot.
- **Electrode store:** RH **< 60%**, temperature **5–30 °C**, re-dry low-H electrodes at **300–350 °C for 1 h/mm** diameter.
- **Paint store:** Fire-rated, ventilated, RH **< 75%**, temperature **5–30 °C**, **FEFO** rule for shelf-life items.
- **Traceability chain:** Mill certificate + Heat number → Yard ID → Drawing reference → Block/Panel → Installed position in hull.
- **Stainless steel storage rule:** **Segregate from carbon steel** to avoid iron-contamination rust staining.
- **Coil storage rule:** Store **vertically (eye-to-sky)**, never flat.
- **Gas cylinder storage rule:** **Upright, chained, capped**, in well-ventilated open-air cage.
- **Corrosion rate conversion:** $\text{CR}_{depth} (\mu m/yr) = \frac{8.76 \times \text{CR}_{mass} (g/m^2/yr)}{7.85}$.
- **Dew point rule:** Condensation occurs when surface temperature falls **below** the air dew point.
- **Classification society audit:** Final **material traceability file** is audited before class certificate is issued — missing one heat number can delay ship delivery.
- **FIFO** for general consumables; **FEFO** for paints and chemicals with explicit shelf life.
- **VCI is not a coating** — it is a **vapour-phase inhibitor** that self-heals and does not require degreasing before use.
- **Plate-to-plate contact** must be prevented by **timber spacers** to avoid crevice corrosion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Treatment of steel in shipyards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Treatment of steel in shipyards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Treatment of steel in shipyards** refers to the systematic sequence of metallurgical, mechanical, chemical, and surface-engineering processes applied to structural shipbuilding steel (conforming to grades such as **ASTM A36, A131, EH36, DH36, and EH40**) from the moment raw plates and sections arrive at the yard until they are protected by a long-term corrosion-prevention system. The objective is to transform mill-rolled steel — which is covered with **mill scale, rust (iron oxides), oils, grease, and surface contaminants** — into a clean, profiled, and chemically stable substrate that will accept protective coatings and remain durable throughout the vessel's service life of **25–30 years**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme treats *Treatment of Steel* as the bridge between **material selection (Module 2)** and **corrosion prevention coatings (Module 4)**. Mastery of this topic is essential for understanding the *Why* behind every paint specification.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Painting" Intuition

Think of steel treatment exactly like **preparing a wall before painting your house**:

- A freshly plastered wall is **rough, dusty, and chalky**. If you paint directly on it, the paint peels in months.
- A homeowner first **washes** the wall (chemical cleaning), then **scrapes off loose flakes** (mechanical descaling), then **sands it** to create a slight roughness (profile/anchor pattern), then **applies a primer** (pre-fabrication primer), and finally **puts the top coats**.
- In a shipyard, the steel plate is the wall, the rust/mill scale is the chalky dust, shot blasting is the sanding, and the coating system is the paint.

The single most important rule: **No coating system in the world — however expensive — will survive on a poorly prepared steel surface.** A **$200 per litre** anti-corrosive will fail in **6 months** over an unprepared substrate, but a **properly applied $20 per litre** epoxy will last **15+ years**.

## 1.3 The Five Sequential Stages (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners consistently ask students to *list and explain* the stages. Memorize this exact sequence.

1. **Material Reception & Identification** — Verification of **MTC (Mill Test Certificate)**, grade marking, and dimensional checks.
2. **Pre-Cutting Surface Preparation** — Initial cleaning of **oil, grease, and loose debris** using solvents/alkaline cleaners.
3. **Cutting, Forming & Welding** — Mechanical/thermal cutting (oxy-fuel, plasma, laser) and edge preparation.
4. **Primary Surface Preparation (Post-Fabrication)** — **Shot blasting / grit blasting** to **Sa 2½** or **Sa 3** finish (ISO 8501-1) with a surface profile of **50–100 µm**.
5. **Coating Application** — Application of **pre-fabrication primer, anti-corrosive, anti-fouling, and top coats**.

## 1.4 Key Standards & Metrics (Must Know)

| Standard | Issuing Body | Purpose |
|----------|--------------|---------|
| **ISO 8501-1** | ISO | Visual surface preparation grades (Sa 1, Sa 2, Sa 2½, Sa 3) |
| **ISO 8503** | ISO | Surface roughness comparison specimens |
| **ISO 12944** | ISO | Corrosion protection by paint systems (C1–C5, Im1–Im3) |
| **SSPC-SP 6 / SP 10** | SSPC (USA) | Commercial / Near-White blast cleaning |
| **NACE No. 1 / No. 2** | NACE | White metal / Near-white metal blast |
| **ASTM A36 / A131** | ASTM | Shipbuilding structural steel grades |
| **PSPC** | IMO | Performance Standard for Protective Coatings (MSC.215(82)) |
| **NORSOK M-501** | NORSOK | Offshore surface preparation & coating |

## 1.5 Why Steel Treatment Matters in Shipbuilding

A modern **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier)** contains approximately **30,000–40,000 tonnes** of steel. The **hull** is continuously exposed to **seawater (highly corrosive — 35,000 ppm salinity)**, **atmospheric salt spray**, **UV radiation**, and **mechanical abrasion from cargo**. A single premature coating failure can cost a shipowner **$1–2 million** in dry-dock repairs. Hence, treatment is not optional — it is the **single largest contributor to a vessel's lifecycle cost**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage 1 — Material Reception & Pre-Treatment

### 2.1.1 Mill Scale: The Hidden Enemy
Mill scale is a **layer of iron oxides (FeO, Fe₃O₄, Fe₂O₃)** formed during hot rolling at temperatures around **900–1000 °C**. Its composition:

- **Wüstite (FeO)** — innermost, **~85%** of layer, unstable below 570 °C.
- **Magnetite (Fe₃O₄)** — middle layer, more stable.
- **Hematite (Fe₂O₃)** — outermost, thin but tough.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Board Mistake:** Students often write *"mill scale protects steel from rust."* This is **wrong**. Mill scale is **brittle, cathodic to the underlying steel, and develops cracks**, accelerating **underfilm corrosion** when moisture penetrates. It **must** be removed.

### 2.1.2 Steel Identification
Yards use **low-stress stamps, vibratory etching, or paint markers** to preserve grade identity (A, B, D, E, AH, DH, EH) through the production chain.

## 2.2 Stage 2 — Surface Preparation Methods (The Heart of the Topic)

### 2.2.1 Mechanical Methods
- **Wire Brushing** — Removes loose rust/scale only. Suitable for touch-ups.
- **Hand Tool Cleaning (St 2 / St 3)** — Equivalent to SSPC-SP 2 / SP 3.
- **Power Tool Cleaning** — Pneumatic descaling, needle guns.
- **Shot Blasting (Centrifugal)** — Steel shot (S 230 / S 330) propelled by impeller wheels. **Most common in modern yards.**
- **Grit Blasting (Compressed Air)** — Uses chilled iron grit, copper slag, garnet, or aluminium oxide. Gives sharper profile.
- **Water Jetting (Hydroblasting)** — Ultra-high pressure water (**> 1,000 bar**). No dust, no profile alteration. Grades: **WJ-1, WJ-2, WJ-3, WJ-4**.

### 2.2.2 Chemical Methods
- **Acid Pickling** — Immersion in **5–10% HCl** or **10–20% H₂SO₄** at **40–60 °C** for **15–60 minutes**, followed by **rinsing and neutralization** with alkali.
- **Alkaline Cleaning** — Removes oils/grease (pH 11–13).
- **Solvent Cleaning** — Wipe/immersion with mineral spirits, xylene.
- **Phosphating** — Converts surface to **iron phosphate crystals** (1–10 µm) for paint adhesion.
- **Chromating (Historical)** — Banned due to **Cr⁶⁺ toxicity** under REACH/RoHS.

### 2.2.3 Thermal Methods
- **Flame Cleaning** — Oxy-acetylene flame at **3,000 °C** to spall off mill scale. Used only for repairs.

### 2.2.4 Visual Surface Preparation Grades (ISO 8501-1)

| Grade | Description | % of Surface Free of Visible Contamination | Use Case |
|-------|-------------|---------------------------------------------|----------|
| **Sa 1** | Light blast | ~70% | Temporary protection |
| **Sa 2** | Commercial blast | ~85% | Atmospheric service |
| **Sa 2½** | Near-white metal | ~95% | **Marine ballast/coating systems (mandatory)** |
| **Sa 3** | White metal | 100% | Immersion critical, chemical tanks |

> [!NOTE]
> **For ship hulls, ISO Sa 2½ (SSPC-SP 10 / NACE No. 2) is the minimum legal requirement under IMO PSPC.**

### 2.2.5 Surface Profile (Anchor Pattern)
Profile is the **peak-to-valley roughness** measured with:
- **Comparator specimens (ISO 8503)** — Visual/tactile, segments labelled "Fine", "Medium", "Coarse".
- **Replica putty + micrometer** — Quantitative.
- **Stylus profilometer** — Most accurate, gives **Rₐ, R_z, R_max**.

**Typical shipbuilding range:** **50–100 µm** (Medium profile, **Rₐ ≈ 12.5–25 µm**).

## 2.3 Stage 3 — Cutting, Forming & Edge Treatment

- **Cold Cutting:** Shearing, sawing, machining — preferred for thin plates.
- **Hot Cutting:** Oxy-fuel (≥ 6 mm), plasma (3–40 mm), laser (≤ 25 mm).
- **Edge Preparation:** Beveling to **30° ± 5°** for full-penetration welds as per **AWS D1.1**.
- **Post-Cut Treatment:** Grinding slag, preheating for thick plates to avoid **Hydrogen Induced Cracking (HIC)**.

## 2.4 Stage 4 — Protective Coating Systems (Brief, Detailed in Module 4)

A typical **IMO PSPC-compliant** ballast tank system:

| Coat | Type | DFT (µm) | Function |
|------|------|----------|----------|
| 1st | Zinc silicate (pre-construction primer) | 15–25 | Temporary corrosion resistance for **6–12 months** |
| 2nd | Epoxy (anti-corrosive) | 150–200 | Primary barrier |
| 3rd | Epoxy (anti-corrosive) | 150–200 | Build-up thickness |
| 4th | Epoxy (tie/sealer) | 50 | Optional |
| Total | — | **≥ 320 µm** | **15-year target life** |

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks:** *"Why is surface preparation the most important step in corrosion prevention?"*
>
> **Answer:** A 2021 NACE study found that **~76% of all premature coating failures** are attributable to inadequate surface preparation — not bad paint, not bad applicators, but **bad surfaces**. The **adhesion strength** of an epoxy on a properly blasted **Sa 2½** surface is roughly **3–5 times** that of a wire-brushed surface.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield — Memorize Before Exam)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $A = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot r \cdot L$ | Cylindrical surface area (pipe/tank shell) |
| 2 | $A = \pi \cdot r^2$ | Cross-sectional area |
| 3 | $V = A_{plate} \cdot t$ | Steel volume for mass calc. |
| 4 | $m = \rho_{steel} \cdot V$ | Mass, where **$\rho_{steel} = 7{,}850 \ \text{kg/m}^3$** |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{m_{req}}{m_{plate}}$ | Number of plates required |
| 6 | $C = \dfrac{V_{paint}}{A \cdot DFT}$ | Theoretical paint coverage (m²/L) |
| 7 | $C_{real} = C \cdot \eta_{transfer}$ | Actual coverage, **$\eta_{transfer} \approx 0.6$–$0.8$** |
| 8 | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i \vert$ | Arithmetic mean roughness (use `\vert` for absolute value) |
| 9 | $R_z = \dfrac{1}{5} \sum_{i=1}^{5}(R_{max,i})$ | Mean peak-to-valley height |
| 10 | $t_{dry} = \dfrac{DFT}{R}$ | Drying time, where **$R$** = drying rate (µm/h) |
| 11 | $P_{corrosion} = K \cdot t^{n}$ | Atmospheric corrosion loss (ISO 9224) |
| 12 | $\text{Salt conc.} = 35 \ \text{ppt}$ (≈ 3.5%) | Standard seawater salinity |

> **Units to remember:** µm (micrometre), m²/L (paint coverage), bar (pressure), °C (temperature), m/s (blasting nozzle velocity).
