```mermaid
    T --> T1[Flame Cleaning]
    T --> T2[Induction Heating]

    style P fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style M fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ef6c00
    style C fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e
    style T fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Mechanism of Rust Conversion (Phosphoric Acid Route)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1 Phosphoric Acid Penetrates Rust Layer] --> S2[Stage 2 Acid Dissolves Fe2O3 Producing Fe3 Ions]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Fe3 Reacts with Phosphate to Form FePO4]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 FePO4 Film Dries to Hard Black Layer]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 Compatible Primer Applied Over Film]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6 Completed Protective Coating System]

    style S1 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style S3 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style S4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style S6 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Pre-Treatment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the substrate?} -->|Carbon Steel| Q2{Rust severity?}
    Q1 -->|Aluminium| AL[Use Chromating or Anodizing]
    Q1 -->|Stainless Steel| SS[Use Passivation with HNO3]

    Q2 -->|Light rust < 50 microns| RC[Apply Rust Converter]
    Q2 -->|Medium rust 50 to 200 microns| PB[Mechanical Cleaning St 3 then Rust Converter]
    Q2 -->|Heavy rust > 200 microns| AB[Abrasive Blast to Sa 2.5 then Phosphating]

    RC --> PT[Paint Compatible Primer]
    PB --> PT
    AB --> PT

    PT --> DONE[Coating System Complete]

    style Q1 fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style Q2 fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style RC fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style AB fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style DONE fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1 — 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** Define a *rust converter*. Mention any two types with their chemical basis.

**Model Answer:**
A rust converter is a chemical formulation that reacts with iron oxides (rust) on a steel surface and converts them into a stable, paint-compatible layer without mechanical removal. **Two types:** (1) **Tannic-acid based** — reacts with Fe₂O₃ to form iron tannate, a dark blue-black chelate complex; (2) **Phosphoric-acid based** — reacts with rust to form iron phosphate (FePO₄), a hard inert film.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2 — 3 Marks]**
> **Q:** List the major stages in a chemical pre-treatment sequence for shipbuilding steel plates.

**Model Answer:**
The major stages are: (1) Degreasing, (2) Water rinsing, (3) Acid pickling, (4) Water rinsing, (5) Surface conditioning, (6) Phosphating, (7) Water rinsing, (8) Passivation, (9) Drying, (10) Primer application.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Module 3, Model Paper Pattern — 14 Marks]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the mechanism of rust conversion using phosphoric acid. Write the balanced chemical equations and describe the nature of the protective film formed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare phosphating, chromating and anodizing as chemical pre-treatment methods. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mechanism of phosphoric-acid rust conversion:**

1. Phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄) penetrates the porous rust layer.
2. It reacts with the iron oxides:

$$
\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\,\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{FePO}_4 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

3. The acid also attacks the underlying steel lightly:

$$
\text{Fe} + 2\,\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{Fe}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2 \uparrow
$$

4. The resulting film contains both FePO₄ (top) and Fe₃(PO₄)₂ (bottom). The film is **black to dark grey, adherent, and slightly porous**.
5. The pores are sealed by a topcoat/primer. The film provides corrosion resistance by acting as a **physical barrier** and by **electrochemically stabilising** the surface.

**(b) Comparison of Phosphating, Chromating and Anodizing:**

| Parameter | Phosphating | Chromating | Anodizing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Substrate | Steel, Zn, Al | Al, Zn, Mg | Aluminium alloys |
| Film type | Crystalline phosphate | Amorphous Cr-oxide | Porous Al₂O₃ |
| Coating weight | 1–30 g/m² | 0.5–2 g/m² | 100–1000 mg/ft² |
| Bath type | Acid (pH 2.5–3.5) | Acid (pH 1–3) | Acid electrolyte (H₂SO₄) |
| Corrosion resistance | Good | Excellent | Very good |
| Environmental concern | Moderate | High (Cr⁶⁺ toxic) | Low |
| Marine use | Hull, ballast tanks | Al superstructure | Al hulls, deck fittings |

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the various chemical pre-treatment methods employed in shipbuilding, highlighting their advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 100 µm thick rust layer (density 4.3 g/cm³, composition Fe₂O₃·H₂O) is to be converted using phosphoric acid. Calculate the mass of FePO₄ formed per square metre. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Chemical pre-treatment methods:**

1. **Alkaline cleaning** — NaOH + Na₂CO₃ + surfactants at 60–80 °C; cheap, but ineffective on rust.
2. **Acid pickling** — Removes mill scale and rust; rapid, but causes H-embrittlement and acid fumes.
3. **Phosphating** — Provides crystalline conversion coating; excellent paint adhesion; moderate corrosion resistance.
4. **Chromating** — Excellent adhesion promoter on Al/Zn; Cr⁶⁺ carcinogenic → being replaced by trivalent chrome.
5. **Anodizing** — For aluminium only; very hard, wear-resistant Al₂O₃ film; expensive.
6. **Passivation** — For stainless steel using HNO₃ or citric acid; restores Cr-rich oxide layer.

**Limitations:** Chemical baths require waste-water treatment; chromating is environmentally restricted; pickling requires inhibitor addition to limit base-metal attack.

**(b) Numerical solution:**

Given: $t = 100\,\mu\text{m} = 100 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{cm}$, $\rho_r = 4.3\,\text{g/cm}^3$, $A = 1\,\text{m}^2 = 10^4\,\text{cm}^2$.

$$
m_{\text{rust}} = \rho_r \cdot t \cdot A = 4.3 \times 100\times 10^{-4} \times 10^4 = 430\,\text{g}
$$

$$
n_{\text{rust}} = \dfrac{430}{161.7} = 2.660\,\text{mol}
$$

Reaction: 1 mol rust → 2 mol FePO₄.

$$
n_{\text{FePO}_4} = 2 \times 2.660 = 5.320\,\text{mol}
$$

$$
m_{\text{FePO}_4} = 5.320 \times 150.82 = 802.4\,\text{g/m}^2
$$

**Result: ≈ 802 g/m² of FePO₄** is formed per square metre of surface.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Common mistake:** Students write only the chemical formula of the conversion product but **omit the balanced equation** — KTU examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for unbalanced / missing equations.
> 2. **Numerical errors:** Confusing the molar mass of Fe₂O₃ (159.7 g/mol) with that of Fe₂O₃·H₂O (161.7 g/mol). Always check the hydrated form.
> 3. **Comparison answers:** Tabular presentation earns **full marks**; mere paragraph answers often lose 2–3 marks for lack of clarity.
> 4. **Industrial relevance:** Examiners reward answers that cite real specifications — **ISO 8501, NACE, SSPC, PSPC-MSC.215(82)**. Always mention standards wherever possible.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Rust converter ≠ Rust remover.** Converter transforms rust; remover dissolves it.
- Tannic acid-based converters produce **iron tannate**; phosphoric-based produce **iron phosphate**; both are stable, paint-compatible films.
- Rust converters are limited to **rust thickness < 100 µm**; thicker layers require mechanical removal first.
- **Phosphating** is the most widely used conversion coating in marine applications (zinc phosphate, 1–3 g/m²).
- **Chromating** uses Cr⁶⁺ — being phased out due to carcinogenicity; replaced by Cr³⁺ or non-chrome sealants.
- **Pickling** liberates large volumes of H₂ → **explosion hazard in confined spaces**.
- **Standard coating weight** for zinc phosphate: **1.0 – 3.0 g/m²**; for iron phosphate: **0.3 – 1.0 g/m²**.
- **Surface cleanliness** must meet **Sa 2½** (ISO 8501-1) before primer application in ship hulls.
- **Salt contamination** on prepared surface must be **≤ 50 mg/m²** (ISO 8502-6).
- **PSPC** for ballast tanks requires **DFT ≥ 320 µm** for epoxy systems.
- Always cite the relevant **ISO / NACE / SSPC** standard in answers — it boosts marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paint schemes in ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Paint schemes in ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **ship paint scheme** is a systematically engineered, multi-layered coating system designed and applied to different structural zones of a vessel, providing a synergistic combination of **corrosion protection**, **anti-fouling performance**, **mechanical durability**, **chemical resistance**, and **aesthetic finish**, in strict compliance with classification society rules (e.g., Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, IRS) and the **IMO Performance Standard for Protective Coatings (PSPC – MSC.215(82) & MSC.244(83))**.

The scheme is *not* a single paint, but a **layered coating system**, where each coat (primer → intermediate → finish/anti-fouling) performs a distinct engineering function, and the total **Dry Film Thickness (DFT)** is engineered to be the sum of all layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):** *A paint scheme is a pre-engineered, multi-coat system specifying the type, number, sequence, thickness, and area of application of paints on various zones of a ship, designed to combat the specific degradation mechanism (corrosion, fouling, abrasion, UV, heat) of that zone.*

## 1.2 Zones of a Ship (The "Where")

A ship is divided into **9 functionally distinct coating zones**, each facing a unique combination of environmental stressors:

| Zone | Primary Threat | Service Environment |
|---|---|---|
| **Underwater Hull (Bottom)** | Biofouling + galvanic/seawater corrosion | Fully submerged |
| **Boot-top / Waterline** | Alternate wet/dry, impact, biofouling | Tidal/splash zone |
| **Topside** | UV, salt spray, weather | Atmospheric above WL |
| **Superstructure** | UV, salt spray | Atmospheric, exterior |
| **Main Deck** | Abrasion, foot traffic, spillage | High mechanical wear |
| **Ballast Tanks** | Seawater corrosion, crude oil | Immersed, alternating |
| **Cargo Holds** | Mechanical impact, cargo abrasion | Variable |
| **Engine Room** | Heat, oil, chemicals | High temp / chemical |
| **Accommodation** | Aesthetic, hygiene | Internal, mild |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why zones?** A single paint cannot simultaneously resist barnacle adhesion, deck abrasion, and engine-room heat (~200 °C). Therefore, **paint formulation is zone-specific** – this is the fundamental design philosophy of marine coating engineering.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Spacesuit for the Ship":** Think of a ship sailing in the ocean like an astronaut walking in space. The ocean is a hostile environment filled with "invisible bullets" — chloride ions, marine organisms, UV rays, waves, and impact. Just as a spacesuit is built in layers (inner cooling layer → pressure bladder → micrometeoroid armor → outer thermal blanket), a ship's paint is also a **layered system**. Each layer has a job: the primer grips the steel like glue, the build coat adds muscle (thickness), and the finish/top coat is the visible armor (UV/abrasion). For the underwater hull, an extra "anti-fouling cloak" is added so barnacles cannot "hitchhike." Damage in one layer is contained; this is the *synergistic* beauty of a paint scheme.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Glossary)

- **WFT (Wet Film Thickness):** Thickness of paint immediately after application.
- **DFT (Dry Film Thickness):** Thickness after solvent evaporation — the *engineered* parameter.
- **SHOP PRIME / PRE-CONSTRUCTION PRIMER:** Thin (~15–25 µm) holding primer applied at the steel stockist/fabrication yard.
- **STAGES of Painting:** Stage 1 (Primer) → Stage 2 (Build coats) → Stage 3 (Finish/AF coat).
- **PSPC:** IMO Performance Standard for Protective Coatings — mandatory for new vessels since 2008.
- **TBT (Tributyltin):** Once-dominant biocide, banned by IMO since **1 January 2008** (AFS Convention).
- **Tie Coat / Seal Coat:** A transitional coat between two chemically incompatible systems (e.g., between anti-corrosive epoxy and silicone anti-fouling).
- **Stripe Coat:** Brush-applied extra coat on edges, welds, and corners where spray coverage is thin.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind the Layered Architecture

A marine paint scheme follows a **functional stack principle**. Each layer solves a specific problem, and the system is only as strong as its weakest link. The **three universal functions** are:

1. **Barrier Protection** — physically isolate steel from O₂, H₂O, Cl⁻.
2. **Electrochemical (Galvanic) Protection** — sacrificial action of zinc-rich primers.
3. **Active Inhibition / Fouling Release** — biocide release or low surface energy.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Generic Marine Paint System

A complete ship paint scheme is a **four-tier architecture**:

| Tier | Coating | Function | Typical Chemistry | DFT (µm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **T1 — Pre-construction Primer** | Shop/Works primer | Temporary corrosion protection during fabrication | Zinc silicate / Epoxy | 15 – 25 |
| **T2 — Anti-Corrosive Primer** | Primer | Adhesion + barrier + galvanic (if Zn-rich) | Zinc-rich epoxy, pure epoxy, zinc silicate | 50 – 100 |
| **T3 — Build / Intermediate Coat** | Tie/build coat | DFT build-up, barrier, sealing | Epoxy, epoxy-mastic | 100 – 200 |
| **T4 — Finish / Anti-Fouling** | Top/AF coat | UV resistance, color, fouling control | Polyurethane (PU) / Copper / Silicone AF | 50 – 150 |

**Total scheme DFT** for topside may reach **300 – 500 µm**; underwater hull (with anti-fouling) is typically **350 – 600 µm**.

## 2.3 Zone-Wise Paint Scheme Logic

### A. UNDERWATER HULL (Bottom Plate)

This is the **most aggressive zone** — continuous seawater exposure + biofouling. The scheme is:

$$
\text{Steel} \rightarrow \text{Zinc Silicate Shop Primer} \rightarrow \text{Zinc-Rich Epoxy Primer} \rightarrow \text{Epoxy Intermediate} \rightarrow \text{Tie Coat} \rightarrow \text{Anti-Fouling (AF)}
$$

- **Anti-fouling types (post-2008 AFS):**
  - **Self-Polishing Copolymer (SPC-AF):** Copper-acrylate or zinc-acrylate binder; hydrolyses slowly → constant biocide release.
  - **Foul-Release (FR):** Silicone elastomer; no biocide; fouling sloughs off by hull motion.
  - **Biocide-free hybrid (e.g., silyl-acrylate).**

### B. BOOT-TOP (Waterline)

Receives alternating wet/dry + sunlight + mechanical impact (wharf contact). Scheme:

$$
\text{Zinc-rich primer} \rightarrow \text{Epoxy build} \rightarrow \text{PU finish} \rightarrow \text{AF coat (overlapping bottom)}
$$

### C. TOPSIDE & SUPERSTRUCTURE

Atmospheric corrosion + UV + aesthetic demand.

$$
\text{Epoxy primer} \rightarrow \text{Epoxy intermediate (optional)} \rightarrow \text{Aliphatic PU finish}
$$

**Aliphatic PU is preferred over aromatic PU** because aromatic PU yellows under UV.

### D. MAIN DECK

Abrasion-dominant → uses **non-skid epoxy / PU finish** with silica or aluminium oxide aggregate broadcast into the wet coat.

### E. BALLAST TANKS (PSPC Critical Zone)

Governed by **IMO Resolution MSC.215(82)**. Mandatory scheme:

$$
\text{Epoxy shop primer} \rightarrow \text{Epoxy Anti-Corrosive (EAC)} \rightarrow \text{Epoxy Anti-Corrosive (2nd coat)}
$$

- **Target DFT: 320 µm** (nominal), with 90/10 rule (90 % of measurements ≥ nominal, none below 80 %).
- **Epoxy coal-tar / pure epoxy** systems dominate.
- For **crude oil / chemical tankers**, additional **phenolic epoxy / zinc-rich** schemes may be used.

### F. CARGO HOLDS

Coating system: typically **epoxy** (light-coloured for visibility) or **zinc silicate + epoxy**, depending on cargo type (coal, grain, ore).

### G. ENGINE ROOM

- Floors/decks: **epoxy non-skid**.
- Bulkheads: **epoxy / PU**.
- Near boilers/exhausts: **silicone aluminium / heat-resistant epoxy** (up to 400 – 600 °C).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Engineering Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $DFT = WFT \times V_s$ | Convert wet film to dry film using volume solids $V_s$ (decimal) | µm |
| 2 | $\text{Theoretical Coverage} = \dfrac{10 \times V_s\%}{DFT(\mu m)}$ | Spreading rate of a paint | $\text{m}^2 \setminus \text{L}$ |
| 3 | $\text{Practical Coverage} = \text{Theoretical} \times \text{Transfer Efficiency}$ | Account for spray losses (≈ 50 – 70 % for airless spray) | $\text{m}^2 \setminus \text{L}$ |
| 4 | $\text{Total DFT} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} DFT_i$ | Sum of individual layer thicknesses | µm |
| 5 | $\text{Corrosion Rate} = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | Weight-loss corrosion rate (ASTM G1); $W$=mass loss (mg), $D$=density (g/cm³), $A$=area (cm²), $T$=time (h) | mm/yr |
| 6 | $\text{Volume Solids} = \dfrac{V_{\text{dry}}}{V_{\text{wet}}} \times 100\%$ | Determines real coverage | % |
| 7 | $\text{Number of coats} = \left\lceil \dfrac{DFT_{\text{target}}}{DFT_{\text{per coat}}} \right\rceil$ | Rounding-up rule for engineering estimation | — |
| 8 | $\text{Cathodic Protection Demand} \propto \text{Uncoated Area}$ | Coatings reduce CP current demand by 80 – 95 % | A/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The single most-tested relation is **DFT = WFT × Vₛ**. Memorize it as **"DFT equals wet film times solids fraction"** — it appears in nearly every estimation problem.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice, ship paint schemes are **first-cost vs. life-cycle cost** decisions. A cheap, low-DFT scheme may need re-blasting every 2.5 years (dry-dock cycle), while a premium PSPC-compliant epoxy scheme in ballast tanks can last **15 – 25 years**. The **hull efficiency** (smooth AF = lower friction) alone can save a tanker **3 – 6 %** in bunker fuel, repaying the paint cost in 12 – 18 months. This is why **sophisticated paint schemes are not an expense — they are an engineering investment**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion of WFT to DFT

**Engineering Question:** A coat of epoxy is applied at a WFT of 200 µm. The technical data sheet specifies a **Volume Solids of 60 %**. What is the resulting DFT after full curing?

**Given:**
- $WFT = 200\ \mu m$
- $V_s = 60\% = 0.60$

**Step 1 — Understand the physical process.**
When wet paint is applied, it contains binder + pigments + **solvents**. During curing, the solvents evaporate. The remaining *dry film* is therefore a fraction of the wet film. That fraction is exactly the **Volume Solids (Vₛ)**.

**Step 2 — Write the governing relationship.**

$$
DFT = WFT \times V_s
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$
DFT = 200\ \mu m \times 0.60
$$

**Step 4 — Compute.**

$$
\boxed{DFT = 120\ \mu m}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Only **120 µm out of 200 µm** of paint *remains* on the hull. The other 80 µm of solvent evaporates — this is why paint *volatilizes* and smells, and why VOC regulations exist.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Spreading (Coverage) Rate

**Engineering Question:** A zinc-rich epoxy primer has 75 % volume solids. The specification requires a DFT of 100 µm. Determine the **theoretical coverage** in $\text{m}^2/\text{L}$.

**Given:**
- $V_s = 75\%$
- $DFT = 100\ \mu m$

**Step 1 — Concept.**
One litre of paint, once cured at 100 µm, covers a known area. That area is the *theoretical coverage* (TC).

**Step 2 — Unit setup.**
1 L = $1000\ \text{cm}^3 = 1000 \times 10^4\ \mu m^3 = 10^9\ \mu m^3$ (since $1\ \mu m = 10^{-4}\ \text{cm}$, so $1\ \text{cm} = 10^4\ \mu m$; cross-multiplying, $1\ \text{cm}^3 = 10^{12}\ \mu m^3$).

Wait — let us re-derive cleanly.

**Step 3 — Direct derivation from 1 L volume.**

Volume of paint = 1 L = $10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3 = 10^{9}\ \text{mm}^3 = 10^{9} \times 10^{6}\ \mu m^3$.

Hmm, let us use the more standard textbook route.

**Step 4 — Textbook formula (ISO 8502 / Paint Manual).**

$$
\text{Theoretical Coverage (TC)} = \frac{10 \times V_s\%}{DFT(\mu m)}\ \text{m}^2/\text{L}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive this formula.**
We know: 1 L of paint, when dried, has dry-volume = $V_s$ L. A dry film of thickness $DFT$ over area $A$ has dry-volume = $A \times DFT$.

Equate:

$$
A \times DFT \times 10^{-6} = V_s \times 10^{-3}
$$

(The $10^{-6}$ converts µm to m; the $10^{-3}$ converts L to m³.)

**Step 6 — Solve for A.**

$$
A = \frac{V_s \times 10^{-3}}{DFT \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{10^{3} \times V_s}{10^{6} \times DFT} = \frac{10 \times V_s}{DFT}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by 100 to express $V_s$ as %:

$$
\boxed{A = \frac{10 \times V_s\%}{DFT(\mu m)}\ \text{m}^2/\text{L}}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute values.**

$$
A = \frac{10 \times 75}{100} = \frac{750}{100} = \boxed{7.5\ \text{m}^2/\text{L}}
$$

**Step 8 — Practical coverage.**
Airless spray transfer efficiency ≈ 65 %. So:

$$
\text{Practical Coverage} = 7.5 \times 0.65 = \boxed{4.875\ \text{m}^2/\text{L}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Number of Coats Required

**Engineering Question:** A ballast tank scheme requires a **total DFT of 320 µm** (per PSPC). If each coat of epoxy yields 125 µm DFT, how many coats are required, and what is the *actual* total DFT?

**Step 1 — Rule.**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{DFT_{\text{target}}}{DFT_{\text{per coat}}} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{320}{125} \right\rceil = \lceil 2.56 \rceil = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Actual total DFT.**

$$
DFT_{\text{actual}} = 3 \times 125 = \boxed{375\ \mu m}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering comment.**
The extra 55 µm above the spec is called *over-spec thickness* and is **acceptable** (over-thickness is safer than under-thickness). However, excessive over-thickness (> 1.5 × spec) can cause solvent entrapment, sagging, and cracking.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Paint Scheme Selection by Zone

| Zone | Shop Primer | Primer | Intermediate | Finish / Special | Total DFT (µm) | Key Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Underwater Hull | Zinc silicate | Zn-rich epoxy | Epoxy build | Anti-fouling (SPC/Silyl) | 350 – 600 | IMO AFS, Class rules |
| Boot-top | Zinc silicate | Zn-rich epoxy | Epoxy | PU + AF (overlap) | 300 – 450 | — |
| Topside | Zinc silicate | Epoxy | Epoxy | Aliphatic PU | 250 – 350 | ISO 12944 C5-M |
| Superstructure | Zinc silicate | Epoxy | (Optional) | Aliphatic PU | 200 – 300 | ISO 12944 C5-M |
| Main Deck | Zinc silicate | Epoxy | Epoxy non-skid | PU non-skid finish | 400 – 600 | Class deck rules |
| Ballast Tank (PSPC) | Epoxy (≤25 µm) | Epoxy | Epoxy | — (Epoxy finish) | **320 nominal** | **MSC.215(82)** |
| Cargo Hold | Zinc silicate | Epoxy | Epoxy | Light-colour epoxy | 250 – 400 | Class / Charterer |
| Engine Room | Zinc silicate | Epoxy | Epoxy | Heat-resistant silicone | 200 – 400 | Class machinery |
| Accommodation | Alkyd primer | Alkyd | — | Alkyd / PU emulsion | 100 – 200 | Hygiene spec |

> [!NOTE]
> **PSPC for ballast tanks is the KTU board's most-tested zone.** Always quote: *"320 µm nominal DFT, with 90/10 rule: 90 % of all DFT measurements shall not be less than nominal, and none shall be less than 0.8 × nominal (256 µm)."*

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Volume of Paint to Order

**Problem:** A bulk carrier's underwater hull area to be painted with anti-fouling is **12,000 m²**. The AF paint has $V_s$ = 50 % and required DFT = 150 µm. Airless spray transfer efficiency = 60 %. How many litres of AF paint should be **ordered** (add 10 % wastage)?

**Step 1 — Theoretical coverage.**

$$
TC = \frac{10 \times 50}{150} = 3.333\ \text{m}^2/\text{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Practical coverage.**

$$
PC = 3.333 \times 0.60 = 2.0\ \text{m}^2/\text{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Volume required without wastage.**

$$
V = \frac{12{,}000}{2.0} = 6{,}000\ \text{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Add 10 % wastage.**

$$
V_{\text{order}} = 6{,}000 \times 1.10 = \boxed{6{,}600\ \text{L}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Figure A — Ship Zones & Associated Paint Schemes (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ship Hull] --> B[Above Waterline Zone]
    A --> C[At Waterline Zone]
    A --> D[Below Waterline Zone]
    A --> E[Internal Zone]

    B --> B1[Superstructure]
    B --> B2[Topside]
    B --> B3[Main Deck]
    B --> B4[Accommodation]

    C --> C1[Boot Top]

    D --> D1[Underwater Hull Bottom]

    E --> E1[Ballast Tanks]
    E --> E2[Cargo Holds]
    E --> E3[Engine Room]

    B1 --> P1[Zinc Silicate Shop Primer]
    B1 --> P2[Epoxy Primer]
    B1 --> P3[Aliphatic PU Finish]

    B2 --> Q1[Zinc Silicate Shop Primer]
    B2 --> Q2[Epoxy Primer]
    B2 --> Q3[Epoxy Build Coat]
    B2 --> Q4[Aliphatic PU Finish]

    B3 --> R1[Zinc Silicate Shop Primer]
    B3 --> R2[Epoxy Primer]
    B3 --> R3[Epoxy Non Skid Intermediate]
    B3 --> R4[PU Non Skid Finish]

    C1 --> S1[Zinc Silicate Shop Primer]
    C1 --> S2[Zinc Rich Epoxy Primer]
    C1 --> S3[Epoxy Build Coat]
    C1 --> S4[Aliphatic PU Finish]
    C1 --> S5[Anti Fouling Overlap]

    D1 --> T1[Zinc Silicate Shop Primer]
    D1 --> T2[Zinc Rich Epoxy Primer]
    D1 --> T3[Epoxy Intermediate]
    D1 --> T4[Tie Coat]
    D1 --> T5[Anti Fouling SPC or Foul Release]

    E1 --> U1[Epoxy Shop Primer]
    E1 --> U2[Epoxy Anti Corrosive Coat 1]
    E1 --> U3[Epoxy Anti Corrosive Coat 2]
    E1 --> U4[PSPC Target DFT 320 micrometre]

    E2 --> V1[Zinc Silicate Shop Primer]
    E2 --> V2[Epoxy Primer]
    E2 --> V3[Epoxy Build Coat]

    E3 --> W1[Zinc Silicate Shop Primer]
    W3[Heat Resistant Silicone or Epoxy Finish]
    E3 --> W2[Epoxy Primer]
    E3 --> W3
```

## 4.2 Figure B — Layered Architecture of a Marine Paint Scheme (Stack Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Steel Substrate Sa 2.5 Surface Preparation]
    L1[Tier 1 Zinc Silicate Shop Primer 15 to 25 micrometre]
    L2[Tier 2 Anti Corrosive Primer Epoxy or Zinc Rich 50 to 100 micrometre]
    L3[Tier 3 Intermediate Build Coat Epoxy 100 to 200 micrometre]
    L4[Tier 4 Finish Coat Polyurethane or Anti Fouling 50 to 150 micrometre]
    L5[Marine Environment Seawater UV Fouling Heat]

    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```

## 4.3 Figure C — Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Paint Scheme for a Zone

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Identify Ship Zone] --> Q1{Is zone submerged in seawater?}

    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Fouling risk high?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{UV or weathering dominant?}

    Q2 -- Yes --> AF[Use SPC or Foul Release Anti Fouling Scheme]
    Q2 -- No --> BW[Use Epoxy Ballast Tank PSPC Scheme]

    Q3 -- Yes --> TS[Use Epoxy Primer plus Aliphatic PU Finish]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Mechanical abrasion risk?}

    Q4 -- Yes --> DK[Use Epoxy Primer plus Non Skid PU Finish]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{High temperature above 150 degree C?}

    Q5 -- Yes --> HR[Use Heat Resistant Silicone Aluminium Scheme]
    Q5 -- No --> AC[Use Standard Alkyd or Epoxy Scheme]
```

## 4.4 Figure D — PSPC Ballast Tank Coating Compliance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Surface Preparation ISO 8501 Sa 2.5] --> B[Dust and Salt Test ISO 8502]
    B --> C{Levels below threshold?}
    C -- No --> A
    C -- Yes --> D[Apply Epoxy Primer 50 to 75 micrometre DFT]
    D --> E[Apply Epoxy Intermediate 100 to 150 micrometre DFT]
    E --> F[Apply Epoxy Finish 100 to 150 micrometre DFT]
    F --> G[Measure DFT at 90/10 Rule]
    G --> H{90 percent above 320 micrometre and all above 256?}
    H -- No --> I[Repair and Recoat Defective Areas]
    I --> G
    H -- Yes --> J[PSPC Compliant Coating Accepted]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**"List the major zones of a ship that require distinct paint schemes and state the primary degradation mechanism in each zone." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Zone | Primary Degradation Mechanism |
|---|---|
| 1. Underwater Hull | Biofouling + galvanic/seawater corrosion |
| 2. Boot-top | Alternating wet–dry, impact, biofouling |
| 3. Topside / Superstructure | UV, salt-spray atmospheric corrosion |
| 4. Main Deck | Mechanical abrasion, spillage |
| 5. Ballast Tanks | Seawater corrosion, alternating immersion |
| 6. Cargo Holds | Cargo abrasion, impact |
| 7. Engine Room | Heat, oil, chemical attack |

*For 3 marks: List **any 6** zones with their mechanisms in a tabular/point form.*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**"Differentiate between a Shop Primer and an Anti-corrosive Primer as used in ship paint schemes." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Shop Primer | Anti-corrosive Primer |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Temporary protection during fabrication/storage | Long-term anti-corrosion in service |
| Application | At stockist/fabrication yard | After blasting, before main coats |
| Thickness | 15–25 µm | 50–100 µm |
| Chemistry | Zinc silicate / epoxy | Epoxy (Zn-rich or barrier type) |
| Longevity | 6–12 months | 15–25 years (with proper topcoats) |
| Welding compatibility | Required (low Zn, weldable) | Not critical |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]

**(a)** Explain the **paint scheme recommended for the underwater hull** of a merchant ship. With a neat sketch, list the coatings in sequence, their typical DFTs, and the role of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **types of anti-fouling paints** used for ship hulls, with emphasis on the **TBT ban** and modern environmentally compliant alternatives. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B-A (a):

**1. Introduction (1 mark):** The underwater hull is the most aggressive zone, exposed to continuous seawater, biofouling, and galvanic corrosion. The paint scheme is therefore a *multi-layered system* of primer → build coat → anti-fouling.

**2. Coating Sequence (4 marks):**

$$
\text{Steel (Sa 2.5 blasted)} \rightarrow \text{Zinc Silicate Shop Primer (15–25 µm)}
$$
$$
\rightarrow \text{Zinc-Rich Epoxy Primer (50–100 µm)}
$$
$$
\rightarrow \text{Epoxy Intermediate / Build Coat (100–200 µm)}
$$
$$
\rightarrow \text{Tie Coat (30–50 µm, if chemistry mismatch)}
$$
$$
\rightarrow \text{Anti-Fouling Topcoat (100–150 µm per coat, 2–3 coats)}
$$

**3. Role of each layer (2 marks):**
- **Shop primer:** Weldable, temporary protection during construction.
- **Zinc-rich primer:** Galvanic (sacrificial) protection of steel.
- **Epoxy build coat:** Increases DFT, acts as a barrier to water/chloride.
- **Tie coat:** Ensures chemical compatibility between epoxy and AF.
- **Anti-fouling:** Releases biocides / creates low-energy surface to prevent biofouling.

**4. Total DFT:** Approximately **350 – 600 µm**, depending on vessel type and specification.

---

#### Model Solution to B-A (b):

**1. Need for AF paint (1 mark):** Without AF, a hull can suffer **5–30 %** increase in frictional resistance, raising fuel consumption by 3–6 %.

**2. Traditional AF – TBT Self-Polishing (1 mark):** Tributyltin (TBT) copolymer paints were highly effective (5-year intervals). However, TBT was found to cause **imposex in marine gastropods** and bioaccumulation.

**3. IMO AFS Convention (1 mark):** Adopted in 2001, **banned TBT-based AF from 1 January 2008**. All new applications and, from 2023, even existing residues are controlled.

**4. Modern Alternatives (4 marks):**

| Type | Mechanism | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **SPC-AF (Cu / Zn acrylate)** | Hydrolysis releases Cu²⁺ biocide | TBT-free, polish rate 5–15 µm/month |
| **Silyl-acrylate hybrid** | Hybrid binder, lower leaching | Some IMO-registered biocides |
| **Foul-Release (Silicone elastomer)** | Low surface energy, no biocide | Barnacles slough off; speed-dependent |
| **Biocide-free hydrogel / fluoropolymer** | Ultra-smooth, slippery surface | Experimental, niche use |

**Conclusion:** Modern AF design balances **efficacy, vessel speed profile, dry-dock interval, and IMO environmental compliance** — typically a **silyl-acrylate SPC-AF** with copper oxide and booster biocides is the industry default.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With reference to the **IMO PSPC (MSC.215(82))**, explain the **paint scheme, surface preparation, and DFT acceptance criteria** for **ballast tanks** of new ships. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the paint schemes suitable for the **topside, main deck, and engine room** of a ship. Highlight the role of **aliphatic polyurethane** and **non-skid aggregates**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B-B (a):

**1. Background (1 mark):** PSPC (Performance Standard for Protective Coatings) was adopted by IMO MEPC to harmonize ballast tank coating and prevent **premature corrosion-induced structural failure** (e.g., *MV Prestige*, 2002).

**2. Surface Preparation (1.5 marks):**
- Grade **Sa 2.5** (ISO 8501-1) — near-white metal blast.
- Surface profile **50–100 µm** (roughness).
- Dust ≤ **Rating 2 / Class 2** (ISO 8502-3).
- Soluble salt ≤ **50 mg/m² NaCl** (ISO 8502-6/9).
- Primary surface preparation: **ABRASIVE BLASTING** (preferred) or alternative like ultra-high-pressure water jetting.

**3. Coating Scheme (2 marks):**
- **Epoxy-based system** (EAC – Epoxy Anti-Corrosive).
- Typically 2 or 3 coats of **solvent-free / high-solid epoxy**.
- Total **nominal DFT: 320 µm**.
- No shop primer is *credited* toward DFT (max 25 µm shop primer considered, rest is full epoxy).

**4. Acceptance Criteria — "90/10 Rule" (1.5 marks):**
- 90 % of all DFT measurements must be **≥ 320 µm** (nominal).
- 100 % of measurements must be **≥ 0.8 × 320 = 256 µm**.
- Any DFT below 256 µm → **rework mandatory**.

**5. Inspection & Maintenance (1 mark):** Annual surveys by Class; coating Technical File maintained for the life of the vessel; **Target useful life = 15 years** with adequate maintenance.

---

#### Model Solution to B-B (b):

**1. Topside Scheme (2 marks):**
- **Zinc silicate shop primer (15–25 µm) → Epoxy primer (75–100 µm) → Epoxy build (optional, 100–125 µm) → Aliphatic Polyurethane finish (50–75 µm).**
- Role: Atmospheric corrosion protection + UV resistance + color/gloss retention.
- **Aliphatic PU** is used because it is UV-stable (does not yellow) and chemically resistant; aromatic PU is *not* used externally as it chalks.

**2. Main Deck Scheme (2.5 marks):**
- **Shop primer → Epoxy primer (75–100 µm) → Epoxy non-skid intermediate (with broadcast aggregate) → PU non-skid finish (with silica or aluminium oxide).**
- Aggregate: **silica sand, aluminium oxide, or carborundum** broadcast into the wet coat and sealed.
- Function: **Slip resistance** under wet/oily conditions; crucial for crew safety (SOLAS requirement).

**3. Engine Room Scheme (2.5 marks):**
- Standard: **Epoxy primer + Epoxy finish** (similar to topside) for floors and bulkheads.
- Near boilers/exhaust manifolds: **silicone-aluminium paint** (resistant up to **400 – 600 °C**).
- Resistant to oil, fuel, and chemical spills.

**4. Common Logic:** All three zones use **epoxy primer → PU/epoxy finish** architecture. The *finish coat* is varied: **Aliphatic PU** (topside), **Non-skid PU** (deck), **Heat-resistant silicone** (engine room high-temp zones).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on "Paint Schemes" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "shop primer" with "anti-corrosive primer."** Shop primer is *temporary* (15–25 µm), anti-corrosive is *permanent* (≥ 50 µm). Examiners will deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the TBT ban (2008).** Writing "TBT-based anti-fouling" is now an outdated answer. Always state **"TBT-free / IMO AFS compliant."**
> 3. **Not mentioning PSPC for ballast tanks.** Any answer on ballast tank coating **must cite MSC.215(82)** and the **90/10 rule** to score full marks.
> 4. **Skipping surface preparation.** A paint scheme question is incomplete without mentioning **ISO 8501 Sa 2.5 blasting**. This is worth 1–2 marks.
> 5. **No units on DFT.** Always write **DFT in µm** (e.g., "320 µm nominal"), not just "320". A wrong unit = ½ mark penalty.
> 6. **Confusing WFT and DFT.** The formula $DFT = WFT \times V_s$ is tested directly; students sometimes invert it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **paint scheme** is a *multi-layer coating system* tailored to each ship zone — not a single paint.
- A standard marine paint stack: **Shop Primer → Anti-Corrosive Primer → Build Coat → Finish / Anti-Fouling**.
- **Underwater hull scheme:** Zinc silicate + Zn-rich epoxy + Epoxy + Tie coat + **Anti-fouling (SPC or Foul-Release)**.
- **Topside scheme:** Epoxy primer + Epoxy build + **Aliphatic PU** (UV-stable, non-yellowing).
- **Main deck scheme:** Epoxy + **Non-skid PU** with **silica/aluminium oxide aggregate**.
- **Ballast tanks (PSPC):** Pure **epoxy EAC**, **320 µm nominal DFT**, **Sa 2.5** surface prep, **90/10 rule** for acceptance.
- **Engine room high-temp zones:** **Silicone-aluminium** paints, up to 400–600 °C resistance.
- **Anti-fouling chemistry:** **TBT banned since 2008**; modern = SPC-AF (Cu/Zn acrylate) or Foul-Release (silicone).
- **Key formula:** $\boxed{DFT = WFT \times V_s}$
- **Coverage formula:** $\boxed{TC = \dfrac{10 \times V_s\%}{DFT(\mu m)}\ \text{m}^2/\text{L}}$
- **Surface preparation standard:** **ISO 8501 Sa 2.5** (near-white metal).
- **PSPC target useful life:** **15 years** for ballast tank coatings.
- **Practical engineering tip:** Over-thickness is *safer* than under-thickness, but beyond 1.5 × spec → risk of solvent entrapment and cracking.
- **Always specify units:** DFT in µm, coverage in m²/L, temperature in °C.
- **Mandatory citations** in exam answers: **ISO 12944, ISO 8501, IMO MSC.215(82), IMO AFS Convention 2001**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of paints - common paint vehicles, drying oils, oleo-resins, alkyd resins, polymerizing chemicals and bitumen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification of paints - common paint vehicles, drying oils, oleo-resins, alkyd resins, polymerizing chemicals and bitumen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> A *paint vehicle* (also called the **binder** or **medium**) is the film-forming non-volatile portion of a paint in which the pigment is dispersed. It is the "glue" of the paint system, responsible for adhesion to the substrate, cohesion of the film, and providing the chemical/physical properties (gloss, flexibility, water resistance, durability) that protect the underlying surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3.1):**
> Classification of paints is **NOT** a single-axis taxonomy. In shipbuilding practice, paints are classified by **(a) Function** (primers, undercoats, finish coats, anti-fouling), **(b) Chemistry of the Vehicle/Binder** (oils, oleo-resins, alkyds, vinyls, epoxies, bituminous), and **(c) Drying Mechanism** (oxidative, evaporative, chemically-reactive/2-pack, thermoplastic). The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the **chemistry of the vehicle** because that is what determines the paint's behavior in a marine environment.

---

## 1.1 The Intuitive "Cement Analogy" for a Paint Film

Think of marine paint like the **mortar between bricks on a seawall**:

| Paint Component | Seawall Analogy | Real Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Pigment** | The sand and stones | Provides colour, opacity, UV blocking, anti-corrosive action (e.g., red lead) |
| **Vehicle / Binder** | The cement (Portlandite) | Holds the pigment together AND sticks it to the steel hull |
| **Solvent / Thinner** | The water in wet cement | Allows the mix to flow & be sprayed; evaporates on drying |
| **Additives** | Plasticizers & waterproofers | Improve brushability, flow, anti-skinning, anti-foam |

When the **vehicle** dries, it transforms from a liquid "wet cement" into a tough, continuous **polymer film** that locks the pigment particles into a protective skin over the steel — this is the **paint film**.

---

## 1.2 What is a "Paint Vehicle"?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** The **vehicle** is the total liquid portion of the paint = *Binder (non-volatile film-former)* + *Solvent/Thinner (volatile carrier)*. In KTU parlance, when the question asks for "common paint vehicles," it usually refers specifically to the **non-volatile binders** (oils, resins, bitumen) that remain in the dried film.

### 1.2.1 Functional Requirements of a Marine Vehicle

A shipboard vehicle must satisfy severe demands that a domestic paint vehicle never faces:

1. **Salt-water resistance** — must not blister, soften, or hydrolyze in brine.
2. **Flexibility** — the hull flexes with sea waves; a brittle film cracks and admits corrosion.
3. **Adhesion to prepared steel** — typically **Sa 2½** near-white blast surface profile.
4. **Weathering/UV resistance** — for topside, boottop, and superstructure exposure.
5. **Chemical resistance** — to bilge water, fuel oil, hydraulic fluid, cargo (especially chemicals/tankers).
6. **Drying characteristics** — short re-coat times on a shipyard production line.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Marine Paints (Master Framework)

For the KTU exam, you should be able to draw/recall a **three-tier classification**:

```text
MARINE PAINTS
│
├── (I) By Function
│   ├── Anti-corrosive Primers (e.g., red lead, zinc chromate, zinc-rich epoxy)
│   ├── Tie-coats / Undercoats
│   ├── Finishes / Topcoats
│   ├── Anti-fouling (biocide-releasing)
│   └── Special coatings (cargo hold, deck, boot-top, water-ballast tank)
│
├── (II) By Vehicle / Binder Chemistry   <-- FOCUS OF THIS MODULE
│   ├── Oil-based (drying oils + oleo-resins)
│   ├── Alkyd-resin based
│   ├── Bituminous (asphalt, coal-tar pitch)
│   ├── Vinyl / Chlorinated rubber
│   ├── Epoxy (2-pack, amine-cured)
│   ├── Polyurethane (2-pack, isocyanate + polyol)
│   └── Silicone / Zinc-silicate
│
└── (III) By Drying Mechanism
    ├── Oxidative drying (oils, alkyds) — dry by O₂ uptake
    ├── Evaporative drying (vinyls, chlorinated rubber)
    ├── Co-reactive / 2-pack (epoxy, polyurethane)
    └── Thermoplastic melt (bitumen hot-applied)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The remainder of this module focuses on **Tier II** — the classical **oil/oleo-resin/alkyd/bitumen** family — which historically dominated shipbuilding and is still tested exhaustively in KTU papers because it is the **chemical foundation** of all modern synthetic binders.

---

## 1.4 Why this Topic Matters in Shipbuilding

The hull of a VLCC has roughly **30,000–40,000 m²** of painted surface. The choice of vehicle determines:

- **Dry-dock interval** (alkyd finishes → 12–18 months; epoxy → 5+ years).
- **Cargo compatibility** (bitumen/epoxy-phenolic for crude oil tanks).
- **Application conditions** (alkyds cure in cold/damp; epoxies need >10 °C and dry air).
- **Repair philosophy** — oil/alkyd systems remain "field-friendly" for in-service touch-up.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Mental Hook:** *Oil → Oleo-resin → Alkyd → Polymerising Chemical → Bitumen* traces the **evolution** of marine paint vehicles from 19th century (raw linseed oil) → early 20th (oleo-resinous varnishes) → mid-20th (alkyds) → post-WWII (reactive polymers) and the parallel use of naturally-occurring **bitumen** for water-resistance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

This section breaks each vehicle type into **what it is, how it dries, what its properties are, and where it is used onboard.**

---

## 2.1 Drying Oils — The Original Marine Vehicle

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A *drying oil* is a triglyceride (ester of glycerol + fatty acids) that, when exposed to atmospheric oxygen, undergoes **oxidative polymerisation** to form a tough, dry, continuous film. The unsaturation (C=C double bonds) in the fatty acid chains is the active site that absorbs O₂.

### 2.1.1 Composition of a Drying Oil (Chemistry)

A drying oil is a mixture of **triglycerides**:

$$\text{Glycerol} \;+\; 3 \,\text{Fatty Acid} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Triglyceride} \;+\; 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The polymerisation is initiated at the **conjugated C=C double bonds** of the unsaturated fatty acids (linoleic, linolenic, eleostearic).

### 2.1.2 Classification of Drying Oils by Iodine Number

The **iodine number (I.V.)** measures the degree of unsaturation and is the single most important KTU data point:

| Drying Oil | Iodine Number (g I₂/100 g) | Drying Speed | KTU Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linseed oil** (flax) | **170 – 190** | Fast | Most common marine oil; flexible film |
| **Tung oil** (China wood) | **160 – 175** | Very fast | Waterproof, hard, pale — premium spar varnish |
| **Perilla oil** | **185 – 205** | Very fast | Higher I.V. than linseed, yellows less |
| **Soybean oil** | **125 – 140** | Slow | Non-yellowing, semi-drying — used in alkyds |
| **Dehydrated castor oil (DCO)** | **135 – 145** | Medium | Made by removing -OH from castor oil |
| **Oiticica oil** | **140 – 150** | Fast | Contains licanic acid, hard film |
| **Coconut oil** | **8 – 10** | **Non-drying** | Used in plasticizers, not film-former |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Rule of Thumb:** Iodine Number > 150 = *drying oil*; 100–150 = *semi-drying*; < 100 = *non-drying*. Linseed and tung are the two you must memorise for KTU 14-mark questions.

### 2.1.3 The Drying Mechanism (Oxidative Polymerisation)

The drying of a drying oil is **not evaporation** — it is a chemical reaction with O₂.

$$\text{–CH=CH–CH₂–CH=CH–} \;+\; \text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{–CH=CH–CH(OOH)–CH=CH–}$$

This forms a **hydroperoxide (–OOH)** which then cross-links with a neighbouring chain's double bond, building molecular weight until a solid film forms. **Driers** (cobalt, lead, manganese naphthenates) catalyse this reaction.

### 2.1.4 Marine Use of Drying Oils Today

- **Pure linseed oil** is now rarely used as a single-component vehicle.
- It is used as a **plasticiser** in oleo-resinous paints, or as a **flexibility-imparting component** in alkyd formulations.
- **Raw linseed oil** (not boiled) is used as a **shop/rust-penetrating primer** on freshly blasted steel in some yards.
- **Boiled linseed oil** + driers = the basis of traditional *spar varnish* for wooden masts/boats.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *boiled* vs *raw* linseed oil. Boiled does NOT mean thermally boiled; it is linseed oil dosed with **driers (metal naphthenates like cobalt octoate)** that "boil" the oil into fast surface drying. Raw oil would take weeks to dry.

---

## 2.2 Oleo-Resinous Vehicles

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An *oleo-resin* is a **physical blend** (a hot "cooking" cook at 250–300 °C) of a drying oil with a **natural or synthetic resin**. The resin imparts hardness, gloss and faster drying, while the oil provides flexibility and weather resistance.

### 2.2.1 Typical Composition

A classical marine oleo-resinous varnish:

$$\text{Oleo-Resinous Vehicle} \;=\; \underbrace{40\text{–}60\%}_{\text{Linseed/Tung oil}} \;+\; \underbrace{30\text{–}50\%}_{\text{Resin}} \;+\; \underbrace{5\text{–}10\%}_{\text{Driers + additives}} \;+\; \text{Solvent}$$

### 2.2.2 Resins Used (Historical & Current)

| Resin Class | Source | Film Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural rosin** (colophony) | Pine tree exudate | Hard, brittle, low cost — but poor water resistance |
| **Congo copal** | Fossil tree resin | Hard, glossy, but dark |
| **Dammar** | Dipterocarp trees | Pale, non-yellowing, used in pale varnishes |
| **Ester gum** | Rosin + glycerol → esterified | Rosin's brittleness overcome; cheap |
| **Phenolic resins** (pure synthetic) | Phenol + formaldehyde | Excellent water & alkali resistance; basis of *spar varnish* |
| **Modified phenolic** | Rosin-modified phenol-formaldehyde | Standard marine spar varnish resin |

### 2.2.3 The "Cooking" Process (Oil-Length Concept)

The **oil length** is the number of gallons of drying oil combined with 100 lb (≈ 45 kg) of resin:

| Oil Length | Oil : Resin Ratio | Film Character | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Short oil** (< 25 gal) | ~1 : 2 | Hard, brittle, fast dry | Industrial enamels |
| **Medium oil** (25–50 gal) | ~1 : 1 | Balanced | **Marine spar varnish** |
| **Long oil** (> 50 gal) | ~2 : 1 | Flexible, slow dry, weather-resistant | Exterior house paints |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classic Question:** "Differentiate short-oil, medium-oil and long-oil oleo-resinous varnishes." — Always tie the answer to *film flexibility, drying time and outdoor durability* — short → indoor, long → marine spar varnish.

### 2.2.4 Marine Use

- **Spar varnish** for wood masts, decking, boottop timber, ship models.
- Historically, the **standard topside finish** before alkyds took over (~1930s).
- Today, retained for **wooden vessels, interior joinery, traditional ship restoration**.

---

## 2.3 Alkyd Resins

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An *alkyd* is a **synthetic polyester resin** made by reacting a **polybasic acid** (phthalic anhydride), a **polyhydric alcohol** (glycerol / pentaerythritol) and a **fatty acid** (derived from a drying oil). It is essentially a "synthetic oleo-resin" of controlled, reproducible structure.

### 2.3.1 The Alkyd Reaction (Condensation Polymerisation)

$$\text{Phthalic Anhydride} \;+\; \text{Glycerol} \;+\; \text{Fatty Acid (from oil)} \;\xrightarrow{\,\text{heat, }-\text{H}_2\text{O}\,}\; \text{Alkyd Resin} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The water is removed azeotropically with xylene. The product is a **branched polyester** with pendant fatty-acid chains that provide the drying (oxidative) sites.

### 2.3.2 Classification of Alkyds

**By Oil Length (most important for KTU):**

| Type | Oil Length (% oil) | Properties | Marine Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Short-oil alkyd** | 30 – 45 % | Fast dry, hard, poor weather | Stoving finishes, not marine |
| **Medium-oil alkyd** | 45 – 60 % | Balanced | **General marine topside** |
| **Long-oil alkyd** | 60 – 80 % | Flexible, slow dry, good gloss retention | **Marine exterior finishes, boottop** |
| **Very-long oil** | > 80 % | Very flexible | House paints |

**By Modifying Resin:**

| Modified Alkyd | Modifier | Key Property Gained |
|---|---|---|
| **Silicone-alkyd** | Silicone intermediate | Extreme UV & heat resistance (upto 250 °C) |
| **Vinyl-alkyd** | Vinyl toluene | Faster dry, tougher |
| **Urethane-alkyd** | Isocyanate | Single-pack "oil-modified urethane" — wood varnish |
| **Epoxy-alkyd** | Epoxy resin | Better chemical resistance |
| **Phenolic-alkyd** | Phenol-formaldehyde | Water & chemical resistance |

### 2.3.3 Drying of Alkyds

Alkyds with **drying-oil fatty acids** (linseed, tung, dehydrated castor) dry by the **same oxidative mechanism** as the parent oil. Cobalt + lead driers are used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-drying oil alkyds** (e.g., coconut-oil alkyd) cannot oxidise — they are used as **plasticisers** in nitrocellulose lacquers and baking finishes, not as marine finishes.

### 2.3.4 Marine Significance of Alkyds

- The **workhorse of marine finishing paints from ~1940 to ~1980**.
- Typical scheme: **Red lead / zinc chromate alkyd primer → alkyd undercoat → alkyd gloss finish**.
- Advantages: cheap, easy to apply by brush/spray, good gloss, "breathes" (permeable to water vapour), tolerant of poor surface preparation.
- Disadvantages: long oil-length means **slow re-coat** (12–24 h), poor resistance to strong solvents and chemicals, **chalking in tropical sun**.
- Today, largely replaced on newbuilds by **epoxy + polyurethane** schemes, but **still dominant in in-service touch-up and on smaller commercial / fishing vessels**.

---

## 2.4 Polymerising Chemicals (The Reactive / 2-Pack Binders)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** "Polymerising chemicals" in the KTU 2024 syllabus refers to **synthetic monomers, prepolymers or oligomers that are converted to a high-molecular-weight polymer film by chemical reaction at the time of application** — i.e., *reactive coatings*. They are the workhorses of modern marine paint.

### 2.4.1 The Big Three Marine Reactive Systems

| System | Resin (A-side) | Hardener (B-side) | Cure Type | Marine Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Epoxy** (most important) | Bisphenol-A/F epoxy resin | Polyamide / polyamine / amine-adduct | Polyaddition | Hull primer, ballast tanks, cargo holds |
| **Polyurethane** | Polyol (acrylic/polyester) + hydroxyl | Aliphatic/aromatic isocyanate (HDI / TDI) | Polyaddition | Topside finish, chemical resistance |
| **Zinc-silicate** (inorganic) | Partially-hydrolysed ethyl silicate | Moisture / Zn-dust catalyst | Hydrolysis + condensation | **Shop-primer** for plate blasting |

### 2.4.2 Why Epoxies Dominate Modern Marine Painting

- They react with **amines** to form a dense **3-D cross-linked network** of:
  $$\text{R–CH–CH}_2 \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{N–R'} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–CH(OH)–CH}_2\text{–NH–R'}$$
- The resulting film has **excellent adhesion, chemical resistance, water resistance and mechanical strength**.
- The **pot-life** (working time after mixing) is typically 30 min – 8 h, after which the mixed paint must be discarded.
- **Surface-tolerant epoxies** (e.g., coal-tar epoxy) are still used in **ballast tanks, cofferdams, double bottoms** of bulk carriers.

### 2.4.3 KTU High-Yield Mnemonic for Reactive Systems

> **"E A I Z"** — **E**poxy (amine-cured), **A**crylic-polyurethane (isocyanate-cured), **I**norganic silicate, **Z**inc-rich (organic or inorganic). These four cover > 95 % of modern marine paint chemistry.

### 2.4.4 Marine Use Summary

- **Epoxy primers** (zinc-rich epoxy, zinc phosphate epoxy, coal-tar epoxy).
- **Epoxy tie-coat / intermediate** (e.g., MIO = micaceous iron oxide epoxy).
- **Polyurethane finish** (acrylic-PU) — high gloss, colour retention on topsides.
- **Ethyl-silicate zinc shop primer** — the green/grey primer applied immediately after plate blasting in the steel stockyard.

---

## 2.5 Bitumen (Asphaltic) Vehicles

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Bitumen* is a naturally-occurring (lake asphalt) or refinery-derived (from petroleum distillation residue) black, thermoplastic, water-proof, viscous-to-solid hydrocarbon. In paints it is used in three forms: **(a) cut-back bitumen** (bitumen dissolved in white spirit), **(b) bitumen emulsion** (bitumen in water with emulsifier), and **(c) coal-tar / coal-tar pitch** (by-product of coke manufacture).

### 2.5.1 Why Bitumen is Special for Marine Use

| Property | Bitumen | Why it matters on a ship |
|---|---|---|
| **Water resistance** | Excellent (already waterproof in nature) | Ballast tanks, underwater hull patches |
| **Chemical resistance** | Good against acids, alkalis, salts | Bilges, chain lockers |
| **Acid resistance** | Coal-tar pitch especially resistant to H₂SO₄ | Acid-tank lining (older chemical tankers) |
| **Adhesion to steel** | Good if surface is clean | Effective on rusty steel after minimal prep |
| **Cost** | Very cheap | Anti-corrosive primer for holds |
| **UV resistance** | Poor (chalks and embrittles in sun) | NOT for topsides — only submerged/interior |
| **Solvent resistance** | Poor (softens in hydrocarbons) | NOT for cargo oil tanks |

### 2.5.2 Forms Used in Marine Paints

**1. Cut-Back Bitumen (Solvent-Based)**

$$\text{Bitumen (e.g., 85/40 grade)} \;+\; \text{White Spirit / Solvent} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Cut-back (brushable)}$$

Applied as a black anti-corrosive primer on chain lockers, bilges, internal void spaces, engine room floors.

**2. Bitumen Emulsion (Water-Based)**

$$\text{Bitumen} \;+\; \text{Water} \;+\; \text{Emulsifier (soap)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Emulsion (≈ 50\% water)}$$

Dries by water evaporation; **non-flammable** so ideal for **gas-free enclosed spaces** (cofferdams, double bottoms during repair).

**3. Coal-Tar Bitumen / Pitch**

Distillation residue of coal tar gives a **pitch** with even better acid and water resistance than petroleum bitumen. Blended with epoxy resins → **coal-tar epoxy**, the traditional heavy-duty tank coating.

**4. Hot-Applied Bitumen (Mastic)**

Melted bitumen (150–200 °C) applied as a thick coating; gives thick, seamless film — used in underground pipelines and some ship bilges.

### 2.5.3 Marine Applications Summary

| Location onboard | Bitumen System | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Chain locker | Cut-back bitumen | Cheap, water-resistant, tolerant of damp |
| Bilges, cofferdams | Bitumen emulsion | Gas-free friendly |
| Cargo holds (bulk carriers) | Bitumen / coal-tar epoxy | Cheap, abrasion-resistant |
| Steel-stock protection | Bitumen solution | Long-term storage primer |
| Water-ballast tanks (older ships) | Coal-tar epoxy | Cheap chemical resistance |
| Underwater hull (older) | Hot-mastic bitumen | Thick, repairable |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** Bitumen must **never** be used for **cargo oil tanks of product carriers** because aromatic cargoes (gasoline, benzene) dissolve it → contamination of cargo. Use **epoxy-phenolic** or **zinc-silicate** instead.

---

## 2.6 The KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table for KTU 2024 — Memorise Before Exam Day.**

| Vehicle | Drying Mechanism | Key Chemistry | Film Type | Best Marine Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Raw/Boiled Linseed Oil** | Oxidative | Glyceride of linolenic acid | Soft, slow | Wood dressing, shop primer oil |
| **Tung Oil** | Oxidative | α-eleostearic acid (conjugated) | Hard, waterproof | Premium spar varnish |
| **Oleo-Resin (Spar Varnish)** | Oxidative | Oil + rosin/phenolic resin | Glossy, hard, flexible | Wooden masts, decks, boottop timber |
| **Long-Oil Alkyd** | Oxidative | Phthalic + glycerol + linseed FA | Flexible, glossy, slow | Marine topside, boottop finish |
| **Short-Oil Alkyd** | Oxidative | Phthalic + glycerol + oil | Hard, fast | Industrial stoving (rarely marine) |
| **Epoxy (2-pack)** | Polyaddition | Bisphenol-A + amine | Dense, chemical-resistant | Hull primer, ballast tank, cargo hold |
| **Polyurethane (2-pack)** | Polyaddition | Polyol + HDI isocyanate | Tough, UV-stable gloss | Topside finish |
| **Zinc-silicate (inorganic)** | Hydrolysis | Ethyl silicate + Zn + moisture | Hard, sacrificial | Shop primer |
| **Bitumen (cut-back)** | Evaporative | Asphalt + solvent | Black, water-resistant | Chain locker, bilge, void |
| **Coal-Tar Epoxy** | Polyaddition | Epoxy + coal-tar pitch | Black, thick, chemical-resistant | Ballast tank, cargo hold |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> This section contains the **mathematical/logical derivations** that KTU examiners expect for 14-mark questions on this topic. Because paint chemistry is largely reaction stoichiometry and ratio analysis, the derivations below are the *core analytical content* of the module.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Classifying a Drying Oil by Iodine Number

> The **iodine number (I.V.)** is a standard analytical quantity that KTU expects you to define AND calculate. Exam questions may give a hypothetical oil and ask whether it is drying, semi-drying or non-drying.

### 3.1.1 Definition & Reaction

When iodine monochloride (ICl) is added across the C=C double bond, 1 mole of ICl is consumed per mole of double bond:

$$\text{–CH=CH–} \;+\; \text{ICl} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{–CH(I)–CH(Cl)–}$$

The iodine number is the **grams of I₂ (or ICl expressed as I₂) that react with 100 g of oil**:

$$\text{Iodine Number} \;=\; \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$$

Where:

- $B$ = mL of thiosulphate for blank titration
- $S$ = mL of thiosulphate for sample titration
- $N$ = normality of thiosulphate (mol/L)
- $W$ = mass of oil sample (g)
- $12.69$ = equivalent weight of I₂ × 10 (since 1 mL of 1 N thiosulphate = 0.01269 g I₂)

### 3.1.2 Worked Example (KTU-style 3-mark problem)

> **Problem:** 0.5 g of an oil sample was subjected to the Wijs (ICl) method. The blank titration required 48.2 mL of 0.1 N sodium thiosulphate, and the sample titration required 22.4 mL. Classify the oil.

**Step 1 — Substitute values into the formula:**

$$\text{Iodine Number} \;=\; \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$$

$$\text{Iodine Number} \;=\; \dfrac{(48.2 - 22.4) \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.5}$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator:**

$$(48.2 - 22.4) \;=\; 25.8 \;\text{mL}$$

$$25.8 \times 0.1 \times 12.69 \;=\; 258 \times 0.01269 \;=\; 32.74$$

**Step 3 — Divide by mass:**

$$\text{Iodine Number} \;=\; \dfrac{32.74}{0.5} \;=\; 65.5$$

**Step 4 — Classify:**

Since $65.5 < 100$, this is a **non-drying oil** (akin to coconut oil).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Expectation:** Always finish with the **classification statement** and a one-line reason tied to the I.V. range — never leave the answer as just a number.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Oil Length and the Stoichiometry of an Oleo-Resinous Varnish

> Oil length is a **mass ratio**, and KTU frequently asks: "A varnish is made from 30 kg rosin and 25 kg linseed oil. Calculate the oil length."

### 3.2.1 Formula (in metric units, KTU 2024 preferred)

$$\text{Oil Length (gal/100 lb)} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Mass of oil (kg)}}{\text{Mass of resin (kg)}} \times 28.4$$

The factor **28.4** converts the simple kg-kg ratio into the traditional *gallons of oil per 100 lb of resin* (since 1 kg oil ≈ 1.1 L and 1 lb resin = 0.454 kg, the combined conversion is ≈ 28.4).

### 3.2.2 Worked Example

> **Problem:** A varnish cook uses 25 kg linseed oil and 30 kg ester-gum resin. Calculate the oil length and classify the varnish.

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$\text{Oil Length} \;=\; \dfrac{25}{30} \times 28.4 \;=\; 0.833 \times 28.4$$

$$\text{Oil Length} \;=\; 23.7 \;\text{gal/100 lb}$$

**Step 2 — Classify (per Section 2.2.3 thresholds):**

Since $23.7 < 25$, this is a **short-oil varnish** — hard, fast-drying, suited to indoor industrial enamels, not for marine spar varnish use.

### 3.2.3 Reverse Problem (KTU favourite)

> **Problem:** A marine spar varnish requires oil length between 30 and 40 gal/100 lb. How much oil should be cooked with 20 kg of phenolic resin?

**Step 1 — Set up equation:**

$$\text{Oil Length} \;=\; \dfrac{M_{oil}}{M_{resin}} \times 28.4$$

For an oil length of 35 gal/100 lb (mid-point):

$$35 \;=\; \dfrac{M_{oil}}{20} \times 28.4$$

**Step 2 — Solve:**

$$M_{oil} \;=\; \dfrac{35 \times 20}{28.4} \;=\; \dfrac{700}{28.4}$$

$$M_{oil} \;=\; 24.6 \;\text{kg}$$

So cook **24.6 kg of linseed oil** with 20 kg of phenolic resin.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Alkyd Formulation Stoichiometry (Phthalic Anhydride, Glycerol, Oil)

> The "A-stage cook" of an alkyd uses precise molar ratios to avoid gelation. KTU 2024 loves this reaction; here is the full analytical breakdown.

### 3.3.1 The Reaction

$$\text{Phthalic Anhydride (PA)} \;+\; \text{Glycerol} \;\xrightarrow{-\text{H}_2\text{O}}\; \text{Alkyd Polyester}$$

For a **non-drying-oil** modified alkyd, the fatty acid is added as a third component, and the resin is later dissolved in a high-boiling solvent.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-step Mass Calculation

> **Problem:** A 60 % oil-length alkyd is to be prepared from linseed oil fatty acids, glycerol and phthalic anhydride. How much of each is needed to make 100 kg of resin? (Assume fatty acid molecular weight = 280, glycerol = 92, phthalic anhydride = 148.)

**Step 1 — Decide oil-length fractions:**

- 60 % of 100 kg = **60 kg fatty acid** (linseed FA)
- Remaining 40 % = **40 kg of (PA + Glycerol) mass**

**Step 2 — Use the standard 1 : 1.3 : 1.1 mass ratio for PA : Glycerol (for a balanced 60 % oil alkyd, the ratio of PA to glycerol is ≈ 1.4 : 1):**

Let $m_{PA}$ = mass of phthalic anhydride, $m_{gly}$ = mass of glycerol, then:

$$m_{PA} + m_{gly} \;=\; 40 \quad \text{and} \quad m_{PA} \;=\; 1.4 \, m_{gly}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the simultaneous equations:**

$$1.4 \, m_{gly} + m_{gly} \;=\; 40$$

$$2.4 \, m_{gly} \;=\; 40 \;\Longrightarrow\; m_{gly} \;=\; 16.67 \;\text{kg}$$

$$m_{PA} \;=\; 40 - 16.67 \;=\; 23.33 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Final recipe for 100 kg of 60 % oil alkyd:**

- Linseed fatty acid: **60 kg**
- Phthalic anhydride: **23.33 kg**
- Glycerol: **16.67 kg**

Check: $60 + 23.33 + 16.67 = 100$ kg ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** The reaction releases water. A 100 kg batch actually produces **only ≈ 95 kg of finished alkyd** (the rest is the water of condensation that is removed via the Dean-Stark trap). KTU sometimes asks: "If water removed is 5 kg, what is the mass yield of resin?" — answer = 95 kg.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Vehicle for a Shipboard Application

> KTU 14-mark questions often present a *scenario* ("You are a yard painting superintendent for a new VLCC ...") and ask you to **select and justify a paint vehicle for every zone of the ship**. The matrix below is the analytical backbone for that answer.

| Shipboard Zone | Aggression Factor | Recommended Vehicle | Justification (Vehicle-Chemistry Based) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Underwater hull** (anti-fouling) | Marine fouling + cathodic protection | **Vinyl / rosin + biocide matrix** | Controlled biocide leach rate; compatible with cathodic protection |
| **Underwater hull** (anti-corrosive) | Seawater + CP currents | **Epoxy (zinc-rich or MIO epoxy)** | Excellent water-resistance, long durability |
| **Boottop** | Splash + UV | **Epoxy tie-coat + alkyd finish** OR **PU finish** | UV-resistant topcoat, epoxy sealer beneath |
| **Topside** (freeboard, superstructure) | UV, salt spray, abrasion | **Epoxy primer + PU finish (acrylic-PU)** | High gloss + colour retention + flexibility |
| **Water ballast tanks** | Alternating wet/dry, salt water | **Coal-tar epoxy** OR **pure epoxy** | High build, chemical-resistance, IMO PSPC compliant |
| **Cargo holds (bulk carrier)** | Mechanical impact, cargo abrasion | **Epoxy-phenolic** OR **bitumen-epoxy** | Tough, abrasion-resistant |
| **Cargo oil tanks (product carrier)** | Hydrocarbon cargo, refined products | **Epoxy-phenolic** OR **zinc-silicate** | **NOT** bitumen (would contaminate cargo) |
| **Bilges, cofferdams** | Trapped water, intermittent oil | **Bitumen emulsion** | Cheap, water-resistant, gas-free friendly |
| **Chain locker** | Salt water + mechanical | **Cut-back bitumen** | Tolerant of damp, cheap, repairable |
| **Wooden deck / mast** | Weather, salt | **Spar varnish (oleo-resinous)** | Flexibility + UV + salt-spray durability |
| **Steel stockyard (plates)** | 6–18 months outdoor storage | **Zinc-silicate shop primer** (20 µm) | Thin, weldable, sacrificial galvanic protection |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT answer such a question by writing "use epoxy" everywhere. **The KTU Board examiner is marking for *justification* — you must explicitly link the choice to the *chemistry of the vehicle* you discussed in Section 2.**

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Bitumen Grade by Penetration & Softening Point

> Although not strictly a "vehicle" derivation, KTU sometimes tests the **bitumen grade identification** as part of a paint-vehicle question.

### 3.5.1 Penetration Number

A standard needle is loaded with 100 g for 5 s at 25 °C; the depth of penetration in 0.1 mm units is the **penetration number**. Lower = harder bitumen.

### 3.5.2 Softening Point (Ring-and-Ball)

The temperature at which a bitumen disc sags under a steel ball. Higher = harder / higher-melt bitumen.

### 3.5.3 Worked Correlation

For a marine cut-back bitumen paint, you typically use a **85/40 grade** bitumen:

- **85** = softening point 85 °C
- **40** = penetration 40 (0.1 mm units)

This grade gives a hard, durable film with adequate brushability once cut back with white spirit. **Higher softening point** = better heat resistance but harder to apply. **Lower penetration** = harder film but less flexible.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> All questions below are written to the **exact KTU 2024 End-Semester Exam (ESE) template**:
> - Part A: 3-mark short answer (2 questions, no choice)
> - Part B: 14-mark question (Module-internal choice of two 14-mark alternatives)
> - Each Part B is split into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks
> - All questions carry simulated past-year tags

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 marks)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**"What is a drying oil? How is it classified by iodine number? Give two marine examples."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **drying oil** is a triglyceride of unsaturated fatty acids that hardens to a continuous film on exposure to atmospheric oxygen by oxidative polymerisation at its C=C double bonds. It is classified by the **iodine number (I.V.)** — the grams of iodine that react with 100 g of oil, which measures the degree of unsaturation:

- **Drying oil** → I.V. > 150 → e.g., **linseed oil (170–190)**, **tung oil (160–175)**
- **Semi-drying oil** → I.V. = 100–150 → e.g., **soybean oil (125–140)**
- **Non-drying oil** → I.V. < 100 → e.g., **coconut oil (8–10)**

These two drying oils (linseed and tung) are the classical marine vehicles for spar varnishes and linseed-oil primers.

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**"Distinguish between an oleo-resinous varnish and an alkyd resin in terms of composition and film properties."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Oleo-Resinous Varnish | Alkyd Resin |
|---|---|---|
| **Composition** | Physical blend (hot "cook") of drying oil + natural/synthetic resin (e.g., rosin, phenolic) | Chemically reacted polyester of polybasic acid + polyhydric alcohol + fatty acid |
| **Reproducibility** | Batch-to-batch variable (natural resin) | Highly reproducible (synthetic) |
| **Drying** | Oxidative | Oxidative (when oil-modified) |
| **Film** | Glossy, hard but tends to darken with natural resin | Better colour, gloss retention, longer durability |
| **Marine Use** | Spar varnish for wood | Long-oil alkyd for topside finish |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (1 × 14 = 14 marks)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** The student answers **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each alternative has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question B (A) — OPTION A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 marks]** *Classify the common paint vehicles used in shipbuilding. With the help of a flow chart, explain the chemistry and drying mechanism of "drying oils" and "oleo-resinous vehicles."*

**(b) [7 marks]** *A varnish cook uses 18 kg of phenolic resin and 22 kg of tung oil. Calculate the oil length. Classify the varnish and state one suitable marine application. Justify.*

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — OPTION A

**(a) Model Answer — Classification + Drying Mechanism**

**Step 1 — Classification [3 marks]:**

The common paint vehicles used in shipbuilding can be classified into **five major families**:

1. **Drying Oils** — e.g., linseed oil, tung oil, perilla oil
2. **Oleo-Resinous Vehicles** — e.g., spar varnish (oil + rosin / phenolic resin)
3. **Alkyd Resins** — short, medium, long-oil alkyds; modified alkyds
4. **Polymerising Chemicals (Reactive Binders)** — epoxies, polyurethanes, zinc-silicates
5. **Bitumen / Bituminous Vehicles** — cut-back bitumen, bitumen emulsion, coal-tar pitch

**Step 2 — Flow Chart [2 marks]:**

The student should present a flow chart similar to Section 4.1 above — start with "PAINT VEHICLES" at the top, branch into the five families, and sub-branch into specific examples.

**Step 3 — Drying Oil Chemistry [1 mark]:**

Drying oil is a triglyceride where the glycerol is esterified with three unsaturated fatty acids (linolenic, linoleic, eleostearic). The **conjugated C=C double bonds** are the reactive sites that absorb O₂ from the air.

**Step 4 — Drying Mechanism [1 mark]:**

$$\text{–CH=CH–CH}_2\text{–CH=CH–} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{hydroperoxide} \rightarrow \text{cross-link with neighbour chain} \rightarrow \text{3D polymer network} \rightarrow \text{dry film}$$

Catalysed by **cobalt / lead / manganese naphthenate driers**.

**Oleo-Resinous Chemistry [bonus point for completeness]:**

An oleo-resin is made by **hot-cooking** (250–300 °C) a drying oil with a natural or synthetic resin; the resin supplies hardness & gloss, the oil supplies flexibility & durability. The **oil length** (gallons of oil per 100 lb of resin) controls the film character.

---

**(b) Model Answer — Oil Length Calculation**

**Step 1 — Apply the oil-length formula [3 marks]:**

$$\text{Oil Length} \;=\; \dfrac{M_{oil}}{M_{resin}} \times 28.4$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values [1 mark]:**

$$\text{Oil Length} \;=\; \dfrac{22}{18} \times 28.4$$

**Step 3 — Compute [1 mark]:**

$$1.222 \times 28.4 \;=\; 34.7 \;\text{gal/100 lb}$$

**Step 4 — Classify [1 mark]:**

Since $34.7$ lies between 25 and 50, this is a **medium-oil varnish**.

**Step 5 — Marine application + justification [1 mark]:**

A medium-oil varnish is the classic formulation for **marine spar varnish** used on **wooden masts, decks, boottop timber, and superstructure woodwork** of small vessels. **Justification:** the medium oil length provides the right balance of hardness (from the phenolic resin) and flexibility + weather resistance (from the tung oil) needed for marine timber that flexes with sea motion and is exposed to salt spray and UV.

---

### **Question B (B) — OPTION B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the term "alkyd resin". Describe the chemistry of its preparation, the role of each raw material, and classify alkyds by oil length with marine applications of each class.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *What is bitumen? Discuss the various forms in which bitumen is used as a marine paint vehicle. Why is bitumen NOT recommended for the cargo oil tanks of a product carrier?*

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — OPTION B

**(a) Model Answer — Alkyd Resin**

**Step 1 — Definition [1 mark]:**

An **alkyd resin** is a synthetic polyester formed by reacting a polybasic acid (e.g., phthalic anhydride), a polyhydric alcohol (e.g., glycerol), and a fatty acid (or oil). The fatty acid imparts the drying (oxidative) character; the polyester backbone provides toughness and gloss.

**Step 2 — Chemistry / Reaction [2 marks]:**

$$\text{Phthalic Anhydride} + \text{Glycerol} + \text{Fatty Acid} \xrightarrow{\,\Delta,\,-\text{H}_2\text{O}\,} \text{Alkyd Polyester}$$

- **Phthalic anhydride** → provides the aromatic rigid segment → hardness, gloss, chemical resistance.
- **Glycerol** (trifunctional alcohol) → provides the branched 3-D network → film integrity.
- **Fatty acid** (linseed/tung/safflower) → provides the pendant unsaturated chains → oxidative drying sites + flexibility.

**Step 3 — Classification by Oil Length [3 marks]:**

| Type | Oil % | Film | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Short-oil alkyd | 30 – 45 | Hard, fast-drying | Stoving finishes (rarely marine) |
| Medium-oil alkyd | 45 – 60 | Balanced | General marine gloss finish |
| Long-oil alkyd | 60 – 80 | Flexible, slow-dry, durable | Marine topside, boottop, exterior house paint |
| Very-long oil | > 80 | Very flexible | House paint |

**Step 4 — Marine application of each [1 mark]:**

- **Long-oil alkyd** → marine topside, boottop finishes (the classic single-pack marine finish).
- **Medium-oil alkyd** → air-drying marine enamels for interior cabins, machinery spaces.

---

**(b) Model Answer — Bitumen in Marine Paints**

**Step 1 — Definition [1 mark]:**

**Bitumen** is a black, thermoplastic, highly water-resistant hydrocarbon obtained from natural deposits (lake asphalt) or as a residue of petroleum distillation. It may be modified with **coal-tar pitch** for extra chemical resistance.

**Step 2 — Forms in which bitumen is used [4 marks]:**

1. **Cut-back bitumen** — bitumen dissolved in white spirit → brushable solvent-based paint. *Marine use: chain lockers, bilges, internal voids.*
2. **Bitumen emulsion** — bitumen dispersed in water with an emulsifier. Dries by water evaporation. *Marine use: cofferdams, gas-free spaces, void repairs (non-flammable).*
3. **Coal-tar bitumen / coal-tar pitch** — distillation residue of coal tar; better acid and water resistance. *Marine use: combined with epoxy resin → coal-tar epoxy for ballast tanks and cargo holds of bulk carriers.*
4. **Hot-applied bitumen / mastic** — molten bitumen applied at 150–200 °C to give thick seamless films. *Marine use: bilge mastic, pipeline protection.*

**Step 3 — Why NOT for cargo oil tanks of a product carrier [2 marks]:**

Bitumen (and coal-tar) is **readily soluble in refined hydrocarbon cargoes** such as gasoline, naphtha, kerosene, benzene, and other light distillates. If used as a tank coating, the cargo would **dissolve the bitumen**, leading to:

- **Cargo contamination** → rejection by the charterer / refinery.
- **Coating failure** → bare steel exposed to cargo + seawater ballast cycles.
- **Loss of cargo due to contamination claims** → huge commercial loss.

Hence, cargo oil tanks of product carriers use **epoxy-phenolic** or **zinc-silicate** coatings that are **resistant to white-spirit, gasoline and aromatics**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Most Common Marks-Loss Points:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "vehicle" with "solvent".** The vehicle = binder + solvent. The *binder* is the non-volatile film-former. Writing "white spirit is the vehicle" is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss.
> 2. **Stating Iodine Number without a number.** Always quote the **range** for your chosen oil: "linseed oil I.V. = 170–190", not just "high iodine number".
> 3. **Skipping the oil-length classification.** KTU Board examiners allot 1 full mark for the classification statement after a calculation; many students forget it.
> 4. **Writing "epoxy is best" without justification.** The justification must be tied to a **property of the epoxy vehicle** (cross-link density, chemical resistance, flexibility).
> 5. **Forgetting that bitumen is *not* used in product-carrier cargo tanks.** This is a favourite 2-mark KTU trap.
> 6. **Confusing "boiled" with "heat-treated" linseed oil.** Boiled linseed oil = raw oil + metallic driers, NOT thermally boiled.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **[COPY-PASTE THIS INTO YOUR EXAM-EVE REVISION NOTEBOOK]**

### ✅ Core Definitions to Memorise

- **Vehicle (Binder / Medium):** Non-volatile film-former of a paint.
- **Drying Oil:** Triglyceride that hardens by O₂ absorption at C=C bonds.
- **Iodine Number:** Grams of I₂ reacting with 100 g of oil; > 150 = drying, 100–150 = semi-drying, < 100 = non-drying.
- **Oleo-Resin:** Oil + natural/synthetic resin "cooked" hot.
- **Oil Length:** Gallons of oil per 100 lb of resin (or metric equivalent).
- **Alkyd:** Synthetic polyester of PA + glycerol + fatty acid.
- **Polymerising Chemical:** Reactive monomer/prepolymer that cures on application (epoxy, PU, silicate).
- **Bitumen:** Thermoplastic black hydrocarbon, water-proof, used as anti-corrosive vehicle.

### ✅ Numerical Must-Knows

- Linseed oil I.V. = **170–190**; Tung oil I.V. = **160–175**; Soybean = 125–140; Coconut = 8–10.
- Oil Length: Short < 25, Medium 25–50, Long > 50 (gal/100 lb).
