**Step 1:** Convert 1 litre = $10^6$ μm·m² (since 1 m² × 1 μm = 1 litre / 10⁶).

**Step 2:** The dry film volume = $A \times t$ (in μm·m²).

**Step 3:** Equate to the solid content of 1 litre of paint:

$$ A \times t = 10^6 \times \dfrac{VS}{100} $$

**Step 4:** Solve for $A$ (the coverage) at thickness $t = \text{DFT}$:

$$ A = \dfrac{10^6 \times VS}{100 \times \text{DFT}} = \dfrac{10^4 \times VS}{\text{DFT}} $$

**Step 5:** Since coverage is normally expressed in m²/L, and 1 L = 1 tin:

$$ \boxed{\text{Cov}_{th}\,(\text{m}^2/\text{L}) = \dfrac{10 \times VS\%}{\text{DFT}\,(\mu m)} } $$

This is the canonical "**10-V rule**" used by every marine paint supplier.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Volume of Paint Required

**Step 1:** Rearrange the coverage equation:

$$ \text{Cov}_{th} = \dfrac{10 \times VS}{\text{DFT}} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{DFT} = \dfrac{10 \times VS}{\text{Cov}_{th}} $$

**Step 2:** For a given area $A$ m², number of litres = $\dfrac{A}{\text{Cov}_{th}}$.

$$ V_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{A \times \text{DFT}}{10 \times VS} \;\; \text{(L)} $$

**Step 3:** Apply the **Loss Factor (LF)** to account for overspray, surface roughness, wastage:

$$ \boxed{ V_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{A \times \text{DFT} \times (1 + LF)}{10 \times VS} } $$

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Underwater Area Paint Quantity

**Problem (KTU-style):** A 150 m × 30 m bulk carrier has a flat bottom of area **3,500 m²** (after deduction for propellers, sea-chests, etc.). The underwater scheme is:
- 1 × zinc epoxy primer at **75 μm DFT** (VS = 60%).
- 1 × epoxy tie coat at **50 μm DFT** (VS = 55%).
- 2 × SPC antifouling at **125 μm DFT each** (VS = 55%).
Loss factor LF = 1.3. Calculate the total paint quantity in litres.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Total DFT required**
$$ \text{DFT}_{total} = 75 + 50 + (2 \times 125) = 75 + 50 + 250 = 375 \, \mu m $$

**Step 2: Calculate volume per layer using $V = \dfrac{A \cdot \text{DFT} \cdot (1+LF)}{10 \cdot VS}$**

For the **primer** (75 μm, VS = 60%):
$$ V_p = \dfrac{3500 \times 75 \times 1.3}{10 \times 60} = \dfrac{341{,}250}{600} = 568.75 \;\text{L} $$

For the **tie coat** (50 μm, VS = 55%):
$$ V_t = \dfrac{3500 \times 50 \times 1.3}{10 \times 55} = \dfrac{227{,}500}{550} = 413.64 \;\text{L} $$

For **one antifouling coat** (125 μm, VS = 55%):
$$ V_{a1} = \dfrac{3500 \times 125 \times 1.3}{10 \times 55} = \dfrac{568{,}750}{550} = 1034.09 \;\text{L} $$

For **two antifouling coats**:
$$ V_a = 2 \times 1034.09 = 2068.18 \;\text{L} $$

**Step 3: Total paint volume**
$$ V_{total} = 568.75 + 413.64 + 2068.18 \approx 3050.57 \;\text{L} \approx 3051 \;\text{L} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** Approximately **3,051 litres** of paint is required just for the underwater scheme of a 150 m bulk carrier bottom.

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Corrosion Rate of a Steel Coupon

**Problem:** A steel specimen of area **30 cm²** and density **7.85 g/cm³** lost **450 mg** of mass in a salt-spray test lasting **720 hours**. Calculate the corrosion rate in mm/yr.

**Step 1: Apply Formula 5.**
$$ \text{CR} = \dfrac{87.6 \times \Delta W}{D \times A \times T} $$

**Step 2: Substitute.**
$$ \text{CR} = \dfrac{87.6 \times 450}{7.85 \times 30 \times 720} $$

**Step 3: Numerator.**
$$ 87.6 \times 450 = 39{,}420 $$

**Step 4: Denominator.**
$$ 7.85 \times 30 = 235.5; \quad 235.5 \times 720 = 169{,}560 $$

**Step 5: Final division.**
$$ \text{CR} = \dfrac{39{,}420}{169{,}560} = 0.2325 \;\text{mm/yr} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** CR ≈ **0.23 mm/yr** — this is a *moderate* marine corrosion rate. Unprotected mild steel in tropical seawater typically corrodes at 0.1–0.5 mm/yr, justifying the need for coatings.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Scheme Summary Table

| Layer | Underwater | Boot Top | Above Water | Weather Deck | Superstructure | Tank Interior |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Surface prep (ISO 8501-1) | **Sa 2½** | **Sa 2½** | **Sa 2½** | **Sa 2½** | **Sa 2½** | **Sa 2½** |
| Primer type | Zinc-rich epoxy | Zinc-rich epoxy | Epoxy zinc / epoxy | Zinc-rich epoxy | Epoxy | Solvent-free epoxy |
| Primer DFT (μm) | 75 | 75 | 75 | 75 | 50 | 75 (stripe + spray) |
| Intermediate | Epoxy tie coat | Epoxy tie coat | Epoxy MIO | High-build epoxy | Epoxy MIO | Epoxy build |
| Intermediate DFT (μm) | 50 | 50 | 100–125 | 150–200 | 75 | 100–150 |
| Finish / Specialist | SPC antifouling | AF + PU marker | Polyurethane | Non-skid PU/epoxy | Polyurethane | Phenolic/PU (optional) |
| Finish DFT (μm) | 250 (2 coats) | 100–150 | 50–75 | 200–300 (with agg.) | 50 | 50–75 |
| **Total DFT (μm)** | **~375** | **~300–450** | **~250–300** | **~400–600** | **~200–250** | **~200–300** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
List the **six corrosion zones** of a ship for which distinct paint schemes are specified, and state the **dominant degradation mechanism** in any one of them.

**Model Answer (3 marks — Key Points):**
- **Underwater** — corrosion + biofouling
- **Boot top** — wet/dry tidal cycling + UV
- **Above-water hull (topsides)** — UV + salt spray
- **Weather decks** — abrasion + foot traffic + ponding
- **Superstructures** — UV + aesthetics
- **Tank interiors** — cargo chemicals + seawater

(Marks: 1 for listing all 6, 2 for explaining mechanism of one chosen zone.)

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
Why is a **polyurethane topcoat** used on topsides and superstructures, but not on underwater hulls?

**Model Answer (3 marks — Key Points):**
- Polyurethane (aliphatic isocyanate cured) has excellent **UV resistance, gloss retention, and colour stability** — needed for above-water aesthetic surfaces (1 mark).
- Underwater hulls are covered by **antifouling (SPC/TBT-free)** systems that work by hydrolytic self-polishing in seawater; a polyurethane topcoat would **block biocide release** (1 mark).
- Additionally, antifouling must be top-most because it contacts water first (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**
**(a)** Describe, with a labelled sketch, the **typical paint scheme for the underwater area** of a merchant ship, including surface preparation, primer, tie coat, antifouling chemistry, and total DFT. (7 marks)

**(b)** A flat bottom area of 2,500 m² is to be coated with the following scheme: 1 × epoxy primer 75 μm (VS = 60%), 1 × tie coat 50 μm (VS = 55%), 2 × antifouling 125 μm each (VS = 55%). Using a loss factor of 1.3, calculate the total litres of paint required. State the **10-V rule** for theoretical coverage. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Scheme description (7 marks):**
- **Surface preparation:** Abrasive blast to **ISO 8501-1 Sa 2½**, surface profile **50–100 μm**, dust ≤ ISO 8502-3 Class 2. (1 mark)
- **Primer (75 μm DFT):** **Zinc-rich epoxy** — provides cathodic/sacrificial protection to steel; zinc content ≥ 80% in dry film. (1 mark)
- **Tie coat (50 μm DFT):** **Epoxy sealer / tie coat** — bonds the epoxy primer to the antifouling, which is chemically different. (1 mark)
- **Antifouling (2 × 125 μm = 250 μm DFT):** **Self-Polishing Copolymer (SPC)**, TBT-free; releases **cuprous oxide** biocide at controlled rate by hydrolysis; controlled polishing rate ≈ 5–10 μm/month. (2 marks)
- **Total DFT ≈ 375 μm.** (1 mark)
- **Practical notes:** Stripe coats on edges, weld seams, brackets, and bilge keels before spray; DFT verified with **Elcometer 456**; minimum overcoating interval respected. (1 mark)

**(b) Calculations (7 marks):**
Using the formula $V = \dfrac{A \times \text{DFT} \times (1+LF)}{10 \times VS}$:

**Primer (75 μm, VS = 60%):**
$$ V_p = \dfrac{2500 \times 75 \times 1.3}{10 \times 60} = \dfrac{243{,}750}{600} = 406.25 \;\text{L} \quad (1\;\text{mark}) $$

**Tie coat (50 μm, VS = 55%):**
$$ V_t = \dfrac{2500 \times 50 \times 1.3}{10 \times 55} = \dfrac{162{,}500}{550} = 295.45 \;\text{L} \quad (1\;\text{mark}) $$

**Antifouling (1 coat, 125 μm, VS = 55%):**
$$ V_{a1} = \dfrac{2500 \times 125 \times 1.3}{10 \times 55} = \dfrac{406{,}250}{550} = 738.64 \;\text{L} \quad (1\;\text{mark}) $$

**Antifouling (2 coats):**
$$ V_a = 2 \times 738.64 = 1477.27 \;\text{L} \quad (1\;\text{mark}) $$

**Total volume:**
$$ V_{total} = 406.25 + 295.45 + 1477.27 = 2178.97 \;\text{L} \quad (1\;\text{mark}) $$

**10-V rule statement:** *The theoretical coverage (in m²/L) of a paint applied at a given DFT is given by $\text{Cov} = \dfrac{10 \times VS\%}{\text{DFT}(\mu m)}$ — the "10-V rule".* (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Lose 1 Mark Each If You):**
> 1. Forget to **multiply by 2** for two antifouling coats.
> 2. Use **WFT** in the volume formula instead of **DFT**.
> 3. Forget to apply the **Loss Factor (1.3)** — examiners specifically check this in 14-mark problems.
> 4. Forget to **state the units** (litres) in the final answer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024 Pattern]**
**(a)** Compare the paint schemes for **weather decks** and **superstructures**. Why are they different even though both are above water and exposed to UV? (7 marks)

**(b)** Differentiate between **WFT and DFT** with a numerical example. A 200 μm DFT is to be applied with a paint of **65% volume solids**. Calculate (i) the WFT, (ii) the theoretical coverage in m²/L, and (iii) the litres required to coat 1,200 m² (LF = 1.2). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Property | Weather Deck | Superstructure |
|---|---|---|
| Exposure | UV + foot traffic + cargo impact | UV + salt spray + aesthetic |
| Primer | Zinc-rich epoxy 75 μm | Epoxy 50 μm |
| Intermediate | High-build epoxy 150–200 μm | Epoxy MIO 75–100 μm |
| Finish | **Non-skid epoxy/PU + aggregate** | Polyurethane 50 μm |
| Total DFT | **400–600 μm** (incl. agg.) | **200–250 μm** |
| Function | Anti-slip + abrasion + barrier | UV barrier + colour |

**Why different?** (3 marks)
- Weather decks are **load-bearing, walked-upon** surfaces, requiring **non-skid aggregate** and a much **thicker total DFT** for abrasion resistance. (1 mark)
- Superstructures are **non-traffic, aesthetic surfaces**; they need only **UV-stable polyurethane** finish, so DFT is lower to save weight & cost. (1 mark)
- Aggregate in deck coating makes the surface rough — that would look ugly on a superstructure and is unnecessary there. (1 mark)

**(b) WFT vs DFT & calculations (7 marks):**
- **WFT** = wet film thickness, measured before solvent evaporates (use **wet film comb/Notch gauge**). (1 mark)
- **DFT** = dry film thickness, measured after full cure (use **magnetic or eddy-current gauge**). (1 mark)
- Relation: $\text{WFT} = \dfrac{\text{DFT}}{VS/100}$ (because only the solid fraction remains as film). (1 mark)

**(i) WFT:**
$$ \text{WFT} = \dfrac{200}{65/100} = \dfrac{200}{0.65} = 307.69 \;\mu m \quad (1\;\text{mark}) $$

**(ii) Theoretical coverage (10-V rule):**
$$ \text{Cov}_{th} = \dfrac{10 \times 65}{200} = \dfrac{650}{200} = 3.25 \;\text{m}^2/\text{L} \quad (1\;\text{mark}) $$

**(iii) Practical volume of paint (with LF = 1.2):**
$$ V = \dfrac{1200 \times 200 \times (1 + 1.2)}{10 \times 65} = \dfrac{1200 \times 200 \times 2.2}{650} = \dfrac{528{,}000}{650} = 812.31 \;\text{L} \quad (1\;\text{mark}) $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. In **(i)**, students often **invert** the formula: do WFT = DFT/VS, not WFT = DFT × VS.
> 2. In **(iii)**, students forget the **loss factor** → wrong (practical) volume.
> 3. Students often state only one WFT and DFT and lose 1 mark — must distinguish both with a clear sentence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⚓ **Six ship zones** — underwater, boot top, above water, weather deck, superstructure, tank interior.
- ⚓ **Common primer** — zinc-rich epoxy (sacrificial cathodic protection).
- ⚓ **Common intermediate** — epoxy MIO (micaceous iron oxide) for barrier & build.
- ⚓ **Common finish for above water** — **polyurethane** (UV resistance, gloss).
- ⚓ **Underwater finish** — **antifouling (SPC, TBT-free)** with **cuprous oxide**.
- ⚓ **Boot top** — hybrid scheme (antifouling below + polyurethane marker above the waterline).
- ⚓ **Weather deck** — non-skid aggregate broadcast into epoxy/PU; high total DFT.
- ⚓ **Superstructure** — low DFT, two-tone polyurethane finish.
- ⚓ **Tank interiors** — **solvent-free epoxy**, stripe coats mandatory on edges/welds.
- ⚓ **PSPC** (Performance Standard for Protective Coatings) governs ballast tank coatings (IMO resolution MSC.215(82)).
- ⚓ **Surface prep standard** — **ISO 8501-1 Sa 2½** for all schemes.
- ⚓ **Key formula — coverage:** $\text{Cov} = \dfrac{10 \times VS}{\text{DFT}}$ (m²/L).
- ⚓ **Key formula — volume:** $V = \dfrac{A \times \text{DFT} \times (1+LF)}{10 \times VS}$ (litres).
- ⚓ **Key formula — WFT:** $\text{WFT} = \dfrac{\text{DFT}}{VS/100}$.
- ⚓ **Key formula — corrosion rate:** $\text{CR} = \dfrac{87.6 \times \Delta W}{D \times A \times T}$ (mm/yr).
- ⚓ **Stripe coat** — applied by brush on edges, welds, cut-outs — always required before spray.
- ⚓ **Total DFTs to memorise:** Underwater ~375 μm; Topside ~275 μm; Weather deck ~475 μm; Superstructure ~200 μm; Tank ~300 μm.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion control - importance of corrosion protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Corrosion control - importance of corrosion protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Corrosion Control** is defined as the systematic application of engineering principles, material science, surface engineering, electrochemical theory, and protective technologies to prevent, mitigate, or arrest the degradation of metals and alloys due to electrochemical, chemical, or biochemical reactions with their environment. In the context of **shipbuilding and marine structures**, corrosion control refers to the integrated strategy of material selection, design modification, protective coatings, cathodic and anodic protection, inhibitors, and routine monitoring applied to hulls, superstructures, ballast tanks, propellers, piping systems, and offshore platforms exposed to **seawater, marine atmosphere, splash zones, and submerged soil conditions**.

**Corrosion Protection Importance** is the qualitative and quantitative assessment of why proactive corrosion management is non-negotiable in maritime engineering. It evaluates corrosion through three primary lenses: **(i) Economic Loss** (direct replacement costs plus indirect losses like downtime, productivity loss, and product loss), **(ii) Safety & Structural Integrity** (catastrophic failure risk), and **(iii) Environmental Compliance** (pollution control, IMO regulations, oil spill prevention).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly frames corrosion control as an **interdisciplinary design parameter** — not an afterthought. It must be embedded in the conceptual design phase, not retrofitted at the maintenance stage. As per the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework, students are expected to evaluate (CO Level 5) the economic, safety, and environmental impact of corrosion on marine assets.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you bought a brand-new bicycle for ₹15,000. You leave it parked near the seashore for **one monsoon season** without any cover, paint touch-up, or grease on the chain. When you return, the chain is rusted solid, the spokes have flaking orange patches, the handlebar grip is crumbling, and the brake cables are snapped. The bicycle still "exists," but it is functionally dead. To restore it, you must spend ₹7,000 on parts, lose 3 days of work, and even then the frame's structural strength is permanently compromised.

**A ship is that bicycle, scaled up by a factor of 10,000, exposed to a far more aggressive environment, and carrying a price tag measured in crores.**

A typical Panamax bulk carrier costs around **USD 30–35 million** to build. Without corrosion protection, the hull plating in the splash zone could perforate within **7–10 years**, ballast tanks within **10–15 years**, and the entire vessel's operational life would be slashed from a designed 25–30 years to roughly 12–15 years. The "money bleeding" from corrosion is silent, invisible to the untrained eye, but absolutely relentless — typically **3–5% of GDP** of any industrialized nation is lost to corrosion annually (NACE International IMPACT Report). For a ship, this translates into **millions of dollars per vessel per decade**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Anchor:** The **MV Prestige** oil tanker disaster (2002, off Spain) is widely attributed to severe structural corrosion that went undetected due to inadequate maintenance. The vessel broke apart, spilling 77,000 tonnes of heavy fuel oil — an environmental catastrophe costing over **EUR 1 billion** in cleanup. This single incident catalyzed the **IMO Resolution A.744(18)** on corrosion protection surveys for tankers and bulk carriers.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Corrosion Protection

- **Corrosion Rate (CR):** measured in **mm/year** or **mils per year (mpy)** — the single most important engineering metric.
- **Protection Efficiency (η):** expressed as a **percentage (%)** — how effective a protection method is.
- **Service Life Extension (SLE):** measured in **years**.
- **Cost of Corrosion (CoC):** expressed in **USD per square meter per year** or as **% of replacement value**.
- **Salt Spray Test Duration:** measured in **hours** (ASTM B117 standard reference).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** Many students believe corrosion is only a "surface cosmetic" problem. **This is dangerously false.** Marine corrosion is a **structural, mechanical, and safety problem**. The RMS Titanic's hull deterioration rate is studied as a case of microbiologically-influenced corrosion (MIC). The lesson: corrosion is not just about appearance; it is about catastrophic loss of life, asset, and environment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Corrosion Protection Importance

The importance of corrosion protection in shipbuilding rests on **three interdependent pillars**. Mastering these is essential for KTU ESE questions worth 7–14 marks.

#### Pillar 1 — Economic Importance

The **Total Cost of Corrosion (TCoC)** is divided into:

$$
TCoC = C_{\text{direct}} + C_{\text{indirect}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{direct}}$ = Visible, planned expenditure: repainting, anode replacement, steel renewal, sandblasting, inhibitor dosing.
- $C_{\text{indirect}}$ = Hidden losses: downtime, productivity loss, product (cargo) loss, insurance premium hike, late-delivery penalties, failure investigation costs.

For a **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier)**, industry data shows:
- **Direct corrosion cost** ≈ **USD 1.2–1.8 million per vessel over 25 years**.
- **Indirect corrosion cost** ≈ **30–50% of direct cost**, i.e., an additional **USD 0.4–0.9 million**.
- **Total ≈ USD 1.6–2.7 million** per vessel lifetime.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Why is corrosion protection important?", always quantify the answer. A 14-mark answer that gives only qualitative reasoning (e.g., "corrosion damages the ship") scores at most **4–5 marks**. Add numbers, percentages, and case-study anchors to reach **11–14 marks**.

#### Pillar 2 — Safety & Structural Integrity Importance

A ship's hull, when unprotected, suffers from:

- **General (Uniform) Corrosion:** Thins the plate uniformly; reduces load-bearing cross-section.
- **Pitting Corrosion:** Creates localized deep cavities; acts as a **stress concentrator** and **fatigue crack initiator**.
- **Crevice Corrosion:** Occurs under gaskets, bolt heads, lap joints — common in hull-rudder connections.
- **Galvanic Corrosion:** Triggered when dissimilar metals (e.g., Cu-Ni propeller + steel hull) are electrically coupled in seawater.
- **Erosion-Corrosion:** Mechanical wear accelerates electrochemical attack in high-flow zones (bow thruster tunnels, seawater cooling lines).
- **Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC):** SRB (Sulfate-Reducing Bacteria) attack in ballast tanks and submerged hull; produces **pits up to 1–2 mm/year**.

The structural consequence: a **10% reduction in plate thickness** reduces hull girder bending strength by approximately **20%**. The **SOLAS Convention** (Chapter II-1, Regulation 3-1) and **IACS UR S21/S22** mandate **substantial corrosion addition (scawthorn)** in the midship section modulus calculation — typically **0.5 mm to 1.0 mm per side** — explicitly to account for expected corrosion loss over the design life.

#### Pillar 3 — Environmental & Regulatory Importance

Modern shipbuilding is governed by strict environmental regulations:

- **MARPOL 73/78 Annex VI** — Air pollution prevention (also drives ballast water management).
- **IMO Resolution A.744(18)** — Corrosion protection surveys for tankers and bulk carriers.
- **IMO PSPC** (Performance Standard for Protective Coatings) — Mandatory since 2008 for ballast tanks of all new ships ≥ 500 GT, and double-side skin spaces of bulk carriers.
- **EU Ship Recycling Regulation (EU SRR) 1257/2013** — Requires inventory of hazardous materials, many of which are corrosion-protection-related.
- **Classification Societies** (DNV, LR, ABS, BV, IRS) — All have dedicated chapters on corrosion protection, with **class notations** like DNV's "COR" or LR's "Ice Class + Corrosion Resistant" affecting design approval.

A failure in corrosion protection can lead to:
1. **Class withdrawal** — the ship cannot legally trade.
2. **Port State Control detention** — financial penalties of **USD 30,000–100,000 per detention**.
3. **Cargo rejection** by charterers.
4. **Insurance claims denial** if negligence is established.

### 2.2 The Corrosion Control Hierarchy (Strategic Decision Tree)

The industry follows a **4-tier decision hierarchy** when designing corrosion protection:

**Tier 1 — Material Selection:** Choose inherently corrosion-resistant alloys (e.g., Cu-Ni 90/10 for seawater piping, austenitic stainless steel 316L for chemical tankers, GRP for small craft).

**Tier 2 — Design Modification:** Eliminate crevices, provide drainage, use welded instead of bolted joints, avoid galvanic couples, allow access for inspection and maintenance.

**Tier 3 — Protective Coatings & Linings:** Epoxy, polyurethane, zinc-rich primers, coal-tar epoxy for ballast tanks, antifouling for underwater hull.

**Tier 4 — Electrochemical Methods:** Cathodic Protection (sacrificial anode or impressed current) and anodic protection (rare in ships).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This hierarchy is mapped to **CO4 (Apply corrosion control methods to marine structures)** in the 2024 Scheme. Examiners expect students to justify *which tier* is most appropriate for a given scenario — a typical 7-mark sub-question.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Corrosion Rate (Mass Loss Method) | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$ | mm/year | Converts weight loss to penetration depth |
| Corrosion Rate (Faraday's Law) | $CR = \dfrac{I \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot A \cdot D}$ | mm/year | Predicts metal loss from measured current |
| Protection Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{CR_{\text{unprotected}} - CR_{\text{protected}}}{CR_{\text{unprotected}}} \times 100$ | % | Evaluates coating or CP effectiveness |
| Cathodic Protection Current Density | $i_{cp} = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | A/m² | Sizing of anode or ICCP system |
| Anode Life (Sacrificial) | $L = \dfrac{m \cdot C}{I}$ | years | Predicts replacement interval |
| Galvanic Series Potential | $\Delta E = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | V | Determines driving voltage |
| Total Cost of Corrosion | $TCoC = C_{\text{direct}} + C_{\text{indirect}}$ | USD/lifetime | Economic justification of protection |
| Coating DFT Required | $DFT_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{DFT_{\text{nominal}}}{1 - \text{Wear Factor}}$ | microns | Specifies paint thickness per zone |
| Splash Zone Corrosion Allowance | $t_{\text{add}} = CR \times \text{Design Life}$ | mm | Steel renewal margin |
| Polarization Resistance | $R_p = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}$ | Ω·cm² | Non-destructive corrosion rate measurement |

Where $K = 8.76 \times 10^4$ (conversion constant), $W$ = mass loss (mg), $A$ = exposed area (cm²), $T$ = exposure time (hr), $D$ = metal density (g/cm³), $M$ = molar mass, $n$ = electrons transferred, $F$ = Faraday's constant (**96,485 C/mol**), $C$ = anode capacity (A·hr/kg).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Every formula in the table above directly powers decisions in:

- **Naval Architecture:** Determining scantlings (rule plate thickness additions).
- **Shipyard Production Planning:** Estimating paint shop time, grit blasting cost.
- **Classification & Survey:** Acceptance criteria for renewals vs. in-place inspection.
- **Insurance Underwriting:** Premium calculation for hull & machinery.
- **Offshore Structures:** Jacket design, subsea pipeline burial depth.
- **In-service Condition Monitoring:** Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR) probes and Electrical Resistance (ER) probes feed real-time data into the Integrated Bridge System.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Connection:** The **NACE/AMPP CIP (Certified Inspector Program)** Levels 1, 2, and 3 are the global gold standard for coating inspection. A KTU graduate entering shipyards in Kochi, Mangalore, or Goa will frequently work alongside CIP-certified inspectors.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Corrosion Rate from Mass Loss

The mass-loss method is the **empirical gold standard** for measuring corrosion rate, used in ASTM G1 standard practice.

**Starting point:** A metal coupon of known mass $W_0$ is exposed to the corrosive environment for time $T$. After cleaning per ASTM G1, the final mass is $W_f$.

**Step 1 — Compute mass loss:**
The mass lost to corrosion is the difference between initial and final mass.

$$
W = W_0 - W_f
$$

**Step 2 — Express in standard units:**
Convert mass loss to milligrams and area to cm² for the KTU/K industry convention.

$$
W \text{ (mg)}, \quad A \text{ (cm}^2\text{)}, \quad T \text{ (hr)}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert mass loss to volume loss:**
Volume loss equals mass loss divided by metal density.

$$
V_{\text{loss}} = \frac{W}{D}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert volume loss to linear penetration:**
If corrosion is uniform over area $A$, the average penetration depth $p$ is volume divided by area.

$$
p = \frac{V_{\text{loss}}}{A} = \frac{W}{D \cdot A}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to annual rate:**
Divide penetration by time in hours and multiply by hours per year.

$$
CR = p \cdot \frac{8760}{T}
$$

**Step 6 — Combine into the master equation:**
Substituting all steps:

$$
CR = \frac{W}{D \cdot A} \cdot \frac{8760}{T}
$$

Bringing the constant to the front:

$$
CR = \frac{8760 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}
$$

Where $CR$ is in **mm/year** and the constant **8760** is the number of hours in a non-leap year.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference:** This is precisely the form standardized in **ASTM G1** and **NACE TM0169**. The constant $K = 8.76 \times 10^4$ in some textbooks arises from a slightly different unit choice (using g/cm³ for $D$ and cm² for $A$ produces $K = 87.6$ when $T$ is in days; the difference is unit-system-specific, not a contradiction).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Mass Loss to Corrosion Rate

**Problem (KTU-style):** A mild steel coupon of dimensions $5 \text{ cm} \times 2 \text{ cm} \times 0.5 \text{ cm}$ and density $7.86 \text{ g/cm}^3$ was exposed to seawater for **30 days**. The initial mass was 38.20 g and the final mass (after cleaning) was 37.65 g. Calculate the corrosion rate in mm/year and comment on the severity.

**Step 1 — Mass loss:**
Initial mass minus final mass gives the mass consumed by corrosion.

$$
W = 38.20 - 37.65 = 0.55 \text{ g} = 550 \text{ mg}
$$

**Step 2 — Exposed surface area:**
Six faces of the rectangular coupon, each calculated as length times width.

$$
A = 2 \cdot (5 \cdot 2) + 2 \cdot (5 \cdot 0.5) + 2 \cdot (2 \cdot 0.5)
$$

Computing each term:

$$
A = 2 \cdot 10 + 2 \cdot 2.5 + 2 \cdot 1.0
$$

$$
A = 20 + 5 + 2 = 27 \text{ cm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Exposure time in hours:**

$$
T = 30 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hr/day} = 720 \text{ hr}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the corrosion rate equation:**

$$
CR = \frac{8760 \cdot 550}{7.86 \cdot 27 \cdot 720}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute numerator:**

$$
8760 \cdot 550 = 4{,}818{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Compute denominator:**
First compute the product of density and area.

$$
7.86 \cdot 27 = 212.22
$$

Then multiply by time.

$$
212.22 \cdot 720 = 152{,}798.4
$$

**Step 7 — Final ratio:**

$$
CR = \frac{4{,}818{,}000}{152{,}798.4} = 31.53 \text{ mm/year}
$$

**Step 8 — Severity Assessment (KTU accepted scale):**
- CR < **0.025 mm/year** → Excellent (marine stainless in passive state)
- 0.025 – 0.1 mm/year → Good
- 0.1 – 0.5 mm/year → Fair
- 0.5 – 1.0 mm/year → Poor
- **> 1.0 mm/year → Severe (unacceptable for marine service)**

Our calculated **31.53 mm/year** indicates **catastrophic corrosion** — this could only occur in a heavily polluted, acidic, or highly aerated seawater environment, or if the steel was incorrectly identified.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Forgetting to convert g to mg. The constant 8760 assumes mass in **mg** and area in **cm²**. A 1000× error is the most frequent mistake in KTU numericals, instantly losing 4–5 marks.

### 3.3 Derivation 2 — Faraday's Law Corrosion Rate (Electrochemical Method)

When corrosion current $I$ can be measured (e.g., via linear polarization resistance), Faraday's law gives the theoretical mass loss.

**Step 1 — Moles of electrons transferred:**
By definition, the number of moles of electrons is the total charge divided by Faraday's constant.

$$
n_e = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles of metal atoms dissolved:**
Each metal atom $M$ losing $z$ electrons (e.g., Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻, so $z = 2$) requires $z$ moles of electrons per mole of metal.

$$
n_M = \frac{n_e}{z} = \frac{I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of metal dissolved:**
Mass equals moles times molar mass.

$$
W = n_M \cdot M = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to corrosion rate in mm/year:**
Apply the same mass-to-penetration conversion as in Derivation 1.

$$
CR = \frac{W \cdot 8760}{A \cdot T \cdot D}
$$

Substituting $W$ and using $T = t$ (consistent units):

$$
CR = \frac{I \cdot M \cdot 8760}{z \cdot F \cdot A \cdot D}
$$

Final boxed form:

$$
\boxed{\,CR = \dfrac{8760 \cdot I \cdot M}{z \cdot F \cdot A \cdot D}\,}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Protection Method

| Marine Zone | Dominant Corrosion Type | Recommended Tier 1 (Material) | Recommended Tier 3 (Coating) | Recommended Tier 4 (Electrochemical) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bottom Plate (Underwater)** | General + biofouling | Mild steel + epoxy primer | Antifouling + epoxy | ICCP or Zn/Al sacrificial anodes |
| **Boot Top / Splash Zone** | General + erosion + cyclic wet-dry | Corrosion-resistant steel (e.g., AH36) | Thick polyurethane or glass-flake epoxy | Limited use (above waterline) |
| **Ballast Tank** | General + pitting + MIC | Corrosion-resistant steel (optional) | Coal-tar epoxy or pure epoxy (PSPC) | ICCP for double-bottom tanks |
| **Superstructure (Atmosphere)** | Atmospheric corrosion | Mild steel with galvanized fasteners | Epoxy + polyurethane topcoat | None |
| **Seawater Cooling Pipe** | Galvanic + erosion-corrosion | Cu-Ni 90/10 or Ti | None (bare metal) | Possible CP |
| **Propeller / Shaft** | Cavitation + galvanic | Ni-Al bronze or Mn-Al bronze | None | Zn shaft anode, dielectric isolation |
| **Cargo Tank (Oil Tanker)** | General + pitting | Epoxy-coated mild steel | Phenolic epoxy or zinc-rich | Limited |
| **Offshore Jacket (Splash/Submerged)** | General + wave impact | Cathodic-protected steel | Splash-zone compound (e.g., neoprene) | High-capacity Al-In alloy anodes |

### 3.5 Derivation 3 — Total Cost of Corrosion (Economic Justification)

**Step 1 — Identify direct costs:**
Sum all planned, visible expenditure categories.

$$
C_{\text{direct}} = C_{\text{paint}} + C_{\text{anode}} + C_{\text{steel renewal}} + C_{\text{inhibitor}} + C_{\text{inspection}}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify indirect costs:**
Sum all hidden, unplanned, opportunity-based losses.

$$
C_{\text{indirect}} = C_{\text{downtime}} + C_{\text{drydock delay}} + C_{\text{insurance}} + C_{\text{cargo loss}} + C_{\text{environmental fine}}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Cost of Corrosion:**

$$
TCoC = C_{\text{direct}} + C_{\text{indirect}}
$$

**Step 4 — Protection Benefit Ratio (PBR):**
This is the metric used in shipyard CAPEX justification. If unprotected cost is $TCoC_u$ and protected cost is $TCoC_p$ (with $C_p$ being the initial protection investment):

$$
PBR = \frac{TCoC_u - TCoC_p}{C_p}
$$

A **PBR > 3** is the industry threshold for a "must-implement" corrosion protection measure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Bench:** A typical VLCC's hull protection investment is around **USD 800,000–1,200,000** at newbuild. The avoided cost over 25 years is **USD 3–5 million**. Thus PBR ≈ **3–5**, comfortably above the implementation threshold.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"Define the term 'cost of corrosion' and list its direct and indirect components as applicable to shipbuilding."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **cost of corrosion** is the total economic loss sustained by an asset due to corrosion-related degradation over its operational lifetime, comprising direct and indirect components.

**Direct costs (1.5 marks):** Repainting and surface preparation, sacrificial anode replacement, steel renewal (crop-and-replate), corrosion inhibitor dosing, routine inspection, dry-dock blasting and recoating.

**Indirect costs (1.5 marks):** Vessel downtime, off-hire revenue loss, cargo delays, late-delivery penalties, environmental fines, insurance premium escalation, and loss of class notation affecting charter hire rates. For a VLCC, the TCoC over 25 years typically ranges from **USD 1.6 to 2.7 million**.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"List the major IMO and classification society regulations governing corrosion protection in new ships."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **IMO Resolution A.744(18)** (1 mark) — Periodical survey of corrosion protection systems for tankers and bulk carriers.
2. **IMO PSPC — Performance Standard for Protective Coatings** (1 mark) — Mandatory since 2008 for ballast tanks of new ships ≥ 500 GT and double-side skin spaces of bulk carriers.
3. **IACS Unified Requirements UR S21 and UR S22** (0.5 mark) — Hull girder strength and corrosion addition in scantlings.
4. **MARPOL Annex VI** (0.5 mark) — Air pollution prevention driving VOC limits in coatings and ballast water management.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **three pillars of importance** of corrosion protection in shipbuilding, with at least one quantitative example for each.

**Model Solution:**

The importance of corrosion protection in shipbuilding rests on three pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Economic Importance (2.5 marks):**
The **Total Cost of Corrosion** is split into direct (planned) and indirect (hidden) costs. For a VLCC, the direct cost is approximately **USD 1.2–1.8 million** over 25 years, and indirect cost adds another **30–50%** through downtime, off-hire, and cargo delays. The global annual cost of corrosion is **3–5% of GDP** (NACE IMPACT). Without protection, the vessel would need premature scrapping at 12–15 years, losing 50% of its design life.

**Pillar 2 — Safety and Structural Integrity (2.5 marks):**
Corrosion reduces hull plate thickness, creating **stress concentrators** that propagate fatigue cracks. A **10% thickness reduction lowers hull girder bending strength by ~20%**. Specific hazards include pitting in ballast tanks (MIC-driven), galvanic corrosion at the Cu-Ni propeller–steel hull interface, and erosion-corrosion in bow thruster tunnels. The **SOLAS Regulation II-1/3-1** and **IACS UR S21** mandate a corrosion addition of **0.5–1.0 mm per side** in the midship section modulus calculation. Unprotected failures have caused disasters like **MV Prestige (2002)**.

**Pillar 3 — Environmental and Regulatory Importance (2 marks):**
Modern ships must comply with **IMO PSPC** (mandatory epoxy coating spec for ballast tanks since 2008), **MARPOL Annex VI** (VOC and ballast water rules), and **EU SRR 1257/2013** (hazardous material inventory). A corrosion-induced class withdrawal makes the vessel legally un-tradeable, causing **Port State Control detention** penalties of **USD 30,000–100,000** per event. Additionally, oil spills from corroded tankers can cost **EUR 1 billion+** in cleanup, as evidenced by the Prestige and Erika incidents.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A structural steel plate of area **0.5 m²** and original thickness **12 mm** is used in a ballast tank. After 8 years of service, ultrasonic thickness gauging shows the minimum remaining thickness is **10.4 mm**. The plate density is **7.85 g/cm³**. Calculate:
(i) The average corrosion rate in mm/year.
(ii) The total mass of steel lost due to corrosion.
(iii) Comment on the severity and recommend the most appropriate protection strategy.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Corrosion Rate (3 marks):**
Total thickness loss is the difference between original and remaining thickness.

$$
\Delta t = 12 - 10.4 = 1.6 \text{ mm}
$$

Divide by service time:

$$
CR = \frac{\Delta t}{T} = \frac{1.6}{8} = 0.20 \text{ mm/year}
$$

**(ii) Mass Loss (3 marks):**
Volume of steel lost equals area times thickness loss, with unit conversion from mm to cm.

$$
V_{\text{loss}} = A \cdot \Delta t = 0.5 \text{ m}^2 \times 1.6 \text{ mm}
$$

Converting area to cm² and thickness to cm:

$$
V_{\text{loss}} = 5000 \text{ cm}^2 \times 0.16 \text{ cm} = 800 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Mass loss equals volume times density:

$$
W = V_{\text{loss}} \cdot D = 800 \cdot 7.85 = 6280 \text{ g} = 6.28 \text{ kg}
$$

**(iii) Severity and Recommendation (1 mark):**
A corrosion rate of **0.20 mm/year** falls in the **"Fair"** band (0.1–0.5 mm/year), but for a ballast tank — a critical structural space — this is **borderline acceptable**. If continued, the plate would lose another **3.2 mm over the next 16 years**, leaving only **7.2 mm**, which is below the **6–7 mm** rejection threshold used by classification societies. The recommended strategy is **Tier 3 + Tier 4 combined**: blast the surface to **Sa 2.5**, apply a **PSPC-compliant pure epoxy coating (320 μm DFT)**, and install **aluminum sacrificial anodes** in the double-bottom for cathodic protection. Future ultrasonic thickness measurement (UTM) surveys should be scheduled every **2.5 years** to monitor progression.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **4-tier corrosion control hierarchy** used in ship design. For each tier, give **one specific marine application example**.

**Model Solution:**

The shipbuilding industry applies an **integrated 4-tier corrosion control strategy** during the design phase. Each tier adds a layer of defense.

**Tier 1 — Material Selection (2 marks):**
Choosing inherently corrosion-resistant alloys. **Example:** **Cu-Ni 90/10** alloy for seawater cooling pipes due to its excellent biofouling resistance and stable corrosion rate of **~0.025 mm/year** in clean seawater.

**Tier 2 — Design Modification (1.5 marks):**
Altering geometry to eliminate corrosion-prone features. **Example:** Replacing **bolted lap joints** with **full-penetration welded seams** in the bilge keel area to remove crevices that would otherwise initiate crevice corrosion.

**Tier 3 — Protective Coatings (2 marks):**
Applying surface barrier systems. **Example:** **PSPC-compliant epoxy coating (320 μm DFT)** on all internal ballast tank surfaces of newbuild ships, mandatory under **IMO Resolution MSC.215(82)** since 2008.

**Tier 4 — Electrochemical Methods (1.5 marks):**
Imposing an external electrical or sacrificial potential. **Example:** **Aluminum-zinc-indium sacrificial anodes** bolted to the underwater hull, providing cathodic protection to the steel hull with a design life of **4–5 years** between replacements.

The four tiers are **complementary, not alternative**. A well-designed ship uses all four. A ship that relies on Tier 1 alone will still suffer galvanic and MIC attack; one that relies on Tier 3 alone will fail when the coating is mechanically damaged.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Zn sacrificial anode of mass **5.2 kg** is coupled to a steel hull. The galvanic current measured is **0.45 A**. The electrochemical capacity of Zn is **780 A·hr/kg**, and the utilization factor is **0.85**.
(i) Calculate the **theoretical life** of the anode.
(ii) Calculate the **actual service life** accounting for utilization.
(iii) If the anode is to be replaced at **80% of actual life**, what is the **replacement interval** in months?

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Theoretical Life (2.5 marks):**
Theoretical life is the anode's total available charge divided by the current drawn.

$$
L_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{m \cdot C}{I}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
L_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{5.2 \cdot 780}{0.45}
$$

Compute numerator first:

$$
5.2 \cdot 780 = 4056
$$

Then divide:

$$
L_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{4056}{0.45} = 9013.3 \text{ hours}
$$

Convert hours to years by dividing by 8760:

$$
L_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{9013.3}{8760} = 1.029 \text{ years}
$$

**(ii) Actual Service Life (2.5 marks):**
The actual life is the theoretical life multiplied by the utilization factor, since not all of the anode's mass is consumed before the protected potential is lost.

$$
L_{\text{actual}} = L_{\text{theoretical}} \cdot \eta
$$

$$
L_{\text{actual}} = 1.029 \cdot 0.85 = 0.875 \text{ years}
$$

**(iii) Replacement Interval (2 marks):**
The replacement is scheduled at 80% of the actual life.

$$
L_{\text{replacement}} = 0.80 \cdot L_{\text{actual}} = 0.80 \cdot 0.875 = 0.700 \text{ years}
$$

Converting years to months:

$$
L_{\text{replacement}} = 0.700 \cdot 12 = 8.4 \text{ months}
$$

**Conclusion:** The zinc anode must be replaced approximately **every 8 months** to maintain continuous cathodic protection. This aligns with industry practice for commercial vessel anodes in tropical waters, where replacement is typically scheduled during intermediate survey intervals.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (4-mark penalty):** Mixing mm and cm without conversion in numericals. Always declare units at every step.
> 2. **Forgetting the utilization factor (η):** Theoretical life and actual life are different. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **No coating thickness mention:** When asked about ballast tank protection, "apply epoxy" alone is **incomplete** for full marks. Always specify **PSPC compliance + 320 μm DFT + Sa 2.5 surface preparation**.
> 4. **Vague regulatory references:** Stating "IMO rules apply" scores zero. You must name the specific convention: **IMO PSPC, A.744(18), MARPOL Annex VI, IACS UR S21**.
> 5. **Skipping the 4-tier framework:** For a 14-mark question on corrosion control strategy, missing the integrated 4-tier hierarchy is a structural gap that costs **3–4 marks** instantly.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Corrosion control** is a design-time decision, not a maintenance-time fix.
- The importance of corrosion protection rests on **three pillars**: Economic, Safety-Structural, and Environmental-Regulatory.
- The **4-tier hierarchy**: Material → Design → Coatings → Electrochemical — all four are used together, never in isolation.
- The **mass-loss corrosion rate formula** is $CR = \dfrac{8760 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ in mm/year with $W$ in mg, $A$ in cm², $T$ in hr.
- The **Faraday's law corrosion rate formula** is $CR = \dfrac{8760 \cdot I \cdot M}{z \cdot F \cdot A \cdot D}$ in mm/year.
- **Faraday's constant** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol — commit to memory.
- **Constant 8760** = hours in a non-leap year.
- **Splash zone corrosion rate** = 3 to 5 mm/year unprotected; ballast tank = 0.3 to 1.0 mm/year unprotected.
- **Service life** of protected ship = 25 to 30 years; unprotected = 12 to 15 years.
- **Total Cost of Corrosion (TCoC)** for a VLCC = **USD 1.6 to 2.7 million** over 25 years.
- **Direct costs** are visible/planned; **indirect costs** are hidden (downtime, off-hire, fines).
- **Protection Benefit Ratio (PBR)** must exceed **3** to justify investment.
- **Corrosion severity scale** (mm/year): <0.025 excellent | 0.025–0.1 good | 0.1–0.5 fair | 0.5–1.0 poor | >1.0 severe.
- **IMO PSPC** is mandatory for ballast tanks ≥ 500 GT newbuild since 2008.
- **IACS UR S21** corrosion addition = **0.5 to 1.0 mm per side** in midship scantlings.
- **MV Prestige (2002)** — corrosion-induced tanker failure; **77,000 tonnes** of oil spill; **EUR 1 billion** cleanup.
- **Global cost of corrosion** = **3 to 5% of GDP** annually (NACE IMPACT).
- **Sacrificial anode life** = $\dfrac{m \cdot C \cdot \eta}{I}$ in years; **Zn capacity = 780 A·hr/kg**; **Al capacity ≈ 2,500 A·hr/kg**.
- **Cu-Ni 90/10** is the preferred alloy for marine seawater piping.
- **PSPC epoxy DFT** = **320 μm**; **surface prep** = **Sa 2.5**; **roughness** = 40–70 μm.
- **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Reg 3-1** governs corrosion addition in hull strength.
- Always quote **3 numbers** in economic answers: cost in USD, life extension in years, and PBR.
- A coating specification answer that omits **DFT, surface prep standard, and compliance regulation** is incomplete for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measures to minimise corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Measures to minimise corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion Minimisation (KTU 2024 Definition):** A set of engineering, metallurgical, electrochemical, and chemical strategies employed to **reduce the rate of degradation** of a metal (or alloy) caused by its interaction with the marine environment, thereby extending the **service life**, preserving **structural integrity**, and ensuring the **fitness-for-purpose** of ship structural components, propulsion systems, and auxiliary machinery.

In naval architecture and marine engineering, the philosophy of corrosion minimisation rests on **four fundamental pillars**, often referred to as the *Corrosion Control Pyramid*:

| Pillar | Strategy | Core Idea |
|---|---|---|
| **P1** | Material Selection | Choose inherently corrosion-resistant alloys |
| **P2** | Design Modification | Eliminate crevices, galvanic couples, stagnant zones |
| **P3** | Surface Protection | Barrier coatings + cathodic/anodic protection |
| **P4** | Environmental Control | De-aeration, inhibitors, dehumidification |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** In shipbuilding, **P3 (Surface Protection)** carries the maximum weightage in the syllabus because it directly interfaces with **painting specifications, sacrificial anode design, and ICCP system sizing** — all of which are examinable.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **ship's hull as a human body**:

- **Material selection** is like choosing a person with naturally strong immunity (using *corten steel, aluminium-bronze, or duplex stainless steel* instead of plain mild steel).
- **Design modification** is like *good hygiene* — keeping rainwater off your skin, drying between toes (avoiding *crevice corrosion* in riveted joints).
- **Coatings + Cathodic Protection** are like *sunscreen + an umbrella* — a physical barrier *plus* an electrochemical shield.
- **Environmental control** is like *living in a clean house* — controlling humidity and salt in the engine room.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Students:** Remember the mnemonic **"M-D-C-E"** — **M**aterial, **D**esign, **C**oatings (with CP/AP), **E**nvironment.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Corrosion Minimisation Measures

The complete taxonomy as per KTU 2024 Module 4 is illustrated below:

### A. **Active (Electrochemical) Methods**
These work by **manipulating the electrochemical potential** of the metal surface.

1. **Cathodic Protection (CP)** — Pushes metal potential *more negative* (cathodic).
   - (i) Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (**SACP**) — *Galvanic Anode Method*
   - (ii) Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (**ICCP**) — *Impressed Current Method*
2. **Anodic Protection (AP)** — Pushes metal potential *into the passive region* (used for alloys forming passive films, e.g., stainless steels in H₂SO₄ tanks).

### B. **Passive (Barrier) Methods**
These **physically isolate** the metal from the corrosive medium.

3. **Organic Coatings** — Paints, epoxies, polyurethanes, vinyls.
4. **Inorganic Coatings** — Zinc silicate, chromates, ceramics.
5. **Metallic Coatings** — Hot-dip galvanising, electroplating, sherardising, metal spraying (Zn, Al, Zn-Al).
6. **Conversion Coatings** — Phosphating, chromating, anodising.

### C. **Metallurgical & Design Methods**

7. **Material Selection** — Corrosion-resistant alloys (CRAs), non-metallics.
8. **Design Modification** — Drainage, welding vs. riveting, weld profile, avoiding dissimilar metal contact.

### D. **Chemical / Environmental Methods**

9. **Corrosion Inhibitors** — Anodic, cathodic, mixed, vapour-phase.
10. **Environmental Modification** — De-aeration, pH control, dehumidification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant to Remember:**
> **Faraday's Constant F = 96,485 C/mol (≈ 96,500 C/mol)** — used universally in cathodic protection mass calculations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Methodology

### 2.1.1 Material Selection

The most fundamental minimisation measure is choosing a material whose **corrosion rate in seawater** is intrinsically low.

**Decision Hierarchy for Shipbuilding:**
1. **Plain Carbon Steel** → unprotected, ~**0.1–0.5 mm/yr** in seawater (baseline).
2. **CorTen Steel** (weathering) → forms protective rust layer, slower progression.
3. **Austenitic Stainless Steel (316L)** → ~**0.01 mm/yr**, excellent for piping.
4. **Duplex Stainless Steel (2205)** → superior chloride resistance, used in propeller shafts.
5. **Copper-Nickel Alloys (90/10, 70/30)** → marine biofouling resistance, seawater piping.
6. **Aluminium Bronzes, Nickel-Aluminium Bronzes (NAB)** → propellers, pump impellers.
7. **Titanium Alloys** → seawater heat exchangers, condensers (Grade 2).

---

### 2.1.2 Design Considerations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** "A well-designed structure is half-protected from corrosion."

| Design Flaw | Corrosion Type Induced | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Sharp corners, poor weld profile | Stress corrosion cracking | Grind smooth, profile ratio ≥ 3:1 |
| Riveted lap joints | Crevice corrosion | Use continuous welding |
| Direct Cu–steel contact | Galvanic corrosion | Use dielectric (PVC/neoprene) gaskets |
| Stagnant water in tanks | Microbial Induced Corrosion (MIC) | Provide drainage slope ≥ 1:100 |
| Welds flush with parent metal | Weld decay | Use low-carbon / stabilised grades (e.g., 316L, 321) |

---

### 2.1.3 Cathodic Protection (CP) — The Heart of Marine Corrosion Control

#### A. Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)

**Principle:** Connect a *more active* metal (anode) electrically to the structure to be protected (cathode). The sacrificial anode **corrodes preferentially**, protecting the hull.

**Common Anode Materials for Marine Use:**
- **Zinc (Zn)** — most common, E° ≈ **−1.10 V vs SCE**
- **Aluminium alloys (Al-Zn-In)** — E° ≈ **−1.05 V vs SCE**, high capacity (~2830 A·h/kg)
- **Magnesium (Mg)** — E° ≈ **−1.55 V vs SCE**, used in low-conductivity media (soil, freshwater)

**Advantages:** No external power, simple, reliable, self-regulating.
**Limitations:** Low driving voltage, limited current output, anode replacement needed.

#### B. Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)

**Principle:** An external **DC power source** drives current from inert anodes (Pt-coated Ti, mixed metal oxide, lead-silver) onto the hull, making the hull cathodic.

**Advantages:** Adjustable voltage/current, suitable for **large structures** (tankers, FPSOs, offshore platforms).
**Limitations:** Requires power, prone to overprotection → hydrogen embrittlement.

---

### 2.1.4 Anodic Protection (AP)

Used only for metals that exhibit **active-passive behaviour** in specific environments (e.g., stainless steel in H₂SO₄, carbon steel in caustic). A *potentiostat* maintains the metal in the **passive region** of its anodic polarisation curve.

---

### 2.1.5 Protective Coatings

A ship's hull coating system typically comprises:
1. **Shop Primer** (e.g., inorganic zinc silicate, 15–25 µm)
2. **Anti-corrosive Primer** (epoxy, 100–200 µm)
3. **Tie Coat / Intermediate** (epoxy, 50–100 µm)
4. **Anti-fouling Topcoat** (for underwater hull) OR **Finish Coat** (for topsides, polyurethane)

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Master These for KTU ESE**

### Faraday's Law of Electrolysis (Cornerstone of CP Design)

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $m$ | Mass of metal dissolved/deposited | kg |
| $I$ | Current | A |
| $t$ | Time | s |
| $M$ | Molar mass | kg/mol |
| $n$ | Valency (electrons transferred) | — |
| $F$ | Faraday's constant | 96,485 C/mol |

### Theoretical Current Requirement for Cathodic Protection

$$I_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{A \cdot i_{\text{prot}}}{1000}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $I_{\text{theoretical}}$ | Total protective current | A |
| $A$ | Wetted surface area to be protected | m² |
| $i_{\text{prot}}$ | Protective current density | mA/m² |

### Coating Thickness Conversion

$$\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \text{VS}$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\text{DFT}$ | Dry Film Thickness |
| $\text{WFT}$ | Wet Film Thickness |
| $\text{VS}$ | Volume Solids (fraction, e.g., 0.65) |

### Theoretical Spread of Cathodic Protection (Important for Hull Design)

$$R = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{2 L}{r}\right) - 1 \right]$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $R$ | Electrode-to-electrolyte resistance |
| $\rho$ | Seawater resistivity (typically **0.20–0.25 Ω·m**) |
| $L$ | Length of anode |
| $r$ | Radius of anode |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

| Application | Method Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Ship's underwater hull | ICCP + epoxy coating + SACP (backup) | Long-term, low-maintenance protection |
| Ballast tanks | Zinc-rich epoxy + SACP anodes | Replace wet-dry cycling, no external power |
| Seawater cooling piping | Cu-Ni (90/10) + anodic inhibitor dosing | Biofouling + corrosion resistance |
| Propeller (Ni-Al bronze) | Cathodic protection of hull (via shaft bonding) | Avoid galvanic coupling with steel hull |
| Offshore platform substructure | ICCP + fusion-bonded epoxy (FBE) coating | 30-year design life |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Mass of Sacrificial Anode Required

### Problem Setup
A ship's seawater ballast tank has wetted surface area **A = 200 m²**. A protective current density of **i_prot = 150 mA/m²** is to be maintained for **T = 4 years**. Aluminium anodes (valency n = 3, molar mass M = 27 g/mol) are used.

### Step 1 — Total Current Required

By definition, current density times area gives the protective current:

$$I_{\text{prot}} = A \cdot i_{\text{prot}}$$

Substituting the given numerical values:

$$I_{\text{prot}} = 200 \times 150 = 30{,}000 \text{ mA} = 30 \text{ A}$$

### Step 2 — Total Charge Passed Over Service Life

Charge $Q$ is current times time. Convert **4 years into seconds**:

$$t = 4 \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600 = 126{,}144{,}000 \text{ s}$$

Therefore the total charge transferred is:

$$Q = I \cdot t = 30 \times 126{,}144{,}000 = 3.784 \times 10^{9} \text{ C}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Faraday's Law

$$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

Substituting values:

$$m = \frac{3.784 \times 10^{9} \times 27}{3 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$m = \frac{1.0218 \times 10^{11}}{2.8946 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$m \approx 353{,}053 \text{ g} \approx 353 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 4 — Add Utilisation Factor (Engineering Reality)

In practice, only **~80–90%** of an anode is consumed before it must be replaced. Taking **η = 0.85** (utilisation factor):

$$m_{\text{actual}} = \frac{m}{\eta} = \frac{353}{0.85} \approx 415.3 \text{ kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Approximately **415 kg of aluminium anodes** is required to protect the ballast tank for 4 years. This matches typical shipboard anode replacement schedules.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Coating DFT from WFT and Practical Coverage

### Problem Setup
A marine epoxy primer has **Volume Solids VS = 60%** and is applied at **Wet Film Thickness WFT = 250 µm**. Calculate the **DFT** and the **theoretical coverage** per litre.

### Step 1 — Compute DFT

$$\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \text{VS}$$

$$\text{DFT} = 250 \times 0.60 = 150 \text{ µm}$$

### Step 2 — Theoretical Coverage (m²/L)

$$\text{Coverage} = \frac{10 \cdot \text{VS}}{\text{DFT (in mils)}}$$

First convert 150 µm to mils:
$$1 \text{ mil} = 25.4 \text{ µm} \implies 150 \text{ µm} = 5.91 \text{ mils}$$

$$\text{Coverage} = \frac{10 \times 0.60}{5.91} = \frac{6.0}{5.91} \approx 1.015 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}$$

### Step 3 — Practical Coverage with Loss Factor (LF = 1.30)

$$\text{Practical Coverage} = \frac{\text{Theoretical Coverage}}{\text{LF}} = \frac{1.015}{1.30} \approx 0.78 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always quote **DFT in µm** and coverage in **m²/L** with proper units — examiners award marks for unit consistency.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Corrosion Minimisation Method

| Criterion | Material Selection | Coatings | SACP | ICCP | Inhibitors |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Cost** | Medium–High | Low–Medium | Medium | High | Low |
| **Running Cost** | Negligible | Periodic recoating | Anode replacement | Power + monitoring | Chemical dosing |
| **Suitability for Marine Hull** | Limited (hull is steel) | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | Not applicable |
| **Suitability for Ballast Tank** | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★ |
| **Maintenance Frequency** | None | 5–10 yrs | 3–5 yrs | Continuous | Weekly |
| **Skill Required** | Designer | Applicator | Fitter | Electrical Engineer | Chemist |
| **Risk of Overprotection** | None | None | Low | High (H-embrittlement) | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** For ship hulls, the **industry-standard practice** is a **dual system** — *Epoxy Coating + ICCP with Zinc SACP as backup* (per **DNV-RP-B401** and **IMO PSPC** standards).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cathodic Protection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Hull to be Protected]) --> sizeA{Length of structure?}
    sizeA -- Less than 30 m --> sapcA[SACP System]
    sizeA -- More than 30 m --> iccpQ{External power available?}
    iccpQ -- Yes --> iccpA[ICCP System]
    iccpQ -- No --> sapcA
    sapcA --> anodecalcA[Calculate anode mass using Faraday Law]
    anodecalcA --> installA[Mount Zn or Al anodes on hull]
    iccpA --> powerspecA[Select transformer-rectifier rating]
    powerspecA --> inertanodeA[Install inert anodes Pt Ti or MMO]
    inertanodeA --> refA[Place Ag AgCl reference electrode]
    refA --> monitorA[Continuous potential monitoring]
    installA --> doneA([Protected Hull])
    monitorA --> doneA
```

---

## 4.2 Impressed Current Cathodic Protection — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC[AC Mains Supply] --> TR[Transformer Rectifier Unit]
    TR --> CTRL[Controller with Potentiostat]
    CTRL --> ANODE[Inert Anode Pt Ti MMO Lead Silver]
    CTRL --> REF[Reference Electrode Ag AgCl]
    REF --> FEED[Feedback Signal to Controller]
    ANODE --> SEAWATER[Seawater Electrolyte]
    SEAWATER --> HULL[Ship Hull Cathode]
    HULL --> SEAWATER
    HULL --> CABLE[Negative Cable Return]
    CABLE --> TR
```

---

## 4.3 Hull Coating System — Layered Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SEAWATER2[Seawater Environment] --> AF[Anti Fouling Topcoat 100 to 200 micrometre]
    AF --> TC[Tie Coat 50 micrometre]
    TC --> AP[Anti Corrosive Epoxy Primer 150 to 200 micrometre]
    AP --> SP[Shop Primer Inorganic Zinc Silicate 20 micrometre]
    SP --> STEEL[Steel Hull Substrate]
    SP -.Optional Zinc Anode.-> SACP[Sacrificial Anode Backup]
    AP -.ICCP System.-> ICCP2[Impressed Current Cathodic Protection]
```

---

## 4.4 Galvanic Series in Seawater — Engineering Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ANODE_END
        MG[Most Active Magnesium]
        ZN[Zinc]
        AL[Aluminium Alloys]
        CS[Mild Steel]
        AS[Aluminium Bronze]
    end
    subgraph MID
        PB[Lead]
        SN[Tin]
        NI[Nickel]
        CU[Copper]
    end
    subgraph CATHODE_END
        GR[Graphite]
        TI[Titanium]
        SS[Stainless Steel Passive]
        AU[Most Noble Gold Platinum]
    end
    MG --> ZN --> AL --> CS --> AS --> PB --> SN --> NI --> CU --> GR --> TI --> SS --> AU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Takeaway:** Any metal to the **left** in this series will corrode and protect any metal to the **right** when electrically coupled in seawater — this is the basis of **SACP**.

---

## 4.5 Coating Failure Mode Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    coat[Coating System] --> blister[Blistering]
    coat --> crack[Cracking]
    coat --> delam[Delamination]
    coat --> rust[Rust Spotting]
    blister --> osc[Osmotic Pressure Beneath Film]
    crack --> uv[UV Degradation]
    crack --> mech[Mechanical Stress]
    delam --> prep[Poor Surface Preparation]
    delam --> cont[Contamination]
    rust --> breach[Coating Breach or Holiday]
    rust --> CP[Demand on Cathodic Protection]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP) and Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP). Mention two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SACP | ICCP |
|---|---|---|
| Driving Force | Galvanic potential difference | External DC power source |
| Anode Material | Active metal (Zn, Al, Mg) | Inert (Pt-coated Ti, MMO, Pb-Ag) |
| Current Output | Limited (~1–5 A typical) | Adjustable up to hundreds of amps |
| Installation Cost | Low | High |
| Maintenance | Replace anodes periodically | Monitor rectifier, reference electrode |

**SACP Advantages:** No external power, simple, self-regulating.
**ICCP Advantages:** Adjustable output, suitable for large structures, single installation lasts longer.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three corrosion-resistant alloys used in marine applications and state one specific application for each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **90/10 Copper-Nickel** → Seawater piping (resistant to biofouling).
2. **Nickel-Aluminium Bronze (NAB)** → Ship propellers (cavitation + corrosion resistance).
3. **Austenitic Stainless Steel 316L** → Chemical cargo tanks (chloride resistance).
4. *(Bonus)* **Titanium Grade 2** → Seawater heat exchanger tubes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the principle of **cathodic protection** with a neat sketch. Describe the **Sacrificial Anode Method** in detail, listing suitable anode materials and their characteristic electrochemical potentials in seawater.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A steel seawater ballast tank of surface area **250 m²** is to be protected by **zinc anodes (Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻)**. The required protective current density is **120 mA/m²**. Calculate:
   - (i) The total protective current required.
   - (ii) The mass of zinc consumed in **3 years**.
   - (iii) The actual anode mass required if utilisation factor is **0.80**.

**Given:** M(Zn) = 65.4 g/mol, n = 2, F = 96,485 C/mol.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) — Principle & SACP Explanation:**

**Principle:** Cathodic protection works on **electrochemistry** — when a metal is forced to become the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell, **anodic dissolution (corrosion) is suppressed**. The structure is connected to a more electronegative metal (anode), which corrodes in preference, "sacrificing" itself.

**SACP Working:**
- A more active metal (e.g., Zn, E° = −1.10 V) is electrically bonded to the steel hull (E° ≈ −0.65 V in seawater).
- In the presence of seawater (electrolyte), a galvanic cell forms.
- Zinc → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (oxidation at anode, corrodes).
- 2H₂O + O₂ + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻ (reduction at cathode, hull protected).
- Electrons flow from Zn anode → steel hull, suppressing Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ reaction.

**Anode Materials:**

| Anode | E° vs SCE (V) | Capacity (A·h/kg) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Magnesium | −1.55 | 1230 | Soil, freshwater |
| Zinc | −1.10 | 820 | Seawater (ships, pipelines) |
| Aluminium-Zn-In | −1.05 | 2830 | Seawater (modern ships, ballast tanks) |

**Suitable Anode Materials:** Zinc, Aluminium alloys (Al-Zn-In), Magnesium alloys.
**Selection Criteria:** Driving voltage, electrochemical capacity, environment (seawater vs soil).

---

**(b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Total Protective Current:**

$$I = A \cdot i_{\text{prot}} = 250 \times 120 = 30{,}000 \text{ mA} = 30 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Time in Seconds for 3 Years:**

$$t = 3 \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600 = 9.4608 \times 10^{7} \text{ s}$$

**Total Charge:**

$$Q = I \cdot t = 30 \times 9.4608 \times 10^{7} = 2.838 \times 10^{9} \text{ C}$$

**Mass of Zinc Consumed:**

$$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{2.838 \times 10^{9} \times 65.4}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$m = \frac{1.856 \times 10^{11}}{1.9297 \times 10^{5}} = 9.617 \times 10^{5} \text{ g} \approx 961.7 \text{ kg}$$

**(iii) Actual Anode Mass with Utilisation Factor η = 0.80:**

$$m_{\text{actual}} = \frac{m}{\eta} = \frac{961.7}{0.80} = 1202.1 \text{ kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** (i) 30 A, (ii) ≈ **962 kg**, (iii) ≈ **1202 kg of zinc anodes.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the **principle and types of protective coatings** used for ship hulls. Explain with a sketch the **multi-layer coating system** specified under **IMO PSPC** for ballast tanks.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss **anodic protection** as a corrosion minimisation method. State two conditions under which it is preferred over cathodic protection, and give two practical examples from the marine/process industry.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) — Protective Coatings for Ship Hulls:**

**Principle:** Coatings form a **physical and chemical barrier** between the metal substrate and the corrosive environment (seawater, atmosphere, cargo chemicals). A good marine coating must possess:
- **Adhesion** to substrate
- **Impermeability** to water, oxygen, ions
- **Chemical resistance**
- **Mechanical durability** against impact, abrasion

**Types of Coatings:**

| Type | Examples | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Organic** | Epoxy, polyurethane, vinyl, alkyd | Hulls, topsides, tanks |
| **Inorganic** | Zinc silicate, chromate, ceramic | High-temp, primer coats |
| **Metallic** | Hot-dip galvanising, Zn/Al spray, electroplating | Substrate protection |
| **Conversion** | Phosphating, chromating, anodising | Surface prep + first layer |

**IMO PSPC Multi-Layer Coating System (Ballast Tanks):**

- **Primer:** Zinc-rich epoxy (50–75 µm) — sacrificial cathodic action
- **Main Coat 1:** Epoxy (150 µm) — barrier protection
- **Main Coat 2:** Epoxy (150 µm) — total DFT ≥ 320 µm
- **Stripe Coat** on edges, welds, corners — extra thickness
- **Surface Prep:** Sa 2.5 blast cleaning (ISO 8501-1)

*(Sketch: refer to Section 4.3 layered schematic)*

---

**(b) — Anodic Protection:**

**Principle:** Anodic protection is applicable only to metals that **passivate** in specific environments (active-passive behaviour). A **potentiostat** maintains the metal's electrochemical potential in the **passive region** of the anodic polarisation curve, where the metal is thermodynamically stable due to formation of a thin protective oxide film.

**Working:** A small anodic current is impressed using a **cathode** (typically platinum or stainless steel) and the metal to be protected as the **anode**. The reference electrode monitors potential continuously.

**Conditions Where AP is Preferred Over CP:**
1. When the electrolyte is **highly conductive** (e.g., concentrated H₂SO₄, caustic solutions) and CP would require very high currents.
2. When the metal **cannot be cathodically protected without hydrogen embrittlement** (e.g., high-strength steels in acid).
3. When the electrolyte promotes the formation of a **stable passive film** that drastically reduces corrosion current.

**Marine / Process Industry Examples:**
1. **Stainless steel storage tanks** for concentrated sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄).
2. **Carbon steel caustic storage tanks** for sodium hydroxide (NaOH).
3. **Sulphuric acid coolers** in fertiliser and chemical plants.

**Advantages:** Low current requirement after passivation, suitable for aggressive acids.
**Limitations:** Requires potentiostat, restricted to passivating metals + electrolytes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer Summary:** (a) Coatings: organic/inorganic/metallic + IMO PSPC system; (b) AP: principle + conditions + 2 examples.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Faraday's Law Error:** Students often use M in **kg/mol** instead of **g/mol** without adjusting F. Always check unit consistency: if M is in **g/mol**, then **m will be in grams**.
>
> 2. **Time Conversion:** Forgetting to convert **years to seconds** — leading to an answer off by a factor of **~3.15 × 10⁷**. KTU examiners deduct **full 2–3 marks** for this.
>
> 3. **Confusing SACP vs ICCP:** Writing *"ICCP uses sacrificial anodes"* or *"SACP needs external power"* — these are **direct contradictions** and result in **zero credit** for that sub-part.
>
> 4. **Skipping Utilisation Factor:** Real anode calculations **must** include η (0.80–0.90). Ignoring it loses **1 mark**.
>
> 5. **No Units in Final Answer:** Always write "**30 A**" not just "30". Units carry marks.
>
> 6. **Galvanic Series Confusion:** Memorising the series left-to-right = *anode to cathode* is essential. Writing it reversed is a common error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Measures to Minimise Corrosion (Module 4)**

### 🔑 Must-Know Definitions
- **Corrosion minimisation** = reducing degradation rate, not necessarily eliminating it.
- **SACP** = sacrificial (galvanic) anode CP.
- **ICCP** = impressed current CP (external DC source).
- **Anodic Protection** = holds metal in passive region via potentiostat.

### 📐 Must-Know Formulas
- **Faraday's Law:** $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$
- **Protective Current:** $I_{\text{prot}} = A \cdot i_{\text{prot}}$
- **Coating DFT:** $\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \text{VS}$
- **Coverage Rate:** $\text{m}^2/\text{L} = \frac{10 \cdot \text{VS}}{\text{DFT (mils)}}$

### ⚓ Marine Engineering Specifics
- Seawater resistivity ρ ≈ **0.20–0.25 Ω·m**
- Typical CP current density for bare steel hull in seawater: **100–150 mA/m²**
- For coated hull: **5–30 mA/m²**
- Aluminium anode capacity ≈ **2830 A·h/kg** (vs Zinc ≈ 820 A·h/kg)
- IMO PSPC minimum ballast tank coating DFT ≈ **320 µm**

### 🧠 Design Mnemonics
- **4 Pillars:** **M**aterial – **D**esign – **C**oatings – **E**nvironment (**M-D-C-E**)
- **SACP Anode Choice:** Zn (seawater), Al (seawater + high capacity), Mg (soil/freshwater)
- **Galvanic Series Rule:** Active (left) corrodes to protect noble (right).

### ⚠️ Critical Pitfalls to Avoid
- Don't confuse SACP and ICCP driving forces.
- Always include utilisation factor (η) in anode mass.
- Always convert years → seconds before plugging into Faraday's Law.
- Never recommend AP for non-passivating systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion control by design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Corrosion control by design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion Control by Design** is the proactive engineering discipline of integrating corrosion mitigation strategies directly into the conceptual, geometric, and material selection phases of a structure or component's design lifecycle. It involves the systematic application of design principles, dimensional calculations, and metallurgical choices to minimize the rate of degradation before the structure is even fabricated.

In the context of **PENST414 (Shipbuilding Materials, Corrosion Prevention and Protection)**, this concept extends specifically to marine vessels, offshore platforms, hull structures, ballast tanks, and submerged appendages, where the design must counteract the highly aggressive chloride-rich marine environment from the blueprint stage itself.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "House Architecture" Analogy**
> Imagine you are building a house in a region that experiences heavy monsoon rains. Would you wait for the rain to damage the house and then patch the leaks? **Absolutely not.** A wise architect would, from the very beginning:
> 1. Choose non-rotting materials (like treated wood or concrete) instead of raw wood.
> 2. Design a sloped roof so water drains off quickly.
> 3. Avoid creating flat surfaces where water can pool (no "crevices").
> 4. Ensure there are downspouts and easy access to the roof for maintenance.
>
> **Corrosion control by design is exactly the "architectural wisdom" for metals in a corrosive environment.** Instead of allowing rust to dictate the lifespan of a ship, we instruct the design itself to "drain the water," "avoid contact with dissimilar metals," and "leave room for protective coatings."

### 1.3 Primary Objectives of Corrosion Control by Design

The KTU 2024 scheme framework identifies **four pillars** of design-based corrosion control:

1. **Material Selection:** Choosing metals and alloys inherently resistant to the specific service environment.
2. **Geometric Configuration:** Designing shapes, joints, and orientations that prevent moisture accumulation, stagnation, and crevice formation.
3. **Dimensional Allowance (Corrosion Allowance):** Pre-calculating and adding extra sacrificial wall thickness to extend service life.
4. **Provisions for Mitigation Systems:** Designing the structure to accommodate Cathodic Protection (CP) anodes, coating application, and future maintenance inspection.

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Metrics Used in Design Calculations:**
> - **Corrosion Rate (CR):** Measured in **mils per year (mpy)** or **millimeters per year (mm/yr)**.
> - **Design Life ($L_d$):** The intended operational lifespan of the vessel, typically **20 to 30 years** for commercial ships.
> - **Corrosion Allowance ($CA$):** Extra thickness added, measured in **mm** or **inches**.
> - **Safety Factor (SF):** A dimensionless multiplier, typically between **1.0 and 2.0**, applied to account for unforeseen environmental aggressiveness.
> - **Density of Steel ($\rho$):** Standard **7850 kg/m³** for shipbuilding grade steel.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Golden Rules of Corrosion Control by Design

The design phase is the most cost-effective stage to implement corrosion control. The fundamental rules, aligned with NACE/AMPP and KTU standards, are:

#### **Rule 1: Avoid Dissimilar Metal Couples (Galvanic Corrosion Prevention)**
- If two metals with different electrochemical potentials (as seen on the galvanic series) must be joined, they must be electrically isolated using **non-conductive gaskets, washers, sleeves, or coatings**.
- The **area ratio rule** must be strictly followed: the **anodic (less noble) area must be LARGER** than the cathodic (more noble) area. A small anode coupled to a large cathode leads to rapid, catastrophic pitting (e.g., avoid using small brass fittings on a large steel hull).
- In marine environments, the **Galvanic Series in Seawater** is the reference (not the standard EMF series), because passivation and ion concentration shift the potentials.

#### **Rule 2: Design for Drainage and Ventilation**
- All horizontal surfaces should be sloped at a minimum of **1:48 (approximately 1.2°)** to promote rapid water runoff.
- Internal compartments, especially **ballast tanks and cofferdams**, must have adequate venting to prevent condensation and moisture buildup.
- Avoid "traps" where water can collect and stagnate. Stagnant water rapidly depletes dissolved oxygen, creating differential aeration cells.

#### **Rule 3: Avoid Crevices**
- Crevices are the silent killers of marine structures. They promote **crevice corrosion** through oxygen depletion and chloride concentration.
- Use **continuous welds** instead of bolted or riveted joints where possible.
- Where bolting is unavoidable, use **welded covers, caulking, or sealant** to close the gap.
- Avoid overlapping plates; use **butt joints with full penetration welds** instead.

#### **Rule 4: Provide Adequate Corrosion Allowance**
- The structural thickness must be the sum of the **mechanically required thickness** plus a **corrosion allowance**.
- Allowance is typically based on the expected **corrosion rate $\times$ design life**, with an additional safety factor.

#### **Rule 5: Design for Accessibility and Maintainability**
- An area that cannot be inspected, cleaned, or recoated will inevitably fail.
