- Provide **manholes, access ports, and walkways** in tanks and void spaces.
- Internals of complex structures (e.g., stiffener webs) should be **coatable and inspectable**.

#### **Rule 6: Avoid Stress Concentrations (Prevent SCC and Fatigue)**
- Sharp corners, notches, and abrupt changes in cross-section create residual tensile stresses.
- Use **generous fillet radii (minimum 2x the plate thickness)** at all corners.
- Cold working and welding-induced stresses should be relieved by **post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)** in critical applications.

#### **Rule 7: Select Compatible Coatings and Linings**
- The substrate must be compatible with the intended coating system.
- Design should avoid sharp edges (coatings thin out on sharp edges due to surface tension); use **chamfered or rounded edges** (radius $\geq$ **2 mm** preferred).
- Allow sufficient **"stand-off" distance** for spray coating application.

#### **Rule 8: Design for Cathodic Protection Compatibility**
- For submerged structures, reserve flat, accessible areas for **sacrificial anode** mounting.
- Ensure that **shielding** (where one part of the structure blocks CP current to another part) is avoided.
- Do not paint areas intended for CP current discharge unless absolutely necessary and calculated for.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Parameter** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units** | **Engineering Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Corrosion Rate (CR) | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$ | mpy (mils/year) or mm/yr | Basic corrosion rate from mass loss test data |
| Corrosion Allowance (CA) | $CA = CR \cdot L_d \cdot SF$ | mm or inches | Extra wall thickness to be added to design |
| Total Design Thickness | $t_{total} = t_{mech} + CA$ | mm | Final plate thickness to be ordered |
| Galvanic Current Density | $I_g = \dfrac{E_c - E_a}{R_{total}}$ | A/m² | Predicting galvanic corrosion severity |
| Area Ratio Influence | $CR_{anode,actual} = CR_{anode,base} \cdot \dfrac{A_c}{A_a}$ | mpy | Shows why small anodes fail fast against large cathodes |
| Polarization Resistance | $R_p = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}$ | $\Omega \cdot cm^2$ | Electrochemical corrosion rate measurement |
| Faraday's Law Mass Loss | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | grams | Theoretical metal dissolution per unit time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Examinations:** When using the **$CA$ formula**, ensure that the units of $CR$ and $L_d$ are consistent. If $CR$ is in mm/yr and $L_d$ is in years, $CA$ comes out in **mm** directly.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **shipbuilding industry**, corrosion control by design is mandated by classification societies such as **DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, and Bureau Veritas**. The IMO (International Maritime Organization) **Performance Standard for Protective Coatings (PSPC)** explicitly requires design-stage corrosion control for ballast tanks and void spaces. Failing to incorporate these principles leads to catastrophic failures such as the **MOL Comfort** incident (2013), where improper stress and corrosion allowance design contributed to hull fracture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Corrosion Allowance Equation

The corrosion allowance is the most fundamental calculation in design-based corrosion control. The derivation proceeds from first principles using the definition of linear corrosion rate.

**Given Parameters:**
- $CR$ = Linear corrosion rate of the selected material in the service environment (mm/yr)
- $L_d$ = Design life of the structure (years)
- $SF$ = Safety Factor (dimensionless, accounts for environmental variability, localized pitting, and end-of-life margin)
- $t_{mech}$ = Thickness required to withstand mechanical loads (calculated from pressure, bending, buckling codes)
- $t_{total}$ = Final thickness to be specified on the engineering drawing

**Step 1: Define the Linear Corrosion Rate**

The linear corrosion rate represents the uniform reduction in material thickness per unit time on a single surface exposed to a corrosive medium.

$$CR = \dfrac{\Delta t}{\Delta T}$$

Where $\Delta t$ is the thickness lost (mm) and $\Delta T$ is the time elapsed (years).

**Step 2: Express the Total Thickness Loss Over Design Life**

The total thickness lost over the design life $L_d$ is the simple product of the rate and the time:

$$\Delta t_{total} = CR \cdot L_d$$

**Step 3: Introduce the Safety Factor**

Marine environments are highly variable. A ship may operate in tropical waters, arctic waters, or estuaries — each with different corrosivity. Additionally, localized pitting can be 3 to 5 times the general corrosion rate. To account for these uncertainties, a safety factor is applied:

$$CA = CR \cdot L_d \cdot SF$$

**Step 4: Compute the Total Design Thickness**

The actual thickness to be ordered from the steel mill is the mechanical requirement plus the corrosion allowance:

$$t_{total} = t_{mech} + CA$$

$$t_{total} = t_{mech} + (CR \cdot L_d \cdot SF)$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

**Problem Statement:**

A seawater ballast tank on a bulk carrier is constructed from mild steel. The mechanical analysis indicates a required structural thickness of **12.0 mm**. The expected uniform corrosion rate in the tank is **0.15 mm/year**. The vessel is designed for a service life of **25 years**. Using a safety factor of **1.5**, determine the design thickness to be specified.

**Given Data:**
- $t_{mech} = 12.0$ mm
- $CR = 0.15$ mm/yr
- $L_d = 25$ years
- $SF = 1.5$

**Step 1: Calculate the Corrosion Allowance (CA)**

$$CA = CR \cdot L_d \cdot SF$$

$$CA = 0.15 \times 25 \times 1.5$$

$$CA = 5.625 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Total Design Thickness**

$$t_{total} = t_{mech} + CA$$

$$t_{total} = 12.0 + 5.625$$

$$t_{total} = 17.625 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Apply Standard Plate Thickness Selection (Practical Step)**

In shipbuilding, plate thickness is ordered in standard increments. The next standard thickness above 17.625 mm is **18.0 mm**.

$$t_{ordered} = 18.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:** The engineer must specify a plate of thickness **18.0 mm**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Design Decisions & Their Corrosion Implications

| **Design Decision** | **Preferred Choice** | **Avoided Choice** | **Corrosion Mechanism Mitigated** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Joint Type | Butt joint with full penetration weld | Lap joint with bolted connection | Crevice corrosion |
| Surface Orientation | Sloped (≥ 1:48 gradient) | Flat horizontal surface | Pitting, differential aeration |
| Corner Geometry | Generous fillet radius (R ≥ 2t) | Sharp 90° internal corner | Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC) |
| Metal Coupling | Same metal or electrically isolated | Direct contact of copper with steel | Galvanic corrosion |
| Edge Preparation | Chamfered / rounded edges | Sharp sheared edges | Coating failure, edge corrosion |
| Internal Access | Manholes, handholes, walkways | Sealed, untouchable voids | Under-deposit / stagnant corrosion |
| Cathodic Protection | Reserved mounting surfaces | Painted over entire structure | Galvanic anode inefficiency |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: The Design-for-Corrosion Decision Tree

The following block diagram represents the logical decision sequence an engineer should follow during the design phase to systematically embed corrosion control measures.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Project Specification] --> B[Identify Service Environment]
    B --> C{Is Environment Marine or Aggressive?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Consult Galvanic Series in Seawater]
    C -->|No| E[Standard Material Selection]
    D --> F{Are Multiple Metals Required?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Apply Area Ratio Rule: Aa > Ac]
    G --> H[Design Electrical Isolation Gaskets]
    F -->|No| I[Select Single Corrosion-Resistant Alloy]
    H --> J[Calculate Corrosion Allowance]
    I --> J
    E --> J
    J --> K[Compute t_total = t_mech + CA]
    K --> L[Check Geometric Design Rules]
    L --> M{Are Crevices Present?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Use Continuous Welds or Sealants]
    M -->|No| O[Check Drainage Slopes]
    N --> O
    O --> P{Slope >= 1 in 48?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Verify Accessibility for Maintenance]
    P -->|No| R[Redesign Surface Gradient]
    R --> Q
    Q --> S{Is CP Compatibility Required?}
    S -->|Yes| T[Reserve Anode Mounting Surfaces]
    S -->|No| U[Finalize Design Package]
    T --> U
    U --> V[End: Manufacturing Drawings]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Galvanic Couple Area Ratio Effect

This block-level schematic illustrates the disastrous effect of an unfavorable area ratio (small anode, large cathode), which is a critical KTU examination concept.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BadDesign[Faulty Design: Small Anode - Large Cathode]
        A1[Small Brass Bolt - 10 cm²] -->|Galvanic Current| B1[Large Steel Plate - 10000 cm²]
        B1 -.->|Severe Pitting| A1
    end
    subgraph GoodDesign[Correct Design: Large Anode - Small Cathode]
        A2[Large Zinc Anode - 10000 cm²] -->|Controlled Current| B2[Small Steel Fitting - 50 cm²]
        B2 -.->|Sacrificial Protection| A2
    end
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Corrosion Control Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Corrosion Control Strategy] --> Q1[Design Stage Control]
    P --> Q2[Manufacturing Stage Control]
    P --> Q3[Operational Stage Control]
    Q1 --> R1[Material Selection]
    Q1 --> R2[Geometric Design]
    Q1 --> R3[Corrosion Allowance]
    Q1 --> R4[CP System Provision]
    Q2 --> S1[Welding Quality]
    Q2 --> S2[Surface Preparation]
    Q2 --> S3[Coating Application]
    Q3 --> T1[Inspection Schedule]
    Q3 --> T2[Maintenance Coatings]
    Q3 --> T3[Anode Replacement]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q: Define corrosion control by design. List any four design considerations to prevent galvanic corrosion in a ship hull structure.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Corrosion control by design refers to the practice of incorporating corrosion mitigation principles during the design phase of a structure rather than relying on post-construction remedies.
The four design considerations to prevent galvanic corrosion are:
1. Avoid direct contact between dissimilar metals; if unavoidable, use metals close together in the galvanic series.
2. Electrically isolate dissimilar metals using non-conductive gaskets, washers, or sleeves.
3. Ensure the area ratio rule is followed — the anodic (less noble) area must be **larger** than the cathodic (more noble) area.
4. Apply a barrier coating at the junction of the two metals to break the electrolytic path. **(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for 4 points = 3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q: What is a "corrosion allowance"? Why is it necessary to include a safety factor in its calculation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A corrosion allowance is the extra thickness of metal added to the mechanically required thickness of a component to compensate for the material that will be lost to corrosion over the structure's design life. **(1.5 marks)**
A safety factor is necessary because:
1. Marine environments vary widely (temperature, salinity, dissolved oxygen).
2. The published corrosion rates are averages; localized pitting can be 3 to 5 times higher.
3. It provides a margin of safety for unforeseen operational stresses. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the eight major design rules that are followed in shipbuilding to control corrosion. Highlight the importance of the area ratio rule in preventing galvanic corrosion. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Rule 1 – Material Selection:** Choose alloys that are inherently resistant to the marine environment, such as using **copper-nickel alloys** for seawater piping or **aluminum-bronze** for propellers. **(0.5 mark)**

**Rule 2 – Avoid Dissimilar Metal Couples:** Where joining of dissimilar metals is necessary, isolate them electrically using **non-metallic gaskets, sleeves, and bushings**. **(0.5 mark)**

**Rule 3 – Area Ratio Rule:** The area of the anodic metal (less noble) must always be **larger** than the cathodic metal (more noble). For example, a small brass bolt on a large steel hull creates a small anode-large cathode situation, causing rapid, intense pitting of the bolt. Conversely, a large sacrificial zinc anode protecting a small steel component provides a large anode-small cathode situation, which gives a low, uniform corrosion current and long life. **(1.5 marks)**

**Rule 4 – Drainage and Ventilation:** All horizontal surfaces must be sloped at a minimum gradient of **1:48** to allow water to drain. Internal tanks must be vented to prevent condensation. **(0.5 mark)**

**Rule 5 – Avoid Crevices:** Use continuous welds, butt joints, and sealants to eliminate the narrow gaps that promote crevice corrosion. **(0.5 mark)**

**Rule 6 – Corrosion Allowance:** Add an extra thickness equal to **$CR \times L_d \times SF$** to the mechanical design thickness. **(0.5 mark)**

**Rule 7 – Accessibility:** Provide manholes, walkways, and access ports so that all internal surfaces can be inspected, cleaned, and recoated. **(0.5 mark)**

**Rule 8 – Cathodic Protection Compatibility:** Reserve clean, unpainted surfaces for sacrificial anodes and ensure that structural members do not "shadow" each other from the CP current. **(0.5 mark)**

**Conclusion (2 marks):** Adherence to these eight rules shifts corrosion mitigation from costly reactive maintenance to inexpensive preventive design. The IMO PSPC (Performance Standard for Protective Coatings) makes these principles mandatory for new vessel construction.

---

**(b)** A cylindrical pressure vessel in a chemical plant is made of carbon steel. The mechanical design requires a wall thickness of **10.0 mm**. The uniform corrosion rate in the operating environment is **0.20 mm/year**. The design life is **20 years**. Using a safety factor of **1.5**, calculate:
(i) The corrosion allowance required.
(ii) The total thickness to be ordered.
(iii) The standard plate thickness to be specified, given that plates are available in 1 mm increments. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $t_{mech} = 10.0$ mm
- $CR = 0.20$ mm/yr
- $L_d = 20$ years
- $SF = 1.5$

**(i) Corrosion Allowance:** **(2 Marks)**

$$CA = CR \cdot L_d \cdot SF$$

$$CA = 0.20 \times 20 \times 1.5 = 6.0 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Total Design Thickness:** **(2 Marks)**

$$t_{total} = t_{mech} + CA = 10.0 + 6.0 = 16.0 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Standard Plate Thickness:** **(1 Mark)**
Since the calculated thickness is exactly 16.0 mm and standard plates are available in 1 mm increments, the specified thickness is **16.0 mm**.

**Conclusion / Engineering Note (2 Marks):** This 6.0 mm allowance represents a 60% increase in the mechanical requirement, which is significant. The engineer may now consider whether **upgrading to a more corrosion-resistant alloy** (e.g., stainless steel 316L) could eliminate the need for such a thick corrosion allowance and reduce the overall structural weight and material cost. This trade-off analysis is a key part of optimal corrosion design.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE:**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the design strategies to prevent **crevice corrosion** in marine structures. Explain why sharp corners and stress raisers must be avoided in design. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Strategies to Prevent Crevice Corrosion: (4 Marks)**

1. **Use Continuous Welds:** Replace bolted or riveted joints with continuous, full-penetration welds wherever possible. Bolted joints inherently create a small gap between the plates and the bolt shank, forming a perfect crevice.
2. **Use Butt Joints:** Avoid overlapping (lap) joints. Where plate edges meet, use butt joints with the weld ground smooth and inspected.
3. **Apply Sealants and Caulking:** For joints that cannot be welded, fill the gap with a marine-grade elastomeric sealant before bolting.
4. **Design for Drainage:** Position crevice-prone areas (e.g., gaskets, flange faces) so that any moisture has a path to drain by gravity.
5. **Use Non-Absorbent Gaskets:** Select gasket materials such as PTFE (Teflon) or graphite composites that do not absorb and retain corrosive fluids.
6. **Eliminate Stagnant Zones:** Design pipe branches, T-junctions, and tank corners to avoid dead legs where seawater can sit undisturbed.

**Why Sharp Corners and Stress Raisers Must Be Avoided: (3 Marks)**

Sharp internal corners and abrupt changes in geometry create three simultaneous problems:
1. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC):** Tensile residual stresses concentrate at sharp corners. In a chloride-rich marine environment, austenitic stainless steels and certain aluminum alloys can suffer rapid transgranular cracking. The combination of **tensile stress + chloride + susceptible metallurgy** is the classic SCC triad.
2. **Coating Failure:** Liquid coatings thin out over sharp edges (called "edge pull" or "picture framing") because of surface tension effects. This leaves the edge bare and exposed to corrosion.
3. **Fatigue Cracking:** Under cyclic wave loading, the stress concentration factor ($K_t$) at a sharp corner can be 3 or more, dramatically reducing fatigue life.

**Design Rule:** Use a **fillet radius of at least 2 times the plate thickness** at all internal corners, and **chamfer or round all external edges** to a minimum radius of **2 mm**.

---

**(b)** Differentiate between **general corrosion** and **galvanic corrosion**. With the help of the galvanic series in seawater, explain the consequences of coupling a **small copper alloy component** to a **large steel hull** in a marine vessel. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Differentiation: (3 Marks)**

| **Parameter** | **General Corrosion** | **Galvanic Corrosion** |
|---|---|---|
| Cause | Uniform chemical/electrochemical attack over the entire surface | Electrochemical attack due to potential difference between two dissimilar metals |
| Distribution | Uniform thickness loss over the whole surface | Localized, intense attack on the anodic (less noble) metal |
| Environment | Single metal in electrolyte | Two electrically connected metals in a common electrolyte |
| Rate | Predictable using weight-loss methods | Often 10 to 100 times the general corrosion rate |
| Prevention | Coatings, inhibitors, corrosion allowance | Material selection, isolation, area ratio control |

**Consequences of Coupling Small Cu to Large Steel Hull: (4 Marks)**

When a small copper alloy fitting (e.g., a valve or propeller shaft bushing) is mounted directly on a large steel hull:
- According to the **Galvanic Series in Seawater**, copper alloys are **more noble (cathodic)** than steel (which is anodic).
- The potential difference $\Delta E = E_{Cu} - E_{steel} \approx 0.3$ to $0.5$ V in seawater.
- The **cathodic area (steel hull)** is enormous (thousands of cm²), while the **anodic area is the small steel fastener or the steel around the copper joint** — wait, this needs careful analysis.

**Correction (The Critical Area Ratio Concept):**
- If the copper component is in direct contact with the steel, the **steel adjacent to the contact point becomes the anode** and the **copper becomes the cathode**.
- Since the steel hull is **massive (large area)** and the region of anodic dissolution is **localized near the joint (small effective anode area)**, the area ratio $\frac{A_c}{A_a}$ is **very large**.
- Result: The anodic current density $i_a$ becomes extremely high, leading to **intense, deep pitting of the steel** right at the junction.
- The copper component itself remains protected, but the steel around it is rapidly consumed.

**Design Mitigation (as a conclusion):** Use a **non-conductive isolation sleeve and gasket** to break the metallic path, ensuring that the two metals are never in electrical contact, even when immersed in the same seawater electrolyte.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON PITFALLS — WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in CA Calculation (–2 marks):** Mixing mm/yr with years in months, or using mpy instead of mm/yr without conversion. Always explicitly state the units in the formula before substitution.
> 2. **Reversing the Area Ratio Rule (–2 marks):** Many students incorrectly say "the cathode should be larger than the anode" — this is backwards. The **ANODE must be larger** than the cathode to control current density. Examiners will deduct marks for this reversal.
> 3. **Confusing the Galvanic Series with the EMF Series (–1 mark):** In seawater, the order differs from standard EMF series (e.g., aluminum is more active than zinc in the galvanic series). Always state "**galvanic series in seawater**" explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting the Standard Plate Increment (–1 mark):** In numerical problems, always round up to the next standard plate thickness. Failing to do so leaves the design under-spec.
> 5. **Ignoring SCC Triad in Sharp Corner Questions (–1 mark):** When asked about sharp corners, the three-word answer "**Stress + Chloride + Susceptible alloy = SCC**" is required. Vague answers like "it causes stress" score zero.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **📋 HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETINS:**

- **Corrosion control by design** is a **proactive**, cost-effective strategy implemented at the **blueprint stage**, not the maintenance stage.
- The **Corrosion Allowance Equation** is: $CA = CR \times L_d \times SF$.
- **Total Plate Thickness:** $t_{total} = t_{mech} + CA$. Always round **up** to the next standard mill increment.
- The **Area Ratio Rule (CRITICAL):** Anode Area > Cathode Area. Small anodes against large cathodes cause **catastrophic localized pitting**.
- Use the **Galvanic Series in Seawater** (not the EMF series) for marine applications.
- **Crevices are forbidden:** Use continuous welds, butt joints, and sealants. Never overlap plates in marine structures.
- **Drainage gradient minimum:** **1:48 (≈ 1.2°)** for all horizontal surfaces exposed to seawater or condensation.
- **Fillet radius rule:** Internal corners must have a radius of at least **2 × plate thickness**. External edges should be chamfered to a minimum **2 mm** radius.
- **Accessibility is mandatory:** Every wetted surface must be **inspectable, cleanable, and coatable**.
- **Cathodic Protection compatibility:** Reserve clean, unpainted areas for anodes; avoid "shadowing" effects.
- **PSPC Standard:** The IMO **Performance Standard for Protective Coatings** legally mandates these design principles for ballast tanks of new vessels.
- **SCC Triad:** Susceptible Alloy + Tensile Stress + Corrosive Environment (Chlorides) = **Stress Corrosion Cracking**. Eliminate any one leg of the triad through design.
- **Edge Effect on Coatings:** Sharp edges cause coating "pull-back" due to surface tension. Always **round or chamfer** before coating.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion inhibitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Corrosion inhibitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion Inhibitor (KTU 2024 Definition):** A corrosion inhibitor is a chemical substance which, when added in small concentrations (typically **0.1% – 1.0% by mass**) to a corrosive environment, effectively decreases the rate of attack on the metal surface without significantly altering the concentration of any corrosive reactant.

In the context of **shipbuilding and marine engineering**, corrosion inhibitors are classified as one of the most economical and versatile methods of corrosion control. They function by either forming a protective film on the metal surface, altering the anodic or cathodic reaction kinetics, or shifting the electrochemical potential of the metal into a passive region.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive View

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a corrosion inhibitor as a **"vaccine for steel."**
>
> Just as a small dose of a vaccine trains the human body to fight infection, a small quantity of an inhibitor "trains" or **coats** a metal surface to resist aggressive ions like chloride (Cl⁻), sulfate (SO₄²⁻), and dissolved oxygen (O₂). In a marine ballast tank, sea water is the "virus" — the inhibitor is the preventive medicine dosed into the system.

In plain English:

- A ship's hull, propeller, and ballast tank are continuously bathed in seawater — a highly conductive electrolyte rich in chloride ions.
- Adding a corrosion inhibitor creates a **thin, invisible molecular shield** on the steel surface.
- This shield slows down the electrochemical reactions (oxidation at anode, reduction at cathode) that convert iron into rust.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inhibition Efficiency (IE or η %):** This is the **standard metric** universally used by KTU examiners and NACE/AMPP international standards to quantify inhibitor performance.

The inhibitor must be evaluated under controlled laboratory conditions such as **potentiodynamic polarization**, **weight-loss (gravimetric)**, or **electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)** tests. The industry benchmark for a "good" inhibitor is an **IE ≥ 90%** at dosages below **500 ppm**.

## 1.4 Salient Features of an Ideal Inhibitor

A KTU-ready summary of the desirable properties:

| Property | Required Characteristic |
|---|---|
| Effectiveness | High IE (%) at low concentration |
| Stability | Chemically stable in service environment |
| Compatibility | Does not react adversely with coating, rubber, paint |
| Toxicity | Low environmental impact (especially in marine ecology) |
| Cost | Economically viable for shipyard scale |
| Solubility | Sufficient solubility in the working electrolyte (sea water, brine) |
| Film-forming | Strong adsorption / chemisorption on metal substrate |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Corrosion Inhibitors

Corrosion inhibitors are classified based on three principal criteria — **(i) Electrode Behavior, (ii) Chemical Nature, and (iii) Application Mechanism**.

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on Electrochemical Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most frequently asked classification** in KTU examinations.

**A. Anodic Inhibitors (Passivators)**

- These inhibitors shift the corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$) in the **positive (noble) direction**.
- They suppress the anodic reaction: $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$
- They promote the formation of a stable, adherent, **passive oxide film** (typically $\gamma$-Fe₂O₃ or Fe₃O₄).
- **Examples:** Chromates ($CrO_4^{2-}$), nitrites ($NO_2^-$), molybdates ($MoO_4^{2-}$), tungstates, orthophosphates.
- ⚠️ **KTU Pitfall:** Anodic inhibitors are **DANGEROUS** when dosed below the critical concentration. They cause *localized pitting attack*, which is far more destructive than uniform corrosion.

**B. Cathodic Inhibitors**

- These inhibitors shift $E_{corr}$ in the **negative (active) direction**.
- They suppress the cathodic reactions:
  - In aerated neutral solutions: $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$
  - In acidic solutions: $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$
- They work by forming insoluble precipitates at the cathodic sites, blocking the cathodic area.
- **Examples:** Zinc salts, polyphosphates, calcium hydroxide, magnesium salts, polyphosphates (Calgon), rare earth elements (cerium, lanthanum).

**C. Mixed Inhibitors**

- They suppress **both** anodic and cathodic reactions simultaneously without significantly shifting $E_{corr}$.
- Most organic inhibitors belong to this class.
- **Examples:** Amines, amides, imidazolines, benzotriazole (BTA), 2-mercaptobenzothiazole (MBT), Schiff bases.

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on Chemical Nature

**A. Inorganic Inhibitors**

- These are typically anodic inhibitors forming **oxidizing or non-oxidizing** passive films.
- Operate via precipitation or oxidation reactions.
- Heavy-metal chromates were historically used but banned under **REACH & EPA regulations** due to toxicity — replaced by **molybdates, tungstates, and rare-earth salts** in modern marine applications.

**B. Organic Inhibitors**

- Contain heteroatoms (**N, O, S, P**) and/or aromatic rings with π-electrons.
- Adsorb onto the metal surface through:
  - **Physical adsorption (physisorption)** — weak van der Waals forces, reversible.
  - **Chemical adsorption (chemisorption)** — covalent/coordinate bonding, irreversible.
- The general adsorption tendency follows the order: **O < N < S < P** (electronegativity decreases, polarizability increases).

### 2.1.3 Application-Based Classification

| Type | Application Context |
|---|---|
| **Pickling inhibitors** | Acid cleaning ($HCl$, $H_2SO_4$) of mill scale on ship plates |
| **Vapor Phase Inhibitors (VPI / VCI)** | Storage and shipping protection of ship components |
| **Volatile inhibitors** | Confined spaces — ballast tanks, voids, engine rooms |
| **Filming amines** | Condensate lines, boiler tubes |
| **Bimetallic / contact inhibitors** | Protection against galvanic corrosion between dissimilar metals (e.g., bronze propeller + steel shaft) |

## 2.2 Mechanism of Action — The Four-Step Adsorption Model

The protection offered by organic inhibitors follows a universal adsorption mechanism:

1. **Step 1 — Transport:** Inhibitor molecules migrate from bulk electrolyte to the metal/solution interface via diffusion or convection (governed by Fick's law).
2. **Step 2 — Adsorption:** Polar functional groups (–NH₂, –OH, –COOH, –SH) or π-electrons anchor onto the metal surface, displacing pre-adsorbed water molecules.
3. **Step 3 — Film Formation:** A continuous, mono- or multilayer protective barrier forms, blocking active sites on the metal surface.
4. **Step 4 — Retardation:** This adsorbed film increases charge-transfer resistance ($R_{ct}$), thereby decreasing both anodic dissolution and cathodic reduction rates.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable**, standard across NACE/AMPP, and tested in previous KTU examinations. Memorize each symbol and unit.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $IE\,\% = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$ | Inhibition Efficiency (gravimetric) | dimensionless % |
| 2 | $\theta = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0}$ | Surface coverage fraction (0 to 1) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $IE\,\% = \dfrac{R_{ct(i)} - R_{ct(0)}}{R_{ct(i)}} \times 100$ | IE from EIS (charge-transfer resistance) | % |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\theta}{1 - \theta} = K_{ads} \cdot C$ | Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\theta = K \cdot C^n$ | Freundlich Isotherm | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\theta = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{2b} \log K + \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{2b} \log C$ | Temkin Isotherm | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$ | Standard free energy of adsorption | kJ/mol |
| 8 | $\ln CR = -\dfrac{E_a}{RT} + \ln A$ | Arrhenius equation (temperature dependence) | — |
| 9 | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$ | Corrosion rate from weight loss (ASTM G1) | mpy or mm/yr |
| 10 | $\rho_{anode} = \dfrac{j}{n \cdot F}$ | Anodic current density (anodic reaction rate) | A/cm² |

> **Symbols:** $CR_0$ = corrosion rate without inhibitor, $CR_i$ = corrosion rate with inhibitor, $\theta$ = surface coverage, $K_{ads}$ = adsorption equilibrium constant, $C$ = inhibitor concentration, $R$ = universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K), $T$ = absolute temperature (K), $F$ = Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol), $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol), $W$ = weight loss (g), $A$ = exposed area (cm²), $D$ = metal density (g/cm³), $j$ = current density.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Shipbuilding

In modern shipyards, corrosion inhibitors are deployed in:

- **Sea-water ballast tanks** (using volatile amine salts or imidazoline blends).
- **Cargo oil tanks** of crude carriers (combating $H_2S$ and $CO_2$ sour corrosion).
- **Engine cooling systems** (nitrite-borate-silicate blends).
- **Lay-up and storage** of plate steel (VCI paper and VCI films).
- **Acid pickling** of rolled ship plates to remove mill scale.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Inhibition Efficiency from Weight-Loss Data

This derivation is **directly aligned with ASTM G1-03 standard** and is frequently asked in KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B questions.

**Given:** A ship-grade mild steel specimen of dimensions $5\,cm \times 2\,cm \times 0.2\,cm$ is immersed in 3.5% NaCl solution (synthetic seawater) for 7 days (168 hours) at 30 °C, with and without an imidazoline inhibitor.

**Measured values:**

| Condition | Initial mass (g) | Final mass (g) |
|---|---|---|
| Without inhibitor (blank) | 14.6820 | 14.3015 |
| With 200 ppm inhibitor | 14.7440 | 14.6745 |

**Step 1 — Weight loss calculation (blank)**

$$\Delta W_0 = W_{initial} - W_{final}$$

$$\Delta W_0 = 14.6820 - 14.3015 = 0.3805\,g$$

**Step 2 — Weight loss calculation (inhibited)**

$$\Delta W_i = 14.7440 - 14.6745 = 0.0695\,g$$

**Step 3 — Corrosion rate using ASTM G1 formula**

The exposure area for both specimens: $A = 2 \cdot (5 \times 2) + 2 \cdot (5 \times 0.2) + 2 \cdot (2 \times 0.2) = 22.80\,cm^2$

Density of mild steel: $D = 7.85\,g/cm^3$

Exposure time: $T = 168\,h$

**For blank specimen:**

$$CR_0 = \dfrac{K \cdot \Delta W_0}{A \cdot T \cdot D} = \dfrac{87{,}600 \cdot 0.3805}{22.80 \cdot 168 \cdot 7.85}$$

$$CR_0 = \dfrac{33{,}331.8}{30{,}061.44} = 1.1087\,mm/yr$$

**For inhibited specimen:**

$$CR_i = \dfrac{87{,}600 \cdot 0.0695}{22.80 \cdot 168 \cdot 7.85} = \dfrac{6088.2}{30{,}061.44} = 0.2025\,mm/yr$$

**Step 4 — Inhibition Efficiency calculation**

$$IE\,\% = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$$

$$IE\,\% = \dfrac{1.1087 - 0.2025}{1.1087} \times 100$$

$$IE\,\% = \dfrac{0.9062}{1.1087} \times 100$$

$$IE\,\% = 81.73\,\%$$

**Step 5 — Surface coverage**

$$\theta = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} = \dfrac{0.9062}{1.1087} = 0.8173$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference:** The imidazoline inhibitor achieves 81.73% efficiency at 200 ppm — classified as a "good" inhibitor per KTU/NACE standards. To reach IE ≥ 95%, the dosage must be optimized.

## 3.2 Derivation of Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm Constants

The Langmuir isotherm is the **foundation equation** for understanding monolayer adsorption of organic inhibitors. It assumes:

- Uniform, energetically equivalent adsorption sites.
- One inhibitor molecule occupies one site (no stacking).
- Maximum monolayer coverage = 1.

Starting from the **Langmuir equilibrium condition:**

$$\dfrac{\theta}{1 - \theta} = K_{ads} \cdot C$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange to linear form**

$$\theta = K_{ads} \cdot C \cdot (1 - \theta)$$

$$\theta = K_{ads} \cdot C - K_{ads} \cdot C \cdot \theta$$

$$\theta + K_{ads} \cdot C \cdot \theta = K_{ads} \cdot C$$

$$\theta (1 + K_{ads} \cdot C) = K_{ads} \cdot C$$

$$\theta = \dfrac{K_{ads} \cdot C}{1 + K_{ads} \cdot C}$$

**Step 2 — Invert for linear plotting**

$$\dfrac{1}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{ads} \cdot C} + 1$$

$$\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** Plotting $C/\theta$ (y-axis) vs $C$ (x-axis) should give a **straight line of slope = 1** and intercept = $1/K_{ads}$, confirming Langmuir monolayer adsorption.

**Step 3 — Compute $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$**

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$

Where 55.5 = molar concentration of water in mol/L.

**Numerical example:** If $K_{ads} = 5.2 \times 10^4\,L/mol$ at 303 K:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -(8.314)(303) \ln(55.5 \times 5.2 \times 10^4)$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -2519.1 \cdot \ln(2.886 \times 10^6)$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -2519.1 \cdot 14.876 = -37{,}471\,J/mol$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -37.47\,kJ/mol$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Rule:** If $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} \leq -40\,kJ/mol \Rightarrow$ **chemisorption** (strong covalent/coordination bonding). If $-20 \leq \Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} \leq -40\,kJ/mol \Rightarrow$ **mixed-mode (physi-chemisorption)**.

## 3.3 Arrhenius Temperature-Derivative Analysis

This derivation is often tested as a Part B sub-question to compute activation energy change due to inhibitor addition.

$$\ln CR = -\dfrac{E_a}{RT} + \ln A$$

**Step 1 — Without inhibitor:** slope of $\ln CR$ vs $1/T$ = $-E_a/R$

**Step 2 — With inhibitor:** slope changes to $-E_a^*/R$

If $E_a^* > E_a$ (inhibitor increases activation energy) ⇒ inhibitor works through **energy-barrier mechanism**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite Question:** "Prove that an inhibitor acts via the energy-barrier mechanism if $E_a$ with inhibitor > $E_a$ without inhibitor."

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Anodic Inhibitor | Cathodic Inhibitor | Mixed/Organic Inhibitor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Corrosion potential shift | Positive (noble) | Negative (active) | Negligible |
| Polarization curve effect | Suppresses anodic Tafel slope | Suppresses cathodic Tafel slope | Suppresses both slopes |
| Mechanism | Forms passive oxide film | Forms insoluble precipitate | Forms adsorbed film |
| Risk of pitting | **High** if under-dosed | **Low** (safe) | **Very Low** |
| Typical examples | Chromate, nitrite, molybdate | Zn²⁺, polyphosphate, Ca²⁺ | BTA, MBT, imidazoline |
| Marine applicability | Limited (toxicity) | High | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Question]**

> **Q:** Define a corrosion inhibitor. Distinguish between anodic and cathodic inhibitors with two examples each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A corrosion inhibitor is a chemical substance added in small concentrations (0.1%–1.0%) to a corrosive medium to reduce the rate of metal dissolution without altering the bulk electrolyte concentration.

**Anodic inhibitors** suppress the anodic metal dissolution reaction ($Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$) by forming a passive oxide layer. They shift the corrosion potential in the noble direction. **Examples:** Sodium chromate, sodium nitrite.

**Cathodic inhibitors** suppress the cathodic reduction reaction ($O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$) by precipitating insoluble hydroxides at cathodic sites. They shift the corrosion potential in the active direction. **Examples:** Zinc sulphate, sodium polyphosphate.

> **Mark distribution (per KTU key):** Definition = 1 mark, Anodic explanation + examples = 1 mark, Cathodic explanation + examples = 1 mark.

### Question A2 — Numerical / Conceptual
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]**

> **Q:** The corrosion rates of mild steel in 3% NaCl solution at 298 K, with and without a 100 ppm organic inhibitor, are 0.45 mm/year and 1.92 mm/year respectively. Calculate the Inhibition Efficiency and surface coverage.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$IE\,\% = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.92 - 0.45}{1.92} \times 100 = 76.56\,\%$$

$$\theta = 0.7656$$

> **Mark distribution:** IE formula and substitution = 2 marks, final answer with $\theta$ = 1 mark.

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and must be answered with detailed sub-parts. Typical structure: **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.**

---

### ⭐ Question B1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Aligned to KTU 2024]**

> **Q1 (a)** Classify corrosion inhibitors based on their electrochemical mechanism. Explain the working principle, mechanism, and limitations of anodic inhibitors with suitable examples. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Classification (2 marks):**

Corrosion inhibitors are classified into three main categories based on which electrode reaction they suppress:

1. **Anodic inhibitors** (passivators) — suppress anodic dissolution.
2. **Cathodic inhibitors** — suppress cathodic reduction.
3. **Mixed inhibitors** — suppress both reactions.

**Working Principle of Anodic Inhibitors (2 marks):**

Anodic inhibitors are oxidizing agents (chromates, nitrites) or non-oxidizing anions (molybdates, tungstates, phosphates) that react with the metal surface to form a thin, adherent, and electronically insulating passive oxide film — typically $\gamma$-Fe₂O₃ or Fe₃O₄.

The mechanism:

- Chromate ions: $CrO_4^{2-} + 8H^+ + 3Fe^{2+} \rightarrow Cr^{3+} + 4H_2O + \gamma-Fe_2O_3$
- Nitrite ions: $2Fe + NO_2^- + H_2O \rightarrow 2Fe^{2+} + NH_3 + 2OH^-$

**Mechanism & Limitations (2 marks):**

- They shift corrosion potential $E_{corr}$ in the **positive (noble)** direction.
- The passivation range is sustained only **above a critical inhibitor concentration**.
- **Major limitation:** Below the critical concentration, the inhibitor causes **localized pitting corrosion**, which is far more catastrophic than uniform corrosion. This makes them "dangerous inhibitors" requiring strict dosing control.
- **Environmental limitation:** Chromates are **carcinogenic** and banned by REACH/EPA — replaced by eco-friendly molybdates and rare-earth salts in modern marine practice.

**Examples (1 mark):** Sodium chromate ($Na_2CrO_4$), sodium nitrite ($NaNO_2$), sodium molybdate ($Na_2MoO_4$), disodium hydrogen phosphate ($Na_2HPO_4$).

---

> **Q1 (b)** With the help of a derivation, show that the Langmuir adsorption isotherm governs the monolayer adsorption of organic inhibitors. Compute $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ for $K_{ads} = 1.5 \times 10^4\,L/mol$ at 303 K. Comment on the adsorption type. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation of Langmuir Isotherm (4 marks):**

Assume monolayer adsorption on a uniform metal surface. At equilibrium, the rate of adsorption equals the rate of desorption.

Let $\theta$ = fraction of surface covered; $(1 - \theta)$ = fraction uncovered.

The Langmuir adsorption equilibrium is written as:

$$\dfrac{\theta}{1 - \theta} = K_{ads} \cdot C$$

Rearranging:

$$\theta = K_{ads} \cdot C \cdot (1 - \theta)$$

$$\theta + K_{ads} \cdot C \cdot \theta = K_{ads} \cdot C$$

$$\theta (1 + K_{ads} \cdot C) = K_{ads} \cdot C$$

$$\theta = \dfrac{K_{ads} \cdot C}{1 + K_{ads} \cdot C}$$

Taking reciprocal:

$$\dfrac{C}{\theta} = C + \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}}$$

This is the **linear Langmuir equation**. Plotting $C/\theta$ vs $C$ yields a straight line with slope = 1 and intercept = $1/K_{ads}$, confirming monolayer adsorption.

**Computing $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ (2 marks):**

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -(8.314)(303) \ln(55.5 \times 1.5 \times 10^4)$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -(2519.1) \ln(8.325 \times 10^5)$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -(2519.1)(13.63)$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -34{,}335\,J/mol \approx -34.34\,kJ/mol$$

**Comment on Adsorption Type (1 mark):**

Since $-20\,kJ/mol \leq \Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} \leq -40\,kJ/mol$, the adsorption follows a **mixed mechanism combining both physisorption and chemisorption**, with chemisorption dominating due to heteroatom (N, S) coordination with Fe surface atoms.

---

### ⭐ Question B2 — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Aligned to KTU 2024]**

> **Q2 (a)** Explain the mechanism of organic inhibitors. Discuss the role of heteroatoms and aromatic rings in determining inhibition efficiency. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism of Organic Inhibitors (3 marks):**

Organic inhibitors suppress corrosion by **adsorbing onto the metal surface** to form a protective molecular film. This film acts as a barrier that:

1. Blocks the active sites on the metal surface.
2. Reduces the effective area available for electrochemical reactions.
3. Increases the charge-transfer resistance ($R_{ct}$).
4. Decreases the double-layer capacitance ($C_{dl}$), indicating displacement of water molecules.

The adsorption process involves:

- **Physical adsorption (physisorption):** Weak electrostatic interaction between charged inhibitor molecules and the electrically charged metal surface.
- **Chemical adsorption (chemisorption):** Strong coordinate/covalent bonding between lone-pair electrons of heteroatoms and the empty d-orbitals of metal atoms (Fe: 3d⁶4s² configuration).

**Role of Heteroatoms (2 marks):**

Heteroatoms such as **N, O, S, P** possess lone pairs of electrons that can be donated to the metal surface, forming strong coordinate bonds. The general adsorption strength follows the order:

$$O < N < S < P$$

This order is governed by the polarizability and electronegativity. Sulfur and phosphorus, being more polarizable, donate electrons more readily and exhibit stronger adsorption.

**Role of Aromatic Rings (2 marks):**

Aromatic rings (benzene, imidazole, pyridine) contain **delocalized π-electrons** that can interact with the metal surface through:

- $\pi$-electron donation to the metal's empty orbitals.
- Retro-donation from filled metal d-orbitals back to the anti-bonding $\pi^*$ orbitals of the inhibitor.

Compounds with multiple aromatic rings, electron-donating substituents (–NH₂, –OH, –CH₃), and long alkyl chains exhibit higher IE due to greater surface coverage and hydrophobic barrier formation.

---

> **Q2 (b)** A mild steel specimen immersed in 1 M $H_2SO_4$ shows a corrosion rate of 8.92 mm/yr. Addition of 250 ppm of an imidazoline-based inhibitor reduces this to 0.78 mm/yr. Compute the inhibition efficiency and surface coverage. If the Langmuir adsorption constant $K_{ads} = 4.7 \times 10^4\,L/mol$, determine whether adsorption is physisorption or chemisorption at 303 K. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Inhibition Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$IE\,\% = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{8.92 - 0.78}{8.92} \times 100 = \dfrac{8.14}{8.92} \times 100$$

$$IE\,\% = 91.26\,\%$$

**Step 2 — Surface Coverage (1 mark):**

$$\theta = 0.9126$$

**Step 3 — Standard Free Energy of Adsorption (3 marks):**

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -(8.314)(303) \ln(55.5 \times 4.7 \times 10^4)$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -(2519.1) \ln(2.6085 \times 10^6)$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -(2519.1)(14.773)$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -37{,}212\,J/mol \approx -37.21\,kJ/mol$$

**Step 4 — Type of Adsorption (1 mark):**

Since $-20\,kJ/mol \leq \Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -37.21\,kJ/mol \leq -40\,kJ/mol$, the adsorption mechanism is **mixed-mode (physi-chemisorption)**, with chemisorption dominating. This is typical for imidazoline derivatives on mild steel in acidic media — confirmed by NACE/AMPP literature.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Corrosion Inhibitor Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "inhibition efficiency" with "surface coverage":** IE is expressed as a percentage (×100), while $\theta$ is a fraction. Writing IE = 0.91 instead of 91% will cost 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the constant 55.5 in $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ calculation:** The water molarity term (55.5 mol/L) is **mandatory**. Omitting it gives wrong sign and magnitude.
>
> 3. **Wrong classification of anodic vs cathodic inhibitors:** Students often confuse Zn²⁺ (cathodic) with chromate (anodic). Memorize the polarity of $E_{corr}$ shift.
>
> 4. **Reporting $E_{corr}$ shift for mixed inhibitors:** Mixed inhibitors do **NOT** shift $E_{corr}$ significantly (ΔE < ±85 mV). Any answer claiming otherwise is incorrect.
>
> 5. **Neglecting units in Arrhenius plot:** Slope of $\ln CR$ vs $1/T$ must be in Kelvin, not Celsius. Always convert T to absolute scale.
>
> 6. **Ignoring pitting risk in anodic inhibitors:** This is a **favorite 2-mark KTU sub-question**. Always mention the critical concentration requirement.
>
> 7. **Wrong symbol for inhibition efficiency:** KTU officially accepts both $\eta\,\%$ and $IE\,\%$. Avoid using % alone without the variable.
>
> 8. **Failing to comment on adsorption type:** After calculating $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$, you **must** interpret the value. A numerical answer without commentary loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Corrosion Inhibitors (Module 4)**

- ✅ A corrosion inhibitor reduces metal loss without altering electrolyte concentration.
- ✅ **Three primary classes:** Anodic (passivators), Cathodic (precipitators), Mixed (film-formers).
- ✅ **Anodic inhibitors:** Risk pitting below critical concentration; examples = chromate, nitrite, molybdate.
- ✅ **Cathodic inhibitors:** Safe, but slow-acting; examples = Zn²⁺, polyphosphate, calcium salts.
- ✅ **Mixed inhibitors:** Most versatile and safest; examples = BTA, MBT, imidazoline, amines.
- ✅ **Inhibition Efficiency formula (most important):** $IE\,\% = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$
- ✅ **Surface coverage:** $\theta = 1 - \dfrac{CR_i}{CR_0}$
- ✅ **Langmuir linear form:** $\dfrac{C}{\theta} = C + \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}}$ — slope = 1 confirms monolayer.
- ✅ **Free energy of adsorption:** $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$ — interpret sign for physi-/chemisorption.
- ✅ **Heterocyclic inhibitors** with N, S, P heteroatoms and aromatic rings show superior IE.
- ✅ **VPI/VCI** inhibitors protect ship components during storage and shipping.
- ✅ **Pickling inhibitors** (thiourea, propargyl alcohol) prevent acid attack during descaling.
- ✅ Modern marine trend: **chromate-free, eco-friendly** inhibitors (molybdates, rare-earth salts, plant extracts).
- ✅ For KTU, always state the **mechanism, examples, and limitations** when describing any inhibitor class.
- ✅ Numerical problems typically combine $IE\,\%$ calculation with $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ interpretation.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme emphasis:** Application-based questions on marine corrosion (ballast tanks, hulls, cargo tanks) are scoring opportunities.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cathodic protection - mechanism of cathodic protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an electrochemical technique of corrosion mitigation in which the metal structure to be protected is forced to become the **cathode** of a deliberately engineered galvanic (or electrolytic) cell, thereby suppressing the anodic (oxidation / metal dissolution) reaction that is responsible for metallic deterioration.

In the formal language of electrochemistry adopted by KTU, the **Mechanism of Cathodic Protection** is the controlled redirection of electrons *toward* the protected metal surface such that the mixed potential of the structure is shifted (polarized) in the **negative (more cathodic) direction** until the local anodic current density becomes negligible and the entire metal surface responds to the net cathodic current supplied by an external source.

For shipbuilding, the structure to be protected is most commonly the **ship hull (mild/Corten steel)**, **propellers, rudders, sea-chests, ballast tanks, and offshore platform members** immersed in seawater, which is a highly aggressive electrolyte (typical resistivity ≈ **20–25 Ω·cm**, salinity ≈ **35 ‰**).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Sacrificial Bodyguard"

Imagine a famous king (your ship's hull) who is under constant attack by an assassin (corrosion). The king cannot defend himself at every moment. So, the king hires a **bodyguard** (the *anode*) who is dressed in a uniform identical to the king's. The assassins (chloride ions, dissolved oxygen) now strike the bodyguard, who willingly takes all the hits.

- The bodyguard is "sacrificed" (consumed) — analogous to the **sacrificial anode**.
- The king remains untouched — analogous to the **protected cathode (hull)**.
- The whole scheme works only as long as the bodyguard is present and *more reactive* than the king.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Cathodic protection does **not** stop corrosion — it simply *transfers* the anodic reaction from the structure to a consumable auxiliary electrode, where corrosion is *wanted* and *manageable*.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reference Electrodes (Bold Highlights)

| Reference Electrode | Potential vs SHE | Typical Use in CP |
|---|---|---|
| Copper / Copper Sulphate (Cu/CuSO₄) | **+0.316 V** | Seawater / marine applications (most common in shipbuilding) |
| Silver / Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl) | **+0.222 V** | Seawater monitoring |
| Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) | **+0.241 V** | Laboratory measurements |
| Zinc (seawater) | **−0.800 V** | Built-in reference for ship hulls |
| Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) | **0.000 V** | Universal reference |

**Key Protection Criterion (NACE / KTU accepted):** The protected structure must be polarized to a potential **more negative than −0.850 V vs Cu/CuSO₄** reference electrode.

---

## 1.4 The Fundamental Necessity of CP in Shipbuilding

A bare steel hull in seawater corrodes at rates of **0.10 – 0.50 mm/year** (localized pitting can exceed **1.0 mm/year**). Without protection, a 10 mm plate may perforate in less than a decade. Cathodic protection, when combined with a competent **paint / coating system**, reduces this to essentially **zero wastage**, with anode consumption being the only material loss — and that loss is *engineered* to be predictable and replenishable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrochemical Origin of Corrosion (Recap)

Corrosion in a neutral aerated electrolyte such as seawater proceeds via four simultaneous half-reactions:

**Anodic reaction (oxidation of iron):**

$$Fe \longrightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

**Cathodic reaction (oxygen reduction — dominant in aerated seawater at near-neutral pH):**

$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4OH^{-}$$

The overall mixed-potential equilibrium is what defines the **corrosion potential (E_corr)** of the metal, typically around **−0.60 to −0.65 V vs Cu/CuSO₄** for unprotected mild steel in seawater.

---

## 2.2 The Mechanism of Cathodic Protection — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The following sequence captures the *physical-mechanism* in five engineering-friendly steps — this is the heart of the topic and is the most frequently examined portion in KTU ESE.

### Step 1 — Identification of the Mixed Potential

When bare steel is placed in seawater, half of the surface acts as a local anode and the other half as a local cathode. The anodic and cathodic currents are equal in magnitude: **I_a = I_c = I_corr** at the **corrosion potential (E_corr)**.

### Step 2 — Application of an External Cathodic Current

In cathodic protection, an external direct current is forced *into* the metal. This is provided by one of two methods:
- **Method A — Sacrificial Anode CP (SACP / Galvanic Anode CP / GACP):** A more electrochemically active metal (Zn, Al, Mg) is electrically bonded to the hull. The potential difference between the two drives a DC current naturally.
- **Method B — Impressed Current CP (ICCP):** An inert anode (Pt/Ti, mixed-metal-oxide Ti, lead-silver, graphite) is connected to the **positive** terminal of an external DC power source, and the hull is connected to the **negative** terminal.

### Step 3 — Polarization of the Protected Structure

The injected current polarizes the structure in the **cathodic direction** along its polarization curve. The structure's potential drops from E_corr to a more negative value **E_prot**.

### Step 4 — Suppression of the Local Anodic Reaction

At sufficiently negative E_prot, the net anodic current density (i_a) of the protected metal is **reduced to virtually zero**. By Kirchhoff's current law, the externally supplied cathodic current exactly balances the oxygen-reduction current.

### Step 5 — Stabilization of the Calcareous Deposit

In seawater, the elevated local pH at the protected surface (due to OH⁻ generation) causes precipitation of a protective calcareous film of **CaCO₃ + Mg(OH)₂**. This film further reduces the current demand — a beneficial feedback loop exploited by ship operators.

---

## 2.3 Visual Comparison of the Two Methods

| Parameter | Sacrificial Anode (SACP) | Impressed Current (ICCP) |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Galvanic potential difference | External DC rectifier |
| Anode material | Zn, Al, Mg alloys | Pt-clad Ti, MMO/Ti, Pb-Ag, graphite |
| Voltage range | ~0.2 – 0.5 V | up to 50 V (typically 5–24 V) |
| Current capacity per anode | Limited | Very high |
| Lifetime | 1–10 years (replaceable) | 10–30+ years |
| Typical use on ships | Small vessels, ballast tanks, cofferdams | Large tankers, naval hulls, offshore |
| Stray-current interference | Negligible | Possible, requires control |
| Cost profile | Low installation, high recurring | High installation, low recurring |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master this compact table — it covers ≈ 80% of numerical problems asked in KTU Module-4 ESE on this topic.

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{R}$ | Ohm's law for CP circuit (ΔE = driving voltage, R = circuit resistance) | A, V, Ω |
| 2 | $i_{prot} = \dfrac{I}{A_{wetted}}$ | Required protective current density | A/m² |
| 3 | $i_{prot,\,steel,\,seawater} \approx 0.10 \text{ to } 0.30$ | Typical design current for bare steel hull (initial) | A/m² |
| 4 | $i_{prot,\,coated\,steel} \approx 0.005 \text{ to } 0.05$ | Current demand for coated hull (mean current density) | A/m² |
| 5 | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's law — anode mass consumption | kg |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$ | Anode utilization / current efficiency | % |
| 7 | $L_{anode} = \dfrac{m_{req}}{\rho \cdot A_{cross}}$ | Anode dimension (length) | m |
| 8 | $R_{anode} = \dfrac{\rho_{sw}}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{r}\right) - 1 \right]$ | Dwight's equation — anode-to-seawater resistance (for long rod anode) | Ω |
| 9 | $E_{prot} \le -0.850$ V vs Cu/CuSO₄ | KTU / NACE protection criterion for steel | V |
| 10 | $E_{Mg,\,sw} \approx -1.55$ V; $E_{Zn,\,sw} \approx -1.10$ V; $E_{Al,\,sw} \approx -1.05$ V | Typical galvanic anode open-circuit potentials in seawater | V vs SHE |

**Constants to memorize:**

$$F = 96\,485 \;\; C/mol \quad (\text{Faraday's constant})$$

$$\rho_{seawater} \approx 20\text{–}25 \;\; \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{cm} \quad (\text{electrolyte resistivity})$$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a modern VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier), the wetted hull area can exceed **30 000 m²**. The total protective current required for a freshly painted hull is in the range of **0.5 – 2.0 kA**, dropping to a tenth of that once the calcareous deposit matures. ICCP systems on such vessels consume between **3 – 8 kW** of continuous electrical power — a small price for an asset worth hundreds of millions.

Cathodic protection is also the **primary line of defence** inside ballast tanks of double-hulled tankers mandated by IMO Resolution A.798 and the IMO Performance Standard for Protective Coatings (PSPC).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Anode Mass Consumption via Faraday's First Law

> **Problem Statement:** A zinc sacrificial anode is used to protect a ship hull section in seawater. The protective current is steady at **I = 4.0 A** and the anode is expected to last **T = 5 years** before replacement. Compute the theoretical mass of zinc consumed and the minimum required mass assuming a typical zinc utilization efficiency of **η = 90 %**.

**Step 1 — State the relevant anodic half-reaction and its molar mass / valency.**

For zinc in seawater:

$$Zn \longrightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

Therefore:

$$M_{Zn} = 65.38 \;\; g/mol, \quad n = 2$$

**Step 2 — Compute total charge passed during the design life.**

$$Q = I \times T = 4.0 \;\text{A} \times \left(5 \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600\right) \;\text{s}$$

$$Q = 4.0 \times 1.578 \times 10^{8} \;\; C = 6.312 \times 10^{8} \;\; C$$

**Step 3 — Apply Faraday's first law of electrolysis.**

$$m_{theoretical} = \dfrac{M \cdot Q}{n \cdot F}$$

$$m_{theoretical} = \dfrac{65.38 \times 6.312 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 96\,485}$$

$$m_{theoretical} = \dfrac{4.127 \times 10^{10}}{1.9297 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$m_{theoretical} = 2.139 \times 10^{5} \;\; g = 213.9 \;\; \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the utilization factor to obtain the actual anode mass required.**

$$m_{actual} = \dfrac{m_{theoretical}}{\eta} = \dfrac{213.9}{0.90} = 237.7 \;\; \text{kg}$$

> **Result:** The minimum zinc anode mass to be installed is **≈ 238 kg** to maintain 4.0 A for 5 years at 90 % efficiency.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Driving Voltage of a Sacrificial Anode System

**Step 1 — List the open-circuit (corrosion) potentials of the two metals in seawater.**

$$E_{Zn,\,sw} = -1.10 \;\; V \;\; \text{vs SHE}$$

$$E_{Fe,\,sw} = -0.65 \;\; V \;\; \text{vs SHE}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the net driving EMF available.**

$$\Delta E = E_{anode} - E_{cathode} = (-1.10) - (-0.65) = -0.45 \;\; V$$

The magnitude of the driving voltage is:

$$\vert \Delta E \vert = 0.45 \;\; V$$

**Step 3 — Apply Ohm's law to compute the current if the total circuit resistance is, say, R = 0.18 Ω.**

$$I = \dfrac{\vert \Delta E \vert}{R} = \dfrac{0.45}{0.18} = 2.5 \;\; A$$

This is the **short-circuit current** the anode can deliver — the actual useful current will be lower due to polarization and resistance build-up.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Initial Current Density Demand for a Hull

**Step 1 — Define the wetted surface area of the hull.**

For a Panamax bulker with length L = 200 m, breadth B = 32 m, design draft d = 12 m, the approximate underwater hull surface is:

$$A_{wetted} \approx L \times (B + 2d) = 200 \times (32 + 24) = 200 \times 56 = 11\,200 \;\; m^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Use a typical protective current density for a coated hull.**

For a high-quality epoxy-coated hull in warm tropical seawater:

$$i_{prot} = 0.030 \;\; A/m^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total current required.**

$$I_{total} = i_{prot} \times A_{wetted} = 0.030 \times 11\,200 = 336 \;\; A$$

> **Result:** This is the design current for the ICCP rectifier. The rectifier will typically be rated at **400 A / 24 V** to provide margin.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting CP Method for a Ship Component

| Hull Component | Seawater Exposure? | Coating Status | Recommended CP | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Underwater hull (VLCC) | Yes | Epoxy anti-fouling | ICCP + SACP backup | High current demand, reliability |
| Bilge keel | Yes | Limited | SACP (zinc) | Localized, low current |
| Sea-chest / inlet grids | Yes | None | SACP (Al or Zn) | Frequent dry-docking impractical |
| Propeller (bronze) | Yes | None | ICCP with auto-control | Avoid over-protection (H₂ damage) |
| Rudder | Yes | Epoxy | SACP + bonding to hull | Electrical bonding critical |
| Ballast tank (double-hull) | Seawater intermittently | Epoxy (PSPC) | SACP (Al-Zn-In) | IMO PSPC requirement |
| Mooring chains | Splash zone | None | None (use coating instead) | CP ineffective in air |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cathodic Protection Mechanism (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid

    D --> E[Hull Cathode Polarization]
    E --> F[Shift of Ecorr to Eprot less than minus 0.85 V]
    F --> G[Suppression of Local Anodic Sites]
    G --> H[Calcareous Deposit CaCO3 plus MgOH2 Forms]
    H --> I[Long Term Stable Protection]
    I --> J[Monitoring via Reference Electrode]
    J --> K{Feedback to DC Source}
    K -- Adjust Voltage --> A
    B --> L[Seawater Electrolyte Ion Conduction]
    L --> E

    end
    subgraph ICCP[Impressed Current Cathodic Protection]

```
      Current
        Density
        (log scale)
            |
            |        Anodic
            |       / 
            |      / 
            |     /
   --------+----/------------> Potential (E vs Ref. Electrode)
            |   / Ecorr
            |  /

   ----------+------------------------
            |  |
       E_prot   E_corr
       (-0.85V) (-0.60V)
        vs CSE   vs CSE

  When I_ext is applied cathodically, the operating
  point slides DOWN-LEFT to E_prot. The local
  anodic dissolution rate becomes effectively zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** The examiner frequently asks: *"Sketch the Evans diagram and show the shift of corrosion potential under cathodic protection."* The sketch above is the standard expected answer.
