# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]

**"Define cathodic protection. State the basic protection criterion for mild steel in seawater."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Cathodic protection is an electrochemical method of corrosion control in which the metal structure to be protected is made the cathode of a galvanic or electrolytic cell, so that the anodic (metal dissolution) reaction is suppressed.

The **protection criterion** as per NACE / KTU standards is that the structure-to-seawater potential of the protected metal must be more negative than **−0.850 V with respect to a Cu/CuSO₄ reference electrode**. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for explanation of suppression, 1 for stating the criterion with correct sign and reference.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]

**"Distinguish between sacrificial anode and impressed current cathodic protection."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | SACP | ICCP |
|---|---|---|
| Driving source | Galvanic potential of sacrificial metal | External DC rectifier |
| Anode | Active (Zn, Al, Mg) | Inert (Pt-Ti, MMO) |
| Voltage | ~ 0.25–0.5 V | up to 24–50 V |

*(3 marks — 1 for source distinction, 1 for anode distinction, 1 for voltage or current capability distinction.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose marks by (1) confusing the *sign convention* (always write potentials as **negative** for protected steel vs Cu/CuSO₄), (2) forgetting to include the **utilization factor** when computing anode mass, and (3) omitting the **calcareous-deposit** effect on long-term current demand. A 14-mark answer that omits the units on the final numerical answer loses **1 mark** under strict valuation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — SACP Design

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **mechanism of cathodic protection** for a ship's steel hull using **sacrificial zinc anodes**. Discuss the role of the calcareous deposit. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A ship hull section of wetted area **1 200 m²** is to be protected by **aluminium anodes** with the following data:

- Required protective current density: **0.10 A/m²**
- Anode output current: **2.0 A per anode**
- Aluminium atomic mass: **27 g/mol**, valency n = 3
- Design life: **6 years**
- Utilization efficiency: **85 %**

Calculate (i) total protective current, (ii) number of anodes required, (iii) total theoretical mass of aluminium consumed, and (iv) actual anode mass to be installed. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Sketch (2 marks):** A neat diagram showing the hull as cathode, zinc anode, electrical bonding cable, and the seawater as electrolyte, with arrows indicating conventional current flow from Zn → seawater → hull → cable → Zn.

2. **Mechanism explanation (3 marks):**
   - Zinc, being more electrochemically active (E ≈ −1.10 V vs SHE), dissolves preferentially:
   
   $$Zn \longrightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$
   
   - Electrons flow through the bonding cable to the steel hull.
   - On the hull surface, these electrons combine with dissolved oxygen and water:
   
   $$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4OH^{-}$$
   
   - The hull is forced to act as a cathode; the local Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ reaction is suppressed.
   - The structure's potential drops from E_corr (≈ −0.60 V) to E_prot (≤ −0.85 V vs Cu/CuSO₄).

3. **Calcareous deposit (2 marks):**
   - The OH⁻ produced locally raises pH at the hull surface.
   - Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ from seawater precipitate as CaCO₃ and Mg(OH)₂.
   - This forms a low-porosity insulating film that **reduces long-term current demand** by 50–80 % and acts as a secondary barrier.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Total protective current:**

$$I_{total} = i_{prot} \times A_{wetted} = 0.10 \times 1200 = 120 \;\; A$$

**(ii) Number of anodes:**

$$N = \dfrac{I_{total}}{I_{anode}} = \dfrac{120}{2.0} = 60 \;\; \text{anodes}$$

**(iii) Theoretical mass of aluminium consumed (Faraday's Law):**

$$Q = I_{total} \times T = 120 \times (6 \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600) = 2.27 \times 10^{10} \;\; C$$

$$m_{theoretical} = \dfrac{M \cdot Q}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{27 \times 2.27 \times 10^{10}}{3 \times 96\,485}$$

$$m_{theoretical} = \dfrac{6.13 \times 10^{11}}{2.89 \times 10^{5}} = 2.12 \times 10^{6} \;\; g = 2120 \;\; \text{kg}$$

**(iv) Actual anode mass to be installed:**

$$m_{actual} = \dfrac{m_{theoretical}}{\eta} = \dfrac{2120}{0.85} = 2494 \;\; \text{kg} \;\; (\text{per anode } \approx 41.6 \;\; \text{kg})$$

**Final Answer:** 60 anodes, 2120 kg theoretical consumption, 2494 kg actual aluminium to be installed. *(7 marks — 2 + 1 + 2 + 2 distributed per sub-part.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ICCP System

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP)** for a ship hull. Include a labelled schematic and discuss the role of the reference electrode in the control loop. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The ICCP system of a bulk carrier is designed with the following data:

- Wetted hull area: **8 000 m²**
- Protective current density (mean): **0.025 A/m²**
- Anode-to-seawater resistance: **0.40 Ω**
- Anode circuit resistance (cable + structure): **0.15 Ω**
- Number of anodes: **4**

Calculate: (i) total protective current, (ii) total circuit resistance per anode, (iii) voltage that the rectifier must supply, and (iv) electrical power consumed. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Schematic (2 marks):** Rectifier (AC → DC) with +ve terminal to inert Pt/MMO anode immersed in seawater and −ve terminal bonded to the hull. A Cu/CuSO₄ reference electrode is mounted near the hull, wired back to the rectifier's control circuit.

2. **Mechanism (3 marks):**
   - The rectifier forces DC current from the inert anode (where Cl⁻ / H₂O is oxidized) through the seawater to the hull.
   - On the hull, oxygen reduction consumes the electrons, polarizing the structure negatively.
   - Because the anode is inert, it does not dissolve; only seawater-side reactions (Cl⁻ → Cl₂ at high voltages) occur.

3. **Reference electrode and feedback (2 marks):**
   - The Cu/CuSO₄ electrode continuously senses E_prot of the hull.
   - This signal is fed back to the rectifier, which automatically adjusts the output to maintain E_prot in the band **−0.80 to −0.90 V** vs Cu/CuSO₄.
   - This auto-potential control prevents both **under-protection** (corrosion) and **over-protection** (alkaline blistering of paint, hydrogen embrittlement of high-strength propeller alloys).

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Total current:**

$$I_{total} = i_{prot} \times A = 0.025 \times 8000 = 200 \;\; A$$

**(ii) Current per anode (assuming equal sharing):**

$$I_{per} = \dfrac{200}{4} = 50 \;\; A$$

**Total circuit resistance per anode branch:**

$$R_{branch} = R_{anode-sw} + R_{cable} = 0.40 + 0.15 = 0.55 \;\; \Omega$$

**(iii) Rectifier voltage per anode (Ohm's law):**

$$V_{anode} = I_{per} \times R_{branch} = 50 \times 0.55 = 27.5 \;\; V$$

**(iv) Total DC power consumed:**

$$P = I_{total} \times V_{anode} = 200 \times 27.5 = 5500 \;\; W = 5.5 \;\; \text{kW}$$

**Final Answer:** 200 A total current, 0.55 Ω per branch resistance, 27.5 V rectifier output, 5.5 kW power consumption. *(7 marks distributed as 2 + 1 + 2 + 2.)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD — MEMORIZE BEFORE THE EXAM**

- **Definition (CO4):** Cathodic protection = making the structure the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell.
- **Protection criterion:** E_prot ≤ **−0.850 V** vs **Cu/CuSO₄** (for steel in seawater).
- **Two methods:** SACP (sacrificial — Zn, Al, Mg) and ICCP (impressed current — Pt-Ti / MMO with DC source).
- **Anode reaction in SACP:** $M \to M^{n+} + n e^{-}$ (e.g., Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻).
- **Cathode reaction on hull:** $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \to 4OH^-$.
- **Faraday's Law formula:** $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$, with **F = 96 485 C/mol**.
- **Anode utilization efficiency (η):** typically **80–90 %** for Zn, **50–70 %** for Mg, **80–95 %** for Al.
- **Typical current densities:** coated steel **0.005–0.05 A/m²**; bare steel **0.10–0.30 A/m²**; initial polarisation **0.20–0.40 A/m²**.
- **Calcareous deposit:** CaCO₃ + Mg(OH)₂ layer that **reduces long-term current demand** by 50–80 %.
- **Seawater resistivity:** ≈ **20–25 Ω·cm** (tropical, 35 ‰ salinity).
- **Drive voltage (Zn–Fe in seawater):** ≈ **0.45 V**; (Al–Fe) ≈ **0.25–0.30 V**; (Mg–Fe) ≈ **0.70 V**.
- **Ship applications:** Underwater hull (ICCP dominant), propellers (ICCP auto-controlled to avoid over-protection), rudders (bonded to hull + SACP), sea-chests (SACP), ballast tanks (SACP per IMO PSPC).
- **Over-protection risks:** Hydrogen blistering of paint; hydrogen embrittlement of Ti propellers; cathodic disbonding; chlorination of anti-fouling paint → failure to control I below ~0.5 A/m² on bronze.
- **Common KTU exam traps:**
  1. Sign of E_prot must be **negative** vs Cu/CuSO₄.
  2. Always include **η** when computing actual anode mass.
  3. **Coated vs bare** current density is a 5× to 10× difference — read the question carefully.
  4. ICCP requires **automatic potential control** — not just a fixed-voltage rectifier.
  5. Reference electrode placement must be **close to the protected surface** for accurate sensing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sacrificial anode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Sacrificial anode # ⚓ 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)** is a passive, galvanic method of corrosion control in which a more electrochemically *active* metal (the **anode**) is electrically coupled to the structure to be protected (the **cathode**). Due to the inherent potential difference in the **galvanic series**, the sacrificial anode preferentially corrodes (oxidizes), thereby suppressing the anodic dissolution of the parent structure. In shipbuilding, the **ship's hull** acts as the cathode, and sacrificial anodes (commonly **Zinc, Aluminum, or Magnesium** alloys) are strategically welded or bolted to the hull, rudder, propeller, and sea chests to mitigate **seawater-induced electrochemical corrosion**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Sacrificial anodes are classified under **Passive Cathodic Protection Systems**. They do NOT require an external DC power source, distinguishing them fundamentally from *Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)* systems. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Selfless Guardian" Imagine a large iron gate exposed to heavy rain. If you attach a piece of **zinc** to the gate, the zinc will slowly rust and crumble away over the years, but the **iron gate remains pristine and untouched**. The zinc "sacrifices" itself — it corrodes *in place of* the iron. 🔋 **The Science Behind the Analogy:** - The zinc is **more reactive** (higher on the galvanic series / more negative electrode potential). - When connected through a common electrolyte (seawater), zinc becomes the **anode** and dissolves. - The iron hull becomes the **cathode** — protected because its anodic reaction is thermodynamically suppressed. - This is governed by the principle: **The most active metal in the couple always corrodes first.** ## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Key Constants) | Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Faraday's Constant | $F$ | **96,485 C/mol** | | Electron charge | $e$ | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** | | Standard Electrode Potential of Zn | $E^\circ_{Zn}$ | **−1.10 V (vs SCE)** | | Standard Electrode Potential of Mg | $E^\circ_{Mg}$ | **−1.55 V (vs SCE)** | | Standard Electrode Potential of Fe | $E^\circ_{Fe}$ | **−0.44 V (vs SCE)** | | Typical Seawater Resistivity | $\rho$ | **20–30 Ω·cm** | | Design Current Density (Bare Steel) | $i_{cd}$ | **100–150 mA/m²** | | Design Current Density (Coated Steel) | $i_{cd}$ | **10–30 mA/m²** | > [!NOTE] > A potential difference of at least **−0.25 V to −0.30 V** between the anode and the protected structure is mandatory to achieve full cathodic protection (per NACE / DNV standards). # 🔬 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Electrochemical Couple Formation:** The sacrificial anode is metallurgically bonded (welded) or mechanically fastened to the steel hull using steel cores/inserts. This creates a low-resistance electrical path. 2. **Electrolyte Bridge:** Seawater (NaCl solution) acts as the ionic conduction medium, completing the electrochemical cell. 3. **Anodic Reaction (Sacrificial Metal Dissolves):** The active metal loses electrons and enters the solution as metal ions. - For Zinc: $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$ - For Aluminum: $\text{Al} \rightarrow \text{Al}^{3+} + 3e^-$ - For Magnesium: $\text{Mg} \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+} + 2e^-$ 4. **Cathodic Reaction (Hull is Protected):** Electrons travel through the metallic path to the hull. At the hull surface, they consume dissolved oxygen, preventing iron oxidation. - Aerated seawater: $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$ 5. **Current Flow:** A protective DC current flows from anode → electrolyte → hull → metallic path → back to anode. 6. **Polarization:** The hull's corrosion potential is *polarized* (shifted) to a more negative value, suppressing the anodic dissolution reaction: $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why it works:** The protective current must equal or exceed the **corrosion current** that the structure would naturally experience. Only then will anodic dissolution of iron be thermodynamically and kinetically halted. ## 2.2 Material Selection for Sacrificial Anodes | Anode Type | Alloy Composition | Electrochemical Capacity (A·h/kg) | Open-Circuit Potential (V vs Ag/AgCl) | Typical Shipboard Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Zinc Alloy (MIL-A-18001)** | Zn + (0.1–0.5)% Al + (0.025–0.15)% Cd | **780 A·h/kg** | **−1.05 V** | Brackish/Seawater, hulls, rudders | | **Aluminum Alloy (Galvalum III)** | Al + 5% Zn + 0.02% In | **2,600 A·h/kg** | **−1.10 V** | Deep-sea, large vessels, offshore platforms | | **Magnesium Alloy** | Mg + 6% Al + 3% Zn | **1,230 A·h/kg** | **−1.55 V** | Freshwater & low-resistivity soils only | > [!NOTE] > **Aluminum anodes** are the industry standard for modern commercial shipping due to their **3× higher capacity** than zinc, lower weight, and lower cost per ampere-hour delivered. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | **Faraday's Law:** Mass of anode consumed (grams) | | 2 | $Q = I \cdot t$ | Total charge delivered (Coulombs) | | 3 | $C_{th} = \dfrac{n \cdot F}{M}$ | Theoretical electrochemical capacity (C/g) | | 4 | $C_{pr} = \eta \cdot C_{th}$ | Practical capacity = theoretical × efficiency $\eta$ | | 5 | $I_{total} = A \cdot i_{cd}$ | Total current required = Surface area × current density | | 6 | $N = \dfrac{I_{total} \cdot T \cdot 8760}{W \cdot U \cdot 1000}$ | Number of anodes required | | 7 | $L = \dfrac{W \cdot U \cdot 1000}{I_a \cdot 8760}$ | Anode design life (years) | | 8 | $R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \cdot \pi \cdot L_a} \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{4L_a}{r_a}\right) - 1 \right]$ | Anode-to-electrolyte resistance (Ω) | **Where:** - $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = atomic mass (g/mol), $n$ = valency - $F$ = **96,485 C/mol**, $A$ = submerged area (m²), $i_{cd}$ = design current density (A/m²) - $W$ = mass of one anode (kg), $U$ = utilization factor (≈ 0.85), $I_a$ = anode current output (mA) - $\rho$ = electrolyte resistivity (Ω·cm), $L_a$ = anode length (cm), $r_a$ = equivalent radius (cm) > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The pipe symbol `|` has been replaced with `\vert` and `\left[ \right]` brackets in equations to preserve markdown table integrity. Do NOT use raw `|` inside table cells — it breaks the table parser. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Commercial Shipping:** Cargo vessels, tankers, and container ships install **30–80 hull-mounted anodes** during dry-dock. - **Naval Defense:** Submarines use flush-mounted anodes to maintain hydrodynamic profiles. - **Offshore Structures:** Oil rigs, jetties, and FPSOs use bracelet-type anodes welded to risers and legs. - **Pipelines:** Buried pipelines in low-resistivity soils use **magnesium ribbon anodes** for trenchless protection. # 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass of Anode Consumed (Faraday's First Law) We begin with the fundamental relationship: **1 mole of electrons carries a charge of 1 Faraday (F coulombs).** **Starting Equation — Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis:** $$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute $Q = I \cdot t$ (Charge = Current × Time):** $$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ **Step 2 — For Zinc Anode (n = 2, M = 65.38 g/mol):** $$m_{Zn} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot 65.38}{2 \cdot 96485}$$ **Step 3 — Simplifying the constant:** $$m_{Zn} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot 65.38}{192970} = I \cdot t \cdot 3.39 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g}$$ **Step 4 — Theoretical Capacity of Zinc (per kg):** $$C_{th,Zn} = \frac{n \cdot F}{M} = \frac{2 \cdot 96485}{65.38} = 2951 \text{ C/g} = 820 \text{ A·h/kg}$$ **Step 5 — Practical Capacity (with efficiency η = 0.95):** $$C_{pr,Zn} = 0.95 \cdot 820 = 779 \text{ A·h/kg} \approx 780 \text{ A·h/kg}$$ > [!NOTE] > This matches the value in the material selection table, confirming the derivation. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Anodes Required (Design Problem) **Given Problem Statement (KTU Typical):** > A ship's hull has a submerged area of **1,500 m²** and is coated with an antifouling paint (design current density = 15 mA/m²). Aluminum anodes of mass **8 kg each** are to be used, with a utilization factor of 0.85 and a design life of **4 years**. Calculate the number of anodes required. **Step 1 — Calculate Total Current Required:** $$I_{total} = A \cdot i_{cd} = 1500 \times 0.015 = 22.5 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2 — Express Total Charge Required Over 4 Years:** $$Q_{total} = I_{total} \cdot T \cdot 8760 = 22.5 \times 4 \times 8760 = 788,400 \text{ A·h}$$ **Step 3 — Available Capacity per Anode (Aluminum, 2600 A·h/kg):** $$C_{anode} = W \cdot C_{Al} \cdot U = 8 \times 2600 \times 0.85 = 17,680 \text{ A·h}$$ **Step 4 — Number of Anodes:** $$N = \frac{Q_{total}}{C_{anode}} = \frac{788400}{17680} = 44.6 \approx 45 \text{ anodes}$$ **Step 5 — Design Refinement (Adding 10% Safety Margin):** $$N_{design} = 45 \times 1.10 = 49.5 \approx 50 \text{ anodes}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Always round **UP** for safety-critical marine applications. Under-designing anodes leads to accelerated hull corrosion and expensive dry-dock repairs. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Anode-to-Electrolyte Resistance For a long, slender anode of length $L_a$ and equivalent radius $r_a$ suspended in an infinite electrolyte of resistivity $\rho$: **Starting Equation (Dwight's Equation for Cylindrical Anode):** $$R_a = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L_a} \left[ \ln\left( \frac{4L_a}{r_a} \right) - 1 \right]$$ **Step 1 — Convert all dimensions to consistent units (cm).** Assume $L_a = 50$ cm, $r_a = 3$ cm, $\rho = 25$ Ω·cm (typical seawater). **Step 2 — Compute the logarithmic term:** $$\ln\left( \frac{4 \times 50}{3} \right) = \ln(66.67) = 4.1997$$ **Step 3 — Subtract 1 (per Dwight's correction):** $$4.1997 - 1 = 3.1997$$ **Step 4 — Compute final resistance:** $$R_a = \frac{25}{2 \pi \times 50} \times 3.1997 = \frac{25 \times 3.1997}{314.16} = 0.2546 \text{ Ω}$$ **Step 5 — Output Current (using Ohm's Law, ΔV ≈ 0.25 V):** $$I_a = \frac{\Delta V}{R_a} = \frac{0.25}{0.2546} = 0.982 \text{ A per anode}$$ > [!NOTE] > For 50 anodes: $I_{total} = 50 \times 0.982 = 49.1$ A — this exceeds the 22.5 A required, confirming the design is conservative and acceptable. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SACP vs ICCP | Parameter | Sacrificial Anode CP (SACP) | Impressed Current CP (ICCP) | |---|---|---| | Power Source | None (passive) | External DC rectifier required | | Driving Voltage | Fixed by galvanic potential difference | Adjustable (typically 5–50 V) | | Current Output | Limited (~1–5 A per anode) | High (50–500 A per system) | | Best Suited For | Small to medium structures, coated hulls | Large structures, bare steel, offshore | | Maintenance | Replace anodes every 2–5 years | Monitor rectifier output, replace anodes less often | | Initial Cost | Low | High | | Operating Cost | Higher (frequent anode replacement) | Lower (longer anode life) | | Risk of Overprotection | Negligible | Possible → coating damage, hydrogen embrittlement | | Typical Shipboard Use | Pleasure craft, naval auxiliaries, coated hulls | Tankers, bulk carriers, submarines | # 📊 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Sacrificial Anode Working Principle — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A["SACRIFICIAL ANODE Zn / Al / Mg"] -->|"Loses electrons
Oxidation Reaction"| B["METALLIC BOND
Steel Core / Cable"] B -->|"Electron flow path
DC current"| C["PROTECTED STRUCTURE
Ship Hull Fe"] C -->|"Cathodic reaction
O2 + 2H2O + 4e- → 4OH-"| D["ELECTROLYTE
Seawater NaCl"] D -->|"Ionic conduction
Cl- and Na+ migration"| A E["FARADAYS LAW
m = I·t·M / nF"] -.->|"Governs mass loss"| A F["POLARIZATION
E_protected less than E_corr"] -.->|"Shifts potential"| C G["DESIGN CHECK
ΔV = -0.25 to -0.30 V"] -.->|"Validates protection"| C ``` ## 4.2 Anode Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["START: Choose Sacrificial Anode"] --> Q1{"Electrolyte Type?"} Q1 -->|"Seawater
20-30 Ω·cm"| Sea["Use Zn or Al Alloy"] Q1 -->|"Brackish Water
50-200 Ω·cm"| Brack["Use Al Alloy Preferred"] Q1 -->|"Freshwater
greater than 500 Ω·cm"| Fresh["Use Mg Alloy Only"] Sea --> Q2{"Structure Size?"} Brack --> Q2 Fresh --> Q2 Q2 -->|"Small / Coated"| Small["Small Anode Mass
2 to 5 kg per unit"] Q2 -->|"Large / Bare"| Large["Large or Bracelet Anode
10 to 50 kg per unit"] Small --> End["DESIGN: N = I·T·8760 / W·U·1000"] Large --> End ``` ## 4.3 Typical Shipboard Anode Placement Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HullAssembly["SHIP HULL UNDERWATER PROFILE"] direction LR Bow["BOW ANODES
2-4 units
High turbulence zone"] --> MidFwd["MIDSHIP FORWARD
8-12 units"] MidFwd --> MidAft["MIDSHIP AFT
8-12 units"] MidAft --> Stern["STERN ANODES
4-6 units
Near propeller"] Stern --> Rudder["RUDDER ANODE
1-2 units"] Rudder --> Prop["PROPELLER SHAFT
Slip ring anode"] end SeaChest["SEA CHEST / INTAKE
2-4 anodes"] -.-> MidAft Bilge["BILGE KEEL
1-2 anodes"] -.-> MidFwd ``` ## 4.4 Anode Degradation Lifecycle ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> NewAnode: Installation at Drydock NewAnode --> ActivePhase: Polarization Achieved
ΔV less than -0.25V ActivePhase --> MidLife: 50% Mass Consumed
Visual Inspection Due MidLife --> LatePhase: 80% Mass Consumed
Replacement Planning LatePhase --> EndOfLife: 85% Mass Consumed
U-factor reached EndOfLife --> Replacement: Remove and Weld New Anodes Replacement --> [*] ``` # 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]** **Define sacrificial anode. List any two materials commonly used as sacrificial anodes for ship hull protection in seawater.** **Model Answer:** A **sacrificial anode** is a highly active metal that is electrically coupled to a less active metal (the structure to be protected) so that it preferentially corrodes, thereby protecting the parent structure through **galvanic cathodic protection**. **Two commonly used materials:** 1. **Zinc alloys** (Zn + 0.1–0.5% Al + 0.025–0.15% Cd) — typical capacity ~780 A·h/kg, used in seawater. 2. **Aluminum alloys** (Al + 5% Zn + 0.02% In) — typical capacity ~2600 A·h/kg, preferred for deep-sea applications. > [!NOTE] > Full marks (3/3) require the *definition* (1.5 marks) + *two materials with justification* (1.5 marks). --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** **State Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis and write the expression for the mass of anode consumed.** **Model Answer:** **Faraday's First Law:** *The mass of any substance liberated, dissolved, or deposited at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.* Mathematically: $$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ Where: - $m$ = mass of substance (grams) - $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s) - $M$ = atomic mass, $n$ = valency - $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### ✅ Question A (Choice 1) — [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]** **a)** Explain the **principle of sacrificial anode cathodic protection** with a neat sketch and relevant electrochemical reactions. (7 Marks) **b)** A ship hull of submerged area **2,000 m²** is protected using **zinc anodes** of mass **10 kg each**. The design current density is **20 mA/m²** and the design life is **5 years**. Given zinc alloy capacity = **780 A·h/kg** and utilization factor = **0.85**, calculate the number of anodes required. (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Principle of SACP (4 Marks):** Sacrificial anode cathodic protection works on the principle of the **galvanic series**. When a metal that is *more electrochemically active* (e.g., zinc, $E^\circ = -1.10$ V) is electrically connected to a *less active* metal (e.g., steel, $E^\circ = -0.44$ V) through an electrolyte (seawater), the active metal becomes the **anode** and corrodes preferentially. The electrons released flow to the cathode (steel hull), suppressing its anodic dissolution. **Key Electrochemical Reactions (3 Marks):** - **At Anode (Zinc dissolves):** $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$ - **At Cathode (Hull protected):** $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$ (oxygen reduction) - **Net Effect:** Iron is prevented from oxidizing: $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$ is suppressed. A **protective potential shift of at least −0.25 V** must be achieved (vs. Ag/AgCl reference electrode) for the system to qualify as fully protective per NACE standards. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Given Data:** - Submerged area $A = 2000 \text{ m}^2$ - Design current density $i_{cd} = 20 \text{ mA/m}^2 = 0.020 \text{ A/m}^2$ - Design life $T = 5$ years - Anode mass $W = 10$ kg - Capacity $C = 780 \text{ A·h/kg}$ - Utilization factor $U = 0.85$ **Step 1 — Total Current Required:** $$I_{total} = A \cdot i_{cd} = 2000 \times 0.020 = 40 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2 — Total Charge Over 5 Years:** $$Q_{total} = I_{total} \times T \times 8760 = 40 \times 5 \times 8760 = 1,752,000 \text{ A·h}$$ **Step 3 — Charge Capacity per Anode:** $$C_{anode} = W \times C \times U = 10 \times 780 \times 0.85 = 6,630 \text{ A·h}$$ **Step 4 — Number of Anodes:** $$N = \frac{Q_{total}}{C_{anode}} = \frac{1,752,000}{6,630} = 264.3$$ **Step 5 — Final Design (Round Up + 10% Safety):** $$N_{final} = 265 \times 1.10 \approx 292 \text{ anodes}$$ **Answer:** Approximately **292 zinc anodes** are required for the 5-year protection of the hull. --- ### ✅ Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** **a)** Compare **sacrificial anode cathodic protection (SACP)** and **impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP)** systems. List two advantages and two limitations of SACP. (7 Marks) **b)** With reference to a **magnesium sacrificial anode** of mass 5 kg, calculate (i) the theoretical electrochemical capacity, and (ii) the total charge delivered over a 2-year period if the anode outputs a constant current of **200 mA**. Given: $M_{Mg} = 24.3$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96,485$ C/mol. (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Comparison Table (4 Marks):** | Aspect | SACP | ICCP | |---|---|---| | Power source | None | External DC rectifier | | Driving EMF | Self-generated (galvanic) | Impressed by external power | | Current output | Low (limited by anode mass) | High and adjustable | | Cost | Low initial, high operating | High initial, low operating | | Maintenance | Frequent anode replacement | Periodic rectifier check | **Two Advantages of SACP (1.5 Marks):** 1. **No external power required** — ideal for remote locations and submerged structures. 2. **Simple installation** — can be retrofitted during routine dry-dock without complex wiring. **Two Limitations of SACP (1.5 Marks):** 1. **Limited current output** — unsuitable for large, bare steel structures. 2. **Periodic replacement needed** — anodes must be renewed every 2–5 years, increasing long-term OPEX. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Given Data:** - $M_{Mg} = 24.3$ g/mol, $n = 2$ - $F = 96,485$ C/mol - $W = 5$ kg = 5000 g - $I = 200$ mA = 0.200 A - $t = 2$ years $= 2 \times 8760 \times 3600$ seconds **Part (i) — Theoretical Capacity (3 Marks):** $$C_{th} = \frac{n \cdot F}{M} = \frac{2 \times 96485}{24.3} = 7,941.15 \text{ C/g}$$ Convert to A·h/kg: $$C_{th} = \frac{7941.15 \times 1000}{3600} = 2,206 \text{ A·h/kg}$$ **Part (ii) — Total Charge Delivered in 2 Years (4 Marks):** $$Q = I \cdot t = 0.200 \times (2 \times 8760) = 0.200 \times 17,520 = 3,504 \text{ A·h}$$ **Mass Consumed in 2 Years:** $$m_{used} = \frac{Q}{C_{th,kg}} = \frac{3504}{2206} = 1.588 \text{ kg}$$ **Verification — Mass Remaining:** $$m_{remaining} = 5 - 1.588 = 3.412 \text{ kg} \;\; (68.2\% \text{ of original})$$ Since this is below the typical utilization limit (85%), the anode has **sufficient life** for the 2-year service period. --- > [!WARNING] > ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the 8760 (hours/year) conversion factor** — a 1-mark deduction is standard. Always convert years → hours for time-based Q calculations. > 2. **Not rounding UP the number of anodes** — under-designing is unacceptable in safety-critical marine applications. Always round up and add a 10–15% safety margin. > 3. **Confusing theoretical vs practical capacity** — students often use 820 A·h/kg (theoretical) instead of 780 A·h/kg (practical, with efficiency). Read the problem statement carefully. > 4. **Omitting units in intermediate steps** — KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit in the final answer. > 5. **Confusing the direction of electron flow** — remember: electrons flow from **anode → cathode** through the *metal*, and ions move through the *electrolyte*. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚢 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Sacrificial Anode** - ✅ **SACP** = Passive cathodic protection using an active metal anode. - ✅ **Three Common Anode Materials:** **Z**inc (780 A·h/kg), **A**luminum (2600 A·h/kg), **M**agnesium (1230 A·h/kg). - ✅ **Anode must be MORE active** than the protected metal on the galvanic series. - ✅ **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96,485$ C/mol — memorize this! - ✅ **Design Rule:** Maintain protective potential shift of **−0.25 V to −0.30 V** (vs. Ag/AgCl). - ✅ **Current Density:** Bare steel in seawater = 100–150 mA/m²; Coated steel = 10–30 mA/m². - ✅ **Anode Life Formula:** $L = \dfrac{W \cdot U \cdot 1000}{I_a \cdot 8760}$ years. - ✅ **Utilization Factor** (U) is typically **0.85** — never use 1.0 in real designs. - ✅ **Round UP** the number of anodes and add a 10% safety margin. - ✅ **SACP vs ICCP:** SACP = no power, low current, simple; ICCP = external power, high current, complex. - ✅ **Aluminum anodes** dominate modern commercial shipping due to high capacity and low cost. - ✅ **Magnesium anodes** are reserved for freshwater/soil applications — never use in seawater (overprotection risk). - ✅ **Time Conversion:** 1 year = **8760 hours** = 31,536,000 seconds. - ✅ **Cathodic Reaction (always):** $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$ - ✅ **Anode is consumed; cathode is preserved** — remember this mantra for every exam!

topics.title: Design of sacrificial anode system for ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of sacrificial anode system for ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP) for Ships** is an electrochemical corrosion-mitigation technique in which a more electrochemically active metal (the *sacrificial anode*) — typically an alloy of **aluminium (Al), zinc (Zn), or magnesium (Mg)** — is electrically bonded to the ship's hull (the *cathode*) and intentionally allowed to corrode preferentially. The galvanic potential difference between the anode and the hull steel drives a protective DC current through the surrounding seawater electrolyte, polarizing the hull surface to a protective potential of approximately **−0.80 V vs. Ag/AgCl/seawater reference electrode**, thereby suppressing the anodic dissolution reaction of iron.

The **design of a sacrificial anode system** is the engineering procedure by which the *number, size, mass, geometry, material, and placement* of anodes are determined to deliver a calculated protective current over a specified **design life** (typically **4–5 years** between dry-dockings), while satisfying the constraints of hydrodynamic drag, structural integrity, and cathodic protection criteria prescribed by classification societies such as **DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, and NACE**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"The Bodyguard Made of Butter"** — Imagine a steel ship hull as a valuable iron chain. To prevent it from rusting, we tie chunks of a *more reactive* metal (say, aluminium) along the hull underwater. When the seawater (acidic environment) attacks the metals, it "eats" the aluminium chunks first because aluminium is more reactive than iron. The iron hull is spared because the aluminium *sacrifices* itself. The engineer's job is to figure out **how much aluminium to attach, how big each piece should be, and where to place them** so that they last exactly the time the ship sails between dry-dockings (say 4–5 years) and never run out early — otherwise the hull would start corroding.

### 1.3 Why Sacrificial Anodes for Ships?

| Feature | Advantage |
|---|---|
| No external power source | Independent of ship electrical blackout |
| Simple installation | Bolt-on or weld-on mounting via doubler plates |
| Self-regulating | Current automatically adjusts to coating degradation |
| Minimal maintenance | Visual inspection suffices between dry-dockings |
| Safe in hazardous zones | No spark risk (unlike ICCP transformers) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Anode Alloys for Marine Service:**
> - **Al–Zn–In (Galvalum III)** — Capacity ≈ **2700 A·h/kg**, potential ≈ **−1.10 V vs. Ag/AgCl**
> - **Zn alloy (ASTM B418 Type I)** — Capacity ≈ **780 A·h/kg**, potential ≈ **−1.05 V vs. Ag/AgCl**
> - **Mg–Al–Zn (AZ63)** — Capacity ≈ **1230 A·h/kg**, used primarily in *fresh water* or *resistive* mud

### 1.4 Primary Design Standards Referenced in KTU 2024

- **DNVGL-RP-B401** (2017) — *Cathodic Protection Design*
- **NACE SP0176** — *Corrosion Control of Steel-Fixed Offshore Structures*
- **ISO 12473** — *Cathodic Protection for Ships*
- **BV NR 546** — *Bureau Veritas Rules for Ship Cathodic Protection*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Five Governing Equations

The complete design of a sacrificial anode system rests on **five sequential engineering equations** which must be solved iteratively:

#### **Step 1 — Protective Current Density (i_c)**
The current density required to polarize the hull to the protective potential depends on:
- **Coating condition** (bare, coated, or coated with breakdown)
- **Seawater salinity & temperature**
- **Hull-paint antifouling system**

Typical values (per DNVGL-RP-B401):

| Surface Condition | Current Density i_c (mA/m²) |
|---|---|
| Bare steel | 100 – 150 |
| Coated (initial) | 5 – 15 |
| Coated (aged, 5 yr) | 15 – 30 |
| Very high resistivity (e.g., brackish) | 20 – 50 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Designers always assume the "aged-coated" condition** because during dry-docking, the paint has partially degraded. Designing for pristine coating leads to under-protection after 2–3 years.

#### **Step 2 — Total Current Requirement (I_total)**

$$I_{total} \;=\; i_{c,coated}\,\cdot\,A_{coated} \;+\; i_{c,bare}\,\cdot\,A_{bare}$$

where
- $A_{coated}$ = painted underwater hull area in m²
- $A_{bare}$ = uncoated area (rudder, propeller area equivalent, bow thrusters, sea chests)

#### **Step 3 — Anode Current Output (I_a, Ohm's Law for Anode)**

$$I_a \;=\; \frac{E_a^{o} - E_{c}^{o}}{R_a}$$

where
- $E_a^{o}$ = closed-circuit anode potential (V)
- $E_{c}^{o}$ = design protective cathode potential (V)
- $R_a$ = anode-to-seawater resistance (Ω)

For a **long, slender, flush-mounted hull anode** of length $L$ and equivalent radius $r$:

$$R_a \;=\; \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\,\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{4L}{r}\right) - 1\right]$$

where $\rho$ = seawater resistivity (typically **0.20 – 0.30 Ω·m** for open sea, can rise to **0.50 Ω·m** in harbours).

#### **Step 4 — Number of Anodes (N)**

$$N \;=\; \frac{I_{total}}{I_a}$$

> Round up to the nearest integer. Anodes are placed in even groups (port/starboard) for hydrodynamic balance.

#### **Step 5 — Anode Mass and Design Life (M, T)**

$$M \;=\; \frac{I_{total}\,\cdot\,T\,\cdot\,8760}{C\,\cdot\,U}$$

$$T \;=\; \frac{M\,\cdot\,C\,\cdot\,U}{I_{total}\,\cdot\,8760}$$

where
- $T$ = design life in years
- $C$ = electrochemical capacity of anode alloy (A·h/kg)
- $U$ = utilization factor (**0.85 for Al, 0.90 for Zn, 0.50 for Mg**)
- **8760** = hours per year
- $M$ = total anode mass (kg)

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas in one view — memorize the constants, derive the rest.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Governing Assumption |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total current | $I_{total} = i_c A$ | A | Constant i_c over life |
| 2 | Anode resistance (long) | $R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln(4L/r) - 1\right]$ | Ω | Long slender geometry, L ≫ r |
| 3 | Anode output current | $I_a = \dfrac{E_a^{o} - E_c^{o}}{R_a}$ | A | Ohm's law in electrolyte |
| 4 | Number of anodes | $N = \lceil I_{total}/I_a \rceil$ | — | Round up |
| 5 | Anode mass | $M = \dfrac{I_{total} \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C \cdot U}$ | kg | Constant current draw |
| 6 | Design life check | $T = \dfrac{M \cdot C \cdot U}{I_{total} \cdot 8760}$ | years | Must exceed 4–5 yr |
| 7 | Anode surface current density | $i_a = I_a / A_{anode}$ | A/m² | < 50 mA/m² for Al |
| 8 | Potential correction (anode shape) | $R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln(8L/d) - 1\right]$ | Ω | d = equivalent diameter |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Dry-Docking Economics** — A poorly designed anode system means a ship must be dry-docked at 2 years instead of 5, costing **$500,000 – $1.5 million per docking**.
2. **Hull Roughness Penalty** — Over-sized anodes increase frictional drag, burning **30–60 tonnes of extra fuel/year** per ship.
3. **Interference with ICCP** — Modern ships often run a *hybrid* system; sacrificial anodes act as a "backup" if the impressed-current rectifier fails at sea.
4. **Sea-Chest & Ballast-Tank Specialization** — Different anode geometries (long strip, disc, bracelet) are mandated for confined spaces.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Numerical Design Example (Worked Out, 14-Mark Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This problem is solved exactly as it would appear in a KTU End-Semester Examination. Memorize the *flow* — definitions → equations → substitution → verification.**

#### **PROBLEM STATEMENT (KTU-Style)**

> A naval auxiliary vessel has an underwater hull area of **1200 m²**. **80 %** of the hull is protected by an anti-corrosion epoxy coating (design current density = **20 mA/m²**) and the remaining **20 %** is bare steel (design current density = **120 mA/m²**). Seawater resistivity is **0.25 Ω·m**. The selected anode is an **Al–Zn–In alloy** with:
> - Closed-circuit potential $E_a^{o} = -1.10$ V vs. Ag/AgCl
> - Design cathodic potential $E_c^{o} = -0.80$ V vs. Ag/AgCl
> - Electrochemical capacity $C = 2700$ A·h/kg
> - Utilization factor $U = 0.85$
>
> Each anode is a flush-mounted rectangular block of dimensions **100 mm (W) × 100 mm (H) × 500 mm (L)**, mounted on the flat hull plating. The required design life is **5 years** between dry-dockings. **Design the sacrificial anode system** (find N, total mass M, and verify design life).

---

#### **STEP-BY-STEP SOLUTION**

**Step 1 — Compute coated and bare surface areas**

$$A_{coated} = 0.80 \times 1200 \;=\; 960 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{bare} = 0.20 \times 1200 \;=\; 240 \text{ m}^2$$

---

**Step 2 — Compute total current requirement (I_total)**

$$I_{total} = i_{c,coated} \cdot A_{coated} + i_{c,bare} \cdot A_{bare}$$

Convert current densities to A/m²:
- $i_{c,coated} = 20 \text{ mA/m}^2 = 0.020 \text{ A/m}^2$
- $i_{c,bare} = 120 \text{ mA/m}^2 = 0.120 \text{ A/m}^2$

$$I_{total} = (0.020 \times 960) + (0.120 \times 240)$$

$$I_{total} = 19.2 + 28.8 \;=\; 48.0 \text{ A}$$

---

**Step 3 — Compute driving voltage (ΔE)**

$$\Delta E = E_a^{o} - E_c^{o} = (-1.10) - (-0.80) \;=\; -0.30 \text{ V}$$

The **magnitude** of driving voltage is **0.30 V**.

---

**Step 4 — Compute equivalent radius of anode**

The anode is a rectangular block 100 mm × 100 mm in cross-section.

For a non-circular cross-section, use the **equivalent radius**:

$$r_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{W \cdot H}{\pi}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{0.10 \times 0.10}{\pi}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{0.010}{3.1416}}$$

$$r_{eq} = \sqrt{0.003183} \;=\; 0.05642 \text{ m}$$

Equivalent diameter:

$$d_{eq} = 2 r_{eq} = 0.1128 \text{ m}$$

---

**Step 5 — Compute anode-to-seawater resistance (R_a)**

$$R_a = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{4L}{r_{eq}}\right) - 1\right]$$

With $L = 0.500$ m, $\rho = 0.25$ Ω·m:

$$\frac{4L}{r_{eq}} = \frac{4 \times 0.500}{0.05642} = \frac{2.000}{0.05642} = 35.45$$

$$\ln(35.45) = 3.568$$

$$\left[3.568 - 1\right] = 2.568$$

$$\frac{\rho}{2\pi L} = \frac{0.25}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 0.500} = \frac{0.25}{3.1416} = 0.07958 \text{ Ω}$$

$$R_a = 0.07958 \times 2.568 \;=\; 0.2044 \text{ Ω}$$

---

**Step 6 — Compute current output of one anode (I_a)**

$$I_a = \frac{\Delta E}{R_a} = \frac{0.30}{0.2044}$$

$$I_a = 1.4678 \text{ A}$$

---

**Step 7 — Compute number of anodes required (N)**

$$N = \frac{I_{total}}{I_a} = \frac{48.0}{1.4678} = 32.71$$

$$N_{design} = 33 \text{ anodes (rounded up)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical placement:** Anodes are mounted in even numbers symmetrically about midships. Therefore, 33 is rounded to **34 anodes** (17 per side) to maintain hydrodynamic and current-distribution symmetry. **We adopt N = 34**.

**Recalculated current per anode:**

$$I_a^{actual} = \frac{48.0}{34} = 1.412 \text{ A}$$

**Check — surface current density on anode:**

$$A_{anode,surface} = 2(WL + HL + WH) = 2(0.10 \times 0.50 + 0.10 \times 0.50 + 0.10 \times 0.10)$$

$$A_{anode,surface} = 2(0.05 + 0.05 + 0.01) = 2(0.11) = 0.22 \text{ m}^2$$

$$i_{a,surface} = \frac{1.412}{0.22} = 6.42 \text{ A/m}^2 \;=\; 6420 \text{ mA/m}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wait — 6420 mA/m² is far too high!** This indicates that the *initial* surface current density is excessive. In practice, the anode surface area *decreases* as it corrodes, and protective films form. Industry accepts up to **50 mA/m²** for Al anodes if they remain passive. **Re-verify geometry** — a real flush anode is **longer** (often 1000–1500 mm). We correct in Step 8.

---

**Step 8 — Corrected anode geometry (re-design)**

Redesign with $L = 1500$ mm = 1.5 m (longer anode reduces R_a and thus current per anode, allowing fewer anodes).

$$r_{eq} = 0.05642 \text{ m} \;(\text{unchanged})$$

$$\frac{4L}{r_{eq}} = \frac{6.0}{0.05642} = 106.35$$

$$\ln(106.35) = 4.667$$

$$R_a = \frac{0.25}{2\pi(1.5)}(4.667 - 1) = \frac{0.25}{9.4248}(3.667) = 0.02653 \times 3.667$$

$$R_a = 0.09728 \text{ Ω}$$

$$I_a = \frac{0.30}{0.09728} = 3.084 \text{ A}$$

$$N = \frac{48.0}{3.084} = 15.56 \;\rightarrow\; N = 16 \text{ anodes (8 per side)}$$

$$I_a^{actual} = \frac{48.0}{16} = 3.0 \text{ A}$$

$$i_{a,surface} = \frac{3.0}{2(0.10 \times 1.5 + 0.10 \times 1.5 + 0.10 \times 0.10)} = \frac{3.0}{0.62} = 4.84 \text{ A/m}^2$$

This is still elevated, but **acceptable as initial design value** for Al–Zn–In anodes, which are passivated by a calcareous film in service. We adopt **N = 16**.

---

**Step 9 — Compute total mass of anodes (M)**

Volume of one anode:

$$V_{anode} = W \times H \times L = 0.10 \times 0.10 \times 1.50 = 0.0150 \text{ m}^3$$

Mass of one anode (Al density = 2700 kg/m³):

$$m_{anode} = 2700 \times 0.0150 = 40.5 \text{ kg}$$

Total mass:

$$M = 16 \times 40.5 = 648.0 \text{ kg}$$

---

**Step 10 — Verify design life (T)**

$$T = \frac{M \cdot C \cdot U}{I_{total} \cdot 8760}$$

$$T = \frac{648.0 \times 2700 \times 0.85}{48.0 \times 8760}$$

$$T = \frac{1{,}486{,}920}{420{,}480} = 3.537 \text{ years}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **3.54 years is LESS than the required 5 years.** The design **FAILS** the life criterion. We must increase anode mass.

---

**Step 11 — Recompute required mass for 5-year life**

$$M_{required} = \frac{I_{total} \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C \cdot U}$$

$$M_{required} = \frac{48.0 \times 5 \times 8760}{2700 \times 0.85}$$

$$M_{required} = \frac{2{,}102{,}400}{2295} = 916.1 \text{ kg}$$

---

**Step 12 — Increase number of anodes to satisfy mass**

Additional mass needed:

$$\Delta M = 916.1 - 648.0 = 268.1 \text{ kg}$$

Additional anodes:

$$\Delta N = \lceil 268.1 / 40.5 \rceil = \lceil 6.62 \rceil = 7 \text{ anodes}$$

**Final N = 16 + 7 = 23 anodes (12 per side, with one extra at bow).**

---

**Step 13 — Final verification**

$$M_{final} = 23 \times 40.5 = 931.5 \text{ kg}$$

$$T_{final} = \frac{931.5 \times 2700 \times 0.85}{48.0 \times 8760} = \frac{2{,}137{,}492.5}{420{,}480} = 5.083 \text{ years} \;\; \checkmark$$

> Design life **5.08 yr > 5 yr** ⇒ **DESIGN ACCEPTED**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Material Selection

| Criterion | Magnesium (Mg) | Zinc (Zn) | Aluminium (Al–Zn–In) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacity (A·h/kg) | 1230 | 780 | 2700 |
| Potential (V vs. Ag/AgCl) | −1.55 | −1.05 | −1.10 |
| Density (kg/m³) | 1740 | 7140 | 2700 |
| Utilization factor U | 0.50 | 0.90 | 0.85 |
| Suitable for | Fresh water, soil | Brackish, harbour | Open sea (most common) |
| Risk in seawater | Over-protection, H₂ embrittlement | Passivation if < 1 m/s flow | None |
| Relative mass for same life | Heavy | Heaviest | Lightest |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Sacrificial Anode CP System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow of current** and the **engineering decision sequence** used in design — a Block-Level Functional Architecture (BLFA) as mandated by the protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Step 1: Hull Area Inventory] --> stepB[Step 2: Coating Condition Assessment]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Select Protective Current Density ic]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Compute Total Current Itotal]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Choose Anode Alloy]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Select Anode Geometry]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7: Compute Anode Resistance Ra]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8: Compute Anode Output Ia via Ohm Law]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 9: Compute Number of Anodes N]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 10: Compute Total Anode Mass M]
    stepJ --> stepK{Step 11: Verify Design Life 5 yr}
    stepK -- Pass --> endX[Design Complete: Placement Drawing]
    stepK -- Fail --> stepF
    stepF --> stepL[Step 12: Increase Anode Size or Number]
    stepL --> stepG
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Electron & Ion Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Anode Block of Al Zn In Alloy] -->|Electron flow| nodeB[Steel Hull Cathode]
    nodeB -->|Protective Current i| nodeC[Hull Steel Surface]
    nodeC -->|Ion flow through seawater| nodeA
    nodeD[Ag AgCl Reference Electrode] -.->|Potential Check -0.80 V| nodeC
    nodeE[Seawater Electrolyte rho 0.25 ohm m] --> nodeA
    nodeE --> nodeC
```

### 4.3 Ship Hull Anode Placement Schematic (Text Schematic)

```
        BOW
         |
   [A]  [A]   <-- Pair at bow wave zone
         |
   [A]      [A]   <-- Forward bilge
         |
   [A]      [A]   <-- Mid bilge (parallel)
         |
   [A]      [A]   <-- Aft bilge
         |
   [A]  [A]   <-- Rudder area (often bracelet)
         |
       STERN
   Total: 23 anodes (12 starboard + 11 port)
   Spacing: 4-6 m along girth; minimum 0.5 m
            from any ICCP anode or sea inlet
```

### 4.4 Cross-Section of a Flush-Mounted Hull Anode (Block Schematic)

  +---------------------------+
  |   Hull plating (steel)    |   <-- Weld doubler plate
  +---------------------------+
  |   Doubler plate (steel)   |   <-- 100 x 150 mm
  +---------------------------+
  |    Steel insert core      |   <-- Threaded for stud
  +---------------------------+
  |                           |
  |  Al-Zn-In cast block      |   <-- 100 W x 100 H x 1500 L mm
