```
              |
              v
       SEAWATER (cathodic current
       returns via electrolyte)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define sacrificial anode cathodic protection for ships. List the three metals commonly used as sacrificial anodes in marine service.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Sacrificial anode cathodic protection (SACP) for ships is an electrochemical corrosion-control method in which a more active metal — called the *sacrificial anode* — is electrically connected to the ship's steel hull. The galvanic potential difference between the anode and hull drives a protective DC current through the seawater, polarizing the hull to a protective potential of approximately **−0.80 V vs. Ag/AgCl** and thereby preventing the anodic dissolution of iron.
- **Three metals (1 mark):** **Aluminium (Al), Zinc (Zn), Magnesium (Mg)**.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*State the role of the "utilization factor" in sacrificial anode design. What is its typical value for Al–Zn–In alloy anodes?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **utilization factor (U)** accounts for the fact that the anode cannot be 100 % consumed before it loses electrical contact with the hull. Some anode mass remains as an "exhausted skeleton" at the end of life. Typical U values: **0.85 for Al alloys, 0.90 for Zn, 0.50 for Mg.** The effective anode mass available for corrosion is therefore $M_{eff} = M \times U$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

> A bulk carrier has an underwater hull area of **1800 m²**. **75 %** of the hull is coated with epoxy paint (design current density = **15 mA/m²**) and the remaining **25 %** is bare steel (design current density = **130 mA/m²**). Seawater resistivity is **0.30 Ω·m**. Use **Al–Zn–In alloy** anodes with:
> - $E_a^{o} = -1.10$ V, $E_c^{o} = -0.80$ V (vs. Ag/AgCl)
> - $C = 2700$ A·h/kg, $U = 0.85$
> - Anode size: **150 mm (W) × 150 mm (H) × 1200 mm (L)**, flush-mounted.
> - Design life = **5 years**.
>
> **(a)** Compute the total current requirement, current output per anode, and the number of anodes required. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Calculate the total anode mass and verify whether the design life is satisfied. If not, propose the corrective action. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Areas:**
- $A_{coated} = 0.75 \times 1800 = 1350$ m²
- $A_{bare} = 0.25 \times 1800 = 450$ m²

**Step 2 — Total current:**
- $i_{c,coated} = 0.015$ A/m², $i_{c,bare} = 0.130$ A/m²
- $I_{total} = (0.015 \times 1350) + (0.130 \times 450) = 20.25 + 58.5 = 78.75$ A **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Driving voltage:**
- $\Delta E = |-1.10 - (-0.80)| = 0.30$ V **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 4 — Equivalent radius:**
- $r_{eq} = \sqrt{(0.15 \times 0.15)/\pi} = \sqrt{0.0225/3.1416} = \sqrt{0.007162} = 0.08463$ m **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Anode resistance:**
- $4L/r_{eq} = 4(1.2)/0.08463 = 56.72$
- $\ln(56.72) = 4.038$
- $R_a = (0.30/(2\pi \times 1.2)) \times (4.038 - 1) = (0.30/7.540) \times 3.038 = 0.03979 \times 3.038 = 0.1208$ Ω **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 6 — Anode output:**
- $I_a = 0.30/0.1208 = 2.483$ A **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 7 — Number of anodes:**
- $N = 78.75/2.483 = 31.72 \rightarrow 32$ anodes (16 per side) **(1.5 marks)**

##### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 8 — Mass of one anode:**
- $V = 0.15 \times 0.15 \times 1.20 = 0.027$ m³
- $m_{anode} = 2700 \times 0.027 = 72.9$ kg **(1 mark)**

**Step 9 — Total mass:**
- $M = 32 \times 72.9 = 2332.8$ kg **(1 mark)**

**Step 10 — Design life check:**
- $T = (M \times C \times U)/(I_{total} \times 8760) = (2332.8 \times 2700 \times 0.85)/(78.75 \times 8760)$
- $T = 5{,}352{,}696 / 689{,}850 = 7.76$ years **(2 marks)**

**Step 11 — Conclusion & Optimization:**
- 7.76 yr ≫ 5 yr ⇒ **design life is satisfied** with **2.76 yr of surplus**. **(1 mark)**
- **Optimization (1 mark):** Since the design is over-engineered by ~55 %, the number of anodes can be reduced to $N_{opt} = 32 \times (5/7.76) = 20.6 \rightarrow 21$ anodes (11 port + 10 starboard) while still meeting the 5-yr criterion. This saves cost and reduces hull drag.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Q-A):** Students routinely forget to **round UP** the number of anodes, or forget to convert mA/m² → A/m². The conversion factor **1000** is a frequent source of off-by-1000 errors. Always write the unit at the end of the equation and cancel it explicitly.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]*

> With neat sketches, explain the **principle of sacrificial anode cathodic protection** for a ship's hull. **(a)** Describe the mechanism with reference to the polarization diagram and identify the role of calcareous deposit formation. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare the suitability of **zinc, aluminium, and magnesium** alloys as sacrificial anodes for a commercial vessel operating in tropical seawater. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Principle (3 marks):**
- When a more active metal (anode) is electrically bonded to the steel hull, the two metals form a *galvanic cell* in seawater.
- The potential difference drives electrons from the anode to the steel hull through the metallic path, and ions through the seawater.
- The steel hull is forced to act as the *cathode*, and its anodic dissolution (Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻) is suppressed.

**Polarization diagram (2.5 marks):**
- Plot potential (V) vs. log(current density).
- Anode polarization curve (Al) and cathode polarization curve (steel) intersect at the **mixed potential** of the system.
- The intersection lies in the **cathodic-protection region** (below −0.80 V vs. Ag/AgCl), ensuring the steel is protected.
- As current flows, oxygen reduction at the steel surface raises local pH, causing **calcareous deposit (CaCO₃ + Mg(OH)₂)** to precipitate.

**Role of calcareous deposit (1.5 marks):**
- The calcareous film acts as an *insulating barrier*, reducing the required protective current density by **50–80 %** during the vessel's life. This is why the **initial** current demand (bare/aged coating) is much higher than the **long-term steady-state** demand.

##### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

| Property (1 mark each) | Magnesium (Mg–Al–Zn) | Zinc (Zn alloy) | Aluminium (Al–Zn–In) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Potential vs. Ag/AgCl | −1.55 V (too negative) | −1.05 V (adequate) | −1.10 V (optimum) |
| Capacity (A·h/kg) | 1230 | 780 | 2700 |
| Behaviour in seawater | Severe over-protection; H₂ evolution; risk of paint blistering | Passivates below 1 m/s flow (ineffective on fast hulls) | Passive film broken by In (indium) dopant — works in all flow regimes |
| Density | 1.74 g/cm³ (light) | 7.14 g/cm³ (heaviest) | 2.70 g/cm³ |
| Best application | Fresh water, soil resistivity | Slow-moving or moored vessels | **All seagoing ships — universal choice** |
| Cost per kg | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Safety concern | Highly reactive — fire risk in dry storage | Cadmium content (banned in some regions) | Inert when dry |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For a commercial vessel in tropical seawater, **Al–Zn–In alloy** is universally selected due to high capacity, low density (less hull weight), and consistent performance across flow regimes. Mg alloys are unsuitable (overprotection and H₂ embrittlement), and Zn alloys risk passivation in high-speed vessels.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Q-B):** Students commonly confuse the **role of the electrolyte** — the seawater is *not* a "conductor" in the metallic sense; it conducts **ions**, not electrons. Always explain current flow as: *e⁻* in metal, *ions* in seawater. Marks are lost when this is muddled.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pin This Before Exam**

- ⭐ **The ONE equation to know cold:** $\;I_a = \dfrac{E_a^{o} - E_c^{o}}{R_a}\;$ — comes up in **every** design problem.
- ⭐ **Driving voltage** is always $|E_a^{o} - E_c^{o}| \approx 0.25$ to **0.30 V** for Al–Zn–In in seawater.
- ⭐ **Equivalent radius for rectangular anode:** $r_{eq} = \sqrt{WH/\pi}$ — never use W/2 directly.
- ⭐ **8760 hours/year** is the conversion constant for all life calculations.
- ⭐ **Al capacity = 2700 A·h/kg, U = 0.85** — these two numbers carry 50 % of marks.
- ⭐ **Bare steel current density = 100–150 mA/m²**; **coated = 5–30 mA/m²** — always use the *aged-coated* value.
- ⭐ **Anodes are mounted in even port/starboard pairs** for symmetry — the *physical* number is always rounded up to a balanced pair.
- ⭐ **The anode is NOT 100 % consumed** — the utilization factor U accounts for the *spent core* that remains after electrical contact is lost.
- ⭐ **The "Big Five" equations** in order: (1) $I_{total} = i_c A$ → (2) $R_a$ from geometry → (3) $I_a$ from Ohm → (4) $N = I_{total}/I_a$ → (5) $T$ from $M, C, U$.
- ⭐ **Verification always comes LAST** — never end a design without confirming $T \geq 5$ years.
- ⭐ **DNVGL-RP-B401** is the de-facto standard examiners expect you to cite.
- ⭐ **Drawing the polarization curve** with the intersection in the cathodic region gets you full marks in the "principle" question.
- ⭐ **Calcareous film reduces long-term current demand** — explain this in every long answer.
- ⭐ **Mg is NEVER used in seawater** — repeat this fact thrice in your answer to signal mastery.

> **End of Module 4 — Sacrificial Anode Design for Ships Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and disadvantages of sacrificial anode system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advantages and disadvantages of sacrificial anode system

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP) System** is a passive electrochemical corrosion mitigation method in which a more electrochemically active metal (the *sacrificial anode*) is electrically coupled to the metal structure to be protected (the *cathode*, e.g., a ship's steel hull). The anode, possessing a more negative electrochemical potential in the galvanic series, preferentially corrodes — *sacrificing* itself — while supplying electrons that suppress the anodic dissolution reaction of the parent structure, thereby driving the protected metal into a thermodynamically immune or passive cathodic regime.

In shipbuilding applications, this is one of the two principal forms of **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** and **Sacrificial Anode CP** — collectively mandated by classification societies such as **DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, and Bureau Veritas** for the hull, sea chests, ballast tanks, rudders, and propeller systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The sacrificial anode system is fundamentally a *galvanic* (not impressed current) protection technique. It requires **NO external DC power source** — the driving potential difference is intrinsic to the dissimilar metal couple.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large steel ship hull as a *busy kitchen* where metal atoms are constantly being "stolen" (corroded) by aggressive seawater. The sacrificial anode acts like a **bodyguard** standing outside the kitchen door.

- The bodyguard (anode, e.g., zinc) gets attacked first because he is "more attractive" to the seawater ions.
- The chef inside (steel hull) remains completely untouched and safe.
- Periodically, the bodyguard must be replaced (zinc dissolves away) — but the valuables inside are preserved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Definition:** Sacrificial anode protection means attaching a "more reactive" metal to the structure you want to save. The reactive metal dissolves *instead* of the structure, like a decoy that absorbs all the damage.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following constants and engineering metrics are repeatedly tested in KTU examinations:

- **Faraday's Constant (F): 96,485 C/mol (≈ 96,500 C/mol)** — the charge per mole of electrons.
- **Standard Electrode Potential of Iron (Fe²⁺/Fe): −0.44 V vs SHE** in seawater (practical value ≈ **−0.55 V**).
- **Standard Potential of Zinc (Zn²⁺/Zn): −0.76 V vs SHE** — hence zinc is anodic to steel.
- **Standard Potential of Magnesium (Mg²⁺/Mg): −2.37 V vs SHE** — highest driving voltage.
- **Aluminum alloy anodes (Al-Zn-In): ≈ −1.10 V vs SHE** — most common in marine service.
- **Typical Protection Criterion: −0.85 V vs Cu/CuSO₄ reference electrode** for steel in seawater.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The sacrificial anode system operates on the principle of **preferential anodic dissolution** within a galvanic couple. The logical sequence of operation is:

1. **Electrical Coupling:** The sacrificial anode (e.g., Zn, Mg, or Al alloy) is bolted, welded, or clamped directly to the steel hull using a low-resistance metallic connection (steel core or copper conductor).
2. **Electrolyte Bridge:** Both metals share a common electrolyte (seawater, with resistivity ≈ **20–30 Ω·cm**).
3. **Galvanic Driving Force:** The potential difference (ΔE) between the anode and the steel cathode acts as the *natural battery* driving the protective current.
4. **Anodic Reaction (at anode):** The sacrificial metal oxidizes and releases electrons.
5. **Cathodic Reaction (at hull):** The released electrons flow into the hull and suppress the iron oxidation reaction by reducing dissolved oxygen at the hull surface.
6. **Continuous Consumption:** The anode gradually dissolves and must be replaced periodically (typically **2–5 years** for ship hulls).

### 2.1.1 Electrochemical Reactions

At the **sacrificial anode (oxidation)**:

$$\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

$$\text{Al} \rightarrow \text{Al}^{3+} + 3e^{-}$$

At the **protected cathode (reduction)** — dissolved oxygen is reduced:

$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}$$

The key result: **no iron atoms leave the steel hull surface** because all the electrons needed for the oxygen reduction come from the sacrificial anode, not from Fe oxidation.

## 2.2 Common Sacrificial Anode Materials

| Anode Material | Practical Potential (V vs Ag/AgCl) | Typical Capacity (A·h/kg) | Consumption Rate (kg/A·yr) | Marine Application |
|----------------|-----------------------------------|---------------------------|----------------------------|-------------------|
| **Zinc (Zn)** | −1.05 | 780 | ≈ 11.2 | Warm seawater, ship hulls |
| **Magnesium (Mg)** | −1.55 | 1230 | ≈ 7.0 | Low-conductivity freshwater, soil |
| **Aluminum-Zinc-Indium (Al-Zn-In)** | −1.10 | 2700 | ≈ 3.3 | Seawater hulls (most common) |
| **Aluminum-Zinc-Mercury (legacy)** | −1.10 | 2700 | ≈ 3.3 | Older marine systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Aluminum-zinc-indium anodes are the *industry standard* for modern ship hulls because of their **very high current capacity (≈ 2700 A·h/kg)** and low consumption rate. Zinc anodes are still used in **warm tropical waters** because aluminum suffers from passivation issues above ~25 °C.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's Law — mass of metal dissolved | kg |
| 2 | $I = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot t}$ | Current derived from anode mass loss | Amperes (A) |
| 3 | $N_a = \dfrac{I_{\text{total}} \cdot S \cdot 8760}{C_a \cdot m_a}$ | Number of anodes required (S = safety factor) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $L_a = \dfrac{m_a \cdot C_a \cdot 8760}{I_a}$ | Anode design life in hours/years | years |
| 5 | $\Delta E = E_{\text{anode}} - E_{\text{cathode}}$ | Driving voltage of galvanic couple | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{R_{\text{anode}} + R_{\text{cathode}} + R_{\text{electrolyte}}}$ | Ohm's Law applied to CP circuit | Amperes (A) |
| 7 | $R_{\text{electrolyte}} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{r}\right) - 1\right]$ | Dwight's formula for cylindrical anode resistance to remote earth | Ω |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{\Delta E_{\text{anode}} - \Delta E_{\text{cathode}}}{\Delta E_{\text{anode}}} \times 100\%$ | Cathodic protection efficiency | % |
| 9 | $i_{\text{protect}} = 10\text{–}30\ \text{mA/m}^2$ | Typical protective current density (bare steel in seawater) | mA/m² |
| 10 | $i_{\text{protect}} = 0.1\text{–}1\ \text{mA/m}^2$ | Typical current density for coated steel | mA/m² |

**Where:**
- $M$ = atomic weight of anode material (kg/kmol)
- $n$ = valency (electrons transferred per atom)
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $t$ = time in seconds (8760 h/yr)
- $C_a$ = electrochemical capacity (A·h/kg)
- $m_a$ = mass of one anode (kg)
- $\rho$ = electrolyte resistivity (Ω·m for seawater ≈ 0.25 Ω·m)
- $L$ = length of anode (m), $r$ = radius (m)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In shipbuilding, sacrificial anodes are deployed in:
- **Hull plating** (external surfaces below the waterline)
- **Sea chests and inlet grids** (high-velocity seawater zones)
- **Ballast tanks** (intermittent seawater exposure)
- **Rudder assemblies and propeller shafts**
- **Cathodic protection of internal tank surfaces** where ICCP wiring is impractical
- **Mooring chains and offshore structures**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation: Anode Mass Consumption from Faraday's Law

Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis states that the mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed.

**Step 1 — Charge passed in time t:**

$$Q = I \cdot t \quad \text{[Coulombs]}$$

*Logic:* Current $I$ flowing for time $t$ delivers a total charge $Q$. This is the fundamental definition of electric current as the rate of charge flow.

**Step 2 — Moles of electrons transferred:**

$$n_e = \dfrac{Q}{F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{F} \quad \text{[mol]}$$

*Logic:* Since one mole of electrons carries exactly $F = 96{,}485$ C of charge, the number of moles is $Q$ divided by $F$.

**Step 3 — Moles of metal atoms oxidized:**

Each metal atom releases $n$ electrons (where $n$ is the valency). So moles of metal dissolved:

$$n_m = \dfrac{n_e}{n} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

*Logic:* If one atom yields $n$ electrons, then the number of atoms is $1/n$ times the number of electrons.

**Step 4 — Mass of metal consumed:**

$$m = n_m \cdot M = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

*Logic:* Multiplying moles of metal by its atomic mass $M$ (kg/kmol) yields the mass consumed. This is the **canonical Faraday's Law of Corrosion** form used throughout cathodic protection design.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Anode Mass Calculation

> **Problem:** A ship hull is protected by **zinc sacrificial anodes**. The protective current required is **15 A**. The anode lifetime must be **3 years**. Calculate the total mass of zinc consumed. Given: $M_{\text{Zn}} = 65.38$ kg/kmol, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.

**Step 1 — Convert time to seconds:**

$$t = 3 \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600 = 94{,}608{,}000\ \text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Faraday's Law:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \\
&= \dfrac{15 \times 94{,}608{,}000 \times 65.38}{2 \times 96{,}485} \\
&= \dfrac{15 \times 94{,}608{,}000 \times 65.38}{192{,}970}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$15 \times 94{,}608{,}000 = 1{,}419{,}120{,}000$$

$$1{,}419{,}120{,}000 \times 65.38 = 92{,}782{,}665{,}600$$

**Step 4 — Final division:**

$$m = \dfrac{92{,}782{,}665{,}600}{192{,}970} \approx 480{,}815\ \text{kg}$$

**Result: ≈ 480.8 tonnes of zinc consumed over 3 years.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** At a current demand of 15 A for a moderately large ship, the anode mass is enormous, which is why anodes are typically distributed as **dozens of smaller units** rather than one bulk mass.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Number of Anodes Required

> **Problem:** Design a sacrificial anode system using **Al-Zn-In anodes** with mass $m_a = 50$ kg each and electrochemical capacity $C_a = 2700$ A·h/kg. The hull requires $I_{\text{total}} = 200$ A continuously. Apply a safety factor $S = 1.25$. Find the number of anodes for a **4-year** service life.

**Step 1 — Total charge needed over 4 years:**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = I_{\text{total}} \cdot t = 200 \times 4 \times 8760 = 7{,}008{,}000\ \text{A·h}$$

*Logic:* Each year contains 8760 hours, so 4 years = 35,040 hours.

**Step 2 — Total anode mass required:**

$$m_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}} \cdot S}{C_a} = \dfrac{7{,}008{,}000 \times 1.25}{2700} \approx 3244.4\ \text{kg}$$

*Logic:* Anode capacity $C_a$ tells us how many A·h are produced per kg. Dividing the demand by capacity gives mass needed. The safety factor compensates for non-uniform current distribution.

**Step 3 — Number of anodes:**

$$N_a = \dfrac{m_{\text{total}}}{m_a} = \dfrac{3244.4}{50} \approx 64.9$$

Rounding up:

$$\boxed{N_a = 65\ \text{anodes}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always round UP.** Partial anodes do not exist in engineering procurement. Under-designing cathodic protection leads to accelerated hull corrosion and classification society non-compliance.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Sacrificial vs. ICCP

| Parameter | Sacrificial Anode CP | Impressed Current CP (ICCP) |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| Power source | None (passive) | External DC rectifier required |
| Driving voltage | Limited (≈ 0.2–0.5 V) | Adjustable up to 50 V |
| Current output | Low to moderate (few A) | High (hundreds of A possible) |
| Initial cost | Low | High (rectifier + wiring) |
| Operating cost | High (anode replacement) | Low (only electricity) |
| Suitability for large structures | Poor for ocean-going vessels | Excellent |
| Suitability for small structures | Excellent | Over-engineered |
| Stray current interference | Negligible | Significant risk |
| Maintenance frequency | Every 2–5 years | Annual inspection |
| Installation complexity | Simple (bolt/weld) | Complex (wiring, rectifier) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Sacrificial Anode CP System

The following Mermaid block represents the **functional block-level architecture** of a sacrificial anode cathodic protection system as deployed on a ship hull. This avoids the limitations of attempting to draw a complex physical hull cross-section in a schematic tool.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Seawater Electrolyte] -->|Ion conduction path| B[Sacrificial Anode e.g. Al-Zn-In]
    B -->|Anodic dissolution Al yields Al3 plus 3e-| C[Metallic Conductor Steel Core or Cable]
    C -->|Electron flow| D[Ship Hull Cathode Protected Surface]
    D -->|Consumes electrons| E[Oxygen Reduction Reaction O2 plus 2H2O plus 4e yields 4OH-]
    E -->|Forms protective OH- layer| F[Calcareous Deposit CaCO3 on Hull]
    A -->|Completes ionic circuit| D
    F -->|Reduces further current demand| G[Steady-State Protection]
    G -->|Monitored via| H[Reference Electrode Cu-CuSO4]
    H -->|Reading should be -0.85 V| I[Protection Criterion Check]
    J[Periodic Visual Inspection] -->|Anode mass loss| K[Replacement Schedule Every 2 to 5 Years]
    K -->|Install new anodes| B
```

## 4.2 Electron-Flow & Material-Loop Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ANODE[Sacrificial Anode Zn or Al] -->|e- flow through metallic bond| HULL[Ship Hull Steel Cathode]
    HULL -->|e- consumed by| ORR[Oxygen Reduction at Hull Surface]
    ANODE -->|Zn2 plus ions dissolve into| SW[Seawater Electrolyte]
    SW -->|OH- ions drift toward| HULL
    SW -->|Completes ionic circuit back to| ANODE
    ANODE -.->|Wears away over time| REPLACE[Anode Replacement Required]
```

## 4.3 Advantages vs. Disadvantages Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Sacrificial Anode System] --> ADV{Advantages}
    START --> DIS{Disadvantages}
    ADV --> A1[No External Power Required]
    ADV --> A2[Simple Installation Bolted or Welded]
    ADV --> A3[Low Initial Capital Cost]
    ADV --> A4[Minimal Maintenance No Electronics]
    ADV --> A5[Negligible Stray Current Interference]
    ADV --> A6[Reliable Operation in Remote Sites]
    ADV --> A7[Suitable for Small Structures and Ballast Tanks]
    DIS --> D1[Low Driving Voltage 0.2 to 0.5 V]
    DIS --> D2[Limited Current Output]
    DIS --> D3[Frequent Anode Replacement]
    DIS --> D4[High Long-Term Operating Cost]
    DIS --> D5[Heavy Anode Mass Required]
    DIS --> D6[Unsuitable for Large Ocean-Going Hulls]
    DIS --> D7[Anode Performance Drops in High-Resistivity Water]
    DIS --> D8[Lower Energy Efficiency]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define sacrificial anode cathodic protection. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Sacrificial anode cathodic protection is a corrosion prevention technique in which a more electrochemically active metal (such as zinc, magnesium, or aluminum alloy) is electrically connected to the metal structure to be protected. The sacrificial anode, being more anodic in the galvanic series, preferentially corrodes and supplies electrons to the protected structure (cathode). This suppresses the anodic dissolution of the parent metal, effectively shifting its corrosion potential into the immune region. The anode is gradually consumed and must be replaced periodically.

*Mark Distribution:* Definition (1), Mechanism (1), Example materials (1).

### Question 2: List four common sacrificial anode materials used in shipbuilding. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four common sacrificial anode materials are:

1. **Zinc (Zn)** — used in warm seawater; potential ≈ −1.05 V vs Ag/AgCl.
2. **Magnesium (Mg) alloys** — used in low-conductivity freshwater and soil; potential ≈ −1.55 V vs Ag/AgCl.
3. **Aluminum-Zinc-Indium (Al-Zn-In) alloys** — the modern industry standard for marine hulls; potential ≈ −1.10 V vs Ag/AgCl.
4. **Aluminum-Zinc-Mercury (legacy)** — historically used but phased out due to environmental concerns about mercury.

*Mark Distribution:* 1 mark for each correctly identified material with its application context (0.75 marks each).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [Choose ONE of the two]

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss in detail the advantages of the sacrificial anode cathodic protection system as applied to ship hulls. Mention any six advantages with engineering justification.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. No External Power Source Required (1 mark):** The sacrificial anode operates purely on the natural potential difference between the anode metal and the protected steel. This eliminates the need for DC rectifiers, AC power cabling, and backup generators, making the system ideal for remote marine locations and ballast tanks.

**2. Simple Installation (1 mark):** Anodes can be directly bolted, welded, or clamped to the hull structure. No specialized electrical work or commissioning by qualified electricians is necessary, reducing installation labor and time.

**3. Low Initial Capital Cost (1 mark):** Since there are no rectifiers, reference electrodes, control panels, or wiring systems to procure, the upfront cost of sacrificial anode systems is significantly lower than ICCP. This is attractive for small vessels, barges, and short-life marine structures.

**4. Minimal Maintenance and High Reliability (1 mark):** With no electronics, no moving parts, and no consumable power supplies, the failure modes are extremely limited. Periodic visual inspection and underwater survey suffice. Reliability in harsh saline environments is exceptional.

**5. Negligible Stray Current Interference (1 mark):** Because the driving voltage is very low (≈ 0.2–0.5 V), the protective current rarely escapes to interfere with neighboring metal structures, neighboring vessels in a harbor, or buried pipelines. This is a major safety advantage in congested ports.

**6. Suitable for Insulated/Coated Hulls and Internal Compartments (1 mark):** Sacrificial anodes are ideal for ballast tanks, cofferdams, and void spaces where installing ICCP wiring would be impractical. The anodes can be spot-welded to internal tank surfaces without auxiliary infrastructure.

**7. Self-Regulating Behavior (1 mark):** The current output of a sacrificial anode is automatically self-limited by its open-circuit potential. As polarization occurs, the current naturally reduces, preventing over-protection and hydrogen embrittlement risks common in improperly tuned ICCP systems.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the major disadvantages of sacrificial anode systems and the engineering limitations that lead to the choice of ICCP for large ocean-going vessels.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Limited Driving Voltage (1 mark):** The maximum potential difference between a sacrificial anode (≈ −1.10 V Al) and protected steel (≈ −0.55 V) is only about **0.55 V**. After polarization losses and anode-to-electrolyte resistance, the *effective* driving voltage is often just **0.2–0.4 V**. This restricts the throw and reach of protective current.

**2. Low Current Output (1 mark):** Because the driving voltage is low and cannot be increased externally, the current delivered is limited by Ohm's Law: $I = \Delta E / R_{\text{circuit}}$. Large hulls with bare steel surfaces demand currents exceeding **500–1000 A**, which a passive galvanic system cannot economically supply.

**3. Frequent Anode Replacement (1 mark):** Sacrificial anodes are *consumed* — by definition, they "sacrifice" themselves. Typical service life is **2–5 years** for ship hulls, after which **dry-docking** is required for replacement. Each dry-docking costs a shipping company substantial capital (often ₹ 1–10 crore depending on vessel size).

**4. High Long-Term Operating Cost (1 mark):** Although initial cost is low, the cumulative cost of periodic anode replacement, dry-docking, and lost revenue during off-hire periods makes sacrificial systems expensive over a vessel's 25–30 year life. ICCP, by contrast, has higher initial cost but minimal anode replacement.

**5. Heavy Anode Mass Required (1 mark):** As shown in Section 3.2, hundreds of kilograms to hundreds of tonnes of anode material may be required for a single large vessel. This adds significant **weight** to the ship, reducing payload and fuel efficiency.

**6. Performance Drops in High-Resistivity Water (1 mark):** In brackish water, estuaries, or freshwater, the electrolyte resistivity rises from ~25 Ω·cm (open ocean) to several thousand Ω·cm. The current delivered by sacrificial anodes drops sharply, leaving the structure under-protected.

**7. Unsuitable for Bare, Large Steel Hulls in Oceans (1 mark):** Because of all the above limitations, classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's) **mandate ICCP** for large ocean-going commercial vessels with bare or minimally-coated hulls. Sacrificial anodes are restricted to coated hulls, small craft, ballast tanks, and supplementary use.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compare sacrificial anode cathodic protection with impressed current cathodic protection. Use a comparative table and explain why sacrificial anodes are preferred for ballast tanks of ships.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Comparative Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Sacrificial Anode CP | Impressed Current CP |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| External power | Not required | DC rectifier required |
| Driving voltage | 0.2–0.5 V (intrinsic) | Up to 20–50 V (adjustable) |
| Current capacity | Limited (few A) | High (hundreds of A) |
| Initial cost | Low | High |
| Running cost | High (anode replacement) | Low |
| Maintenance | Periodic anode swap | Annual electrical check |
| Stray current risk | Negligible | Must be managed |
| Best suited for | Coated hulls, ballast tanks, small vessels | Large bare steel hulls |

**Why sacrificial anodes are preferred for ballast tanks (3 marks):**

1. **No electrical infrastructure required inside the tank** — ballast tanks are sealed compartments flooded and drained intermittently. Installing rectifier cabling, reference electrodes, and monitoring wires inside a tank prone to slamming loads and corrosion is impractical and unsafe.
2. **Intermittent operation favors passive anodes** — sacrificial anodes work whenever the tank is flooded with seawater; no power-up procedure is needed. ICCP cannot function during ballast voyage segments where power is intentionally cut.
3. **Local overprotection risk is eliminated** — sacrificial anodes self-limit their current, preventing hydrogen evolution and coating damage inside enclosed ballast tanks where hydrogen accumulation could pose explosion hazards.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the mass of aluminum anode consumed per year when delivering 50 A continuously. Take M = 27 kg/kmol, n = 3, F = 96,485 C/mol. State any assumption made.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Assumption (1 mark):** The aluminum anode operates at 100% current efficiency (no passivation or non-Faradaic side reactions).

**Step 1 — Time per year in seconds (1 mark):**

$$t = 365 \times 24 \times 3600 = 31{,}536{,}000\ \text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Faraday's Law (2 marks):**

$$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values (2 marks):**

$$m = \dfrac{50 \times 31{,}536{,}000 \times 27}{3 \times 96{,}485}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator (1 mark):**

$$50 \times 31{,}536{,}000 = 1{,}576{,}800{,}000$$

$$1{,}576{,}800{,}000 \times 27 = 42{,}573{,}600{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Final result (1 mark):**

$$m = \dfrac{42{,}573{,}600{,}000}{289{,}455} \approx 147{,}067\ \text{kg/yr}$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 147.1\ \text{tonnes of Al per year}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert years into seconds is the single most common mistake. Always use **t in seconds** with $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, OR convert charge to A·h first using **1 year = 8760 h**.
>
> 2. **Atomic Mass vs. Molar Mass Confusion (–1 mark):** Zinc atomic mass is **65.38**, NOT 65. Magnesium is **24.31**, NOT 24. Aluminum is **26.98**, NOT 27 (close but not exact). Use precise values from the periodic table.
>
> 3. **Valency Error (–2 marks):** Aluminum loses **3 electrons** (valency = 3), zinc loses **2**. Magnesium loses 2. Writing the wrong valency is a guaranteed half-mark loss.
>
> 4. **Rounding Up Anode Count (–1 mark):** In design problems, *always round UP* the anode count. Never write "64.9 anodes" as the final answer.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Safety Factor:** Real designs include $S = 1.15$ to $1.5$. Examiners expect this.
>
> 6. **Confusing SACP with ICCP terminology:** Students often mix up "sacrificial" (passive galvanic) with "impressed current" (active DC-driven). The former uses *no power source*; the latter does.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** Sacrificial anode CP = passive galvanic corrosion protection using a more active metal that preferentially corrodes.
- **Core Principle:** Anode dissolves; cathode is protected by electron influx; no external power needed.
- **Most Common Marine Anode:** **Al-Zn-In alloy** (≈ 2700 A·h/kg capacity).
- **Driving Voltage:** Only **0.2–0.5 V** effective — this is the *fundamental limitation*.
- **Protection Criterion:** Hull must be polarized to **−0.85 V vs Cu/CuSO₄** reference.
- **Faraday's Law (must memorize):** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$
- **Constants:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; 1 year = 8760 h = 31,536,000 s.
- **Typical Bare Steel Current Density:** 10–30 mA/m² in seawater.
- **Typical Coated Steel Current Density:** 0.1–1 mA/m².
- **Service Life of Hull Anodes:** 2–5 years; replacement requires **dry-docking**.
- **Anode Attachment Methods:** Welding, bolting, or clamping to steel core insert.
- **Best Applications:** Coated hulls, ballast tanks, small craft, sea chests, internal compartments.
- **Poor Applications:** Large bare-hull ocean vessels, freshwater tanks, high-resistivity electrolytes.
- **Key Advantages:** No power, simple, reliable, no stray current, low initial cost, self-regulating.
- **Key Disadvantages:** Low current, frequent replacement, heavy mass, not scalable, high long-term cost.
- **ICCP vs. SACP Rule of Thumb:** Small + coated → **SACP**. Large + bare + ocean-going → **ICCP**.
- **Classification Society Mandates:** DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, Bureau Veritas — all enforce CP for ship hulls per SOLAS and IMO regulations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the *driving electrochemical principle* (preferential anodic dissolution) before listing advantages or disadvantages. Examiners award **1–2 marks** just for stating the correct mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impressed current cathodic protection system in ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST414 - SHIPBUILDING MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Impressed current cathodic protection system in ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** is an active electrochemical corrosion-mitigation technique in which an external, regulated **Direct Current (DC) power source** — typically a Transformer-Rectifier (T-R) unit — drives a protective current through **inert, dimensionally stable anodes** mounted on the submerged hull, forcing the entire hull structure to behave as a **cathode**. By overriding the natural anodic dissolution reaction of the steel hull (Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻), the system polarizes the protected surface to a predetermined protective potential (typically **−0.85 V vs. Ag/AgCl/Seawater reference electrode** at the most distant, "shadowed" point), thereby suppressing metal loss to a negligibly small rate (≤ 10 μm/year).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Battery in Reverse"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine a leaking water tank (the corroding steel hull) where water (electrons) keeps dripping out from a hole (the corrosion site). A **sacrificial anode** is like placing a softer, water-filled sponge *inside* the tank that soaks up the leak — it dissolves itself to save the tank. An **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) system** is fundamentally different: it is like **pumping water BACK into the tank from an outside reservoir** using an electric pump (the rectifier). The pump continuously refills the tank, so no water (no metal) is ever lost. The **anode** in the ICCP system is just a wire/rod that *delivers* the current into the seawater — it is not consumed the way a sacrificial zinc anode is.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Quantitative Benchmarks for Seawater ICCP Design:**
> - **Design current density for bare/coated steel hull:** **i_c = 100 – 150 mA/m²** (new hull) and **30 – 50 mA/m²** (well-coated hull in service).
> - **Protective potential band:** **−0.80 V to −0.95 V** versus Ag/AgCl/Seawater reference.
> - **Total hull wetted area for an average VLCC:** **≈ 5,000 – 8,000 m²** below waterline.
> - **Standard sea-water resistivity:** **ρ ≈ 0.20 – 0.30 Ω·m** at 25 °C, salinity 35 ‰.
> - **Typical DC bus voltage on ship:** **15 – 50 V DC** at **50 – 600 A** capacity.

### 1.4 Position of ICCP in Ship Corrosion-Control Hierarchy

| Hierarchy Layer | Method | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (Primary) | High-build epoxy / anti-fouling coating | Barrier to electrolyte |
| 2 (Secondary) | **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** | Polarizes coating-defect sites |
| 3 (Tertiary) | Sacrificial (galvanic) anodes | Backup at propellers, rudders |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ICCP in modern shipbuilding is **not a substitute for coating**; it is the **electrochemical supplement** that protects microscopic coating holidays (pinholes, scratches) that inevitably occur during service. The combination reduces the required ICCP current by a factor of **5 to 10** compared to bare-hull operation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Electrochemical "Why" — Polarization Theory

In a freely corroding ship hull, mixed-potential theory dictates that the anodic current (metal dissolution, $i_a$) and the cathodic current (oxygen reduction, $i_c$) at any point on the hull are **equal and opposite** at the corrosion potential $E_{corr}$:

$$i_a(E_{corr}) = i_c(E_{corr}) = i_{corr}$$

When the ICCP rectifier injects an external cathodic current density $i_{app}$ into the hull, the net anodic (corrosion) current is reduced to:

$$i_{a,net} = i_{corr} - i_{app}$$

When $i_{app} \geq i_{corr}$, the hull surface is **fully polarized** into the cathodic region — corrosion is thermodynamically and kinetically suppressed. The operating point on the Evans diagram now sits on the cathodic Tafel slope at a potential more negative than $E_{corr}$, i.e., the **protective potential $E_{prot}$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Analogy:** Think of the hull as a leaking bucket. As you pour water *into* the bucket from the ICCP pump, the water level (potential) rises. Once the level exceeds the rim, no water can leak out — the bucket is "fully polarized."

### 2.2 System Architecture — Subsystem Breakdown

A shipboard ICCP installation comprises **five functional subsystems**:

1. **DC Power Source — Transformer-Rectifier (T-R) Unit**
   - Converts ship's 440 V / 60 Hz AC supply → regulated DC output.
   - Modern units: silicon-diode bridge + thyristor-controlled (SCR) tap-changing for automatic potential control.
   - Output rating: typically **50 A, 100 A, 200 A, 300 A, 600 A** at 15–50 V DC.

2. **Impressed Current Anodes (the "Pumps")**
   - Materials: **Platinized Titanium (Pt/Ti)**, **Lead-Silver Alloy (Pb-2% Ag)**, **Mixed Metal Oxide (MMO/Ti)**, **Platinum-Niobium clad**.
   - Geometry: slender rod (Ø 20–25 mm × 600–1500 mm) or flush-mounted disc (Ø 100–300 mm) for high-speed vessels.
   - Quantity: typically **2 – 8 anodes** on the hull of a large commercial ship.

3. **Reference Electrodes (the "Thermometers")**
   - **Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl/Seawater)** — primary control sensor.
   - Pure **Zinc** — secondary check electrode.
   - Quantity: **2 – 4 reference cells** per ship, mounted near and far from anodes to detect "throwing power" coverage.

4. **Dielectric Shielding**
   - Thick (≥ 250 μm) **glass-flake epoxy or neoprene rubber** coating around anode mounting pads.
   - Prevents accelerated paint breakdown due to high local current density and acidic (low pH) chlorine/liberated-oxygen microenvironment.

5. **Control & Monitoring Loop (Automatic Potential Control)**
   - Microprocessor-based controller reads reference-electrode potential → compares to setpoint → adjusts rectifier output via SCR phase-angle firing.
   - Equipped with **over-voltage, under-voltage, and anode-failure alarms** (per IMO and classification society rules).

### 2.3 Why ICCP Over Sacrificial Anodes? — The Engineering Trade-Off

| Parameter | Sacrificial (Galvanic) Anode | Impressed Current (ICCP) |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Galvanic potential difference (~0.25 V for Zn-Fe) | External DC (15–50 V) |
| Current output | Limited by anode self-corrosion rate | **Independent of electrolyte chemistry**; can be regulated |
| Anode consumption | Linear, high (≈ 11 kg Zn/A·year) | Very low (Ti/MMO essentially inert) |
| Wetted-area coverage | Localized; poor throwing power | **Excellent**; full hull |
| Cost of protection (large hull) | High — many tonnes of zinc/aluminum | Low — small anodes + electricity |
| Maintenance | Periodic anode replacement (dry-docking) | **Continuous in-service adjustment** |
| Suitability for large ships | Inefficient | **Standard for VLCCs, naval combatants** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU High-Yield):** For ships with wetted hull area **> 500 m²**, ICCP is almost universally more economical. Below this threshold, sacrificial systems may be preferred for simplicity.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable. The KTU 2024 Scheme places heavy emphasis on numerical design problems — memorize the symbols and the units.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{total} = A_{hull} \times i_c$ | Total protection current = hull area × design current density | A ; m² ; A/m² |
| 2 | $N_{anode} = \dfrac{I_{total}}{I_{a}}$ | Number of anodes required | dimensionless ; A ; A |
| 3 | $R_{a,long} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L_a}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L_a}{r_a}\right)$ | Electrolytic resistance of a slender cylindrical anode | Ω ; Ω·m ; m ; m |
| 4 | $R_{a,disc} = \dfrac{\rho}{4 r_a}$ | Resistance of a flush-mounted disc anode | Ω ; Ω·m ; m |
| 5 | $L_{life} = \dfrac{m_{anode} \times \eta}{I_{a} \times 8760}$ | Anode design life (years) | yr ; kg ; A |
| 6 | $V_{rect} = I_{total}\,(R_{anode} + R_{cable} + R_{hull-seawater})$ | Required DC voltage at rectifier | V ; A ; Ω |
| 7 | $E_{prot} = E_{corr} - \Delta E_{polarization}$ | Protective potential = corrosion potential − required polarization shift | V ; V ; V |
| 8 | $W_{power} = V_{rect} \times I_{total}$ | DC power consumption of ICCP system | W ; V ; A |
| 9 | $\eta_{anode} = \dfrac{m_{consumed}}{m_{installed}}$ | Anode utilization factor (≈ 0.75 for Pt/Ti, ≈ 0.85 for MMO) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $i_{c,field} = \dfrac{I_{a}}{4\pi r^{2}}$ | Far-field current density decay from a point anode | A/m² ; A ; m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Convention Note:** All modulus and natural-log operations are written using `\vert`, `\ln`, and parenthesized fractions so that the markdown table structure is preserved without `pipe` collisions.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Beyond ship hulls, the same ICCP architecture protects:
- **Offshore wind-turbine monopiles** (growing maritime adjacent market).
- **Harbour pier piles, jetties, and quay walls.**
- **Submarine pipelines** crossing seabeds.
- **Cargo holds and ballast tanks** of chemical/product tankers (where a long slender anode can be inserted through a cofferdam access).

The design equations remain identical — only the geometry and current-density assumptions change.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Cathodic Protection Current

**Step 1 — Define the wetted hull area.**
For a ship of length $L_{pp}$ (perpendiculars), beam $B$, and design draft $T_d$, the wetted lateral area is approximated by the **Simpson's-rule or block-coefficient formula**:

$$A_{hull} \approx L_{pp} \times (2T_d + B) \times C_{block}$$

where $C_{block}$ is the prismatic/block coefficient (0.65 – 0.85 for tankers, 0.55 – 0.65 for fine-lined vessels).

**Step 2 — Identify the design current density.**
From the KTU Table 4.2 (module syllabus) for **coated steel hull in tropical seawater**:

$$i_c = 30\ \text{mA/m}^{2} = 0.030\ \text{A/m}^{2}$$

> *(For uncoated hulls or initial polarization, use $i_c = 150\ \text{mA/m}^{2}$.)*

**Step 3 — Multiply to obtain total current.**

$$I_{total} = A_{hull} \times i_c$$

**Worked Numerical Example (VLCC-typical values):**

$$A_{hull} = 320\ \text{m} \times (2 \times 12 + 50) \times 0.80 = 320 \times 74 \times 0.80 = 18{,}944\ \text{m}^{2}$$

$$I_{total} = 18{,}944 \times 0.030 = 568.3\ \text{A}$$

Therefore, the rectifier must be rated at **≥ 600 A** (next standard size).

### 3.2 Derivation: Number of Anodes

**Step 1 — Determine current per anode from manufacturer data.**
For a standard platinized-titanium rod anode of length 1.0 m and diameter 25 mm, the manufacturer-stated continuous current rating in seawater is typically:

$$I_{a} = 75\ \text{A} \text{ (conservative)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the count, rounding up.**

$$N_{anode} = \left\lceil \frac{I_{total}}{I_{a}} \right\rceil$$

**Applying the worked example:**

$$N_{anode} = \left\lceil \frac{568.3}{75} \right\rceil = \lceil 7.58 \rceil = 8 \text{ anodes}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Anode Resistance and Rectifier Voltage

**Step 1 — Slender-cylinder anode resistance (Sunde's equation, exact for $L_a \gg r_a$):**

$$R_{a,long} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L_a}\ln\!\left(\frac{4L_a}{r_a}\right)$$

Plugging in $\rho = 0.25\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$, $L_a = 1.0\ \text{m}$, $r_a = 0.0125\ \text{m}$:

$$R_{a,long} = \frac{0.25}{2\pi(1.0)}\ln\!\left(\frac{4 \times 1.0}{0.0125}\right) = \frac{0.25}{6.283}\ln(320) = 0.0398 \times 5.768 = 0.230\ \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Disc anode resistance (flush-mounted, for high-speed craft):**

$$R_{a,disc} = \frac{\rho}{4 r_a} = \frac{0.25}{4 \times 0.075} = 0.833\ \Omega \quad (r_a = 75\ \text{mm disc})$$

**Step 3 — Sum circuit resistances.**
Cable resistance: $R_{cable} \approx 0.05\ \Omega$ (typical 50 m of 70 mm² copper).
Hull-to-seawater back-resistance: $R_{hull-seawater} \approx 0.02\ \Omega$.

$$R_{total} = R_{a,long} + R_{cable} + R_{hull-seawater} = 0.230 + 0.05 + 0.02 = 0.300\ \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Rectifier DC voltage demand.**

$$V_{rect} = I_{total} \times R_{total} = 568.3 \times 0.300 = 170.5\ \text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Reality Check:** A 170 V DC requirement on a ship is **impractically high**. Either (a) the number of anodes must be increased to reduce $I_{total}$ per anode, OR (b) parallel-connection of anodes is used to lower the per-anode current — the effective circuit resistance per anode then drops. The final design iterates $N$ until $V_{rect}$ falls in the **15 – 50 V DC** practical range. Re-running with **16 anodes** in parallel gives $I_a = 35.5$ A per anode, which raises $R_{a,long}$ only slightly, and total $R$ falls to roughly $0.04\ \Omega$ → $V_{rect} \approx 22.7$ V ✓.

### 3.4 Derivation: Anode Life (Linear Mass-Loss Model)

For inert anodes (Pt/Ti, MMO/Ti), the anode does not chemically consume. The life is limited by mechanical/electrical failure — usually rated by manufacturers as **15 – 20 years continuous service**.

For **consumable impressed-current anodes** (e.g., Pb-Ag, graphite, high-silicon cast iron), the life follows Faraday's law adapted to mass:

$$L_{life} = \frac{m_{anode} \times \eta}{I_{a} \times 8760}$$

**Worked Example — High-Silicon Cast Iron Anode (consumable type):**
- Installed mass: $m_{anode} = 25\ \text{kg}$
- Utilization factor: $\eta = 0.50$
- Current per anode: $I_a = 35.5\ \text{A}$

$$L_{life} = \frac{25 \times 0.50}{35.5 \times 8760} = \frac{12.5}{311{,}080} = 4.02 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{years} \times \ldots$$

*Re-expressing with units balanced (kg · A⁻¹·yr⁻¹ in denominator using the conversion that 1 kg ≈ constant depending on anode electrochemical equivalent — for high-Si iron, $k \approx 0.5\ \text{kg/A·yr}$):*

$$L_{life} = \frac{m_{anode} \times \eta}{k \times I_a} = \frac{25 \times 0.50}{0.5 \times 35.5} = \frac{12.5}{17.75} = 0.704\ \text{years}$$

> This is **why inert anodes (Pt/Ti, MMO/Ti) dominate shipboard ICCP** — consumable impressed anodes last less than one year per 25 kg install, requiring impractically frequent dry-docking.

### 3.5 Complete Numerical Worked Problem (Exam-Style, 14-mark format)

> **Problem:** A product tanker has wetted hull area $6{,}500\ \text{m}^{2}$, is fitted with a high-quality epoxy coating (design current density $i_c = 35\ \text{mA/m}^{2}$), and uses MMO/Ti rod anodes of length $1.2\ \text{m}$ and radius $0.015\ \text{m}$. Each anode is rated at 60 A continuous. Seawater resistivity is $0.22\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$. Determine:
> (a) Total protection current, number of anodes, and the rectifier DC voltage.
> (b) Anode life if the anode has 8 kg of active coating, utilization 0.85, and electrochemical equivalent 1.1 kg/A·yr.

**Solution (a):**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{total} &= A_{hull} \times i_c = 6{,}500 \times 0.035 = 227.5\ \text{A} \\[4pt]
N_{anode} &= \left\lceil \frac{227.5}{60} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.79 \rceil = 4\ \text{anodes} \\[4pt]
R_{a,long} &= \frac{\rho}{2\pi L_a}\ln\!\left(\frac{4 L_a}{r_a}\right) = \frac{0.22}{2\pi(1.2)}\ln\!\left(\frac{4 \times 1.2}{0.015}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.22}{7.540} \ln(320) = 0.02918 \times 5.768 = 0.1683\ \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I_a^{actual} &= \frac{I_{total}}{N} = \frac{227.5}{4} = 56.9\ \text{A} \\[4pt]
R_{total} &= R_{a} + R_{cable} + R_{hull-seawater} = 0.1683 + 0.05 + 0.02 = 0.238\ \Omega \\[4pt]
V_{rect} &= I_{total} \times R_{total} = 227.5 \times 0.238 = 54.1\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{total} = 227.5\ \text{A}, \quad N = 4, \quad V_{rect} \approx 54\ \text{V}\,}$$

> *(The voltage is slightly above 50 V — a designer would step up to 5 or 6 anodes to bring it down to ~36 V.)*

**Solution (b):**

$$L_{life} = \frac{m_{anode} \times \eta}{k \times I_a^{actual}} = \frac{8 \times 0.85}{1.1 \times 56.9} = \frac{6.80}{62.59} = 0.1086\ \text{years} \approx 40\ \text{days}$$

**Interpretation:** With only 8 kg of consumable coating, an MMO/Ti anode in *consumable mode* is impractical. This confirms that MMO/Ti anodes are used as **inert** substrates with a theoretical life of 15–20 years, limited only by coating delamination rather than mass loss.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    A5 --> A6[Anode 1 MMO Ti Rod on Hull Port Side]
    A5 --> A7[Anode 2 MMO Ti Rod on Hull Starboard Side]

    A5 --> A9[Anode 4 MMO Ti Rod on Hull Bottom Forward]
    A6 --> A10[Seawater Electrolyte]
    A7 --> A10
    A8 --> A10
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11[Hull Steel Wetted Surface Cathode]
    A11 --> A12[DC Bus Negative Return Cable to Hull Earthing]
    A12 --> A4
    B1[Reference Electrode 1 AgAgCl Near Anode 1] --> B3[Microprocessor Potential Controller]
    B2[Reference Electrode 2 AgAgCl at Hull Midpoint Far from Anodes] --> B3
    B3 --> A4
    B3 --> B4[Alarm and Monitoring Display in Engine Control Room]
    C1[Dielectric Shield Epoxy Neoprene 250 micron] --- A6
    C1 --- A7
    C1 --- A8
    C1 --- A9
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]** Define **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)**. List its **four essential components**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> ICCP is a method of corrosion protection in which an external DC power source forces a protective current through inert anodes into the hull, making the entire hull act as a cathode and shifting its potential into the protective range.
>
> The four essential components are:
> 1. **DC power source** — Transformer-Rectifier (T-R) unit.
> 2. **Impressed-current anodes** — Pt/Ti, MMO/Ti, or Pb-Ag alloy.
> 3. **Reference electrode(s)** — Ag/AgCl/Seawater cell for potential sensing.
> 4. **Control loop** — Automatic potential controller with alarm and monitoring.

*(Allocate 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing components with one-line function each.)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]** Why is **dielectric shielding** essential around the mounting of an impressed current anode on a ship hull? What material is commonly used?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Around an ICCP anode the local current density is extremely high, producing **acidic** by-products (H⁺ and Cl₂ from water oxidation) and **alkaline** zones on the hull (from O₂ reduction). Both attack and blister the paint coating rapidly.
>
> A **dielectric shield** — typically **250 – 500 µm of glass-flake epoxy, neoprene rubber, or polyurethane** — is applied in a 1 – 2 m radius around each anode to spread the current density and protect the coating from accelerated deterioration.
>
> *(1 mark each for: the problem stated, the function of shielding, the material identification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 marks) — Choice 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** — (a) Explain with a **neat Evans diagram** how an impressed current cathodic protection system suppresses corrosion of a ship hull. **(7 marks)**
> (b) A VLCC has a wetted hull area of $7{,}200\ \text{m}^{2}$ and is coated with a high-quality anti-corrosion epoxy system. Using a design current density of $40\ \text{mA/m}^{2}$, calculate: **(i)** the total protection current, **(ii)** the number of platinized-titanium rod anodes required if each delivers 80 A, and **(iii)** the DC voltage required at the rectifier bus if the combined anode, cable, and back-resistance is $0.25\ \Omega$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. *Draw axes:* Log current density (y) vs. potential (x). Plot anodic curve for Fe dissolution and cathodic curve for O₂ reduction in seawater. Mark $E_{corr}$ where the two curves intersect; the corresponding current is $i_{corr}$. *(2 marks)*
2. *Show external source:* Draw a horizontal arrow (or shifted cathodic curve) representing the applied current $i_{app}$ from the rectifier. Explain that the hull potential is forced to $E_{prot}$, more negative than $E_{corr}$. *(2 marks)*
3. *Define protective band:* Mark $E_{prot}$ between **−0.80 V and −0.95 V** vs. Ag/AgCl reference. Note that beyond −1.10 V, hydrogen evolution begins, causing **hydrogen embrittlement** and **coating blistering** — the "over-protection" danger. *(2 marks)*
4. *Conclude:* Corrosion rate (anodic current) is reduced from $i_{corr}$ to nearly zero once $i_{app} \geq i_{corr}$. *(1 mark)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)}\quad I_{total} &= A_{hull} \times i_c = 7{,}200 \times 0.040 = 288\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(ii)}\quad N &= \left\lceil \frac{I_{total}}{I_{a}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{288}{80} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.60 \rceil = 4\ \text{anodes}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(iii)}\quad V_{rect} &= I_{total} \times R_{total} = 288 \times 0.25 = 72\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Comment to examiner:** $V_{rect} = 72\ \text{V}$ exceeds the practical 50 V ceiling — student should note this and recommend increasing anodes to 5 or 6, which would bring $V_{rect}$ to ≈ 48 V. *(1 mark reserved for engineering judgment.)*

*(Step-by-step marks: 1+1 for (i), 1+1 for (ii), 1+1 for (iii), 1 for practical-comment conclusion.)*

#### **Question B (14 marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** — (a) Compare and contrast **impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP)** and **sacrificial (galvanic) anode cathodic protection** for ship hulls. State at least **four** distinct points of comparison. **(7 marks)**
> (b) A ship is fitted with **6 flush-mounted disc anodes**, each of radius $80\ \text{mm}$, on a hull with a total wetted area of $4{,}000\ \text{m}^{2}$ and design current density $50\ \text{mA/m}^{2}$. Seawater resistivity is $0.25\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$. Calculate: **(i)** the total current, **(ii)** the current per anode, **(iii)** the resistance of a single disc anode, and **(iv)** the DC rectifier voltage if cable and back-resistance total $0.08\ \Omega$ per anode branch. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| Comparison Axis | Sacrificial (Galvanic) | Impressed Current (ICCP) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Self-driven (potential difference) | External DC rectifier |
| Anode consumption | Continuous — anode must be replaced | Minimal (inert MMO/Pt/Ti) |
| Current range | Low (limited by alloy potential) | High, adjustable (up to 600 A) |
| Cost on large hull | High — tonnes of Zn/Al | Low — kgs of Pt/Ti + electricity |
| Monitoring | Difficult (open-circuit checks only) | Continuous reference-electrode feedback |
| Suitability by hull size | Small craft (< 500 m²) | **Large ships (VLCCs, naval)** |

*(1.5 marks per well-explained point × 4 points = 6 marks; 1 mark for an introductory sentence distinguishing the two.)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)}\quad I_{total} &= 4{,}000 \times 0.050 = 200\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(ii)}\quad I_{a} &= \frac{200}{6} = 33.3\ \text{A per anode}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(iii)}\quad R_{a,disc} &= \frac{\rho}{4 r_a} = \frac{0.25}{4 \times 0.080} = \frac{0.25}{0.320} = 0.781\ \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(iv)}\quad V_{rect} &= I_{total} \times (R_{a,disc}/N + R_{cable+back}) = 200 \times (0.781/6 + 0.08) \\
&= 200 \times (0.130 + 0.080) = 200 \times 0.210 = 42.0\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Practical Conclusion:** $V_{rect} = 42$ V is within practical range — design is acceptable without modification. *(1 mark for engineering judgment.)*

*(Step marking: 1.5 + 1.5 + 2.0 + 2.0 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in ICCP questions:**
> 1. **Unit mixing** — using current density in mA/m² without converting to A/m² before multiplication. (3-mark deduction typical.)
> 2. **Forgetting to round UP** the number of anodes. A 3.6 result must be 4 anodes, not 3.6 or 3.
> 3. **Confusing protective potential ranges** — students write −0.85 V without specifying the reference electrode (Ag/AgCl, Zn, or Cu/CuSO₄ all give different numerical values).
> 4. **Over-protection** — failing to mention the −1.10 V hydrogen-evolution limit. Examiners specifically look for this safety boundary.
> 5. **Ignoring dielectric shielding** — in 14-mark answers on ICCP design, omitting shielding typically costs 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing Pt/Ti and Pb-Ag** — Pt/Ti and MMO/Ti are **inert**; Pb-Ag and high-Si iron are **consumable** impressed anodes. Examiners test this distinction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):**
> - ICCP uses an **external DC source** to drive current through **inert anodes** into the hull.
> - The hull is the **cathode**; the anode is just a current-delivery terminal, NOT consumed (if inert).
> - **Four components:** T-R rectifier + anodes + reference electrodes + automatic controller.
> - **Reference electrode:** Ag/AgCl/Seawater is the standard for ships; protective potential = **−0.80 V to −0.95 V**.
> - **Over-protection threshold:** more negative than **−1.10 V** causes H₂ evolution, blistering, embrittlement.
> - **Design current density:** **100–150 mA/m²** (bare hull) ; **30–50 mA/m²** (coated hull in service).
> - **Anode materials (inert):** Pt/Ti, MMO/Ti, Pb-Ag. **Anode materials (consumable):** graphite, high-Si cast iron.
> - **Why dielectric shielding?** Acidic/alkaline micro-environment at anode mounting attacks coating.
> - **Three killer formulas:**
>   $$I_{total} = A_{hull} \times i_c, \quad N = \lceil I_{total}/I_a \rceil, \quad V_{rect} = I_{total} \times R_{circuit}$$
> - **Rule of thumb:** ICCP is preferred for wetted hull area **> 500 m²**.
> - **Sunde's equation** for long-rod anode resistance: $R_a = (\rho / 2\pi L_a) \ln(4 L_a / r_a)$.
> - **Disc anode resistance:** $R_a = \rho / 4 r_a$.
> - **Faraday anode-life** for consumable anodes: $L = (m \eta) / (k I_a)$.
> - **ICCP + Coating = mandatory combination**; never substitute one for the other.
> - **Automatic potential control** uses an SCR-thyristor feedback loop with the reference electrode.
